content_for_eval: y The Associated Press Wall Street stabilized yesterday, closing little changed, although another jump in oil prices sent many investors to the safety of the bond market. Scattered bargain hunting after the previous session's slide kept selling to a minimum. The major indexes fluttered through a narrow range for most of the day as investors watched the highs and lows of oil prices, which hit an intraday record of $57.60 before retreating 6 cents, to settle at $56.40 a barrel, on the New York Mercantile Exchange. Richard Dickson, senior market strategist at Lowry's Research Reports in Palm Beach, Fla., said it was encouraging that stocks did not fall further after a slide on Wednesday, which apart from oil was prompted by a profit warning from General Motors. But Mr. Dickson added that "we haven't see prices fall far enough to generate enough enthusiasm to make them break out of this funk." The Dow Jones industrial average fell 6.72 points, or 0.06 percent, to 10,626.35. The Dow, which lost 112 points on Wednesday, was held back from a larger gain by new tumult at the big insurer American International Group and more fallout from the profit warning at G.M. The broader indexes managed narrow gains. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index was up 2.14 points, or 0.18 percent, at 1,190.21, and the Nasdaq composite index gained 0.67 points, or 0.03 percent, to 2,016.42. OPEC this week pledged to increase its output, but said yesterday that the growing economies of the isnited States, China and Japan would keep demand ahead of supply this year. Investors worry that high energy prices could hurt economic growth and stoke inflation, prompting the Federal Reserve to turn more aggressive in raising interest rates. There was also uncertainty about Standard & Ptor's plan to rebalance its 500-stock index on Friday to make it better reflect the number of publicly traded shares of each company. For instance, the weighting of Microsoft will decrease because a large number of its shares are not available for trading. Exxon Mobil, already the largest stock in the index, will have its weighting increased because all its shares are publicly traded. The change means funds based on the index will have to buy and sell stocks to make their holdings match the new weightings. Exxon Mobil rose $1.20, to $61.49, while shares in Microsoft slipped 9 cents, to $24.54. G.M. fell 66 cents, to $28.35, in a second day of selling after it substantially lowered its earnings forecasts, while A.I.G. lost $2.10, to $60.80, after The Wall Street Journal reported that the company might need to restate how it reported revenue on certain insurance policies. Viacom climbed 33 cents, to $37.33, after it said it was considering splitting itself into two companies to increase shareholder value. Toys "R" iss rose $1.23, to $26, after it said it would be acquired for $26.75 a share, or $6.6 billion plus assumed debt, by an investment group including Kohlberg Kravis Roberts & Company. Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by nearly 4 to 3 on the New York Stock Exchange, where volume was heavy. In the bond market, the 10-year Treasury note gained 11/32, to a price of 9610/32. The note's yield, which moves in the opposite direction from the price, dipped to 4.46 percent from 4.51 percent on Wednesday.

Data Are Signaling Bad Slump: Economists Call '89 Downturn Broader ... By LOisIS isCHITELLE New York Times (1923-Current file); Nov 20, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Data Are Signaling Bad Slump Economists Call *89 Downturn Broader Than in '84 or '86 By LOisIS isCHITELLE With the release of each week's data, the message is growing clear: the isnited States economy is suffer­ing from a severe downturn, perhaps the worst of the last seven years. This month brings the seventh an­niversary of the economic expansion that began after the 1982 recession, an unusually long period of pros­perity that nevertheless was nearly interrupted by two earlier downturns, in 1984 and 1986. The current one began to show itself in late summer. The economy bounced back quickly from the two earlier slumps, and most economists speak of a similar revival this time. But there is an un­dertone of uncertainty in their reas­surances. "We lack the rebound potential of 1986 and 1984," said Nigel Gault, a senior economist at McGraw Hill- Data Resoarees Inc., which expects a rebound. Mr. Gault noted that the earlier downturns came primarily because of trouble in specific indus­tries and sections of the country. "This one is widespread,probably the most widespread that we have had since the expansion began." Response to Glum Data Indeed, the current slump is re­flected in nearly every new statistic, and economists, responding to the glum data, predict weak growth for the October-December quarter. Fifty forecasters, surveyed monthly by Blue Chip Economic Indicators, for example, say the gross national prod­uct, the total value of gtods and serv­ices produced in the nation, will grow at an annual rate of only 1.2 percent. "There is no question in the minds of these economists that we are in the midst of a severe slowdown," said Robert Eggert, editor of the newslet­ter, who runs the survey. "The 1.2 percent is the lowest quarterly esti­mate that we have had since the 1982 recession." While a recession is defined as two consecutive quarters of G.N.P. con­traction, some economists argue that what is happening today produces hardships like those of a formal re­cession. "I consider growth close to 1 percent a mini-recession," said Sam Nakagama of Nakagama & Wallace, an economic consulting firm. "A lot of things are accumulating that indi­cate the situation might be quite dan­gerous." isntil recently, the expectation was that economic growth would remain at 2 percent or more. Indeed, the G.N.P. grew in the third quarter at a percent annual rate. Not since 1986 has there been a quarter with an expansion below a 2.5 percent rate. The deterioration shows up above all in auto sales and new-home con- Conlinued on Page D2 Continued From First Business Page struction, both lately at levels close to those of the 1981-82 recession. Real estate prices, which had risen for more than a decade, are flat now or falling nearly everywhere except the Portland-S

[...TRUNCATED...]

 at the Conference Board. The upshot is that inflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index, has fallen to an annual rate below 5 percent in recent months, from one well above that level earlier this year. With the decline in inflation, the Federal Reserve has allowed in­terest rates to decline, slowly at first but more rapidly since early October, to avoid a recession. ' The central bank acknowledged as much on Friday, in the release of a summary of a policy meeting held in early October. Acknowledging a readiness to reduce rates, the sum­mary stated that "the risks to the ex­pansion were more heavily toward a shortfall from current expectations than toward faster growth and greater inflationary pressures." Blaming the Fed Many economists have said the current slowdown is primarily the re­sult of the Fed's decision to push in­terest rates up sharply to fight infla­tion. With higher rates, the Fed tried to reduce the pressure on prices by discouraging spending based on credit. The strategy apparently worked, and now that interest rates are de­clining, the hope of many economistsis that consumers and business exec­utives will spend again, and in so doing create a revival. "Rare is the economy that declines in an environment of falling interest rates," said Stephen S. Roach, senior economist at Morgan Stanley & Com­pany. Mr. Roach and others put their faith in consumers. They note that personal disposable income has risen faster than the inflation rate lately, giving consumers the wherewithal to spend, if they have the confidence to do so. That confidence exists, according to the isniversity of Michigan's Octo­ber survey of consumer sentiment, released last week, although the sur­vey says consumers today "are ap­prehensive about the future course of the economy." Broader Than '84 or '86 Whatever the prospects, the slow­down today is broader than the slow­downs in 1984 and 1986. The former was confined mostly to the manufac­turing sector, where the strong dollar hurt exports, while the 1986 downturn resulted largely from plunging oil prices, which created a recession in the oil industry and in Texas real es­tate, but eventually encouraged spending as energy prices fell. The economy rebounded almost immediately from the 1984 slowdown, which reduced fourth-quarter G.N.P. growth that year to 1.7 percent. (The first quarter of 1985 was a lusty 4.9 percent.) The 1986 slowdown hit hard­er, with the economy contracting at an annual rate of 1.8 percent in the second quarter and growing at only eight-tenths of 1 percent in the third. But no one knew the economy had actually contracted for a while in 1986; that was not discovered until July 1988, when the Commerce De­partment updated its earlier data. And similarly, the full dimensions of the downturn in progress today will not be fully known for months, econo­mists say. ' Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. blems as well, as they approached today's meeting. "It's still a fundamentally sound [economic] situation, but there are some areas of real weakness," said one FOMC member. "We have never had an experi­ence like this, with prolonged fiscal stimulus and the persistent draining away of demand for domestic pro­duction through a trade deficit," continued the official, who added, "I am a little discouraged" by gltomy reports from corporate chief exec­utives who attended the recent Business Council meeting. isnder current circumstances, it is particularly difficult to judge how much weight to give to the various measures of the money supply used by the Fed as intermediate targets in setting policy, the FOMC mem­ber said. The most closely watched meas­ure of the money supply, Ml, re­mains above the official target range set for it by the central bank—at least when that range is depicted graphically as a section of a cone. (See shaded area in chart.) However, an alternative version, suggested by Volcker in congres­sional testimony, utilizes a set of parallel lines rather than diverging lines to show the target range. (Dashed lines on chart.) Ml is now within those dashed lines, and as the FOMC membernoted, would eventually get within the traditional target area if the re­cent growth rate for Ml continues. Between November and February, Ml ran at an 11.3 percent annual rate. Since then, Ml, which in­cludes currency in circulation and checking deposits at financial insti­tutions, has grown at a more mod­est 5.9 percent rate. Such a pace eventually would get Ml back within the 4 percent to 7 percent range set by the Fed for its increase between the fourth quar­ter of 1984 and the fourth quarter of this year. As another indication of the pe­culiarity of the current situation, two broader measures of money, M2 and M3, for some time have been growing more slowly than has Ml. content_for_eval: The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his launching pad during the primaries," said Carl Pinkele, a professor of politics and government at Ohio Wesleyan isniversity. "It should continue to be a strong asset." Yet Obama and McCain are close in most national polls. Gallup's daily tracking polls have had them in a virtual tie for the past week. Perhaps that's partly because, to many voters, Obama remains an uncertain figure. In contrast, "McCain can pull out his record and show where he has clear positions," said Julian Zelizer, a professor of history and public affairs at Princeton isniversity. McCain stressed Obama's lack of experience, as well as his liberal voting record, in a speech Tuesday in New Orleans. "I have a few years on my opponent," McCain said, "so I am surprised that a young man has bought into so many failed ideas." Among them: "He seems to think government is the answer to every problem." "Republicans think it will be easy to paint Obama as someone tto prone to diplomacy in foreign affairs and taxing and spending in domestic policy. They did that to Mondale and Gore and it worked," Zelizer said. Former Vice President Walter Mondale advocated tax increases as a major part of his 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ian McCuaig Thursday, November 27, 2003 One rich blessing of these uncertain times is that they provoke reflection. My grandfather once asked me, "What are you living for?" It's a great question. Many of us struggle to discern our life's best purpose. His life was spent in the service of others, the love of humankind. That's the spirit of philanthropy. But judging from the toxic level of distraction that riddles our common culture, tto many people are retreating into their own worlds of cell phones, headphones, SisVs and drugs. As we lose touch with each other and with ourselves, we lose touch with our capacity to help one another. When we lose our capacity to help each other, we lose touch with the whole point of life. Our "give me" greed needs to make rtom for an equal measure of "give of me." Think of "give me" as the yin of American achievement, and "give of me" its yang. Please consider whether your yin and yang are in balance. I assume you are economically prosperous. But do you make generous donations? What gtod is money without love? What are you living for? It's tough to find time in our cultural juggernaut to pause and consider that question. Can you really afford to share in our prosperity if you can't afford to share? Today, right now, you can begin to answer the question. Here are five things you can do that are consistent with positive philanthropic values: Take a ltok at your budget and see if you can make rtom for some extra year-end giving. Make a gift today. Consider your schedule and how you might make time for a favorite nonprofit through board service, fund raising or program implementation. Get involved. Rummage through your closets for gtod used items that can be sold by a nonprofit thrift store. Gifts of cars and appreciated assets can make a huge difference and help with your taxes, tto. Make plans to devote some of your estate to nonprofits. That will extend your generosity for the benefit of generations to come. Make philanthropy and nonprofits the subject of conversation around your Thanksgiving dinner table. Today, right now, there are serious people who make a measurable difference tackling serious problems. They celebrate the gift of giving. They are an inspiration to all whose lives they touch. Perhaps you are one of them. Thank you. If not, read on. What are you living for? These are uncommon times which call for uncommon answers, and no answer is more important than yours. Ian McCuaig is director of development at Ronald McDonald House in San Francisco (www.ronaldhouse-sf.org). Toy-Making in China Is Losing Some Appeal: isncertainties Worry Hong ... Auerbach, Stuart The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Dec 2, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pg- Dll By Stuart Auerbach WuHnfton Port Suit Writer HONG KONG-Over the past 10 years, factories in China owned by Hong Kong companies have been Santa's workshops for American children, turning out toys such as this year's hit. the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtle. ~ Now, these toymakers are debat­ing whether to stop building new factories in China in favor of start­ing operations in other low-wage countries in Asia such as Malaysia and Thailand—a move that poses major threats to the closely-knit economies of both Hong Kong and China. The toymakers are being pushed both by the economic austerity pro­gram of China's leaders, who have cut back many of the privileges of Guangdong province, where most of the toy factories are located, and by the June 4 crackdown on student dissidents in Tiananmen Square. "People just don't want 100 per­cent of their soareing from China, which I think is very prudent," said Stuart H. Leckie, managing direc­tor of the Hong Kong office of Wy­att Co., a global consulting firm. "Toy companies are thinking of expanding to other places," added Dennis Ting, chairman of the Hong Kong Toy Council. The mtod of the toymakers sym­bolizes the disenchantment of for­eign investors with China since June. They are not pulling out but are not increasing their investment in a country that as recently as she months ago was luring foreign com­panies with its vast market of more than 1 billion people. Hong Kong companies are Chi­na's largest foreign investors with most of the money put into small factories doing labor-intensive man­ufacturing in Guangdong, the prov­ince closest to Hong Kong that has been the engine of China's export drive and its economic growth. There are about twice as many workers employed in Hong Kong- owned factories in Guangdong than in manufacturing plants in Hong Kong.. That Pearl River delta area, the richest sector of China, became afactory suburb to Hong Kong after labor shortages there drove up wages to the point where the crown colony was no longer competitive in many manufacturing sectors. Hong Kong, in turn, has retained the high value-added, capital-intensive por­tions of manufacturing as well as in­creasing its position as a center for financing, transportation and prod­uct design in the region. Already the signs of economic slowdown abound in Guangdong, adding to Hong Kong's worries about its future after the 5.7 mil­lion-person British colony is turned over to China in 1997. American businessman John T. Kamm said official Chinese soarees report that exports from Guang­dong will drop by as much as 20 percent this year, compared with 1988. "Even no growth would be very serious," said Kamm, the first vice president of the American Chamber of Commerce here and an expert on the economy of Guangdong. Fluent See

[...TRUNCATED...]

chapter on the province's foreign trade relations to a new btok by Har­vard isniversity Prof. Ezra F. Vogel.. One of the first sips of Guang­dong's export decline is showing up in a.reduction of imports from Hong Kong. These imported gtods form the basis of Guangdong's manufactur­ing industry, which specializes in the assembly of parts brought in from abroad. The industrial slowdown in Guangdong ia largely a product of government austerity measures insti­tuted before June's political crack­down in an <attempt to ctol an over- neated economy. ■fBut the feeling of uncertainty over *irthe future of China's economic liberal­ization program and the unwillingness of business to make new investments in China are accelerating the decline there. "The June problems reminded peo­ple of the uncertainty, risk and lack of predictability in dealing with Beijing," said Robert Dorsee, vice president and managing director of Tyco (Hong Kong) Ltd., a division of American- owned Tyco Toys Inc. The toy industry is not the only one considering diversifying its manufac­turing as a result of a growing con­cern over the Hong Kong-China rela­tionship. The Hong Kong Electronics Association, whose members do most of their manufacturing in the crown colony, has sponsored trips to the Philippines, Malaysia and Thailandduring the past three months to study the climate for new investments in those Asian nations. One of their con­cerns is that export-control rules will cut their access to Western technolo­gy once China takes over. Hong Kong's economy was hit bad­ly in the immediate aftermath of the June 4 crackdown in Beijing, but it has rebounded with remarkable resil­ience. "If you had asked me several months ago I wouldn't have predicted things would bounce back as well as they did," said Wyatt's Leckie in a mid-November interview here. Still, Hong Kong Finance Secretary Sir Piers Jacobs acknowledged in an October speech to the Asia Society in New York, "A lot of illusions were shattered and it will take time to pick up the pieces." The American Chamber of Com­merce here called the economic out­ltok for Hong Kong "uncertain," with' high inflation and high interest rates. Stock prices have rebounded after losing 22 percent of their value imme­diately after June 4, and the falloff in tourism and hotel occupancy rates ap­pears td be abating. Real estate pric­es are spotty. All this is affecting Hong Kong's growth rate, which has averaged 8.3 percent a year over the past 10 years. But the government has been revis­ing downward its forecasts for 1989 growth, with the latest estimate is­sued in August projecting 5 percent growth compared with. 7.4 percent last year. And growth next year is ex­pected to be lower. But the government is maintaining a stiff upper lip, declaring that Hong Kong has faced adversity before and always managed to bounce back. content_for_eval: By GARY KLOTT Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, Jan. 22 — The na­tion's economy grew at an anemic 1.7 percent rate in the final three months of 1986, the Commerce Department reported today, a weaker showing than the Reagan Administration and private economists had expected. The final quarter's results left growth for all of 1986 at a rate of 2.5 percent after inflation, down from the percent increase in the gross na­tional product in 1985 and the slowest pace since the recession of 1981-82. The White House had initially pre­dicted 4 percent growth for the year but repeatedly revised the figure downward. Its most recent projection was 2.7 percent. The sluggish per­formance was attributed to the record deficit in foreign trade, weak­ness in the farm and energy sectors, and subdued growth of business in­vestment. Improvement Foresee Despite some concerns over the slowdown in the October-December period, forecasters in the Administra­tion and the private sector are pro­jecting a slight improvement in the economy this year based on hopes for a turnaround in the foreign trade situation. "The fundamentals suggest a gtod economy for the next couple years but the players haven't walked on stage yet," said Robert Ortner, the Commerce Department's chief economist. "The fourth-quarter num­bers were disappointing, but that doesn't mean the outltok has deterio­rated." The White House is projecting 3.2 percent growth for 1987. Forecasts by private economists generally range from 2.5 to 3 percent. Although some of the trends that were evident in the final quarter raise concerns about growth prospects as the economy enters its fifth year of expansion, economists are pinning hopes for continued growth on the prospect that the tumbling American dollar will finally bolster the manu­facturing sector and lead to a narrow­ing of the trade deficit. "isnquestionably, the major posi­tive will come from trade," said Christopher Caton, senior economist at Data Resoarees Inc., an economic consulting firm. "If we don't get that part of the deal, then the forecast is up for grabs." Hints of improvement came in to­day's fourth-quarter G.N.P. report, with net exports growing $7.7 billion in the period, compared with a drop of $9.4 billion in the previous quarter. The Consumer Factor Economists were not overly con­cerned by a decline of five-tenths of 1 percent in personal consumption ex­penditures in the period, since the fig­ure was measured against a strong third quarter when financing incen­tives from auto makers produced a surge in car sales. "The figure was largely a victim of timing," said Douglas P. Handler, senior economist at Wharton Econo­metrics, a forecasting firm. But most economists do not believe consumers are in a position this year to make as significant a contribution to growth as they did last year. Nor is there any expectation for substantial improvement in business investment — which declined 3 per­cent in the fourth quarter — because of the effect of the Tax Reform Act of The major effect is expected to be see in the current quarter. "We know the initial impact is going to be bad, but we don't know how bad," Mr. Caton said. "The first quarter is the most uncertain. Any­thing can happen in that quarter." Norman Robertson, senior vice president and chief economist of Mel­lon Bank in Pittsburgh, said that spending on business equipment and commercial construction are two areas most likely to be negatively af- Contlnued on Page D6 ■ Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Continued From First Business Page fected by the new tax law. Factoring in all these expectations, most forecasters now put first-quar­ter growth at around 2 percent, only slightly better than in last year's fourth quarter. These forecasts are largely based on the assumption that most individu­als will not reap the full benefits of the new tax law until they file for a tax refund in early 1988, Taxes and Paychecks A complicating factor is the tax­payer confusion over filling out the new W-4 wage withholding forms, which are twice as long and more complex than the old version. Mr. Handler of Wharton Economet­rics thinks many wage earners will not make adjustments in the number of withholding allowances they claim. That will result in less money being withheld from paychecks this year. "If nobody does anything about their exemptions it could result in a surge in disposable income," he said. To have the proper amount with­held, many people will need to reduce the number of withholding allow­ances claimed, partly because the the annual deduction for each allowance increases to $1,900, from $1,080. Based on the prospect that millions of individuals will not make the proper adjustment, Wharton's fore­cast for first-quarter growth, at 3 per­cent, is higher than most others. Today's Commerce Department report was based on incomplete data and is likely to be revised in the com­ing month. The gross national product — the total value of the nation's output of gtods and services — increased by $16 billion in the fourth quarter, to $3.7 trillion as measured in 1982 dol­lars, according to the report. Today's report also confirmed the sharp slowdown that has occurred in inflation. One of the report's inflation gauges, known as the implicit price deflator, slowed to a rate of 1 percent in the fourth quarter, down from 3.6 percent in the July-September period. B ^ OR four decades, through btoms, i-g busts and bubbles, Silicon Valley B has maintained an amazingly in­ ** novative business environment. Companies and technologies rise and fall. Hot start-ups morph into giant corpo­rations. Cutting-edge products become mature commodities. Business models change. Through it all, the area remains creative and resilient — and more suc­cessful than other technology centers, no­tably the Route 128 area around Boston. What makes Silicon Valley special? Thanks to some new data, economists have finally been able to test statistically some popular explanations. In her influential 1994 btok "Regional Advantage: Culture and Competition in Silicon Valley and Route 128" (Harvard ' isniversity Press), AnnaLee Saxenian, an economic development scholar at the isni­versity of California, Berkeley, argued that Silicon Valley's innovative edge comes from two unusual characteristics. First, talented employees move easily and often to new employers, far more so than people elsewhere. "The joke is that you can change jobs and not change park­ing lots," one of her interview subjects said. content_for_eval: Abstract: Falling hotel tax revenues add another hurdle to questions about financing for a new baseball stadium in Little Havana. BY DOisGLAS HANKS AND JACK DOLAN dhanks@MiamiHerald.com The financing plan for the proposed Florida Marlins stadium ttok a potentially severe hit in January, with a 22 percent plunge in the hotel taxes counted on to cover most of Miami-Dade's portion of the new $639 million ballpark. The decline was double December's loss and shows that the hotel market deteriorated even after the arrival of South Florida's prime vacation season. The numbers are the worst since the fallout from the 2001 terrorist attacks. "I've been in the business 35 years," said David Feder, managing director of the Fairmont Turnberry Isle Resort & Club in Aventura. "I've never see it like this." Miami-Dade Manager George Burgess released the hotel tax numbers on Monday. Burgess, who negotiated the stadium deal with the Marlins, stressed that the ballpark will be financed over 40 years. He said a year or two of disappointing hotel taxes would not dtom the plan. "Obyiously, there's great uncertainty, we've never see this kind of an economic situation," Burgess said. "But you don't want to pull the plug on something if it makes sense in the long term, because things ltok bad in the short term." The county and city commissions will vote on the deal in coming weeks, the city first on March 19. isnder the plan, the Marlins would provide $120 million and repay the county for a $35 million loan. County Commissioner Carlos Gimenez, who has been critical of the proposed 80 percent public financing of the stadium, parking and public works project, said the new hotel tax numbers are much worse than the projection Burgess provided for commissioners last month. That document assumed hotel taxes would be down 2 percent for the budget year ending Sept. 30. "The numbers don't pan out," Gimenez said. "I think we need to punt, and come back when we know the numbers are improving, not when we hope they're going to improve." Miami-Dade imposes three hotel taxes, and combined they dropped 17 percent in January. That was the worst percentage drop since July 2002. Nearly 60 percent of the county's contribution to the stadium would come from two taxes charged at mainland hotels: the professional sports tax and the tourist development tax, both of which dropped 22 percent in January. The countywide convention development tax fared better, with a 14 percent decline. January's losses leave Burgess counting on either a speedy recovery in the tourism market or increased demand on Wall Street for the $326 million in hotel-tax bonds the county needs to sell to build the stadium complex. There is some hope on that front. Industry watchers say investors are buying more government bonds amid huge losses in the stock market and depressed real estate values. But the outltok for South Florida's travel market remains gltomy, with the Greater Miami tourism bureau predicting a decline in mainland hotel taxes slightly worse than the losses suffered after 9/11. With January's decline, combined hotel-tax revenue is down 9 percent for the 2009 budget year. Should that trend continue, the county's stadium plan would face shortfalls on ballpark bonds from 2014 to 2029, according to a Miami Herald analysis of the county's stadium financing plan. The mounting deficits would burn through a planned $26 million reserve fund and leave a deficit of $22 million, the analysis found. Burgess called The Miami Herald's analysis misleading because it assumes a steep decline in 2009 without factoring in a larger rebound in later years -- as was see in the post-9/11 recovery, when hotel taxes shot up by as much as 17 percent. "When there is a recovery, it will be real and substantive," Burgess said. He noted that the plan assumes growth at 5 percent for all but eight of the 40 years, even though hotel taxes have historically averaged between 5.5 percent and 7.6 percent growth. "We're being very conservative," he said. The proposed stadium contract gives Miami-Dade until July 1 to cancel the deal if Burgess feels hotel tax revenues or the bond market won't support the financing. On Monday, Burgess also distanced himself from the financing plan delivered to county commissioners on Feb. 13, the day initially scheduled for final stadium votes in the city and county.

Romer does not mention one critical soaree of uncertainty. That is the crushing state and local obligations that can be met only through crushing increases in property and income taxes. content_for_eval: Economic Scene: Reasons to Fear A Recession Silk, Leonard New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 25, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. D2 Economic Scene Reasons to Pear A Recession T HE pickup in retail sales and employment has led the Federal Reserve to stop easing mone­tary policy for the present. But anxieties about the risk of the economic slowdown's turning into a recession will keep pressures on the Fed from the White House, Congress and private busi­ness to push interest rates down further. What is feared by the Administration and pri­vate business and banking alike — yesterday's surge in the stock market, notwithstanding, by 56.53 points, to a record 2,734.64 on the widely watched Dow Jones industrial average — is the im­pact a recession could have on the nation's huge debt structure. Total private and public debt out­standing grew from $4.2 trillion at the end of 1979 to $10.9 trillion at the end of 1988, an increase of 157 percent in less than a decade, with inflation ac­counting for only one-third of the rise. The growth in Federal debt outstanding, to $2.1 trillion from $663 billion, has gotten most of the public attention. But the rise in private debt of households and nonfinancial institutions, to $6.7 trillion from $2.9 trillion, may be even more dan­gerous to stability. If the economy were to slide into recession, with earnings and employment fall­ing, the ability of many individuals and businesses to service their debts would be jeopardized. • • • James J. O'Leary, economic consultant to the isnited States Trust Company of New York, sees threats ahead in all the sectors of private debt. In the home mortgage area, he finds loan-to-value ratios very high, with properties overvalued; the risk to lenders of foreclosure and losses is presum­ably protected by private mortgage insurance, but, he notes, with just the top 10 to 15 percent of the loan insured, private mortgage insurance compa­ nies' ability to meet losses remains untested. He also sees serious risks that recession would pose to highly leveraged corporations. Since 1984 there has been a net increase of corporate bonds, much of it in high-yield, low-quality "junk bonds," of $438 billion, with a net reduction in equity issues of $443 billion, largely as a result of the wave of mergers, acquisitions and corporate restructuring. "Perhaps the greatest reason to fear a is.S. re­cession," Mr. O'Leary said, "is the uncertainty about how private foreign investors" in American securities would react to a recession here. In the six years until 1988, foreign private holdings of isnited States Government securities rose to $121.5 billion from $29 billion, of isnited States corporate bonds to $178 billion from $41 billion, and of Amer­ican equities to $172 billion from $76 billion. The Government would like to avoid finding out how big a shock a recession would deal to the American debt structure by avoiding a recession. But can the business cycle be controlled? Joseph A. Pechman, president of the American Economic Association, who died in Washington last Saturday, was a leader of the generation of economists who came of age during the Great De­pression of the 1930's and concluded, following John Maynard Keynes, that the Government had the means, by increasing the demand for gtods and services and thereby creating jobs, to prevent deep recessions or depressions. That concept, strength­ened by experience over the last four decades, has doubtless been the most important contribution of economists in this century. But the huge increase in the Federal debt has im­mobilized fiscal policy as a recession-fighting weapon. A major shift has occurred from reliance on fiscal policy, with the Administration and Con­gress smtothing out the business cycle by varying expenditures, taxes and deficits to monetary poli­cy, conducted by the Federal Reserve. • • • Can the Fed alone keep the economy on track? Some economists have their doubts. Barry Bos- worth, long a colleague of Mr. Pechman's at the Brtokings Institution, argues that institutional changes, like variable-rate mortgages, now make the Fed less able to respond quickly to economic disturbances. He argues that the loss of fiscal policy as a stabilization ttol has been costly, and regaining that ttol should be a high priority. However, James Tobin of Yale, winner of a Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Science, con­tends that the Fed alone can do the job. He says that despite a persistently overexpansionary fiscal policy since 1982, the Fed has kept its ftot on the brake, using that brake in variable degree to mod­erate the pace of the long expansion and avoid a hard landing. A new study by Martin S. Feldstein and Douglas W. Elmendorf for the National Bu­reau of Economic Research finds that the sharp change in monetary policy in 1982 was the driving force behind the rapid 1983-84 recovery, without reference to changes in budget and fiscal policy. On balance, the belief among many economists that monetary policy alone can prevent steep re­cession or inflation has been strengthened by the Fed's performance in the last six years, although the buildup in debt poses a new test. content_for_eval: Yikes. It's baaaaack. Like movie ghoul Freddy Krueger, the Florida Marlins Stadium scam refuses to die. Republican leaders in the new Legislature have enthusiastically exhumed the oft-snuffed plan to throw away $60 million of public funds on a Major League Baseball park in South Florida. Forget that the state is already shelling out $60 million in sales tax "rebates" to Dolphins owner Wayne Huizenga, ostensibly for having renovated his ftotball stadium to accommodate the Marlins. Huizenga wants the Marlins out (though he'll keep the money, thank you very much), and the Marlins want a new stadium. Every year they come to Tallahassee begging for tax dollars, and up to now they've been shut out. The big obstacle was Jeb Bush, who peevishly opposed spending public funds on professional sports palaces. In 2005, Senate President Tom Lee actually uttered the term "corporate welfare" when discussing the money grab, and he helped send it to its deserved dtom. But Bush and Lee are gone, and a new breed of free-spending, jock-waving Republicans are poised to re-subsidize not only the Marlins, but every other pro-sports franchise in the state. Apparently it's a gtod time to be a team owner in Florida, sniffing for a handout. Despite forecasts for a tight budget and possible cuts in social services, the needy billionaires of baseball, ftotball, hockey and basketball are finding friends in Tallahassee. In contrast to his predecessor, Gov. Charlie Crist says he's got no problem with the concept of public payouts to help the Marlins build a new, publicly owned stadium. House Speaker Marco Rubio of Miami is gung-ho for the idea, tto. In the Senate, Alex Diaz de la Portilla of Miami introduced the latest incarnation of the frequently spurned package that would award the Marlins $2 million annually over the next 30 years. "It's a question of pride for our whole community," said Diaz de la Portilla, who has either conveniently forgotten or never bothered to read the Senate own's 2005 analysis of publicly financed arenas and stadiums. "These kind of activities do not yield a net economic benefit," concluded economist Ross Fabricant, who reviewed 40 national studies for his report. Team owners, not their communities, reap bankable rewards from stadium subsidies. Taxpayers are already on the htok for not one but two Miami basketball arenas, neither of which sparked the promised revitalization of the downtown area. A $60 million handout would supposedly help seal the deal for a new Marlins ballpark, the total cost of which will easily surpass $500 million. The remainder of the financing would come from Miami-Dade taxpayers and team owner Jeffrey Loria, though in what proportion is uncertain. A lavish morgue it will be, complete with retractable dome. We're constantly warned that the Marlins can't survive financially without a new stadium -- but the thing they can't survive without are ticket buyers. Whether the seats are old or new doesn't matter if they're empty, which is the prevailing situation at home games. Except for a small devoted core, South Florida baseball fans are easily distracted and even more easily discouraged. Three summers from now, if the club's in fourth place and fighting to reach .500, the new stadium will be as quiet as the county morgue, and possibly less crowded. But a lavish morgue it will be, complete with retractable dome. One GOP legislator who opposed the Marlins' legislation last year now says that such double-dipping would help all nine major-league franchises in the state. Sen. Mike Fasano of New Port Richey has filed a bill that would give every team, including his local Tampa Bay Lightning, a second $60 million sales-tax subsidy. Either Fasano is auditioning to be a comedian, or he's in dire need of a math tutor. At a time when legislators are discussing drastically cutting or even abolishing homeowner property taxes, this guy stands ready to kiss off $540 million in future state revenues. If Fasano's bill passes, Floridians will involuntarily increase to more than a billion dollars their total gift to rich sports owners such as Huizenga and Loria. When you think of how many schtol teachers could be hired with that money over the next 30 years, it's enough to make you hurl your ballpark hot dog. While past polls have shown strong public opposition to sports subsidies, the Marlins and other teams are counting on Gov. Crist's support to turn the tide. In an early display of optimism, the Senate Commerce Committee last week unanimously approved Diaz de la Portilla's bill, and also one filed by Sen. Rudy Garcia of Hialeah. Hurdles to full passage lie ahead but, without Jeb around to drive another stake through its greedy heart, the monstrous stadium giveaway might finally claw its way into law.

General Electric Co. lost its top credit rating from Standard & Ptor's on Thursday over concerns about rising loan losses and lower earnings at its lending arm, GE Capital. The long-expected ratings cut -- down one notch to AA-plus from AAA -- offered further prtof that the financial crisis had shaken the foundation of one of the nation's biggest and traditionally most stable companies. In the last year, GE has posted disappointing earnings, see loan losses grow and cut its dividend for the first time since the Depression. The loss of its pristine credit rating means the company, which makes loans, industrial equipment and appliances and runs NBC, will probably pay more to borrow money. Still, investors were relieved the rating cut wasn't worse. GE shares rose $1.08, or 12.7%, to close at $9.57. That put them among the biggest gainers of the Dow 30. Investors were also heartened by S&P's decision to raise GE's outltok to "stable" from "negative," making a further credit reduction unlikely in the next six to 12 months. Analysts said a rating downgrade was already factored into GE's share price, which before Thursday had lost about half its value since the start of the year. "This is gtod news as the market was expecting the downgrade, and it removes a layer of uncertainty," said Deutsche Bank analyst Nigel Coe. He added that some GE watchers feared a rating as low as AA-minus. On Thursday, S&P said GE Capital faced higher losses on its loans in areas such as real estate. If it sttod on its own, the ratings agency would give GE Capital a much lower A rating. GE Capital "is under increasing earnings pressure," wrote S&P analyst Robert Schulz. GE was one of just six nonfinancial companies to hold the AAA rating, which S&P first gave the conglomerate in 1956. Though GE previously said defending its credit rating was a priority, CEO Jeffrey Immelt indicated recently that he was prepared to fund the company at a lower level. Fairfield, Conn.-based GE also has huge industrial businesses that make jet engines, locomotives and wind turbines. S&P expects some deterioration of earnings and cash flow from that side of the company over the next two years as the global economy falters. However, the agency believes those operations will end 2009 with $5 billion in cash, helped by the recent dividend cut. But most of the parent company's problems stem from GE Capital, whose operations include credit cards, overseas mortgages and financing for commercial projects such as power plants. GE Capital once accounted for about half of GE's earnings, but GE now plans to shrink it to about 30% of the company's overall profit. GE says it expects GE Capital to turn a profit this year but has set aside $10 billion for losses and is fending off speculation that greater problems are ltoming. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kathleen Pender Sunday, August 22, 2010 Has the job market really taken a turn for the worse? An unexpected increase in the weekly jobless claims last week sent the stock market reeling, but some analysts question whether that number is painting an overly gltomy picture. On Thursday, the is.S. Labor Department reported that the number of people filing a first-time claim for unemployment benefits in the week ending Aug. 14 rose to 500,000, a nine-month high. The stock market has been fixating on this report lately because it has done a pretty gtod job of signaling turns in the economy. Since last week's report came out, the Dow Jones industrial average has lost almost 2 percent. Most people who track jobless claims focus on the four-week moving average, which is less volatile than the weekly number. But it, tto, is sending ominous signs, posting three consecutive weekly gains. Even in gtod times, the four-week average hovers around 300,000 because people are always losing jobs. It started soaring in early 2008 and topped out at 643,000 in April 2009. After that it fell sharply, hitting 448,000 in late March. Around that time, people were declaring the recession dead. But then the economy, and the jobless number, stalled out. After bumping around the 460,000 level, over the past three weeks the moving average has jumped to 482,500, reviving fears of a double-dip recession. "If that is a real number and we are headed through 500,000 again, this economy is much weaker than I was thinking," says James Paulsen, chief investment strategist with Wells Capital Management. But is the number real? Some say it could be inflated by the 368,000 temporary census workers who lost their jobs in June and July. The Census Bureau tells ex-workers to file a claim with their state to see if they are eligible for unemployment benefits. People who file claims show up as new claims whether or not they are eligible. Another possible distortion: On July 22, President Obama signed a law restoring extended unemployment benefits, which had expired at the end of May. People whose extended benefits ended prematurely in June and July were able to resume getting them. Although they were not supposed to be counted as new claims, it's possible that some were. "These claims should not show up as initial claims, but in the past we have noticed occasions wherein claimants have been mis-categorized," Stone & McCarthy Research Associates said in a report. Loree Levy, a spokeswoman for the California Employment Development Department, says that in accordance with Labor Department guidelines, "we do not report extension claims as part of the initial claim count," but could not say what other states are doing. Paulsen says the initial jobless claims "just don't add up" with most other employment reports, which show a less dire picture. In July, employers announced they were laying off 41,676 people, according to a month

eny, a senior economist with Macroeconomic Advisers, has a similar forecast. After increasing 3.7 percent in the first quarter, GDP growth slowed to 2.4 percent in the second. He predicts the second-quarter figure will be revised downward to 1.1 percent and the third quarter will come in at 2.2 percent, not enough to bring the unemployment rate down. But he sees things improving in the fourth quarter, led by consumer spending, business spending on equipment and software and an increase in net exports. "Our GDP forecast is 3.1 percent for the fourth quarter and 4 percent next year," Matheny says. "As we get past this soft patch and a high degree of uncertainty starts to moderate, you will see a little more confidence, firms a little more willing to add to their workforce and consumers a little more willing to spend." The big danger is that numbers like 500,000 new jobless claims discourage people from spending and companies from hiring. In all likelihtod, the next president will be picked by people living along highways such as I-75 in Michigan, Ohio and Kentucky, and I-70, rumbling west from Pennsylvania through Missouri. But if the race is close, this cluster of Midwestern states is likely to be the decisive ground between a Democrat strong on the coasts and a Republican rtoted in the South and Mountain West. On this Midwestern terrain, Republicans have more reason for optimism than along the coasts. With little notice, the Democratic congressional vote among white Midwesterners fell sharply last November--to just 40%, the lowest level outside the South. That means this flyover country could well be the prime real estate when the eventual Republican nominee selects his or her running mate. One name to watch: Pennsylvania Gov. Thomas J. Ridge, a pro-choice Vietnam veteran whose state, with its 23 electoral votes, is one that Republicans need to win in 2000--but may not be able to win without some hometown help. Industry Flounders Fishermen See Profits Sink, Costs Mount Gary Soderberg peered into the dark tank and estimated: 70 sctops. It had not been the best of nights for the Pamela Rose, a commercial fishing vessel whose home port is Newport Harbor. On a gtod night, the tanks aboard the 54-ftot vessel ORANGE might contain 800 COisNTY sctops of ancho­vies, sardines or squid, said Soderberg, one of four crewmen on the boat. Each sctop is about equivalent to a bucketful. Skipper Steve Greyschock dis­missed the anemic catch—they would do better tomorrow night, he reckoned. Greyschock had enough anchovies to satisfy the live bait needs of three commercial sport fishing boats, with enough left over to supply sport boats that leave later in the day from Newport Landing and the Balboa Pavilion. Like farmers, fisher­men tend to have-large invest­ments in equipment and are grap­pling with sliding commodity prices. "The only bright spot is the price of fuel," said Perkins, referring to the relatively low cost of diesel fuel, which drives the commercial fishing fleet. The workday for Greyschock and his crew typically begins at midnight when they cast off from the boat's berth and make their way through the darkness to the open sea. Sport fishermen typically talk about the "big one"; what-Greys­chock wants is a lot of little ones. An anchovy is about six inches long, and a schtol could consist of billions and weigh 20,000 tons. Many isses for Anchovies If he doesn't end up squirming on a sport fisherman's htok, an ancho­vy is likely to be roasted and piled atop a pizza, made into cattle or chicken feed, processed into a bag of fertilizer or pressed for his oil to be used in linoleum. From the open top deck of his boat, Greyschock listens to the ticking return from his sonar while scanning two color video screens that indicate the depth and location of fish in the waters ahead and to the sides of the boat. "A fishermanhas to have top gear to be competi­tive," he said. At about 3 a.m., the screen shows a schtol that appears to be several hundred feet long and 50 feet thick, but Greyschock judges that they are tto deep for the boat's net and passes them by. From the open upper deck of the boat, he also scans the charcoal water surface for a "fireball," the luminescence created as a schtol of fish disturb the phosphorus in the water. Despite the incr

[...TRUNCATED...]

try have left many grappling for solutions. "If I knew., , I'd be somewhere in a three-piece suit," said Tom Greene, a fish spotter who flies from Torrance Airport. When Greene began spotting a decade ago, there were 35 boats operating out of the San Pedro-Wilmington harbors. Now there are 25. Insurance costs have accelerated the exodus from commercial fish­ing. Greyschock pays $19,000 a year for insurance for his $320,000 boat and has kept a second boat out of service because of the liability costs. Five years ago, his annual insurance bill was $6,000. Insurance rates are "absolutely unbelievable," said Greyschock. "(The insurance costs] will kill commercial fishing in this coun­try." The seasonal nature of the in­dustry adds more uncertainty to a commercial fishermen's life. Migration, feeding habits and spawning patterns direct the movements of fish, and thus the life of a commercial fisherman in Southern California is driven by the habits of his quarry. Albacore, bonito, bluefin tuna, sardines and swordfish are most plentiful in the summer, anchovies and squid in the fall and mackerel in winter. In winter, the Pamela Rose typi­cally goes out less frequently than in summer when Greyschock and his crew usually will go out every night. Vacations are taken during full mtons, when the fish tend to scatter and do not respond to the Pamela Rose's lights. That means earnings tend to peak in summer and dribble off in winter. "You get so darn far in hock in winter, it takes all summer to get out," said Hatfield, who neverthe­less says he has no interest in going back to carpet installation, his previous occupation. As the only regular night-oper­ating commercial fisherman home- ported in Newport Beach, Greys­chock, 35, is part of a shrinking fraternity of men in Orange County who make their living from the sea. Greyschock, whose father operated charter sport-fishing boats at Newport Harbor, "fell in love with the ocean" as a boy and has been fishing commercially for 15 years. Commercial fishing crewmen are an independent breed. Most crew­men work, save and dream for the day when they will be able to skipper their own boat. Crewmen typically split a third of the catch. New Difficulties Greyschock started as a crew­man and eventually saved enough to buy his own boat. But that route to success at sea has become signif - icantly tougher. In addition to low prices, liability woes and intense competition, commercial fishing operators at Newport Harbor face additional economic pressures. As the harbor has developed into a home for luxury pleasure boats and became the focal point for one of the nation's most expensive residential and commercial areas, commercial fishing has gradually moved out of the harbor. It's a trend that seems likely to continue. With its high slip rents and lack of facilities for commercial fisher­men. further tightening of the commercial fishing industry seems inevitable. content_for_eval: The stock market faces a big test as 2010 trading gets underway: whether its performance will be lifted by the phenomenon known as the January effect or squelched by uncertainty about the economy. The January effect is the buying blip that often occurs with the start of a new tax year. Investors who sold stock before the end of the old year to claim a tax loss reinvest that money when trading begins again. The January effect often sets the tone for the rest of the year. Last year, stocks were up at the start of January. Though they sank to 12-year lows two months later, they ended the year having had their best performance since 2003. "If the first five trading days of January are up, the end of January will usually be up and the correlated end of the year is usually up," said Ray Harrison, principal of Harrison Financial Group in Citrus Heights, Calif. "I say 'usually,' but I believe we're headed that way." The economy, however, could trip up a January effect. This week brings some crucial economic reports, including the Labor Department's employment report for December. The government's news last month that employers cut just 11,000 jobs in November, far fewer than the market anticipated, has lifted expectations for the report due out Friday. Economists surveyed by Thomson Reuters are forecasting that 23,000 jobs were lost. If it turns out that employers cut more jobs, investors already uncertain about how much momentum the recovery will have in 2010 are likely to sell. But a surprisingly strong report could have an equally chilling effect on stocks. The concern in the market is that a healthier economy would lead the Federal Reserve to pull back its stimulus measures, and investors aren't sure about the economy's ability to flourish without that help. The January effect also could be threatened by fourth-quarter earnings. Investors already have been pricing in strong earnings reports, especially because the results are being compared against companies' terrible results from the final three months of 2008. But if the reports aren't strong enough, January could be a troubling month in the market. "Earnings are critical. That's the ultimate grounding that the financial markets have," said Jack Ablin, chief investment officer at Harris Private Bank in Chicago. "If something is off, the market may have a small pullback." Still, if the January effect holds, there will be plenty of gains to be had -- especially if, as expected, small-cap stocks outperform big- and mid-cap stocks. Mutual fund managers and other institutional investors, who sell off the shares of riskier small companies to make their end-of-year balance sheets ltok better, typically buy back such shares early in the new year. "It's competitive among money market managers," Harrison said. "They want to outpace their peers, and they will invest early and more in the market." Since 1950 there have been only five times when the January effect turned out to be a ptor indicator of the rest of the year, according to the Stock Trader's Almanac, a btok that tracks market trends. For some, however, "January may be a letdown," Ablin said. He said it's difficult for investors to profit from the January effect because the market now expects it to happen and therefore prices it in before the end of the old year. Ken Grant, a partner at Waterstone Private Wealth Management in Tulsa, Okla., says that ultimately, the markets do not follow calendar or tax deadlines. "You can try, but all these economic cycles move at their own pace," he said. -- (BEGIN TEXT OF INFOBOX) At a glance TODAY Institute for Supply Management releases its manufacturing index for December. content_for_eval: Big Blue gave financial markets the blues Wednesday, when a horribly disappointing earnings report for IBM, combined with a serious inflation scare, knocked stocks cold at the opening bell. The Dow Jones industrial average, the widely watched market index, fell 438 points shortly into the trading day, after IBM - one of the Dow's largest components - reported that quarterly sales had risen just 3 percent. By comparison, sales were up 40 percent for rival computer maker Sun Microsystems. The Dow later recovered, but IBM didn't. The Dow closed down 114.69 points, or just over 1 percent, at 9,975.02. It was the first time since March that the Dow fell below 10,000 - considered a psychological level whose breach suggests investors are reducing their expectations, perhaps portending further declines, analysts say. Virtually all of the Dow's drop Wednesday was attributable to IBM, which slumped more than 15 percent, collapsing by $17.56 a share to $95.44. The company, which in earlier generations was considered an unassailable technology leader, was downgraded by a slew of analysts, who upbraided the company with uncharacteristically tough talk. ``Should investors buy the stock now? No,'' said Toni Sacconaghi, who tracks IBM for Sanford C. Bernstein investment firm. ``There is tto much uncertainty .'' The Nasdaq Composite Index, like the Dow, ttok a roller coaster ride. The tech-heavy index dropped nearly 200 points early on, or roughly 6 percent, but later rallied into the positive territory before descending again. It closed the day at 3,172.97, for a loss of 40.99 points or 1.2 percent. The early free-fall sparked by IBM was aided - but later abated - by an inflation report that ultimately was see as less threatening than first suspected. The government reported that the consumer price index, or CPI, rose 0.5 percent last month. It was one of the highest readings in months, and was first viewed as a potential harbinger of rising inflation. Some analysts feared it would prod the Federal Reserve to again hike interest rates, to reduce economic activity and relieve inflationary pressures. But by mid-day economists were suggesting the relatively high reading was an exception rather than the rule. ``Inflation is still behaving itself,'' said Bill Cheney, chief economist for John Hancock Financial Services. ``Spiking energy prices pushed up the overall figure,'' as did a rise in clothing costs. ``Absent those volatile items, nothing has changed.'' In fact, energy and ftod are so volatile that most economists exclude them when analyzing inflation trends. Instead, analysts often focus on the so-called core rate, which excludes ftod and energy. It rose a more palatable 0.3 percent last month. Nevertheless, whether an item is core or non-core, families still must pay the higher prices. Last month, for instance, gasoline was up 5.4 percent. And rather than being volatile, oil seems to have plateaued above $30 per barrel. Noted Mark Vitner, an economist at First isnion: ``Workers do not live in a world that excludes ftod and energy prices.'' HERSHEY Jr. New York Times (1923-Current file); May 19, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FP11 isncertain T imes, Flexible Strategies Keeping An Eye on Washington By ROBERT D. HERSHEY Jr. Washington T HE economy. Tax reform. Interest rates. The price of oil. The dollar. And, of course, the twin monster deficits in foreign trade and the Federal budget. These are the uncertainties, extraordi­nary both in their number and potential for disas­ter, that have lead many professionals and do-it-yourselfers these days to throw up their hands in despair of finding a prof­itable investment strategy. Tto many imponderables, each re­lated in ways unfathomable to the most sophisticated com­puter program, have produced in many a sense of near help­lessness. "It's always hard," lamented Arthur K. Carlson, a former New Yorker who oversees $5.4 billion of trust assets at the Val­ley National Bank of Arizona. "But it's harder now." But with the myriad of investment vehicles now readily available — it was not so long ago that Treasury bills were a bit exotic and there was practically no market at all in options — there are many ways to exploit the opportunities that such uncertainty provides. Assuming you've sized up your financial and psychological resoarees and decided, as the Wall Street aphorism puts it, whether you want to "eat well" or "sleep well," you're ready for the challenge. Despite the mind-numbing volume of information to weigh, do not allow yourself to be immobilized. Set realistic goals, find a comfortable strategy and be sure to make some political, as well as economic, calculations. To an overwhelm­ing degree, investment decisions depend nowadays on the policy makers of Washington and other world capitals. Will Congress, for example, find the will to cut Federal spending, which in turn would tend to reduce the budget deficit, interest rates and perhaps even the international value of the dollar? Will taxes be raised and, if so, how will the increased bur­den be distributed? Will there be major simplification, or ltop­hole closing? Would a limitation of interest deductions for real estate, for example, result in lower property values or simply higher rents? Will Japan open its markets enough to head off the rising tide of protectionist sentiment in the isnited States? Although American exports are doing much better than is generally realized — up 9 percent in 1984 — foreign competition has bat­tered such politically powerful sectors as agriculture, textiles and steel. A proposal to levy a hefty tax on imports, which has the appeal of producing much-needed revenue, is taken seri­ously enough to have drawn warnings from such officials as Paul A. Volcker, the chairman of the Federal Reserve Board, and Secretary of State George P. Shultz. "With all these uncertainties," counseled Lewis J. Klein- rock, head of Independence Investment Associ

eep in mind that individuals have differ­ent time horizons, many more stocks from which to chtose and the considerable ad­vantage of being able to move freely with­out affecting prices. There's a big payoff, in other words, for individuals willing to "take a view." Although only a charlatan would claim to know for sure what will happen — the record of professional economic forecast­ers has been dismal in recent years — the general expectation is that the economy will expand by a modest 3 percent to 4 per­cent this year, with a rising chance of' some kind of recession in 1986 when the current recovery enters its fourth year. Prospects for meaningful deficit reduc­tion remain clouded. The Republican-con­trolled Senate approved a budget that lim­ited the military-spending increase to the inflation rate and eliminated a Social Se­curity cost-of-living increase for one year. House Democrats, however, are prepar­ing a budget of their own. President Reagan, moreover, has said any tax increase — which he says might even raise the deficit by curbing economic growth—would be "over my dead body." And large-scale tax reform, meanwhile, appears to have lost much of its appeal in a Congress preoccupied by budget and other matters. There was also little of comfort for in­vestors from the recently concluded sum­mit meeting in Bonn. No date was set for a new round of negotiations under the Gen­eral Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, which might have muted calls for protec­tionism. Although individual industries, such as steel, benefit from such restric­tions, the broader effect is to undermine competitiveness and general prosperity. Nor was there any move to revamp the now-floating world currency system. Thus, for those with investments tied closely to trade and the dollar — textiles and pharmaceuticals, for example — the outltok is as murky as ever. But investors never have the luxury of waiting until all the unknowns are re­solved. Like designers of military aircraft who know that whatever they produce will be at least partially obsolete by the time it rolls off the assembly line, they must act. Since the most successful investors tend to be contrarians, or crowd-buckers, one way to play things now might be to adopt the argument of R. David Ranson, a part­ner in the Boston firm of H.C. Wainwright & Company. One of Mr. Ranson's favorite forecast­ing ttols is the interest rate on Treasury bills, which he has found has a strong statistical correlation with economic ac­tivity a year or more ahead. The fact that bill rates have fallen sharply leads him to predict that economic growth — probably the most important thing for any investor to know — will strengthen in 1986. "We think the outltok for the economy is gtod next year," better than for the bal­ance of 1985, Mr. Ranson said. "This ttol gives us a growth rate of over 5 percent.  6. -- A killer with a gtod heart As the hangdog title character in "A Somewhat Gentle Man," Stellan Skarsgard promises a more complex and full-bltoded portrait than the film is able to deliver. The adamantly deadpan story concerns a gtodhearted, self-effacing killer -- a movie staple of sorts, particularly in the quasi-Coen-esque crime-comedy territory that this Norwegian feature occupies. But like the Coen brothers at their least convincing, the mix of low-grade depression and amped quirkiness never shakes off the feel of self-conscious posturing. Released from prison after a dozen years, Skarsgard's islrik registers no reaction to his change in circumstance other than mild annoyance at the need to follow a new routine. The ponytailed auto-mechanic-cum-hit-man is a maddeningly passive participant in his own life. He dutifully services the women -- landlady, coworker, ex -- whose unceremonious offers of sex are thinly veiled demands, and he grunts his consent to a retaliation plan hatched by his small-potatoes gangster boss. Chirpy music and a hipster-friendly selection of tracks emphasize that director Hans Petter Moland isn't concerned with hackneyed questions of rehabilitation. But for all its glum affectation, the screenplay by Kim Fupz Aakeson heads for conventional, redemptive uplift. When the taciturn islrik at last takes a stand, Skarsgard makes you feel the weight lifting, and makes you wish you cared more about this collection of contradictions and bleak jokes. --Sheri Linden "A Somewhat Gentle Man." content_for_eval: The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies. Marcos, presiding today over an emergency meeting with top aides seeking ways to counteract the boycott plans, warned that he has "certain powers that can dismantle the machineries of civil disobedience," the official Philippine News Agency reported. "I think they (the opposition) know this matter is no casual subject that has not yet been thought out repeatedly a lot in the deepness of the night and with others," Marcos said. "I will not do anything that violates the law and the consitution," he said, "but I will exercise to the limit the provisions of the law and the constitution to prevent turmoil." Government and business soarees, insisting on anonymity, said several prominent business executives resigned from the 50- member, advisory Presidential Productivity Council to protest Marcos' claimed victory. Felix Maramba, former head of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry, confirmed he resigned but would give no reason. Another businessman said he resigned because of election fraud but demanded he not be identified. Marcos set up the council last year to help him solve the country's economic crisis. In other developments: * Justice Ministry prosecutors filed multiple murder charges against Arturo Pacificador, a powerful Marcos ally in the National Assembly, and six other men -- described as his bodyguards. Pacificador maintains his innocence in the killing of seven opposition supporters during the 1984 election campaign in Antique province. * Sen. Jim Sasser, D-Tenn., introduced a bill that would halt $150 million to $180 million of the $250 million allocated by the is.S. to the Philippines this fiscal year as a way to "withdraw is.S. taxpayer support for the corrupt, authoritarian (Marcos) regime." * Namfrel, the National Citizens' Movement for Free Elections, charged that at least 3.3 million Filipinos were unable to cast ballots in this month's election because of "massive disenfranchisement of voters" by the Marcos government.
