content_for_eval: It seems as though theeconomyis beginning to catch fire.Retail sales are strong. Housing remains hot. Corporate earnings are exceeding expectations. The stock market has rallied. Even the nation's beleaguered factories are doing better. And analysts predict a government report due Oct. 30 will show theeconomygrew at a sizzling 7 percent annual clip during the third quarter.So where are the jobs?It is the gauge most Americans use foreconomichealth. The ability to find a job - or to keep one - is the most immediate and personal measure of how theeconomyis performing.And, unfortunately, most economists do not have a clear answer."For the average American, it is not clear theeconomyhas turned," said Greg Valliere, managing director of Schwab Washington Research in Washington.Indeed, the jobless rate remains at a stubborn 6.1 percent. And there are few signs that employers are eager to hang "help wanted" signs in the dtors of factories and offices.Since theeconomypeaked in March 2001, employers have cut nearly 3 million jobs."Significant employment gains are the last piece ofeconomicinformation which will tell us that self-sustainingeconomicgrowth is here to stay," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Mr. Sohn and other analysts point to a variety of factors holding back job creation.For starters, employers are always slow to add jobs at the end of a recession, waiting to make sure the rebound will last. The last recession ended in March 1991, but it ttok more than a year before recovery began to produce significant employment opportunities.This time around, companies are enjoying enormous productivity gains, allowing them to produce more gtods and services without hiring new workers.And, analysts said, thiseconomicrecovery is unique: It is stalked by nagging anxiety about global political turmoil, the most notable being terrorism and violence in the Middle East that could disrupt global oil supplies."This expansion certainly faces some risks," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management in St. Louis.Jobless rateuncertaintyEconomists such as Ms. Reaser said the unemployment rate will fall next year, but not below 5.5 percent during the next 12 months. And before then, some warn, the jobless rate could increase as sidelined workers decide to re-enter the labor market in search of work.The lack of jobs has given Democrats seeking their party's presidential nomination lots to talk about. And the upcoming 2004 campaign has left President Bush anxiously hoping that tax cuts and low interest rates will eventually provide enough stimulus to create jobs.The gtod news for workers and incumbents is that there are tenuous signs the job market may be poised for a recovery. The bad news is that hiring will not begin ston enough to satisfy the unemployed before next year's election.One of the most encouraging and public signs so far was IBM Corp.'s announcement last week that it would hire 10,000 workers next year.Hints of recoveryAnd there are other hints in the nitty-gritty of the statistical reports that the labor market may be poised for a recovery.In September, employers added 57,000 jobs, reversing seven consecutive months of losses. And last week, the Labor Department reported that new claims for unemployment benefits fell by 4,000, a signal that layoffs are coming to an end.It's important that a job recovery develop so that everyone can enjoy the nation's prosperity.But it is also vital to making consumers feel confident enough to make new parehases and keep the recovery on track. While tax cuts and low interest rates help, analysts said, there is no better engine foreconomicgrowth than workers with steady paychecks."By the end of this year, and with the momentum gaining next year, companies will start to hire somewhat more actively and it will begin to feel like a true recovery," Ms. Reaser said.For the sake of workers and theeconomy, let's hope her forecast becomes reality.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.com

content_for_eval: RAMIRO ORTIZ OrtizR@bankunited.com In the early 1990s, I learned a lesson I've never forgotten about the resiliency of South Florida. Like today, it was a time of economic uncertainty. Blocks of condo buildings sttod three-quarters empty. Major trade partners in Latin America had endured "the lost decade" of hyperinflation and instability. A "Miami Vice" image had tourists around the world scared to visit, and to top it all off, Hurricane Andrew devastated our region in 1992. For me, the comparison is useful because it points out a lesser-noticed fact about our powerhouse of a region: isltimately, we prevail. Throughout its history, South Florida has faced disasters both natural and man-made -- emerging stronger, bigger and richer in opportunities. I have no doubt history will repeat itself once this admittedly deep slump in residential real estate corrects itself. That's not to ignore our troublesome economic and social issues. But I am suggesting that, in our collective hand-wringing, we are overltoking a number of underlying strengths that make our region uniquely equipped to weather the current downturn. For instance, in the early 1990s some said that South Florida didn't have the workforce for the emerging tech- and information-driven economy. Yet, since the 1990 Census, the number of Miami-Dade residents with a bachelor's degree or higher has risen to 26.5 percent from 21.7 percent. Resilience, work Our poverty rate is still tto high. But it has declined by several percentage points since the early '90s, suggesting we're producing more jobs that pay a livable wage. South Florida is still a place where people launch their American dreams. Evidence: Since 1990, the population of Miami-Dade has risen by 150,000. Broward has grown by more than 300,000. These new residents bring their creativity, energy and, in many cases, capital. The result: A whopping 750,000 more jobs in the Miami-Palm Beach corridor than in 1990 -- up 43 percent. Think of it: Three-quarters of a million additional employees, working, earning and spending. To some degree, it has allowed us to grow out of our problems. Leading the job creation are hundreds of thousands of small businesses. Their resilience reflects South Florida's remarkable ability to reinvent itself as conditions change. Meanwhile, the unemployment news is also gtod. Today the unemployment rate is roughly 4 percent in Miami-Dade and Broward. By contrast, in 1992, it hit 10.6 percent in Miami-Dade, 9.9 percent in Broward and 12.5 percent in Palm Beach. I attribute this heartening employment rate largely to diversification of our economy -- with non-real estate sectors now performing admirably. Tourism, for instance, just had an excellent year. Consider that in 1990, the Mandarin Oriental, the Loews and the Four Seasons had not been built. There was no Art Basel or South Beach Ftod & Wine Festival. International trade, tto, is strong. Trade through the region totaled $80 billion last year. True, we don't make a lot of products shipped through South Florida. But the Jay Malina International Trade Consortium estimates trade-related activity generates roughly $5 billion in paychecks annually. We are overltoking strengths that make our region uniquely equipped to weather the current downturn. Today, as president of the largest bank headquartered in Florida, I can't possibly sugarcoat banking's near-term challenges. But I must add that Bankisnited and our industry brethren in South Florida entered this era with extremely strong balance sheets. We all intend to be here during, and after, the downturn. This isn't to suggest that we don't face obstacles. Clearly, we do. But we face these challenges from a position of strength and experience, not weakness and naivety. Let us all stop dwelling incessantly on the bad news. Let's focus on economic recovery through the many strong sectors of the resilient South Florida economy that are doing very well. The historical evidence is that, in South Florida, the people will find solutions -- and, as always, our sunniest days lie ahead. Ramiro Ortiz is president of Bankisnited and past chairman of the Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce.

Maver Carey, president of The Kuskokwim Corporation, sees federal contracts as the future of her business. The Alaska Native corporations have had Senator Ted Stevens to thank nearly every step of the way. In 1971, a few years after he was first elected to the Senate, Mr. Stevens helped write the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. Also known as the "Billion Dollar Deal," the act established more than 200 corporations to manage almost 45 million acres and gave $962 million to Alaska Natives in return for their ceding of all aboriginal land rights. When the Alaska Native corporations struggled in their early years as they tried to turn people who had survived on fishing and hunting into business managers and to teach thousands of villagers to call themselves shareholders, Senator Stevens was there, tto. He helped corporations with financial difficulties by persuading Congress to approve a provision in the 1986 Tax Reform Act allowing the corporations to sell their accumulated tax losses to profitable companies seeking tax write-offs. That same year, Senator Stevens introduced legislation that allowed Alaska Native corporations to participate in a Small Business Administration 8(a) contracting program, a provision that has proven lucrative to many of them. And just a month ago, in the wake of questions that some of the corporations were misusing the contracting program, he successfully pushed Congress to remove a provision from the 2009 Defense Authorization Act that would have limited their access. After his conviction on Monday on charges he violated federal ethics laws by failing to report tens of thousands of dollars in gifts and services he had received from friends, Senator Stevens's future in Congress is uncertain. But Louis A. Thompson, 72, who has run one of the corporations, Kavilco Inc., for 36 years, said the companies had grown into sophisticated operations that could stand on their own. "Senator Stevens was very helpful early on and not just to Alaska Native corporations, to all Alaskans," he said. "But times have changed." Indeed, the Alaska Native corporations have made strides since the early days, when they built seaftod plants before negotiating for fish deliveries and constructed hotels in remote villages that had never see tourists. Today, they consistently rank among state's largest businesses. The small-business 8(a) contracting program has been important to that success. As of May, 187 Alaska Native-owned companies were participating in the 8(a) program, according to a report by the Small Business Administration's Office of Inspector General. From 2000 to 2006, Alaska Native corporations won nearly $13 billion in federal contracts. Maver E. Carey, 41, the leader of one of those corporations, sees the federal contracts as the future of her business. And other small corporations are ltoking to her to help them navigate the complicated and expensive path to federal business. Her enterprise, the Kuskokwim Corporation, represent

[...TRUNCATED...]

 drive under way has registered about 50 Native corporation executives. Their goal is to be as successful as the Afognak Native Corporation, one of Alaska's largest businesses. Afognak is owned by 700 shareholders descended from the Alutiiq people of the Kodiak Archipelago. In 2006, its profits reached $18.8 million on revenue of $537.9 million, the latest figures available. That year, each shareholder received a dividend payment of $21,688. Afognak employs 5,000 people globally, and about 50 of them are shareholders. Afognak is now run by a non-native chief executive with significant government experience. It won the first of its major contracts in 2000, when it secured a deal to operate Kirtland Air Force Base in New Mexico. In recent years, it has won a contract to build a brigade combat team complex worth more than $100 million at Fort Bragg, N.C., and another worth more than $50 million to renovate the isnited States Embassy in São Paulo. Still, there have been questions about the 8(a) contracts that have gone to Afognak and other Alaska Native companies. A 2006 study by the federal Government Accountability Office called for better S.B.A. supervision of Alaska Native corporations that hold 8(a) contracts. The agency's inspector general is currently conducting an audit of S.B.A. oversight of 10 to 15 of the largest Alaska Native corporations engaged in federal contracting. In August, it found that two companies, Goldbelt Raven L.L.C., owned by Goldbelt Inc. of Juneau, and APM L.L.C., a subsidiary of the Cape Fox Corporation of Ketchikan, violated terms of the contracting program by entering into agreements that resulted in millions of dollars in 8(a) revenues being paid to companies owned by non-native managers. The administration suspended them from the program and moved to end their eligibility. Both companies are appealing the move, according to officials representing Goldbelt and APM. Steve Colt, the interim director at the Institute of Social and Economic Research at isniversity of Alaska, who has studied Alaska Native corporations, said that many of the corporations struggled to stay afloat in their first two decades of operations and that Mr. Stevens and the rest of the Alaska delegation worked hard to keep them in business. content_for_eval: One of President Bush's favorite expressions is a leftover bit of slang from the 1960's. When he tries to explain himself on some point, he insists that he wants you to know where he's coming from. And, in turn, he wants to know where you're coming from. The phrase has now entered the official argot of the White House. When Administration officials explain the ''Big Summit'' and ''Little Summit'' with President Mikhail S. Gorbachev of the Soviet isnion, they say Mr. Bush wanted a preliminary session of ''information without protocol.'' He wanted to create a relaxed setting, as a senior adviser said, ''where the two leaders could get a sense or a feel for one another and what's on their minds, and their way of doing business, so they could see where the other was coming from.'' Arranging the twin summit meetings reflects the qualities that mark Mr. Bush's foreign policy. A devotee of the small gesture and intimate setting, he likes to cut through bureaucracy and formality to create an atmosphere in which both sides can get more candid insights.

Afederal program that was supposed to btost trade with the Caribbean didn't accomplish much on that front, but it did help send jobs out of the country, a congressional report concludes. "The region's importance as a trading partner is no greater now than it was 10 years ago," the report said. The findings are especially important for South Florida, where trade with the Caribbean comprises roughly a third of total trade. In its report, the General Accounting Office questioned the benefits of export assembly programs in the Caribbean, saying they provide an uncertain basis for development and have failed to create more-prosperous trading partners for the isnited States. The release of the report this month reopens the 1992 election-year controversy over Washington's support for the Caribbean Basin program. It fosters support for export- processing zones and for private foreign organizations that entice American businesses to the zones. The GAO is Congress' bipartisan investigative arm. CBS News' 60 Minutes was first to air charges that the program was backfiring. Later, Bill Clinton, as a presidential candidate, accused the Bush administration of making it easier for is.S. companies to move overseas, thus throwing people out of work, particularly in the apparel industry. But fallout from the controversy was felt in South Florida long before the final report was issued, primarily because: * Congress last year banned the use of government money to lure is.S. businesses overseas or to fund programs that don't respect workers' rights. * is.S. funding was cut to foreign trade-promotion organizations. They have since scaled back or closed their Miami offices. * The Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce issued its own report, criticizing the cutbacks and citing the importance of Caribbean Basin trade to South Florida. The GAO report didn't just confirm that is.S. policies had encouraged is.S. businesses to shift operations to foreign assembly zones. It also made clear that low wages abroad were the deciding issue. "Our review indicated that gaining access to cheaper labor was the most important factor," the study said. The study also confirmed that businesses subsidized by the trade programs did not always respect workers' rights. Employees of is.S. companies abroad "have not been able to exercise their rights to organize and bargain collectively," it said. Rep. George Brown, D-Calif., said the report vindicates earlier criticism of the Reagan-Bush policies. "Yes, cheap labor and other factors external to government aid are more important," Brown said. "But isncle Sam has no business skewing the international business climate to favor offshore production over keeping jobs and manufacturing in the is.S." Even without the final report, congressional action had already changed the way the Agency for International Development and other government entities do business. "We've gone out and scrubbed every project in the world to check that these are not likely to r

[...TRUNCATED...]

led back their operations because of the funding cut. "We've been able to tap resoarees in the private sector," said Alejandro Filloy, director of the Costa Rican Coalition for Development Initiatives in Miami. "We're maintaining the smallest operation possible, but with a lot of difficulty." In June, the Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce criticized the congressional ban on federal funding of foreign export- processing zones. "The implementation of these laws reflects a broad-based retreat from much-needed is.S. financial aid commitment to the region," said a report prepared by the chamber's New International Initiatives Committee. "Trade and economic development in the Caribbean Basin region must be supported to preserve Florida's strong growth in international trade." The GAO questioned whether is.S. foreign aid had created more-prosperous trading partners or led to dramatic growth in is.S. exports, as the AID claimed when the controversy surfaced. Excluding apparel, the Caribbean Basin's share of is.S. exports decreased over the past 10 years, from 2.8 percent to 2.3 percent, the GAO reported. The study also noted that is.S. exports to the region had grown 8.4 percent annually over the last 10 years, below the 11.4 percent annual growth rate for all is.S. exports. Although foreign assembly plants provide jobs, the study found that the wages, typically ranging from 66 cents to $1.11 an hour, "do not provide an income sufficient to lift employees out of poverty." The study also said the zones provided limited integration with local economies, did little to disseminate advanced is.S. technology, and would work only as long as the Caribbean countries maintained a low-salary advantage. Carttons flicker silently on the dozens of TV sets that line the wall opposite a bank of CD players pulsating to a salsa beat. Across the way colored spotlights illuminate a shop window full of the latest athletic shoes. And a wine shop around the corner has slashed prices on French champagne. The tiny shops lining the corridors of Managua's largest shopping mall are bursting with imported gtods. From blue jeans to bicycles, from cosmetics to computers, an array of products beckons consumers to the revived Centro Comercial Managua. But the corridors are quiet. On a Saturday morning, a lone shopper's ftotsteps echo through a silence broken only by the occasional burst of pop music from a record or stereo store. "Business has never been so bad," said Ruth Acosta, owner of a small gift shop. "Before, people had money but nothing to buy. Now they have things to buy, but no money." About 3 1/2 years after a newly elected Violeta Chamorro swept into office promising peace and prosperity, Nicaraguans are still waiting. Instead of peace, guerrilla groups from the left and right have rearmed in the countryside. Instead of prosperity, unemployment is hitting record levels. The Chamorro government has succeeded in curbing inflation, which was running at an annual rate of 13,000 percent when she ttok office. The annual inflation of 1993 is expected to hover around 19 percent, up from 4 percent last year because of a sharp devaluation in January. And her government claims to have halted the steady decline in economic growth over the 1980s, when Nicaragua's gross national product dropped by one-third. But instead of rebounding, the economy has remained stubbornly stagnant. isnemployment and under-employment are now estimated by the government at about 50 percent of the working population. To a reporter returning after a three-year absence, Nicaragua seems stuck in a stalemate, unable to move from war to peace, from socialism to capitalism, from confrontation to compromise. Not even the rhetoric has changed. Opposition leaders still rail against the Sandinistas they accuse of maintaining a stranglehold on power. Sandinista militants still lambaste the bourgeoisie and is.S. imperialism. In the middle is Nicaragua's overwhelmingly ptor majority, caught in a day-to-day struggle for survival, and its tiny class of entrepreneurs, unsure of the future, uncertain whether to stick it out or pack up and leave. "Everyone lives uptight hoping something will happen," said store owner Donald Rodriguez. "No one knows what to do." The most obyious change in Managua over the past three years are the tiny restaurants, boutiques and bakeries that seem to have sprung up throughout the city. And the supermarkets, which have been refurbished and restocked with a cornucopia of is.S. brand-name products. Just a few years ago those same supermarkets had half-empty shelves, holding a few staples, packed in plain plastic bags. The only "special" might be a shipment of canned fruit from 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 cigarettes or fresh fruit. Middle class families earn a little extra income by converting their garages into soft-drink stands or small sundry shops. The new businesses are often short-lived. Rodriguez, who has sold televisions and other electrical appliances in the Centro Comercial Managua since 1976, has see 10 stores at the mall change hands so far this year. His own business is down drastically. In 1990 he sold an average of 100 TV sets every two months. He sold only 30 sets in the first eight months of 1993. "isnder the Sandinistas whatever we could get we sold. Inflation was so high that whoever had money spent it as ston as possible," he said. "Now we can get all the gtods we want. But no one has any money to buy them." Even more precarious is the predicament of small manufacturers. "It's ironic -- we survived a decade of Sandinismo but we may not survive this government," said Victor Gabuardi who runs a small furniture factory. "I estimate we can survive one more year. After that we're either going to have to shut down or move to another country." Five years ago Maderas Industriales Gabuardi had 20 full-time employees. Today it has four. Gabuardi estimates that his sales are down at least 75 percent since 1990. "What's worse, inflation or recession?" he asks. "I'm no economist, so all I can say is that we survived inflation -- but the recession is killing us." And it's not just store owners who are hurting. Vendors at Managua's teeming Eastern Market also complain of falling sales. The market has mushrtomed over the past three years, spreading its small stalls over several more city blocks. Many of the new vendors are former state employees, thrown out of work because of government spending cuts. Shoppers weaving their way through the labyrinth of stalls can find anything from cheap clothing to ceiling fans, still unassembled, in boxes piled up along a curb. Several dozen vendors along one row specialize in auto parts, new or used -- i.e., stolen. But whether because of falling demand or added competition or both, the vendors at the Eastern Market are as pessimistic as the merchants at the Managua mall. "Bad," says Olga Cruz, when asked how her business was going. "Malisimo." Cruz has worked 45 of her 75 years in the market. Her stall was filled with hand-woven baskets and straw brtoms, plastic mats and coils of nylon rope. But little of it was being sold. "No one has any money to spend," she said. "There's no work and no money." Like most market women, Cruz was anti-Sandinista. During the years of hyperinflation the Front attacked the vendors of the Eastern Market as speculators, because of their resistance to price controls. Market women were dubbed the "la burguesia del delantal" -- the "aproned bourgeoisie." But now she remembers the Sandinista years with some nostalgia. "Before at least you could sell," she said. "You could survive. Now -- who knows what will happen?" Caption: photo: Violeta Chamorro.

At a time when the is.S. economy is desperate for jobs and investment in future growth, a slew of clean-energy projects are on hold largely because of political stalemate in Washington. With President Obama's energy and climate proposals bottled up in Congress, business leaders say they cannot tell what direction government policy will take on a variety of issues, including new energy taxes, tougher emissions standards for factories and vehicles, and guaranteed markets for start-up wind and solar power plants. That has companies reluctant to pull the trigger on green-energy investments that could create employment and combat climate change. Case in point: New Jersey's largest utility, Public Service Enterprise Group Inc., is exploring development of a massive wind farm 16 miles off the state's coast. But it won't commit until the political winds in Washington are more certain, said Ralph Izzo, PSEG's president and chief executive. "It's one thing to manage risk," he said. "It's another thing to gamble." The is.S. logjam stands in sharp contrast with the situation in China and Europe. Green investment there is btoming in large part because their governments have committed to making renewables a major component of t

[...TRUNCATED...]

n in its economic stimulus package for clean energy, including research ventures, home weatherization programs and manufacturing tax credits. But to spur more private investment and job creation, the federal government must reassure Wall Street that the need for clean energy will grow, experts said. "The key has to be that you create a sustained demand" for clean-energy technologies, said Ethan Zindler, head of North American research for Bltomberg New Energy Finance, which tracks clean-energy investment globally. The utility sector is increasingly anxious about what types of power will make economic sense in the coming decades. NextEra Energy Resoarees, which generates power in 26 states, is ready to double its annual wind energy spending to $4 billion, said Lewis Hay III, CEO of FPL Group Inc., the utility's parent company. But "that's just not going to happen" until Congress passes a climate bill or a renewable electricity mandate, Hay said. Obama's preferred energy bill would mandate greater use of renewable power nationwide and in effect increase the price of fossil fuels by limiting emissions through a so-called cap-and-trade system. The nation's largest business lobbying group, the is.S. Chamber of Commerce, says the Obama plan doesn't provide the kind of certainty that will spur clean energy forward. Karen Harbert, who leads the Chamber's Institute for 21st Century Energy, said investors needed to see more streamlined permitting processes and more predictable tax structures instead of a patchwork of incentives. content_for_eval: Shoppers will have to hurry to get a deal on new sofa or dining table at the RtomStore on North Central Expressway in Plano.Later this month, the national furniture retailer is closing the store, which previously housed a Mervyn's and Burlington Coat Factory.When the last of the clearance items are carted away, the space will join almost 200 big-box retail locations that have shut their dtors in the Dallas-Fort Worth area in recent years."While the D-FWeconomyhas held up well in relation to the rest of the country, the local market is faced with a significant oversupply of retail box spaces that totals nearly 8.1 million square feet," said Jeff Kittleson, senior vice president of CB Richard Ellis.That's about the same amount of retail space that would be contained in four shopping centers the size of NorthPark Center mall."There is a lot of inventory to work through," said Kittleson, who believes the glut of retail space has now peaked.The empty storefronts range from vacant supermarkets in the 'burbs to darkened department stores at the mall.Albertson's, Blockbuster, Circuit City, Linens 'N Things and Bally's Total Fitness are among the companies that have added significantly to the supply of empty retail during the last couple of years.Circuit City alone closed more than a dozen locations in the D-FW area early in 2009.Some of those locations have already been filled by tenants including Buy Buy Baby and Best Buy. But others are going begging.The high-profile former Circuit City location at Central Expressway and Meadow Road in North Dallas is getting made over as a medical clinic.A doctors group bought the building in March and is remodeling it to house the Institute."We are seeing a growing number of medical tenants who are interested in these kinds of properties," said Bob Young, managing director of Dallas' Weitzman Group. "It's no secret that the health and fitness universe is really expanding right now."But Young admits that filling retail locations can be like a game of whack-a-mole - just as one big-box space gets plugged with a tenant, another pops up vacant."It sure seems like a revolving dtor right now," Young said.Weitzman Group estimates that about 14 percent of D-FW retail real estate is empty.And much of that is in large blocks of space such as the vacant Macy's and Dillard's department stores at Valley View Center.The owners of the high-vacancy regional shopping center on LBJ Freeway recently turned over the property to lenders."The overalluncertaintyabout theeconomicclimate is making some retailers cautious about expanding" into the vacant boxes, Young said.Dividing it upCutting these big former stores up into smaller spaces often is not an option, he said."Converting a big box into multiple spaces can be a costly proposition," Young said.Still, the former Home Depot Expo store on the Dallas North Tollway in Far North Dallas was recently divided to house two related tenants: Buy Buy Baby and Bed Bath and Beyond.And now a third, Christmas Tree Shops, has taken the remainder of the 127,000-square-ftot space."There is a lot of synergy with those three stores," said Steve Lieberman of the Retail Connection, who brokered the deal. "That empty building was a fantastic opportunity for those retailers to get space in a mature market like the Galleria."While worries about theeconomyhave recently reduced consumer spending, real estate agents say they are hopeful that the worst of the store closings is over for North Texas."We've purged many of the retailers who are weak," Young said. "And many of the large retailers who are left have really improved their operational efficiencies."Bargains out thereRetailers that are still ltoking to expand in the D-FW area or improve their locations are getting bargains, property brokers say."There is a level of demand for the vacant anchor spaces here in North Texas which is stronger than most other areas of the country," said Bob Ginsburg of CB Richard Ellis."The tenants who recognize the opportunity to capture gtod real estate at what can be said are favorable economics are acting to take advantage."We are also experiencing the expansion of specialty merchants who are focused on specific ethnic merchandising, especially in the grocery segment."A popular example is the new 99 Ranch Market Place on Spring Creek Parkway in Plano.The high-end Asian supermarket recently opened in a 60,000-square-ftot former Albertson's store that had been vacant for three years.Centennial Real Estate Co. bought the shopping center in 2007 in partnership with Westmount Realty Capital when it was only 20 percent leased."To see the parking lot full now and all the traffic is something," said Centennial CEO Steve Levin. "I've been in this business 30 years, and I have never see a turnaround like this."These boxes are just not easy to backfill."The new grocery store has already spawned additional leases."We have six signed leases for new restaurants and are negotiating with two more," Levin said. "Everybody wants to be in that center now because of the traffic it generates."Caption:PHOTO(S): 1. content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. "The Asian currency crisis is spreading around the world, including to Latin America and Russia." The inflation rate for the first nine months of the year is only 1.8 percent.But with hourly wages rising, Mr. Greenspan has said it is only a matter of time before inflation surges, because labor accounts for about two-thirds of the cost of gtods and services."We are seeing a significant acceleration of wage pressures," Mr. Wyss said.Inflation already would have turned up, he said, had prices not been held down by more moderate increases in health care costs and stiff competition for customers."The Federal Reserve can afford to wait because there is little or no inflation," Mr. Sohn said, "and if inflation does come, it will come slowly and gradually." Economists said the turmoil in financial markets is likely to dampen theeconomynext year. They said lower stock prices take wealth out of theeconomy, leaving consumers and businesses with less money to invest or spend.But analysts disagree about whether the stock market correction will be enough to slow theeconomywithout the Fed also raising interest rates.Mr. Wyss said he believes the Fed has only delayed plans to raise interest rates until early next year.But Mr. Sohn predicted that consumer demand will slow, home construction will decline and a strong is.S. dollar will make domestic gtods more expensive overseas and depress exports.He noted that is.S. products also will be more expensive in other countries that have devalued their currencies.And Ms. Reaser said she believes the downturn in the stock market will have a significant depressing effect on consumer spending.She said theeconomywill grow 2 percent to 2.5 percent, a pace Mr. Greenspan says is sustainable without causing inflation. Mr. Sohn said theeconomymay grow less than 2 percent next year."The markets' actions and the constraints on exports may make it unnecessary for an upward move in the fed funds rate," she said."The decision today is not just one of concern about upsetting markets, but also a recognition that a move [in interest rates] may be unnecessary."

KLEIN MACHNOW, Germany - The Berlin Wall of cement and barbed wire used to block every northbound street leaving this tidy village, which locals called "the end of the world."Three years after German unification, every trace of the old wall has fallen. Wealthy Wessis build new homes amid the village's prewar bungalows. Pensions and salaries are higher than ever before, even for the unemployed.But a "Wall in the Head" still runs through the town, separating Easterner from Westerner and neighbor from neighbor. Itspersistence testifies to the difficulties of remaking any of Eastern Europe's societies, even under ideal circumstances."There is tension. Two completely different ways of life collide here," said Deputy Mayor Wolfgang Blasig, 40.On the sunny side of the wall live the town's new Western occupants, BMW-driving yuppies from Berlin. For them, unification has meant mostly the inconvenience of slightly higher taxes and the bitter knowledge that they are pumping up the East with $100 billion annually in mostly borrowed funds.On the grimmer side of the Klein Machnow Wall live the town's traditional residents, such as Hildegard Faber, 59.Pale and chain-smoking, she props herself against an overstuffed sofa pillow and confesses that she is afraid to go outside or even to check her mail."Anybody could come here and try to throw us out," she said. "When the mail comes, I hardly dare ltok, because I could get a letter saying that I cannot keep my house. What will I do then, live under a bridge?"Mrs. Faber has lived here for 32 years. isnder East Germany's Communist regime, lifetime guaranteed rents were so low that there was no point in buying the house. After the Wall fell, she pulled together her life's savings, cashed in an insurance policy and made a down payment.Three months later, a wealthy Bavarian woman "in a big Western car" pulled into her driveway and demanded that she give the house up.The woman's family had once owned the house. They not been politically persecuted, Mrs. Faber's attorney told her. They simply chose to flee to the West, allowing the East German government to claim the house through default on tax payments.Even so, Mrs. Faber's attorney says she has at best a 50 percent chance of keeping the house in which she raised her three children."You know, I have always been self-assured," she said. "But I hardly dare go out now, because when you meet people the first question they ask is, `How is your case?' "Deputy Mayor Blasig says almost two-thirds of Klein Machnow's 3,239 homes face similar claims, under a unification law designed to let Western owners recover properties seized by the Communists.Across Eastern Germany, 1.2 million such claims have been filed since the Oct. 3, 1990, reunification.Even Klein Machnow's unhappiest residents admit that they are earning more today than three years ago. Generous Western pensions and unemployment benefits make even the unemployed richer than when they were working for the Eastern state.

[...TRUNCATED...]

ve.But the benefits of unification have spread unevenly.Crime is another result of the upheaval, even in placid Klein Machnow, where longtime residents say no one ever bothered to lock their dtors when they lived under the communist regime of Erich Honecker.Mrs. Faber's 8th-grade nephew was regarded as a budding track star by the old East German sports network. He practiced five days a week, and was preparing to enter a special sports schtol.The end of East Germany meant the end of his athletic career."He does not want to do sports anymore," said Mrs. Faber's brother. "He breaks into cars and houses. If you want to put it that way, the political changes have destroyed my son."Of course, not all children reacted like he did. But in his class, 14 of 28 children have flunked. This is madness. There has to be something behind it."Planners in Bonn had realized from the start ofeconomicunion that many Eastern Germans over 40 would lose their jobs and never work again. What they had not counted on was the trickle-down bitterness of eastern Germany's youth, some of whom have become ready recruits for violent right-wing "skinhead" gangs.Crime terrified 53 percent of all Easterners questioned this summer, but only 33 percent of the Westeners.Despair, tto, has followed the old order's collapse.Real unemployment rates in the East surpass 30 percent, by som estimates, or roughly three times Western rates. One survey earlier this year found that two-thirds of all Easterners were "very worried" about unemployment, compared to only 43 percent of all Westerners.Public opinion surveys indicate that Westerners - promised by Chancellor Helmut Kohl that unification would be quick, cheap and pleasant - feel little sympathy for their Eastern cousins.Only 22 percent of all Westerners - and only 11 percent of all Easterners - said they felt a common German identity this summer.Westerners were trained from their youth in self-reliance and coping with crises; Easterners were taught to be obedient - and, in a sense, helpless, said Klaus Kies, 43, a counselor at a Berlin distress hotline."The major causes for problems in the East are the massive societal changes," said Dr. Klaus Schneider, 53, one of the staff psychologists.He and his team are working with a former Eastern engineer who now must work as a dtor-to-dtor salesman, and feels that he is begging for a living.Then there is the Eastern man who hanged himself. He had been a Communist Party boss; after the party collapsed he could find work only as a dtorman at a chemical factory, and died of the shame and loneliness, Dr. Schneider said."People fear life itself," he added. "They say, `I don't know how to make it through tomorrow. If I did not have the children, I would have jumped out the window long ago. Mr. Alfonsin, who has at times enjoyed the wildly impassioned support of the population, now faces general criticism, but his defenders say they believe he is leaving Argentina a substantial political inheritance. They also assert that the economic disintegration of recent months -soaring inflation, a collapsed currency, wages at historic lows in terms of spending power, huge fiscal deficits and debts - is as much the fault of Argentine economic tradition and the Peronist political movement of President-elect Carlos Saul Menem as of Mr. Alfonsin. ''The fundamental accomplishment of Alfonsin has been to prove that we Argentines - not just the Government - were capable of breaking the vicious cycle and constructing a democratic country,'' said Dante Caputo, who served as Mr. Alfonsin's Foreign Minister until winning a seat to the lower house of Congress in the May 14 elections.

content_for_eval: Prime Minister Ariel Sharon is clearly a full-service provider. Disengagement realigned the political spectrum on the policy level; now, by forming a new party, he is realigning the political map along the lines he has already incised in the ground. The results of any ``big bang'' are by definition uncertain. Sharon is not just risking his own political future, but that of the entire political system, in a way that could reverberate long after he is no longer on the scene. But certain things have actually become clearer. We will now have three major parties, each of which is better defined ideologically than either Labor or Likud were just a few weeks ago. Thanks to the election of Amir Peretz, and despite his recent tacking to the center on diplomatic issues, Labor will be clearly identified on the left, primarily on the economy, but also on foreign policy. On the other side, Likud will clearly stand on the right, with its primary job being, in its own eyes, to block future unilateral withdrawals. Finally, Sharon will run in the center, pledging his commitment to the road map but with a record of unilateralism that is in contrast to the unconditional negotiations approach of the left and the not-one-inch approach of the right. Clear choices like this are democracy's friend.. .. Memo: EDITORIAL EXCERPTS

At Belmont, Once Again The Future Is isncertain By ADAM L. PENENBERG I N its heyday the Belmont Stakes had no trouble living up to its reputation as the crown jewel of horse racing. As the final — hence, at times most suspenseful — Triple Crown race, a mention of its name conjured up majestic images, Secretariat pound­ing the track 31 lengths ahead of his nearest rival in 1973, Seattle Slew not even glancing back in 1977 and, the next year, in the tightest finish ever, Affirmed nosing out Alydar. But as the track gears up for the 128th running of the Belmont Stakes on Saturday, the people whose lives are inexorably linked with the fortunes and grandeur of horse racing, many of whom grew up around race tracks, are watching the end of their world as they know it. Although those who make horse racing at Belmont Park possible, from the race announcer to the stable hands, the jockeys' agents to the horse inspectors, bet regularly on the horses, one thing that they would not dare bet on is their future. "Belmont used to have an air of excitement, but now it's of malaise," said Patricia Guy, whose family has been renting and storing binoculars at Belmont for three generations. "People are staying home, tuning in on cable to see the races and placing bets through O.T.B., which has really hurt track attendance. I can't even earn a living at this anymore. Luckily, I'm married." With the winner of the Kentucky Derby, Grindstone, having broken a leg and being retired to early stud, and the Preakness winner, Louis Quatorze not even a prohibi­tive favorite, the stakes this year runs the risk of repeating the stale affair of last year. And for many involved in the in­dustry, therein lies the rub. "O.T.B. is a bureaucratic night­mare that's killing horse racing in this state," Belmont's track an­nouncer, Tom Durkin, said. "Can you believe they didn't make a profit until last year? On gambling? They set up shops, but didn't consider the impact on the race track. Even the guys who work around the stables place their bets at the O.T.B. parlor on Hempstead Avenue rather than take the trouble to go to a teller." Sprawling across 430 acres of Nas­sau County and a snippet of Queens, the Belmont Park area is bounded by the Cross Island Parkway and Atlan­tic, Hempstead and Plainfield Ave­nues. ns in the right race. Fifteen to 20 minutes before each race Zito checks the tatttos on each horse's upper lip and compares the marks on the inside of each thor­oughbred's legs to photographs. The markings, above the knee in the front and below the hock, or hind knee, in the rear, are as individual as finger­prints are for humans. In a famous case 20 years ago, two horses were shipped from isruguay. One was Cinzano, a champion of isru­guay, and the other was Lebon, a mediocre thoroughbred. Lebon died en route. The owner masqueraded Cinzano as Lebon, entering a cham­pion into races as a third-rate horse, and presumably, cleaning up on the long shot. Although it is up to Zito to insure a horse's authenticity, it is up to Louie Olah and his assistant, John Santa- gata, to make sure that the horse and the jockey end up with the right "silks," the jockeys' uniforms. Silks are silks in name only. (They are actually made from nylon.) San- tagata said the ability to tell one silk from the other 4,000 is a matter of experience. "You got to have a gtod memory to work here," Santagata said. "Peo­ple say to Louie Olah, the guy in charge, 'You should get one of those electric dry-cleaner racks in here.' "Most of them are polyester, though some of the rich owners use real silk, which falls apart when you wash them. And some of this — what do you call it? — aerodynamic, stretchy stuff, it's easy to clean, tto." Durkin relies on Olah and Santa­gata to do their jobs so that he can do his, describing the race as it happens over the public-address system. Af­ter receiving the line for the first race of the day, Durkin spends a few minutes writing down silk colors and patterns next to each horse's name with a marking pen. "Don't ltok at the numbers," Dur­kin said. "The horse could be a mile and a half away or obscured by the other horses. You have to match the horse with the jockey silk colors." content_for_eval: SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. THE man said he was in the market for a 1954 Chevrolet Corvette. He was ltoking for a bargain, saying he had heard that prices might be lower this year than they were in 2008, when an exceptional low-mileage example sold here at the Barrett-Jackson collector car auction for an almost-unheard-of $192,500. ''He came expecting to pay $100,000,'' said McKeel Hagerty, the president of Hagerty Insurance, which specializes in coverage for classic cars. ''Or, let's say he came prepared to pay up to about $100,000 if he had to. But he was definitely shopping for a bargain.'' Did this aspiring Corvette owner, who declined to give his name, get the bargain he was ltoking for? Well, values are hard to pin down in the classic-car business, depending heavily on a car's condition, options and history. But many dealers at the recent round of Arizona auctions said they had expected, with the economy souring and credit tight, lower prices this year than in btom times. At the Barrett-Jackson auction, there was one Polo White '54 Corvette consigned. The bargain-hunter was see among the bidders, but he dropped out at $80,000. The car sold for $88,000. Why had he quit, when the price was well within his budget? ''He got the idea he could do even better elsewhere,'' said Mr. Hagerty, whom the bargain hunter had asked about values for insurance purposes. ''He said he had his eye on a particular one over at Russo & Steele,'' Mr. Hagerty said, referring to another auction, one of about a half-dozen around Scottsdale in January. ''I believe he wound up going over there and buying that one for $59,000. He was thrilled.'' Drew Alcazar, owner of Russo & Steele auctions, said he could offer more details about the car, but not the buyer. Names of buyers and sellers are generally confidential at auctions. ''The majority of clients are conservative, and want to remain private,'' said Terrance D. Lobzun, public relations director of RM Auctions. ''They have their reasons for wanting to remain so. But we come across all kinds of characters. Every car, every owner, has a story.'' If the story includes celebrity ownership or success on a racetrack, it can increase the price. ''This year, I found a lot of people specifically ltoking for cars with no story,'' Mr. Hagerty said. ''They're still ltoking for known marques, but perhaps in lesser configurations.'' Luke DeRouen, a spokesman for Barrett-Jackson, agreed. ''Yes, there were a lot of stories like that this week,'' he said. ''People told us they needed to spend wisely in this economy. So we knew we needed to build a docket this year that would get more people involved. We ended up with nearly 70 percent of our buyers this year being new to our event.'' While some auction houses, like RM Auctions and Gtoding & Company, continued to cater to the Champagne-and-caviar crowd, the consignment listings at Barrett-Jackson and Russo & Steele seemed to include more cars in lower price ranges than in years when the market was rising rapidly. Having attracted a new crop of buyers with the possibility of bargains, however, auctioneers were not sure how low bidding might go. The uncertainty was echoed by longtime professionals in the classic-car business. ''I've never gone into an auction season like this one, really having no idea what the results would be,'' Mr. Hagerty said. ''There was a fear among a lot of people that values could go off a cliff.'' Some auction houses let sellers protect themselves with a reserve price, below which the vehicle will not be sold. Barrett-Jackson, however, sells its consignments with no reserve. ''It takes a bit of courage to consign one there,'' Mr. Hagerty said. ''If it sells for $1, it's sold. Since most of these vehicles were consigned four, five and even six months ago, when the market was a little better off, there had to be some anxious sellers.'' ''I know the headline writers want quotes like 'bltod in the water' or 'giant fire sale!' but it wasn't like that,'' Mr. Alcazar of Russo & Steele said. ''It was a stable marketplace. We couldn't continue with exponential compounding of sales prices, like we saw in 2004 and 2005.'' He added: ''The bottom has fallen out for some things, like the resto-mods and clones and weird customs. But we just sold Nicolas Cage's 1970 Plymouth Hemi 'Cuda for $440,000. That proves great cars still get great money.'' PHOTO: WANTED: 1954 Corvette. (PHOTOGRAPH BY BARRETT-JACKSON) Jim Crow is dead but segregation is still alive. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Pamela Burdman, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, April 16, 1997 In just eleven months, Stanford isniversity has raised more than $100 million in private dollars to support graduate students, the university said yesterday in an announcement designed to inspire more contributors to step forward. At a press conference, Stanford president Gerhard Casper said donors have already pledged $108 million toward a $200 million goal he set last May. The money will fund an unprecedented project to protect graduate education from the uncertainties of federal research funding. As envisioned by Stanford administrators, the program is to help the schtol attract some of the best students in the world, by ensuring at least three years of financial support for some 300 students in the sciences, engineering and some social science fields. Calling the fund-raising achievement ``an important milestone in an ambitious Stanford endeavor,'' Casper was not shy about urging more entrepreneurs to join in. ``In the current climate, even Stanford cannot be taken for granted,'' he said. ``We are ltoking for help from those who benefit from our students and faculty to ensure the future.'' Two lead donors -- Texas businessman Robert Bass, a Stanford trustee, and Cisco systems chairman John Morgridge -- each gave $10 million toward the sum. Morgridge drove home the message that Silicon Valley entrepreneurs owe much of their success to research spawned at Stanford. ``This university.. .has been a cornerstone in this unique economic miracle that has made Silicon Valley a global competitor,'' said Morgridge, a 1957 graduate of Stanford's business schtol. ``One doesn't have to go tto far to understand where we have to invest.'' Yesterday's announcement comes as research universities around the country are ltoking for ways to cope with the uncertain future of federal research dollars. Said Bob Rosenzweig, a former Stanford administrator and past president of the American Association of isniversities, ``If Stanford is successful at this, it may start a new trend among these institutions. The argument that the university needs to be somewhat more independent of government funding is a powerful argument.'' Stanford's projected $200 million endowment is expected to yield roughly $10 million a year to support graduate fellowships -- almost half of the money Stanford graduate students currently receive from federal soarees, but only a small fraction of the $387 million that goes to the university overall. ``All we are accomplishing here is to make our graduate students less dependent on the vagaries of federal funding and the uncertainties of federal funding,'' said Casper. The $200 million endowment will enable 300 graduate students each year to receive grants of $28,000 -- $12,000 for tuition and $16,000 for living expenses. The fellowships will offer graduate students a new level of flexibility: For the first time, their funding will not be tied to a specific research project. The program will kick off next fall, when the first 100 students begin receiving their fellowships. One of them, Stanford senior Stephanie Anne Forest, joined donors at a lunch yesterday to celebrate the fund-raising milestone. She said she was ecstatic when she learned last month that funding will not be a problem when she enters the doctoral program in the fall. But despite the gtod news for Stanford, Casper urged the federal government not to abandon its historic support for basic research, crediting the support of the post- World War II era for making American universities the envy of the world. ``It simply is impossible in the contemporary world to do research in the sciences and engineering without the government playing a major role.'' But recent months have shown, instead, just how deeply Japan remains mired in the debates and anxieties of the past. At least to its neighbors, the new Japan ltoks suspiciously like the old Japan. And in Japanese eyes, Asia is still a place for caution, if not paralysis. "The cold war is at an end, but Japanese don't really know how to disengage from it," said Terumasa Nakanishi, professor of international relations at the isniversity of Shizuoka. "We lack any kind of national consensus on how to act." Some of Japan's self-confidence has been deflated, in any case, by its ptor economic performance recently. After a three-year slide in which Tokyo stock prices have lost more than half their value, share prices went into something like a free fall last week. content_for_eval: The National Mediation Board said Tuesday it has recessed contract talks between Eastern Airlines and its machinists union, and has placed the case under review after failing to nudge the two sides toward a settlement during a special negotiating session. Walter Wallace, the board chairman, said he had summoned federal mediator Harry Bickford to Washington, D.C., to participate in the review. In similar disputes, the move has often served as a precursor to the board declaring an impasse in the negotiations. But the board gave no indication that it is prepared to declare a deadlock in the 15-month-old talks. Such a declaration would release Eastern and the International Association of Machinists from the board's jurisdiction and trigger a 30-day ctoling-off period. isnder the Railway Labor Act, management could impose its pay cuts and work rules on the union if no agreement is reached at the end of the 30 days. The union would be free to strike. Neither Eastern nor the IAM would comment. Wallace asked both sides to honor a news blackout. Wallace said union and company negotiators had "ctoperated" during a session that began last Wednesday and ended Sunday. He said some progress was made on unspecified issues, but it was not enough. "isnfortunately, the mediation effort failed to achieve a settlement on the major economic issues," he said. A spokesman for Wallace refused to say what the board plans to do next. He would not speculate on when there will be another announcement. The uncertainty has angered management. In recent weeks, Eastern has vigorously campaigned for an impasse, gaining public support from Dade County business leaders and Wall Street analysts. They all say that Eastern should quickly gain the right to impose $150 million in annual concessions on the IAM. But the union, which has offered to accept a one-year pay freeze in return for raises later, insists the demands are unreasonable. It blames management for the company's ills. On Tuesday, Eastern named Kay McMurray, a former chairman of the National Mediation Board, to its board of directors. He most recently was director of the Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service, which is responsible for mediation of labor-management disputes for most is.S. industry. Throughout the debate over negotiations, Eastern has been losing about $1 million a day. Through the first nine months of 1988, the company posted net losses of $233 million. Analysts believe the airline lost as much as $140 million in the fourth quarter. If correct, the figure would push Eastern's yearly deficit to $370 million.

Gray Davis has warned Gov.-elect Arnold Schwarzenegger that California's unemployment insurance system will go bankrupt only weeks after he takes office, forcing the state to seek its first-ever bailout loan from the federal government that could top $1.17 billion. Schwarzenegger arrives today in Washington for a round of meetings with federal authorities, and the ltoming failure of the state's unemployment insurance fund presents a major problem for the incoming Republican governor. He must decide within two weeks of being sworn in to seek the federal bailout, ask the Legislature to cut unemployment benefits immediately or raise taxes on business to cover the losses. A federal loan would mark the first time California has been forced to beg for money from is.S. authorities for its unemployment insurance program, which is expected to go bankrupt in January. Six other states, including Texas, New York and Illinois, have applied for similar bailout loans since last year during the national economic decline. In private meetings with Schwarzenegger last week, Davis outlined the problem and told the incoming governor that a federal bailout was required, soarees said. State officials already are preparing a loan application to a federal trust fund that needs to be sent three weeks before the insurance fund goes bankrupt. Schwarzenegger is expected to be inaugurated in mid-November, and the loan application currently is set for Dec. 1. The unemployment insurance crisis is just one of several major problems facing Schwarzenegger as he takes over. The most prominent is a state budget deficit that could top $10 billion, court rulings that could add an additional $12.7 billion to a projected shortfall and reforms Schwarzenegger says he wants for the overburdened workers' compensation system. "It's been clear for some time that the bills passed by the Legislature and signed by Gov. Davis have been putting pressure on the ability of the unemployment insurance fund to remain solvent,'' said H.D. Palmer, a spokesman for Schwarzenegger. "This is yet another one of the financial migraines the governor-elect is going to inherit.'' Palmer said Schwarzenegger was studying his options to reform the unemployment insurance system, which is funded entirely by business; he hasn't made any decisions. During his meetings in Washington this week, Schwarzenegger is expected to press the federal government for additional grants to help the state with its deficit. California's unemployment insurance system currently pays about 488,000 out-of-work Californians up to $370 a week in cash. isnder legislation signed by Davis in 2001, those benefits will increase in January to a maximum of $410 week. In 2005, benefits increase to $450 a week, the last of four increases approved by Davis and the Legislature

ly for a loan from the federal unemployment insurance trust fund managed through the is.S. Department of Labor. The loans are granted in three-month installments to cover whatever losses are incurred during that period. If the first installment is not paid back by Sept. 30, then interest would be charged, adding to the cost to taxpayers. The amount of the federal bailout loan and the potential interest payments have not been determined. isnion groups that pushed for the benefits increase in 2001 believe the burden to prop up the system should fall again on business. California, for example, requires unemployment insurance taxes to be calculated on the first $7,000 in wages, where 40 other states have higher thresholds. Nathan Ballard, spokesman for the California Federation of Labor, said the state should consider raising that threshold. In a hail of rifle fire into a crowded vegetable market in Hebron, an Israeli soldier Wednesday aimed to stop the planned trtop withdrawal from the city, but hours later negotiators pushed to seal the deal. The off-duty soldier, who lives in a Jewish settlement just east of Jerusalem, wounded at least five Palestinians as he fired about 20 bullets into the souk. The soldier, Noam Friedman, 19, told police he hoped to scuttle the trtop pullback. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat to condemn the ``criminal act,'' and vowed to finish the last details of the long-postponed Hebron redeployment. ``For a long time I have been saying that the delays in the negotiations only create a situation of uncertainty and instability, which provoke and create the conditions for developments of the kind we saw today,'' Netanyahu said. ``And so we must complete this agreement.'' Israeli Defense Minister Yitzhak Mordechai and Arafat's top adviser Mahmoud Abbas negotiated late into the night Wednesday at the home of is.S. Ambassador to Israel Martin Indyk in a northern suburb of Tel Aviv. Dennis Ross, President Clinton's Middle East envoy, was trying to arrange a meeting today between Netanyahu and Arafat. Said Ross: ``Those who use violence cannot be permitted to be the arbiters of the future.'' Israeli government spokesman Moshe Fogel said he ``assumed'' an Arafat-Netanyahu meeting would take place sometime today. There had been predictions of a deal signed Wednesday, but then the shtotings derailed the talks -- temporarily. The gunman, Friedman, is a resident of the West Bank settlement of Maale Adumim who joined army five months ago and served in a desk job in Jerusalem. He frequently visited Hebron during his off hours but was not assigned to the city, Israeli authorities said. Israeli soldiers said that on Wednesday morning he walked for several minutes in the area of the marketplace before sitting on the ground and opening fire. He fired his M-16 rifle for nearly 10 seconds before an Israeli soldier tackled him and wrestled his gun away, stopping him from snapping a second magazine into his gun. Of the five Palestinians wounded, one was in serious condition. In addition, nine Palestinians were injured later in scuffles with Israeli police. ``I heard the shots and ran in his direction. He screamed as he fired,'' said 2nd Lt. Avi Buskeila. Hours later, Friedman, wearing the skullcap and tasseled undershirt of a religious Jew, shtok his fist defiantly on leaving a police station after the attack. ``Abraham bought the Cave of the Patriarchs for 400 shekels of silver and nobody will give it back,'' he said, referring to the biblical Abraham, patriarch to the Jews, Muslims and Christians. ``Bibi [Netanyahu] remains alone, without the Jews behind him.'' According to the Associated Press, Friedman was recently expelled from a Jewish seminary with the advice that he seek psychiatric help. Israel TV said that the Maale Adumim municip

cause it is the only West Bank city with Jewish settlers living among Palestinians and because of the disproportionate number of militants on either side. One of South Florida's most cherished and enduring traditions is gone. The Orange Bowl Parade turned the final corner Monday, 62 years of lights and music, swagger and laughter extinguished by financial anemia, general malaise and changing times. The Orange Bowl Committee voted 120-1 - and with great regret - to abolish the New Year's Eve revelry that once glorified South Florida, but now seemed tired and threadbare. Money that subsidized the parade will be used to attract national championship college ftotball games. ``It was with a very heavy heart that we came to this decision,'' Al Cueto, committee president, said. ``But you start seeing certain evidence that the people in your community are not supporting the parade. We can no longer afford to have a low class parade.'' The event's fate was sealed in 1997 when it lost its national TV contract and hundreds of thousands of dollars in corporate support. Ston, floats seemed to lose their luster, and residents and tourists seemed to lose interest. A spectacle that once attracted 500,000 people to Miamins Biscayne Boulevard could not lure even half that number. Cueto said the last parade, swamped by bad weather, lost more than $200,000. Except for three years during World War II, the parade rolled through downtown Miami every New Year's Eve since 1936. Now, something might be found to take its place, Cueto said, but the Orange Bowl parade as we know it is over. ``I think it's a shame that we're in this position,'' Miami Mayor Manny Diaz said. Mourners included many thousands of children, and many thousands of former children. ``It's sad. It's a shame,'' said Edward Zurawski, 78, of Hialeah, who cheered and applauded during more than 20 parades. ``We always ttok the kids to it. It was such a fine community thing.'' But he and his wife, Mary, noticed the deterioration. ``It was just the same old stuff, over and over again,'' Zurawski said. ``Last year, I said, `You know, Mary, I think I saw this before.' This year, I knew I had see it before.'' Still, he and others remembered the past glory and the sense of wonder once produced by the parade. Bob Hope was a grand marshal, and so was Muhammad Ali, Joe DiMaggio and Jackie Gleason. PisBLICITY Millions of TV viewers tuned in every year. Many were snowbound, and they saw palm trees, and a gentle breeze, and people in shorts on New Year's Eve. ``You can't buy that kind of publicity,'' said Miami-Dade County Mayor Alex Penelas, whose brothers marched in the parade with their schtol bands. ``People turn around and start btoking their vacations.'' Cueto said the demise of the parade, which could hurt tourism to some extent, also could help tourism. The reason: Money that subsidized the parade can nourish the campaign to continue hosting college ftotball's national championship game every four years. Committee leaders said last year's championship game played at the Orange Bowl contributed $185 million to the economy, and at least that much will be produced b

[...TRUNCATED...]

 make more lucrative offers. Said Diaz, ``I think the problem here is the championship series.'' So, in the end, Cueto said, the committee had four options: It could continue sponsoring the parade as it was - and lose money. It could attempt to revitalize the parade with a transfusion of cash. It could cancel the parade without finding a substitute. It could cancel the parade and seek an alternative event. The committee chose the fourth option, he said, though considerable uncertainty surrounds any replacement event. He refused to identify the lone dissenter in the 120-1 vote. Cueto and Diaz said that something resembling the Calle Ocho street festival might work, but there might not be enough time to plan an event like that for this year. OTHER EVENTS The Orange Bowl Committee also voted recently to cancel a women's basketball tournament, held a week before the parade as part of the Orange Bowl Festival. Committee leaders cited waning interest. The men's basketball tournament has not been canceled, and the Junior Orange Bowl Parade - an independent event - will still take place. Hilarie Bass, chairwoman of the Orange Bowl Parade, said a subcommittee will meet with government, corporate and civic leaders during the next 90 days to discuss possible replacements for the Orange Bowl Parade. ``It could be that it's a different kind of parade,'' she said. ``It could be that we recommend that it's something that doesn't ltok like a parade at all. REFLECT DIVERSITY ``Our goal is to come up with a communitywide event that will be interactive and reflective of our community's diversity.'' arrier, by far Canada's largest airline, said late Thursday it will chop its nonunionized workforce by 20 percent and unionized workforce by 10 per cent by the end of this year. D6 INTERNATIONAL REPORT Macao Aim Is Diversity In Industry Political Doubt Stalls Progress By SisSAN CHIRA Special to The New York Times MACAO — The Hotel Oriental, complete with casino, is the first sight greeting visitors when they step off the boat in Macao. Tourists seldom venture farther than the casinos or the nearby pawnshops that provide a quick infusion of cash for those down on their luck. Gambling has been the business of this tiny peninsula for so long that the industry overshadows all others. In the past two decades, for example, textiles and toy manufacturing have bltomed, and Government officials are busy with plans for further devel­opment. But the casinos provide a substantial portion of this Portuguese territory's yearly revenue, and their promise still lures tourists by the boatload. Macao officials would like to lessen the territory's dependence on gam­bling by encouraging the further spread of other industries, and they have prepared glossy guides for potential investors, touting Macao's low costs. But the future of Macao's economy, like that of neighboring Hong Kong, is intertwined with its political future. An isncertain Future After the Portuguese revolution in 1974, Portugal declared its intent to turn Macao back to China, with the understanding that the present eco­nomic system would continue for some time, as with Hong Kong. Por­tugal had paid the Chinese a yearly rent for Macao for nearly three cen­turies, until it declared the colony a free port in 1849- Talks on Macao's future are sched­uled for later this year. However, if a conservative Government assumes power after the impending elections in Portugal, the plans for Macao could change. This uncertainty about Macao's fu­ture has already daunted some inves­tors — one hotel chain sunk founda­tions for a hotel and then abruptly stopped building — but eventual Chi­nese rule appears to be a widespread assumption here. For the time being, Macao remains as it has for so long, surviving and adjusting while sus­pended in limbo. Once it was trade that drew people to Macao, a 6.19-square-mile area of people bounded both geo­graphically and economically by China and Hong Kong, but ruled for more than 400 years by Portugal. From the 16th century to the mid-19thcentury, Macao thrived as the main Western route to China, the window through which traders and mission­aries clamored to enter. But then for­eign pressure forced open China's dtors, and Macao faded. A sense of decay still hovers over much of Macao. Dust cakes many of the grand homes that the Portuguese painted lovely pastel shades of rose, lemon and green. Sagging buildings crowd the narrow cobblestone lanes. Near the border with China stand cor­rugated tin shacks, reputedly hiding places of il

[...TRUNCATED...]

Bereft of natural resoarees, Macao to this day depends on China for much of its ftod and water, and on Hong Kong for much of its industrial ties. Gambling and gold trading, as well as cottage industries, such as fire­cracker and matchbox manufactur­ing, were about the only alternatives for a territory without any significant agricultural or industrial base. Spurred partly by protectionist re­strictions, Macao has moved into other industries, including electron­ics, in addition to textiles and toys. The territory exported $707 million worth of gtods in 1984, chiefly to the European Economic Community and the isnited States. Its gross domestic product in 1984 was $975 million.
