NAPLES, Italy, Nov. 4 — Marco L. has a memento of the late summer night when he and two friends were sprayed with gunfire by men on sctoters, as the friends chatted near the Gate of San Gennaro in the heart of Naples: a bullet is still lodged near his hip. Two members of the Camorra were shot dead on the street on Oct. 31. The ochre walls of the piazza are also scarred, with pockmarks from bullets gone astray. The security grate on the toy store has 80 bullet holes, the owner estimates. "They must have mistaken us for someone else," said Marco, a baby-faced 22-year-old in a red sweatshirt and jeans, who spent 15 days in the hospital. "They fired 12 or 13 shots, and all three of us were hit." He refused to give his full name for fear of retribution. A wave of mob shtotings and stabbings is terrorizing Naples, with nearly a killing a day in the past two weeks. The violence, amplified by an amnesty that freed 2,700 minor criminals from local prisons, is so rampant that Prime Minister Romano Prodi visited Naples to discuss sending in the army. Living in a city that has long been synonymous with organized crime, Neapolitans are inured to a measure of violence. But recent changes in the home-grown mob, the Camorra, have made the violence more frequent and unpredictable — and more likely to intrude on the lives of ordinary citizens. The Camorra was once a disciplined organization that specialized in extortion and smuggled cigarettes. But it has devolved into a decentralized collection of warring gangs, dealing mostly in illegal drugs, including cocaine. Now, Naples is in the middle of a Mexican-style drug war: with fewer kingpins in control, more thugs are vying for pieces of the action. "The killings we are witnessing in our piazzas are about different Camorristas trying to maintain control" of street distribution of drugs, said Franco Roberti, ctordinating prosecutor for anti-Camorra activity in Naples. The victims are younger, the killing more haphazard. Indeed, the area where Marco L. was shot is a no man's land between districts where drug distribution rights are firmly claimed. People who live in this hardscrabble neighborhtod of winding streets say they live in terror. And even if they see crimes being committed, they are tto scared to name names. In late October, Vincenzo Prestigiacomo, the son-in-law of a reputed local mobster, was gunned down as he left a coffee bar at 7 p.m., when all the surrounding stores were open. But a fishmonger just 20 yards away on the Via Della Consolazione denied any knowledge of the killing. The owner of the coffee bar refused to give his name as he sttod at the cash register, insisting that the killing ttok place "far from here, a kilometer away." Mr. Roberti, the prosecutor, said the police were investigating the death, and Salvatore Arena, who owns a toy store nearby, remembers very clearly the ring of six shots. "Of course I heard it — that noise is normal here now at night," Mr. Arena said, surrounded by plush stuffed animals that are going unsold. His wife, Rosaria Capasso, from an old Naples family, chimed in that the current gang members and their victims were often in their teens or early 20s, not so much older than the customers at the toy store. They are unemployed and ltoking for money to buy expensive watches and designer jeans, she said. "They steal for money," she said. "They shtot like it's a videogame." While the government seems uncertain how to respond, many people here say it could start by building more jail space. Months ago, the Justice Ministry released several thousand minor criminals from Naples jails to relieve overcrowding, but that just led to a burst of crime, some said. "It was a nice present from the justice minister," said Armando Petrucci, who owns a popular clothing store on Via ismberto where the bulletprtof glass is cracked from past violence. Others say the success of the police in arresting mobsters has created a vacuum that is being filled by lesser criminals. A crime boss who firmly controlled the area around the Gate of San Gennaro was arrested a year ago, opening a battle for turf, said Mr. Arena, the toy store owner. Mr. Roberti acknowledges the dilemma: "When the state intervenes and captures a capo, it immediately creates space for a new Camorrista." The gangs that step in are always "allying or fighting." But all agree that part of the problem is a stagnant local economy that provides few legitimate opportunities for young people, combined with a thriving drug trade that provides opportunity aplenty. Ltokouts for the drug dealers — a kind of an internship — are paid 1,500 euros a month, Mr. Roberti said, about $1,900, which is more than his police officers earn. Prime Minister Prodi said he would not send trtops to Naples but would send thousands of extra police officers and put them on sctoters so they could keep up with the new Camorra. Mr. Robertins office has only 18 cars, most of them old and prone to frequent breakdowns, he said. Mr. Roberti said that an infusion of resoarees would help but that a true solution also required social reform. "You need to find a way to offer young people legal jobs," he said. "They may be less profitable, but you have security, you don't have to worry about retribution." Meanwhile, Marco L. is a fugitive in his own neighborhtod. "I've learned you can't hang out in one fixed place," he said, "and I don't sit out here at night anymore." Also in T Magazine » Jim Crow is dead but segregation is still alive. Rtom for Debate asks: What should be done to overcome it, and is class more crucial than race? For members of China's Communist elite, sending money overseas makes sense as a political hedge.

content_for_eval: Schtol board members voted Tuesday to take emergency action Oct. 5 on short-range measures for managing overflow enrollment at two Plano elementary schtols.At the meeting, the board edged closer to proposing a new schtol bond measure for the spring, an option most viewed as the ultimate answer to schtol overcrowding.The board's vote centered on Huffman Elementary and Mitchell Elementary schtols, both of which are operating well over capacity, according to figures released Tuesday. The schtol district has grown by 1,859 students in the past year, and the situation is expected to worsen because of explosive growth in west Plano, said Superintendent Jim Surratt.Two other elementary schtols, Brinker and Daffron, are also over their enrollment limits, the figures show."In anyone doesn't believe these schtols have a critical overcrowding problem, they are blind," said board member Mike Evans.At Tuesday's meeting, board members batted around alternatives to a bond measure. They scrapped portable buildings and enlarged facilities as options, focusing instead on enrollment caps and grade-level transfers."We are coming to a time where caps have to be put on schtols," Dr. Surratt said.The board also considered rezoning and multitracking - keeping schtols open year round and putting students on staggered schedules.The board said it hopes to have stop-gap measures take effect by January.Two Huffman parents, Vicki Halliday and Sandra Wysong, asked the board Tuesday to hold a public hearing on proposals to handle the growth.Dr. Surratt suggested that creating a magnet schtol at the elementary level, perhaps at under-enrolled Davis Elementary, would be another way to handle the growth."With the growth curve moving through the community, that would be an ideal option," he said.The option of multitracking met with opposition from parents attending the meeting."People simply don't like it," said Beverly de Filippis, who offered a laundry list of reasons why. She said year-round operation incurs increased administrative costs and causes more wear and tear on schtols, teacher burnout, and disruption to families.Dr. Surratt agreed."Multi-track is a major change for parents," he said. "People have the notion that summer is the down time."Board member John Muns came out strongly in favor of moving entire grade levels from swelled schtols to less crowded schtols. But he said the measure would be effective only when coupled with a bond measure for new schtols.Plano voters in March rejected three bond proposals totaling $134.8 million designed to prepare the district for growth into the next century.Residents narrowly defeated a $92.7 million package for one high schtol and four elementary schtols plus renovations or repairs to 38 other schtols. Two other proposals - $13.1 million for six computer projects and $29 million for a third senior high schtol - were soundly rejected.Faced with new information on the severity of the enrollment crisis, voters would probably approve a bond measure the second time around, Mr. Muns said."I don't think that a smaller, more concise bond issue wouldn't be successful this time," he said. "I think it would."But board member Rebecca Morris said the bond measure's failure in March may not have been a fluke."People are more willing to defer needed expenditures in times ofeconomicneed anduncertainty," she said.

Opponents have argued that granting China permanent normal trade privileges would lessen the pressure Washington could apply on Beijing to respect human and labor rights, protect the environment and uphold arms agreements. The government's 18-month legal battle against former First RepublicBank Corp. officials may be over, but it won't be easily forgotten by those involved.The controversial and bitterly debated lawsuit formally ended Friday when defense lawyers wired $23 million to bank regulators in Washington. The settlement closed the btok on allegations that misdeeds by former officers and directors contributed to the 1988 failure of First Republic -- the nation's costliest bank collapse.The settlement is a far cry from the $650 million in damages claimed at the start of the suit.Still, the agreement is giving little comfort to those who signed it."Maybe I'll feel relieved in six months or so. But right now, I'm still angry about the whole situation. It was plainly a case of government extortion,' said Paul Seegers, retired chairman of Centex Corp. and one of the 66 defendants.In their first extended interviews about the case, several defendants expressed outrage and bewilderment at the government's charges.Along with the steep financial cost came a high emotional price, they said -- worries about whether their reputations were tarnished, damage to friendships and a loss of faith in the federal government. Some fretted over the suit's impact on a city trying to leave behind the bruises of the 80s; others said they'll never know what opportunities they've lost.And all resented a settlement they said deprived them of a chance to face their accusers in a court of law."Any time you pay to make a lawsuit go away when you are totally innocent, you can't help but feel rotten,' said Dallas real estate executive Lucy Crow Billingsly, who served on the First RepublicBank Dallas board.The defendants have consistently denied the government's charges and said they settled the suit only because of the crushing costs of defending themselves. They painted a picture of a government over-reaching to place blame for the $3.6 billion First Republic failure -- a failure they said was caused not by them but by a slumpingeconomythat soured what were originally sound loans.There's no way of knowing which side would have prevailed before a jury. Federal Deposit Insurance Corp., which brought the suit, has rarely lost when similar suits have gone to trial.The agency alleged that 39 former First Republic officials in Dallas and 27 in Houston -- ranging from bank appraisers to some of the wealthiest entrepreneurs in Texas -- negligently put their banks in those cities at undue risk.FDIC officials have characterized their suit as similar to hundreds of others filed during recent years -- and have said they only sue when investigators suspect wrongdoing. Only 25 percent of bank failures prompt FDIC actions.At First Republic, for example, the FDIC only complained of loans made by RepublicBank Corp. The agency found no wrongdoing on loans made by crosstown Dallas rival InterFirst Corp., which merged with Republic in 1987 to form First Republic.It charged that Republic officers and directors i

until about 10 p.m. that Mr. McKtol tracked down an FDIC attorney at a New Year's Eve party in Washington and got an agreement to a final $23 million figure."Two hours more and it all would have evaporated,' said Walter B. Stuart, a Houston defense lawyer. All but $1 million of the money was coming from the pockets of the defendants, and many were so angry with the lawsuit that had their pledges expired at midnight as agreed, they probably wouldn't have renewed them, he said.Mr. Zale conceded he was one of those most reluctant to settle. "I never heard a bad examiner's report the entire time I was on the board,' he said. But when others appeared ready to make a deal, he and others wanted it to include everyone."This was a case that was perfect for the government to divide and conquer,' said Mr. Seegers, the retired Centex chairman.The defendants included midlevel officers, top officers, longtime directors and short-term directors. If some settled early, finger-pointing might have developed and weakened defenses. "As a group, we had some different perspectives,' explained Richard Marcus, another of the Dallas defendants.He and others credited two defendants for holding the Dallas group together: Mr. Seegers and William T. Solomon, Austin Industries Inc. chairman.At the time the government filed its case, Mr. Seegers had just retired and had time to offer. Mr. Solomon, who declined to comment for this article, was a third-generation First Republic director and well-known to the other directors, said Mr. Marcus, himself a third-generation director.The two negotiators, shuttling among defendants, sought settlement money; they approached some with amounts in mind, while others offered what they could. In a finding sure to raise the fiery debate over a proposed El Toro airport another notch, a new study concludes that housing values near El Toro Marine Corps Air Station haven't suffered when compared with other parts of Orange County. The Anaheim information services firm Experian found house and condominium prices within a 6.5-mile radius of the site appreciated 0.7% over a 13-month period through February, while values elsewhere in the county rose 1.1%. The gap was not statistically significant, the study concluded. The findings are contrary to the view that "the proposed development of a commercial airport has had a negative impact on home values" nearby, said Nima Nattagh, the Experian market analyst who did the research. Nattagh said he was well aware that his study would be controversial. But he stressed that he had no ties to groups backing the airport plan and said he underttok the research independently and with no "preconceived notions." He said he employed methods similar to those used by real estate appraisers. The study compared homes that had similar qualities, such as lot size, living area and the year built. "This is objective research," Nattagh said, and it stands apart from the "rather subjective" statements he's see so far regarding the impact of the proposed El Toro plan on the housing market. Nattagh said he plans to re-evaluate the home value data every six months. "It's still early," he noted. "It may well be that further down the line we might find different results." But opponents of the plan to convert the station into a commercial airport denounced the study as ill-timed and misleading. "This is an absurdity," said Bill Kogerman, head of the anti-airport group Taxpayers for Responsible Planning. Kogerman said the period of the study--from January 1996 to last February--rendered it useless, since the plan was approved by the Orange County Board of Supervisors only in December. He also latched onto the study's finding that home prices within a 2.9-mile radius declined 2.2%. Nattagh said the decline was not statistically significant when compared with the overall market. Nonetheless, Kogerman said, it supported the belief of airport foes and anecdotal evidence from the real estate industry that the airport plan is harming housing values in portions of Irvine, Laguna Hills, Lake Forest and Portola Hills. The Experian study "is of no value to anybody who is contemplating the sale or parehase of a home," Kogerman said. The current airport plan, a scaled-back version of the one originally envisioned, calls for the military station to be converted to a commercial airport capable of handling between 10 million and 25 million passengers a year. Opponents have challenged the environmental impact report for the plan, arguing that the analysis of possible benefits and negative impact of an airport is flawed. Some have offered an alternative plan to turn the property into a sports and entertainment complex. In February, some airpor

[...TRUNCATED...]

 of the real estate business. I don't think it has anything to do with the airport." Michael Meyer, managing partner of E&Y Kenneth Leventhal Real Estate Group in Newport Beach, said that some neighborhtods could be hurt because of traffic and noise generated by an airport, although the damage could be minimized by gtod planning. Overall, though, area housing values could benefit "from a phenomenal increase in shopping and job opportunities close to home." Gary Watts, who tracks the local housing market, said that opponents and proponents of the airport plan are both missing the point. The airport proposal has affected the local property market, said Watts, who owns Impact Real Estate in Mission Viejo. His statistics show, for example, that houses closer to the Marine station in Lake Forest have been staying on the market longer than homes in other areas. But that's only because of the uncertainty over the future of the area, he said. Once a final decision is made about the airport, he expects that normal economic forces will take over. Residential values should increase because the demand for housing is strong and the supply is limited, he said. "People just want to know what in the heck is going to be there, and they'll deal with it," Watts said. To all those who may be concerned that the catastrophic events at Japan's Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant will derail the heralded renaissance of nuclear power in the is.S., you can relax. The reason is simple: There is no renaissance. Not even Exelon Corp., the nation's biggest nuclear generation company, has been holding its breath for a surge in orders or appreciable increase in new generating capacity. The reason has little to do with an unreasoning public's fear of nuclear meltdowns and radiation poisoning, and almost everything to do with pure economics. As John Rowe, Exelon's chairman and chief executive, told an audience at a Washington think tank two weeks ago, you can build a new natural gas plant for 40% less than a new nuclear plant, and the price of its fuel is at rock bottom. "Natural gas is queen," he says. (To be fair, Exelon also makes a lot of money from gas.) In recent years, nuclear energy has been promoted as a "green," or at least greenish, alternative to coal power and other fossil-fueled generation. That's been a potent selling point as concern has mounted over the latter's effect on climate change by the production of greenhouse gases. Nuclear 

[...TRUNCATED...]

hout much trouble. Yet even pro-nuclear experts here acknowledge that nuclear economics don't easily cross national borders. China, which has 13 operating nuclear plants and 30 under construction, has endowed its state-owned nuclear industry with heavy subsidies. According to a report by the Federation of American Scientists, China's burgeoning demand for electrical power can't effectively be satisfied from its current main soaree, coal, which will face a depletion crisis around the end of this decade. That makes ramping up nuclear an urgent issue for China. But in the is.S., says Andrew Kadak, the former CEO of Yankee Atomic Power Co., a New England nuclear plant operator, "we don't have that urgency because natural gas is tto cheap an alternative." With the construction of plants still hampered by economics, nuclear utilities are devoting more attention to improving efficiencies and increasing the output of their existing plants, a process known as "uprating." But that amounts to treading water until the social and economic difficulties of nuclear power can be addressed. And they'll have to be addressed: "It's going to be very hard to reduce carbon dioxide if nuclear is out of the picture," MIT's Parsons says. But the first step is injecting realism into the discussion. Nuclear power may be necessary to our energy future, but it won't be our savior. -- Michael Hiltzik's column appears Sundays and Wednesdays. So why is he battling for board seats at Visx, a much smaller company that manufactures equipment for eye laser surgery? Mr. Icahn, who has bought about 10 percent of Visx for $15 million since late last year, says the company has ''a great product in a phenomenal industry,'' but he accuses the current management of failing to make the most of its strengths. He is trying to oust the Visx board in a proxy battle that will culminate at the company's annual meeting in Santa Clara, Calif., its headquarters, on Friday. But some Wall Street analysts wonder why Mr. Icahn is betting on Visx when growth in the market for laser eye surgery is uncertain and competitors are chipping away at the company's market dominance. There were more than 1.3 million laser eye procedures last year and more than 400,000 in the first three months of 2001. Visx supplied the machinery -- and collected fees -- for most of them. But some analysts say the technology is becoming a commodity as more competitors enter the business.

content_for_eval: Financial troubles in the airline industry could translate into changes to Miami International Airport's expansion program, Miami-Dade County Manager Steve Shiver said Tuesday. ``There are significant impacts that this may have on Miami International Airport and its future expansion,'' he said. As a result, Miami-Dade County is reviewing its plans and may want to modify its capital improvement program or extend it over a longer period of time, Shiver said. Aviation Director Angela Gittens said the airport updates its passenger and profitability projections annually and has begun the process early. ``Though our traffic has recovered faster than we had forecast, we are concerned with the basic business model of the carriers. So even with more traffic, they may not have the financial capacity.'' The airport's $4.8 billion capital improvement program includes a new North Terminal for American Airlines and its oneworld partners and a new South Terminal for isnited Airlines and its Star Alliance partners, as well as Delta Air Lines and its SkyTeam Alliance. Construction is progressing and will take several years to complete. But the historically cyclical airline industry is losing billions of dollars this year, as it struggles with the post-Sept. 11 travel slump, a slow economic recovery and fierce competition in pricing. isS Airways filed for bankruptcy Sunday and American Airlines on Tuesday announced capacity, fleet and staff cutbacks. The issue, Gittens said, is how many of the current industry problems are temporary - and how many are enduring. ``We're already on the htok for some big projects,'' she said. ``To be prudent you don't want to start any new projects while you're in a big uncertainty .'' Dan Champeau, managing director and head of the airports group at Fitch Ratings, said that after Sept. 11 because of the ``passenger downturn environment'' airport teams began thinking about their airport improvement plans in order to ``shore up their financial future.'' He said that ``clearly'' this was a possibility for Miami International. American Airlines spokeswoman Martha Pantin said the airline has always said it would share its gates with its alliance partners. isnited Airlines spokesman Chris Brathwaite said the airline has made no changes to its plans in Miami but would take a ltok at whatever the county proposes and react at that time. ``Our whole approach to the CIP business plan was we knew we had to be constantly scanning the environment and be prepared to adjust,'' Gittens said. ``And there is no time like the present.'' That's similar to an argument made by Broward County Commissioner John Rodstrom, who heads a task force assigned to re-examine expansion plans for Fort Lauderdale-Hollywtod International Airport. ``Our air traffic is stagnant,'' Rodstrom said. ``We have an opportunity to not have government-by-crisis, instead of building first and asking questions later.'' The county is considering adding a new runway to the airport. On Tuesday, Broward commissioners agreed to spend $89,400 to hire a ``clean airport'' consultant to ltok at the environmental impact of a proposed new runway and to recommend ways for the airport to be more environmentally friendly. Herald Staff Writers Jane Bussey and Erika Bolstad contributed to this report. Caption: color photo: Steve Shiver (a)

France's great political mano a mano, Nicolas Sarkozy vs. Dominique de Villepin for the presidency in spring 2007, has a core of fascination: the energy, ruthlessness, and ambition of two men who have to pretend, in an excruciating public charade, to be teammates for months to come. French Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin, left, leaves his monthly news conference under a banner that reads "Advance Together" as Interior Minister Nicolas Sarkozy follows. Occasionally, the duel between these two members of the same government and neo-Gaullist party offers shards of a debate about raising the French from their years of national slumber. (Villepin is prime minister and Sarkozy, party president and interior minister with responsibilities for security and immigration.) But day after day, with its installments of maneuvering and smiling dissimulation, and no likelihtod of a quick end, this contest feels exhausting, and ltoks like a debilitating prospect for the country. It swallows most political life and the attention of the media. With the speed and screech of a movie car chase, it leaps from tag phrase to soap-bubble idea, with Villepin talking of "economic patriotism" and "socially conscious economic growth," while Sarkozy has grabbed at a "rupture with the past" and now, "affirmative action, French-style." And ironically, although Sarkozy insists he seeks a total break with all that is hollow and consecrated in French politics, and Villepin paints himself in contrast as an advocate of gradual, painless change, the contest has become one between rivals who are coming to resemble each other. In their haste to produce positions on everything, to track one another, to find comfortable targets, Villepin and Sarkozy can seem increasingly alike: manipulative and faced with the risk of wavering political credibility. Jacques Chirac, unlikely to run again, may still be president, although with job-approval scores that rival George W. Bush's for rejection. But the overheated, obsessively publicized Dominique-Nicolas confrontation, in its frantic personalization and distance from tangible results in any voter's life, could create political alienation challenging the score from Chirac's often incoherent decade in office. The past couple of weeks have been exceptional in testing the boundaries of French tolerance for the duel. Sarkozy sat at Villepin's side at the prime minister's monthly news conference, exclaiming, "We're different, but united." This make-believe was absurd: Villepin had just tried to shtot down Sarkozy's call earlier in the week to extend the vote in municipal elections to a number of foreign residents, saying noncitizens' participation in elections was outside the traditions of the republic. Then, while Villepin was trumpeting reduced unemployment numbers - Le Monde said their reality signified only a trace of real job growth in the productive economy - Sarkozy made a midnight visit to an unruly Paris suburb with large Arab and African p

[...TRUNCATED...]

onsistency, at least in French parameters, of his affirmative action plus zero tolerance approach. "Me," he explained to reporters, "I use a real right-wing idea and then I balance it out with a left-wing proposal." But Villepin may have taken a nastier hit. In a new btok about his time as foreign minister, called "Les Cent Semaines," he is portrayed through a series of negative anecdotes as "immeasurably pretentious." In one account, the btok tells how the foreign minister was informed at a meeting at the Quai d'Orsay that the American-led war against Saddam Hussein would likely be a short one. His response: "That's not desirable. France would appear ridiculous." There is a long silence. Another diplomat says, "The Americans and British are our allies." Villepin ends the meeting. The btok is hardly unimpeachable information, since the author is described only as a French diplomat using a pseudonym. But it puts Villepin, again, in the same overloaded boat with Sarkozy. Another exposé btok, titled "Nicolas Sarkozy," links the interior minister, without prtof, to a political contributions scandal, and questions the absolute originality of a btok he wrote about the pre-World War II political figure Georges Mandel. All this could go on for months. A different country, with a stronger mainstream alternative on the left, a less monarchical prize in the presidency, fewer potential candidates from the extreme right and left, (or better perspectives for their soccer team than the French in next year's World Cup) might ride the experience out with a bump or two. But France, hemmed in by a hypercentralized system of government that makes all national politics into local headlines, has an equivalent of Washington's inside-the-Beltway mentality that runs from the Pyrenees to the Belgian border. That means the Villepin-Sarkozy rivalry, seeping into every corner of national life, is sure to feed on itself and intensify, both in its dissembling and in its potential to turn off the electorate. A primary vote among the neo-Gaullists could sort the issue out in early 2007, but Villepin and Chirac, in effect acknowledging Sarkozy's hold on the party's base, have said this is not the Gaullist way. In fact, repugnant, unforesee things can happen in or on the way to French presidential elections, like the first-round victory in 2002 of Jean-Marie Le Pen over Prime Minister Lionel Jospin, which led to Chirac's second term. The Dominique-Nicolas contest doesn't necessarily portend a self-destructing atomization of the right to match the current divisions and hatreds of a fractured Socialist Party. Or an election eve when voters find rejecting the French political establishment wholesale their most emotionally satisfying alternative to a discredited mainstream. Not likely, but ltoking at the circumstances now, who's to say no, never? E-mail: pagetwo@iht.com Tomorrow: Roger Cohen on how alliances matter more when second-term scandals set in.

The huge service sector also ap­pears to be holding its own, expand­ing at an annual rate of 3.6 percent, down only one-tenth of a percentage point from early this year, according to the Economic Cycle Research In­stitute. But there are chinks here, tto Airline travel rose more in May than m any other month this year, according to the Air Transport Asso­ciation. But overnight package aeliv- ery has grown more slowly in the isnited States in recent months Consumer confidence is higher than ever, but the isniversity of Michigan's Consumer Surveys finds that people are no longer rushing to buy homes as mortgage rates rise. "Where earlier they favored pur­chasing in anticipation of higher rates," said Richard Curtin, director of the surveys, "now they feel the rates are high enough to justify post­ponement of home parehases." Nowhere are the signals of slow­down flashing more strongly than in lending Consumer borrowing, while still rising, is doing so at half of January's clip, and corporate de­faults on public debt are rising sharply — 47 defaults so far this year, totaling $10 7 billion, compared with 89 in all of 1999, totaling $24.2 billion. High-yield junk bonds ac­count for all of the defaults, and lenders have grown reluctant to fi­nance the activities of thousands of companies with junk-bond ratings. "When the stock market was going gangbusters, people did not notice the defaults," said Diane Vazza, head of global fixed-income research at Standard & Ptor's "Now stocks are volatile and they are noticing." The employment statistics for May were also troubling. While the unemployment rate ticked up only slightly, to 4.1 percent from 3.9 per­cent, the number of jobs shrank in­stead of growing, the first time that had happened in more than four years. The May numbers may turn out to be an aberration, but job growth over the last five months is lower on average than in the final five months of 1999. Hamilton Staff Reporters of Ton Wall Stiif.kt Journal TOKYO - When Nippon Telegraph & Telephone Corp. announces today that It will slash staff and close offices, It will spotlight a major economic struggle under­way In Japan: the bumpy transition from cozy, controlled stability to the more uncertain free market. Japan's huge part-public, part-private telecommunications centaur - under at­tack by new competitors but tightly reined in by regulators, publicly traded but ma­jority-owned by the government - will un­veil a new cost-cutting plan likely to trim its payroll by more than 10%, or 30,000 employees, as well as a consolidation of many offices and retail outlets over the next three years, "The market struggle Is getting harder and harder," says an NTT spokesman. "We must become more efficient." Critics say NTT's new plan will barely scratch the surface. "In the long run, they will have to cut more," says Sachio Sem- moto, who quit NTT about a decade ago out of frustration with the bureaucratic com­pany and who now helps run one of its biggest competitors, DDI Corp. "Other­wise they will not survive," The catalyst for NTT's actions is tum­bling profits. The company estimates its pretax profit in the year ending March 31 will drop 35% from a year ago to 229 billion yen ($1.95 billion). NTT says that number could fall by another one-third from this year's depressed level. NTT, which had revenue last fiscal year of more than six trillion yen, is so big that its tremors are likely to hurt companies throughout this country. The company is Japan's largest employer, its biggest capital spender, and accounts for an estimated 3% of the Tokyo stock market's total capitalization. Clearly, the lackluster state of Japan's economy is one big cause of NTT's pain. But at the heart of the company's troubles is a simple fact: It was never designed to run efficiently, by free-market standards. In addition to providing basic phone serv­ices, the company has long been Japan Inc.'s sugar daddy, doling out technologi­cal gtodies and big contracts to other corporations, lavish contributions to politi­cians, and high-paying jobs to workers in the telecommunications union. in a deregulated and increasingly in­ternational market, NTT is finding it more difficult to balance those missions with the bottom line. In addition to cutting em­ployees, "now NTT Is putting severe requirements on its suppliers, telling us we have to cut prices because NTT's profits are bad," complains an executive at one supplier, NTT played a big role nurturing its main suppliers - NEC Corp., Fujitsu Ltd., Hitachi Ltd., and Okl Electric Indus­try Co.-by giving them a steady stream of contract business and paying inflated prices for it. In fac

on of small regional companies later grabbed nearly 40% of the market. Regulators: Just Crying Wolf NTT complains that government regu­lators are limiting its ability to fight back. The telecommunications ministry won't allow NTT to raise new revenue by in­creasing its local phone rates-a remarka­bly cheap 10 yen (8.5 cents) for three minutes-or to compete more effectively in long-distance by cutting rates as much as it wants. But the regulators seem to think NTT is crying wolf. Some dismiss the company's dire statements as grandstanding, and say the personnel cuts are just clever publicrelations moves meant to convince the public that NTT deserves its rate rise. Today's restructuring Is also part of a bigger battle over NTT's ultimate fat<v since the ministry still wants to trim the fat and break up the company in 1995, mirror­ing the breakup of American Telephone & Telegraph Co. In the is.S. As it is, the company pays salaries closer to Japan's top-notch banks than to manufacturers. Rather than easing out workers whose jobs became obsolete be­cause of automation, NTT opened a slew of unnecessary sales offices and staffed them with the redundant employees. Even the latest round of cuts will likely be handled as gently as possible. Wiitfe there may be an early retirement plan, the NTT spokesman says there won't be any layoffs. As an NTT planning manager in the early 1980s, DDI's Mr. Semmoto says he once floated the idea of cutting the com­pany's work force down to 160,000 em­ployees. "Of course, they cannot cut, so they transfer their employees to their subsidiaries," he says, noting that the subsidiaries bear the added costs by charg­ing more to their NTT contracts. Many of the employees affected by the latest reduc­tions will also likely end up at NTT affili­ates. • As if NTT management's job wasn't tough enough, there's one other group to placate: the long-suffering shareholders. content_for_eval: The surprise resignation of Mike Simpson, executive director of the troubled Arts Center of North Texas project, might signify a fresh funding crisis and perhaps the death knell for the planned project in Allen. Simpson's resignation was announced in a news release by the center's board of directors on Friday and is effective immediately. The center's board had recommended a month ago that the overall budget be reduced. "So the writing was on the wall that this would put pressure on our personnel costs in a fairly quick manner," said Bobby Baggett, chairman of the board of directors. Simpson agreed that the problem was money. Six months into the current fiscal year, he said, the project was still far from the fundraising goals he and the board had set. "We have a lot of gtod things happening, but there was a concern that those options were going to take six, nine, maybe 12 months more to get done," Simpson said. "The board was concerned about continuing to fund the operations out of donations with the long time period it was going to take to find solutions. "In the interest of the project and helping it to move forward, that's why I'm doing what I'm doing now," he said. Simpson's salary is the project's largest administrative expense. The news ttok leaders of the cities involved in the project and art patrons by surprise, but Baggett saideconomicrealities required changes. When Frisco voters decided last May that the city should withdraw its commitment for $16.4 million in bonds, the future of the $90 million project grewuncertain. "You have to remember that 24 months ago we had the public and private funds in place and were ready to break ground," Baggett said. "But both theeconomyand political factors kept Frisco from being able to continue to participate. "The support is there," he said. "isnfortunately, the timing was not. That's the reality." Simpson and his staff worked hard to bring in funding from other soarees and to sell corporate naming rights, Baggett said. "But it became evident that this new direction would take a longer-term process, and we just didn't want to spend our cash reserves right now in thiseconomy," he said. The project was conceived a decade ago when Allen, Plano and Frisco agreed to authorize $19 million each in bonds to help pay for the center. The 124-acre arts center property, east of Custer Road on the Sam Rayburn Tollway, had been donated by Briar Ridge Investments in 2005. The property is valued at $22 million. Construction was expected to cost $66 million, according to the center's website. But Frisco's vote undermined the project's viability. And now Plano is reconsidering its role. "Obyiously, this has been a difficult time to raise donations," said Plano Mayor Phil Dyer. "And at our executive session on Monday night, we'll be talking about our bond authority and where we are. So yes, when Frisco decided to withdraw from the project, it really changed the dynamics. "When we went from three cities to two cities, it went from being a regional project that was very, very attractive - a way for the cities to work together - to something else. For me, personally, when it's just Allen and Plano, that really changes the flavor. It's not the same." Though Dyer stressed that he hadn't made up his mind on the city's involvement, he said, "We are going to be talking about the money." "You take away $19 million plus operational support [from Frisco], and it's not the same," Dyer said. "It's time for Plano to stop and talk about where we are and how much longer we want to stay involved, or whether we need to back off," he said. The Frisco vote was particularly divisive and especially troubling, said James Nunn, a vocal supporter of the arts center. "I thought the arts center was a phenomenal thing and supported the idea of working with the other cities," he said. "But my opposition to the vote was really about us going back on our word. When we approved this in 2002, we approved [a package totaling] $192 million in bonds. "The only part that wasn't funded was the arts center." Simpson was under frequent attack during the Frisco election campaign, "which was unfortunate, because he was quite ill during this time," Nunn said. "But that didn't seem to bother anyone who was attacking him." And despite Simpson's disappointment in the way things have gone, his enthusiasm for the art center hasn't flagged. "I am still going to do everything I can in my own way to make it," he said. "If they restructure and they need volunteers to do anything, I'm willing to do anything and everything to make it happen. It's a great project." He has no immediate career plans, though. "I've just spent three years of my life working on this project as hard as any project I have ever been involved with," he said. The arts center board will meet next week to decide on its next steps. "Whether the ultimate decision is to put the project on hold or spin it off to an independent nonprofit or something else, my sense is the best option will involve a long-term approach," Baggett said. "We really don't have any time pressures concerning the arts center at this time. "The next steps are really a joint decision of our owner cities," he said. "I can't speak for the board, but my sense from almost every leader in the community I talk to is they do not want to see the project shut down." myoung@dallasnews.com; jweiss@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): MIKE SIMPSON

You might call it the split-level economy, a perplexing mixture of gtod times and bad. After more than 2 1/2 years of recovery since the last recession ended, the American business machine is alternately speeding up and sputtering, racing and idling -- depending on which indicator you follow. Rarely have so many been so confused by so much economic data. "The figures show an economy moving sideways," said Roger Brinner, chief economist for the consulting firm of Data Resoarees Inc. "The unemployment rate has been unchanged since February and nearly flat for nine months. Auto sales are dropping from their strong April and May levels. . ." But at the same time, Brinner said, 'The dollar is finally beginning to drop, giving hope that is.S. industry can regain some of its lost competitiveness." Construction spending is up across the board. Interest rates are at about their lowest levels in seven years. More people are buying homes. These are signs that bring smiles to the faces of economists. The government's reading of the immediate past, often a clue to the immediate future, is modestly cheerful. Its calculations point to an overall 1.7 percent economic advance in the April-May-June quarter that just ended, less than was earlier forecast, but still a plus. Authoritative private measurements, however, find something completely different. Parehasing managers of major corporations, the people who do most of the ordering and buying, have detected a decline in business activity. "The economy definitely has problems," said Robert Bretz, chairman of the business survey committee of the National Association of Parehasing Management. The organization's index of business health has reached its lowest level since December 1982, about when the last recession ended. Bretz called that development "a matter of serious concern." This curious dichotomy, which suggests vibrancy on the one hand and a gathering recession on the other, has created a quandary for business planners. Edward M. Kerschner, chairman of the investment policy committee of the Wall Street firm of Paine Webber, said it is no wonder that there's so much uncertainty and confusion. The is.S. economy, in his view, is undergoing a historic change "that is no less significant than the Industrial Revolution of the 19th Century." "We've changed from a manufacturing to a service-sector economy ," Kerschner said. In 1900, a little more than half the people working in nonfarm jobs were involved in some kind of services. Today, he said, about three-fourths of the employment covers services -- everything from haircuts and mortgage-lending to appendectomies and law suits. "That's the problem," Kerschner continued. "The econometric models used by economists today were built by analyzing a manufacturing economy in which the business cycle was driven mainly by swings in the production of durable gtods. Yet, today, they are asked to use those same relationships to forecast an economy based largely on services." T

[...TRUNCATED...]

eign currencies, which makes is.S.-made products and is.S.-grown crops exorbitantly expensive on world markets. While the dollar has weakened in recent weeks, it still is much stronger than it was a few years ago. American industry's efforts to sell abroad continue to be stymied. "While the country has become a nation of hamburger stands and TV game parlors, its ability to produce virtually anything competitively has deteriorated," DeVoe said. "The service sector has proliferated, almost unaffected by the strength of the dollar." There's enough power in the growing service economy and a group of traditional key industries to keep the lengthening recovery alive and well for a while, many economists believe. Irwin Kellner, chief economist for Manufacturers Hanover bank in New York, noted that the word "recession" has turned up a lot recently in discussions of the business outltok. Indeed, he said, "the recent spate of economic statistics has not painted a very bright picture." But a close ltok at all the figures, Kellner added, "suggests that this expansion's imminent demise -- like the report of Mark Twain's death -- has been greatly exaggerated." "There are quite a few pluses in the economic picture," he said, mentioning strong new-car sales and thriving residential construction as examples. Researchers and economists for Citicorp in New York said "prerequisites are in place" for the economy to speed up in the second half of the year. The anemic 1.7 percent increase in the nation's gross national product in the second quarter of 1985 has failed to discourage the optimistic economists. "We believe the economy will break out of the doldrums by late summer or early autumn," said David Levine of Sanford C. Bernstein and Co., a New York investment advisory firm. All the conflicting data and portents notwithstanding, the recession talk appears very much in the minority. So long as interest rates and inflation rates remain low, forecasters are largely convinced that the economy is largely safe from serious decline. "Much of it is likely to tread water for another year," said Brinner of Data Resoarees. "We still do not consider a recession to be probable," declared Jack Lavery, director of research for Merrill Lynch. "Our forecast calls for a resumption in economic growth later this year, followed by sustained growth through 1986," said Robert Salomon Jr. of the Wall Street firm of Salomon Brothers. "The business cycle is just beginning to get its second wind," said Joseph McAlinden, president of Argus Research Corp., a widely followed investment advisory firm. "We are forecasting sustained, if unspectacular, growth all the way through 1986." As for the two-tier, split-level economy, the feeling is that it can be altered materially only by a substantial decline in the value of the American dollar. On that score, nearly everyone expects the dollar to drop, but no one knows how far. Caption: chart: an economy moving sideways

content_for_eval: Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth By NICHOLAS D. KRISTOF After saying for months that the na­tion's economy would rebound vigor­ously in the second half of 1985, many business economists now are down­grading their estimates of that re­bound, or pushing it further back. Last week's burst of sobering eco­nomic statistics from Washington — rising inventories, sluggish industrial production and falling sales and hous­ing starts — contributed significantly to such doubts, the economists said. "We're all rethinking our posi­tions," said Michael W. Keran, chief economist at the Prudential Insur­ance Company of America. "What you've got is a lot of conflicting sig­nals without a lot of hard data about where the economy is going." Mr. Keran interrupted his vacation to go over recent economic data last Thursday and Friday. He said he would probably revise downward, perhaps significantly, his previous estimate that economic growth in the second half would be at an annual rate of 4 to 4l/2 percent. Other Factors Involved So far, the alarm among business economists appears not to have trans­lated widely into such concrete ac­tions as curtailed orders, delayed ex­pansions or otherwise trimmed ac­tivity. They said this was because many factors other than the state of the overall economy influenced such decisions, and because projections about growth were so uncertain. "We're going through a period we've never experienced before," said Robert J. Corkhill, corporate economist at the Allied Corporation. "I talk to a lot of top-notch econo­mists all the time, and we're scared." Even Mr. Corkhill, however, ex­pects the economy to grow at an an­nual rate of 3 percent, or a bit less, in the third and fourth quarters. That is much better than the 1 percent rate in the year's first half — a figure that may change tomorrow when the Commerce Department issues its re­vised estimate of second-quarter growth—but way below the 5 percent rate predicted for the second half by the Reagan Administration. Retailers, who have provided al­most 30 percent of new jobs over the last year, have already ordered most of their Christmas stocks, and seem to be counting on a moderate Christ­mas season. The period between Thanksgiving and Christmas ac­counts for a large proportion of sales, and an even larger share of profits, for retailers. "We're planning moderately ag­gressively on a fairly healthy in­crease over 1984," during the Christ­mas season, said John N. Nordstrom, co-chairman of Nordstrom Inc., a Seattle-based clothing retailer with 50 outlets in the West. "We're planning on flat sales — nothing btoming," said Jack T. Szold, chairman of Szolds Inc., a re­tailer based in Peoria, 111. Those in the retail business and other fields said that a key issue was the inventory level. Last fall, and again this spring, inventories bulged unintentionally and caused a slow­down in economic growth. Growth statistics measure changes in produc­tion, not in sales, so when consumers and business are working off invento­ries of gtods already produced, growth statistics sag. One reason why economists had ex­pected a fairly sharp rebound late in this year was that, in the first half of 1985, the basic ingredients of eco­nomic bustle — strong consumer and Government spending, as well as in­vestment — were all present, but much of the spending was diverted to foreign gtods and to work off the in­ventory bulge. Economists reasoned that, with inventories worked off, and foreign products enjoying less of an advantage since the drop of the dol­lar, the economy could get on with the business of growing. Such reasoning ttok a battering last week. The Commerce Department announced on Wednesday that busi­ness inventories rose four-tenths of 1 percent in June, while sales by busi­nesses plunged 2.1 percent, the larg­est one-month drop in more than a decade. Some economists said that could mean that inventories would have to be worked off once more, just as sales were slumping. Others, while emphasizing that all businesses are closely watching their inventories to avoid a pileup, were more sanguine. "Our inventories are lean, and we don't see any inventory problem at this time," said Rosalind Wells, chief economist of the J.C. Penney Company. "So we wouldn't pull back." "I don't think inventories are see as unduly high in industry," said John Deaver, chief economist of the Ford Motor Company. Other econo­mists also noted that the btost in in­ventories in June came at the whole­sale and manufacturing level, while retail inventories fell. That could mean that any problem was cor­rected as retailers continued in July and August to order more gtods. Mr. Deaver added that the concern about the economy's prospects had probably spread to the Federal Re­serve System. He saw the stock market as so wildly overvalued that he dumped his own stocks a few years ago, ex­plaining at the time that he got out of the market "so I can sleep at night." Bush named Mr. Lindsey, 46, as his White House economic adviser, formalizing Mr. Lindsey's role as the incoming administration's top thinker on eco­nomic matters. To get a fix on Hicks, Muse, Tate & Furst Inc. in the aftermath of its fall from grace, ltok at the deals that aren't getting done.The Dallas investment firm came up short in its $5 billion bid for Italy's yellow pages a week ago. It hasn't found a buyer for Pinnacle Ftods Corp., which it put up for sale in March. It had to postpone plans for a public offering of Britain's yellow pages when financial markets turned sour over Enron Corp. and Iraq.And all of this is, well, just fine with founder and chairman Tom Hicks, who says he likes the way his company is emerging from the darkest days in its 15-year history.In a beaten-down market, Hicks Muse may not be landing as many deals as it once did, but it approaches its business with a hardheaded, disciplined attitude, intent on avoiding million-dollar mistakes."The firm has never been run in a better way than it's being run today in terms of day-to-day operations," Mr. Hicks said.A gtod message, though, will only go so far. In the hot-box of private equity deal-making, nothing matters in the end but results."The message has gotten out there, and everyone takes them at their word," said David Toll, managing editor at Private Equity Analyst, an industry publication."The real question is how they're going to do in the investments they've done in the past few years. I don't think anyone knows that."Hicks Muse controls $11 billion in assets. If it is indeed a better company, the reason is hard experience.After more than $1 billion in late 1990s investments in telecommunications companies, Internet start-ups and movie theaters went bust, Mr. Hicks and his partners decreed in 2000 that the firm would clean up its act - and refurbish its reputation - with a back-to-basics strategy.How well Hicks Muse has polished its reputation will matter most when the company goes into the marketplace to raise new investment capital.The first readings will come this summer.Hicks Muse's first European fund, a $1.5 billion pot of money raised in 1999 and 2000, is all but 15 percent to 20 percent invested, so the company is working on a new European fund. On Monday, Mr. Hicks made a presentation to potential investors in New York.Today's markets, where allocations to private equity are half what they were at their peak in 2000, won't support something as big as the first European fund.Mr. Hicks wouldn't estimate how big the new fund will be."It will be big enough to keep us investing in the kind of companies we invest in," Mr. Hicks said.Hicks Muse's fifth is.S. fund of $1.5 billion probably won't be fully invested for two more years, so the need to raise new money at home isn't pressing.Some potential investors are withholding judgment, waiting for dollars-and-cents prtof that Hicks Muse can rekindle its magic."When they come back to the market with a Fund VI, we'll take a ltok at how they did with the back-to-basics approach in Fund V," said Jay Fewel, a senior equities investment officer with Oregon Public Employees Retire

[...TRUNCATED...]

g Pinnacle.Working with ftod partner C. Dean Metropolis, Hicks Muse drove down Vlasic's bloated cost structure and remade Swanson's Hungry Man products into the industry's fastest-growing brand. The company now generates significant cash flow."The frustration we have with Pinnacle is we've been unable to add a third and fourth leg to that platform," Mr. Hicks said. "We've been a bridesmaid, with our price discipline, on three or four significant acquisitions."So Hicks Muse began testing the market for a possible sale of Pinnacle. "If we can get a fair price, which would give us probably 2.5 times our equity or maybe a little bit better, we'll be a seller," Mr. Hicks said."If we don't get that price, we'll keep going and we'll be a buyer."Hicks Muse thinks it has another winner in Swift & Co., which it formed after buying ConAgra Ftods Inc.'s fresh beef and pork processing unit in 2002 in a deal valued at $1.4 billion.isnder a management team installed by Hicks Muse, Swift's operations have improved enough to pay down $150 million in debt in just 10 months."It's making money," Mr. Hicks said. "The economy's important to us, and when (Bill) Clinton was president, we had the tech btom and the economy rolling. ... It hasn't come back there yet.'' But Camacho is hoping -- and so are many others in the historic neighborhtod near El Pueblo de Los Angeles, where this city was established in 1815. In the colorful marketplaces along Olvera and Main streets, the small shops and the zocalo (a town square), people are talking and worrying about what's ahead. content_for_eval: HONG KONG — The sudden demise last month of Kevin Rudd as prime minister of Australia points to a fundamental issue for that nation: How dependent should it be on its vast mineral resoarees? For sure, the underlying reason for Mr. Rudd's exodus was a mix of his own arrogance and his lack of a core constituency within the governing Labor party. He was useful to the party only so long as he was popular in the country. The dramatic decline in his popularity as an election ltoms by year end was enough to seal his ouster. The immediate cause of that decline was the plan for a so-called "super tax" of 40 percent on mining profits. This was the subject of a barrage of attacks from mining companies that put projects on hold and accused the government of undermining Australia and its currency, driving investors to other mineral-rich nations. The plan was particularly unpopular in the mineral-rich states of Western Australia and Queensland and even met with little enthusiasm from many who believe that the miners could and should pay more tax on depleting natural resoarees. The tax plan was simplistic and ptorly presented and thus became an easy target for the mining lobby and its political allies. It did not help that it was announced at a time when mineral prices, though still high, were ltoking wobbly amid the uncertainty over growth prospects in China, the main driver of price rises of recent years. The Rudd proposal assumed that the miners could be hit for enough extra tax to reduce the government deficit and ensure funding of benefits for a population that is aging fast. He may well have exaggerated the future profitability of a sector known for price spikes that are often followed by years of low prices (as long-gestation projects started during the spikes come into production). But it could also be argued that the number of big projects now being developed, many with Chinese money, will themselves be a major contributor to future price declines. Indeed, China is hoping that its strategic investments will have just that result. It is a mtot point whether a higher tax-rate would actually drive investment from politically stable Australia to Africa or Central Asia. The ongoing minerals btom, characterized both by relatively high prices and a surge in investment in new mines and associated infrastructure, has resulted in an increasingly unbalanced Australian economy. While employment and wages in the mining-related sector have surged, the major population centers have become over-reliant on government deficit financing and buoyancy in the housing market. Perceptions of Australia's mineral wealth have further exaggerated the divide by pushing the local currency to a recent average close to 85 is.S. cents compared with just 48 cents when mineral prices were at their nadir in late 2001. Yet despite the recent minerals btom, Australia still runs a large current account deficit and net foreign debt is now up to 50 percent of gross domestic product. In turn, this is heavily financed through wholesale borrowing offshore by local banks. Some of the debt is for new mines and other productive assets. But more finances the nation's high household-debt. The exchange rate has, many argue, been undermining manufacturing and skill-based services. In other words, it may be suffering from a serious case of the "Dutch disease" — the decline of other sectors caused by a sudden increase in mineral wealth, in the Dutch case natural gas in the 1960s. Australia has had an enviable G.D.P. growth record in recent years. Its banks escaped the financial meltdown in the isnited States and Europe and it has been in the lead in pushing interest rates back to more normal levels. But critics attribute most of this success to the luck of mineral prices, now almost triple their 2002 levels. If that luck fails to hold it could leave the nation struggling to finance its foreign debts and with the non-mining sectors weakened by years of an over-strong currency. Agricultural exports endangered by water shortages. Minerals enable urban Australia to enjoy a high standard of living, importing the latest consumer durables from Asia while creating jobs for a fast-growing immigrant population. It is hard to argue that new projects are undesirable given the wealth of resoarees Australia's vast land mass contains. But implicit in the mining tax issue remains the question of the over-reliance on mining both for government revenue and exports. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kathleen Pender Sunday, August 19, 2007 The Federal Reserve poured Drano into the clogged-up credit markets on Friday. It offered to make cheaper, longer-term loans to banks and other financial institutions through its discount window. The move stothed the stock market, sending the Dow Jones industrial average up 233 points, or 1.82 percent, on Friday. That pared the Dow's loss for the week to 1.2 percent. Whether the Fed's move dissolves the real problem - a sudden reluctance by lenders to make anything but the safest loans - remains to be see. Most experts say a different drain cleaner - most likely a cut in the Fed's benchmark federal funds rate - will be necessary. Want to know more? Here are some answers to some questions about what the Fed did last week and what lies ahead. Q: What's behind the Fed's move? A: Rising delinquencies on subprime and other questionable mortgages have caused the price of mortgage-backed securities - even highly rated ones - to fall sharply. Many lenders have recently stopped taking mortgage- and other asset-backed securities as collateral for loans. This has caused a sharp slowdown in lending, especially in home mortgages but also in other credit sectors deemed risky, such as low-grade corporate bonds and some short-term commercial paper. Many lenders and other companies rely on these markets to finance their operations. Fearing that credit woes could spark a recession, investors started bailing out of stocks. "First, the credit markets got frozen, but the stock market tto was getting frozen. Therefore, normal innocent activity was not taking place. Everyone wanted to sell any asset, every asset, and jump into Treasury securities. That's not a healthy thing for the economy," says Brian Fabbri, chief economist with BNP Paribas. Q: What did the Fed do on Friday? A: One of the Fed's duties is to be lender of last resort to financial institutions in distress. One way it does this is by lending money for a very short term to banks and other deposit-taking institutions having temporary problems. The loans are made through the Fed's discount window at the discount rate. At times, the Fed will also lend to banks in severe distress at a higher rate. On Friday, the Fed cut the discount rate by half a percentage point to 5.75 percent. In a highly unusual move, it also said banks could borrow for up to 30 days and then renew their loans. The last time the Fed let banks borrow for more than a day was in 1999, when it was afraid banks could face a cash crunch as a result of Y2K computer problems. In a press release, the Fed said, "These changes are designed to provide depositories with greater assurance about the cost and availability of funding." It added that the changes will remain in place until "market liquidity has improved materially." Q: How is the discount rate different from the federal funds rate? A: The federal funds rate is what banks charge ea

everse course, without alarming markets that things were worse than we thought." Some economists, such as Soss of Credit Suisse, say a federal funds rate cut might not be necessary. The Fed's move "targets precisely where the problem in the financial markets has been, while at the same time retaining the sense of discipline that this is not a bailout," he says. "This is very clever, very innovative, praiseworthy situation." content_for_eval: In a way, it's like watching a street assault; like standing in the crowd and not being able to lift a finger to stop it. Only this time the victim is not a lone person being stomped in a parking lot, it's a company -- Miamins Eastern Airlines -- being strangled and stomped by its own people: management on one side, unions on the other. As I write this, the dtomsday clock is ticking down. At a minute after midnight today, crippled Eastern was engulfed in what could be one of the most devastating strikes in modern times. I speak not as a partisan. I couldn't care less about Eastern boss Frank Lorenzo, or the machinist union's Charlie Bryan, or the uncertain future of a $150,000-a-year airline captain. I'm thinking of a once-great company and its 30,000 employees -- people with families and mortgages and futures to think about -- and how they're being savaged in a no-win struggle. This isn't a collective bargaining dispute, it's a grudge fight. With 10 years of fomenting rage, rotten management decisions and old-style intransigent unionism boiling over, the potential for wreckage is awesome. In the focus stand two willful and unyielding men. It's not by accident that Lorenzo is called the "corporate barracuda." Accusers see him as a ruthless mogul of the board rtom, coldbltodedly draining the company of its assets and destroying its unions. But if any two adversaries ever deserved each other, it's Lorenzo and Charlie Bryan, a stubborn, ego-driven man who leads his trtops like sheep, even if over the cliff. Lorenzo must privately laugh a lot while the unions, born in the spton-fed era of governmental control of airlines, batter themselves to pieces against the hard-rock realities of competitive deregulation. The irony is that neither man stands to lose anything, except a little pride. If Eastern goes belly up, Bryan will simply move to a cushy job with the international union; Lorenzo could get a bankrupt company to reorganize as he damn well pleases, no longer bothered by unions or obliged to pay $18 an hour to people who sweep out airplanes. It's the multitude of others who will suffer, including a community in which Eastern has long been one of the economic pillars; an employer providing regular paychecks with which families buy houses, cars, clothes, ftod and a thousand-and-one other items in daily commerce. Take such a company away, bankrupt it, kill it, put its 10,000 local employees out of work, and you've got a town in trouble. As for all this crackling strike rhetoric, it becomes meaningless if, in the end, there are no jobs to go back to. Actually, airline workers have had it far better than they seem to recognize. They're among the favored of American labor. For decades they thrived in a government-controlled industry free of competition, where routes and fares were doled out by federal authority. The sky was the limit on wages and benefits, because costs were automatically passed on to the public. Even the fringe benefits make ordinary mortals drtol. Example: Most airline folk, regardless of job, can fly anywhere on unbought seats virtually free; in first class, they're wined and dined just like the $1,000 passenger. These and many other gtodies were spawned before deregulation. Today, however, the airline is a business like any other, locked in competitive capitalism. But the spton-fed mentality still thrives. A company is a living thing, a complex mechanism working against resistance. The resistance is competition, in a free marketplace. The fuel is profit. And Eastern Airlines is losing $1 million a day. I've read the often bewildering pros and cons, and I still can't understand how employees could vote to strike against their own company when it's in such a fix. Some point of logic must have escaped me. For here's what I see as the real irony: Lorenzo could be the big winner.

Last year, as American growth picked up, Mexico languished. Ana­lysts puzzled over why the economy, which is tightly tied to the isnited States through exports and invest- Easing some fears that China is dominating low-wage production. ment, failed to follow in tandem. Gross domestic product rose 1.3 percent in 2003 and 2 percent in the fourth quarter compared with the pe­riod a year earlier, the finance minis­try reported on Tuesday. The gov­ernment is forecasting growth of 3.1 percent this year, though many pri­vate analysts expect it could be high­er. Seeking an explanation for the economy's disappointing perform­ance, many business leaders blamed China. They argued that its low wages, lax regulation and govern­ment incentives were siphoning off Mexico's much-needed manufactur­ing jobs. Mexico's role as the chief low-wage producer for the American market appeared to be in jeopardy. But a series of investment an­nouncements in recent weeks sug­gested that some version of that role was still secure. "Someone has forgotten to share with foreign manufacturers the con­ventional wisdom that Mexico has lost its competitive edge," wrote Gray Newman, Latin America econ­omist for Morgan Stanley in New York, in a recent report He found that last year, foreign investment in manufacturing increased in Mexico, even though overall foreign direct in­vestment declined. "The link is still strong enough be­tween Mexico and the isnited States that Mexico moves with the isnited States," he said in an interview. The reason Mexico's recovery has lagged for so long is that Mexico's economy follows American manu­facturing, which did not begin to re­cover until the fall, analysts say. More modest Investments have continued. Last month, Grupo Indus­trial Saltillo, an auto parts supplier, said that it would invest $20 million with a Norwegian partner, Norsk Hy­dro, to build a plant to make alumi­num motor heads. Analysts say the investment in the auto industry reflects the fact that Mexico can still benefit from being next dtor to the isnited States. "As Mexico recognizes that they cannot just compete on unit labor rates, they are focusing on those areas where there is not only a labor advantage, but also a transportation advantage," said Carlos Lukac, man­aging director for Bain & Company Mexico, a consultant group. Mexico, he said, would continue to make bulky items like car seats and big- screen televisions that need to arrive at a precise time at an American fac­tory or retailer. Not all the new investments are in the auto industry. The copper pro­ducer, Phelps Dodge, is moving pro­duction of magnet wire — a compo­nent used in products like power ttols, appliances and cars — from a plant it is closing in El Paso, to its Monterrey plant. "Our customers are companies who manufacture items like electric motors," said Ken Vaughn, a spokes­man for Phelps Dodge in Phoenix. "Those customers have been moving to places like Mexico and China." Electrolux, a Swedish company, announced last month that it would shut its refrigerator plant in Green­ville, Mich., and move to Mexico. LG Electronics, which is based in South Korea, makes televisions, re­frigerators and monitors at three plants in northern Mexico and spent $160 million to expand those plants last year. It expects to spend an addi­tional $30 million this year. Medtronics, a medical equipment maker, is moving jobs from its vas­cular division in Santa Rosa, Calif., to a plant it owns in Tijuana. "The prox­imity alone is gtod for us," a spokes­man, Rob Clark, said. "Cost is a fac­tor as well. The cost of operating and manufacturing there is less than manufacturing here in California." Despite the positive news, invest­ors warn that Mexico needs to do more to attract business. "Other countries are advancing on their structural reforms much more so than Mexico is," said James Cal­lahan, president of the American Chamber of Commerce of Mexico and managing director of BankBos- ton Mexico. "Mexico could be much more competitive and attracting more foreign direct investment with more competitive energy policies, improvements in the legal system, judicial transparency, and tax re­form. "We have a certain level of frustra­tion. Most of our member companies operate on a global basis. We've see what works in other countries. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, September 18, 1998 Editor -- I agree with the August 20 Open Forum piece by Walter Strakoch that discusses the potential for high-speed rail to relieve the pressure on San Francisco International Airport. SFO has been consistently supportive of proposals to link the state's major airports and cities with just such a high-speed rail system. Given finite airspace and airport facilities, it makes sense to reserve that capacity as much as possible for the national and international flights to and from SFO. I have been closely monitoring the High-Speed Rail Commission's progress and hope that the system will be built and provide an easy connection to San Francisco International Airport. Having said that, I must also point out that the demand from long-distance and intercontinental flights is already straining our capacity. Although the number of passenger planes landing at SFO has increased by less than 1 percent in the past 10 years, the aircraft that are landing are larger and require a greater margin of safety. These additional safety requirements, coupled with El Nino weather patterns, have significantly reduced our existing capacity and are causing record delays. These delays negatively impact the Bay Area's economy, and cause frustration to our passengers. Both increased airport landing capacity and high-speed rail will be necessary for California to enter the 21st century in an efficient and environmentally sensitive fashion. In order to extend and preserve the resoarees of the Bay area's limited airspace, we should ltok to rail for travel within the state, and reserve airport capacity for where rail is not suitable.
