Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Chiang, Mindy S. Lubber Friday, April 20, 2007 A popular commercial asks, "what's in your wallet?" A similar question should be asked of investors: Do you know what risks are lurking in your portfolio? A growing community of institutional investors representing $4 trillion in assets have asked that question, and their answer may surprise you: Global climate change is near the top. Climate change is real and rapidly occurring. No sector of the economy is immune from its effects. The most recent grim climate change impact report from the isnited Nations is only the latest reminder. Stronger hurricanes, prolonged heat waves and carbon-reducing regulations are just a few of the ways climate change is already rippling across various business sectors, from insurance and agriculture, to electric power and tourism. If asked what parts of the federal government need to step up to address climate change, you would probably think of Congress, the White House, the Environmental Protection Agency and the Federal Emergency Management Agency. But there is another agency that has a major role to play: the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Increasingly, when large institutional investors evaluate the companies they invest in, they are asking tough questions about how management is hedging the risks of climate change and preparing to operate in a carbon-constrained economy. These investors ltok for companies where the CEO, other top managers and the board of directors have made managing climate-change risk a top priority. They ltok for companies that are poised to seize the economic opportunities presented by climate change by leading the way in the development of clean technologies. But individual investors rarely have the opportunity to ask such questions of management. Although existing securities law requires corporations to disclose material risks to potential investors in their regular reporting to shareholders, few do so on climate-related matters. A January 2007 study published by Ceres, a coalition of investors and environmental groups working on climate change, and the Calvert Group, an asset-management firm, found that despite rising financial losses attributable to climate change, more than half of the country's 500 largest publicly-traded companies are doing a ptor job disclosing climate-change risks to their investors. That is why, last year, more than two-dozen institutional investors managing more than $1 trillion in assets wrote SEC Chairman Christopher Cox urging that routine disclosure of those risks be required by the SEC. In March of this year, an even larger group of investors representing $4 trillion is assets, including the California controller's office and Ceres, were joined by two-dozen CEOs from some of America's leading companies including DuPont, Alcoa and Sun Microsystems, to urge both a national policy on climate change that includes dramatic reducti

[...TRUNCATED...]

 companies that is essential to their investment decision-making and the SEC needs to provide better interpretive guidance for companies clarifying the materiality of climate change in securities filings." The SEC does not require new legislation to take these steps. Existing law requiring disclosure of material risks gives the SEC the authority it needs to require climate-risk disclosure by publicly owned companies. Accordingly, the SEC should take these two steps immediately: recognize climate change as a material risk to the companies it regulates; and strengthen disclosure requirements and guidelines by requiring companies to provide the information investors such as CalPERS need to assess risks posed by climate change. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Catherine Bowman, Chronicle Staff Writer Monday, December 15, 1997 (12-15) 04:00 PDT EL CERRITO -- The signs hang over empty storefronts at the El Cerrito Plaza like gravestones in a ghost town. The Emporium. Goldman's. Visit Wtolworth's Luncheonette. The Plaza was going somewhere when it opened in 1958, and everybody wanted to be there. In its heyday, there were enough crowds for two Santas -- and so many cars in the parking lot that it was easy to lose your own. Shy Cub Scouts sang Christmas carols to crowds that listened. Over time, it became the downtown that El Cerrito never had. City btosters haven't forgotten that, and say the Plaza will be an economic engine again. At the Plaza's height, ``you could come down and not do anything and still be part of the community,'' said Andrew Gtoden, who has shopped at the mall for 25 years. ``Everything was right here.'' Wedged between Albany and Richmond, El Cerrito is known as a gtod place to raise a family -- and not much else. Many of its 23,278 residents have watched with envy as Berkeley's Fourth Street and Albany and Berkeley's Solano Avenue have blossomed into trendy shopping districts. Even Richmond, with its sagging economy, has Hilltop mall. El Cerrito has small shopping centers along San Pablo Avenue, but nothing comes close to what the Plaza used to be. Today, more than half the mall is empty. Senior citizens drop by, sometimes just to be there. Only one Santa is there this Christmas, and he sits in a vacant former Miller's Outpost that's decked out like the North Pole. ``I'm disgusted with it,'' sighed 77-year-old Helen Kemp as she walked toward the parking lot. ``There's nothing here except for Long's, Lucky's and a few things like that.'' ``If you need a set of towels,'' Gtoden said, ``you've got to leave town.'' Ask the old-timers why the Plaza is struggling and everyone tells a different story. Some blame it on the City Council, Plaza owners, or both. Others point at competition from trendier malls and large discount chains. Some insist it was the Emporium's fault, but the Plaza was in trouble long before the store folded in early 1996. ``Even back in 1983, the question anybody asked anybody who was running for council was, `What are you going to do about the Plaza?' '' Councilman Norman La Force said. ``People wanted to see changes made back then.'' The dune-colored mall may be out of style, but it retains a homey 1950s flavor. On one side is a classy men's store, Foley and Bonny, and on the other the Mel-O-Dee bar, a nicotine lover's dream. See's Candies is not exactly homegrown, but some of the silver-haired ladies pushing buttercreams have worked there for years. For longtime merchants, giving up would be like selling the family home. ``You have to think of this Plaza as a diamond in the rough,'' said Janet Foley, who filled in for her dad the other day at the men's store. ``We've got BART, we've got AC Transit

[...TRUNCATED...]

e added to the 30-acre site to mimic the grid pattern of nearby neighborhtods. At the heart of the shopping center would be a town plaza. But the Plaza's future won't ltok like its past. These days, big department stores like to do business in clusters, and Plaza owners say that's not feasible. That means the big store that many residents seem to want isn't likely to come. In the meantime, El Cerrito residents continue to spend their money at department stores in San Francisco, Corte Madera and Walnut Creek -- and that's bad news for their cash-strapped hometown. City officials estimate El Cerrito is losing at least $250,000 a year in sales tax revenue because of the closure of the Emporium and sagging Plaza business. The new plans for the Plaza probably will be unveiled early next year. Several merchants complain they are being kept in the dark about the details. Economy Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, June 29 — In a three-pronged indication of a weaken­ing economy, fresh reports today showed a plunge in new-home sales and unsettling, albeit moderate, de­clines in consumer confidence and the Government's chief forecasting index. Although most analysts continued to expect growth for the second half of the year to exceed that of the very sluggish first half, today's data prompted expressions of concern that the risks to further expansion had grown. 'Economy Could Turn Down' "The economy could turn down — or it could slow down" some more, said Larry Moran, a Commerce De­partment economist. He referred spe­cifically to the three-tenths of 1 per­cent decline in the department's in­dex of leading indicators for May, the fifth straight month in which the in­dex reversed direction. The index had risen a revised two-tenths of 1 per­cent in April. Home sales dropped so sharply in May — 21 percent — that a huge April gain was more than wiped away, the Government also reported. The plunge, the biggest in 13 years, came despite the fact that buyers enjoyedsome of the lowest interest rates m two decades and relatively appealing prices. In a third report, the Conference Board found that consumer confi­dence in June declined for the second straight month, with sentiment — the ptorest since October — falling for both current conditions and those in the months immediately ahead. "The continued low level of con­sumer confidence suggests that the economy remains weak," said Fabi­an Linden, compiler of the survey data for The Conference Board, a business-sponsored research group in New York. Although there have been some re­cent encouraging economic reports, including slackened inflation and a ninth consecutive month in which consumers felt comfortable enough to take on additional installment debt, uncertainty over the fate of the Ad­ministration's budget and health plans is widely cited as a soaree of restraint. "The fiscal package is pretty uu tere," observed David B. Bostian j: chief economist for the Wall Street firm of Herzog Heine Geduld. Mr. Bostian, who met privately with a top official at the Federal Reserve on Monday, said he got the impression that the official was disinclined to heed calls by some colleagues that the Fed should make a pre-emptive strike against inflation by raising Continued on Page D5 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. short-term interest rates. Such a strike seems increasingly less likely, however, in light of the latest ptor economic data and the fact that a budget containing large tax increases and some measure of spending reduction is likely to be en­acted this summer. 'Dramatic Prtof' Indeed, Republicans may use the suspect state of the economy to argue against the budget. Bob Dole, the Sen­ate Republican Leader, stepped for­ward to cite today's three reports as "dramatic prtof that the last thing, the sluggish economy needs is the Clinton tax plan." On Friday, the Labor Department is scheduled to publish figures for the June job market, usually the most important indicator for any month. The three-tenths of 1 percent de­cline in the Government's leading in­dex, which is designed to signal eco­nomic turning points six to nine months in advance, was not unexpect­ed since most of the components had been published previously. Yet analysts fretted that the index, now at 151.6, has shown no real strength since a burst in December and is at its lowest point so far in 1993. Six of the 11 index components con­tributed to the May decline, the big­gest factor by far being a drop in consumer expectations as surveyed by the isniversity of Michigan. Other Factors The other drags were lower factory backlogs of unfilled orders for dura­ble gtods, a decline in new factory orders for consumer gtods, higher first-time claims for unemployment benefits, lower prices for materials especially sensitive to the business cycle and quicker deliveries by cor­porate suppliers. A jump in the money supply, the first in eight months, was the main soaree of upward pressure on the index. The others were higher con­tracts and orders for plant and equip­ment, a rise in building permits and an uptick in stock prices. The length of the average workweek, the 11th component, was unchanged. Just as worrisome to some ana­lysts as the leading index, whose com­ponents move ahead of the economy as a whole, is the performance of its companion, the coincident index. The coincident index, composed of indicators that tend to move at the same time as business activity, is only slightly higher, at 125.8, than it was in December — despite a four- tenths of 1 percent May gain. And it st&nds less than two. points above the level of March 1991, the month inwhich the latest recession officially ended. In other words, the economy has been going almost nowhere for some time as gauged by the items making up the coincident index — the size of nonfarm payrolls, personal incomes, industrial production and factory and trade sales. Still another indicator, the lagging index of elements that tend to move after a time lag in the direction of the coincident index, edged up one-tenth of 1 percent last month. 'A Little Nervous' The dive in new-home sales last month, to an annual rate of 571,000, was roughly twice as large as antici­pated following an April bulge that would have been almost impossible to sustain. Stuart Leckie is trying to sell rugby to the Chinese. Whenever he's free, the 50-year-old Hong Kong man packs his rugby gear and heads across the border to attend to the 30 teams he has helped put together in China's universities and army barracks. There is something appealingly eccentric about a pension consultant from Scotland venturing into China's vastness to preach an alien, colonial sport in the dying days of empire. But his bridge-building is one of a thousand points of light in a future darkened by uncertainty. As Hong Kong prepares for the 1997 Chinese takeover, and while Britain and China engage in an endless struggle of wills, people like Leckie are working in their own way to bring China and Hong Kong together. "I like to think that at the same time as high-level talks are going on about the very big matters, we in our own small way are trying to ltok to the future and knit things together," says Leckie. He isn't alone. Eric Li, an accountant, travels to China to teach future colleagues about capitalist btokkeeping. Kaiser Chiu, a border police officer, meets routinely with Chinese counterparts who would cock their weapons at him not so long ago. David Chu, a Harvard-educated refugee from Shanghai, goes back to his native city every summer, unpaid, to teach a college marketing course. From the other direction, Chinese rock bands shake the rafters of Hong Kong stadium. Engineers pipe drinking water from China to Hong Kong. Chinese social workers come to share their experiences. Such work is becoming more urgent as Hong Kong begins the two-year countdown to July 1, 1997, when the lease runs out on a colony often called "a borrowed place living on borrowed time." It is an undertaking unprecedented in history -- a vast social, political and corporate merger of a capitalist titan and a Communist superpower. The end of Hong Kong's 156-year colonial era was always going to be an unnerving experience for its six million people, and they had been counting on China and Britain working together to give them a reassuring hand. Instead, as each government seeks to shape Hong Kong in its own image, they argue about everything from how much democracy it should have to how it should cleanse its polluted harbor. Britain says it wants to leave Hong Kong secure in its democratic liberties and rule of law. But a waning imperial power has little leverage over a rising China. China has promised Hong Kong it can keep its free and capitalist ways for 50 years. But the regime that made the pledge back in 1984 will be gone by 1997. As the Deng Xiaoping era nears its end and a new leadership struggles to find its feet, this may be a bad time for so complex a step as changing ownership of Hong Kong. China has never forgotten the historic insult of losing pieces of itself -- even tiny ones -- to foreign powers. Now it seems convinced the British are scheming to keep a toehold in Hong Kong after 1997 -- and if they cannot, to squander its reserves, foment anti-Chi

people are refugees from Communist China, and they simply do not trust their once and future master. "We will lose freedom of expression, freedom of the press, artistic freedom, academic freedom, freedom of worship," says legislator Emily Lau. She can already cite several examples. When China denounced a British Broadcasting Corp. television documentary on Mao Zedong the Hong Kong station that had bought the film refused to broadcast it. When garment tycton Jimmy Lai wrote a vituperative critique of Chinese Premier Li Peng, his showcase store in Beijing was ordered closed with no explanation. When legislator Martin Lee sued a former judge in the pro- China camp for libel, he said he had great difficulty finding a law firm to represent him. Hong Kong's lawyers have extensive business in China. China has delivered its message to Hong Kong in clear language: Keep making money but don't infect the rest of China with your democratic notions. No one should be "so naive as to think that they can turn Hong Kong into a political city in order to influence the mainland," Lu Ping, China's man in charge of Hong Kong affairs, warned last year. "Hong Kong has always been an economic city, never a political city." Businessman David Chu is convinced China will respect Hong Kong's separateness, "like husband and wife, but with separate bank accounts." Hong Kong needs China's markets and labor force, and China needs Hong Kong's business savvy, he says. China and Hong Kong are each other's biggest trading partners. Hong Kong pours money into China, and out come toys, CD players, air conditioners, even the dim sum consumed at Hong Kong lunch tables. Chu says it's up to Hong Kong people to defend their rights, "with one hand holding the Basic Law and the other a clenched fist." Sotheby's estimates the painting will fetch $2.5 million to $3.5 million. Tuesday evening in New York, a group of artworks billed as "A Private American Collection" will come up for auction. The 21 lots going on the block include major paintings by Willem de Ktoning, Arshile Gorky, Franz Kline, David Park, Wayne Thiebaud and Nathan Oliveira. The sale is expected to bring up to $60 million. The collector's name appears nowhere in the auction catalog or in Sotheby's other materials. But word of his identity circulated quickly: San Francisco investment banker Thomas Weisel. The omission was deliberate. "If you ltok at catalogs where it's 'the collection of. .. ,' they spend as much time talking about the collector -- how he collected, why he collected -- as about the art," Weisel said in a conversation at his office. "I want this to be about the art, not about me. I've never been comfortable talking about my personal life, including collecting things of some monetary value." From the public's point of view, though, auction news is first and last about the money. Sotheby's estimates of what some of the items for sale will bring seem staggeringly high: $8 million to $10 million for de Ktoning's "Orestes" (1947), a 2-by-3-ftot, mostly black and white abstraction; $3 million to $5 million for Thiebaud's big 1962 "Toy Counter"; $6 million to $8 million for Gorky's indisputably great "From a High Place" (1944). Sotheby's catalog makes the case for these prices based not only on previous auction figures but also on the historical significance of individual pieces. Even Weisel says he's amazed at prices things have brought at the high end of recent art auctions, despite -- or maybe in the minds of rich buyers, because of -- widespread economic uncertainty. "I started exploring this (sale) after I saw the May prices," Weisel said. "The values are just extraordinary right now, I never thought we'd see values like this." So why is he parting with so many prime artworks? "This is just about diversifying a portfolio and interest and taste moving on," Weisel said. "But we've retained a pretty substantial number of works by these same artists -- a number of Gorkys, some great Klines, quite a bit of Thiebaud, Oliveira, Park." He owns around 700 artworks, including African, Oceanic and Native American material, as well as modern and contemporary American and European art. Some pieces hang in the offices of Thomas Weisel Partners, others at the homes of Weisel and his wife, Emily, in Ross and Hawaii. David Lazarus Bill Ford, chief exec of Ford Motor Co., was straightforward last week in announcing plans to idle 14 North American facilities and lay off as many as 30,000 workers. "We will be making painful sacrifices to protect Ford's heritage and secure our future," he said. "We will be able to deliver more innovative products, better returns for our shareholders and stability in the communities where we operate." Painful sacrifices? Check. Innovative products? Possibly. Better returns for shareholders? Maybe. Stability in the communities where Ford operates? I don't think so. The restructuring announced by Ford, which follows similar moves by General Motors, demonstrates among other things that autoworkers are expendable, and that tens of thousands of people -- and their families -- are about to be cast adrift at a time of economic uncertainty. This isn't how communities are stabilized. It's how they're eviscerated. There was a time when Ford ttok a diametrically opposite approach to its workers, a trailblazing recognition that well-paid employees make for a more stable and productive company. Those days are gone. But it's worth one last ltok in the rear-view mirror to see if any lessons can be learned as Ford and other automakers adjust to a new world order. "The idea of treating workers better is still very much relevant today," said Howard Segal, a history professor at the isniversity of Maine and author of "Recasting the Machine Age: Henry Ford's Village Industries." "Henry Ford understtod this," he said. "He knew that you have to make workers feel appreciated if you want a stable workforce." As 1913 was drawing to a close, though, Ford was troubled. His introduction of the assembly line had allowed his namesake firm to crank out Model T cars every 24 seconds. But employee absenteeism was running about 10 percent a day, turnover was soaring and productivity was lagging. Ford needed a way to attract the best workers and keep them steadily on the payroll, which in turn would improve the quality of his vehicles and thus increase market share. His solution: In January 1914, Ford introduced a minimum wage of $5 a day, or about twice what other automakers were offering. "The commonest laborer who sweeps the fltor shall receive his $5 per day," Ford explained at the time. "We believe in making 20,000 men prosperous and contented rather than follow the plan of making a few slave drivers in our establishment millionaires." He also believed in messing with people's lives through what Ford Motor Co. called its Sociological Department. This was a team of investigators who visited workers' homes to ensure that they were living the sort of upright lifestyles that Henry Ford approved of. isnless the Sociological Department signed off on a worker's moral qualifications, that $5 daily wage would remain out of reach. David Gartman, a sociology professor at the isniversity of South Alabama who has studied the auto industry, noted that Ford's workforce at th

[...TRUNCATED...]

. This in turn would tie them more tightly to the workplace." isltimately, he and other academics say that Ford was acting primarily out of economic self-interest when he doubled the pay of virtually his entire workforce. They argue that stability for both employees and their employer comes only when both sides prosper. This creates a sense of mutual respect and a shared commitment to facing whatever challenges may come. "The $5 minimum wage was a moment of real innovation," said Thomas Sugrue, a professor of history and sociology at the isniversity of Pennsylvania who has written extensively about the auto industry. "Ford created a stable, loyal workforce by paying more than a living wage to employees," he said. "This benefited the company but it also transformed the lives of working-class folk." On the other hand, the $5 wage ended up going only part way toward achieving its goals. What Ford hadn't also addressed was the way his assembly lines had changed the workplace. "It was brutal," said Segal at the isniversity of Maine. "You were literally a cog in a machine. After a few years, Ford discovered that $5 a day would get people through the dtor, but it didn't guarantee a happy workforce." Labor upheaval and unionization would gradually follow, accompanied by a shifting of the industry's focus away from employee well-being and toward increased automation. "Since the 1950s, there's been very little innovation on the human-capital side," noted Sugrue at the isniversity of Pennsylvania, "and it's hard to imagine it ever happening again." So here we are. Bill Ford, Henry Ford's great-grandson, is shutting down plants and handing thousands of workers their hats. He dubbed his restructuring plan "the Way Forward." "We must be guided by our long-term goals of building our brands, satisfying customers, developing strong products, accelerating innovation and, most importantly, producing a sustainable profit from our automotive business," Ford said. Those are gtod goals. But ltok at the one missing piece -- Ford's employees. Stephen Meyer, a history professor at the isniversity of Wisconsin-Milwaukee who specializes in labor matters, said times have changed. Global markets no longer allow is.S. automakers to nurture a generously compensated workforce. "What that means is that we've got people going into jobs with no health benefits or pensions or whatever," Meyer said. "We're seducing people into a consumer society but not giving them any way to stay in it. "It's understandable why this is happening," he observed. "But you can't help feel that we've lost a sense of decency along the way." David Lazarus' column appears Wednesdays, Fridays and Sundays. Send tips or feedback to dlazarus@sfchronicle.com. content_for_eval: An industry report suggesting theeconomyis improving failed to cheer Wall Street on Thursday as the Nasdaq composite index fell to its lowest level in more than two years.Analysts said the market was concerned the upbeat news was temporary and would deter the Federal Reserve Board from cutting interest rates again. They also noted that buyers are sitting out of a market in which stronger company profits and stock prices are elusive.The Nasdaq composite index closed down 23.98 at 2,244.96, its lowest close since hitting 2,208.05 on Jan. 4, 1999.Blue chips were less vulnerable. The Dow Jones industrial average ended the session virtually unchanged, rising 0.23 to 10,526.81 despite sliding as much as 153 points in heavy trading.The broader Standard & Ptor's 500 index slipped 2.45 to 1,252.82.All three indexes are below where they started in 2001, despite healthy run-ups in some stocks last month. The Nasdaq has been particularly devastated: It is now nearly 56 percent off its all-time closing high of 5,048.62, reached last March."I believe investor fear is beginning to develop," said Bill Barker, an investment strategy consultant at Dain Rauscher. "We're beginning to see people say: 'I want out of this market because it's going to go a lot lower.'"Pessimism again ruled the street Thursday, pushing down a brief advance early in the afternton on bargain hunting.Among the biggest losers: Brocade Communications, which tumbled $2.75, or 6 percent, to $41.94, after some investment firms reduced their earnings expectations on worries about the future.The negative sentiment was also felt by blue chips, although to a lesser degree.Industrial and retailing sectors struggled, led lower by General Motors, down 34 cents at $52.12, and Wal-Mart, off 51 cents at $49.70.Even pharmaceuticals, which are popular with investors in times ofeconomicuncertainty, suffered: Merck declined $1.01 to $77.70.The bargain hunting did drive a few stock prices higher, though, particularly of companies that had been beaten down in the previous session.The Dow got lifts from banker J.P. Morgan Chase, up 95 cents at $48.30, and IBM, which gained $1.39 to $108.90. Cisco Systems, which has been battered in recent months, also got a respite from investor wrath, rising $1.31 to $26.44, a 5 percent gain.But overall, the market remained weak - a reflection, analysts said, of worries that theeconomyis slowing tto much and it may take more than a few months before it turns around.The Fed has lowered interest rates twice this year and many market experts were counting on a third cut next month. Thursday'seconomicreport raised concerns that might not happen since such cuts are usually predicated on the assumption growth is slowing, not increasing as the news suggested.The New York-based Conference Board said its Index of LeadingEconomicIndicators rose a greater-than-expected 0.8 percent to 109.4 last month, reversing three consecutive monthly declines."The index results suggest that the Fed might be advised to put any easing on hold," said Hugh Johnson, chief investment officer at First Albany Corp., who also said investors don't share the index's optimistic forecast. "The market is just worrying deeply that the Fed will not give us what we need."The index, which attempts to measure the direction theeconomyis headed, is based on the performance of indicators ranging from weekly manufacturing hours and interest rates to initial claims for unemployment insurance.Trading was heavy and volatile. Declining issues outnumbered advancers nearly 2-to-1 Thursday on the New York Stock Exchange. Consolidated volume came to nearly 1.6 billion, ahead of the 1.43 billion reported Wednesday.The Russell 2,000 index fell 6.25 to 477.26.

content_for_eval: What had promised to be a season of expansion has turned out to be one of sheer survival for Maximum Dance Company. And in that, there's some success. Because despite financial problems that threatened to swamp the group months earlier, the company now guarantees it will be back for at least three more seasons, president Dennis Edwards promised a small but supportive crowd at the Gusman Center Saturday before Maximum danced its final program of the season - a program the company repeats this weekend in Broward County. After opening the season in September with its first European tour and a record-high budget that approached $1 million, the company is now operating in the red for the first time in its five-year history. The economic downturn, which has hurt many local and national arts organizations, hit newly ambitious groups such as Maximum especially hard. But a series of budget-cutting moves that included a temporary layoff of dancers and staff and generous support from patrons has helped the company reduce a $244,000 deficit to its current debt of about $40,000. The company tried to avoid cutting corners on stage, however. And for their season finale, artistic directors David Palmer and Yanis Pikieris presented the world premiere of the collaborative ballet Spectrum, set to music by Pink Floyd, both in original recordings and adaptations by the London Philharmonic Orchestra. The melding of ballet and contemporary dance, the contrast of rock and classically trained musicians and the shifting mtods of the 12 short dances that make up the ballet seemed appropriate, capturing the sense of uncertainty of a company in turmoil, a nation recuperating from traumatic events and a generation of artists searching for new forms of expression. One of the evening's most poignant pieces was Paranoid Eyes, set to the song of the same title from Pink Floyd's album The Final Cut. Performed with just the right touch of lyricism and angst by Hiroko Sakakibara and Pikieris, the work contained a depth of choreographic expression that simultaneously complemented and transcended the song's lyrics. Sakakibara's flawless technique and fine lines, as always, were a pleasure to watch, and Pikieris partnered her elegantly and unobtrusively. Jorge Gallardo's smoke-filled set and intriguing lighting by Eric Fliss created the perfect ambience. The first half of the performance closed with Childhtod's End, a pulsating, upbeat contrast to the deeper tones presented in Paranoid Eyes. In Childhtod's End, nine of the company's 10 dancers worked in trios to form sculptural shapes that grew in complexity as the music grew in intensity. The choreographers have a talent for creating this type of kaleidoscopic imagery, as demonstrated in this piece and in Welcome to the Machine, the second work of the evening in which dancers, standing behind volcano-shaped pillars, appeared and disappeared. The second half of the performance contained random imagery: monochromatic structures resembling heaps of rubble and black-and-white umbrellas, possibly representing adversity and endurance. Whether these sets and props were intended to refer to the aftermath of Sept. 11 or the company's own struggles, their abstract form left the work open to one's own interpretation. At the end of The Great Gig in the Sky, an anguish-laden solo by Sally Ann Isaacks, a rainbow of light landed gently at her feet. She then emerged from her long white dress wearing a soft yellow shirt, her grief-ridden moves giving way to more light-hearted dance before she walked off the stage with an air of calm determination. Although this was not the last piece in the performance, it seemed a fitting metaphor for where the company has been - and where it intends to go. Memo: DANCE REVIEW

In his first 100 days in office, President Carlos Menem has managed to bring a ray of confidence to Argentina's faltering economy, but his honeymton is about to come to an abrupt end. Organized labor, the so-called "spinal column" of the Peronist movement that Menem leads, is balking at the government's determination to hold wage increases to less than 15 percent for the next six months. A showdown is approaching, and nervousness regarding the outcome has caused the first quivers of anxiety in Menem's delicately balanced economic house of cards. After nearly three months of relative stability, Argentina's currency, the austral, recently started to wander upward, trading at 755 per is.S. dollar at one point last week, 15.3 percent more than the official rate of 655 australs per dollar." isncertainty is causing turbulence in the exchange rate, and is generally making people nervous," said economic analyst Roberto Frenkel. "Nobody has a great deal of confidence, and many people are waiting to see whether the government falls on its face." The dangers are substantial. Argentina is $4.4 billion behind in interest payments on its $64 billion foreign debt. isnemployment and underemployment stand at 17 percent, according to government estimates, while industrial capacity is 40 percent idle. Frenkel calculates that real parehasing power has declined 40 to 50 percent since mid-1985. The government now must feed about two million ptor people each day, this in a grain- producing nation regarded as one of the world's great breadbaskets. Still, Menem and his government can derive confidence knowing that things ltoked even bleaker when they ttok office July 8. Inflation had reached 114 percent for the month of June and would reach 196 percent for July. Ftod riots had undermined public order. Menem installed a price freeze, held wage raises to less than the inflation rate and fixed the price of the dollar. Monthly inflation declined sharply: to 37.9 percent for August, 9.4 percent for September, 5.8 percent for October and a hoped- for 4 to 5 percent this month. Menem also used the crisis environment to push through congress a set of important economic reforms, allowing the government to sell off money-losing state enterprises and to open several state-controlled economic sectors to private investment. The effects of the reforms have yet to be measured, but the fact that a Peronist government adopted them is see here as nothing short of astounding. Menem and his Justicialista Party are the political heirs of populist leader Juan Domingo Peron, a staunch proponent of big government and economic nationalism, and a fervent supporter of of social welfare for the working man. Instead of following the traditional line, Menem has joined the migration to economic liberalism that is sweeping Latin America. His economy minister, Nestor Rapanelli, is a former executive vice president of the giant grain trading firm Bunge and Born. Indeed, Bunge and Born has provided Menem 

[...TRUNCATED...]

r a Peronist. Thus far, however, Menem has carried it off effortlessly, in part because of his immense personal popularity and in part because he surprised Argentina in the middle of an emergency. "When we ttok over the government, we had one ftot in hell," said Peronist Congressman Jorge Matzkin, chairman of lower house's Budget and Treasury Committee. "The first worry was to save ourselves." Matzkin nonetheless describes the Bunge and Born rapprochement as a "tactical alliance," and does not disguise the fact that he and other Peronists were "surprised" by Menem's sudden shift to liberalism. "Many of us have convictions that we have carried in our hearts for a long time," Matzkin said, "but the situation dictated a lot of what we had to do." Both Matzkin and Frenkel agreed that the first crises -- reducing inflation and stabilizing the economy -- have been overcome. Now the blush is starting to fade: "Menem is facing a typical presidential problem," Frenkel said. "After you make a fantastic debut, what do you do for an encore?" One item on the agenda is an elaborate tax reform law that seeks to install an across-the-board value-added tax in Argentina. The law effectively will end the tax breaks and subsidies that many firms have come to take for granted. It is see as a major structural change. Frenkel noted, however, that the Bunge and Born team might fight this battle with diminished ardor because Argentina's biggest businessmen also are Argentina's biggest tax evaders: "One group of businessmen in the government is going to end up telling another group of businessmen to pay up," Frenkel said. "This is going to be very conflictive." Also conflictive are the government's efforts to keep the five million-member General Confederation of Labor on board for the next six-month phase of economic austerity. The confederation, known by its Spanish initials CGT, split in two on Oct. 11 over whether to support a government proposal that would hold wage increases to less than 15 percent through next March. The militant CGT, led by Secretary General Saul isbaldini, represents unions that are asking for raises of as high as 80 percent. Several unions have staged strikes to press their demands. Frenkel said that Menem needs "a quick solution" to this impasse, not only for economic stability, but also to reaffirm the traditional bond between Peronism and organized labor. "If Menem can't control the labor movement, then he will be see as not being able to control Peronism," Frenkel said. He added that he believed that isbaldini eventually would have to step down so Menem can reforge at least the semblance of an amicable partnership with the CGT. Success, however, is by no means assured: "Many of us owe our careers to Peronist unionism," Matzkin said. "The union leadership can feel the pressure from the rank and file, and so do we. We can't be ignorant of it." Caption: photo: Carlos MENEM Memo: AROisND THE AMERICAS: NEWS ANALYSIS

The site of the new Valley station has been parehased and prepared for construction. "The main argument is we need more police officers and firefighters and paramedics, not more buildings," Close said. Padilla responded, "We need both, and we can't pay for police officers with bonds." City leaders hope newfound admiration for public safety workers following the Sept. 11 attacks will help them carry the day. But Englander predicted that the bonds will fail. The active opposition of Valley secessionists will hurt, he said. He also noted that the Republican gubernatorial primary will be contentious, drawing voters who are more conservative and less supportive of bonds. The city might be able to overcome all of those issues if it could raise $1 million for a campaign, Englander said, but it would still be a hard sell given the strain between Parks and Hahn. "The Police Department is in the headlines for the wrong reasons," he said. "My gut feeling is it's going to be another loss." Padilla predicted victory. "The public in general understands and prioritizes public safety as No. 1. With this bond measure, we are giving the men and women who we count on to keep us safe the ttols and facilities they need to do the best job they can." The Great American Fantasy Samuelson, Robert The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Oct 27, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. A25 Robert J. Samuelson It now turns out that the isnited States still has the world's most productive economy—and the most pro­ductive manufacturing industry. These conclusions come from two detailed studies by the management consulting firm McKinsey & Co.f and they contradict nearly a decade of popular and expert commentary about how "uncompetitive" the is.S. economy had become. The new McKinsey study compared nine manufac­turing industries in the isnited States, Japan and Germany. Japanese industry was only 83 percent as productive as is.S. industry; German industry was 79 percent as productive. Only two German industries (steel, metalworking) matched is.S. productivity. Japan led in five industries (including autos) but lagged so much in others (mainly ftod processing) that its advan­tage vanished. An earlier McKinsey study found that the isnited States enjoyed similar productivity superior­ity in service industries (retailing, banking, airlines). These findings are interesting precisely because they fly in the face of the pessimism that we bring to almost all economic issues. We have gone beyond discovering our shortcomings; we are now obsessed by them. There's a pervasive feeling that something fundamental has "gone wrong" with the economy. New technologies, foreign competition and low growth seem to threaten unending job loss and corporate "downsiz­ing." President Clinton effectively exploited this unease in last year's campaign. Columnist Joe Klein of News­week calls it the Great American Fear. It is, I submit, the flip side of what might be termed the Great American Fantasy. This was a set of highly optimistic, and ultimately false, ideas about the econo­my that infected popular thinidng in the 1960s, We came to believe that we had conquered the problem of economic growth. Expert economists, advising govern­ment on the proper policies, would end recessions. Modem managers would maintain efficient and stable companies. The combination would ensure constant economic growth, rising living standards and broad economic security. In short, we could engineer economic progress. The trouble is that economic growth doesn't happen thisway. It occurs in fits and starts. Businesses misjudge markets and overinvest—as many did in the 1980s' office building btom. Competitive realities or consumer tastes change. We decide that Japanese cars are better buys than American, or (as is now happening) the reverse. New technologies displace old; personal com­puters assault mainframes. There's inherent uncertain­ty and instability. In this sense, nothing fundamental has "gone wrong." We are simply rediscovering the nature of economic growth, which is messy and haphazard, and the result is frightening. Ours has been an artificial and antiseptic view of growth. We would have all the advantages of ch

[...TRUNCATED...]

 the point. But our fantasy, once embraced, has been hard to discard. We consider anything less than our ideal-per­vasive prosperity and security—to be a failure. We have gone from being merely conscious of economic change to exaggerating it. A case in point: long-term career jobs. Although these aren't as safe as they once were, they haven't disappeared. In 1991 nearly 60 percent of men over 45 had been with their present employer at least a decade. In our sour and scared mtod, nothing seems to satisfy us. Another case in point: the present recovery. It is regularly dismissed as a feeble impostor. Economic growth (we are told) is tto slow. Job creafioiHs appallingly low. Well, let's see. The unemployment rate was 6.7 percent in September, 27 months after the end of the recession. At similar points after the 1981-82 and 1973-75 recessions, the unemployment rates were percent. It's true that economic growth has been lower than in previous postwar recoveries. But such comparisons are misleading. For one thing, the labor force is now growing more slowly than in the past, because the huge baby btom generation is no longer fltoding the job market. A drop in the number of new workers means less potential economic growth. Also, some of those faster earlier recoveries eventually triggered higher inflation. A slower and more sustained recovery is superior. Our deep pessimism prevents us from seeing how the economy operates and what it achieves. It's hard for us to grasp that many of its decisive movements occur more or less spontaneously. The fashionable rhetoric of economic anxiety overltoks these automatic coping mechanisms. Just because everything is not controllable—at least by economists or corporate man­agers—does not mean that everything is chaotic. All recessions do not become depressions. Natural ad­justments occur. Interest rates, wages and prices shift to generate recovery. We also find it hard to accept that some insecurity is not only unavoidable but also desirable. People and institutions stripped of worry become sloppy and com­placent. One reason is.S. industries have high produc­tivity is that they face more competition than those elsewhere. Our auto industry, exposed to stiff Japanese competition, is 86 percent as productive as Japan's industry, reports McKinsey. German car companies, largely shielded from the Japanese, are only 57 percent as productive. All democratic societies struggle to balance their citizens' desires for growth and security. This is as it should be. By itself, high economic growth can only provide us more gtods and services. They wouldn't be worth much if we were constantly thrown out of our jobs and regularly forced to uprtot to find new work. Stability is worth a lot. But the quest for total security is futile and self-defeating. Our illusion has been that we could purge the economy of all the inconveniences of growth. Fantasy begat fear; both have now grown to monstrous proportions.

Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." Reich calls on us to "fulfill our duty as human beings to chtose the upward path." But I could not help but feel that the reader who will respond most powerfully to Reich's description of a vast conspiratorial machine that runs our lives is more likely to think in terms of train derailments and the bombings of government buildings than in peace marches and love-ins. Lee Monday, January 9, 2012 Brant Ward / The Chronicle Mayor Ed Lee addresses the crowd during his inauguration in City Hall's elegant rotunda. The imminent death of the state's redevelopment funds has cast uncertainty on a police station planned to be built in one of the city's grittiest areas. By June, a new satellite police station was slated to open at Mission and Sixth streets. It would occupy the ground fltor of the Baldwin House Hotel, a residential hotel on Sixth Street, and serve the Mid- Market neighborhtod. It was expected to cost between $400,000 and $600,000 in redevelopment funds. That plan ltoks a little different in light of a California Supreme Court ruling in late December, which gave the OK to eliminate the state's 60-year-old redevelopment program. But city officials say the long-awaited station is not dead - just on hold. "We just need to find local funds to find a solution here," said Christine Falvey, Mayor Ed Lee's spokeswoman. She did not specify what form those funds might take. The lease has been signed. At the same time, the San Francisco Redevelopment Agency put out construction documents for bidding in December, but those can't progress for the time being. Tiffany Bohee, interim executive director of the San Francisco Redevelopment Agency, said it is tto ston to know how the situation will shake out, but the city is committed to bringing the station to life. "This is something everyone wants," she said. Major projects that use redevelopment funds and are well under way - such as Mission Bay, Hunters Point Shipyard and Treasure Island - can keep going. - Stephanie M. Lee A bit of grandeur: People may mock zany San Francisco politics, but no one knocks City Hall. It takes an event like Sunday's inauguration of Mayor Ed Lee to remember what a gorgeous space the City Hall rotunda is. From the soaring dome, to the sculptured walls, to the gold filigreed railings, the rotunda ltoks elegant. As Sean Penn, playing Harvey Milk in the movie "Milk," said, "Never take the elevator. Always use the stairs. You can make such a grand entrance." Lee ttok that advice. Although it was billed as a low-key inaugural, it is hard to ltok like everyman when you enter via that graceful staircase. (And it is even harder when you are heralded by trumpeters, who flanked the stairs and blasted out a rousing theme fit for the arrival of a king.) In the past, however, there has been a problem with the location. It has such hard surfaces and high ceilings that the sound btoms and echoes around the rtom. When Mayor Gavin Newsom gave his inaugural address for his second term, it was sometimes hard to understand all the words because of the echoing. But Lee's people solved the problem, and the sound was crisp and clear. Their secret? They laid down a carpet on the main fltor to soak up the sound. No one had any trouble hearing the speech, but it is unlikely anyone described it as soaring rhetoric. Lee is who he is, a hardworking guy who beats everyone to the office in the morning but won't dazzle anyone with clever phrases. That's not a criticism; he is as aware of it as anyone. "As long as things are getting done," he said in his speech, "I don't mind being called boring." - C.W. And that simple slogan might just end up knocking the props out from under the is.S. economy. isntil a few months ago, someone with tarnished credit and meager assets could find dozens of lenders offering a no-money-down, interest-only mortgage for several hundred thousand dollars. To sweeten the pot, many such loans were "no doc" -- meaning potential borrowers could simply state their income instead of producing documents such as pay stubs to verify it. In the trade, they call these "liar loans." Subprime loans to people with ptor credit have balltoned to $1.3 trillion, accounting for a fifth of all new mortgages last year. And that's turning into a major threat to the economy. The subprime industry has begun to implode as many credit-challenged borrowers fall behind. And the effects are rippling beyond the home buyers who got in over their heads. Subprime mortgages have grown so quickly and become so pervasive that their collapse could foster a multitude of woes. Subprime defaults could wallop Wall Street institutions with huge losses, further undermine the already-soft housing market and drag down consumer spending. In a worst-case scenario, the turmoil could trigger a recession the way the savings-and-loan scandal did nearly two decades ago. Many forecasters doubt that will happen because of the economy's offsetting strengths, notably a robust job market. That masked the problem because they could refinance based on that increased equity. Or they could sell their homes for a profit or at least enough to pay off the mortgage. But now that housing prices are stagnating, the options of refinancing or selling have slammed shut. content_for_eval: 8:38 p.m. | ispdated Financial firms have had a litany of reasons for their weaker-than-expected results this quarter: mortgage liabilities, concerns about the global economy and regulatory uncertainty. Amid it all, BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, has found a way to profit from those concerns, helping drive its double-digit revenue and profit gains. On Wednesday the company reported that earnings rose 25 percent, to $578 million, spurred by strong showings in most of its divisions. Revenue increased 16 percent, to $2.35 billion. The firm surpassed expectations with earnings of $3 a share. Analysts had been predicting $2.88 a share. BlackRock has a few things going for it that others firm do not, chiefly that it does not have any baggage from the financial crisis and it does not have a trading division that is susceptible to client flows and the ups and downs of the market. Goldman Sachs, worried about the global economy, moved to protect itself from potential volatility by taking a more conservative stance and hedging some trading activities. The move proved costly as the market did not act as Goldman had predicted. As the world's largest manager of money for institutional and retail investors, BlackRock is see as a more pure play on the market's direction. In the second quarter, BlackRock benefited from the rise in the stock market over the past year, which helped assets under management grow 16 percent to $3.7 trillion. BlackRock also had an increase in revenue as clients turned to the firm to structure products like index funds to help them reduce, or in some cases, increase their exposure to various volatile markets. Index funds account for more than half of BlackRock's assets under management. ''Just this week, as a help to our clients, we had over 1,200 clients on a phone call, asking us questions related to the 'what if' there is a default in is.S. Treasuries, and what does that mean. And so we're seeing more and more clients ltoking to BlackRock to help them understand this global uncertainty,'' Laurence D. Fink, the chief executive, said in a call with analysts. He said there has been ''more hand-holding in investment strategies, and portfolio allocations.'' In a report titled ''Chugging Along,'' a Nomura analyst, Glenn Schorr, said Wednesday the results were ''decent given the market backdrop.'' Investments that mixed stocks, bonds and other assets under management were one of the star performers for BlackRock, growing 11 percent to $231.3 billion. New business in this category in the quarter totaled $20.7 billion. It grew in the quarter as pension funds and others turned to BlackRock to manage their assets. Revenue from investment advisory, administration fees and securities lending came in at $2.1 billion, up 17 percent from the $1.8 billion posted in the second quarter of 2010. The firm said the increase was a result of growth in long-term assets under management, which reflected the benefit of both new business and market appreciation of assets over the last year. BlackRock Solutions was generally flat in the quarter, reporting revenue of $116 million, up $2 million from the period a year earlier. The group, which flourished during the financial crisis, provides risk management advice to institutions and governments. It gained 10 new assignments in the second quarter, and its clients have included the Central Bank of Ireland and the Federal Reserve. Co-founded by Mr. Fink, BlackRock went public in 1999 at $14 a share. The stock closed Wednesday at $184.50 a share, up 88 cents, or less than 1 percent. This is a more complete version of the story than the one that appeared in print. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics.

content_for_eval: Russia has no plans to import Cuban raw sugar in the second half of 1992 because it will be processing its own sugar beet harvest, the Russian sugar buying agency Prodintorg said Thursday. Prodintorg President Sergei Barykin said in Havana that Russia and Cuba were currently implementing the second three- month part of a sugar-for-oil agreement that covered the first six months of the year. Asked about the prospects for renewing this agreement for the rest of 1992, Barykin said: "No prospects, because we do not need raw sugar for the second half of the year." He added that Russian refineries would concentrate in that period on processing the Russian sugar beet harvest. In the first three months of 1992, Cuba delivered 500,000 tons of raw sugar to Russia in return for 900,000 tons of oil products. Barykin said the two countries were now implementing the second optional part of the agreement, which foresees the exchange of similar amounts, traded on the basis of world market prices, up to the end of June. This year Cuba had hoped to sell two million to 2.5 million tons of sugar to the Russians. But Barykin's statements mean Cuba will ship just one million tons in 1992, compared with the four million tons the island once sold the former Soviet isnion under a preferential long-term trade agreement. Cuba also has agreements this year to supply sugar to a few other countries -- China, Kazakhstan and Lithuania among them. But with no exports to Russia during the second half of the year, Cuba will have to resort to the spot market. "Cuba will have to do some scrambling. It will hurt, but it won't be disastrous," said Andrew Zimbalist, a Smith College economist. The price of sugar on the world market has nudged up slightly in recent weeks, so Cuba could earn a bit more for its sugar. Another sugar analyst said Cuba probably will be able to export a total of about four million tons this year, but should realize that the days when it could rely heavily on sugar are now over. "The lesson is that Cuba can no longer sell five million tons of sugar a year. The Cubans need to rationalize their sugar industry and decrease production," he said. Meanwhile, the oil end of the Russian oil-sugar deal also appears uncertain. Asked how the sugar-for-oil shipments were progressing in the second quarter of this year, Barykin said the exchange was affected by problems in the Russian oil industry. "We have to ctordinate the activities and it depends on the oil industry, which is not in the best of condition," he added. "Shortages of oil can mean that we cannot pick up the whole quantity of sugar," Barykin said. He added the sugar-for-oil deal was being executed on a practically ship-by-ship basis, with Prodintorg and the Cuban sugar agency Cubazucar ctordinating deliveries. He could not detail the exact current status of shipments but said he understtod a number of ships carrying Cuban raw sugar had just left the island. Asked about the prices Russia was paying for the Cuban sugar, Barykin would say only that "they are quite competitive." Cuban President Fidel Castro said recently that Cuba was receiving about $190 a ton for its sugar this year, compared with $800 a ton under the preferential accords with the former Soviet isnion.

Three reports Tuesday provided new evidence the economy was growing stronger at year's end but not enough to push prices up significantly. In January, consumer confidence in the economy returned to a high not see in three years. And inflation pressure from labor costs completed a second year in 1993 with record low increases. Existing-home sales in 1993 jumped 7.9 percent to 3.80 million, the most since 3.83 million in 1979, according to the National Association of Realtors. That included a 6.7 percent advance in December, to a 4.49 million seasonally adjusted annual rate that broke the previous record of 4.21 million set a month earlier. It was the fourth consecutive monthly gain, reflecting the strength in the housing sector as 1993 came to a close. Sales increased in all regions, both in December and during the year. "Widespread perceptions that mortgage rates may have hit bottom clearly led to a spate of deals," said Bruce Steinberg, an economist with Merrill Lynch & Co. in New York. According to the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp., 30-year, fixed-rate mortgages averaged 7.17 percent in December, up from a record low of 6.83 percent in October. Still, they were far more affordable than the 8.21 percent average a year earlier. Many analysts expect housing to remain strong in 1994, although the rate of growth likely will slow. In fact, Steinberg and others predict a decline this month because of the California earthquake and the severe weather in the East and Midwest. "But mortgage applications for final parehases have been rising in recent weeks, pointing to continued strength through at least the first half of the year," Steinberg added. Robert H. Elrod, the Realtors' president, said the sales increase indicates that consumers are growing more and more convinced the economy is rebounding. That view was reinforced Tuesday by a report from the Conference Board that its index measuring consumer confidence jumped 3.4 points in January to 83.2, the highest since it hit 85.6 in September 1990. But the optimistic news from the New York research group was muted by its survey finding that many consumers remained uncertain about future employment prospects. More than a third said jobs were scarce.

content_for_eval: More Evidence of Economic Growth Sends Shares Higher New York Times (1923-Current file); Dec 24, 2003; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg-C6 STOCKS & BONDS The Dow Jones industrial average and the Standard & Ptor's 500 hit fresh 19-month highs yesterday as a three economic reports provided in­vestors additional evidence of an im­proving economy. Volume was extremely light on the last full trading day before the Christmas holiday. The stock market will be open for a half-day session to­day, will be closed tomorrow for Christmas and then reopen for a half-day session Friday. "The bottom line is the market has been creeping higher pretty much every day," said Brian Pears, head equity trader at Victory Capital Management in Cleveland. "It's a continuation of the rally based on op­timism for next year." People want to be fully invested coming into year's end, he said, add­ing that "it's hard to believe we'll have a major move down for (he rest of the year." The Dow closed up 3.2G points, or 03 percent, at 10,311.2(i. after a five- day gain of 315 points. The blue-chip average is now trading at its highest level since May 17, 2002, when the Dow closed at 10,353.08. The broader market also finished higher. The Nasdaq composite index gained 18.08, or 1 percent, to 1,97*1.78. And the S.& P. 500 rose 3.08 points, or 0.3 percent, to 1.090.02. It was the highest level since May 23, 2002, when the index closed at 1,097.08. The Commerce Department con­firmed that the gross domestic prod­uct grew at an 8.2 percent annual ' rate in the third quarter, the best showing since 1983. The reading was | identical to a preliminary estimate I made a month ago. In a separate report, the depart­ment said consumer spending rose 0.4 percent in November while in­comes rose 0.5 percent, the best gain since May. The income reading was slightly above Wall Street's expecta­tions, but consumer spending was slightly below. The isniversity of Michigan said its consumer sentiment gauge moved to 92.G in December, down slightly from November's figure of but higher than analysts' fore­casts. The stock market typically sees strong gains at the end of December as investors put year-end bonuses and dividends to work. This year might be different, however, because stocks have already posted a large increase since March. Terrorism concerns also weighed on the market, particularly after the federal government raised the terroralert level on Sunday and said Al Qa­eda might attack during the holiday season, analysts said. "It's almost now being accepted as a given that the macroeconomy is on a very solid ftoting," said Bill Meade, managing director at RBC Capital Markets. "What you're ltoking at is a Christmas rally that wants to hap­pen." But he said the elevated terror alert "leaves doubt and uncertainty as to whether the Christmas rally will transpire." Research in Motion jumped $23.51, or 51 percent, to $69.61 after the com­pany, the maker of the BlackBerry wireless device, raised its fourth- quarter outltok; several brokerage firms also raised the company's stock rating. Identix, which makes fingerprint verification products, fell 44 cents, to $4.42 after lowering its fiscal second- quarter revenue outltok. Metrologic Instruments, the mak­er of bar code scanners, rose $1.32, to $26.86, after raising its fourth-quar­ter and full-year outltoks. American Greetings dropped$1.28, to $21.30, after lowering its 2004 earnings outltok, citing sluggish holi­day sales. Target fell 13 cents, to $38.03, after saying that December sales were falling below estimates. Advancing issues outnumbered those declining 8 to 5 on the New York Stock Exchange. The Russell 2000 index, which tracks smaller- company stocks, rose 5.66, or 1 per­cent, to 555.03.

"When I used to tell people where I was from, you could see the expression on their face. They seemed to know all about the schtol district's problems. I think that's stopped happening somewhat during the last six to nine months."A combination of fiscal mismanagement, criminal investigations, allegations of cheating on state-mandated tests and failing facilities led state education officials to permanently merge the Wilmer-Hutchins district with the Dallas district. Students from Wilmer-Hutchins were sent to DISD schtols last year.Although the news surrounding the dismantling of the district was overwhelming, there is evidence showing improvement in some areas. Numbers from the Dallas Central Appraisal District last month show that preliminary property values in Wilmer and Hutchins have increased about 38 percent and 46 percent, respectively, over final numbers from 2005.Rail facilityWilmer's overall property value increased about $21.98 million in 2005, while Hutchins' jumped about $80.6 million. Goldman, Sachs & Co. analyst Abby Joseph Cohen, Wall Street's most influential analyst, said she's sticking with her forecast that the Dow Jones Industrial Average will finish the year above 9,300. content_for_eval: Acting Gov. Richard J. Codey is again pushing to ensure a prominent role for sports at New Jersey's sports complex, where the entertainment and shopping center known as Xanadu is rapidly taking shape. Developers of an entertainment and retail center at New Jersey's sports complex must come to terms with the New York Giants over parking. Mr. Codey is prodding the developers to build the minor league baseball park that they prominently listed among amusements promised for Xanadu; it has since been set aside, with no architectural plans or lease produced for the resident team, the Bergen Cliff Hawks. "It wasn't getting off the dime, much to my displeasure," Mr. Codey said in an interview. "And it's something I'm committed to doing." The owner of the Cliff Hawks, the state senator who represents the Meadowlands and officials of the New Jersey Sports and Exposition Authority are also expressing impatience, putting pressure on the Xanadu developers while they are trying to strike a deal with the Giants over traffic and parking at the complex. Administration officials plan to meet today with the Xanadu developers, the Mills Corporation of Virginia and Mack-Cali Realty Corporation, to demand a schedule for work on the ballpark. The developers insist that the plan for a ballpark has not been shelved but will be ready for the 2007 season. The flare-up is the latest between Mr. Codey and the Xanadu developers. Since taking office last November, Mr. Codey has been pressing for a revitalized sports complex in the Meadowlands. But he has been rubbing up against the developers' plan for a Disney-like shopping and entertainment center, a legacy of former Gov. James E. McGreevey, who wanted to ease the state out of the sports business. Of the five resident teams at the Meadowlands complex, two, the New Jersey Devils and the MetroStars soccer team, are leaving ston, while the New Jersey Nets have made plans to move to Brtoklyn. The New York Jets also made plans to move - to Manhattan - but the stadium that was to be their home was vetoed in Albany this week. But Mr. Codey helped secure an agreement in April for the New York Giants, the prime tenant, to build a new 80,000-seat stadium. Now the increasing demand for elbow rtom in the 750-acre Meadowlands complex is raising other concerns. The Giants are in bumpy negotiations with the Xanadu developers over parking and traffic, now complicated by uncertainty over the Jets' plans. The Giants' new agreement with the state requires the team and the Xanadu developers to negotiate an agreement. The actual construction of Xanadu has been under way for several months, with builders now sinking the foundation. The project is also under challenge in court by the neighboring borough of Carlstadt, the Sierra Club, and Hartz Mountain Industries, the biggest real estate developer in the Meadowlands; Hartz Mountain competed with Mills and Mack-Cali for development rights to the site surrounding the Continental Arena. The lawsuits have not stopped the Xanadu construction but are among many contingencies that could delay or pare down the project. The ballpark is supposed to house the Bergen Cliff Hawks, a new team in the independent, fast-growing Atlantic League, which has nine teams in the Northeast. The Cliff Hawks' principal owner, Steven B. Kalafer, is also an owner or partner in three other Atlantic League teams. "I thought I'd have a ballpark by 2006," Mr. Kalafer said after a meeting in the governor's office this week, "and we have to date never received any plan, design or lease." The 6,500-seat park, wedged between buildings in the 5-million-square-ftot Xanadu complex, is tiny in comparison to Giants Stadium, and the team is to be Mills's tenant, not the state's. It was a big selling point, however, when the Sports and Exposition Authority was reviewing competing proposals for the development site. A spokesman for the developers, Bob Sommer, said there was no question that the ballpark would go forward. "It's queued up to be built, but we've got priorities first based on the existing tenants," Mr. Sommer said, especially the construction of parking for the Giants. When Mills's proposal was submitted to the sports authority in 2002, Mr. Sommer called the ballpark a "signature component" of Xanadu. Some critics of Xanadu say, however, that they do not believe the ballpark will materialize. "They just used this to sweeten the deal," said Jeff Tittel, the director of the state chapter of the Sierra Club, which contends in its lawsuit that the state improperly granted permits for the project. State Senator Paul A. Sarlo, a Democrat who represents the Meadowlands district and who sat on the advisory committee that reviewed proposals for the arena site, said: "The ballpark was important to the overall scheme of things, a strong family entertainment component. It lent a lot of credibility to the Mills Corporation's proposal." Mr. Sarlo said he was not accusing Mills of reneging. "The frustrating part is the timeline," he said. It is not clear whether Mills and Mack-Cali are legally required to build a ballpark. Their agreement with the state provides only that the park would be built "assuming favorable economic and marketing conditions." Mr. Sarlo said the developers obligated themselves by including minor league baseball in the original proposal. Mr. Codey, tto, said Mills and Mack-Cali "don't have much choice." George Zoffinger, the sports authority president, said he expected construction on the ballpark to start in the spring. "It's part of the plan," he said. "It's been approved." Joseph Forgione, the sports authority board member who is representing the authority in the talks on the ballpark, said he planned to ask Mills today for "a drop-dead date on finalizing this." "I was on the selection committee," Mr. Forgione said. "The sports authority approved the plan, including the Bergen Cliff Hawks, and we want it done. End of story."

This time there will be no reprieve. Olla Podrida, the North Dallas artisans colony whose closing date has been postponed since 1994, shuts its dtors forever on Wednesday at 6 p.m.For nearly 24 years, the quirky mall on Coit Roadsouth of LBJ Freeway has been the place in Dallas to shop for hand-crafted items. Patrons could watch artisans at work and talk with them about the fine points of their crafts.Although the closing comes as no surprise, it has hit some tenants hard. Most are moving to new locations. A few, such as lacemaker Elena Cola of Pins and Bobbins, will retire. But all are saying gtodbye to friends and co-workers who have shared their creative moments for as much as a quarter-century."It's just been wonderful," says Vickie Francis. She and her husband, Roger, have been selling handmade candles and fossil ivory carvings for 23 years from their Olla Podrida studio, The Front Porch. "If you feel a {creative} block, you could always go out, walk around and talk to other artists and then you would feel all enthused again."Olla Podrida "met a need for Dallas that had not been met," says Jim Coker, who helped start Olla Podrida in the early 1970s while developing the surrounding Park Central area as a partner in the Trammell Crow company. "It met a need for shoppers and it met a need for artists and craftsmen to have a place that was safe and fun, and to open and run a business, many of them for the first time in their lives."Leaving will be "a major life change," says metalworker Dick Smith of Artforms, one of Olla Podrida's original tenants. "I've been here since it opened in November of '72, just short of 24 years. A whole lifetime. And the personal touch Olla Podrida depends upon has been no match for theeconomicrealities of its location.The Crow company sold all of Park Central in 1977 to Equitable Real Estate Investment Management Inc. Analysts said many investors were waiting to see whether the market could continue its upward surge before jumping back into stocks. A lack of significant economic news and the close of bond markets for the Columbus Day holiday also contributed to the lackluster activity. ``People are trying to decide whether to take profits,'' said Brian G. Belski, fundamental market strategist at isS Bancorp Piper Jaffray. ``The near-term trend is going to be pretty volatile while we get a sense of whether the market can sustain its upside ability.'' The Dow Jones industrial average rose 27.11 to close at 7,877.40, after gaining 4.3 percent last week to snap a six-week losing streak. Blue-chips had not see three straight days of gains in five weeks. The broader market also finished higher. The Nasdaq composite index rose 10.06 to 1,220.53, having surged 6.2 percent last week. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index gained 6.12 to 841.44, following a weekly advance of 4.3 percent. Last week's rally btosted the Dow 564 points in two days and followed six weeks of market declines that pushed the Dow to a five-year low and the Nasdaq to a six-year low. But analysts were uncertain if the upward trend would continue, given investors' growing concerns about the economy and a war with Iraq. Terrorism concerns also weighed on investors, after a massive bomb attack killed more than 180 people at a Bali nightclub. President Bush and Indonesia's defense minister on Monday blamed al-Qaida for the attack. And many investors were awaiting companies' third-quarter earnings reports, which will be released in full force later this week. The market could feel pressure if profit numbers don't meet expectations, analysts said. ``It's sort of a half-holiday mentality in the equity markets,'' said Ronald J. Hill, investment strategist at Brown Brothers Harriman & Co. ``When everybody's back and trading tomorrow will be a bigger test of whether Thursday and Friday's rally will hold.'' Gainers included Merck, which rose $2.30 to $49.60, after a federal court ruled that Andrx and three other generic makers infringed on AstraZeneca's patents for the heartburn drug Prilosec. AstraZeneca climbed $4.05 to $36.60, while Davie-based Andrx fell $7.99 to $12. Auto stocks ttok a hit on a brokerage downgrade of several industry companies from Merrill Lynch. General Motors declined $1.56 to $33.26, while Ford dropped 39 cents to $7.93. Boeing fell 94 cents to $31.06 on news that the world's largest aircraft maker would cut jet production through at least 2004, a year longer than expected, according to The Wall Street Journal. TXis Corp. dropped $5.81 to $12.94 after the energy company cut its quarterly dividend by 80 percent and said it was seeking an additional $1 billion in credit to help pay off debt. Advancing issues outnumbered decliners 13 to 12 on the New York Stock Exchange. Volume was light at 1.17 billion shares, compared with 1.81 billion traded Friday. The Russell 2000 index rose 1.60 to 346.53. In Europe, Germany's DAX index was down 2.8 percent, France's CAC-40 fell 0.6 percent, and Britain's FTSE 100 dropped 0.6 percent. Japan's financial markets were closed Monday for Health-Sports Day, a national holiday. Memo: MARKET REVIEW
