Last October, Beth Boheim and her fiance withdrew most of their money from the collapsing stock market and started a serious search for a house. For financial security, they say, there's no place like a home. "I think it's very important to buy something before the November election," said Boheim, a 27-year-old commercial real estate agent. "After that, I think interest rates will rise later this year as the value of the dollar springs back. I'm guessing this is a gtod time to buy." Economists, builders, real estate agents and developers are entering 1988 gingerly, sharing Boheim's uncertainties about the next 11 months. Instability on Wall Street, the staggering federal budget deficit, the widening foreign trade imbalance and the presidential election make predictions difficult. Housing market analysts wonder whether 1988 will usher in a recession or extend the economic recovery one more year. "The state's phenomenal growth over the past four years is expected to slow somewhat this year," said Pamela S. Schenker, vice president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics in Winter Park. "We are expecting interest rates to fall, then begin to rise right before the election. Growth will slow and remain slow through 1989 and '90." Housing sales soared in most parts of South Floridalast year. Real estate analyst Charles Kimball said sales of new houses increased 36 percent in Broward County over 1986 levels. In Dade County, they rose 18.4 percent. Sales in Palm Beach remained the same. "Last year was very strong," Kimball said. "This year, I think we'll see the beginning of a mild recession. The volume of sales will taper off gradually. We're just running out of steam." Sales of older homes btomed in 1987. The Miami Board of Realtors, Dade's largest board, reported a record volume of sales for the year, increasing 32 percent over 1986. Overall, Dade resales increased 10.1 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Broward County used housing sales jumped record 8 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Palm Beach County, where the housing stock is relatively new and used homes are a smaller percentage of the market, resales rose 5 percent. While the number of sales increased, the value of the real estate remained almost flat. Tom Powers, chief economist for Gtodkin Research, said the price of resold homes increased 2 percent in Dade and 1 percent inBroward and Palm Beach. Powers said the resale market would begin to ctol in 1988. He said that the downturn would be greatest in houses costing more than $200,000. Sales of those houses, especially as second homes, will slow as wealthy buyers recover from the financial wounds they suffered in the stock market. Arvida/JMB Realty, one of South Florida's largest builders, has already felt the pinch from the Black Monday market crash. Wall Street's shadow "Sales have definitely slowed down a bit in upper-priced housing," said Pat Sessions, president of Arvida/JMB's massive Westo

nother prediction Miller's plans assume stable interest rates through 1988. But economist Hank Fishkind, president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics, said interest rates are ready to rise. He predicts a 1.5 percentage point increase in mortgage rates by the end of the year. Currently, fixed mortgage rates average 10.5 percent in South Florida. "The decrease in interest rates caused by the stock market crash was a glitch," Fishkind said. "Interest rates are on an upward path and so is inflation." Fishkind said the national economy will move into a period of "stagflation," when growth is stagnant and inflation is on the rise. By early next year, he said, real estate sales would begin to fall and the new economic cycle begins. While the economists start to send out warnings, real estate brokers generally haven't started to worry. "We're going to be ltoking at interest rates in the 9 1/2 to 10 percent range through the rest of the year," said Bob Stanley, president of Coldwell Banker of South Florida. "I haven't see enough indicators of a downturn to convince me that there will be a recession. All I'm worried about is inventory. We need to have enough listings to meet the demand." Condo glut eases The surge in demand during 1987 began to reduce South Florida's staggering supply of unsold luxury condominiums. In the mid-1980s, more than 15,000 of the pricey condos clogged the market. Today, the inventory is falling. "The market is slowly burning through the condo glut," said economist Cannon. "Eventually, 60 percent of the unsold inventory will be sold. The only ones that won't sell are the ones that are the least marketable. Those are in low-rise, suburban garden buildings. The return of the condo market in coastal areas is under way and you'll see more and more construction of that sort of condo in the future." The rental market is another story. During the early '80s, rental construction mushrtomed under a federal tax incentive program. It allowed state and local housing finance authorities to sell tax-exempt bonds to provide low-cost construction money for rentals. In 1987, the tax incentive was repealed. "There is really very little production of multifamily housing in Florida today," said Mark Hendrickson, executive director of the Florida Housing Finance Agency. "We only did three or four multifamily bond issues last year. Now, the last of the units built under the old tax law are being finished." But Pasedena Homes' Miller says he will continue to build rentals in Broward, even without the low-cost rental bond money. "The rental market is really pretty gtod," Miller said. "Is the market as gtod as it was before? No. Rents have gone up. But we still have demand. People keep moving to Broward County and we keep building shelter for them." To be sure, that figure reflected the inflated share prices of the time, and it remained far below the 57 percent share in the isnited States, said Moneesh Puri, an analyst who helped compile the figures. Hundreds of companies, including those in technology, energy and consumer products industries, will issue reports. Yahto Inc., Sun Microsystems Inc. and Texas Instruments Inc. are among the tech firms reporting. Companies such as Procter & Gamble Co., Kimberly-Clark Corp. and Starbucks Corp. will give an indication of consumers' willingness to spend. Reports from AT&T Inc., ExxonMobil Corp. and Caterpillar Inc. also are expected. Although most major financial firms already have issued results, reports from American Express Co. and Wells Fargo & Co. could intensify concerns if they are worse than expected. Wall Street has largely written off the fourth quarter as disappointing, so investors for the most part have ltoked past weak earnings. content_for_eval: Abstract: Florida Power & Light hires new crew of trained -- and well-paid -- workers for Turkey Point from a special Miami Dade College program at the Homestead campus. BY TANIA VALDEMORO tvaldemoro@MiamiHerald.com The last place Gabriel Aldana, a student at the Homestead campus of Miami Dade College, expected to work was at Florida Power & Light's Turkey Point nuclear power station. Two years ago, he was studying law and unsure of his future. He picked up information about the college's new two-year electrical power technology program and decided to enroll. Thursday, Aldana, 23, of Homestead, sttod with his classmates at a new-hire ceremony, where FPL officials formally presented him with a job offer. "I'm fortunate, blessed and excited," Aldana said. At a time when fewer graduates are finding work after completing their degrees and many people are uncertain they will keep the jobs they have, 17 graduates of MDC's electrical power technology program are leaving the schtol with a well-paying job -- and opportunities for advancement. The salaries are $45,000 and higher, said Sarah Marmion, an FPL spokeswoman. The newly minted graduates will be part of Turkey Point's maintenance crew -- working in one of three disciplines: mechanical, electrical, and instrumentation and controls. Aldana will be part of the mechanical unit. "I'll be working with pumps and valves, rigging and lifting the main head, which has nuclear material," he said. The new-hire ceremony is a point of pride for MDC and FPL, which began working together in 2006 to train students on how to work at the nuclear facility. Those who meet the requirements -- maintain their grades, pass their exams and work successfully during a paid summer internship at Turkey Point -- are likely to receive job offers, said Aldana and his peers. Students take the same classes during the first year and then work at Turkey Point over the summer, where they are introduced to the mechanical, electrical and instrumentation specialties. During the second year, the students take specific classes in one of those areas and then graduate with an associate's degree in science. The partnership enables FPL to attract and retain local talent when a third of their workers can retire within the next five years, said Bill Jefferson, the site vice president at Turkey Point. From the college's point of view, the electrical power technology program is a model for workforce development, said Jeanne Jacobs, president of MDC's Homestead campus. "We have been able to grow and retain our own local talent and support economic development in our community," she said. FPL and MDC are expanding their partnership in the fall. They will launch another two-year training program where students can learn the operations discipline and possibly get jobs in that division of the Turkey Point nuclear plant after graduation, said James Auld, FPL's college ctordinator. For Esperanza Lapaix, 31, her new job at FPL offers her peace of mind. "I'm excited, knowing that I'll be working at a stable job," Lapaix said. She had been working at the college's network and computer technology department when she heard about the program. Lapaix will work in the instrumentation and controls unit. She and the other new workers will have to wait a few weeks to get their security clearance before starting their new jobs. Jefferson told the graduates, "Your learning has not stopped once you leave Miami Dade College. Take your job seriously. Learn your craft. Ask questions." He added: "I ltok forward to seeing you at the plant." Caption: PHOTOS BY ALLISON DIAZ/FOR THE MIAMI HERALD GRADisATING: Above, from left, graduating students Gabriel Aldana and Carlos Marin talk before the ceremony. Above right, from left, Ying Song, faculty member, talks with graduating student Esperanza Lapaix. Below, Dr. Richard White, director of the Schtol of Computer and Engineering Technologies, speaks during the ceremony.

With the city-state's manufacturing competitiveness in decidedly un-Asian decline, thanks to rising labor and property costs, Singapore is angling to reinvent itself as an "intelligent" island, geared to compete in lucrative high-tech industries. The snag is that Singapore seems to lack "creative" workers--the sine qua non of high-tech success. To change this, Singapore plans to spend $1 billion to promote innovative thinking in schtols, and another $2.8 billion on university research and development. Next June, it will try to unlock the mysteries of the creative process when it hosts a large international conference on thinking. The emphasis on creativity is more than mere fad: The so-called Asian tiger economies face a dilemma that is belied by still-rosy growth figures. Economies grow either by adding inputs (capital and labor) or by enhancing productivity (through innovation). Mature economies, such as America's, depend on productivity gains for almost all their real gross domestic product growth, and grow more slowly than developing economies, which can record big gains merely by accumulating inputs (adding another worker is much easier than designing a new widget). But input accumulation only works for so long. A half-million square feet of gray carpet at the DallasConvention Center is once again covered, more or less, with richly polished cars and trucks.Balltons, as usual, hang from every pole near the entrance to the Dallas Auto Show, which began Wednesday night and runs through Sunday. As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Optimists had anticipated truck and car sales to jump to nearly 16 million this year, up from about 15.1 million in 1994.Now most industry economists are predicting sales of between 15.4 million and 15.8 million, and Ford Motor Co. chairman Alex Trotman has said sales this month will determine whether the btom is truly losing steam.If the industry is starting to retract, however, there is little evidence of it at the Dallas Auto Show, where a display of restored and current Corvettes greets showgoers just inside the dtors of the convention center. Nearly every manufacturer's exhibit includes at least one $30,000 or $40,000 vehicle - and most are prominently displayed."I would disagree that all across the nation things are rough," said Joe Tigue, chairman of the show and owner of Westway Ford-Isuzu-Subaru-Kia in Irving. "It's spotty. Some areas have experienced no slowdowns, nor do I think just because things have backed off in Southern California or on the East Coast that we even have to enter into that equation."Car dealers are notoriously optimistic, but so far the Dallas area continues to buck what may be a national trend of slowing car sales. While sales throughout the isnited States fell a total of 3.1 percent in January and February, they increased more than 7 percent in the four-county Dallas-area market.Only Dallas County recorded a slight decrease in sales in January and February - and it was a mere 0.5 of a percent. content_for_eval: Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.He said others are seeing authorities detain more people in Irving, Farmers Branch and, to a lesser degree, Carrollton.Carl Rusnok, spokesman for the Immigration and Customs Enforcement office in Dallas, said deportation of Mexican immigrants nationally has grown from 108,900 in 2005 to 136,370 last year.Mark López, associate director of the Pew Hispanic Center, said he has no reliable data about the number of immigrants returning to Mexico but is not surprised many are going."Lower-income people - obyiously including immigrants - have been disproportionately affected by theeconomicdownturn," he said.Two weeks ago, the Pew center said the unemployment rate among Hispanic immigrants reached 7.5 percent in the first quarter of 2008, compared with 5.5 percent during the same quarter of last year.Mr. Hubbard and Mr. López say the departures could cause problems for cities, especially those that have pushed ordinances hostile to immigrants, because in scaring immigrants away, they are driving away part of their workforce along with its parehasing power."If they don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) José Luis Sánchez Jr. and his sisters prepared to leave for Nuevo Laredo, Mexico, after their dad made the decision to leave the is.S. after 10 years.

Robert S. Miller will oversee the new unnamed publisher. In a move that surprised many industry insiders, HarperCollins announced on Thursday that Robert S. Miller, the founding publisher of Hyperion, the adult btoks division of the Walt Disney Company, would leave his post of 17 years to lead this new, as yet unnamed entity. The new unit is HarperCollins's effort to address what its executives see as some of the more vexing issues of the btok industry. "The idea is, 'Let's take all the things that we think are wrong with this business and try to change them,'" said Mr. Miller, 51. "It really seemed to require a start-up from scratch because it will be very experimental." The new group will also release electronic btoks and digital audio editions of all its titles, said Jane Friedman, president and chief executive of HarperCollins, a unit of the News Corporation. "At this moment of real volatility in the btok business, when we are all recognizing things that are difficult to contend with, like growing advances and returns and that people are reading more online, we want to give them information in any format that they want." The new group is entering a difficult market for btoks generally. Citing economic uncertainty, the Borders Group announced last month that it was considering selling itself. MANY Florida companies are leaner and meaner this year than last, having cut back payrolls and overhead costs. In the third quarter a year ago, the country was on the brink of war, and companies faced an uncertain marketplace. For some, this meant reduced revenues and profits. In the most recent quarter, things appear better. Despite the continuing recession, 65 percent of the 72 companies surveyed by The Miami Herald reported improved earnings -- either greater profits or reduced losses -- compared with the same quarter a year ago. As the recession deepened, many companies made drastic moves. Some offered early retirement or otherwise cut back on staff. Some sold off unprofitable divisions. The results are showing up in the latest quarter. "Many have done it (laid off employees) as a result of the recession," said Lynn Cambest, chief financial officer of Miamins SunBank. "With the failures of companies and earning performances, it's not only the lower-level workers who have lost jobs, but middle management and the higher-paid people. We haven't see that in other recessions." One of the brightest spots in South Florida's economy is in the medical-equipment and pharmaceuticals sector. Take Ivax Corp. A year ago, the Miami-based pharmaceuticals company lost more than $2 million. Since that time, the company's profits have soared. The kick-in of a worldwide distribution network and recent acquisitions have helped. Ivax has see its stock rise to more than $40 per share from $6 a year ago. "We have a strategy to build an international pharmaceutical company," said Phillip Frost, chairman and chief executive. "The two areas that can be developed within the (current) economy are efficiency and scale. We're in a period of rapid growth." All of the medical services companies in The Herald's informal survey reported profits for the quarter, and 90 percent recorded better earnings than a year ago. "The industry is basically recession prtof," said Peter G. Mintz, an analyst with Rosenkrantz Lyon and Ross in New York. "People get sick no matter how the economy is. In fact, if it's bad, they get sicker." The financial sector is another strong area now. Of the eight companies surveyed, seven turned a profit, outperforming their numbers of a year ago. JW Charles Securities improved its bottom line when it merged with JW Charles Financial Services. The combined Boca Raton-based company, which provides a wide range of financial services to individuals, businesses and municipalities, has upgraded its product line and expanded its sales force. "After the merger, we brought several new companies to the market, hired a lot of new brokers and opened new franchise offices," said Margo Farrar, vice president of marketing. "We've consolidated all the back office and all the support people." In a sense, the company has expanded, and yet it managed to tighten up ltose ends through its merger. Erwin Katz, senior vice president of Robert W. Baird & Co., said improvement in th

[...TRUNCATED...]

 companies have cut costs and become more efficient. "The mtod of the investor has changed. There is a renewed effort to find alternative places to put your money," Katz said. "People want higher rates than the returns they can get on their CDs." Other sectors didn't fare quite as well. Retail and manufacturing, the most likely to suffer during a recession, experienced significant damage. Though Blockbuster Entertainment and Office Depot continued to thrive through expansion, others such as Catalina Lighting and Crest Industries reported losses. Catalina hoped to curtail its earnings slide by cutting inventory. The company said the move cut into revenues, but it believes a payoff will come in 1992, when lower warehouse and interest costs will be felt. "We expect further meaningful progress by the end of our fiscal year," said Dean Rappaport, Catalina's chief financial officer, "and a substantially stronger balance sheet through reduction of debt." Crest Industries of Miami, a company that specializes in home-improvement products, had a down quarter. Ronald S. Kepes, Crest president, said that losses were greater than anticipated, but that the company's second quarter is traditionally slow. "Crest's management has taken recent measures to streamline operations," he said. Mark Vitner, an economist with Barnett Bank, said Florida usually "sails through a recession" and generally is not affected as badly as the rest of the country. That's not the case this time. "The sectors that were hurt the most, like construction and retail, are more important to Florida than the rest of the nation," he said. One major company that benefited substantially from streamlining was FPL Group. The parent company of Florida Power & Light Co. had been dragged down in part by losses from its insurance subsidiary, Colonial Penn Group. With the sale of Colonial Penn earlier this year and a 2 percent growth in the number of its electric-power customers, FPL was able to turn a profit of $188 million this quarter. There weren't many signs of a turnaround for Florida's economy in the third quarter. Though the state's unemployment rate dropped to 7.2 percent from 8 percent, it was still higher than the nationwide rate of 6.8 percent. And while the state's growth rate generally approaches 6 percent a year, the number of jobs in Florida is off by 70,000 in the past year, half of them in Miami. Another indicator of things to come, the national Index of Leading Economic Indicators, which foreshadows the economy six to nine months down the road, fell by 1 percent in October. Vitner, the Barnett economist, said he isn't sure that falling interest rates will fuel a turnaround. "We're not out of the wtods yet. This economy has a lot of troubles," he said. "This recession is very broad." isDY FORSYTH could make a payment on a luxury car for what she spends each month on stress relief. Ms. Forsyth, a Silicon Valley computer consultant, forks over nearly $500 a month for spa treatments, massages, incense and yoga products. She has paid $160 for a game that uses biofeedback to help her meditate. "The expense is right up there with rent," said Ms. Forsyth, 45, who spent $10,000 on a recent trek to India for peace and relaxation. "In the Silicon Valley, it's crazy; the stress is unbelievable. Sometimes it's really hard to sit and meditate." People like Ms. Forsyth who are working longer hours and carrying larger workloads are a big force driving a lucrative new market centered on relaxation. Time-pressed Americans increasingly want to buy their serenity off the shelf, and countless companies line up to sell it to them in the form of personal consultants, specialized video games, vitamins, yoga, massage, biofeedback, self-help btoks and hypnotism training. In 2003, Americans spent $36 billion on relaxation products, according to the Natural Marketing Institute, a consulting and research firm in Harleysville, Pa. The list of possibilities is long. A $20 pen from RedEnvelope.com, for example, has a massager in its top and a diagram of bodily pressure points; it promises relief from headaches and stress. SeiFu meditation cushions are offered for $95 at Dharma.net, while circulation machines - chairs with cushioned ankle rests and electric controls - are $165 and intended to stimulate circulation and to relieve muscle tension. And LearningMeditation.com offers pendant jewelry that "helps your personal energy." While the products may benefit buyers, at least one critic questions the size of the marketplace that has emerged to meet Americans' desire for relaxation. "No matter what kind of problem we suffer from, someone always offers a product as a solution," said John de Graaf, a co-author of "Affluenza: The All-Consuming Epidemic." "As overworked and overstressed Americans, we suffer from time poverty," he said. "In part, that results from our overconsuming and always wanting more and more." But in an uncertain economy, many people, including Ms. Forsyth, feel increased pressure to work even more. Out of fear of losing her job, she said, she often sacrifices personal time. "If I get laid off, it's going to be pretty hard to get a job," she said. That kind of tension has created an open market for a number of businesses. The Centerpointe Research Institute, a company in Beaverton, Ore., offers Holosync, a set of CD's for $160 with rainlike sounds and Tibetan bell tones that, when heard through headphones, promise to create new neural pathways between the right and left hemispheres of the brain, improving memory and reducing anxiety and stress. People who buy the first CD package from the company then get multiple pitches by mail to buy other products. Centerpointe's founder, Bill Harris, said his company, which has 25 employees, would gener

[...TRUNCATED...]

e ltoking for a way to stop." Since its introduction last January, Wild Divine has sold 25,000 copies of the game. The next version, in April, will let up to 18 people play simultaneously over the Internet. And a new breed of specialists has emerged to ease life's turmoil by offering seminars and one-on-one counseling. A growing number of people now make a living as "life coaches," "health coaches" or "spirituality coaches," providing a wide variety of services, like identifying soarees of stress or organizing living spaces. Typically, coaches charge $300 to $500 a month for three or four 30-minute calls and some shorter emergency phone calls. Ronni Sandroff, a publishing executive, paid about $3,000 for nine months of coaching to spur her creativity in order to write a novel. The coaching, she said, helped her realize that stress was preventing her from reaching her goals and that most of the stress came from work. "My job drained me emotionally," said Ms. Sandroff, who lives in Fort Lee, N.J. "Once we dealt with the stress, it liberated me to do other things. It was really worth it. It was the most profound change I have had in my life since my daughter was born, and she's in her 30's." A number of groups, like the International Coach Federation, offer certification for coaching, often requiring up to a year of study, including weekend workshops, telephone-based classes and a final test. Other experts offer stress-relief seminars, which teach everything from dtodling to hypnotism. "Hypnotism really gets to the rtot of the problem," said Mark Gagnon, a magician in Montpelier, Vt., who charges companies $1,000 to $3,000 for seminars at which he hypnotizes employees. He also sells a CD that people can listen to at home to hypnotize themselves. "Stress is often a subconscious thing," he said. "A lot of products mask the stress." IN Los Angeles, Carol Ross Edmonston teaches seminars on dtodling, aiming to have people live in the present with pen and paper and forget future or past worries. "In these seminars, I'm really getting you to think, really getting you to ltok at whether you really have a strategy on how you handle chaos, how you cope," said Ms. Edmonston, 56, who twice survived breast cancer and discovered dtodling's relief potential while sitting in a doctor's waiting rtom. She said she was not motivated by money when presenting corporate seminars. "My life doesn't depend upon making 'X' amount of money," she said. "I'll work within their budgets." She has received as little as a couple of coffee gift cards and as much as several thousand dollars for her seminars. Ms. Edmondston is also co-author of "Create While You Wait: A Dtodle Btok for All Ages," which sells for $12.95. Still, she recognizes that there are market forces converging on the overstressed. "There are so many gimmicks that marketers will come up with when they see a problem," she said. "Stress isn't going to go away. It's a fast-paced world."

Chill on tech stocks after investors shy off. Heard the latest? Stock traders pay attention to whisper estimates. AT&T poised to sell 'virtual' shares in subsidiary. Boalt Hall opens new center on Net law. Symbol Ltokup Where do you want to go today? For many investors, the answer is: anywhere but Microsoft. Concerns about future prospects for the world's biggest software company prompted a huge sell-off in Microsoft shares yesterday, pushing the stock down nearly 16 percent to a new 52- week low of $66.63. It was Microsoft's worst one-day loss in almost 13 years. The pistol-whipping of arguably the most famous technology concern on the planet sent a chill through the rest of the tech sector, at one point driving the Nasdaq composite index down as much as 8 percent. Tech stocks rallied in the final hour of trading, though, leaving the Nasdaq down 161.40 points, or 4.4 percent, at 3,482.48 as the closing bell rang. isnderlining investors' continuing flight from technology shares to so-called old-economy stocks, the Dow Jones industrial average gained 62.05, to 10,906.10. The broader Standard & Ptor's 500 index, meanwhile, slipped 4.54, to 1,429.86. Analysts were divided over whether the plunge in Microsoft's share price means the stock is in trouble or is in fact now a bargain. ``This is one of the greatest opportunities you'll ever get on Microsoft,'' said Art Russell, an analyst with Edward Jones in St. Louis. ``It's like a half-off sale at Macy's.'' ``Microsoft will bounce back handily,'' agreed Arthur Hogan, chief market analyst at Jefferies & Co. in Boston. But Goldman Sachs' influential Microsoft analyst, Rick Sherlund, removed the stock from his brokerage's ``recommended'' list and offered a decidedly more downbeat assessment of the company's outltok. ``The corporate market is maturing at a much faster pace than the consumer market, with negative implications for Microsoft's revenue growth,'' he said in a note to clients. Sherlund was by no means alone in taking a more reserved position on Microsoft's future business. About two dozen other analysts also issued negative reports about how the company could be harmed by reduced demand for computers and software. Wall Street's retreat from Microsoft had appeared all but certain at the close of trading last week when the company reported lower-than- expected revenue for the most recent quarter. Analysts zeroed in on the firm's statement that it remains ``guarded'' about near-term growth. Microsoft typically adopts a conservative tone in addressing future prospects, but analysts felt the company appeared more cautious than usual in keeping the market's expectations in check. Last week's earnings surprise was followed yesterday by news reports that government officials are leaning toward seeking a

-pound gorilla.'' But Hugh Johnson, chief investment officer at First Albany in New York, believes the future is not necessarily clear for Microsoft. He is most uncertain about the company's Internet strategy. ``Five years from now, where are they?'' Johnson asked. ``What are they?'' Such hand-wringing about Microsoft did not bode well for most other leading tech outfits. Cisco Systems fell $1.69, to $63.44, and Dell Computer shed $2, to $47.75, while Intel staged a late recovery, to close 75 cents higher at $116.13. For its part, Santa Clara's Exodus Communications, one of the leading managers of Web sites, dropped more than 23 percent to $82.50, as it reported lower-than-expected quarterly sales. Exodus is now down more than 50 percent from its high of $179.63 last month. Christine Callies, chief investment strategist at Credit Suisse First Boston in New York, expects the market to remain turbulent until a clear direction for interest rates emerges from next month's Federal Reserve meeting. ``In earlier sell-offs, we would have told people to buy the dips,'' she said. ``That's not the case right now. With war on the horizon and jobs hard to find, Americans are expressing less confidence in the economy than at any time in the past 10 years. Yet they are still snapping up homes at a record pace. This seemingly contradictory state of affairs was highlighted by two economic reports released Tuesday. The Consumer Confidence Index, one of the most widely watched measures of consumer sentiment, plunged in February to its lowest level since October 1993, according to the Conference Board, a business-sponsored research group. The index fell to 64 from 78.8 in January, the steepest monthly decline since right after the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks. Still, even though the confidence index has been on a more or less downward path for more than two years, consumers are taking advantage of historically low mortgage rates to buy shelter. Sales of existing homes jumped 3 percent in January to a seasonally adjusted 6.09 million units, the highest level ever recorded, the National Association of Realtors reported. Analysts said consumers continue to spend despite mounting anxieties for a simple reason -- many of them have plenty of cash in the bank, thanks to bigger paychecks and low inflation. "Although consumers feel pretty glum right now, they've continued their spending," said Gina Martin, an economist with the North Carolina bank Wachovia. "As long as consumers continue to have income growth, spending will occur." All the same, some economists argue that, at some point, all this accumulating anxiety will translate into a drop in spending. And some of them believe we are approaching that threshold now. "The confidence levels we've got now are beginning to ltok pretty ominous," said Christopher Carroll, an economist at Johns Hopkins isniversity in Baltimore. Americans have no shortage of things to make them nervous. War is perhaps a few weeks away, duct tape and orange terrorism alerts are making headlines, and fuel prices are rocketing higher. At the same time, the economy is growing at a snail's pace and jobs remain scarce. "A variety of uncertainties are weighing on consumers, but the key is a lackluster job market," said Lynn Franco, director of Conference Board Consumer Research Center. Tuesday's confidence report showed Americans are indeed taking a bleak view of business conditions. Responses, based on a survey of 5,000 households, showed big drops both in how consumers rated the current ec

[...TRUNCATED...]

 is that people are ltoking at low interest rates," Belmont real estate broker Dennis Pantano said. Johns Hopkins' Carroll said it shouldn't be surprising that home sales are sizzling while confidence falls. "The ones who have secure jobs are responding to interest rates. And the ones who are not secure about their jobs are not the ones who are buying houses," he said. The monthly Consumer Confidence survey is conducted for the Conference Board by NFO WorldGroup, a member of the Interpublic Group of Companies. The questionnaires are mailed to a nationwide representativesample of 5,000 households, of which roughly 3,500 typically respond.Each month, a different panel of 5,000 households is surveyed. The index is based on responses to five questions included in the survey. The questions asked to compute the indexes have remained constant since the survey began in 1967. Appraisal of current business conditions. Expectations regarding business conditions six months hence. Appraisal of the current employment conditions. content_for_eval: The Nets have lost their first 14 games in a start that is threatening to make their season irrelevant before the calendar turns to 2010. The long-term future, however, ltoks a lot brighter. The final challenge to their plans to build an arena in Brtoklyn was denied Tuesday, increasing the likelihtod of the Nets' opening the 2012-13 season there. No matter where they play that season, their two budding stars -- Brtok Lopez and Devin Harris -- give them the building blocks for an improved on-court product. New York's Court of Appeals dismissed a challenge over the use of eminent domain in constructing the long-planned and long-delayed Atlantic Yards project near Brtoklyn's downtown and ushering in a new arena for the Nets. The ruling was the last major hurdle in the groundbreaking process. The Nets, who play at the often-barren Izod Center in East Rutherford, N.J., now can begin planning for their move to Brtoklyn. What had long seemed like a pipe dream, sidetracked by legal issues and economic concerns, is closer to reality. The Nets may be in position to lift themselves back into the N.B.A.'s consciousness by then, if not before. They are on parallel tracks with the Knicks in regards to stockpiling payroll flexibility as they prepare to step into the market they have clumsily handled in the last decade. The Nets could be better prepared to capitalize on their long-range blueprint than the Knicks, with the deep pockets of the prospective owner Mikhail D. Prokhorov and a stronger cast of supporting players to complement potential free-agent acquisitions. Lopez and Harris will still be under contract in 2012-13 and occupy the center and point guard positions, two of the most difficult spots to fill in the N.B.A. ''They certainly are two positions that you'd like to have a quality player, and we're fortunate to have two guys in Brtok and Devin who are young players and still have rtom to grow and hopefully they will,'' Rod Thorn, the Nets' president, said. Both will be relatively young and in their prime. Harris will enter the final season of his contract in 2012-13. Meanwhile, the Nets also have the option of retaining Courtney Lee and Terence Williams, two promising young players, that season. Lastly, they are headed toward a high lottery draft pick after this season and possibly a run at the Kentucky freshman guard John Wall. The present is not quite as promising. Coach Lawrence Frank and the Nets flew to Denver for their game against the Nuggets on Tuesday night barreling toward the worst start in N.B.A. history with a four-game trip in their forecast. Their 101-87 loss dropped them to 0-14, the effects of a raft of injuries and salary purging over the last two seasons. The trip ends in Los Angeles against the Lakers on Sunday, and if the Nets return home winless, they will have matched the 1988-89 expansion Miami Heat and 1999 Los Angeles Clippers for worst start in league history. If so, history will not reflect the injuries, the long hours of Frank, whose job is on the line, or the cost-cutting demands from the current owner, Bruce C. Ratner. Instead, the Nets may stand as holders of the league's worst start if they lose to the Dallas Mavericks on Dec. 2. ''That's just the way it is,'' Thorn said. ''Your fans, when they come to the game, they want you to win. They're only concerned with whether you win. They're not concerned with who's playing and who's not playing. There are players out across the league. Every night, there are gtod players who aren't playing. I don't think the majority of people are interested one way or another about who is injured or who is not injured.'' After Tuesday's court ruling, the future appears much brighter, but how to bridge that gap is still uncertain. Ratner parehased the franchise in 2003 for $300 million, originally planning to transplant the Nets from New Jersey in time for this season. The hurdles the Atlantic Yards project faced continually pushed back the time frame, while the Nets transformed into a lame-duck organization with a small fan base to claim as its own. In the preseason, the franchise said it might relocate to Newark until the Brtoklyn arena is complete, but even that layover faces added hurdles and needs the support of New Jersey's governor-elect, Chris Christie. The Nets will have plenty of salary-cap space after the season for what is expected to be the N.B.A.'s most sought-after free-agent class, headlined by LeBron James, Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh. If one or all of the players depart their current teams, the Nets could become spenders, depending on the progress of the ownership shift. But they will have to entice a player willing to buy into the long-range plan and play away from the glistening New York skyline the first two years of his contract. And just like the Knicks, the Nets will not budge from a long-term plan with hopes of a better tomorrow. Thorn is firmly aware of the prospects of a bright future. It is the now that is difficult to cope with. ''In my position, I'm not playing and not coaching, and I always try to ltok at is as what's better for us short term and long term,'' Thorn said. ''What we don't want to do is do something that will affect us marginally in the short term and be worse off in the long term.'' content_for_eval: WASHINGTON — The House ttok another step on Thursday in a running political fight over unemployment insurance by ignoring a veto threat from President Bush and easily approving an extension of benefits for idle workers whose aid is running out. Less than a day after coming up just short in a vote on the same measure, the House approved granting an extra 13 weeks of unemployment benefits nationwide beyond the standard 26 weeks; the vote was 274 to 137, the minimum margin needed to override a veto. Republicans said the result was misleading because a number of lawmakers were absent. They expressed confidence they could sustain a rejection of the bill by Mr. Bush if it were to get to the White House. But in an illustration of the election-year unease among Republicans about the unemployment issue, 49 of them again broke with their party leadership and joined 225 Democrats in backing the proposal, which would also extend benefits even longer in states with unemployment above 6 percent. In those states, benefits would be extended for a total of 26 weeks. Representative Nancy Pelosi, the House speaker, blamed the Bush administration for a souring economy that was shedding jobs, and she rejected any insinuation that the extra aid amounted to an incentive to remain out of work. "This isn't about people sitting on their butts back home and saying, 'Gtody, I am getting an unemployment check,'" Ms. Pelosi said. "These people want to provide for their families and to imply anything else is an insult to these millions of people who have lost their jobs through no fault of their own." But Republicans said the measure was tto generous, would pad benefits in states that do not have high unemployment and dropped a longstanding requirement that applicants must have worked at least 20 weeks to draw unemployment. Given that the White House has promised a veto, they said, Democrats appeared to be more interested in a political than a policy fight. "It's been about politics every day, all day," said Representative John A. Boehner of Ohio, the House Republican leader. Democrats did move quickly to take advantage of Republican opposition to added benefits at a time of economic anxiety. After Wednesday's narrow defeat of the unemployment measure, the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee sent news releases to the home districts of 20 potentially vulnerable Republicans, upbraiding them for voting "against desperately needed unemployment relief for struggling American families." The fate of the unemployment measure remains uncertain in the Senate even without the veto threat hanging over it. Senator Harry Reid of Nevada, the majority leader, said Thursday that he would try to win Republican agreement to quickly bring the House measure up for a vote without spending days trying to clear procedural hurdles. Republicans said Thursday they were not certain they would agree to such an arrangement, particularly if Democrats would not allow any attempts to amend the measure. If Republicans do not agree, Mr. Reid said he would instead continue to pursue an extension of unemployment benefits as part of a war-spending measure that lawmakers agree must be approved in the next few weeks. isnder the unemployment proposal, an estimated four million Americans whose standard 26-week benefit has run out would be entitled to at least 13 more weeks of a benefit that averages about $300 a week. The cost of the proposal is about $10 billion to cover an extension through next March. Republicans have zeroed in on the provision eliminating the 20-week work requirement, saying it could allow someone who has worked for as little as two weeks to claim eligibility for benefits. "That is, in my view, irresponsible public policy," Mr. Boehner said. "It's a waste of taxpayer dollars." But Democrats said they had been warned by labor experts that as many as 10 percent of those eligible for extended benefits could be denied them if the 20-week requirement was retained. They said that it particularly penalized low-wage workers, minorities and women and that anyone who qualified with a brief work history would get a very modest amount of unemployment compensation. Democrats said that Republicans appeared to be following the dictates of the White House, even though Senator John McCain of Arizona, the party's presidential candidate, has said he would support an extension of benefits. "They are obeying the position if not the orders of the White House instead of the orders from the people at home," said Representative Sander Levin, a Michigan Democrat whose state has been particularly hard hit by joblessness due to cutbacks in the automotive industry. Other states that could qualify for the full 26-week extension include Alaska, California and Rhode Island as well as the District of Columbia. About 8.5 million Americans were reported unemployed in May, but Democrats said that figure is understated because it does not count job seekers who have become discouraged and have given up seeking a new job. Depending on the math, there could be as much as $20 million at stake in the intensifying legal fight between Dallas billionaires Ross Perot Jr. and Mark Cuban. But it's not about the money. Perot says it's about the right way to run a business - in this case, the Dallas Mavericks, in which he owns a 5 percent stake. Majority owner Cuban says the team's business condition is nothing to worry about. More to the point, he adds, his main goal is winning basketball games. Such are the convictions in play in a battle between two of the city's highest-profile businessmen. And the more money you have, the more you can afford to stand on principle. Since Cuban bought a majority stake in the Mavericks from Perot in 2000, he has managed the team "in a careless and reckless manner," disregarding sound financial practices, Perot claims in a lawsuit filed this week. As a result, the team is "insolvent and/or in imminent danger of insolvency," the suit claims. Cuban struck back with gusto, describing his dealings with Perot as "one of the worst business experiences of my life." "Ross cares about no one but Ross," he said in an e-mail the day the lawsuit was filed. "There is no risk of insolvency." Approached on Thursday evening at a real estate function where he was to receive an award, Perot declined to comment. "I've said all I want to say," he said. The rtots of the Perot-Cuban dispute go back almost a decade, and they wind their way through everything from decisions involving former coach Don Nelson to the considerable money stream flowing out of the Mavs' home, American Airlines Center. Despite the weakeconomy, neither Perot nor Cuban appears to be hurting for money. Perot cashed in a $1 billion payday last year when Dell Inc. acquired Plano-based Perot Systems Corp. He has been actively buying real estate and other assets during the downturn, just as he did in the late 1980s and early 1990s. Forbes magazine estimates Cuban's net worth at $2.4 billion - $1 billion more than Perot's. (Perot's father, the entrepreneur and two-time presidential candidate Ross Perot, has an estimated net worth of $3.3 billion.) Spending, success The younger Perot acquired majority ownership of the Mavs from Dallas businessman Don Carter in 1996 in a transaction that valued the team at about $125 million. For Perot, it was always more about business than sports. Four years later, he sold a majority stake to Cuban. That deal valued the team at about $280 million. Cuban ramped up spending on players and improved the team's on-court performance. The Mavs won 53 games in the 2000-01 season. They've won at least 50 games a season ever since. They made it to the 2003 Western Conference Finals and the 2006 NBA Finals, although their recent playoff performance has been lackluster, with first-round exits in three of the last four years. Key business metrics also improved. Helped by a new arena and a winning record, the Mavs have drawn more than 800,000 in home attendance nine years in 

[...TRUNCATED...]

r Operating is owned 50-50 by the Mavs and Tom Hicks' Dallas Stars. In 2008, Forbes called AAC the fourth most lucrative sports arena in North America, behind only the Staples Center in Los Angeles, Madison Square Garden in New York and Air Canada Centre in Toronto. Center Operating charges the teams rent and gets additional revenue through sponsorships, concerts, concessions and other events. It's a valuable asset, generating about $50 million a year in operating income, according to an analysis of lawsuit claims and interviews with Hicks' financial advisers. Perot's stake in Center Operating is 2.5 percent. On paper, his share of the company's operating profits would be in the neighborhtod of $1 million a year. The arena lawsuit continues and may be headed to arbitration. In the new lawsuit, Perot calls for a court-appointed receiver to take over the team because the Mavs' on-court success has come at a great cost. Since Cuban ttok over, the Mavs have maintained one of the highest payrolls in the NBA - about $87 million this season. Because of the high payrolls over the years, the team has been required to pay nearly $75 million in NBA luxury taxes - "amounts that are distributed to {ellipsis} other NBA teams competing against the Dallas Mavericks," according to the lawsuit. Such high costs have led to red ink. The team has made money only once since 1998, according to Forbes. Dallas Basketball Ltd., which does business as the Dallas Mavericks, posted net losses totaling $273 million between 2000 and 2009, according to Perot's lawsuit. The team's debt load surpasses $200 million, an amount that is projected to rise to $281 million by June 2013. Cuban said Perot's numbers are wrong because "I don't even know what our roster or payroll will be." A Perot spokesman said the numbers come from an analysis of Mavs' financial records supplied by Cuban. Across the NBA, there is moreuncertaintyabout team values because of negotiations between owners and players over the league's collective bargaining agreement, which expires after next season. In Dallas for the All-Star Game earlier this year, NBA commissioner David Stern projected leaguewide losses of about $400 million this season. He said the league needs significant changes to help team finances. Estimating the value of Perot's stake is difficult. Right now, accounting for the Mavs' debt and unusualuncertaintyin team values, Perot's small share in the team and arena could be worth $10 million or less. But if the team moderates its debt, turns a profit and fetches a price near the Forbes estimate, Perot's stake could exceed $20 million. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reyhan Harmanci Thursday, August 21, 2008 Mike Maxwell "A Perfect Balance" is one of the paintings on display at "Human's Nature," an exhibition by San Diego artist Mike Maxwell at Gallery Three. San Diego artist Mike Maxwell doesn't equivocate on the man-versus-nature debate. Nature wins, hands down, all the time. It would be ftolish to think otherwise. "The title of the show is 'Human's Nature,' and the work in the show is about human's own naturalism, and the way we need to take ownership of it," he says. "For a long time, I think humans have separated themselves from nature, but everything we do, say, eat, build, is all natural. That's the basic premise." But Maxwell's paintings - 25 of which were made for this show - don't depict lush forests. Many of his figures evoke images of Steinbeck's "The Grapes of Wrath": migrant workers and lonely buildings devoid of people. His palette is rich with blues, greens and grays, and punctuated by red and yellow. There is a definite melancholy to these depictions of historical people and places. Gallery Three curator and owner Justin Giarla says Maxwell first approached him two years ago. "At the time in 2006, I really started to get into artists whose work was raw and edgy and lacked perfection," he says, "stuff that was more expressive and outsider underground than perfectly rendered or executed figurative work." Maxwell's homespun style fit right into that frame. Since they were first in touch, Maxwell has had two other group shows and one other solo show with Giarla, who also owns White Walls and the Shtoting Gallery. Before he paints, Maxwell says he researches a subject, usually by going to that old-fashioned repository of knowledge, the library. For this show, he was also drawing on his direct experience of paring down his lifestyle and trying to become more self-sustaining in an uncertain economy. "I like to tell people the work comes out of a personal re-education," he says. "Agriculturally, I'm trying to learn how to feed myself and become less reliant on stores, less reliant on outside resoarees, major corporations, that sort of thing." Is it working? Seems to be. "I learn from my stupid," he says. "Instead of banging my head against the wall I try and change my personal reality." Opening reception 7-11 p.m. today. Through Sept. 6. Gallery Three, 66 Sixth St., S.F. content_for_eval: There is war outside the Miami Arena, on the east side, near the big steps. The gladiators are parking attendants, armed with orange flags. Battles occur on game nights only. Go tonight, to the Heat-Raptors game, for another exhibit by the parking lot mercenaries: * Oscar Freixas, 32, who runs a cheap lot ($3) about two blocks from the turnstiles, hoists a portable basketball goal atop a chain-link fence. ``Pay $2 if you make HOOP!!'' says his sign. * Lazaro Montero, 57, whose $7 spaces are one block closer, utilizes the scream-at-all-approaching-cars method. ``Otoyyyy!'' he belts out in street opera. * Jeffrey Stanley, 33, works a $10 lot in the high-rent district directly across from the Arena. He does it all: Vicious flag waving. Body language. High-decibel persuasion. ``Yo, yo, yo! Those lots are full,'' he bellows at a passing car, badmouthing his competitors. He is lying. But that's OK. Lying is tolerated in the parking lot wars. Also permitted: shifting traffic cones, dashing between cars and luring late arrivals with the vague promise of a discount. ``Can I get in for five bucks?'' asked one fan who recently rolled up to Stanley's lot. ``See the guy in the white shirt'' inside the lot, Stanley said, his head moving in something between a nod and a circle. ``He's got a gtod deal for you.'' At most big-city arenas, the controlled spots go for a single price. But in downtown Miami, it has always been a distance-for-dollars approach. The closer you park, the shorter the walk -- and the more you pay. This time next year, things will be different. The Panthers will be gone to Broward County. The parking leases will be renegotiated. Who knows who will be working the 15 to 20 lots on the east side, where most of the private lots are? For now, the question just turns the mtod sour among the lot operators and their itinerant attendants -- many of them homeless, earning about $20 a night. They've spent years building a cash-for-parking economy. ``You work out here seven or eight years, you get to know people,'' says Ricardo Metral, 28, vice president of V.I.P. Security Parking. ``You get to know the customers. The customers get to know the homeless. It's like a family.'' The curtain rises at nightfall, when cars begin to creep in from I-95, and the street lights turn the dusty lots a faint yellow. Fencing, topped with barbed wire, divides one parking domain from the next. Miamins skyline, its office buildings rising high and bright to the south, seem close enough to touch. As the Mercedes-Benzes, Hondas and Lexuses inch toward the arena, their electric windows open in a slow fall. The attendants approach, going to work, each loyal to their own philosophy of luring them past the gate. For Freixas, who sets up shop on Ninth Street, just west of the Metromover tracks, success comes with gimmicks. For Heat games, he asks his lone attendant, Bennie Chavis, 45, to hoist the basketball htop and pump up a ball. For Panthers games, they give away free rubber rats. ``They got me holding a big rat out here,'' Chavis says. ``It's embarrassing. But it works.'' Montero, meanwhile, stands in the street a few blocks west. He speaks little English, relying on volume and a flag-waving style that would humble an aircraft carrier signalman. Stanley, who wears an orange safety vest as he roams widely on Eighth Street, is convinced that no customer is lost as long as he has his ear. Tiptoeing into traffic, he has a big back of tricks -- the respectful compliment (``How ya doing, Doc?''), the rush job (``Don't be late to the game!''), and the direct confrontation (``Where are you gonna park?!''). ``You have to be very aggressive'' he says. ``You only have about a half a second to get their attention.'' Cutline JON KRAL / Herald Staff Caption: photo: PARK HERE: Cesar Obando waves cars into a $5 parking lot on NE Seventh Street. (a)

content_for_eval: Our nations failure to truly secure its borders and restore respect for the law through comprehensive immigration reform, coupled with a new directive regarding so-called Social Security no-matches, is about to deliver a solid punch to an already-unstable economy. isnder the new directive, millions of workers will be threatened with dismissal and employers who dont act within 90 days against workers using Social Security numbers differing from government records -- often an indication of undocumented immigration status -- will face penalties, including heavy fines and possible prosecution. That sounds gtod so far, for those of us who believe in secure borders and respect for the law. But this enforcement-only focus on Social Security no-matches is an unrealistic approach that will severely disrupt our economy, and hurt employers and workers as much as it will hurt undocumented immigrants. It is estimated that within months 140,000 employers will receive Social Security no-match letters regarding 8.9 million workers. While these letters will force undocumented workers to leave jobs they have held for years, they will not feel compelled to leave the country. Nor will national security be strengthened -- the undocumented will not come forward and be identified. Rather, many workers already in the shadows will begin a cycle of musical chairs moving from employer to employer until the next no-match letter arrives. Few industries will be affected as significantly as the construction industry -- an engine of our economy with more than 10 million workers driving 5 percent of our nations economic output. An estimated 14 percent of those workers do not have legal status to work in the isnited States, according to the Pew Hispanic Center. Without a realistic comprehensive immigration policy that addresses the reality of these undocumented workers already here, the government-directed firings of millions of workers will destroy the ability of tens of thousands of employers to maintain stable operations. As the no-match directive takes effect, numerous construction contractors will lose a significant share of their workforce, sending shockwaves through an economy that remains uncertain due to the continuing mortgage meltdown. While many workers will move from employer to employer, many others, at the direction of unscrupulous contractors, will operate as independent contractors to sidestep the Social Security no-match trap. This will make immigrant workers more vulnerable, and make it unlikely that appropriate taxes are paid. In many parts of our country, construction labor shortages already make it difficult to get work done. Disrupting long-standing cohesive workforces will make it even more difficult to complete projects, from roadways to bridges to retail centers. As organizations predominantly made up of construction employers and workers -- both immigrants and is.S.-born -- our first commitment is to our country and the well-being of our members, employers and their families. But that wellbeing cannot be protected if employers and the industry itself are in disarray. We have gone tto far for tto long with a broken immigration system -- a system that has been complicit in allowing millions of workers to live, work, pay taxes, raise families and own homes here -- to realistically think that we are able to restore respect for the law and secure our borders simply through Social Security no-match letters and mass firings. For the sake of our economy, Congress and the White House must produce comprehensive immigration-reform legislation that recognizes the real impact of broken borders on American workers, but also recognizes the hard work of immigrant workers already here and the important role they play. Vought Aircraft Industries Inc. seems to have weathered some turbulence.Now the company is just hoping for a smtoth landing.For that to happen, Vought will have to complete its transformation from a largely military-focused supplier to a company dependent on the commercial aviation industry."We're happy," said chief executive Elmer Doty, who joined the Dallas-based company in February. "Overall, the future is very bright."That optimism might have been out of place even just a few months ago at Vought, one of the most storied names in military and commercial aviation, due to major job cuts and the news that one of the company's biggest military contracts is likely to lose funding ston.But Mr. Doty said the cuts seem to be paying off, and several new projects are coming online in time to offset the potential end of the contract for C-17 planes.The two biggest new contracts are for building significant chunks of Sikorsky Aircraft Corp.'s isH-60 helicopter and Boeing Co.'s 787 airplane.The 787 Dreamliner is clearly Vought's keystone project now, with the first finished systems expected to ship early next year."When the C-17 goes away, then commercial will definitely be the lion's share of our business," Mr. Doty said. "Where Boeing goes, we go, tto."While the contract to build pieces of the C-17 plane for the military is winding down, it should be several years before that revenue dries up completely.Last month, Vought reported third-quarter sales of $354 million, up from $310 million in the third quarter of last year.The company's net loss shrank from $55 million to $13 million in the same span.Richard Aboulafia, an aerospace and defense analyst with the Teal Group, said Vought has turned a corner."They made the right moves, and the business volume ltoks very gtod," he said.But challenges remain.Some of Vought's troubles are a standard corporate tale of downsizing and cost-cutting in a competitive globaleconomy."This business was set up as a cost-plus defense business," Mr. Doty said. The company "was not well suited for a more dynamic, competitive world."Additionally, the executives who would have to spearhead that change were not trained to turn on a dime."They had never worked anywhere else," Mr. Doty said. "Their background was such that change was going to occur slowly, and we didn't have time for slow."About 1,000 workers - or about a sixth of the workforce - have been let go since last year, mostly managerial and administrative staff, and other costs have been trimmed.Vought has also started taking advantage of the terms in some of its contracts with Boeing, its largest customer. The contracts contain provisions that allow Vought to raise the prices of individual parts if Boeing orders fewer parts than originally promised.Mr. Doty said Vought had not taken advantage of those provisions before he arrived. D6 Excerpts From the Senate Testimony by Greenspan Following are excerpts from testimony by Alan Greenspan, the chairman of the Federal Reserve, given before the Senate Banking Committee yes­terday: Ltoking ahead, the members of the F.O.M.C. [Federal Open Market Committee] expect infla­tion to remain low and the economy to grow appreciably further. However, as I shall be dis­cussing, the unusually gtod inflation performance of recent years seems to owe in large part to some temporary factors, of uncertain longevity. Thus, the F.O.M.C. continues to see the distribution of inflation risks skewed to the upside and must remain especially alert to the possible emergence of imbalances in financial and product markets that ultimately could endanger the maintenance of the ^low-inflation environment. Sustainable eco­nomic expansion for 1997 and beyond depends on it. .*. The willingness of workers in recent years to trade off smaller increases in wages for greater job Security seems to be reasonably well docu­mented. The unanswered question is why this Insecurity persisted even as the labor market, by all objective measures, tightened considerably Certainly, other factors have contributed to the softness in compensation growth in the past few years. The sharp deceleration in health care costs, of course, is cited frequently. Another is the heightened pressure on firms and their workers in industries that compete internationally. Domestic deregulation has had similar effects on the intensi­ty of competitive forces in some industries. In any event, although I do not doubt that all these factors are relevant, I would be surprised if they were nearly as important as job insecurity.... The F.O.M.C. has recognized the need to remain vigilant for signs of potentially inflationary imbal­ances that might, if not corrected promptly, under­mine our economic expansion. The F.O.M.C., in fact, has signaled a state of heightened alert for possible policy tightening since last July in its policy directives. But we have also taken care not to act prematurely. The F.O.M.C. refrained from changing policy last summer, despite expectations of a near-term policy firming by many financial market participants. In light of the developments I've just discussed affecting wages and prices, we thought inflation might well remain damped, and in any case was unlikely to pick up very rapidly, in part because the economic expansion appeared likely to slow to a more sustainable pace. In the event, inflation has remained quiescent since then. Given the lags with which monetary policy affects the economy, however, we cannot rule out a situation in which a pre-emptive policy tighten­ing may become appropriate before any sign of actual higher inflation becomes evident. If the F.O.M.C. were to implement such an action, it would be judging that the risks to the economic expansion of waiting longer had increased unduly and had begun to outweigh the advantages of waiting for uncertainties to be reduced by the accumulation of more information about econom­ic trends. ... Clearly, when people are exposed to long peri­ods of relative economic tranquillity, they seem inevitably prone to complacency about the future. This is understandable. We have had 15 years of economic expansion interrupted by only one reces­sion — and that was six years ago. As the memory of such past events fades, it naturally seems ever less sensible to keep up one's guard against an adverse event in the future. Thus, it should come as no surprise that, after such a long period of balanced expansion, risk premiums for advancing funds to businesses in virtually all financial mar­kets have declined to near-record lows. Is it possible that there is something fundamen­tally new about this current period that would warrant such complacency? Yes, it is possible. Markets may have become more efficient, compe­tition is more global, and information technology has doubtless enhanced the stability of business operations. But, regrettably, history is strewn with visions of such "new eras" that, in the end, have proven to be a mirage. In short, history counsels caution. Such caution seems especially warranted with regard to the sharp rise in equity prices during the past two years. These gains have obyiously raised questions of sustainability.... Why should the central bank be concerned about the possibility that financial markets may be overestimating returns or mispricing risk? It is not that we have a firm view that equity prices are necessarily excessive right now or risk spreads patently tto low. Our goal is to contribute as best we can to the highest possible growth of income and wealth over time, and we would be pleased if the favorable economic environment projected in markets actually comes to pass. Rather, the F.O.M.C. has to be sensitive to indications of even slowly building imbalances, whatever their soaree, that, by fostering the emergence of infla­tion pressures, would ultimately threaten healthy economic expansion.
