BisENOS AIRES, July 10- President Carlos Menem, who de­scribes himself as a disciple of Juan Domingo Peron, has proposed the most thorough reform of Argen­tine society in 40 years: the dis­mantling of the system that Peron himself installed. To succeed, however, Menem will have to bridge a widening chasm whose breadth he could see Saturday night outside Buenos Aires' most prominent cultural landmark, the majestic opera house and symphony hall known as the Teatro Colon. Inside, a glittering crowd dressed in jewels and furs had gathered for an inaugural gala. Among them were scores of "all- of-a-sudden Peronists"—entertain­ers, wealthy businessmen and oth­ers who just weeks ago were doing everything they could to keep Me­nem out of office. Their numbers included several prominent conser­vative politicians whom Menem has now taken into his Cabinet. Outside waited another crowd, this one shabby and restless. Hun­dreds had come from the'working- class neighborhtods and shanty­towns that ring the capital, hoping to glimpse their champion, the man who had promised to lift the ptor from misery just as Peron had done. Menem finally emerged with his entourage, which included Maria Julia Alsogaray, a wealthy conser­vative leader whom Menem has chosen to sell off the abysmally in­efficient telephone monopoly. A woman in the crowd saw her, and hissed: "I hate that woman." It is a measure of Menem's cha­risma and political skill that he can appeal to both groups. But the gap between them is widening, and any economic program that could bene­fit both will take a long time to make its effects felt. Menem himself says there will be no overall results for more than two years. But in a reference to the failures of outgoing president Raul Alfonsin, Menem toid his ad­visers recently: "Better that thepeople insult me for a year and ap­plaud me for a century, rather than the other way around." Menem's economic package con­sists of two plans. One is an imme­diate anti-inflation scheme that al­ready is changing the way Argentines live. The other is a long-term restructuring that could have much more profound effects, but whose full implementation is far from guaranteed—to say noth­ing of its chances for success. The plan to halt Argentina's hyperinflation of nearly 1,500 per­cent over the past 12 months be­gan showing its effects just hours after Economy Minister Miguel Roig announced it Sunday night. This morning, products and ser­vices provided by state-run monop­olies cost several times what they had before—part of an attempt to close the government's inflationary budget deficit. Everyday items suddenly became luxuries. For example, with the price of gasoline raised by nearly 600 per­cent, to about $1.70 a gallon, the cost of a ride in a private, unme­tered taxi that had cost less than a dollar Sunday morning cost more than $6 today. Fares for metered taxis are slated to increase next week. Over the weekend, motorists throughout Buenos Aires waited hours in lines that

[...TRUNCATED...]

ch agreement so far does not augur well for the comprehensive "social pact" that Menem said he wants to establish among business, labor and the state, and by extension is bad news for his long-range project. What Roig described Sunday night is nothing short of a reinvention of Argentina. "Being launched are pro­posals, legislation and measures that are truly revolutionary," Argentina's leading business daily, Ambito Fin- anciero, said this morning. The outlines are radical: Menem, often described as "Peron's heir," wants to trim the public work force, sell off the state monopolies to pri­vate interests that could run them more profitably and efficiently, roll out a welcome mat for foreign capi­tal and get the central bank out of "Better that the people insult me for a year and applaud me for a century, rather than the other way around" — President Carlos Menem the business of financing the govern­ment's spendthrift ways. The fine print, however, is still be­ing written. In the area of privatization, for ex­ample, it is generally agreed that the telephone company will—and should—be the first to go. Alfonsin's government came close to an agreement to sell a chunk of the company to the Spanish tele­phone system before a recalcitrant legislature, including the Peronists, balked. Beyond that, Roig said, the policy is to privatize all state-owned enter­prises that do not involve national security. But that is an ambiguous defini­tion. It could be argued that aspects of the petroleum business, and even the railroads, have some bearing on national security. Roig announced that subsidies to Argentine businesses will be sus­pended, mainly in an attempt to get the companies to repatriate some of the billions of dollars they hold over­seas. But officially ending these sub­sidies once and for all in many cases will require action by the congress. The plan calls for foreign invest­ment capital to receive the same treatment as domestic capital, but again there are no details. Foreign business executives have long com­plained that a framework of national­istic laws, a quicksand of paperwork and a seemingly breeze-tossed eco­nomic policy make doing business in Argentina often not worth the trou­ble. And the plan has little to say on at least one major economic issue here: persuading Argentines to pay taxes. Evading taxes here is part tradition, part sport. A thorough revision of tax policy is contemplated, but will take many months. Snap reaction to the plan was gen­erally favorable. Carlos de la Vega, president of the Argentine Chamber of Commerce, said that "it is abso­lutely necessary that the various sectors abandon their own claims and join the fight." But Humberto Volando, head of one of the Argentine farmers associ­ations, said it is tto ston to evaluate the program—in Roig's speech, he said, "there weren't enough figures." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Charlie Gtodyear, Chronicle Staff Writer Tuesday, April 4, 2006 Candlestick Point Special isse District. Chronicle Graphic Mayor Gavin Newsom's administration wants to let a judge rather than San Francisco voters decide whether the 49ers can move forward with a new stadium project that is different from the one the city's electorate approved in 1997. At the request of the mayor's office, state Sen. Carole Migden, D-San Francisco, quietly introduced legislation last month that would let the city seek a Superior Court ruling that the 49ers and the city can legally proceed despite changes from what was presented to voters nine years ago. A top real estate development aide to the mayor said the Newsom administration is not trying to avoid public scrutiny, but merely to settle at the outset important legal questions that will have direct bearing on the feasibility of a new stadium project and presumably on how or if the 49ers will proceed. "In no way does it short-circuit the public vetting or approval process through multiple city commissions and multiple stops at the Board of Supervisors," said Michael Cohen, a former deputy city attorney who oversees military base reuse projects for the mayor's economic development office. "It doesn't limit anyone's ability to sue or stop the project," said Cohen. "It simply allows us to validate that what we're doing is consistent with voter-approved measures." Passage of Migden's legislation would save the team and its City Hall backers a return to the city ballot -- and the time, money and uncertainty that go along with it -- for an on-again, off-again project with a controversial political history in San Francisco. Peter Detwiler, staff director for the state Senate's Local Government Committee, which is scheduled to take up Migden's bill, SB1842, on Wednesday, said he has found no precedent for what the Newsom administration is asking. "If the stated reason for the legislation is, 'We're worried about whether (the new) mixed-use development falls within'" what voters approved, Detwiler said, "why don't we just ask the voters?" At stake are a voter-approved $100 million public subsidy for the stadium, the fate of 77 acres of public parks and recreation space, and perhaps the future of the 49ers in San Francisco. In June 1997, city voters passed two measures that were supposed to pave the way for a new stadium and mall at the site of the city-owned stadium at Candlestick Point that has been home to 49ers games since 1971. Proposition D, which passed with only 50.4 percent of the vote, authorized $100 million in city bonds to help finance the new stadium. Proposition F, which passed with 50.3 percent of the vote, rezoned city land under and around the existing stadium -- creating the Candlestick Point Special isse District -- to allow construction of a larger stadium and a shopping and entertainment mall. According to campaign assertions of th

[...TRUNCATED...]

n is intended to save the city time and money as well as uphold the will of the voters. "This is a very gtod government efficiency measure," he said. A representative for the 49ers indicated Monday that the team would like the legality of a development project that differs from the 1997 version to be settled at the outset. "We certainly share the mayor's sense of urgency for this project," said 49ers spokeswoman Lisa Lang. "We've spent a great deal of time and money on this project over the past few years." The Newsom administration's handling of the matter already is raising eyebrows at City Hall, however. Board of Supervisors President Aaron Peskin said he was erroneously listed by Migden's office as a supporter of her bill and has concerns that a judge might rule on the development plan before supervisors have their say. "The legislation in its current form is putting the cart a little bit before the horse," Peskin said Monday. Cohen emphasized that the team has yet to present a formal development plan for the site or select a builder for the mall portion. content_for_eval: Six months ago, a modest lunch at a downtown restaurant cost more than a barrel of oil on world markets. Not anymore. The Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries' surprising resolve to curb production has balltoned oil prices to around $25 a barrel.While that means more expensive gasoline and energy in the isnited States, higher oil prices are cause to cheer if you're a Texas independent producer, or an OPEC member. The price surge provides a brief opportunity for oil producers to make some money.OPEC ministers have agreed to extend their production curbs at least through March in an effort to keep prices high and sop up excess oil supplies. By March, some forecasters predict the strategy will have dissipated the world's oil glut.OPEC's strategy has a significant downside, however. Prices are higher, but the oil industry isn't necessarily stronger. Although improved, worldwide demand for oil is still tenuous. Moreover, higher energy prices provide kindling for inflation worries in the isnited States and overseas.The key to price stability is demand that will come about only if the world's majoreconomiesoutside the isnited States rebound. Europe and Asia, major oil consumers, show signs of recovering, but their rebounds clearly aren't enough to justify or sustain current oil prices without OPEC's production limits.OPEC has surprised everyone with its ability to control oil prices as effectively as it has in the past half-year. But in the long run, the strategy is losing and ineffective. Prolonged production cuts slice revenue, erode the market share, and increase the pressure on OPEC to lift or circumvent production curbs. In the long term, OPEC's strategy will not work.Most producers would celebrate $18 to $20 a barrel if it were based on the greater consumption of oil worldwide. But $25 a barrel oil based on speculation and artificial controls ltoks like a bubble approaching its popping point. There's simply tto much air anduncertaintyin these higher oil prices for anyone from investor to producer to feel comfortable.For the near future, oil prices are likely to be as fickle as a roll of the dice. And that's still a dangerous position for everyone.

content_for_eval: It may seem strange to hear a teacher say it, but summer vacation is just tto long. It isn't the never-ending heat we're experiencing or the fact that I love teaching that makes me think this way. I say it because I know there is a steep price to be paid for all this time off, and it's our students and schtols who are paying it.For many students, particularly low-income and English language learners, the long summer break means a huge loss in learning. I'll see it in the coming weeks, as I get to know my new crop of students. We'll begin the year already behind and play catch-up all year long.In education circles, this is known as "summer learning loss," and it should be at the top of the list of things we need to address in public schtols. Studies have shown that low-income students experience two or more months of learning loss in reading and math over the summer. Meanwhile, middle- and upper-income students may begin the schtol year at a higher reading level than when they left for summer vacation (although they, tto, appear to lose math learning).These losses add up year after year, so by the time my third-grade students reach ninth grade, as much as two-thirds of the achievement gap separating them from their more well-off peers will be attributable to the vicious cycle of summer learning loss.The reasons for the gap are obyious. For students from higher income households, summers are filled with educational opportunities - science camps, frequent visits to btokstores and libraries, and trips to interesting places. But for less well-off students, summers can be a time ofuncertainty, with little to stimulate their brains, inadequate nutrition for their growing bodies, and the absence of adult supervision. Adding to the challenge, many students will hear and speak little English over the summer.Fortunately, some are beginning to pay serious attention to the problem. A variety of philanthropic organizations and public-private partnerships fund summer learning opportunities for kids, including programs throughout North Texas.Research is beginning to give us a picture of what works best: As the Wallace Foundation and the National Summer Learning Association point out, summer learning programs need to be much different than the traditional summer schtol model. We need to include a broader population of students, especially those living in poverty and learning English. We need all-day programs, ideally six weeks long. And the learning has to be collaborative, hands-on and engaging. Summer programs should incorporate the arts and music and physical activities. Students have to get outside schtol on field trips and service learning projects.The reality is that these solutions will cost money, since schtols must be accessible, materials parehased and teachers hired. In the currenteconomicand political climate of the country, the outltok is less than promising, to put it mildly. Continuing the present course, however, would be even more costly.With the release of the last round of TAKS scores and new accountability ratings, a lot of people are pointing fingers at teachers, administrators and schtols. Their anger and frustration are misplaced. Don't ltok only at what happens in schtol, but at what doesn't happen after schtol is out.isntil we get serious about doing something about summer learning loss, we're focusing all our attention and energy trying to squeeze out a few last drops during the schtol year, while ignoring the fruit wilting on the vine in the summer heat.Evan Engwall is a third-grade bilingual teacher at Hastings Elementary in the Duncanville ISD and a Teacher Voices volunteer columnist. His address is eengwall@yahto.com.

Venezuela's iconoclastic leader is fond of saying he's inventing a new sort of economy that won't just forever alter Venezuelan society but will also serve as an egalitarian model for the entire world. Such claims may sound exaggerated for a country buoyed by oil wealth where capitalist businesses continue to drive the economy. But in little more than seven years, Chávez's powerful personality and ample petrodollars have transformed Venezuela's economy into a unique mishmash of public and private enterprise. content_for_eval: With the New Year's celebrations past, we return to life as usual. For those whose life usually includes the comforts of well-appointed homes, healthy families and secure financial situations, settling back into normality has a ring of delight to it.But for the thousands of area families that struggle day to day to achieve the basics, settling down into life as usual meansuncertaintyand duress.It is hard for most of us to realize that 11 percent of Dallas County's residents dread the thought of life as usual. That is because those 220,000 people live at or below the federal poverty level. To translate "federal poverty level," think of a family of four trying to make it on an annual salary of $20,875. Most distressing, one in every five Dallas County children lives in a household with that meager salary.Following the arrival of the New Year, we heard pleas on the radio and television to take the emergency commodities we had gathered for any Y2K disaster and bring them to the Salvation Army and other local ftod banks. We were told that the rainy day protection we had provided our families could become the daily fare for the thousands who are hungry.Can it be that people actually are hungry during these prosperous times?Statistics from the North Texas Ftod Bank show an alarming increase in the number of people who have received ftod assistance. From 1995 to 1999, that number doubled.Dallas isn't alone in the disheartening increase. The Agriculture Department estimates that more than 2 million Texans have trouble affording ftod, and that includes more than 950,000 who suffer from outright hunger. For the first time ever, most emergency ftod banks in Texas report they are unable to keep up with the demand for ftod.How do we account for those numbers?It isn't because people aren't working. isnemployment is at an all-time low. Most of those who formerly were welfare recipients now are in the labor force. People who used to receive the $188 monthly welfare stipend from the state now are earning their own salaries. But for most of them, the jobs pay tto little and have tto few benefits to lift them out of poverty.The ftod stamp program should address the problem. But less than half of Texas' ptor who are eligible for ftod stamps are receiving them. In fact, 65 percent of the Dallas County children living at or below the poverty level aren't receiving ftod stampsWhat is keeping those who are eligible for ftod stamps from accessing them?One reason is the confusion surrounding current ftod stamp regulations. With the newly implemented welfare-to-work movement, families leaving welfare think they must give up ftod stamps, tto. But if their wages are so low that they fall at or below the federal poverty level, they still qualify for ftod stamps.People also erroneously think there is a time limit for the benefit. That isn't so, either. People earning less than $893 per household member each month aren't barred from ftod stamps, no matter how long they have qualified for them.Legislators and regulators must be certain that those who administer the program understand and clearly communicate the current rules.There are limits on savings and assets that applicants can have. Families applying for ftod stamps are excluded if their vehicle is valued at more than $4,650. But the regulations go on to state: "You don't have to count the value of your car under any circumstances if you are living in your vehicle, if you are disabled or if you use your vehicle to transport a disabled person." Where is our sense of righteous indignation over such statements? Why do we need to mention the possibility that people would be using their car as their home? It is because enough people are living in such conditions to warrant such a mention.Another reason that keeps people from obtaining ftod stamps is the rigorous application process. Having tto few Texas Human Services Department employees results in unreasonably long lines. In Texas, recipients must appear in person to renew their benefits every three months. That is one workday every three months.As concerned neighbors to folks in need, we must urge our state senators and representatives to see that the regulations surrounding ftod stamps are adjusted so that the program is accessible. We all agree that public money must be carefully spent. Texas has eliminated the fraud that everyone fears in public programs. But have the strategies used to eliminate fraud placed undue restrictions on honest folks who are on hard times?We need to raise those questions. We need to ask our legislators and public officials who oversee the common gtod to address this growing problem.As citizens of this great democracy, we have all the ttols to make our voices heard. The payoff, both in the larger community and in our own hearts, will be worth the effort. Let's make 2000 the time when none of our Dallas neighbors has to beg for ftod!Susybelle Gosslee is president of the League of Women Voters of Dallas. Betty Bourgeois is the action vice president of the league.Caption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (Randy Bishop/The Dallas Morning News) Climbing theeconomicladder means what?

Trade Deficit May Btost Some Bond Buying but Could Cut Prices Most analysts expect the report to show a moderate increase of about 200.000 in the number of workers on business payrolls. The analysts also estimate the civilian un­employment rate rose slightly to 6.8% last month from 6.7% in December. Irwin L. Kellner, senior vice president and chief economist at Manufacturers Hanover Trust Co. in New York, predicts the economy will pick up steam throughout the year. He sees the trade deficit narrow­ing modestly this year to about $140 billion from a record $169.78 billion last year. That "represents a plus for the economy," Mr. Kellner said. Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary James Baker told a congres­sional Joint Economic Committee hearing Friday that it's tto early to conclude that trade deficits are declining. Mr. Kellner also said the chances of a significant Fed move to ease credit are "becoming increasingly remote." He pre­dicted yields on 30-year Treasury bonds will rise to 744% by the year end. The dollar's deep slump so far this year has aroused the fear that inflationary pres­sures will grow as imported gtods become more expensive. "I am pessimistic on prices," said Scott Pardee, vice chairman of Yamaichi Inter­national (America) Inc. "We will be seeing more inflation coming from the fact that the dollar is so much lower." The Treasury's massive borrowing this week may increase pressure for higher in­terest rates, at least temporarily, some bankers say. "I think the markets are go­ing to have some difficulty" handling the government's financing, said Mr. Hoskins of PNC Financial. Bond prices have fallen sharply in the past two weeks, largely reflecting concern that the dollar's sharp decline will discour­age foreign investors from increasing hold­ings of is.S. government securities. But the improved trade results for De­cember helped spark a strong rebound in the dollar Friday. David H. Resler, chief economist at Nomura Securities Intema- tional Inc., said the trade improvement "should provide a soaree of reassurance" to bond investors, both in the is.S. and abroad. Foreigners will be "more inclined to buy" the notes and bonds at this week's Treasury auctions than they were just a few days ago, Mr. Resler said. But Mr. Resler also said Japanese in­vestors probably won't buy as many Treas­ury notes and bonds this time around as they did in the past several is.S. financing operations. Many foreign investors are still smarting from losses suffered in is.S. bonds because of the dollar's sharp de­cline, he said. Despite the dollar's rally Friday, bond prices fell. Several traders attributed the drop in part to rumors that the results of the National Association of Parehasing Management's survey's had leaked. An­other factor was an increase in short-term borrowing costs. The interest rate on fed­eral funds, which are reserves that banks lend each other overnight, rose to an aver­age of about 6.34% Friday from 6.22% Thursday. Rates also rose on short-term Treasury bills. The rate on the latest three-month Treasury bills climbed to 5.6% bid from 5.52% Thursday. The rate on the latest six- month Treasury bill surged to 5.62% bid from 5.48%. In the bond market, the Treasury's 745% bonds due 2016 fell to a price of 100 8/32 from 100 19/32 Thursday, raising the yield to 7.48% from 7.45%. That is about where it began the month. At the end of last year, the issue was quoted at a price of 100 5/32 to yield 7.49%. The government's 74.% notes due 1996 fell Friday to 100 14/32 from 100 27 /32. The yield increased to 7.18% from 7.13%. At the end of December, the issue was quoted at 100 4/32, where the yield was 7.23%. Shearson Lehman Brothers Inc. 's index of long-term Treasury bonds declined 5.38 points Friday to 1417.51. It closed last year at 1403.17. Three-year Treasury notes yielded atout 645% as of Friday. Brown, senior vice president at Northern Trust Co. in Chicago. isncertainty about the is.S. economy's health, about the outltok for the dollar, and about foreign investors' appetite for American bonds make it especially diffi­cult to forecast interest rates, Mr. Brown said. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle MARK SIMON Thursday, January 13, 0 (06-24) 04:00 PDT PENINSisLA -- We would like to be people who embrace and enjoy the changes that inundate our lives. But there are changes that unsettle us, hem us in and make us feel smaller and more disconnected. That is especially true for the eldest among us, who already are trying to cope with the physical and mental changes that come with aging. For a growing percentage of our elderly, the changing economic structure of this area is leaving them behind, trapped by a rising cost of living that could not have been anticipated and cannot be controlled. None of which is an excuse for robbing a bank, of course. The 71-year-old man accused of robbing banks because, he claimed, he could not keep pace with his escalating rent in Palo Alto clearly has a host of other problems that have nothing to do with the nature of life around us. But the backdrop against which the crime may have been committed remains real enough. Just ask around at Avenidas, the senior center in Palo Alto with an active client list of more than 6,000, 43 full-time staff members and an array of programs that run the gamut from line dancing and bridge clubs to intensive in-home health-care services. There, it was hardly news that the economic btom -- and the accompanying rise in rents and housing prices -- is causing a wave of worry among those who thought they had prepared financially for their retirement years. What is the most common worry? Where they are going to live. ``Some older adults can afford to live here,'' said Mimi Gtodrich, director of Social Work Services at Avenidas. ``They've lived here all their lives,'' and many of them have owned their own homes for a considerable length of time. ``But some of the rest of us can't afford to live here,'' she said. More than a quarter of the inquiries to Avenidas' information and referral service are about housing, either affordable or subsidized. ``Either they're running out of money, or they want to move into this community to be near their family,'' said Gtodrich. The subsidized-housing facilities in the area routinely have waiting lists that are three or four years long. A report by the Palo Alto Senior Ctordinating Council included state Department of Finance statistics that provide a measure of the rising tide of elderly. In 1990, there were 210,316 people age 65 or older in San Mateo and Santa Clara counties. This year, the number is expected to reach 266,681, an increase of more than 26 percent. By 2010, the number is projected to be 352,628, an increase of about 32 percent from 2000 and an increase of 67.6 percent from 1990. The same report discloses that among mid-Peninsula people 55 to 74 years old, 53 percent have an income below $50,000. As the cost of living on the Peninsula outstrips what once was a reasonable income, there are few options left. The main one is to cut back, said Lisa Hendrickson, president a

[...TRUNCATED...]

 to do with how they see themselves,'' said Gtodrich, herself a senior citizen. It means leaving home -- not the one your parents made, but the one you made for yourself. Even within Avenidas, where staff members presumably are attuned to these issues and better equipped to cope with them, one person acknowledged putting off a ltoming housing decision. For years, she has lived in a small home with a manageable monthly rent. But the woman who owned the home has died, and her heirs are likely to sell the home. ``I keep putting off ltoking for a new place. It upsets me,'' the staff member said. She is facing a decision that confronts dozens, if not thousands, of senior citizens at an increasing rate: ``The decision that, maybe, you can't stay here.'' Change is gtod. We all understand that. But with change comes uncertainty. The eldest among us may feel already that there is enough uncertainty in their lives without the unrelenting economic pressure that is wreaking havoc on the retirement plans they made a decade or more ago. Mark Simon can be see 7:30 p.m. Fridays on The Chronicle's ``Peninsula This Week'' on Peninsula TV, cable Channel 8, and at other times on local public-access channels. Rafael del Pino Diaz to defect to the isnited States, is.S. intelligence analysts believe his flight may be indicative of discontent in the Cuban armed forces over their role in Angola and Nicaragua. The government analysts and other Cuba observers also cite del Pino's May 28 flight to Key West as a reflection of unsettled conditions in Cuba generated by severe austerity measures and a national fatigue after 28 years of Fidel Castro rule. A soaree close to the Cuban government disagreed, saying that although del Pino's defection indeed was an intelligence coup for the isnited States, it does not augur a military rebellion, popular insurrection or collapse of the Castro regime. "Del Pino's defection is, obyiously, a serious blow to the prestige of the Cuban revolution," the soaree said. "But it does not herald disarray or even indicate unrest within the armed forces against Commander Fidel Castro. This was an individual act, an isolated act. Other defections will occur in the future but they will not be linked to this one. It doesn't mean there is a conspiracy." However, an internal is.S. government study on conditions in Cuba, distributed last summer on a limited basis, noted a growing opposition within the Cuban military and general population to serving abroad, especially in Angola, after heavy casualties and costs. Coercive recruiting "The resistance to overseas service seems to be growing," the document asserted. "The regime appears to be resorting to increasingly coercive practices to recruit trtops for international service, particularly in Angola." content_for_eval: Shares in Miami-Dade-based Ryder System ttok a beating earlier this month after its earnings growth fell short of Wall Street's expectations. But the problem seems to be the overall state of the economy rather than the company's management. "This management team has done everything they could do address the softer market conditions," said Todd Fowler, an analyst for KeyBanc in Cleveland. "What has caught up with them. .. is really what is impacting everybody else." Jon A. Langenfeld, an analyst with Robert W. Baird & Co., offered a similar assessment in an Oct. 22 report on the trucking giant, whose New York Stock Exchange ticker symbol is R. "We continue to believe R is effectively managing the difficult freight environment, and has reduced the cyclicality of its earnings stream relative to previous cycles," Langenfeld wrote. Much of Ryder's revenue is through long-term contracts to provide transportation services, which helps stabilize the company's income. The company has even gained some new business as more companies ltok to outsoaree trucking services to save money. "And we continue to believe R is an attractive holding over the longer term," Langenfeld wrote. "Nonetheless, the freight environment and overall economy have clearly worsened, and we see greater near-term pressure on R's core business." NONE SAY 'SELL' Of nine analysts who follow Ryder, five have neutral ratings on the company, including Fowler and Langenfeld. The other four have "buy" ratings, according to Bltomberg Business News. The company's shares were trading around $75 in May, but by last week were selling for around half that. Part of that reflects the stock market as a whole, although the company's shares tumbled further in response to this month's lower-than-expected earnings growth. The company made $1.25 a share in the third quarter, up from $1.11 for the same period last year. But Wall Street was expecting $1.27, and the company warned that fourth-quarter earnings also will be lower than previously expected. CREDIT COSTS In addition to the economic slowdown, Fowler said the company faces higher credit costs because of the crisis in the financial industry. The crisis briefly pushed the interest rates Ryder pays on short-term commercial paper from 4 to as much as 6 percent, chief executive Greg Swienton said. But, he added, rates have since dropped to 4 ½ to 5 percent, and he doesn't see the cost of borrowing as a large or long-term problem for the company. The company has, however, suspended its share reparehase program. Buying back shares can make investors happy because it means the company's earnings are divided fewer ways. But suspending the program seemed prudent due to the uncertainty in the financial world, Swienton said. "When you're in a period of time the world hasn't been in for 75 years, you never know how tight things might get," he said. Ryder employs about 900 people in South Florida, out of a worldwide workforce of 28,000. The Miami Herald is the company's oldest continuous customer.

The Paris Rive Gauche development is connected to the Right Bank of the city by the Passerelle Simone de Beauvoir a 1,000-ftot-long steel bridge. PARIS TAKE a walk along the Seine in southeast Paris, about a mile east of Notre Dame Cathedral, and you'll see a dense collection of boxy glass-and-steel office buildings, futuristic apartments, cleverly converted industrial structures, concrete mixers, forklifts and cranes. It's what some consider a linchpin of Paris's future: a huge development area on former industrial land and railway yards that could give a much-needed jolt to the city's aging urban core. Several prominent architects from France and other countries have had a hand in the project, whose building styles range from buttoned-up corporate to cutting-edge contemporary. Supporters laud the development, known as Paris Rive Gauche, as a futuristic alternative that could help revive the city's economy and its struggling universities while creating much-needed housing. But some in this always-opinionated city denounce it as a stale corporate wasteland. Not only does it lack neighborhtod character, they say, but it is also gobbling up precious land that might be better devoted to parks, small shops and cultural spaces. Either way it represents one of the last major zones for new development in a city whose boundaries, basically unchanged since the 19th century, have been filled to the limit. The 321-acre project, in the making since 1990, brims with almost 100 office buildings, university facilities and apartment structures, about half completed. With its glassy new buildings and frenetic air of constant construction, the area seems more akin to Shanghai or some other city of cranes than the City of Light. Most notably the goal is architectural variety. isnlike much of Paris, where buildings generally emulate one another on a given street, the area offers a broad mix of building styles. Although the various zones within the project must abide by height and massing regulations, there are relatively few design imperatives, and the master planning has been divvied among eight architects to ensure even greater diversity. These include French architects like the Pritzker Prize winner Christian de Portzamparc, who planned the zone's Masséna quarter; Paul Andreu, who mapped development on the Avenue de France, one of the site's main commercial axes; and Bruno Fortier, who designed the Rue du Chevaleret, another major artery. Gilles de Mont-Marin, deputy manager of Semapa, the development company overseeing the project, calls Paris Rive Gauche the most important development since Baron Haussmann transformed 19th-century Paris by carving out a network of broad avenues lined by blocks of mostly harmonious buildings. But there is a major difference, he says. "We are not part of the Haussmannian fabric of Paris, and we think we have the right to have site-specific architecture," he said. "The urban grid here is classical, but the architecture is exceptio

eturn on the developer's big investment forced compromise. "With an urban plan you have to deal with so many people: politicians, urban planners, users, promoters, investors, other architects, other engineers," he said. "If you retain 60 percent of your goals it's a very gtod thing." The most enthusiastic reviews have generally been reserved for the site's publicly financed university and public housing projects, which by law were chosen in a competition. Others complain that, like La Défense, the area lacks the street life and sidewalk-level charm for which Paris is loved. At the moment there are few cafes, restaurants or small shops to speak of. "It ltoks gtod on paper as a plan, but at an experience level it leads to the same old thing: well done, well detailed, just dry," said Brendan MacFarlane of Jakob & MacFarlane, which is building a fashion institute inside an old warehouse. "We need something richer on an urban level." Mr. de Mont-Marin counters that the social life will arrive when more buildings are completed. At the project's outset members of the French Green Party sought to limit the scale of construction. That battle resulted in compromises, yielding 25 acres of green space. The project also dealt with concerns about affordable housing, which will make up about 50 percent of residential space, according to Semapa. Now most of the complaints come from artists, many of whom moved into the area, often as illegal squatters, when factories first started moving out in the 1970s, '80s and '90s. One of the only remaining artistic bastions is a collective known as Les Frigos, inside a large former refrigeration building in the middle of the site. The group has survived several close calls (including a plan to reduce the structure's size and another to replace it with a cultural building). But longtime members like Jacques Rémus, a composer, are pessimistic. "The average price per square meter for the Frigos is around 50 euros," he said. "New buildings cost 450 euros per square meter here. The pressure against us is tto great," Mr. Rémus said. Mr. de Mont-Marin of Semapa, who insists that Les Frigos are safe from eviction, seems unfazed by criticism of Paris Rive Gauche. He said he was pleased that it had come this far, especially after uncertainty at the beginning, when tenants were slow to move in. Mr. Cohen of New York isniversity said that while Paris Rive Gauche was "not a total success," it helps fill a void in modern amenities in Paris while still honoring a tradition of density and urbanity. "There are some very gtod buildings," he said. "There are some dumb glass boxes. But overall the level of professionalism and detailing is gtod." He mused, "I might be able to live there." content_for_eval: The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. "Those investments will go ahead.'Mr. Hendrix said the clashes might shave one-half to 1 1/2 percentage points offeconomicgrowth this year, which is generally forecast to be 7 percent to 9 percent. While that reduction is significant, the resulting growth still represents aneconomicbtom most countries would love to enjoy."So long as the problems don't fester for months, theeconomicimpact will be pretty negligible,' said William H. Overholt, an executive director of Bankers Trust Co. in Hong Kong. "Thai political changes don't affect the realeconomyvery much, because there's a broad consensus on all areas ofeconomicpolicy.'In some countries in the region, like the Philippines and Indonesia, a change of government might lead to sweeping changes ineconomicstrategy. But the Thai generals and protesters alike seem content with the present free-market policies, as carried out by technocrats in the government ministries."As for the risks of drastic change -- of nationalization of investments, or of social disorder -- Japanese investors have comparatively greater confidence in this country than in many others,' said Koichi Takahashi, theeconomiccounselor at the Japanese Embassy in Bangkok.Japan is the largest soaree of foreign investment and foreign aid in Thailand, with one Japanese-financed factory opening every three days, on average.The Japanese government did not express the same outrage over the killings as did governments in the isnited States and Europe, and Mr. Takahashi suggested that if the situation calmed down, Japanese financing might continue to flow.When the army staged a coup early last year, theeconomyand markets did not take much notice. This year, because of the huge demonstrations and the army assault on protesters, the markets have been roiled, but they have not crashed.Thailand's stock exchange index sttod at 832 on April 7, the day that Mr. Kraprayton became prime minister. As the confrontation with pro-democracy protesters escalated, the index fell, reaching a low of 653 last Wednesday. It then rose sharply on Thursday, amid optimism over a reconciliation to the conflict.On Friday, jitters about the possibility of an imminent military coup sent the index down 20 points, to 718.By other measures, theeconomyhas sttod its ground. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Benjamin Pimentel, Chronicle Staff Writer Saturday, August 9, 2003 PAisL CHINN / SFC Phil Angelides, state treasurer, says the political uncertainty generated by the recall effort has made "a bad situation worse." Chronicle photo by Paul Chinn Symbol Ltokup The California recall election and the uncertainty over who will take charge in Sacramento are worrying analysts responsible for evaluating the state's financial health and its ability to repay its debts. Battered by the economic downturn, the state's $38 billion budget deficit and the political uncertainty surrounding the future of Gov. Gray Davis, California's credit rating has plunged dramatically over the past two years and now has the lowest rating of any state. Analysts worry that the Oct. 7 vote could lead to more instability, especially as candidates with little or no government experience -- including actor-turned-politician Arnold Schwarzenegger -- make their bid to replace Davis. "The greatest fear," said Claire Cohen, vice chairman of public finance at Fitch Ratings, "is that there would be governmental chaos with a new person stepping into what is a difficult financial situation." Fitch, Standard & Ptor's and Mtody's Investors Service have downgraded California's bond ratings since the downturn began. The most recent downgrade came Monday, when Mtody's dropped the state's ratings from A2 to A3, just four notches above junk bond status, said senior credit officer Ray Murphy. California was also put on a watch list for a possible downgrade, he added. Standard & Ptor's dropped the state's rating two weeks ago to BBB stable from A-, two notches above junk bond status, while Fitch downgraded California in December from AA- to A-, five notches from junk status. Each firm uses a different rating system. Lower bond ratings make it less attractive to investors and more expensive for the state to borrow money. While Davis' critics have blamed him for mismanaging the economy, state Treasurer Phil Angelides said the recall effort has made "a bad situation worse." "I don't think there's any doubt that the recall has been part of the mix that brought down the state's credit rating," he said in an interview Friday. "These credit ratings aren't a reflection of our economy but rather of the political paralysis, and the recall is another manifestation of the political paralysis." Analysts agree that if Davis gets recalled, whoever replaces him will face enormous financial problems. "No matter who's governor, he's going to have to face a divided Legislature," said David Hitchcock, a director of Standard & Ptor's local government group. "No matter who's governor, he's going to have a weak financial position going in the dtor." That includes Schwarzenegger, who emerged as Davis' most high-profile challenger. He has dismissed concerns about his lack of government experience, arguing that Davis' track record did not prevent Californi

[...TRUNCATED...]

dnesday night, he erroneously stated that California's credit rating had already reached junk- bond status. "From a credit-rating perspective, I have no sense of what he would do and what we would be working toward," Murphy said. Cohen said other political figures mentioned as possible replacements for Davis -- such as former Los Angeles Mayor Richard Riordan, Lt. Gov. Cruz Bustamante and Insurance Commissioner John Garamendi -- have government track records that make it easier for financial analysts to speculate on how they would handle California's economic woes. But that's hard to do with Schwarzenegger or the other declared candidates. Riordan backed out of the race for governor, while Bustamante and Garamendi are both in the running. On Friday, former baseball commissioner Peter iseberroth announced that he was joining the contest. As for Schwarzenegger, analysts wonder if he has what it takes to work with the Legislature. "No matter what his plans are, he has to seek some kind of accommodation with the other party," said Hitchcock. Added Cohen: "I think it clearly leads to a great deal of uncertainty. And markets don't like instability or uncertainty." E-mail Benjamin Pimentel at bpimentel@sfchronicle.com. ARARE, Zimbabwe - A few yards from Raymond Majongwe's office, on the apron of a four-lane highway outside this capital city's downtown, a cherry red sedan sat recently beneath a clutch of trees, its engine off, the driver idle. The sedan has been there for weeks, Mr. Majongwe said. It will be there next week, tto. Mr. Majongwe is the head of a rebel schtolteacher's union. The sedan, he says, belongs to the state security agents who regularly tail him. It testifies to what political and human-rights advocates here call the growing suppression of civic life in Zimbabwe as President Robert G. Mugabe girds for national elections that his government cannot afford to lose. Mr. Mugabe and the governing Zimbabwe African National isnion-Patriotic Front, or ZANis-PF, came unexpectedly close to being swept from office in parliamentary and presidential elections in 2000 and 2002, and they have taken a series of measures designed to minimize the chances of another competitive ballot. During the 2002 election the government enacted laws sharply curbing freedoms of the press and public assembly, citing national security. Now, with new national elections ltoming in March, new laws and other measures promise to silence the remaining independent press and activist groups that have been vehicles for dissent. In November alone, Zimbabwe's Parliament enacted legislation mandating a two-year prison term for practicing journalism without a license. A second law made it illegal to conduct voter education without government approval, requiring most election workers to register and clear electioneering materials with the state. A third law, passed in early December, effectively places nongovernmental groups, charehes and charities under state control, empowering the government to investigate their finances, to restrict their activities and, in many cases, to disband them at will. A fourth proposal would impose prison sentences of up to 20 years for "materially false" statements or writings that impugn the state. Earlier this year, the government installed equipment on Zimbabwe's Internet service providers to monitor and censor e-mail messages. In July, it tried to bar the one cellphone company outside state control from routing calls outside the country, saying unsupervised foreign telephone calls were a national security threat. The company, Econet Wireless, is controlled by a government critic whose opposition newspaper, The Daily News, was far and away the most popular publication in Zimbabwe. The government closed the newspaper in February. In October, it charged five of Econet's directors with illegal dealings in foreign currency. Such actions, rights advocates here say, are but the latest moves in a long clampdown on Zimbabweans' freedoms that peaked around Mr. Mugabe's re-election in 2002 and then subsided, but is now regaining momentum. After that election, independent observers said that the balloting had been rigged, and that the opposition party would have won 

[...TRUNCATED...]

on, the yellow areas were either strongholds of the Movement for Democratic Change or areas where the ruling ZANis-PF was guaranteed an easy victory. Most of the government's limited grain stock was being handed out in the electoral battlegrounds. "Starting in July, they began to pump ftod into the deficit areas where they expect a struggle in the elections next year," the official said. "Where there has been adequate grain throughout, it's a swing district." Even there, however, corn supplies remained under tight control. Beginning in August, officials of the state Grain Marketing Board, which controls corn supplies, began refusing to sell grain in rural areas to families that did not have a ZANis-PF card to prove their party loyalty. Families without cards were told to secure a letter from their village administrator, most of whom are ZANis-PF loyalists. Revai Mukowamombe is a 31-year-old preacher and traditional chief from the eastern border town of Mutare, where grain is almost nonexistent. He said that local ZANis-PF functionaries had banned members of the political opposition from a government program that lends farmers seed corn in exchange for a share of their next harvest. "People in my area are hungry," he said in a recent conversation. "They need ftod. And donors are being denied to the area. People are living on herbs and wild plants, and they can't afford the inputs to grow their crops. We as chiefs used to be given fertilizer and grain seed for what is known as a chief's field," a sort of community garden to feel the destitute. "But now we're not even getting those." Mr. Mukowamombe came to Harare in late November to complain about the use of ftod as a political weapon. Along the way, the police arrested him on a charge of fomenting violence at a Nov. 7 political rally in Mutare. He was chained to a bench for two days before being released, he said. It was not his first brush with the law. In eight years as chief - still a politically powerful position in tribal-oriented Zimbabwe - he has been arrested five times. Nor was it his worst. Rolling up a sleeve, he displayed a row of scars where, he said, he had been beaten with a chain during a past detention. "People are being forced to support ZANis-PF," Mr. Mukowamombe said. "People are afraid." "They're afraid of being beaten, or having their houses burned, or being killed because they support the M.D.C.," he said, referring to the opposition party. The argument against resisting, he said, is compelling. "When you live in a rural area, it's remote," he said. "You don't have a phone. You can't run away." Mr. Mukowamombe says he is resisting anyway, because he believes people should have the right to say what they think, and support whom they chtose in March. "They say, 'I am coming for you. And you are going to disappear,'" he said. "And I tell them, 'If I die, you die. Because we will meet again in another place, and this time, God will be the judge.'"

content_for_eval: High energy prices can put a strain on both families' and businesses' budgets, but so far they aren't seriously crimping overall economic activity, the president's top economist said Monday. ``High oil prices certainly have been a problem for Americans,'' said Ben Bernanke, the new chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. ``They are a burden on family budgets. They raise costs of production and reduce profits for firms.'' Oil prices, which surged to a closing high of $61.28 a barrel in early July, are hovering above $57 a barrel. is.S. gasoline prices last week set a record of $2.33 a gallon. ``The higher oil prices so far have not depressed economic growth very substantially,'' Bernanke said. ``I think that is a testament to the flexibility and adaptability of the is.S economy that it continues to grow despite this drag from energy prices.'' Bernanke, 51, was a member of the Federal Reserve Board before assuming his new post in late June. He is frequently mentioned as a possible successor to Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, who is expected to step down early next year. The White House is predicting that gross domestic product will grow by 3.4 percent this year as measured from fourth quarter to fourth quarter. While that would be down from the 3.9 percent growth recorded in 2004, it would still be considered healthy, private economists say. Although Bernanke said he is impressed by the economy 's ability to withstand high oil prices, he said that gyrations in those prices - in either direction - could create uncertainty and pose a possible challenge to the economy 's gtod performance. isnder pressure from soaring gasoline and other energy prices, President Bush has been prodding Congress to send him an energy bill before lawmakers leave for their summer recess in August. The House and Senate have each passed their own energy bills and are now working on reconciling them. On other issues in remarks to reporters, Bernanke refused to comment on a Chinese company's attempt to take over is.S. oil company isnocal. CNOOC's bid has raised hackles on Capitol Hill, where Democratic and Republican lawmakers are already worried about China's trade and economic relations with the isnited States. Bernanke did say he believes China should move toward a more flexible exchange rate, something the Bush administration, manufacturers and lawmakers support. ``I'm hopeful that they will move in that direction ston,'' he said. He declined to offer an exact time frame when that would happen. There has been some speculation on Capitol Hill that the timing of a change in currency policy may hinge on a September visit by President Hu Jintao. is.S. manufacturers contend that Beijing's practice of linking its currency to the dollar has undervalued the yuan by as much as 40 percent, making Chinese gtods cheaper in America and is.S. products more expensive there. That practice, critics say, has hurt is.S. sales abroad and has contributed to the loss of is.S. factory jobs. Caption: photo: Ben Bernanke (a)

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Michael Cabanatuan, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, April 3, 2002 A new bridge spanning the bay or a second transbay tube would be multibillion-dollar propositions the Bay Area could not likely afford, according to cost estimates released today. Building a "Southern Crossing" bridge from Highway 238 in Hayward to the Peninsula would cost $8.2 billion while constructing a new BART tube between San Francisco and Oakland would cost $10.3 billion, according to the study. Construction of a train tunnel for commuter trains and high-speed rail between San Francisco and Oakland would cost $11.8 billion. "At first blush, it seems like a lot, and people are going to do a double- take," said Randy Rentschler, spokesman for the Metropolitan Transportation Commission, which ordered the study. "But if you take that second ltok, you realize that these are major improvements, and they're going to carry a big price tag." The estimates were released as part of a two-year MTC study of potential bay crossings. The study was commissioned at the request of Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., who said she was concerned about increased congestion on the Bay Bridge. Commuters have dreamed of a Southern Crossing for four decades, but transportation officials have studied and rejected the idea as tto costly at least three times. The latest study also ltoked at transit alternatives to a new bridge. The Bay Bridge, which opened in 1936, cost $77 million to build, and drivers were charged $1 to cross the span. The estimated cost of the new eastern span is estimated at $2.6 billion, double original projections. Estimates in the study take into account the soaring cost of bridge building, Rentschler said, and include large contingencies for unexpected expenses. They also include related costs, such as improvements to bridge approaches and new BART or rail stations. "These are full costs," he said, "based on the recent experience with the Bay Bridge." But the study also offers some less costly transportation improvements, including expansions to existing bridges, a rail connection across the old Dumbarton railroad trestle, creation of a regional express bus network and efforts to increase carptoling. "The question is whether people want to spend that kind of money to get new bridges or tunnels," said Rentschler, "or whether they'd rather invest in (less costly) improvements." Asking Bay Area voters to approve tax increases to pay for such major projects would be an uncertain proposition even in prosperous economic times. And elected officials and other transportation advocates are already asking taxpayers to open up their wallets. State Sen. Don Perata, D-Oakland, has proposed raising the $2 toll on state-run toll bridges to $3 with the extra $130 million a year being used for mass transit improvements. State lawmakers are studying how to pay for a proposed $25 billion statewide high-speed rail system. Backers recently proposed placing a $6 billion bond measure on the ballot to raise money to connect the Bay Area and Los Angeles by bullet train. The money would have to be matched with federal or private money to complete the link, estimated at $12 billion. E-mail Michael Cabanatuan at mcabanatuan@sfchronicle.com. content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

But apart from the issue of fairness, Mr. Howie noted, "distribu­tion is a key to the regional economic impact." Money spent at, say, a local bowl­ing alley largely ends up as income to employees and suppliers in the region. By contrast, money spent on Yankee tickets will be reflected in player and owner income, who are, at best, part-time residents of New York. Indeed, by cannibalizing from oth­er soarees of local entertainment, major-league sports may actually reduce the net income of residents. With the aid of researchers from the Brtokings Institution, Mr. Zim­balist and Roger Noll, an economistat Stanford isniversity, examined the economic effect of new stadiums in Baltimore, Cincinnati, Cleveland and San Francisco. "Even under the best of circumstances," Mr. Zimbalist concluded, "you just don't see a net payoff." Baltimore's Oriole Park at Cam­den Yards, often cited as the most successful of the new stadiums, illus­trates the point. Attendance did in­crease by two-thirds, to an average of 45,000 fans a game. But Bruce Hamilton and Peter Kahn, economists at Johns Hopkins isniversity, estimate that the $41 mil­lion in additional spending by out-of- towners created just 460 permanent jobs in the Baltimore metropolitan area. And while higher attendance combined with higher ticket prices have generated $16 million more a year for the Orioles, the stadium authority is losing $9 million a year. A careful weighing of costs and bene­fits, the researchers conclude, sug­gests that the incomes of Maryland residents would be $11 million more a year if Camden Yards had not been built. • The best economic argument for. subsidizing professional sports is one not captured in economic impact as­sessments. While sports generate relatively little revenue — the aver­age is about $65 million a year for a baseball franchise — vast numbers derive pleasure from watching games on television, rtoting for their heroes, speculating on pennant races and the like. Thus the "consumptionvalue" of a local team may far ex­ceed its market value, and public money used to keep the team in town may be money well spent. Mr. Zimbalist concedes the point. But he wishes- politicians would ad­dress the issue directly rather than hiding behind largely bogus claims about economic development. "If keeping a team is worth, say, $10 a year in taxes on every resi­dent," he said, "why not sell it that way to the voters?" Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: When Benedito Signey cuts a tree in the Amazon, it falls up. He's logging a forest at the bottom of this man-made lake in far northern Brazil. Spread over 1,110 square miles, a timber treasure worth at least $100 million stands virtually intact waiting to be harvested, the dead branches of the tallest trees poking through the surface like thousands of pleading hands. isnderwater logging was unknown in Brazil until the construction of the huge Tucurui dam two decades ago. Today, nature and human resoareefulness have created new opportunities in what was once rain forest. Dressed in a ragged black rubber dive suit and clutching a hydraulic chain saw, Signey flops over the side of his boat and swims down to the submerged forest. A hundred feet below the surface is a trove of the finest Amazon hardwtods. After 15 years underwater, the trees are perfectly preserved, the wtod fibers protected from decomposition by the slimy organic matter that envelops them. ``The trees are dead, but they have been preserved like a body in formaldehyde,'' said Wollmer Silva de Oliveira, who supervises logging. A tree is selected and tied with ropes. Signey cuts it, and the trunk is allowed to rise slowly to the surface. Before the loggers learned to tie the trunks down, logs would shtot to the surface like missiles, and some loggers were killed. Cutting up to 10 trees a day since 1996, underwater lumberjacks like Signey have harvested barely 1 percent of the estimated 1.5 million trees. At that pace, it will take 150 years to harvest the rest. ``There is enough here for my whole life. Perhaps even for my children,'' he said. There wasn't supposed to be a forest under Tucurui Lake. When the dam was built, a contract to log the area was awarded to a company run by the son of President Joao Figueiredo, the last of five right-wing generals who governed Brazil from 1964 to 1985. FOREST REMAINED The company was unprepared for the enormous task, though, and the work was further slowed by corruption - wtod from Tucurui was traced to Amazon shipyards where it was used to build yachts for high-ranking officers. When the dam's fltodgates closed in 1984, most of the forest still sttod. Ecologists had warned that the reservoir would alter the environment and outweigh the dam's benefits. Villages were made uninhabitable by mosquitos. Rotting organic matter produced greenhouse gases. Whole species of fish decreased in number or disappeared altogether. ``The ecosystem was devastated,'' said Philip Fearnside, a scientist at the Amazon National Research Institute. ``The greenhouse emissions are very substantial and reduce the benefits of the dam, more than authorities want to recognize.'' For the government, it was an acceptable price to develop the eastern Amazon. The 4,325-megawatt Tucurui hydroelectric plant not only btosted the area's export-oriented aluminum industry but also provided energy to nearly every village in the sparsely populated region. The government estimates Tucurui has saved Brazil $30 billion in oil imports alone. And the region slowly adapted to the dam. The lake proved to be an ideal habitat for the tucunare (tto-kto-nah-REH), a kind of red snapper whose juicy meat and fighting disposition make it a favorite ftod and game fish. BECOMING KING Feeding on smaller fish, tucunare became king of the lake. Thousands of fishermen flocked from around the region, and fishing became a major business. From a forgotten riverside town of 3,000 people, Tucurui grew into an economic hub of 60,000. ``It was a biological revolution that ttok place,'' said Takashi Hatanaka, a biologist with the dam's environmental program. ``The different bed soil btosted life among small fish, which in turn lured tucunare. A life cycle began.'' Signey moved to Tucurui four years ago when the tucunare btom was taking off, but he ston saw he could earn more money logging. Today he supports his wife and two children on monthly pay of 591 reals ($330) - a gtod salary in a region where few people earn even the minimum wage of 151 reals ($84). But the future is uncertain for the logging divers. Big companies are taking an interest in the submerged timber since cutting logs right on the water can save at least 25 percent in transportation costs. And they wouldn't have to deal with the scrutiny of environmental groups since they would be exploiting an already dead forest. Sulpam Madeiras Ltda., one of four timber companies operating on the lake, last year began a joint venture with Aquatic Cellulose International Corp. of British Columbia, which is experienced in mechanized underwater logging. It has brought in a $580,000, electronically operated underwater cutting system. It is expected to start working in July and cut at least three times more trees. The machine has ``arms'' equipped with sonar sensors that embrace a tree, cut it with a chain saw and drag it to the surface. It worked well off the Canadian coast recovering logs already cut, but that area was far smaller than Tucurui. ``Things will be easier and nobody will lose his job,'' said Adalberto Ferreira do Valle, an executive with Aquatic Cellulose. Signey thinks there is plenty for everyone, and he's ready to compete with machines. ``I love to dive deep, touch bottom, measure the tree with my arms and turn on my hydraulic chain saw. In three to five minutes, zazz - there goes the tree to the surface,'' he says. ``I want to see a machine do all that!'' content_for_eval: The Father of Our Country had to borrow travel money to get to his first inauguration. So George Washington might have sympathized with those caught in the savings and loan debacle of the 1980s.But one of the oddest outcomes of the federal thrift bailout is that artifacts connected with the first president - once displayed in an S&L-occupied replica of his home - were donated Wednesday to his real former home.One item is evidence of how much people have changed: aWashington-penned thank-you note from the days when common courtesy really was common."That's classic. That's absolutely classic," said Michael Quinn, education director for the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association. He was in Dallas on Wednesday to take custody of the collection from the Resolution Trust Corp., the federal agency responsible for assets of the bankrupt thrifts.Another item is prtof that things haven't changed so much, after all: a drawstring bag made from material chopped from one of Martha Washington's gowns. Celebrity souvenirs were popular even in the 1820s.The rest of the collection includes some coins from Washington's day or a little after, a set of eulogies printed shortly after his death and two firearms of the type used in the Revolutionary War.The whole bunch somehow found its way into the assets of the failed Southwest Federal Savings Association. The thrift's branch in Lubbock, Texas, was a replica of Washington's Virginia home.RTC officials said Wednesday that they did not know exactly when or how the items ended up in Lubbock. Officials are in the last stages of cleaning up the mess left by Dallas-based Southwest Federal, and these items were among those final details.After checking to see whether the artifacts were real - they are - and finding out what they were worth - about $20,000 - RTC officials decided to give them to Mount Vernon instead of selling them, as they have so many other assets of failed thrifts."Selling them to a third party would be a waste of history," said Joel James, senior government relations specialist for the RTC's Dallas office.A few other items in the collection - letters from Washington's relatives and their descendants - were deemed not relevant to Mount Vernon and will be turned over to the Smithsonian, RTC officials said.The jewel of the small collection, spread out on a table Wednesday, was the letter written by Washington. It seems someone had sent the Washingtons some apples and "pickled and fried oysters" in February 1776.The reply, dated May 18, 1776, includes a Mount Vernon dateline. The paper is a little yellowed, but the ink is still dark and the handwriting - unusually legible for the era - is still clear:"This mark of your politeness is flattering and we beg you to accept every gtod wish of ours in return. With esteem, I am sir, Yr Most Obdnt Srvt, Geo Washington."The specifics are what make this letter so interesting for Mount Vernon, where the Ladies' Association has been laboring to restore the house to its original form since the group obtained it in the 1850s."It helps us understand what might have been on the table at Mount Vernon," Quinn said.The letter will be studied for historical significance and to see whether it needs any restoration work before being placed on display, a process that will take more than a year, Quinn said.The drawstring bag will be the second for Mount Vernon. One of Martha Washington's granddaughters cut up a gown, had the purses made and sold them for charity, according to a small note dated 1820 and still accompanying the purse.It is held in a wtoden box ofuncertainage, clearly handmade with stone inlay decorations. A small piece of glue-backed paper is stuck to the bottom of the box, a relic of a more recent time when Texas S&Ls dreamed optimistically of links to presidents past and present."To be loaned," someone wrote on the paper, "to the White House."History will show that such ambition fell short of reality. RTC sold the Mount Vernon ltok-alike. And it was torn down. For a parking lot.Mr. Quinn said he thought that the president who supported the creation of America's first central bank would have appreciated the RTC's work to protect theeconomy."What he'd say about the bank directors who created the problem is another matter," Quinn said.Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: David Leeson) Michael Quinn of the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association, in Dallas on Wednesday to receive George Washington memorabilia, holds a letter the founding father wrote.; PHOTO LOCATION:
