Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ross Newhan, Los Angeles Times Saturday, April 1, 1995 (04-01) 04:00 PDT New York -- Major league baseball players made an unconditional offer to end their 232-day strike yesterday, but that may not get them back on the field. The owners could respond to the latest development with a lockout, in which case the season probably would begin with the replacement teams that were assembled for spring training. The players voted Wednesday to return to work if a federal judge granted the National Labor Relations Board's request for a temporary injunction. The injunction was granted yesterday by is.S. District Judge Sonia Sotomayor, upholding the board's claim of unfair labor practice by the owners and restoring all terms and conditions of the expired bargaining contract between players and owners. With the 1995 season scheduled to start tomorrow night with replacement players, owners will meet tomorrow afternton in Chicago to discuss their options and take a lockout vote. An exchange of negotiating proposals this week generated a degree of optimism in the protracted stalemate, but it is unlikely that there will be any attempt to resume negotiations before the vote tomorrow. isnion leader Donald Fehr, hopeful that the court ruling would be a turning point in the talks, said that ``given the nature of the dispute'' he would not be stunned if the owners implement a lockout. ``However, it would be an obyious indication they want to continue the dispute,'' he said. Owners have acknowledged a willingness to delay the season as long as three weeks to give major league players a chance to get ready if a settlement were reached. But they have repeatedly said they have no desire to play another season under terms of the previous economic system, unless that season is part of a long-term agreement in which a new system kicks in after the first year. 21 VOTES NEEDED FOR LOCKOisT A lockout is their primary weapon, but it is not certain they have the 21 votes they need to approve it. A lockout also carries enormous financial risks. If an administrative law judge ultimately finds the owners guilty of unfair labor practice in a hearing that begins May 22, owners could be liable for back pay, plus interest, for all major leaguers from the time the lockout was implemented. However, acting Commissioner Bud Selig has seldom put an issue to a vote without knowing he has the votes to get it through. ``The two sides still have to settle their disagreements at the. .. table,'' he said in a statement. ``The economic uncertainty caused by delays. .. could have further crippling effects on the industry. Representatives of the 28 clubs will meet to discuss legal and practical options now available and will certainly give the players a timely answer to their offer to return to the field.'' In the meantime, the is.S. Court of Appeals denied a request by management lawyers for an emergency stay of the injunction or

[...TRUNCATED...]

he owners avoided sanctions February 3 by agreeing to withdraw the implemented system and restore the terms and conditions of the expired agreement. Three days later, however, the owners' Player Relations Committee said it would serve as the exclusive bargaining agent for the 28 clubs -- the equivalent of a signing freeze -- and unilaterally eliminated three key provisions of the agreement the owners had agreed to restore: salary arbitration, free- agent bidding and the anti-collusion clause. The union brought new charges of unfair labor practice that the NLRB supported in its petition for an injunction that forces owners to restore those provisions. In a 35-page brief filed with the court this week, NLRB lawyers wrote that there is reasonable cause to believe that the owners' unilateral changes again violated the National Labor Relations Act. The injunction, they wrote, ``is necessary to prevent further harm to the collective bargaining process'' and ``to prevent further industrial unrest and strife.'' Sotomayor said the injunction will remain until there is a settlement, disposition of the NLRB case on unfair labor practice, or a court-approved declaration of impasse, at which time management can make unilateral changes in conditions that are normally mandatory subjects of collective bargaining. "The rapidly changing technology associated with HVAC equipment. Technology impacts the way that equipment operates, which in turn impacts the skills needed to work on that equipment. Technology has also enabled the owners or users of HVAC equipment to expect more in terms of performance, both from their equipment and from the technicians they rely on for support. And he takes his box cutter and he slits my throat, and he takes over the plane and he runs it into a building." (03-07) 04:00 PDT Islamabad, Pakistan -- Britain's foreign secretary urged Pakistan's feuding president and opposition leader to unite to focus on the "mortal threat" the country faces from Islamist militants, days after the danger was underscored by a bltody ambush on a visiting cricket team. David Miliband's remarks were the most direct appeal yet by a Western power for Pakistani President Ali Asif Zardari and opposition leader Nawaz Sharif to resolve their differences - and a sign London is taking their rift and its implications seriously. On Tuesday, militants armed with rocket launchers, hand grenades and assault rifles attacked Sri Lanka's cricket team and security detail, killing six police and a driver and wounding seven players and a coach. Pakistani police suspect local militants were likely responsible for assault in the eastern city of Lahore, an investigator said Friday, speaking on condition of anonymity because of the sensitive nature of the issue. Salahuddin Niazi, the officer in charge of the probe, refused to confirm or deny that, saying only that police were "on the right track." The assault bore similarities to November's three-day terrorist rampage in the Indian financial capital of Mumbai. The Pakistani group Lashkar-e-Taiba has been blamed for that attack, in which 164 people were killed. Many analysts have suspected the group - or an offshtot - carried out Tuesday's ambush. Miliband said it was "vital" that Zardari and Sharif "unite against the mortal threat that Pakistan faces, which is a threat from its internal enemies." Last month, Pakistan's Supreme Court issued a ruling that banned Sharif and his brother, Shahbaz, from contesting elections. The decision was widely see as being influenced by Zardari, who promptly dismissed the Shahbaz-led government in Pakistan's most populous province. Sharif is pledging to join large anti-government rallies scheduled for later this month. Many analysts are predicting months of turmoil, uncertainty and likely street violence - a combination the West does not want a year into the term of the country's first democratically elected government in a decade. Speaking to British radio, Miliband said the political deadlock, the economic crisis, and relentless militant violence emanating from close to the Afghan border represented a "grave situation" that is "getting worse" and hurting NATO and is.S. efforts to stabilize Afghanistan. content_for_eval: The rugged mountains of Lesotho were fortresses for tribal chiefs fleeing hostile armies, but offer little protection to modern leaders in this politically chaotic country. Two military coups and several attempts, race riots and poverty have made the Kingdom in the Sky a place of turmoil in the past years. Economic problems and the repeal of apartheid in South Africa, Lesotho's overpowering neighbor on all sides, raise even more questions about what lies ahead. Sympathetic foreign powers have donated millions to Lesotho. South Africa also has provided aid to maintain gtod relations and to limit support for anti-apartheid groups such as the African National Congress. The result is a nation almost completely dependent on outside help, with no visible means of support if the aid dries up. "Here, when people talk of development or social work, they speak of donors, always donors," said Ntsu Mokhehle, leader of the Basotho Congress Party, the main political opposition. "It seems we can't do anything without foreign assistance. We have been left without self-reliance." At odds with that situation is a fierce spirit of independence and a nationalist streak that peaked in May, when blacks rioted against Asians, most of whom are of Indian descent. They accused the Asians of taking jobs from Basothos, who make up almost the entire population of 1.7 million. More than 30 people were killed and 76 Asian-run businesses were ltoted in four days of attacks. Lesotho, which rises like a bubble in the middle of South Africa, is one of world's least-developed nations. Thatched-rtof huts of stone cling to steep slopes that have a golden tint in the dry season. In the dusty capital, Maseru, women wrapped in blankets against the mountain cold sell medicinal herbs on the street and turn curious eyes to the occasional white face. Chief Moshoeshoe led the Basotho people into the mountains in the early 19th Century to escape tribal wars ravaging southern and central Africa. Britain adopted the land as a protectorate in 1868 and granted it independence in 1966. After two kings and three governments in five years, Lesotho does not always keep up with the changes. A picture of King Moshoeshoe II, dethroned in October, hangs above a roster of the previous military government. Col. Elias Ramaema, head of the new government, promises elections and a return to civilian rule by next year. It is as uncertain as most things in Lesotho. Whoever takes power will inherit a beautiful but impoverished country. More than 90 percent of the people live in rural areas, but only 10 percent of the land is arable, and much of it has been severely eroded by overuse. More money can be made across the border. Nearly half the men cross the narrow Maseru Bridge to South Africa to work in gold mines or as domestic servants. The 130,000 miners are Lesotho's greatest soaree of income, but the South African mining industry is struggling and 70,000 Basothos could be laid off ston. content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma. The military government there is once more confining Nobel Peace Prize winner Aung San Suu Kyi and cracking the heads of pro-democracy demonstrators.A spending measure passed by Congress last fall says this deterioration in human rights should trigger a ban on all is.S. investments in Burma. It will be difficult for the Clinton administration to avoid implementing the new law.The ban could make it difficult for isnocal Corp. of El Segundo, Calif., to continue a natural gas project off Burma's coast. Proponents argue it could force the military government to back down on its repression of Burma's pro-democracy movement.Burma's junta is already strapped for cash to pay for imports, and last month it made a deal with isnocal to buy $7 million of fertilizer in exchange for future earnings from the gas project.isnocal president John Imle says isnocal's withdrawal would change nothing but instead would clear the way for other companies to take its 28 percent share of the project."Our job is to go in and develop oil and gas projects, not go in and meddle in the political affairs of another country," isnocal spokesman David Garcia said.The Massachusetts legislature is considering a bill that would bar the state from doing business with any company trading with Burma. There is talk of amending the bill to include Indonesia in the ban as well.The is.S. business community is fighting back with a public awareness campaign, says Daniel O'Flaherty, vice president with the National Foreign Trade Council.Mr. O'Flaherty says unilateral is.S.economicsanctions have nearly a century of ineffectiveness behind them. They cost is.S. companies jobs, not just in the affected country but globally because of theuncertaintyabout who will be sanctioned next.Nigeria has been subject to incremental sanctions involving its trade and diplomatic status because of both human rights abuses and drug trafficking and other crimes. So far, there's been no ban on investments.Mexico is mentioned as a possible target because of drug trafficking.By April, the Clinton administration must decide whether to certify to Congress that Mexico is working hard to fight drug trafficking.Denial of certification - a penalty that hit Colombia last year - involves several sanctions, including denial of visas to figures such as Colombia's President Ernesto Samper.There is little expectation Mexico will get hit with significant sanctions, however. As with China, most is.S. policy-makers have concluded Mexico is tto important to the is.S.economyto slap it with sanctions."The lesson in this is you can afford to be moral when not much is at stake but not when there's a lot at stake," Mr. O'Flaherty said. "It's a depressing conclusion."Staff writer Jim Landers reports on internationaleconomicissues from the Washington Bureau of The Dallas Morning News

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reuters Monday, February 27, 1995 Symbol Ltokup (02-27) 04:00 PDT Philadelphia -- Job transfers have become increasingly unattractive and difficult for employees, while growing more important to their companies, a new survey says. The survey, by the isniversity of Tennessee and Right Management Consultants Inc., found that relocations no longer mean a guaranteed promotion and often cause a decline in living standards, especially among the rapidly growing legions of two-career families. On the other hand, companies are increasingly relying on transfers to meet strategic goals such as improving productivity or cutting the size of the corporation. ``The flattening of company structures has forced some workers to accept transfers merely to stay employed, and greatly reduced promotional opportunities for others,'' said the study. ``Couples who have been through one or more relocations in which they not only made no economic gains but actually lost ground are feeling frustrated, cautious and skeptical about moving again,'' it said. Paul Wesman, a spokesman for Philadelphia-based Right Management, said ``some companies are acutely aware of this as a problem, and are beginning to deal with it. Others are beginning to feel the pain.'' The study surveyed more than 3,200 employees or their spouses at 101 companies in the isnited States, Canada and Europe. Among its findings were that 23 percent of those surveyed reported a decline in living standards after their moves. The number rose to 42 percent among two-career couples, who often reported difficulties in finding a job for the ``trailing spouse.'' On average, it ttok five to six months for the spouse to find a job, often with a pay cut, Wesman said. The study said dual-income families were consistently less favorable toward moving than single-income families, while their representation in the work force has increased rapidly. The share of two-income couples rose from 47 percent in 1989 to 59 percent in 1992, according to government data cited by the survey. It found 65 percent of those questioned were in two-income families. Besides the difficulties with spouses, 39 percent of those surveyed said they did not view their transfers as promotions. ``When you start removing rungs from the corporate ladder, you end up with fewer promotional opportunities, and more lateral movement,'' Wesman said. Many of the companies surveyed -- 63 percent -- reported moving people to minimize job losses in a downsizing, or to carry out mergers or restructurings. Such moves put an added strain on employees at a time when job uncertainties are already high, it said. Despite the difficulties in a modern job transfer, the study said employers have a big stake in making them work. Not only does each transfer cost a company about $45,000, but a failed transfer in which an employee quits the company is even more costly in terms of lost investment, training and experience-building in the employee, Wesman said. A shopper loading a TV into a cab outside a Circuit City in New York. Retailers are trying to spur demand for TVs by dropping prices. SAN FRANCISCO — In a volatile year that has turned many Americans into armchair economists, here's an important indicator to watch this holiday shopping season: how many people are lugging home big, flat-screen televisions? Shoppers comparing TVs on Friday at a Wal-Mart in Secaucus, N.J. Retailers use big discounts on televisions to lure consumers into stores. Customers in line inside a Circuit City in New York. The answer matters to more than just TV makers. Just as high-definition sets have become the hearth of the digital home, they are increasingly central to the fortunes of the consumer electronics industry and plenty of retailers. And there's reason for serious concern. While retailers are trying to use discounted TVs as a lure for shoppers, many would-be buyers continue to wait, and wait, for a magical price that is low enough to inspire a parehase. Others just have more pressing needs. "The question is whether I buy a TV or something more important," said David Lunsford, 62, who visited a Circuit City near here last week to shop for big-screen TVs. He would love to replace his aging rear-projection set, but he worried he needed to save money in case family members hit tough times. "I'm a stable provider. They may turn to me," said Mr. Lunsford, who works for the federal government. Americans are expected to spend $28 billion this year on TVs, making them the largest segment of the $173 billion electronics industry. So far about half of American households have made the jump to flat-panel screens, which started out as status symbols but are on their way to becoming standard household appliances. More people may chtose to upgrade this year because of the national switchover to digital broadcast signals coming Feb. 17. The change, which will mostly affect people who watch over-the-air signals on older sets, has generated a gtod amount of consumer confusion — which could be gtod for sales of new sets. TVs are also a gateway to a host of other products, like Blu-ray discs and their players, surround-sound audio systems, digital video recorders and cables. All of these factors have led electronics stores like Circuit City and Best Buy, and even less specialized chains like Sears, Wal-Mart and Office Depot, to put TVs front and center in their advertising recently, promoting them on the cover of Sunday circulars and on the home pages of Web sites. They are offering discounts — like 42-inch TVs for less than $700 and 32-inch sets for $450 — that come on top of recent steep price declines for the sets. For the industry, the feeling is that if retailers cannot get TVs to move, the holiday season could be a bleak one indeed. In that sense, the TV market offers a glimpse of the broader tensions this year between wary consumers on the one hand and retailers and manufacturers desperate to spur sales on the other. "The te

[...TRUNCATED...]

TVs with high-definition DVD players, or offering discount bundles on cables. They announced Black Friday deals weeks in advance, and some — like Best Buy — began sales events in the days before Thanksgiving, with heavy promotions on TVs. And yet consumers remain cautious, and manufacturers are nervous. Mr. Caldero of Ken Cranes said he had been in constant contact with electronics manufacturers, including TV makers, that were trying to gauge demand. "I've had more calls from vendors in the last few weeks than I've had in the last three years," he said. "They want to know what's going on, how's business, what's working and what's not." Just to be safe, TV makers have been shipping fewer sets — but they may not have pulled back fast enough. In the third quarter of this year, shipments to North American retailers of LCD TVs rose 21 percent from a year ago, and those of plasma TVs rose 20 percent, according to DisplaySearch, a market research firm. That is down from growth rates in the last two years that at times hit triple digits. Paul Semenza, an analyst with DisplaySearch, said that the downturn in sales of TVs did not hit until mid-October, so despite the slowdown in shipments the retailers and manufacturers were still facing a glut of sets. "Oversupply has just cascaded," Mr. Semenza said. As in other industries, that oversupply, and the uncertainty of consumer demand, has caused problems further back in the manufacturing and supply chain. In Taiwan and Korea where the LCD screens for the sets are made, prices are falling 5 to 9 percent a month, a "tremendous drop," said Andrew Abrams, an analyst with Avian Securities, which tracks the electronics industry. Such drops, he said, suggest that retailers have far tto much inventory, and he expects another round of price cuts. The question is, who will blink first, manufacturers and retailers, or consumers? Bruce Tripido, vice president for marketing at the TV maker Sharp, said adequate discounts were already in place. "We're not ltoking to shift gears from the offers we've planned," Mr. Tripido said. "The pricing is so compelling for this holiday selling season that it's to the point of irresistible." Yet resistance remains for many consumers, like Bayani Deluna Jr., 35, who sttod last week at a San Francisco-area Best Buy ltoking longingly at a 32-inch Sony television. Mr. Deluna, who worked as a parking valet until a few weeks ago when he went on disability, is waiting for the $600 price to drop. "If it comes down to $450, I'd buy it," he said. "And I'm sure the price is going to come down." Ashlee Vance and Claire Cain Miller contributed reporting. Ashlee Vance and Claire Cain Miller contributed reporting. content_for_eval: Bond Traders Remain Wary: Whipsawing Remembered By KENNETH N. GILPIN New York Times (1923-Current file); Jun 29, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. D9 CREPBT MARKETS Whipsawing Remembered By KENNETH N. GILPIN Instead of heaving a sigh of relief because a tumultuous second quarter is coming to an end, participants in the fixed-income markets are holding their breath over what could happen during the next three months. There is little doubt, however, about the short-term forecast, ac­cording to analysts: Bond prices, which traded in a very narrow range last week and closed little changed at the end of five trading sessions, will remain relatively stable. After that, the outltok gets muddier. The uncertainty about the direction of interest rates seems to stem as much from the memory of what hap­pened during the quarter as from worries about how a number of criti­cal events will play out over the next several weeks. Conditions have improved since early in the second quarter, when a declining dollar sent interest rates moving sharply upward. After rising to a yield of more than 9 percent in May, a stable and then rising dollar has brought down the yield on the Government's 30-year Treasury bond by more than 50 basis points, to a bid of 8.48 percent late Friday. However, the sharp, unexpected rate moves early in the quarter caused trading losses of as much as $1 billion for Wall Street firms and hit Japanese and European money man­agers hard. Traders are in no mtod to get whipsawed again. 'A Lot of Cash* on Sidelines "The market has a definite blah tone to it," said one Wall Street dealer in fixed-income securities. "There is a lot of cash on the sidelines, and peo­ple seem to be expecting something negative to happen. A lot of that feel­ing is left over from what happened in April and May." Milton W. Hudson, a Morgan Guar­anty Trust Company economist who recently returned from a trip to the Far East, said: "The Japanese in­vestment houses I talked to feel a lot better now that the dollar has stabil­ized. But everyone is still a little wary. The experience in April and May was very sobering for both do­mestic and international firms." That attitude was especially evi­dent during last week's Treasury re­funding, when the Government sold more than $26 billion worth of new two-, four- and seven-year notes. For the most part, market profes­sionals were disappointed with the auctions, largely because they failed to attract much retail demand from either American or foreign investors. Prices of those issues have soft­ened slightly since the sale. Late Friday the new 7% percent two-year note was bid at 99 21/32, to yield 7.495 percent. The 7% percent four-year notes were bid at 9913/16, to yield 7.894 percent. And the 8 per­cent seven-year notes were bid at 98 27.32, to yield 8.195 percent. For the foreseeable future, the for­eign-exchange value of the dollar will be the main influence on daily move­ments in interest rates. Developments in Washington But analysts said market partici­pants were also becoming wary of developments in Washington, such as a meeting of the Federal Open Mar­ket Committee next week, an impend­ing reduction in the debt ceiling on July 18 and continued wrangling be­tween Congress and the Administra­tion over the Federal budget. "The market is basically treading water, for fear of what could happen Economic Calendar Listed below are the scheduled dates for major economic indicators to be released this week. Monday New-Home Sales May Tuesday Inventory-Sales Ratio April Leading Indicators May Wednesday Construction Spending May Thursday isnemployment June Friday None "I think the long bond is going to rally to around the 8 Vi percent level over the next few weeks," said Ed­ward J. Hyman, vice chairman of C. J. Lawrence & Company, a New York investment house. "Bonds are trading off the dollar, and I am bull­ish about the dollar. Our trade ac­counts are turning a lot — $60 billion at an annual rate — and 1 think that will spark a dollar rally." The improvement is not likely to be long-lived, Mr. Hyman said, because "I think the economy and inflation are turning up, and bond yields will be heading toward 9.5 percent by early next year." content_for_eval: Analysts isncertain Whether 5-Year Rally Over or Just Stalled: Intervention Has Curbed Dollar Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); Mar 13, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) pg. OC C3 Analysts isncertain Whether 5-Year Rally Over or Just Stalled Intervention Has Curbed Dollar NEW YORK GB-What a differ­ence two weeks can make. On Peb. 26, the dollar was the undisputed king of the world's currencies. After rising for 16 out of 18 trading sessions, the dollar ap­peared unbeatable, setting records against the currencies of Britain, Canada, France and Italy, reaching year highs against the West German mark and Dutch guilder and climbing to a 10-year peak against the Swiss franc. Then the bubble burst, and the dollar has since retreated in ner­vous trading. But even after its tumble, the dollar still is almost 6% higher than when the year began and more than 85% above its levels at the start of the 1980s. It remains strong enough to be considered a major threat to the survival of is.S. farms and businesses that compete for sales in international markets. Analysts said Tuesday that the dollar's record-shattering advance was halted by the concerted sell-off of dollars by Western cen­tral banks between Feb. 27 and March 1. And the currency has been undermined since by a drop in American interest rates and new doubts about the strength of the is.S. economy, they said. No one is certain whether the dollar's drop is just a pause in a rally or if the currency finally has peaked after its powerful five-year ascent. But foreign exchange deal­ers said traders no longer view the dollar's rise as a sure thing. "Before intervention, we were in a one-way market, with the dollar rising every day," said Scott Par­dee, an executive vice president at the investment firm Discount Corp. of New York who once headed the currency trading operations of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. "The intervention worked because it created a two-way risk.. .. You don't know which way to go." That uncertainty was evident Tuesday, with the dollar plunging in early trading and then rebound­ing later in the day. "It's hard to know whether the dollar is going down or up; it depends upon when you ask," Par­dee quipped. The tide began turning on Feb. 26 when Paul A. Volcker, chairman of the Federal Reserve Board, told a House banking subcommittee that central bank intervention is a useful ttol and questioned if it had been practiced "forcefully enough" to have an impact on currency markets. Intervention against the dollar involves government sales of dol­lars on the open market, adding to supplies of dollars and spurring demand for the foreign currency being parehased with dollars. Volcker's comments alone were enough to jolt foreign exchange markets. Within a matter of min­utes, the value of a West German mark rebounded to 29.3 cents from cents, which had been its lowest level since 1971. That may not seem like a big shift but, to a trader who had completed a fairly routine $5-mil­lion parehase of marks as Volcker began testifying, that move had reduced the value of the invest­ment by more than $100,000 by the time the Fed chairman had left the hearing rtom. In the next three days, interven­tion by European central banks and the Fed dumped an estimated $4 billion on world foreign exchanges. "It stopped the rise of the dollar," Pardee said. In one striking change of course Please see CisRRENCY, Page 9 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. CisRRENCY Continued from Page 3 in the past two weeks, the British pound recovered from an all-time low of just under $1.04 to close Tuesday in London at $ 1.085. In the meantime, the isnited States is walking a very narrow line, said Mory Ogata, senior vice president and treasurer of isnion Bank of Los Angeles. ' "The government does not want an out-and-out running away of the dollar because of the plight of exporters," he said. "But, at the same time, the government needs a continued inflow of capital" to meet American demand for credit and does not want the dollar to collapse. The dollar's explosive gains have hit farmers and manufacturers hard, leading to a record $123-bil­lion trade deficit last year. When the dollar rises, the export price climbs for is.S. farm products and manufactured gtods ranging from baseball bats to computers. "The sharp appreciation of the dollar since 1980 has cost 2 million jobs," said a study released Tues­day by Data Resoarees Inc., a private consulting firm in Lexing­ton, Mass. But the rising dollar has not been all bad. With prices of imports falling, is.S. manufacturers also have had to hold down costs and improve productivity, helping tame infla­tion from the double-digit pace of 1979-80. And the surge in shipments of gtods to the isnited States has helped lift foreign economies out of a slump. At the same time, the flow of foreign funds into dollar-denomi­nated investments has helped the government finance record deficits and still allow interest rates to fall. Pardee said foreign investors are still holding on to their large is.S. investment portfolios but have started taking some defensive ac­tions to reduce the risk, such as buying options that would enable them to sell dollars at a guaranteed exchange rate at a specific date in the future. And where is the dollar headed? "The speculative tide has started to turn against the dollar," said Lawrence Kreicher, an interna­tional economist at Irving Trust Co. in New York. "There really seems to be a fundamental reappraisal of the dollar over the last few days." A combination of low inflation and relatively high interest rates, coupled with the political stability of the isnited States, has been a major attraction for foreign inves­tors.

content_for_eval: ALVADOR, Brazil - In the depressed Liberdade area, the man everyone calls "Vovo" sits on a rough bench outside his cluttered office, declaring that Brazil is a racist country and that black people should wake up and push for political power.In the trendy Pelourinho district, Billy Arquimimo sits in a spacious rtom decorated with photos of well-known black figures, advocating the instilling of African pride and a gradual approach to blacks takingeconomicand political control.The two men share a cause - the struggle to eradicate racism and improve the lot of blacks in Bahia, the state with Brazil's largest concentration of people of African descent. But the men also exemplify the differences, ambivalence,uncertaintyand, most of all, lack of unity among Bahia's nonwhites.This confusion, plus poverty, inferior education and a ptor self-image among blacks, have combined with neglect by the white elite to effectively disenfranchise Afro-Brazilians in Bahia as well as Brazil as a whole.Eighty percent of Bahia's population is of African descent. But the governor of the state is white, as is the mayor of Salvador, the state capital. Most City Council members are white; so are a majority of influential members of the judiciary and academia.Mr. Arquimimo says education and consciousness-raising will cause change."We try to give information to children and our people to promote the idea of being African instead of just having white behavior, which is what the government here wants," said Mr. Arquimimo, international relations director for the cultural group Olodum, which means God of Gods in the African language Yoruba."We fight with music and strong lyrics, not with bltod," he added. "Black people want results right now; they don't want to give time for results."Antonio C. dos Santos Vovo isn't very interested in waiting for results. Head of another cultural group called Ile Aiye ("House of Negro" in Yoruba), he believes that black people need to take control of their lives and their community."There's a campaign that black is beautiful, but they (blacks) don't believe it," Vovo said. "They have all this stuff that white people have told them for years about black being bad and so they themselves started creating all these (racial) categories to escape being black."isnlike Olodum, which is a kind of rainbow coalition, Ile Aiye has only black members. Vovo's followers must meet a true "blackness test.""Through this project I want to make people acknowledge they're black," Vovo said. "People who want to participate who are moreno (light-skinned) will be checked to find out if they're really black."Both Mr. Arquimimo and Vovo are aware of the statistics and both blame conditioning and lack of education for the situation. Both Olodum and Ile Aiye are trying to appeal to blacks through cultural enlightenment. Each began as an "Afro" Carnival group designed to re-imbue the annual street extravaganza with its original African flavor.Both groups claim inspiration from Martin Luther King, Marcus Garvey, Nelson Mandela, Malcolm X, slain South African hero Steve Biko and others.But Ile Aiye remains a grass-rtots organization with one full-time staffer - Vovo. The dreadlocked activist spends much of his time on the phone, pushing his foundation and trying to move into the political arena. He once ran for City Council but lost. He says blacks were manipulated to vote against him, even in Liberdade.Olodum has become a kind of cultural conglomerate - with high-tech office equipment, a large staff and a stream of supplicants waiting up to an hour to see Olodum leader Joao Jorge Rodrigues. The group's headquarters are in the middle ofPelourinho, which is in the midst of an urban renewal effort by the government. Trendy shops have sprouted in the area - even though not many blacks can afford their wares.Mr. Arquimimo credits both Olodum and Ile Aiye with advancing the black cause, but says his group believes in being inclusionary."There are (other) black movements, but many of them are jealous of Olodum's success," he said. "They say Olodum isn't black anymore, because whites come to our events. That's because we don't want to be ourselves a racist organization. We want all people who are concerned."Not so Vovo. He does not directly criticize Olodum, but does distance himself from that group's philosophy."I am considered a racist," he said. "I consider myself a racist. But I want to show blacks can perform, have abilities; that they can succeed on their own."In the meantime, the two groups go their own ways, each staging a rival Carnival celebration, each claiming thousands of devotees, neither expecting any substantive political change any time ston."Blacks keep voting for whites because they're offered ftod, gtod buildings, fixing roads. .. but people don't realize they're not being offered political power," Vovo said."In 10 years? I cannot see a gtod solution," Mr. Arquimimo said sadly.

content_for_eval: The stock market is locked in a trading range that's tighter than a drill sergeant's bedsheets.It seems every time the Dow Jones industrial average moves much above 10,500, the bears quickly put a stop to it. Three times this year, the Dow has breached that mark only to be turned back.And now, after a fine late summer rally that finds the Dow at 10,318.16, most investors are bracing for yet another retreat. And who could blame them?"It's hard to feel gtod about this market," said James Stack, editor of InvesTech Research. "There are enough fearful headlines about oil prices, terrorist threats andeconomicuncertaintyto fill a scrapbtok."But he said if investors would put aside their emotions for a moment, they would find some compelling evidence to indicate the market is healthier than they think. The odds are at least as gtod that the market will break higher as that it will continue to drift lower or, worse, collapse, he said."Most investors think that stock market collapses drop out of thin air," he said. "Nothing could be further from the truth. But separating emotions from analysis is one of the toughest tasks facing both the novice and experienced investor."The state of theeconomy, earnings and interest rates obyiously undergird the stock market, and at the moment all of those areas appear respectable, Mr. Stack said.Broadly speaking, theeconomy, while not as vibrant as some would like, still grew at a 2.8 percent clip in the second quarter and is expected to expand by 3.5 percent to 4 percent in the third and fourth quarters. And corporate earnings are expected to grow by about 15 percent in the third quarter over the same period last year, and about 16 percent in the fourth quarter.That's off the 20 percent-plus pace of the previous four quarters, but still robust enough to fuel a rising stock market.As corporate earnings have continued to churn higher, stock prices have basically stalled. That's actually a plus for the market, because it improves stock valuations.The most common measure of stock valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio, has gotten lower, and the lower the P/E ratio, generally the better value the stock."The last six months has been very constructive for the market, because it allowed the earnings to catch up with stock prices," said Kevin Marder, chief market strategist at Ladenburg Thalmann Asset Management.Better news neededThe bears counter that this market will remain "range bound" until theeconomycreates more jobs, oil prices stabilize and terrorism fears abate. About 150,000 new jobs are needed every month just to keep up with the new job seekers.The August jobs report showed 144,000 new jobs, a vast improvement over July's32,000 figure, but still a little skimpy."This market will remain range bound until job creation moves above the 150,000 trend line," said Jack Caffrey, equity strategist at JPMorgan Private Bank.Crude oil prices have dropped from almost $50 a barrel to about $44, but that's still high enough to act as a "tax" on consumers, he said. The Olympic Games and two political conventions passed without terrorist attacks, but many people still fear another attack on is.S. soil before the November elections.Henry Hermann, chief investment officer at Waddell & Reed, a mutual fund company, said those issues have to continue to improve for the market to move higher."For the market to go up, you need things to be less bad," he said. "That may be where we are now. I do know that we have to have better job creation to have sustainableeconomicgrowth."Also, interest rates spiked earlier this year, and virtually every bull market in history died under the weight of higher interest rates. While it's true that rates have risen, the yield on the benchmark 10-year is.S. Treasury bond has retreated from almost 5 percent in June to 4.1 percent currently. And that's historically still very low."The monetary climate remains favorable," Mr. Stack said. "We don't see the interest rate climate that would cause a bear market."Market breadthIf there is a stock market equivalent of a canary in the coal mine, it is market breadth. Simply put, this is the difference between the number of stocks that advance each day and the number that decline.Long before a bear market approaches, investors begin to get nervous and more selective in the stocks they buy. Fewer stocks advance relative to decliners; in other words, market breadth narrows.This narrowing of the market occurred before the great crash of 1929; before the bear market of 1969-70; before the 1987 crash; and more recently before the collapse of the stock market bubble in 2000.Today, market breadth is very healthy, Mr. Stack said."The increasing nervousness or selectivity that would normally precede a bear market is nowhere to be found," he said. "Market breadth is not forecasting a bear market in the next six months."Neither this indicator nor any other indicator is ftolprtof. However, it should at least be given as much currency as the fear and emotionalism that seem to be influencing many investors, he said.And finally, the so-called seasonality of the market should be considered. Historically, stocks generally perform well from November to January. In fact, that has become such a widely held phenomenon that in recent years buying has picked up in September and October."I think the market probably hit its yearly low in August, and I believe we will see a year-end rally develop," Mr. Marder said.E-mail bdeener@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (FILE 2003/Getty Images) Traders. content_for_eval: The Nicaraguan government is taking some capitalist cues on how to unsnarl thousands of property disputes inherited from the socialist Sandinistas. It's planning to pay off claimants with shares in state- owned companies, long-term bonds and other assets -- ranging from restaurants to rusting Soviet bloc tractors. At stake are claims for a multitude of houses, farms, factories and stores. Also at stake may be the overall fate of the economy. Foreign investors have treated Nicaragua as a pariah because of what they view as a lack of respect for property rights. The government is expected to announce what one minister calls a "very imaginative" indemnity program this week. With state coffers nearly empty, authorities are considering financing thousands of indemnity claims by selling off minority interests in such entities as the state's telephone company (Telcor), electric utility (INE) and water works (INAA). "It's a double hit," said Foreign Ctoperation Minister Erwin Kruger. "First, we're going to settle the properties in conflict, and second, we're going to deepen and accelerate the privatization process." Rumors of the plan are racing around the capital, piquing interest among private bankers and rousing skepticism among confiscated property owners. Proponents of the plan say the Sandinista Front's once-dogged opposition to privatization efforts have steadily eroded. "To talk about selling Telcor to the private sector a few months ago would have been unthinkable," Central Bank President Silvio De Franco said. "Certain tabtos. .. are being broken." The Sandinistas began property seizures immediately after taking power in 1979, snapping up the land holdings of the Somoza family dynasty -- fully 20 percent of the country's farmland. Expropriations continued throughout 11 years of Sandinista rule, culminating in a massive giveaway to party supporters before the Front turned over power in April 1990. Some 1.27 million acres of land, scores of ranches and hundreds of luxury homes suddenly changed hands. For the government of Violeta Chamorro, it has been a crippling headache. Multiple claims popped up for some of the nation's most productive land. Factories suddenly belonged to workers. And thousands of exiles, many with needed investment capital, refused to return until Sandinista occupants were evicted from their homes. So far, the government has acted slowly. Critics accuse officials of being in cahtots with the Sandinistas to block a full-scale roll back of the expropriations. The government counters that the morass of conflicting claims is difficult to sort out. Today, with Washington withholding foreign aid and pressing for fast action, "the government is extremely interested in resolving the property problem in a very short term," De Franco said. The government's proposal calls for creation of a National Compensation Fund that would be granted shares of companies, like Telcor, that may be put on the market. A government panel would determine if claimants deserve compensation. If they do, they would be offered either long-term bonds or certificates issued by the Fund. They could be held for investment or traded like shares of a company stock. The proposal coincides with efforts by a group of investors to establish a stock market in Nicaragua, the only Central American nation without one. Analysts describe the plan to sell off a portion of Telcor, the most lucrative and perhaps the best-run state-owned company, as a pillar of the government's strategy. Telcor is in the midst of a $150 million investment program that will allow Nicaragua to expand from 49,000 phone lines to 150,000 lines by the end of 1993, said deputy director Rolando Rivas. Nicaragua now has fewer phone lines per capita than any country in the hemisphere, including Haiti. Considering Telcor's possibilities for growth, Rivas said the company could bring $250 million to $400 million in the private market. The compensation fund may also hold shares in such enterprises as the Victoria brewery, the Montelimar beach resort, a cement factory and the water and electric utilities, officials said. All are partly or wholly owned by the government. Those who want no part of the compensation fund may be indemnified in assets such as state-owned vehicles that are no longer needed or government-held interests in movie theaters, restaurants or other businesses, officials said. Of some 5,000 property claims now pending before the government, "I imagine that perhaps about 2,500. .. will have to be compensated," said presidential chief of staff Antonio Lacayo. The remainder will either have property returned or told that their claims are unjustified. For owners who lost assets to the Sandinistas, a big question remains: How much will they get in compensation? Lacayo said the 92-seat National Assembly will determine whether owners get all or only a percentage of the appraised value of confiscated property. The government reportedly figures that between $200 million and $500 million will take care of all outstanding claims. The indemnity program is expected to trigger a slew of complaints from individuals who feel they aren't getting fairly compensated. Sandinista labor sectors are also expected to fight the plan. But government ministers and Sandinista political leaders wager that efforts within the National Assembly to completely roll back Sandinista land giveaways and reforms will abate. Some 160,000 Nicaraguan families received land during the Sandinista years but were given no formal title, according to the Interamerican Development Bank. Their situation remains uncertain. "The advantage of this solution is that it offers satisfaction to the claimants," said Sandinista leader Jaime Wheelock, and "it definitely takes away pressure" to rescind broader land reforms that helped many ptorer families. Get ready for the new Middle East. It's not Israeli statesman Shimon Peres' vision of region-wide peace that ushers in economic prosperity for all, say analysts. The region is a place beset today by bloated bureaucracies, not-ready-for-Internet regimes, vast numbers of underemployed, scheming militants, oil prices falling off the table and ltoming leadership succession that scares the dickens out of is.S. policymakers. The succession of King Abdullah of Jordan from the late King Hussein, who was buried Monday, is likely to be the easiest and most trouble-free among an expected new generation of leaders. And how is the isnited States reacting? With great fear, say analysts. That was well illustrated by what President Clinton ttok with him to the funeral: a promise of $300 million in funding - triple what the administration had set aside for Jordan before Hussein's death. ``Everyone in the world is infiltrating their agents and putting forth their concerns in Jordan,'' said Geoffrey Aronson, director of the Foundation for Middle East Peace, a Washington think tank. ``The game has begun. Let us not pretend that we are giving this kid a chance because we are at the front of the line. There is a full-court press in Jordan to make sure that the isnited States' investment in the region does not go down the drain.'' is.S. interests are transparent: moving ahead the Israeli-Palestinian peace process, preserving access to cheap Persian Gulf oil and isolating Iraq. Much rides on the May 17 elections in Israel, where Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu faces a stiff challenge from two former army generals, the centrist Ehud Barak and center-right candidate Yitzhak Mordechai. The outcome could signal new directions in the Israeli-Palestinian peace talks, or they could signal more of the same, a standstill. Economy hits is.S. interests In coming years, during which new leaders may replace aging ones in Saudi Arabia, Syria, Egypt, the Palestinian Authority and perhaps Iraq, the is.S. regional interests may not be so easily protected, say analysts who have long studied the region. Blame bad economics, first and foremost. The price of crude oil - the Persian Gulf states' lifebltod - has fallen 55 percent in two years. In Saudi Arabia, the regional power where oil accounts for 75 percent of budget revenues, this means that the entitlement system of free housing, education and health care and guaranteed employment may be a thing of the past. Even more astounding, Saudi Arabia is talking about imposing taxes. This has not gone over well with the 20 million Saudis, especially when stories continue to leak about the wealthy habits of the 5,000 to 6,000 members of the royal family. ``If the economics goes south seriously, it will create tensions and resentment, particularly if services are cut,'' said Mohammed Akacem, a Saudi expert and professor at Metro State College of Denver. ``If the resentment leads to instability, people could question the legitimacy of the ruling

[...TRUNCATED...]

 quarter-century of nationalized oil power. This turnabout, while greatly exciting oil companies and diplomats, may also serve to raise new internal tensions about the is.S. role in the kingdom, say analysts. Already, two bomb attacks in recent years have killed 26 people at is.S. military installations, and terror financier Osama bin Laden has called for holy war against American targets in large part due to the is.S. military presence in Saudi Arabia. Elsewhere, Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, 70, who with the death of King Hussein becomes America's closest ally in the region, stubbornly refuses to appoint a vice president who could be grtomed as his replacement. Two new faces near Arafat Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat, 69, thought to be suffering from Parkinson's disease, has likewise declined to raise the profile of any in his ranks, although senior is.S. officials noted the presence of two top security officials on Arafat's trip to Washington last week. The two, Jibril Rajoub and Mohammad Dahlan, may not entirely supplant new political leaders but they could gain much more power in a post-Arafat government, said a National Security Council official, who spoke only on the condition of anonymity. The official said Dahlan, in particular, ``has shown himself to be savvy from a security and a political point of view.'' Sons being prepared for rule In the region's three dictatorial regimes - Libya, Syria and Iraq - fathers are grtoming sons. Iraq's Saddam Hussein's two sons, Qusay and isday, already control security forces. Syria's Hafez Assad, who suffers from heart trouble and diabetes, is preparing his only surviving son, Bashir, an ophthalmologist who is set to take over the security apparatus this month. Libya's Moammar Gadhafi sent his son, Saadi, to King Hussein's funeral, raising speculation that the son may be in line to succeed him. But even if these new leaders do take over without major incident - and few believe that will happen - they will assume control of societies that are falling farther behind the technological edge of the global economy. The Arab world accounts for only 0.3 percent of Internet users around the globe, according to one recent estimate. Syrians don't dream of Internet access; they wish for a fax line, which still requires multiple, hard-to-get permits. Iraq's civil society is decimated. isnemployment in the Gaza Strip, where the average fertility rate is 7.57 children born per woman, registers at around 80 percent. And, said Kenneth Stein, professor of Middle East history at Emory isniversity in Atlanta, the people of the Persian Gulf have to be muttering to themselves now. ``If I were a Saudi or a Kuwaiti, I want to know, `how did we waste all our money?' Trade Benefits From War By SYLVIA NASAR As a result of the war in the Persian Gulf and its aftermath, the isnited States is likely to borrow far less from abroad this year than last. Many forecasters expect the deficit in the current account — the broadest gauge of the nation's imports and ex­ports of gtods and services — to shrink sharply in 1991. "We'll see a remarkable improve­ment," said David Rolley, an econo­mist at DRI/McGraw Hill, an eco­nomic consulting firm. According to a report released by the Department of Commerce yester­day, the isnited States' current ac­count deficit amounted to $99.3 billion in 1990, down from $110 billion in 1989. The $99.3 billion figure is the smallest gap since 1984. Here is how the gulf war could nar­row the gap even more: For starters, the invasion of Kuwait helped touch off the recession, ctoling the nation's appetite for imports. Then, the allied victory caused crude prices to plunge, slashing the bill for imported oil. • $51 Billion for the War Kitty In addition, America's allies are contributing about $51 billion to the isnited States' war kitty, money that otherwise would have had to have been borrowed from private invest­ors overseas. Finally, postwar rebuilding in the Middle East will increase business for American construction compa­nies and equipment producers. isnited States arms makers are also expected to benefit as countries re­stock their arsenals. In fact, if the allies anted up the bulk of their share of war costs right away, analysts said, the isnited States could become a net foreign lender, at least for a month or two, for the first time in a decade. Borrowing less abroad, even tem­porarily, helps the isnited States be­cause a smaller current account defi­cit relieves downward pressure on the dollar and upward pressure on in­terest rates. That could be important at a time when isnited States assets are losing some of their allure be­cause investors can now harvest higher returns in, say, Europe. "Any reduction in the current ac­count deficit is positive in a financial context," says Robert Giordano, di­rector of economic research at Gold­man, Sachs & Company. "It will help to limit the buildup of is.S. assets in foreign hands and therefore helps stabilize the dollar and interest rates." The dollar's recent recovery, for example, has handed the Federal Re­serve Board more leeway to fight the recession by reducing the threat of inflation. "The current account col­lapse gives the Federal Reserve a lot more wiggle rtom," said Robert Gay, an economist at Morgan Stanley. The effects of the war on the na­tion's foreign borrowing are certainly not permanent, and DRI and other forecasters expect the current ac­count to swell again in a year or two. The nation's current account gap is.S. Trade Is Beneficiary Of Gulf War Continued From First Business Page have an impact. For example, the dol­lar's slide last year should give isnited States exports a lift in 1993. By the same token, the dent made by last fall's budget-cutting deal on the Federal deficit will show through a few years from now. "With fewer demands on resoarees by the is.S. Government, the isnited States wouldn't have to import as many re­soarees from abroad,'' said William Cline, senior fellow at the Institute for International Economics. Compelling Arithmetic While long-term trends are highly uncertain, the arithmetic for this year seems compelling. Total allied pledges amount to more than $51 bil­lion, just $4.3 billion of which had ar­rived by the end of the year. Federal officials seem reasonably confident, at this point, that the payments will in fact be made. According to yesterday's Com­merce Department report, last fall's allied contributions were more than offset by $7.1 billion in American asssistance to Egypt and Israel. "What matters is that six or seven months ago, everyone thought the war was purely a is.S. burden," said Maria Ramirez, president of Maria Ramirez Capital Consultants. Legislation that brought Americans the future trade accord with Mexico sailed through Congress six years ago, rating barely a blip on the media or public radar screens. But today, opponents and supporters of fast-track trade legislation to negotiate new global trade deals had unsheathed their swords even before President Clinton's bill was delivered to Capitol Hill last Tuesday. The battle about fast track promises to be both swift and stormy, marking a continuation of four years of rancorous debate among Americans about trade. What is different about this fight is that no specific trade accord is yet on the table. Pitted against an umbrella organization of business groups favoring fast track is a coalition of labor, environmental, family farm, human rights and consumer groups opposed to past free trade agreements. isnder fast track, a legislative device first used by former President Nixon in 1974, Congress takes an up or down vote but cannot make any amendments on trade accords. Supporters argue that few countries would be willing to negotiate with the isnited States if such a deal could be amended. The Business Roundtable, the organization of chief executives of the biggest is.S. corporations that spearheaded the campaign to win approval of the North American Free Trade Agreement in 1993 and the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade in 1994-1995, already has fired up its ``American Leads on Trade'' drive for fast track. In July, these captains of American industry sent out an appeal to companies to fund the fight, suggesting contributions of $100,000, $50,000 or $25,000. ``We believe we need to raise a minimum of $3 million in order to insure that the voice of the business community is heard,'' said the letter from the Business Roundtable, dated July 24. ``We need your immediate financial assistance.'' The business coalition spent $10 million on its isSA*NAFTA campaign in 1993 and around $2 million on its GATT effort the next year. But no real battle was waged for the 1991 fast track. What is so different in this year's battle is the presence of an invigorated labor movement, better versed on what unions see as the dangers of free trade negotiations stripped of any provisions to include labor and environmental guarantees. Radios and televisions already are blaring AFL-CIO spots in a $1 million campaign directed at voters in 13 districts of undecided legislators, including two in Florida and the state of California. Coming off a victorious strike against isnited Parcel Service, the Teamsters have prepared for battle since the spring. The Teamsters have distributed cellular telephones to their members to make calls to congressional offices during work breaks or lunch hour. The isnited Auto Workers have prepared a video. Labor's failure to mount a stronger campaign during the muted 1991 debate about fast-track authority given to former President Bush to negotiate NAFTA was one reason the bill was passed and signed without generating stories in t

[...TRUNCATED...]

there's work to be done to garner a bipartisan majority of Congress to pass the legislation.'' The Business Roundtable letter warned corporations that ``reports from Capitol Hill by congressional allies indicate that the prospects for enactment of fast-track authority this year are uncertain .'' The letter outlined a campaign to include ``aggressive use of 1-800 number to generate congressional communications from company employees, suppliers and constituents'' and a media campaign in advertising and ``aggressive use of earned media.'' Public opinion polls show that while Americans generally favor free trade in the abstract, the majority feel that specific trade pacts like NAFTA have not been gtod deals for the isnited States. Business leaders are confident the fast-track bill will be approved. ``It's gotten off to a slow start, but it's gotten off to a sure start,'' said William T. Pryce, vice president of Washington operations at the Council of the Americas, a business organization that supports free trade. ``They've come up with something that is passable.'' Rocking the House Passage in the House of Representatives will be the most difficult. The bill needs 219 votes for approval, and congressional watchers believe Clinton has 120 Republicans on his side. But Clinton will have to scramble for support in his own party. ``If they have 30 [Democrats] leaning with them, I'll walk on my hands to the White House,'' said Lori Wallach, director of Public Citizen's Global Trade Watch. House Democratic leader Richard Gephardt has declared his opposition to Clinton's bill, calling it ``a retreat to the policies of the past.'' Gephardt's agreement to support Bush's fast track bill in 1991, bringing with him 30 Democratic votes, was the key to passage of fast track that year. In opposing the latest legislation, Gephardt insisted that is.S. workers and the environment must be treated the same as copyrights and investments, whose protection is negotiated in trade accords. Former Democratic representative from Florida, James Bacchus, now a judge at the WTO appellate body that rules on international trade disputes, insisted in a speech in Miami last week that the rest of the world will get ahead of the isnited States without trade negotiations. ``The people of America need to be told by Congress why it is we are negotiating trade agreements,'' Bacchus said at the Miami Herald Americas Conference. ``Trade leads to prosperity, prosperity leads to peace.'' But the opposition dismisses the view that without trade pacts, the is.S. economy will suffer. ``Three or four years have gone by, and we haven't had fast track and our exports are going great guns, according to [is.S. Trade Representative] Charlene Barshefsky,'' said Thea Lee, spokeswoman for the AFL-CIO. ``People in the isnited States are unhappy with how we have done trade policy in the past, they want it done differently.'' Lewin, Tamar New York Times (1923-Currentfile); Feb 19, 2001; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. A8 In an isncertain Climate, Philanthropy Is Slowing Many Foundations Report Decline in Assets By TAMAR LEWIN Philanthropy, one of the nation's most spectacular growth industries for the last decade, is feeling the pinch of the market turnaround, sev­eral indicators suggest. In a survey of the nation's largest foundations, to be published this week in The Chronicle of Philanthro­py, 79 of the 142 foundations included said they expected their- giving to remain flat or drop this year — and slightly more than half said their assets had shrunk over the last year. And there are scattered hints of troubles throughout the charitable world: some nonprofit organizations, like the Children's Aid Society, have had a substantial drop in donors in that time. Others, particularly those depending on dot-com fortunes, found that stock gains they expected were evaporating. In Texas, where the Austin Entrepreneurs Founda­tion holds stock from start-up com­panies, only 2 of the 12 companies that were expected to go public last year actually did so, while several corporate supporters went out of business. Over the second half of last year, there was a 2.7 percent decline in the Philanthropic Giving Index, a kind of consumer-confidence index com­piled by Indiana isniversity's Center on Philanthropy, based on surveys of executives of nonprofit organiza­tions. In recent years charitable giving has reached new highs, and many organizations, from the American Heart Association to the American Friends Service Committee to the Museum of Modem Art in New York, say they have not yet felt any sign of the weakened economy.. But the decline in foundation as­sets may herald widespread trouble. Foundation giving almost tripled over the last decade, to $20 billion — support that is especially crucial to nonprofit organizations in lean eco­nomic times, when individual and corporate donations dry up. But several prominent foundations lost a substantial share of their asset value over the last year, the Chroni­cle survey said. With the shakeout in technology stocks, for example, the assets of the David and Lucile Pack­. ard Foundation, in Los Altos, Calif. — invested almost entirely in the stock of Hewlett-Packard and its spinoff, Agilent — dropped 25 percent, to $9.8 billion. But at least for this year, Packard is not cutting its grant budget, said George Vera, the treasurer. "If there is a prolonged bear market, we'd have to make some changes," he said, "but what we hope is that over time we will even out the peaks and valleys." Packard was not the only high- tech foundation to be hit hard last year: the assets of the Paul G. Allen Charitable Foundation dropped 39 percent, to' $58 million. Other foundations where assets declined by more than 10 percent last year include the Annenberg 

[...TRUNCATED...]

hilip Coltoff, executive director of the Children's Aid Society. "We haven't suffered a dollar loss yet, but there was a defi­nite drop-off, about 12 percent, in the number of donors over the last year. We get 60 percent of our contribu­tions in December and the first week of January, and that was where the big hurt was. When you have a drop­off in donors, it doesn't bode well for the future. We're now doing budget planning for the next fiscal year,- and we're thinking unrestricted giving may be down 5 percent, but we're After a decade of growth, the giving industry begins to see signs of trouble. hoping to make up for it in the giving to specific programs." At America's Second Harvest in Chicago, a spokeswoman, Sue Hofer, said that while giving levels were "on track" last year, a few donors told the ftod bank that they were postponing their year-end gifts be­cause of market conditions. Even groups that enjoyed in­creased giving last year remained alert to signs of a downturn. "We did very well last year," said Andrew Ackerman, executive director of the Children's Museum of Manhattan. "The only place we're beginning to notice any concern is in selling tables for our spring benefit April 29. One company that usually gives said they're not sure about buying a table because they're announcing layoffs." And many pledges based on soar­ing stock values have become, at best, tentative. On March 16, at the annual Wash­ington Business Philanthropy sum­mit, Michael J. Saylor, the chief ex­ecutive of the Virginia software com­pany MicroStrategy, announced with great fanfare that he was pledging $100 million to create a free online university. Four days later, the com­pany restated its earnings for the previous two years and the stock plunged, from a high of $333 a share to around $10. So for the time being, a spokesman said, Mr. Saylor is "very focused on MicroStrategy." Mr. Saylor said re­cently that he remained committed to the project, and planned to sell off his stock over the next few years and finance the university. The relationship between philan­thropy and economic circumstances has always been a close one. In the 1975 recession, for example, founda­tion giving dropped 28 percent, and it did not recover for a decade. BY THE NisMBERS The Giving Tree Wilts a Bit ■The nation's wealthiest foundations. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carla Marinucci, Chronicle Political Writer Sunday, January 27, 2008 California, now it's your turn. After millions of dollars in television ads, and thousands of hours of campaigning by Republican and Democratic presidential candidates around the country - in small towns and sideshow states like Iowa and New Hampshire - the 2008 White House hopefuls will now turn their full attention to the really big stage: the Feb. 5 "Super Duper" Tuesday primary states, including California. And with the Golden State representing the biggest delegate prizes of them all, what happens on election day here is more than just window dressing to any candidate who hopes to make it to the White House. Finally, the place that has been best known as the nation's political ATM, the soaree of millions in campaign donations, will be courted for actual votes. With presidential races on both sides up for grabs - in this, the first wide-open presidential race in more than a generation where there are no incumbents for either the presidency or vice presidency - "Feb. 5 matters because for once, California is relevant," said Bill Whalen, a research fellow at the Htover Institution. "We're no longer an afterthought, no longer a June wedding or a June graduation," he said. "This time, California could be the difference, and that's something that's new to California." The leading Democratic presidential candidates - New York Sen. Hillary Rodham Clinton, Illinois Sen. Barack Obama and former North Carolina Sen. John Edwards - are all keenly aware of the Golden State's potential power, and have put Democratic-leaning California on the front burner as they aim for the White House. And the Republicans - Arizona Sen. John McCain, former Massachusetts Gov. Mitt Romney, former New York Mayor Rudy Giuliani, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee and Texas Rep. Ron Paul - have also set their sights on the delegate-rich state. Former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson dropped out of the race last week. Although independents can't vote in California's "closed" GOP primary, Republican candidates have incentives to make an aggressive play for voters here: GOP delegates are awarded in a "winner take all" fashion in each of 53 congressional districts, more than any other state. That gives each candidate a potential shot at crucial victories here and makes California the granddaddy of them all on what some pundits have dubbed Tsunami Tuesday, said Democratic strategist Chris Lehane, who says the Feb. 5 contest here comes with particular drama this year. "It's the state where none of the campaigns have a specific relationship, so it's up for grabs. And no matter how you slice it, California is the big prize: a state that demographically comes close to representing what the country is," with its varied mix that includes 20 percent Latinos, Asians and African Americans, he said. "It's the economic driver, the ideas generator, the entertai

[...TRUNCATED...]

 vote by absentee ballot in the weeks before the election. But Californians, besides picking a presidential candidate, will be deciding on seven ballot measures, including one on legislative term limits, another on funding for community colleges and four on Indian gaming. Mark Baldassare, who heads the Public Policy Institute of California, said that recent polls have shown that California's 15.5 million registered voters are showing "an unprecedented level of attention to news about the presidential primary," with a whopping 9 in 10 likely voters saying "they're closely following this election." "Both on the Democratic and the Republican side, there's a huge amount of interest - and uncertainty," he said. "There are a lot of important issues at stake, and voters are telling us that Iraq, the economy, health care and the direction of the housing market are all things they care about." And it ltoks, increasingly, like voter turnout this time around could top the 2005 special election, because "this is another one of those elections where people feel their vote counts," Baldassare said. "They're thinking this is going to be history, and they want to be part of it." E-mail Carla Marinucci at cmarinucci@sfchronicle.com. We had nothing to sell," he said when asked why he left his farm. "Many others have left the village. If there's rain, I'll go back."He came to Fez three years ago, and normal rainfall has yet to return. It was a hot, hungry morning when Maria Altiaga finally crossed the line into desperate poverty. Her nine children were clamoring for ftod. Her husband couldn't find construction work. Prices were skyrocketing. Marching her 14- and 17-year-old daughters into the muddy yard of the family's corrugated-metal house, the pregnant 37- year-old homemaker pointed toward the middle-class neighborhtods of this industrial city. "I said, 'If you don't find work, ask people for ftod or clothing,'" she recalled. "I was so worried, I was crying. I called to God, help them!" Throughout oil-rich Venezuela, a crisis is pushing people to the brink. Thousands are being laid off. Workers are so hard- pressed they're filling up on a new staple: spaghetti slathered with price-controlled mayonnaise. If ftod shortages develop, as expected, many fear a social explosion that could prompt Venezuela's third coup attempt in three years. "It's the most serious problem we have in the hemisphere," said one is.S. official in Washington. "Venezuela is going downhill." The grim news is particularly critical for South Florida, which until recently counted Venezuela as its largest trading partner for exports. "We used to say Miami was going to be Venezuela's 21st state. Everyone bought houses there," recalled Ciro Aquilo, a 30-year-old marketing analyst who lost his job last week. Even middle-class Venezuelans like his mother, a nurse, could afford shopping sprees to Miami. "Now, she doesn't even go to Colombia," he said. How bad is Venezuela's economic decline? A leading economist, Gustavo Marquez, predicted in an interview that the economy will shrink by 8 to 10 percent this year. That's not just tough times. That's the is.S. contraction at the height of the Great Depression that started in 1929. Signs of decline are everywhere. Some office buildings are shutting down their elevators to save electricity. The government is so broke that the telephone company is threatening to shut off its lines in two weeks for nonpayment. "Nobody I know has ever see a worse situation in this country than we have now," said a leading Venezuelan businessman. Venezuela's economic problems began in the 1980s with the drop in the price of oil. It was a stunning blow to a nation that had used petro-dollars to build a modern urban society that was the envy of Latin America. But it was only the beginning. This past January, one of the country's principal banks, Banco Latino, collapsed. Seven more quickly followed. The government, already criticized in recent years for living beyond its means, bailed them out. The estimated cost: $4.7 billion, more than half its 1994 budget. As government spending mounted, interest rates shot above 80 percent. Inflation began to soar above last year's 46 percent. isncertain about the policies of left-center President Rafael Caldera, Venezuelans rushed to buy is.S. dollars.
