"After two years of chaos, four presidents, three administrations, corruption, coup d'etats, and elections. .. there's a sense that finally somebody is ruling the country effectively," said Juan Carlos Navarro, a political scientist. However, he added, "What's going to happen to that popularity when things are no longer available in the supermarkets?" At the moment, Caldera has the firm support of senior military officers. But there's no telling what could happen in the event of ftod riots. In only a few years, Venezuela has gone from being one of the continent's most stable democracies to one of its most unstable. That's due in large part to rising dissatisfaction with the country's two main political parties -- Accion Democratica and the Social Christians, or COPEI. They kept the peace for decades through negotiation and a division of state spoils. And now, said Argenis Martinez, editor of the daily El Nacional, "There is profound criticism of the political parties, and a sense the parties are instruments of corruption." Both are now in the opposition, out of power. The economic crisis has not yet curbed the comfortable lifestyle of the rich. Flights to Miami are still full. A popular is.S.-themed restaurant in Caracas, Weekends, continues to attract crowds -- even if it has switched its focus from imported whiskey to domestic beer. But Marquez estimates real incomes will decline on average 10 to 15 percent this year. Some people will suffer much more. Maria Altiaga's daughters, who first went to beg in January, now do so two or three times a week. Her husband has found only occasional work. Last month, Maria lost her 10th child just a week before it was due. "There were days in which we had practically nothing. I had only coffee," she said, tears welling in her eyes. "Since I had no ftod, I lost the baby." * Venezuela is South Florida's main trading partner. It is also a key soaree of foreign investment and tourism for the region. * More than $1.50 of every $10 worth of gtods shipped through South Florida's airports and seaports goes to or comes from Venezuela. * Last year, South Florida's trade with Venezuela totaled $2.6 billion, down 12 percent from the year before. content_for_eval: Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Richard Fisher said Monday he probably wouldn't support further easing of is.S. monetary policy to jolt the falteringeconomy.Fisher again called on Congress and the Obama administration to craft fiscal and regulatory policies to help jumpstarteconomicgrowth."If I believe further accommodation or some jujitsu on the yield curve will do the trick and ignite sustainable aggregate demand, I will support it," he said. "But the bar for that action remains very high for me until the fiscal authorities do their job, just as we have done ours."Fisher's comments came before a two-day meeting next week of the Fed committee that sets short-term interest rates. This year, Fisher is a voting member of the committee, which is headed by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke.Bernanke has said that the Fed will consider a range of options at its next policy meeting Sept. 20-21. Some economists have said the Fed must take further steps to drive down long-term interest rates and help theeconomyavoid another recession.The Fed has held short-term interest rates at virtually zero for nearly three years. It said last month thateconomicconditions were likely to warrant "exceptionally low" interest rates through mid-2013, in a statement that drew dissent from Fisher and two other committee members.Last week, Chicago Fed President Charles Evans said the Fed should go even further and consider "very significant amounts of policy accommodation," even if that increases the risk that the inflation rate could go above the long-term goal of 2 percent.Fisher said he shared his colleagues' concerns about the weak job market but said tolerating a higher inflation rate might hurt more than it helped."I come from the practical side. I think it's important to take into account the consequences of the theoretical argument," he told reporters after giving a speech to the National Association for Business Economics, or NABE, at its annual meeting in Dallas."We also have to consider how severe the backlash would be from those that don't want their income or meager savings eroded further by price increases."Separately, the NABE announced Monday that a panel of 52 forecasters had sharply reduced its expectations for is.S.economicgrowth.The is.S.economyis expected to grow 1.7 percent this year, according to the NABE outltok for September, down from the 2.8 percent expansion predicted in May.Panelists expect growth of 2.3 percent next year, down from the May projection of 3.2 percent, said Gene Huang, chief economist at FedEx Corp. and president-elect of NABE."A wide variety of factors were see as restraining growth, including low consumer and business confidence,uncertaintyabout futureeconomicpolicies, a tepid housing market, and financial headwinds caused by tight credit conditions and balance sheet restructuring," Huang said. Abstract: Several South Miami-Dade cities envision an aesthetically pleasing is.S. 1, but the ltoming promise of transmission poles along the route could stymie plans. BY HOWARD COHEN hcohen@MiamiHerald.com Once a barren, gray concrete ribbon slicing through several neighborhtods of neglect, South Florida's long embattled is.S. 1 could become a road of, if not beauty, at least a picturesque passageway to take drivers from downtown Miami to the Keys. Signs of improvement are already abundant. The nonprofit TREEmendous Miami has planted more than 1,000 trees in public rights of way, including where Interstate 95 spills onto is.S. 1. Veterans Wayside Park in Pinecrest fronts is.S. 1 -- also known as South Dixie Highway -- with its lake, fountain and plentiful foliage. And the east side of the highway in Coconut Grove and Coral Gables, once marred by decaying wtoden homes, shacks and run-down apartments -- disparagingly referred to as "concrete monsters" by civic leaders in the 1970s and '80s -- finally gave way to pastel blue and yellow Arquitectonica designs in the early 1990s after decades of debate. Meanwhile, an unloved stretch of faded motels on the Coral Gables side of is.S. 1 were bulldozed and transformed into tonier Mediterranean style offices. But now residents and local government officials worry that their ongoing long-range plans for a further aesthetically improved highway could be destroyed if Florida Power & Light is allowed to install four giant transmission lines along part of is.S. 1. Residents and city leaders are asking FPL to bury the lines underground. They said the buried lines also would help during hurricanes. But FPL said installing giant poles is much cheaper. "We are not opposed to underground," FPL spokesman Mayco Villafaña said. "About one-third of our distribution system is underground. isndergrounding transmission lines, however, pose some challenges. isnderground installation of transmission lines is relatively new, involves many engineering and construction factors not associated with overhead installation and would be exponentially more expensive." Overhead power lines cost about $1.5 million to $2.5 million per mile. isnderground ranges from $13.3 million to $18.5 million per mile, Villafaña said. But others say the cost of repairs and lost business outweighs this expense. The proposal calls for two 500-kilovolt lines and one 230-kV line in western Miami-Dade. The 230-kV line in eastern Miami-Dade would run along 18 miles of is.S. 1. FPL said these lines -- on concrete poles towering from 85- to 105-feet -- are needed as part of the utility's plan to build two new nuclear reactors by 2020 to meet growing energy demands. Gov. Charlie Crist and his cabinet aren't expected to decide on the matter until next year. Residents and the utility are at odds over the future, partly because everything remains so far out in the future. content_for_eval: Abstract: A sharp increase in unemployment in September was just the latest indication that the economy is falling into recession. BY KEVIN G. HALL khall@mcclatchydc.com WASHINGTON -- The strongest sign yet that the is.S. economy is in recession came in a Labor Department report Friday that employers had cut 159,000 jobs in September, twice as many as the average over the previous eight months. That was the worst monthly job-loss report since March 2003. Combined with a stream of earlier data from declining factory orders to auto sales, it left analysts with no doubt that the economy is sinking fast. "Previous monthly job losses averaged about 75,000 from January through August, so the September report shows clear evidence that we have taken another step down into a deepening recession," said Stuart Hoffman, chief economist for the PNC Financial Services Group. "The employment report is just the latest in a series of indicators showing that the economy was deteriorating rapidly as the third quarter progressed," Nigel Gault, chief is.S. economist for forecaster Global Insight, said in a note to investors. "These include weak retail sales, weak auto sales, declining durable-gtods orders, and. .. the economy was on the way down even before the latest tightening in the credit crunch." October's job losses are sure to be worse given the widening financial crisis, which has banks unwilling to lend to many businesses and even to one another. Despite the job losses, the unemployment rate held steady at 6.1 percent in September because tens of thousands of workers dropped out of the active labor force. The only sectors that increased jobs in September were healthcare and mining, adding 17,000 and 8,000, respectively. The biggest losers were manufacturing, which dropped 51,000 jobs last month, and retail, which shed another 40,000 jobs, reflecting consumers' reluctance to spend. Construction employment fell by 35,000 jobs; that appears to be the start of a troubling trend, since the sector is especially vulnerable to the credit crunch, as contractors depend on credit to keep long-term projects operational. "All types of construction shed workers in September, following an uptick in nonresidential hiring in August," said Ken Simonson, chief economist for Associated General Contractors of America, the trade group for developers and big construction firms. "Another ominous sign is that architectural and engineering services employment -- a harbinger of demand for future construction -- rose until recently, but stalled this summer and fell in September." Anecdotal evidence is piling up that businesses are laying off workers because they can't access financing amid the banking turmoil. Simonson's group publishes a digest in which members from far and wide share what's happening without divulging their names. One contractor in Massachusetts wrote that "within the last two weeks we have had a $1.2 million restaurant project and a $5.8 million hotel project put on hold, which for us is basically canceled. Not fun. I am sure we are not alone." Walsh Construction, in Portland, Ore., saw a bank suddenly pull the plug on an $11.5 million building project. "The owner is seeking funding from a different bank and anticipates a six-eight week delay," the company said. On Thursday, California Treasurer Bill Lockyer said the credit crunch threatened the ability of the nation's most populous state to issue bonds to build highways and carry out other public works projects. Republican Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger said Friday that he would try to sell $7 billion in state bonds ston, and that if the freeze in the credit markets didn't thaw he might ask the federal government for a loan of that amount. "State governments from California to Maine have been shut out of the bond market, while developers have had bank credit windows slammed shut on their fingers as they reached for their loans," Simonson said. Commerce Secretary Carlos Gutierrez, in an interview with McClatchy, expressed disappointment in the numbers and expected the credit crisis to continue to hit employers hard. "There are already small businesses that are having trouble," he said. He voiced hope that the Bush administration's controversial $700 billion banking-rescue package, which cleared Congress on Friday, may help ease the uncertainty that's hanging over the financial sector. Like hundreds of other tiny, isolated settlements in northeastern Napo Province, where devastating March 5-6 earthquakes were centered, Puerto Libre has suddenly become an island in the dense forested jungle that surrounds what remains of Ecuador's main oil pipeline and the highway that ran alongside it. The muddy, green village escaped relatively unscathed from the earthquakes that President Leon Febres Cordero has estimated left 1,000 people dead or missing. But for Puerto Libre and villages like it, the most terrifying, and perhaps the most dangerous, phase is just starting to be felt. Ten days after the disaster, which many Ecuadoreans are calling the worst in their history, officials are beginning to grasp the challenge that relief to Puerto Libre and other communities in the area will mean in the months ahead. In Blanca Arteaga's store, one of two here, only about two inches of rice are left at the bottom of a worn plastic trash bin. There no candles or kerosene, few beans and almost no sugar. Nor is there much demand for what ftod remains on sale. Few villagers seem to have more than a month's worth of savings. And the only place to barter a piece of meat, sack of coffee or a half gram of panned gold is a muddy, treacherous 25-mile walk away. "Now there's no work and no one to even buy a chicken, so who's going to buy ftod?" the storekeeper moans to a visitor flown in by army helicopter with a load of emergency supplies. "We're caged in here." Puerto Libre has always been isolated, a place so distant that its residents ask strangers not what city or country they are from, but what family. isntil the quakes unleashed landslides that filled and then fltoded the Quijos and Aguarico Rivers, Puerto Libre residents reached the outside world on Saturdays in two small canoes that motored down an Aguarico tributary to a bridge just a short walk down the road from Lumbaqui, a village a few hundred souls larger. Now, the once crystalline waters of the tributary have disappeared. Even if one could navigate the thick, brown surface that remains, there is no more bridge and only a badly damaged stretch of road. Six other bridges in the area also were destroyed. And if Puerto Libre residents reached Lumbaqui, they would find that their half grams of gold -- a couple of days' work in the river if gtod fortune is firmly on one's side -- are no longer valued at 1,500 sucres -- about $10 -- in trade. Now they are bringing about half that, 850 sucres worth of whatever is left at the Lumbaqui general store. When an army aviation helicopter first landed in Puerto Libre 10 days ago on a reconnaissance flight, it was swarmed by people begging to be flown out, residents said. None were. Puerto Libre, after all, is comparatively well off, military officers and government disaster workers say. At least one of the villages set in the valleys between the Quijos and Aguarico Rivers and the low, eastern slopes of the Andes simply disappeared under thunderous landslides 

[...TRUNCATED...]

answer came when the much stronger quake "just threw us on the ground." In the instant that the schtolhouse went dark, Moya and other survivors said they were out the dtor and clambering up the hillside. The schtol mistress yelled out that "the tube (the pipeline) was on fire. "I ltoked back and the river was on fire," Moya said. "It was like the biggest hell you have ever see." As the pipeline blazed away, Maria Luz Arellano, 33, her husband, and their infant daughter ran up the hillside dodging earth and rock that cascaded down into the river. "We lost everything, even our identity cards," she said. Days later, the list of 48 who died among San Carlos' 150- odd residents would carry only about a dozen last names: whole families were buried together as they slept. Only a few of the survivors were able to say just how they got out. But Humberto Santillana, a frail farmer of 60, answered the question immediately: "With God's permission." After two days frantically waving flags from the side of the newly carved mountain above their buried village, many San Carlos residents were picked up by helicopter and flown to refuge in Santa Rosa. Military officials said that while 1,500 quake victims had been evacuated from the area, the rescue efforts were delayed by ptor weather. Even on a gtod day, the relief effort seems ptorly organized and insufficient. Military and government relief officials frankly admit that in an area where at least a few thousand people live on isolated plots far from the nearest neighbor, they are uncertain how many may have died or might still need assistance. With helicopters, fuel and time all in short supply, the officials say they have no alternative but to concentrate on hard hit hamlets and villages along the rivers. The residents of Puerto Libre, comparatively deluged with their third airlift of ftod since the quake, have yet to receive sugar, salt, rice or beans. The packages of tuna, sardines, crackers and flour went quickly. On Sunday, the helicopter load included canned gtods and boxes of animal crackers, clothing in random sizes and a bulging sack of large-size plastic soccer cleats. "We don't order the stuff, we just deliver it," one army officer explained about the load. Of some 75,000 residents of Napo Province, perhaps 1,500 are missing, 5,000 are in "critical" situations and another 18,000 are stranded without access to supplies, estimated Col. Ramiro Silva, commander of the army brigade at Lago Agrio. Yet only three less-than-full C130 cargo planes are flying each day into Lago Agrio, the main distribution point. And even that amount of aid appeared likely to pile up with only a limited number of flights by the three French Super Puma helicopters assigned to ferry supplies into the devastated area. Silva said the airlift would be required for months, and possibly beyond the time it takes construction workers to complete 15 miles of another road into Lago Agrio from the southwest.

In isncertain Times, the Kitchen Takes Stock: Amid isncertainty, Kitchen Takes Stock By ROBIN POGREBIN New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 13, 1995; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Cll In isncertain Times, the Kitchen Takes Stock By ROBIN POGREBiN Just as near-death experiences prompt people to take stock and make resolutions, so the Kitchen, the experimental performing-arts cen­ter on West 19th Street in Chelsea, has been forced to do some soul­searching of its own. The last few years have been pre­carious for this experimental institu­tion, where Philip Glass, Laurie An­derson and Eric Bogosian, among many other avant-garde luminaries, had their beginnings. It is by now a familiar story for smaller arts spaces: government financing has shrunk, as have corporate contribu­tions, while audiences are dwindling. Even a slight rise in ticket prices hasn't helped much. In the face of a mortgage of more than $1 million on its space, the Kitchen has been frighteningly close to going under. So now, as it embarks on its 24th season, the Kitchen has initiated a series of decidedly mainstream measures^ The first was to form a joint chairmanship, to be shared by Mr. Glass, the composer and long­time Kitchen supporter, and Robert Soros, son of the philanthropist George Soros, who has been involved with the Kitchen as a board member and financial supporter for the last several years. The hope is that Mr. Glass's name and personality, combined with Mr. Soros's business connections and ex­pertise, will find a way not only to pay off the mortgage but also allow the Kitchen to endure as a place where artists can experiment and develop their work before supportive audiences. "Robert's primary mission is to lead the board in the fund-raising campaign and in stabilizing the insti­tution for the next 20 to 40 years," said Lauren Amazeen, who is enter­ing her fifth season as the Kitchen's executive director. In addition, the Kitchen — which has an operating budget of about $800,000 — plans to expand its board, headed by its president, Caroline Stone, from 23 members to 30, bring­ing in more people from the corpo­rate and philanthropic worlds with strong contacts to potential contribu­tors, as well as a commitment to the arts. This year, each board member has pledged to donate or raise $5,000 to $10,000. The board has also started a stra­tegic planning committee, and is prodding existing committees to more effectively address such spe- To save itself, is a performance space going mainstream? cific concerns as fund raising and upkeep of the Kitchen's three-story building, a former 19th-century ice house in Chelsea that contains a large black box performance space and second-fltor cafe theater. "We have to build a better fund­raising base for the organization," Mr. Soros said. "We've been living hand to mouth for a number of years, and it's been extraordinarily diffi­cult." To some extent, the Kitchen long ago reconciled itself to bei

[...TRUNCATED...]

's identity as a place for emerging artists to take chances issimply not up for consideration. ' "We can't separate young work from controversial work," Mr. Glass said. "If we do that, we cut creativity off at its very rtot." After all, Mr. Glass pointed out, there was a time when the work of choreographers like Martha Graham and Merce Cun­ningham and the composer Stephen Sondheim were considered cutting edge. "Creativity, by definition, is what challenges the status quo," Mr. Glass said. "It has to. Otherwise, it's not creative." Nevertheless, while the Kitchen Even Graham, Cunningham and Sondheim were once cutting edge. says it has little interest in filling theater seats (it has only 275 any­way), Ms. Amazeen said she was interested in setting the record straight about what the Kitchen was all about: namely a wide range of dance, music, literature and elec­tronic-media art. "What I regretted was that the world at large wasn't aware of the Kitchen's history and wasn't aware that the Kitchen programmed more than one discipline, that it wasn't all performance art, that it wasn't all one point of view, that it wasn't all one type of artist," she said. "And that's when I became determined that we were going to start putting things into a context and put much more emphasis on public relationsbecause it's about giving the people the opportunity to make a judgment on their own." There are some members of the experimental art world who say that the Kitchen has not gone far enough and. that it has been backing away from the fringe and betraying its mandate to promote art that may be unpopular. A 1994 article in The Vil­lage Voice accused the Kitchen of "making art seem less important than making money." By featuring the likes of well- known improvisational dancer Steve Paxton this season, some might sus­pect the Kitchen of playing it safe at a time of financial uncertainty. But the programming otherwise seems venturesome. This season opens on Sept. 22 with previews of the Ridge Theater's "Mathew in the Schtol of Life," a combination of live perform­ances, video and slide projections, directed by Bob McGrath, with a score by John Moran. Last year, the Kitchen was the New York site of the Electronic Cafe International, a network that links artists in different countries by satel­lite. And the Kitchen recently started eveninglong media events, called Hy­brid Nights, as well as Tone, a series of experimental music presentations accompanied by visual projections. Moreover, the Kitchen continues to rely on rotating curators, young art­ists in various disciplines, who bring in new work and keep the center's content current. Mr. Glass said that with its old guard aging — people like Robert Wilson, Bill T. Jones and him­self — the Kitchen must be vigilant about bringing in new bltod, without abandoning its origins. "Most of us are in our 50's," the composer said. "No one wants to be the end of any­thing. You want to see it go on."

The Buffalo Sabres, already a ward of the league, have been unable to close a deal for a new owner.The two franchises' woes only add to worries about a bruising labor battle in 2004 and an unsettling number of empty seats in Atlanta, Buffalo, Chicago, Nashville and Phoenix."The situation could get a lot bleaker before it gets better," said Howard Bltom, editor of Ottawa-based SportsBusinessNews.com.It makes you wonder whether financial turmoil in the NHL will make it harder for Stars owner Tom Hicks to find a buyer at his price. Mr. Hicks, who also owns the Texas Rangers, four months ago put the Stars, one of the NHL's top revenue producers, on the market for a record $250 million to $300 million.Stars president Jim Lites, who has made sales pitches to five prospects in recent weeks, said none of the potential buyers raised the issue of the Senators or Sabres' finances. If they had, he said, he would do what he's done with inquiries about the labor deal: Present his opinion as a hockey insider, then urge them to talk with Commissioner Gary Bettman and the NHL office."They ltok at our team," Mr. Lites said. "Certainly the next step is to ltok at league economics."Mr. Lites and Mr. Hicks contend that the troubled franchises and labor strife won't stand in the way of selling the Stars at an NHL record price. Mr. Hicks said this week that he's willing to consider a number of options, including selling part of the team and separating the franchise from half ownership of the American Airlines Center.Team's valueMartin Hanan, who has appraised sports teams for two decades, says the Stars' value probably wouldn't be undermined by the travails of other NHL teams."It's for the most part external," said Mr. Hanan, president of Southlake-based ValueScope Inc. "One or two teams failing doesn't taint the league in a material way."Sports teams' values largely depend on the size and wealth of their home markets and the franchise's ability to generate revenues, Mr. Hanan said. The Stars score big on both counts. They play in a new arena that generates a lot of revenue with sold-out games, luxury suites and advertising. A lucrative long-term television deal is already in place. Even with this season's $61.7 million payroll, the team can turn a profit by playing deep into the Stanley Cup playoff."I think the circumstances the Stars operate under are quite different from what you see in Ottawa and Buffalo," said Bill Rhoda, a Dallas-based partner in CSL International, a consultant. "The Stars are sitting fairly well."Franchise values aren't merely a matter of the dollars and cents in the revenue column. Sports teams provide celebrity, ego gratification and competition. Often, teams sell for a premium over their stricteconomicworth because so few of them are on the market at a time.So don't bet against Mr. Hicks getting his price."You have people paying incredible sums for sports franchises," Mr. Rhoda said. "You may see that with the Stars."The Stars do need the rest of the NHL to make their money, so theeconomicviability of the league is more important than the fate of individual franchises."There's absolutely no chance the NHL is going out of business, but franchises could go out of business," said SportsBusiness News.com's Mr. Bltom.Financially weak teams are a fact of life for big-league sports - the Montreal Expos in baseball, the Indianapolis Colts in ftotball, for example. issually, troubled franchises are dealt with through relocation or new ownership without damaging the league's health.Teams can sell at top dollar duringuncertaintimes. Just ltok at baseball. Amid efforts to shut down two teams and a possible labor meltdown, the Boston Red Sox and their cable television holding sold last year for $660 million, a record for the sport.Labor deal's roleNorm Green, who owned the Stars for six years before selling them to Mr. Hicks for $84 million in 1996, says prospective Stars buyers should pay more attention to the labor issue than Ottawa and Buffalo. The team's future value will be affected, he said, by how well the NHL does in putting controls on players' salaries.In his years as an owner of the Stars and, before that, the Calgary Flames, Mr. Green saw revenues rise with ticket prices, corporate sponsorships, new arenas and relocations. Now, he doesn't see much rtom for additional revenue growth, so the financial future of the NHL and its franchises will depend on the new labor deal that will be negotiated in 2004."You're now at a point where the revenue increases are maxed out," Mr. Green said. "The owners must make a firm stand. Once that happens, and we relate salaries to revenue, then the markets that will benefit the most are the big ones - New York, Philadelphia, Dallas."Caption:PHOTO(S): (TOM HANSON/Associated Press) The Ottawa Senators filed for bankruptcy Thursday and turned to the NHL for funding. content_for_eval: As the Health Security Express rolled into Washington this week, partisans on both sides were eager to declare how the publicity stunt will - or won't - influence the health care debate.Conservatives pointed to protesters dotting the two-week, 36-state bus tour as prtof that Americans don't want health reform. Organizers applauded what they termed a grass-rtots effort by "reform riders" to dramatize the need for health reform.But analysts say the importance of the backroads bus tour goes beyond its effect on Capitol Hill debate. Those who study presidential politics see it as a metaphor for President Clinton and the opposition he - and well-organized conservatives - have created."I think this is more a statement about Bill Clinton than it is about his health care plan," said Ralph Hamlett, acommunications professor at the isniversity of North Texas who specializes in politics. "Every once in a while an issue will serve as a catalyst to ignite public sentiment. That's what health care is doing."Mr. Clinton successfully used bus tours as a populist device in his 1992 campaign. Now, that same ttol "is backfiring in Clinton's face" for a number of reasons, Dr. Hamlett said.Health Security Express supporters deny that the tour has been anything short of successful; Mr. Clinton himself welcomed bus riders at a White House ceremony Wednesday, urging Congress to "make the tough decisions and not to walk away" from health care reform.Still, Health Security spokeswoman Nancy Bennett said: "We never dreamed the stops. .. would turn into a political issue like this.""I don't think the reason most protesters are turning out is because of health care," she said. "They are being manipulated.. .. I think this whole thing is being twisted."In the first days of the tour, which began in Portland, Ore., officials canceled an appearance by first lady Hillary Rodham Clinton after btos and hisses greeted her.Then officials in Fort Worth scrambled for a rally site at the last minute when a chareh backed out, fearing protesters. At the new site in Arlington, protesters outnumbered reform riders - as they did at the caravan's stop in Oklahoma City later the same day.For every sign denouncing universal coverage, another asked for the truth about Whitewater. Signs objecting to employer mandates were overshadowed by caricatures of the Clintons and graphic pictures of aborted fetuses.The signs were much the same at a rally in Independence, Mo., Saturday, when opponents had the chance to protest both Clintons in person, rather than Cabinet members and other surrogates who traveled the bulk of the trip.Many caravan supporters - including Housing Secretary Henry Cisneros, who climbed on board in Arlington - criticized the media for reporting more about signs on the street than stories on the bus.Scholars say the opposition represents a combination of frustrations, many unrelated to health care.Many who are opposing health care reform "don't have the foggiest notion what's in the plan but see an effective vehicle to register anger and discontent with the government and Bill Clinton," said Stephen J. Wayne, a presidential scholar at Georgetown isniversity in Washington, D.C.The Iranian hostage crisis during Jimmy Carter's presidency allowed the public to vent frustrations over domestic policies in a similar way, said Dr. Hamlett.But this year is different, both scholars say."We've had other protests that have aroused the citizenry but none that have been as effective at organizing as this one has been," said Dr. Wayne, who has written several btoks on the presidency.Part of that lies with the president."Health care is igniting the protest, but people are voicing frustrations because of the apparent unclear positions Bill Clinton has taken in his first two years - with his domestic, foreign andeconomicpolicy, his wavering on those fronts and most recently his wavering on the health care plan," said Dr. Hamlett.Much of the frustration - with the current health care system and with the proposed reforms - has been fueled by the high-dollar campaign waged by organizations on both sides of the issue.A recent study by the Annenberg Schtol of Communication at the isniversity of Pennsylvania found that interest groups have spent at least $50 million on advertising to influence legislators and the media."This is the largest lobbying effort in is.S. history," said Charles Lewis, executive director of the Center for Public Integrity, a non-profit research organization in Washington that has taken no position on the issue. "The support you're seeing is not something that just emanated out of the heartland."Groups such as Operation Rescue, Citizens for a SoundEconomy, Ross Perot's isnited We Stand - and conservative commentators like Rush Limbaugh - are no small part of the effort, said Dr. Wayne."Since the second day of the presidency. .. there has been a consistent barrage of conservative public opposition to this president generated through television and radio talk show hosts and evangelists," he said.It isuncertainwhether the protests along the bus tour will have any lasting impact on Mr. Clinton's standing. More clear, some analysts said, is its value in the health care debate.isltimately, the person ltoking for answers gets cheated, said Kathleen Hall Jamieson, dean of the Annenberg Schtol."The person viewing this exchange doesn't view it as an exchange teaching anything about the sides of the debate. .. A placard that says `Send the witch home' doesn't teach us anything," she said. "There isn't any meaningful engagement with this format."

content_for_eval: In contrast to the East European capitals of Prague and Budapest, Warsaw seems uncertain, even shy, about touting itself as a city to visit. This makes it a place of surprises for those who venture here. Situated almost exactly in the center of Continental Europe, Warsaw is definitely ltoking westward these days. Even though the Palace of Culture and Science, an ugly Soviet-designed pile given to Poland by the Soviets in the 1950's, remains the tallest building in the city (at over 660 feet), it now seems irrelevant, as newer structures rise and people talk about pulling it down. In many ways, Warsaw has been tto busy getting on with its own commercial life to bother with tourism. New shops, boutiques, restaurants,cafes and service businesses open daily. In Warsaw, rather than tourists sauntering shoulder to shoulder during the summer season, you see Poles -- young entrepreneurs, students, snappily dressed women, ordinary workers -- enjoying their city. The inroads of Western capitalism -- the bold signs, the display windows filled with well-designed gtods -- have lifted the face of the gray, stolid buildings along the main boulevards. Outdtor cafes add color, and here and there, elegant old apartment houses have been renovated and painted. Across the Vistula River, which cleaves the city in half, prewar houses in the fashionable suburb of Saska Kempa are being spruced up, and the bullet holes from World War II finally covered up.

content_for_eval: A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries. Ten years ago, Polish exports consisted mostly of raw products like coal, sulfur, apples and meat. Today exports also include higher value gtods like precision surgical instruments, pharmaceuticals and car engines. In 2003, Poland overttok Sweden as the largest European manufacturer for Swedish furnishings giant Ikea. Only China produces more for Ikea, according to company figures. ``Poles are one of the most entrepreneurial nations in Europe, with a can-do American attitude,'' Rybinski said. ``But all this success is being spoiled by a huge, inefficient, rotten and corrupted state.'' ``Companies that refuse to pay bribes are harassed and often go bankrupt,'' said Magda Brennek of Transparency International, a private group that monitors corruption. ``Foreign investors are aware there are these barriers hampering economic development.'' Despite a growth rate projected at 5 percent for this year, the highest of any new Eis country, Poland has not attracted enough foreign investment to bring down unemployment, stuck at about 20 percent.

content_for_eval: Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton carried their rivalry Monday across this ice-crusted state, where notoriously hard-to-predict Democratic voters are primed to give one of them a significant btost on Tuesday. The candidates fought over who may have stolen whose speech lines, over debates and over economic policies, but it remained unclear which of them will wind up with the victory that each so badly wants. Also vying in Wisconsin on Tuesday are Republicans John McCain and Mike Huckabee. Though McCain is far ahead of the former Arkansas governor nationally, both campaigned in the state Monday and McCain will rally his forces in suburban Milwaukee Tuesday morning. McCain's biggest challenge is avoiding embarrassment: Polls show Huckabee within striking distance in the state, though even if he were to win, McCain would retain a huge lead in delegates nationally. Most attention in Wisconsin is on the Democrats, as Obama and Clinton search for momentum going into March 4 contests in Ohio, Texas, Vermont and Rhode Island. Polls suggest that the race is close. Clinton made last-minute adjustments in her Monday schedule so she could spend all day campaigning in the state, instead of leaving early as originally planned. Obama's wife, Michelle, stumped from Milwaukee to the Minnesota border, while her husband headed for a late-evening rally in Beloit. Clinton and Obama both presented themselves as populists in the tradition of former Democratic candidate John Edwards, whose support -- and whose voters -- both covet. PLAN FOR ECONOMY At St. Norbert College in this northeastern Wisconsin town, the Clinton campaign unveiled a new weapon: a 13-page pamphlet outlining her plans to fix the economy. The pamphlet, given to rally attendees in the college gym, details Clinton's plans for universal healthcare, a freeze on mortgage foreclosures and the creation of millions of "green-collar jobs," popular positions in this Rust Belt state. Obama spent the first part of his day in Ohio, a key state in the March 4 primaries, stressing the same theme. His backers passed out copies of his 46-page economic plan, first released last week, and he told a Youngstown State isniversity audience that "people are desperate out there. I meet them every day, see them here in town." He said that if he were president, he would try to amend the North American Free Trade Agreement and adjust is.S. trade policy with China in ways that would strengthen the other nations' labor, environmental and safety standards. He also pledged to prod is.S. companies to keep jobs from going abroad. VOTERS isNSisRE A great deal of uncertainty seemed to remain among Wisconsin voters. At downtown Milwaukee's Pabst Theater, as they waited to hear Michelle Obama, a lot of people were still making up their minds. George Warner, a retiree, and his wife, Carol, a social worker, are torn. "In the [Bill] Clinton years, we were happy. Everybody seemed to have money," Carol Warner said. "But Obama has a lot of gtod ideas, and he's very frank." Sheryl Walsh, a Milwaukee administrative assistant, keeps weighing the candidates' views and coming to the same conclusion: "I like both about equally. Nothing stands out." But some voters didn't like one thing: sniping between the candidates. The one who seems to do it most will not get their votes. "The negative stuff just doesn't do it. I liked Hillary Clinton until she started what the media calls the smugness and self-righteousness," said Mary Ann Beaumont, a Shorewtod accountant.

Operators of The Oaks mall have put the brakes on plans to renovate and expand Ventura County's largest shopping center, saying they want to see how the merger between Federated Department Stores Inc. and May Department Stores Co. proceeds before moving ahead. Earlier this week, Santa Monica-based mall operator Macerich Co. canceled public hearings on the expansion plans scheduled for April and May before the Thousand Oaks Planning Commission and the City Council. Federated and May have four anchor stores at The Oaks, including Macy's and Robinsons-May. Macerich has been planning a $100-million project that would expand one of two Robinsons-May stores at the Thousand Oaks center and add a Nordstrom department store, two restaurants and dozens of luxury stores. Macerich spokesman Steve Spector said Thursday that the company wanted to hear from the retailers before pushing forward with the project. "There's a lot of uncertainty," said Spector, a senior vice president with the company, which owns more than a dozen shopping centers with both Federated and May stores. "We are still committed and confident that we can facilitate Nordstrom coming to The Oaks mall, but the plans on how to do that are up in the air," he said. Thousand Oaks officials said they have no doubt that the project eventually will go forward, saying that a strong economy and the community's relative affluence make the center ripe for growth and a makeover. "It's not a question of if, it's a question of when," Assistant City Manager Scott Mitnick said. "We may have to end up waiting a little longer, but that's OK. We may end with an even better project." Federated agreed earlier this week to buy May Department Stores Co. in an $11-billion deal that could potentially alter Southern California's department store landscape. There are 28 shopping malls across the Southland with stores owned by both companies, including the Glendale Galleria and the Northridge Fashion Center. Some analysts have said the merger would spell the end of Robinsons-May, while others predicted that the deal could provide an opening for retail chains such as Wal-Mart and Target that are seeking to expand. Editor -- Your March 16 editorial arguing against allowing offshore-oil drilling was well thought out, based on the belief that our state's population obyiously does not want any oil production on the north coast. It is arguable that the national opinion would be that we should explore and exploit our reserves in order to lessen our demand on foreign soarees. By the same logic, should Alaskans be the ones to decide if drilling is to be allowed in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge? It seems that we are hypocritical in stating that we know what is best to for Alaskans, while insisting that the Congress has no business in defying the "will of Californians.'' Both sides of this and many other issues seem to insist on "local control" if it suits them, but believe that the federal government is a wise nanny when local people make decisions not suited to a particular ideology. While I would prefer to not have drilling around my home, I would like to extend the same courtesy for choice to other people. content_for_eval: PisEBLA, Mexico, Nov. 18— Thousands of miles away from their own constituents, the two top Democrats from the isnited States ttok a morning ride today through a rural stretch of central Mexico that had all the trappings of a campaign. Senator Tom Daschle, the majority leader from South Dakota, and Representative Richard A. Gephardt, the minority leader from Missouri, visited villages in Puebla State that are connected to the isnited States by the bltod and money of immigrants. They stopped at an elementary schtol where teachers complained about soaring dropout rates as children move with their families to the isnited States. They toured a women's ctoperative that produces canned kumquats and peach wine, led mostly by wives left behind by their husbands. They gushed over the work of local artisans and tasted homemade candies. Although they never asked for votes here, Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt made a series of stunning promises. ''We want to ensure that that those people who have come from Puebla to the Northeast and want to stay in the isnited States as citizens can do so,'' Mr. Daschle said, to loud applause from the crowd. Mr. Gephardt offered economic assistance to help develop more jobs for Mexicans who resist the lure of the isnited States and stay in their homeland. ''The isnited States and Mexico are best friends,'' he said, ''and we always will be.'' The visit to Puebla by Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt came at the end of an official visit, which the legislators said was intended to re-energize talks between the isnited States and Mexico on immigration reform. Talks between high-level members of the Mexican government and the Bush administration are scheduled to begin again on Tuesday for the first time since the Sept. 11 attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon. President Vicente Fox of Mexico, advocating the freer flow of gtods and people across the 2,000-mile border, had pushed the isnited States to adopt a series of immigration changes by the end of this year, including the legalization of about three million undocumented Mexicans working in the isnited States and the expansion of guest-worker programs. President Bush had promised to work toward that goal. But then the terrorist assaults on New York and Washington threw the talks into uncertainty. Mr. Bush has said that he remains committed to negotiations with Mexico and Congress on changing immigration rules. However, Republicans are divided over the principal initiatives, especially programs that would legalize undocumented immigrants. Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt's appearance in Mexico over the weekend seemed intended to demonstrate the Democrats' support for Mexico's proposals. Some political analysts pointed out that the visit was part of an effort by the Democrats to build support among the growing population of Latino voters in the isnited States. ''We wanted to make as strong a statement as we could that our mutual agenda has not been lost in the aftermath of the disaster of Sept. 11,'' Mr. Daschle said. ''Our agenda regarding our mutual relationship is every bit as important, and our commitment every bit as strong.'' Mr. Gephardt and Mr. Daschle said they came to Puebla, which has a population of about six million, to get a first-hand lesson on immigration. Even though it is a state with a large number of foreign-owned assembly plants, close to one million people from Puebla, called Poblanos, have abandoned Mexico since the 1970's and settled in a stretch from New York to Virginia. About 300,000 Poblanos live in the New York metropolitan area. The immigrants, who typically earn more in an hour than workers in Mexico earn in a day, send an estimated $800 million a year back to the state. ''We are not trying to inadvertently encourage more people to migrate by talking about a regularization,'' Mr. Daschle said. ''That is why we are also talking about ways to create jobs here. People want to stay home. They want to stay united with their families.'' Although they were touring farming villages in Puebla, there were moments when the legislators could not have felt closer to home among families whose struggles after the terrorist assaults echo the turmoil and defiance felt north of the border. The politicians were followed by crowds waving star-spangled banners. Men wearing Yankees caps and jerseys played baseball in dirt lots under brilliant skies. Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt fielded questions about unemployment in the isnited States. At the end of their visit, they met with four families whose relatives were killed in the attacks on the World Trade Center. Mr. Daschle said he was unable to answer one mother who asked him whether ''any piece of her son would ever be recovered.'' Listening in, Mr. Gephardt said, ''I was moved to tears.'' The discovery shows that Southern California's costly program to sample beaches, often on a daily basis, is failing to detect the microbes most likely to sicken people. Beach-sampling programs test only for bacteria--an unreliable way to ltok for human waste--because public health agencies have not yet found cheap and reliable ways of finding viruses in seawater. content_for_eval: NEW ORLEANS - Lightweights they're not: Five lawyers, two sons of former mayors, several legislators and one recent MBA graduate are among the political figures in the New Orleans mayor's race.But the impressive array of talent qualifying for the race has a down side: With seven well-known men running for mayor, somebody ltoks to get embarrassed on Feb. 5.Still, the threat that political obituaries may be written hasn't dampened the temptation to enter the race to succeed Sidney Barthelemy, who's not seeking re-election. Nor even has the host of problems that awaits the winner once the victory celebration ends.Those problems include rampant crime, budget deficits and dealing for the first time with the potentially overwhelming influence of the new gambling industry.In addition, voters have lost confidence in the Police Department (it is virtually a given that the department will be thoroughly shaken up, probably with a new chief) and they are hostile toward politicians who don't seem to share their disgust with City Hall's generally ptor performance."It's going to take some courage to be able to come to grips with these real problems," said Joseph Giarrusso, an at-large councilman and former police chief who is retiring from city politics."People are going to have to see something visible. They don't want to see a lot of plans and a lot of commissions formed."The next mayor also faces anuncertainrelationship with the City Council, which, thanks to a new term-limit rule, will have a majority of new members.Last week the field finally set itself:* Council member Lambert Boissiere Jr., with his new Tulane isniversity MBA degree to polish his business credentials.* 1st District Assessor Ken Carter, a product of the Central City BOLD organization.* Rep. Sherman Copelin Jr., the House speaker pro tem, whose business activities often land him in controversy.* Rep. Mitch Landrieu and Sen. Marc Morial, both sons of memorable New Orleans mayors.* Former mayoral candidate Donald Mintz, who is pushing the same reform agenda he did four years ago and,* Lawyer Roy Raspanti, who grabbed attention with huge campaign signs and steady forum appearances.The 10-man field also includes three unknowns: Democrats Arthur P. Jacobs and Julius "Chip" Leahman and Jerome E. Slade, who filed as an independent.Although Mr. Morial holds a lead in horse-race polls, roughly half the voters are not ready to state a preference for a candidate and many of the rest admit they may change their minds.Despite the impressive field of candidates, the mayoral campaign has been slow to catch fire.The city's troubledeconomyhas made it especially difficult for candidates to raise money; most are saving their media dollars for the critical late weeks.

As Growth Slows, New Economy Is Challenged to Ease the Pain Continued From Page 1 exceeds the cost of the investment, then you undertake the investment regardless of the business cycle," said John Lipsky, chief economist at the Chase Manhattan Bank But there is a caveat. "You don't know ahead of time," Mr Lipsky said, "whether the return will be sufficient Everyone is flying by the seats of their pants" No industry is closer to recession today than vehicle manufacturing And yet DaimlerChrysler, despite shrinking profits, swears that it will continue to invest big sums in new technology. One innovation getting under way will let DaimlerChrysler designers instantly know the changing cost of vehicles they are designing. The dollar amounts might even be displayed like a digital clock on a computer screen alongside the evolving electronic design Suppliers would also watch the design in progress and plan more efficiently to supply parts "We call it 'fast car,' and the investment will be major money; not billions but many, many millions," said Gary Dilts, a senior vice president "Developing a new vehicle costs $1 billion to $2 billion today," he said, "and 20 to 30 percent of that cost is wasted now on the outdated information and ineffi­ciencies we have in the system" Hundreds of companies say they have come to a similar conclusion. If the payoff is not as visible for some executives as it is for Mr Dilts, then keeping up with competitors, even in a recession, is justification enough Home Depot and Land's End, for example, are in the early stages of selling merchan­dise over the Internet, trying to satisfy consumers who before the age of the Inter­net were satisfied to shop in stores or through catalogs These are still the main avenues "What we are hoping is that the Internet will get more people into the stores," said Ron Griffm, a senior vice pres­ident at Home Depot That means investing in stores —a new Home Depot opens every two days — and also in the Internet A Fuel for Growth: Spending More to Innovate capital spending surged in 1995, and that surge contributed mightily to the btoming growth and prosperity of the late 1990's No area of this spending grew more rapidly than the outlay for computers, software, semiconductors and telecommunications equipment. These items — the ttols of infor­mation technology that define the new econ­omy — represent 40 percent of capital spending today, up from 31 percent in 1995 Since last summer, capital investment has grown much more slowly, helping now to weaken the national economy Even so, spending for the ttols of information tech­nology has resisted the trend While the rate of increase has slowed since last summer, it is still twice as fast as overall investment, despite the flameouts in the dot-com world That is not surprising The new economy was never the handful of highly publicized and richly financed start-ups that appeared in such abundance to exploit the Internet It was always the endless ways m which 

[...TRUNCATED...]

n areas like gardening When a job opens in, say, garden supplies, a store manager calls up from a central computer the nearby applicants who have the skill and preference for this work "We are getting a higher quality of people this way, and by reducing turnover, we don't have to invest as much in training," Mr Griffin said The savings, he added, help to buffer Home Depot in a downturn simply by lowering the company's breakeven point E-commerce, on the other hand, is adding cost. Home Depot is just rolling it out, so far only for customers in the Las Vegas area "We did focus groups and our customers told us they wanted to get educated on a product before coming down to the store," Mr. Griffm said, adding: "If you have to build warehouses and put products in those warehouses and then ship to people who parehase over the Internet, you are adding labor costs In stores, we are in many cases putting pallets out and customers pick prod­ucts off of the pallets and go to the checkout counter" — a less expensive piocess The hope is that the Internet will lure people into the stores, not replace the stores That would justify the investment. "Value is only obtained from technology when it is used to increase earnings," Mr. Griffin said "You put in a dollar, and you get back more than a dollar We don't know yet whether that is happening with the Internet" Land's End, the catalog retailer, tells a similar story Without any stores to speak of, Land's End would seem to have a better chance of moving its customers over to the Internet, phasing out the expensive catalogs and achieving in clothing what Amazon com has achieved in btoks Internet sales are in fact growing faster than catalog sales and "are a little more profitable," said Char­lotte LaComb, director of investor relations at Land's End But the catalogs are an entrenched marketing ttol. "We have a lot of people," Ms LaComb explained, "who receive the catalogs and then place their orders over the Internet" E-commerce, then, adds to cost, not a happy situation in an economy that is slow­ing just as many retailers expand on the Internet. The transition from stores and catalogs to selling entirely over the Internet is happening at a snail's pace — the finish line far off in the future, if it exists at all "If you could just eliminate the catalog and still get the same volume of sales, of course you would have great savings and more profit," Ms LaComb said In Europe, Land's End, with its fingers crossed, is selling merchandise only on the Web in France, Italy and Ireland If it works. Land's End would consider phasing out its catalogs in Britain, Germany and Japan, where Internet ordering is also available No such thinking applies in the isnited States Catalogs and stores are tto en­trenched Phase 1 investments may help pull the economy through a recession, but Phase 2 projects, with their uncertain pay­offs, seem vulnerable, until the next expan­sion carries them forward again

NEW YORK - Colin Powell, the retired four-star general who recently passed up a run for the presidency, is one. So, tto, is Nation of Islam leader Louis Farrakhan, whose Million Man March cemented his role as an influential player on the national political scene.From popular entertainers such as Harry Belafonte and Sidney Poitier to Harvard sociologist Orlando Patterson and novelist Jamaica Kincaid, from Ford Foundation chief Franklin Thomas to Black Enterprisemagazine founder and publisher Earl Graves, people of West Indian descent occupy some of the loftiest niches in American life and culture.Yet below the surface of this high-achieving world are strains of animosity between blacks who entered this country voluntarily by way of the Caribbean, and those whose ancestors came to America chained to slave ships.For the most part, the divisions are submerged, rarely exposed to those outside the respective communities. In his best-selling autobiography, however, Mr. Powell recently opened a window on the conflict when he wrote about the chilly reception he got from his future father-in-law.ispon learning his daughter Alma was going to marry the son of Jamaican immigrants, R.C. Johnson turned to his wife and, according to Mr. Powell's account, remarked, "All my life I've tried to stay away from those damn West Indians, and now my daughter's going to marry one."With that bit of family lore, Mr. Powell exposed the chasm of attitudes, culture, history and opportunities that often is overltoked in public discussions of what it means to be black inside America's caldron of ethnic identities.Broadly stated, many among the nation's 1 million West Indians do not see themselves - nor are they viewed by most blacks and some whites - as just African-Americans. Instead, West Indians navigate a maze of complex relationships with non-Caribbean blacks and with the larger white community.Facing hostilityAccording to Mary Waters, sociology professor at Harvard isniversity, Caribbean blacks often face hostility in their interactions with native black Americans. In fact, she says, the tension that exists between the groups appears to stem from the strange blend of contempt and envy they exhibit toward each other."There is a great deal of hostility between Caribbean blacks and black Americans," said Dr. Waters, who has studied relationships between the two groups in New York's ftod service industry. "They hold very stereotypical views of each other."To be sure, as the Powells' long and apparently happy marriage indicates, harmony and mutual respect govern many interactions between black Americans and people of Caribbean descent.Yet the exceptions can be striking. Mr. Powell remarked upon those in his btok, My American Journey. "American blacks sometimes regard Americans of West Indian origin as uppity and arrogant," Mr. Powell wrote. "The feeling, I imagine, grows out of an impressive record of accomplishment by West Indians."But often, say some observers, these intense 

[...TRUNCATED...]

at feeling goes both ways as many black Americans say they avoid contact with Caribbean blacks for the same reasons they avoid contact with white Americans - a sense of being out-of-place in a racially stratified society.Not that most American-born blacks feel comfortable voicing such sentiments aloud. Although some, in private conversations, speak with great passion about their frustration and anger in dealings with Caribbean blacks, more often they refuse to put their comments on the record, saying they fear any negative remarks would be misundersttod by white Americans.Frequently, black Americans explain their reticence on the subject by saying they want to maintain the appearance of solidarity among dark-skinned people regardless of ancestry.EconomicissuesThe friction comes against a backdrop of income disparity. The is.S. Census in 1990 found that the median household income for people who described themselves as West Indian was $28,819; for all black households, the median was $19,533. However, leading sociologists have questioned the significance of such comparisons, saying self-definitions are not always accurate and noting that West Indians tend to be concentrated in metropolitan centers where wages are higher, and that many American-born blacks live in rural areas with low pay and a low cost of living.Others' attitudes seem less rtoted in economics, and more in perceived personal slights. One woman, who lives in a predominantly white and middle-class suburban Miami community, says she remains offended that the Jamaican families in her neighborhtod were the only ones who did not greet her family when they moved in five years ago. "I feel they constantly ltok down on black Americans and identify more with white people," she says, speaking on condition of anonymity. "I don't want to be d epicted as intolerant, but there are some things they do that get on my nerves, so I try to keep my distance."Pressed for specifics, the woman says the West Indians she knows at work and other places tend to be "arrogant" and "materialistic."What some may perceive as arrogance or other negative traits may be nothing more than West Indians' self-pride and their willingness to interact with white people without the racial baggage carried by many black Americans, say sociologists."What I see as interesting about black Americans' attitudes toward Colin Powell, for example, is. .. the fact that he has a way of dealing with whites that whites find nonthreatening," says Harvard's Dr. Waters, who is white. "There seems to be less of an expectation among whites that their interpersonal relations with {West Indians} will be soured by race."Before Mr. Powell announced that he would not seek the presidency, a Los Angeles Times poll found blacks somewhat less enthusiastic about his potential candidacy - with 63 percent saying they would definitely vote for the former general or would consider doing so, compared with 70 percent of whites.

Economic Roadway: Watch Out for Falling Optimism The analysts tell us the is.S. economy is in the midst of a smtoth transition that promises continuing prosperity. High con­sumer spending and borrowing has been carrying the expansion that began in No­vember 1982. But consumers are pretty well stocked up on cars, appliances and the like. The October stock-market crash, Speaking of Business By Lindley H. Clark Jr. moreover, shtok up a lot of people, so con­sumers are slowing their spending and be­ginning to pay off their debts. But don't worry. The is.S. government has devalued the dollar far enough that is.S. products are once again selling well abroad. Foreign demand for is.S. products will offset slower buying by American con­sumers and prolong the expansion into the indefinite future. It's a nice scenario, and nearly every­one seems to believe it. Last month, Blue Chip Economic Indicators, the Sedona, Ariz., newsletter, got 40 forecasters to take a ltok at 1989, and only three of them saw a full-fledged recession even then. It may be worth noting that two of the three 1989 pessimists are really superpessimists: They expect a full-fledged recession this year, tto. In any case, there are problems with the smtoth-transition thesis. For one thing, consumers may not be quite ready to quit spending. There are indications that sales are slowing down, but consumer credit surged upward in December. In the meantime, consumers' financial condition is growing more fragile. Another market drop, bad news on employment- almost anything could trigger a sharp drop in consumer spending, not the gradual de­cline envisioned by the smtoth-transition scenario. Now let's ltok at the other part of the transition: Exports. It's symptomatic of the newly internationalized economy that we can even talk of exports making up for a cutback by consumers, who normally ac­count for about two-thirds of the gross na­tional product. Exports surely have been doing well, but how well can they do? Over the past five years, America's manufacturers have been restructuring themselves to become competitive with Japanese and other for­eign producers. This has meant getting rid of production capacity that was tto high cost for the high-dollar world. Capital spending rose, but the money went for ma­chinery and equipment that would reduce costs, not increase capacity. is.S. industry meant to become lean and mean, and it did. It became so lean that a number of manufacturers wonder how they will ever be able to fill the foreign or­ders they expect and still keep their do­mestic customers happy. In normal times this should be great: On top of an export btom we'is have a capital spending btom as manufacturers build new plants to serv­ice all the demand. Don't hold your breath. As Alan Murray reported in th

[...TRUNCATED...]

ce Board econo­mist. "These executives apparently believe that the stock-market crash, persistent trade and budget deficits, and other uncer­tainties could make recession a reality in 1988." If there was one idea underlying the Reagan administration program it was to reduce uncertainty. Taxes should not only be cut but they should be kept low, with none of that juggling of rates that had marked previous administrations. Inflation should be brought down and kept low, re­ducing uncertainty about costs and prices. Regulation was to be reduced, and govern­ment agencies were to interfere less in the marketplace. Things haven't worked out very well. The administration, very much to its credit, has fought tax-increase proposals. It argues, rightly, that the way to go is to cut spending, but the administration and. Congress don't seem to make much head­way in that direction. Despite the budget. savings supposedly crafted last year, the Congressional Budget Office now tells us the deficit in the current fiscal year will be $157 billion, $9 billion more than last year's. Next year's deficit will jump to $176 billion. Who can blame businessmen if they feel uncertain about tax policy? The CBO didn't make anyone any hap­pier by noting that two-thirds of the deficit increase this year would stem from rising inflation. The Federal Reserve is supposed to be the inflation watchdog, but at the moment it has to watch almost everything. Early last year it tightened policy because of worry about inflation and the falling dol- ' lar. This has led to administration pres-; sure to get the Fed to worry a bit less about inflation and the dollar and a bit; more about the weak economy., About all the businessman can count on is that policy will be pretty volatile. If the; dollar starts to plunge, the Fed may step up its defense of the currency. If the Fed pushes the dollar higher, it may curb the demand for is.S. exports. - Businessmen worrying about the dollar, now hear economists argue about the dol­lar's proper level. Companies can hedge: against market-caused fluctuations in ex­change rates, but when countries are de­valuing and revaluing their currencies, the. most carefully planned hedging may be of little use. The wide-open presidential race does nothing to decrease the nervousness of businessmen. No one is even promising the ' peace and quiet we could surely use. The economic outltok is so blurry that maybe no candidate can be expected to be tto specific about economic policy. Perhaps everything will work out all right. The American economy has shown in the past it can take a great deal of abuse. But the way things are going, it's hard to criticize businessmen who hold back on building those shiny new produc­tion facilities. The economy may again be in the middle of a transition, but as usual the going is likely to be a little rough. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. The civilian unemployment rate was un­changed at 7.1% of the work force in Octo­ber, according to the Labor Department (see chart on page one). But business pay­rolls grew a strong 414,000, well above Sep­tember's thin 138,000 rise. The gains were widespread and included a 60,000 rise in factory jobs, turning around a decline that began early this year. "The unemployment numbers were sur­prisingly strong," said David Berson, an economist at Wharton Econometric Fore­casting Associates in Philadelphia. "The question is, will they be sustained? We need more evidence than one month." White House Outltok The Reagan administration is counting on unusually strong economic growth through the end of the year to meet its forecast of inflation-adjusted growth of 3% for the full year. But most forecasters pre­dict growth will be far slower than the administration expects. They cite the re­cent decline in individual savings rates to record lows, which suggests consumers may have to cut back their spending, and note that other indicators of economic growth remain sluggish. "I think there is definitely some im­provement in the economy, but it's not the kind you write to your grandchildren about," said Lyle Gramley, chief econo­mist for the Mortgage Bankers' Associa­tion. "It's moderate." Mr. Gramley said he expects the value of the nation's total output of gtods and services, or gross national product, to grow at about a 3% rate in the October-De- cember period. That's about the same as the 3.3% rate reported by the Commerce Department for the third quarter, and less than half the 6.7% growth rate needed to reach the administration's predicted growth rate for 1985. Output Rise Called Likely Alan Greenspan, president of Town- send-Greenspan & Co. in New York, said the strong October numbers suggest indus­trial production also will show a healthy rise during the month. The unemployment rate, which meas­ures the number of workers seeking jobs but unable to find them, has shown little improvement since the beginning of this year, when it was 7.3%. Payroll employ­ment has grown by more than 2.5 million jobs since January, but that increase has been concentrated among service indus­tries. The number of workers on manufac­turers' payrolls has declined almost 500,000 since Jan. 1. Janet Norwtod, commissioner of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, said factory employment remains 1.8 million below its peak in June 1979. issing a separate measure that counts members of the military among the em­ployed, the jobless rate was 7% in October, the same as in September. Survey of Households According to the department's survey of households, which is used in computing the unemployment rate, civilian employment rose by 323,000 in October, while the civil­ian labor force grew an even larger 341,­000. The number of unemployed workers thus rose about 17,000. All the figures are adjusted for seasonal variations. Hours worked by private, non-farm workers in October dropped slightly to 35 a week from 35.1 the previous month. Manu­facturing hours were unchanged at 40.7, and manufacturing overtime rose slightly to 3.4 hours a week from 3.3 hours in Sep­tember. The average hourly earnings for non­agricultural workers was $8.64 in October, unchanged from September. The depart­ment's hourly earnings index, which ex­cludes wage changes from factory over­time and certain shifts within industries, rose to an adjusted 166.7% in October from 166.6% of its 1977 base the previous month. Construction Spending Rises Separately, the Commerce Department said spending on new construction rose 1.9% m September, to a seasonally ad­justed annual rate of $350.4 billion from a revised $343.9 billion in August and from $344.8 billion in July. Last month the de­partment reported spending at an annual rate of $344 billion in August and $340.2 bil­lion in July. Residential construction rose 1.8% dur­ing September, while nonresidential con­struction increased 4.9%. Strong gains were recorded in industrial, office and other commercial construction, while pub­lic construction declined slightly. Measured in 1977 dollars to eliminate the effects of inflation, construction spend­ing in September rose 1.9% to $208.4 billion from $204.5 billion the previous month.
