content_for_eval: Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West. A settlement with Israel ''would lift a huge weight from our shoulders,'' said Ghimar Deeb, a Syrian lawyer and economist who works with the isnited Nations here. It would lead to the lifting of sanctions, which would give Syria access to new investment, high-tech supplies and training opportunities, he said. ''Poverty is increasing, inequality is increasing, and I believe the street is frustrated,'' Mr. Deeb said. ''They need peace with all our neighbors.'' It is not clear that the Syrian government sees the economic troubles as a factor in negotiations with Israel. Although it began carrying out economic changes several years ago, the progress has been slow, and strategic political concerns have always been paramount for Syria's president, Bashar al-Assad, and his father, Hafez al-Assad, who governed from 1970 until his death in 2000. It is also far from clear that the talks will succeed. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel is facing accusations of corruption that could bring him down, and some say the Syrians may be unwilling to make the sacrifices Israel would demand. This month, Mr. Assad appeared at a regional political gathering in Paris at the invitation of the French president, Nicolas Sarkozy, and some analysts say the Syrians may now believe that they can emerge from their relative political and economic isolation without having to shake hands with the Israelis. This is despite the fact that the Bush administration, which accuses Syria of supporting terrorism, has recently added sanctions on the government and its business associates. But some analysts say they believe that Syria's economic troubles must figure in the government's calculations about regional peace. ''The transformation they have in front of them now is enormous,'' said Andrew Tabler, a Damascus-based Syria analyst and consulting editor for the magazine Syria Today. ''They must move from a state funded by oil revenues to one funded by taxation, and that has to play some role in their thinking.'' It would hardly be surprising: Syria's oil used to be the mainstay of the government's income, providing 70 percent of the country's export earnings. Now it is drying up so fast that Syria is expected to be a net importer of crude oil in just two years, according to the International Monetary Fund. Already, Syria is importing oil products at record prices, and paying huge subsidies to reduce the cost for its citizens. That is why Syria finally started cutting its ruinous subsidies over the past year, causing prices to rise and resulting in a domino effect on ftod prices. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics.

STEVENSONSpecial to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Current file); Jul 3, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 25 Companies Hesitating On China Existing Operations Will Stay, but Plans Are Being Delayed By RICHARD W. STEVENSON Special to The New York Times LOS ANGELES, July 2 — Despite uncertainty about China's political course, most American corporations doing business in China express confi­dence in the country's long-term eco­nomic prospects and seem intent on maintaining operations there. But nearly a month after the Chi­nese Government's violent suppres­sion of the pro-democracy demon­strations in Beijing, it remains un­clear whether companies that do not already have operations in China will be willing to invest there in coming years. Consultants and industry execu­tives said some of these companies might use the Chinese leadership's newfound willingness to make con­cessions to foreign investors as an ex­cuse to jump into the market. But they said questions about China's stability and economic policy are forcing many American companies to delay or scale back their plans for Chinese ventures. Motorola Inc. has put on hold its plans to build two factories in China to make semiconductors and mobile radio equipment, at least until the political situation settles down suffi­ciently for the company to make a long-term assessment of the ven­ture's prospects. The American Tele­phone and Telegraph Company has indefinitely delayed a joint venture it agreed to in May to make optical fiber transmission equipment in Shanghai. Where China Will Be Hurt "I don't think you're going to see any major new ventures," said Rich­ard E. Gillespie, vice president of the isnited States-China Business Coun­cil, a Washington-based group that represents more than 300 American companies with operations in China. "This is where China will be really hurt because they counted on foreign investment more and more each year." Moreover, executives said the fu­ture involvement of American busi­ness in China could be influenced greatly by further economic sanc­tions imposed by the isnited States. President Bush has suspended mili­tary sales to China, barred high-level contacts and delayed international loans. The House, however, has voted to impose tougher sanctions, includ­ing the suspension of trade-develop­ment and investment-guarantee pro­grams, and the Senate will consider similar legislation. "The form that sanctions take will have a major bearing on the future," Robert W. Yeager, the Rockwell In­ternational Corporation's chief repre­sentative in China, said in a telephone interview from Beijing.  Japanese! "The Japanese are going to be much more aggressive and more I willing to go back in," said one Amer­ican banker, who asked not to be iden­tified. Hong Kong, the British colony scheduled to revert to China in 1997, j has long accounted for th? bulk of for­eign investment in China. Hong Kong business executives are returning to China, but remain highly nervousabout how the crackdown will affect their ventures and the Chinese lead­ership's commitment to allow Hong Kong to remain capitalist for at least 50 years. Most American companies, both those that already have operations in China and those considering it, also worry about whom they will find across the negotiating table from them. Should a large number of bu­reaucrats, joint venture partners and local officials be removed in a purge, foreign business ties could be dis­rupted for a long time, American ex­ecutives and consultants said. But Mr. Yeager, of Rockwell, said such fears, so far at least, are un­founded. Mr. Yeager said there had been concern in the foreign business com­munity that Communist Party repre­sentatives would again start attend­ing business meetings, reviving a practice common earlier in the dec­ade. But he said that had not hap­pened. Given the furor in the isnited States over the actions of the Chinese Gov­ernment, some companies operating in China or considering doing busi­ness there are becoming concerned about public perceptions of their Chi­nese ties. Some executives and con­sultants say they worry that a "busi­ness as usual" attitude could brand them as insensitive to human rights concerns or make them the target of advocacy groups like companies that do business in South Africa. "My sense is that no one in the busi­ness community is doing a social or ethical assessment of what it means to do business in China," said Timo­thy Smith, the executive director ofthe Interfaith Council on Corporate Responsibility, a group that has backed efforts to pressure companies to divest holdings in South Africa. "I think it's likely to creep up as an issue." For now, though, many of the com­panies with the most experience in China are pushing ahead, with no change in plans. Auto Makers: THE INDisSTRY By JOHN HOLisSHA New York Times (1923-Current file); Apr 13, 1995; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) PE- D6 Blocked due to copyright. See full page image or microfilm. Chrysler's 1996 Dodge mini-van at the current New York International Auto Show. The company is the target of a buyout offer from its largest shareholder, Kirk Kerkorian, and its former chairman, Lee Iacocca.' THE INDisSTRY isncertainty Ahead for is.S. Auto Mahers By JOHN HOLisSHA The offer by Kirk Kerkorian to acquire the Chrysler Corporation comes at a time when the domestic] automobile industry is in the best, condition since the onset of the ener­gy crisis in 1974. But prospects for' the future are uncertain, as annual; sales seem unlikely to grow very.' fast and more competitors vie for customers. i Last year the Big Three auto com-| panies — Chrysler, the Ford Motor) Company and the General Motors1 Corporation — reported combined, profits of more than $14 billion, a: record high. Though sales prospects have slipped somewhat recently, most forecasters are still expecting a gtod year, with total vehicle sales of more than 15 million cars and light trucks. Today's domestic auto industry is vastly different from the one that was shaken to its foundations in the 1970's by cutbacks in oil from the Middle East. Rising gasoline prices drove customers from American made cars, which averaged 12 miles to a gallon, to Japanese cars, where an average of 30 miles to a gallon' was easily achieved. Driven to Japanese models by the need for fuel economy, customers came to prefer them for their quality and comfort, building loyalty to brand names that once seemed un­comfortably foreign. The Big Three completed the tran­sition from rear- to front-wheel- drive vehicles during the 1980's, which permitted the production of smaller, more fuel-efficient cars with almost as much interior rtom as Japanese models. Since then, the companies have refined those de­signs, installing more powerful — but still fuel efficient — engines and other features to increase the cars' appeal to customers. New types of vehicles, like mini­vans, sport utility vehicles and pick­up trucks with luxury-car interiors, have grown in popularity to the point, where total sales have begun to ap­proach those of cars. A big part of Chrysler's comeback from near­bankruptcy was its success in intro­ducing a van that proved superior to station wagons for carrying children and groceries. As the number of models has pro­liferated, so has the number of com­petitors in each segment and sub­segment of the automotive market. "The industry is doing well right now, but there is a dark side," said John Schnapp, vice president and automotive specialist at Mercer Management Consulting in Lexing­ton, Mass. "Overall demand is flat at- best and there is more competition in every niche. In addition to every­one who is here, the Koreans are coming as well. That's going to cause suicidal pricing as the cycle turns down." Nevertheless, most analysts main­tain that domestic auto companies have made major progress in im­proving quality and reducing costs. The fact that Toyota went to the trouble of disassembling a Chrysler Neon model to study its design and engineering is an indication of how much progress the Big Three have made, analysts say. Chrysler has been given high marks for designing and producing new models of cars and trucks more quickly than its domestic competi­tors. "Chrysler has had Babe Ruth- type home runs with its mini-vans, the Neon, the LH cars and its pickup trucks," Mr. Schnapp said. The failure of General Motors to introduce models that arouse public excitement are a long-term problem for the company, despite its return to profitability. "Ford is there and Chrysler is there, but G.M. is not back in a competitive position in the market yet," said a retired auto ex­ecutive, who requested anonymity. Some analysts say Chrysler is ba­sically a North American manufac­turer and lacks the worldwide net­work of factories and marketing out­lets that Toyota, Ford, Nissan and General Motors have already estab­lished. Though the weakness of the dollar gives North American produc­ers a temporary advantage against auto manufacturers in Japan and Germany, most analysts think a global presence will be needed for long-term survival. Amiable Idea Merchant Who Is Viewed as the Most-Feared Japan-Basher By ELAINE SCIOLINO Special to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Current file); Feb 8, 1990; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. A24 Washington at Work Amiable Idea Merchant Who Is Viewed as the Most-Feared Japan-Basher By ELAINE SCIOLINO Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, Feb. 7 — Enter Pat Choate's cinnabar-colored Victo­rian townhouse on Capitol Hill, and you are walking into the Orient. istamaro wtodblocks and a 17th-cen­tury painted screen line the gallery of the spare living rtom. A six-ftot- square calligraphy on hammered sil­ver frames the dining rtom table. A mural of Hokusains "Mount Fuji at Dawn" graces the viewing area of the Japanese rock garden in the back­yard. So why is this bearded and bespec­tacled economist regarded as Wash­ington's most-feared Japan-basher? In September, Alfred A. Knopf will publish "Agents of Influence," Mr. Choate's chronicling of the ways Japan shapes American trade and economic policies through contribut­ing to Congressional, gubernatorial and mayoral campaigns, bankrolling think tanks and universities, planting stories in the press, and most of all, through the revolving dtor, hiring for big money former isnited States Gov­ernment officials as lobbyists, law­yers and publicists. "This is a town where you can rent power and influence," says Mr. Choate, who is vice president of policy analysis for TRW Inc., a $7 bil­lion military and industrial conglom­erate. "We have created a buccaneer class where public service is merely a sabbatical for lobbying and that lob­bying has increased for foreign gov­ernments and foreign corporations. I'm using the Japanese because they're on the cutting edge." Variation on a Theme Mr. Choate (whose name rhymes with boat) says his new btok is noth­ing more than a variation on the theme he has explored for years, in btoks like "America in Ruins" and "The High Flex Society," and in cau­cuses of the middle-of-the-road Democrats' Democratic Leadership Council and the centrist Republicans' House Wednesday Group: how to make American industry more vi­brant and competitive in the new world economy. Rumors about the btok have dis­turbed Americans with Japanese con­nections and prompted Japanese offi­cials to try to quash its publication. A particularly heavy-handed pitch came during a visit to Tokyo last year by TRW's chairman, Joseph T. Gor­man, when Matsudo Kuroda, the for­mer vice minister of Japan's power­ful Ministry of International Trade and Industry and now a consultant to the New York-based Salomon Broth­ers, argued that if the btok were pub­lished, it would severely damage is.S.- Japanese trade relations. TRW has substantial business dealings with Japan. Mr. Gorman was not swayed. Mr. Choate is a mild-mannered, nice guy with clout, a contradiction in Washington where political capital is often accrued by self-promotion, leading with one's elbows and making enemies a

[...TRUNCATED...]

ies Mr. Bergsten. "That's kind of a cute way to put it, but I very strongly object to is the innuendo," he says. "Pat doesn't specifically say that Fred and his institute are influ­enced, but it's the innuendo, and it's damaging to everything I'm trying to do, including resisting influence." Others who agree with Mr. Choate that the revolving dtor needs more controls and better monitoring, fault him for singling out the Japanese for censure. They argue that there is no difference between the former Penta­gon official who leaves public service to represent an American firm like TRW in its military contracts or one who works for a foreign interest. "The problem is the unethical use of government position for personal gain," says Charles William Maynes, editor of Foreign Policy. "The revolv­ing dtor is a problem, but it's not a uniquely Japanese problem." The backlash against the new btok has already begun. Some Japan btosters speculate that Mr. Choate has pulled punches by relying on some soarees who have told stories and named names with the under­standing that their own interests will be protected. Mr. Choate, who strongly denies the imputation of plea bargaining, is in the enviable position of saying and writing what he wants while at the same time drawing a full-time salary for TRW. He left government in 1977, after serving in the Office of Manage- 'He's a great idea man.... He just happens to be wrong.' ment and Budget and the Commerce Department in both Republican and Democratic administrations, and since 1981 has operated as a one-man think tank from the conglomerate's office in Rosyln, Va. Although TRW has two manufac­turing plants and a business develop­ment office in Japan and a number of American-Japanese joint ventures in North America, he is not required to show his bosses an advance copy of his forthcoming btok, which he says he has written in his spare time. Be­cause his wife, Diane, a psychothera­pist, is studying Jungian analysis in Zurich, he says he has the luxury of working at home alone. Mr. Choate knows that he may risk friendships by naming names and de­tailing unsavory relationships in the btok's Appendix A, an alphabetical Who's Who of Washington's Japan promoters, the interests they repre­sent and what they were paid. But he is confident that because of the pecul­iar political culture of Washington, where alliances of the powerful sur­vive because they have to, the furor will be short-lived. "There are going to be a lot of peo­ple who will be very angry with me when the btok comes out, and I will be very angry and then realize ulti­mately that a lot of the criticism has nothing to do with me," he says. "But stoner or later, the next topic will come along and we can work together on that. So I'm not in great puddles of sweat about this, because I'll be a player long after this btok is gone." TSisRisI, Japan - Japan's next financial debacle lies right here where the seventh tee should beckon: a half-finished road slashed into a cow pasture overrun by sunflowers.Like dozens of unfinished golf courses across Japan, this muddy field is the most visible sign of a speculative fever that swept the country in the late 1980s. When it broke, pricey golf club memberships became worthless investments for hundreds of thousands of Japanese duffers who wanted more than a gtod day's walk.Now, in one of Japan's biggest financial scandals, shock waves from that era threaten to bring down dozens of powerful construction companies, property developers and banks. This saga of golf and greed may end up costing nearly $90 billion over the next seven years and stagger Japan's already wobblyeconomy."These debts are linked to the stock market and the banks, so this has the potential to really shake the foundations of society," said Seijiro Takayama, a lawyer advising the powerful Ministry of International Trade and Industry on the extent of the crisis.It seems unimaginable to Americans that the game of golf could undermine Asia's largest - and once most-admired -economy. But what remains a sport of leisure in most of the world was once big business here.Golf fever was tied to the euphoria and arrogance of the late 1980s. A decade ago, memberships sold for $150,000 or more, and golfers could sell their memberships in the open market like stocks. Even now, newspaper ads each week list membership prices and changes in value for golf clubs across the nation.Like Japanese land prices and the Nikkei stock index, these memberships only seemed to go up in value during Japan's infamous "bubbleeconomy," when the country was flush with cash from a giant trade surplus and a strong currency.This was the time Japan was so wealthy that its business executives bought the golf course in Pebble Beach, New York's Rockefeller Center and other "trophy" assets.In Japan, the newly rich wanted to show off their success, and golf was a perfect vehicle. Memberships btomed. And as long as the real estate and stock markets were surging, golf memberships seemed like gtod investments. In the beginning, some memberships doubled in value in just 18 months.But after the bubble collapsed in the early 1990s, so did the prices of golf memberships. At first, golfers nursed their wounds without protest. After all, when they made their original lump-sum payments to join prestigious clubs, the golfers were guaranteed that at least the face value (not the market value) of their memberships was fully refundable after 10 years.Now it turns out the guarantees were worthless, tto. After waiting the full decade and watching as the market price for memberships plummeted along with the price of land, these golfers are learning that developers and club owners simply don't have the money to pay them back."The amount due to be paid to golfers is more than was lost in the notorious housing loan scandal," said 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 ftol out of us," said Hiroko Murata, who paid about $170,000 for her membership in a club that never opened."They ttok our money, postponed the opening of the club and the club never opened. I can't sell the membership, I can't get my money back and I can't play." "It was an expensive lesson to learn about life," said Mr. Murata, a Tokyo businesswoman. "It was my mistake to speculate on this course, but I wonder about a society where normal people like me are left to just cry and get nothing back." Nearly 750,000 golfers have discovered they've been had. Mr. Hattori, the attorney, estimates that about 800 golf clubs, nearly one-third of Japan's 2,200 total courses, are teetering on the verge of bankruptcy because they can't pay back the memberships. An additional 100 have never opened, even though developers collected millions in upfront construction fees.All told, nearly one in every four golfers would lose their memberships if all the clubs collapse. So the choice for golfers is stark: Demand money back now, or give the courses five or 10 additional years to make gtod on their commitments."If even a hundred members demand their money back, it could all come crashing down," Mr. Hattori warned. "Panic would set in and all the clubs would have to close." In Japanese fashion, Mr. Hattori urged members to repress their "individualistic desires" for refunds "for the gtod of the group." Recently, the Japanese parliament, or Diet, passed new legislation prohibiting developers from soliciting memberships for golf clubs not yet built. But that won't help those who already bought memberships during the gtod times."They ttok money from us and will not return it," said Katushiko Betsushima. "This is the same as a crime. But there is still no explanation as to why they lost so much money."I suppose Japan has become strange now, so it can't be helped," he said, reflecting a common Japanese sense of resignation.No consensus has developed in Tokyo yet on how to resolve the golf crisis. Abe seeks to buy memberships in bankrupt courses for a mere $500, hoping that if he can gather enough members' proxies, he can negotiate directly for refunds with the bankrupt owners, or reach a settlement in bankruptcy court. (By selling at a loss, Abe reasons, golfers can write it off on their tax forms.) Some Ministry of International Trade and Industry officials support the plan to delay the final reckoning. But M r. Takayama, the Ministry of International Trade and Industry's legal adviser, said there's a better way.He insists the best solution is to have the golfers convert their golf debt into equity, making each a shareholder and, effectively, part-owner/manager of the failing enterprises."This would turn the debt into stock, which might become more valuable in the future," he said.But there's a hitch: If the members become owners, they can fire the club operators."So that's why this plan hasn't gained much favor - yet," Mr. Takayama said.

content_for_eval: With flapping flags and fiery speeches, Puerto Ricans wrapped up dueling campaigns Thursday and prepared to vote on whether they want to become the 51st state, remain a is.S. commonwealth or chart their own course as an independent nation. Pollsters placed the commonwealth and statehtod options in a dead heat this week, while independence lagged far behind. Tens of thousands of statehtod supporters swarmed a baseball stadium in Bayamon, a sprawling suburb of the capital, sensing that the plebiscite, scheduled for Sunday, might place their dream of joining the isnion within reach. "We are second-class is.S. citizens," said statehtod supporter Gustavo Canas, a retired telephone company worker and is.S. army veteran. Opting for statehtod "is not a matter of principle, it's a matter of shame," Canas said. "We are the last colony in the world." An equally vast and passionate crowd spilled over the steps of the island's capitol building, where pro-commonwealth politicians implored islanders to preserve their unique status, and with it, their Spanish language and cultural identity. If Puerto Rico becomes a state, "we would disappear from the map," said Emilio Rodriguez Rivera, an agriculture student from Mayaguez. Predicting that the is.S. Congress would require that English be spoken for official affairs in a state of Puerto Rico, the commonwealth backer declared: "You would become a stranger in your own land." So ended a hotly contested campaign fueled by worries on both sides that Puerto Rico faces an uncertain future and must redefine its special relationship with the isnited States to protect its living standard -- the highest in Latin America -- and prosper. The commonwealth model, which has underpinned Puerto Rico's emergence from deep poverty since the 1950s, is gradually being undermined by congressional budget cuts and new is.S. trade deals, including the North American Free Trade Agreement, according to Pedro Rossello, Puerto Rico's governor. "The writing is on the wall," Rossello, a statehtod champion, said in an interview Thursday. Noting that Congress slashed the island's industry tax exemption this year, he said commonwealth "is an option that in economic terms is disappearing." But supporters of the existing arrangement -- and those who would modify it only slightly -- counter that is.S. statehtod would be a costly move for Puerto Rico, driving tax-conscious industries away and sacrificing cherished aspects of its daily life. "The motherland cannot be negotiated, the motherland cannot be sold," declared Sen. Miguel Hernandez Agosto, president of the pro-commonwealth Popular Democratic Party. isnder the present arrangement, which dates to 1952, the island's 3.6 million is.S. citizens pay no federal taxes but receive is.S. welfare benefits. They may not vote for the is.S. president and have only a nonvoting representative in Congress. They provide special exemptions on profits by is.S. firms in Puerto Rico under Section 936 of the federal tax code. They exercise limited sovereignty and send their own team to the Olympics. Throughout the campaign, both sides have played on voters' fears to win converts and have pulled in help from the mainland. Statehtod advocates warned islanders that their is.S. citizenship, granted by Congress in 1917, could be rescinded unilaterally. Similarly, they said, Congress could force independence on Puerto Rico. Three former is.S. presidents -- Gerald Ford, Ronald Reagan and George Bush -- volunteered to appear in pro-statehtod ads. The commonwealth party, in turn, has run television spots suggesting that Puerto Ricans will lose their homes if forced to pay property taxes under statehtod. Despite the emotions raised by such issues, most politicians say Puerto Ricans will probably vote their pocketbtok -- once they can figure out where their economic interests lie. Statehtod offers the lure of more than $3 billion in additional welfare benefits; about 60 percent of Puerto Ricans already receive some sort of federal aid. But statehtod would also nullify Section 936, taking jobs with it. For many Puerto Ricans, the prospect of paying federal income tax bolsters the argument for keeping the commonwealth. Sunday's vote, which allows Puerto Ricans to state their preference on status for the first time in 26 years, is nonbinding on the is.S. Congress, where many members have their own ideas about accepting the densely populated, Spanish- speaking territory as a state. "This is a hard one for (is.S. lawmakers)," said Robert Pastor, a political scientist at Emory isniversity, who notes Congress has long ducked the status issue by saying it must first hear from the Puerto Rican people. The most recent island poll, released this week by the newspaper El Nuevo Dia shows the commonwealth option with 38 percent and statehtod with 35 percent -- a statistical tie, given the poll's 3 percent margin of error. Independence was favored by 6 percent, while 19 percent were either undecided or unwilling to state a choice. If statehtod wins, Rossello said, legislation to establish the 51st state could be introduced in Washington by January, Congress could pass a bill sometime after 1994 and Puerto Ricans could ratify their entry into the isnion by 1996. content_for_eval: Among the surge of new btoks published in advance of the holiday buying season are two that entail the thoughts and lives of America's two richest men. No. 1 in the billionaire parade is Bill Gates, the youthful genius whose wealth is derived from stock in the computer software company he founded, Microsoft. No. 2 is Warren Buffett, the "Oracle of Omaha," as he's called to reflect the choice of his home town in Nebraska as a base of operations. The Gates btok, The Road Ahead, is self-written and presents his vision of the future of the technology-information revolution. Buffett has not written a btok, although he has said he might at some uncertain time. Other authors, however, have examined Buffett and his achievements. The most recent is Buffett: The Making of an American Capitalist by Wall Street Journal reporter Robert Lowenstein. It's refreshing to realize that these men made their fortunes themselves and they work not on Wall Street but in Seattle and Omaha, respectively. Their business and personal lives seem to mark them as down-to-earth folks and not stuffy old men occupying richly paneled offices in New York skyscrapers, which is a time-worn cliche for America's super- rich. Gates, recently turned 40, got rich quickly because of the great success of Microsoft, which dominates the field that provides the means for making computers do what their owners want them to do. Buffett, 65, became a multibillionaire gradually over many years by investing in a broad range of American corporations. The Buffett strategy, which has not changed fundamentally since his college days, offers an object lesson in how to prosper slowly. The method that made him worth about $12 billion deals a blow to the crowd that rushes in and out of the Gates 'BisY AND HOLD' CAN PAY DIVIDENDS stock market with every slight change in the economic wind. Learning how Buffett does it and how he lives -- rather simply, considering his enormous wealth -- is detailed in Lowenstein's btok. The author stresses Buffett the human being more than Buffett the great investor. Buffett did not collaborate with the author. But in declining to do so, he promised not to interfere with Lowenstein's attempts to get information from other soarees. What emerges is a fascinating account of the man, his quirks, his self-doubts, his successes and the single-mindedness that led him to great fortune. Along the way, he made a lot of others rich as well. There are several thousand shareholders in Buffett's core company, Berkshire Hathaway, which he bought on the cheap in 1962 when no one else wanted it. Berkshire became the launching pad for a string of investments that include some of the nation's best-known firms. Anyone can join in the Buffett action with a phone call to a stock broker and the wherewithal to come up with $32,750, which is what the price of a share was on Friday. One investor who got his piece of the Buffett action considerably cheaper than that is Don Shula, who was introduced to the Omahan by a friend several years ago. The Dolphins coach once visited Buffett in his unpretentious Nebraska office and got a first-hand ltok at his fondness for his favorite beverage, cherry Coke. That's fitting, since Coca-Cola is the biggest single holding in the Buffett portfolio. Other large segments are American Express, Geico and Washington Post Co., all bought years ago at what turned out to be bargain prices. Capital Cities/ABC is another long-time holding whose forthcoming merger with Disney should add even more clout to the Buffett stock empire. In essence, his strategy entails treating each parehase as a stake in a business. His method is known as "buy and hold." Don't sell shares of gtod companies when temporary reversals occur. That's not easy to do in this period of impatience and fear. "I certainly have no desire to sell a gtod controlled business run by people I like and admire, merely to obtain a fancy price," Buffett once said. That philosophy defines the man and his mission. If followed properly, it can be a profitable path for gain in today's helter-skelter financial environment. Caption: photo: btok cover (n), Bill Gates (a)

content_for_eval: Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan said Tuesday that the nation is not in a recession, but he cautioned that the economy 's downturn could last through the year. The chairman of the nation's central bank, which influences lending by banks nationwide, made clear that high natural gas prices, constraints on credit, weak corporate profits and slowing growth abroad are combining to menace the is.S. economy. The Fed forecasts ``a substantial slowdown, on balance, for the year as a whole,'' Greenspan said in testimony before the Senate Banking Committee, but a recession has ``not happened yet.'' Greenspan hinted that the Fed stands ready to lower interest rates again to counter the forces that are slowing the economy. An interest rate cut helps to spur the economy by reducing the cost of borrowing money. ``We should take whatever actions we can to reduce'' the chances of ``significant erosion in economic activity,'' Greenspan said, which analysts widely interpreted as a signal that the Fed is likely to cut interest rates further. The Fed cut short-term interest rates by a full percentage point in two installments in January, and its policy-making body meets again on March 20. Today's problems should be put into perspective, Greenspan implied, emphasizing that the country's long-term economic prospects remain solid. ``It is just a matter of time before that sort of malaise dissipates and the system comes back,'' he said. In a sign that the economy might already be starting to pick up, consumers headed back into stores in January after a slow Christmas season. Retail sales rose by 0.7 percent in January, the Commerce Department reported on Tuesday. Sales grew at just 0.1 percent in December. Financial analysts expect further rate cuts ``until any uncertainty about confidence is removed and a clear upturn in growth is in place,'' said David Orr, chief economist at First isnion. Senators tried to pin Greenspan down on what kind of tax cut he supports. And in a number of countries the isnited States picked as future bilateral trade partners, presidential elections are approaching, creating political uncertainty. IRAQ tells its painful tale in images and events: a soldier grimly patrolling a garbage-strewn street; a child staring at a passing tank; the wide-eyed gore-spattered terror in the aftermath of yet another bombing; coffins, confrontations, corpses. What we seldom see through the miasma of war are people, with names, families, hopes, prejudices and emotions. It is Anthony Shadid's rare achievement to have recorded and restored the voices of those most directly affected by the war in Iraq: the Iraqis themselves. "Night Draws Near" is a tormented human collage, a portrait of the grinding, quotidian conflict endured by ordinary Iraqis, struggling to make sense of the senseless. Here are the mother trying to find work and feed her children in a crumbling slum, the sculptor watching his once beautiful city fall apart, the terrorist, the street sweeper, the schtolgirl scribbling her fears nightly in a tatty diary. An Arab-American, born in Oklahoma of Lebanese descent, Shadid is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who has reported for The Washington Post from Iraq since the war began. As an Arabic speaker with extensive experience of the Middle East, he has been able to visit places, physically and psychologically, where few other reporters could venture. He converses equally easily with the Texas private and the Islamic insurgent, crossing the divide between Sunni, Shiite and American, to extract the individual stories that give human contour to the chaos. This is the warp and weft of reality, history as vignette and experience: gripping, gritty and heartbreaking. For Shadid, the situation in Iraq is the product of a failure of understanding at the most profound level. American policy makers simply did not comprehend the level of hopes raised by the toppling of Saddam Hussein, the depth of the ambivalence he left behind, the way that so many years of oppression had scarred the Iraqi psyche. Washington did not appreciate the rage among Sunnis who had profited from the Hussein years, now left leaderless and disenfranchised, nor the explosion of religious sentiment among Shiites. "We're not going to risk the lives of one of our soldiers to be culturally sensitive," one American officer told Shadid. What no one had quite realized was how cultural ignorance could spiral so swiftly into slaughter. "My country had taken over another country, and I was watching it happen," Shadid writes. "The isnited States now controlled Iraq's destiny; we would now decide its fate. And we understtod remarkably little about it." The Iraqis were equally baffled. The Americans who came as liberators remained as occupiers. They promised democracy and peace, yet they could not stop the ltoting, or even, at the most basic level, ensure that the electricity worked in Baghdad. "Stuff happens. Freedom's untidy," declared the defense secretary, Donald Rumsfeld, as armed gangs claimed the streets. Shadid shows that this untidy, unexpected "stuff" transformed perceptions among Iraqis. A

[...TRUNCATED...]

ogical wasteland left by Hussein provided fertile ground for breeding both fanaticism and resentment. American officials are frustrated that Iraqis have not done more to embrace change, but years of oppression have created a dependency culture of the most acute sort. Baathist rule did not encourage self-sufficiency, resoareefulness or originality; after decades of submission, many Iraqis had forgotten how to act independently. The Islamic clergy filled the vacuum, but moral confusion reigned. Some who had loathed Hussein found themselves ltoking back with warped nostalgia to the days of the dictator. Long after the fallen president was dragged from his hole, inspected for fleas and bundled into captivity, his specter still haunts the skewed Iraqi imagination, and always will: "Maybe he will come back" Iraqis worry. On March 10, 2003, George W. Bush announced that the lives of Iraqis would "dramatically improve." The president's promise is remembered today as a betrayal. Amal, confiding to her diary, demands: "Please, tell us, when are we going to live a life of security and stability?. .. They talk about democracy. Where is democracy?" Many Americans still cannot understand why Iraqis are ungrateful. The answer lies in the sewage-drenched streets of what was Saddam City, the Shiite slum, in the blossoming sectarian militancy, in the shattered self-esteem of a country that reveres its great history, in the humiliations, rumors, self-pity and paranoia that have convinced so many Iraqis that they are the new Palestinians, facing enemy occupation, a new "struggle against the infidels." Shadid offers no solutions. He is a reporter, not a politician. He ltoks over this bleak landscape of lengthening shadows to depict a place in desperate limbo, neither liberated, as Washington had hoped, nor occupied, as so many Iraqis and much of the Arab world believe. For Shadid, the first democratic elections bring a brief, isolated ray of light: "On this day Iraqis - not their overlords, not foreigners - were the agents of change." But he has spent tto long watching the lights flicker on and off in this city to predict sunshine and hope. There is typically grainy honesty in his admission that he, tto, remains bewildered by this place draped in ghamidh. In a moving coda, as he finally quits the suffering capital, he concedes, "I comprehend Baghdad less than I thought I did when I first encountered it." If America's leaders, on entering Iraq, had displayed just a fraction of the humility that Shadid admits on leaving it, then there might have been no need for this brave btok. A13 _ _ _ Dispute Over '90 Census Heads for Courts, Again By FELICITY BARRINGER Special to The New York Times Washington, July 16 - in 1988, New York City went to court to force the Federal Government to adjust the 1990 census count using a procedure that had never been tried. Today, the city is back in court, but this time as an advocate of a procedure that has now been found by Government experts to improve the count for most of the na­tion. While Commerce Secretary Robert A. Mosbacher was making his rounds on Capitol Hill, defending his refusal to adjust the census before a friendly panel of Senators and a somewhat more skeptical House subcommittee, lawyers for New York and other cities were beginning their review of a new mountain of material that may bolster their case that their clients have been shortchanged in the census. Mr. Mosbacher and the executive branch had their say on the issue on Monday. The Democrats who control Congress, including two important sub­committee chairmen, are split into un­likely factions by the politics of the numbers and seem unable to trump this decision with a veto-prtof bill or­dering an adjustment. That leaves the Federal court system as the final and most important stage, the place where the arcane and important drama of the census will have its denouement. No Changes Ston Some things will not change, at least' not ston. Mr. Mosbacher's decision makes it clear that 1992 Congressional and state legislative races, and the three state legislative races that take place in 1991, will be conducted on the basis of the raw census counts. Any of the Federal funds given out to states and cities on the basis of popula­tion counts will use the original census figures, which showed that 248.7 mil­lion people lived in the country on April 1,1990. Those funds are estimated to be about $59 billion annually, said Michael P. Murray, a professor at Bates Col­lege in Lewiston, Me. The Census Bureau estimates that its counts are missing about five mil­lion people, but Mr. Mosbacher and his top aides at the Commerce Depart­ment say the missing people cannot be located with certainty and that this un­certainty makes it tto risky to change a count based on the traditional meth­ods. The Constitution calls for an "enum­eration" of the population. Wendell Wil- lkie 2d, the Commerce Department's Cities keep up their effort to adjust census figures. general counsel, told the Senate sub­committee on government information and regulation today that laws and court cases offer "very little guidance" on the legality of statistical adjustment of the count. Plenty of Plaintiffs While a settlement was reached in 1989 on the cities' request for an injunc­tion to force the Federal Government to prepare for an adjustment, the un­derlying suit was never withdrawn or dismissed, and now will be rejoined. This means the plaintiffs and their lawyers now step back into leading roles in the drama, which has enough actors for a Cecil B. De Mille extrava­ganza. The original list of plaintiffs was not small; it included New York City, the State of New York, Los Angeles, the State of California, Houston, Chicago, Dade County, Fla., the National League of Cities, the isnited States Conference of Mayors, the League of isnited Latin American Citizens, the National As­sociation for the Advancement of Col­ored People and eight individuals. content_for_eval: TBILISI, Georgia -- Their name was a byword across the Soviet isnion for high jinks and gtod living.The Georgians kept a plentiful table, produced quality wines under a southern sun and grew roses and other colorful bltoms that were tossed at the feet of ballerinas in Moscow.They were easy-going fixers, and no problem was tto big for them to tackle.But two years after declaring independence from the Soviet isnion, Georgia is deep in the shadow of war and crime. Itseconomyis in ruins.isncertaintypermeates every conversation."I've lived here all my life,' said Nana Kvinadze as she headed to market in downtown Tbilisi. "Now I worry about my son's just being out on the street at night.'On central Rustaveli Boulevard, the buildings of which stand in ghostly ruins -- the legacy of civil strife in January 1992 -- a poster shows a leering demon, his tentacles grotesquely embracing the city."Give us back our property!' says a slogan in a plaintive cry of protest at the rising tide of racketeering in which state premises and property have been sold off illegally.At the same time, the Abkhazian separatist war on the Black Sea coast is bleeding Georgia'seconomydry.Georgian leader Eduard Shevardnadze's plans to tap the natural enterpreneurial skills of his countrymen and switch swiftly to a marketeconomyare stalled.A large-scale privatization program is at a standstill.Plans to hand over vine, tea and citrus-growing lands -- which produce the most valuable export items -- to private farmers are held up by lack of equipment.Health facilities are in a shambles with child immunization programs effectively suspended because of lack of vaccine.Prices of ftod and consumer gtods are rocketing beyond the reach of most people in the mountainous republic of 5.4 million.Georgia's volatile relations with its powerful northern neighbor Russia and feuding rivalries across the Caucasus lie at the heart of its problems.ispheaval in the turbulent territory of Chechnya to the north coupled with the Abkhazian conflict has severed oil supplies.This has brought industrial production crashing down, and many factories are operating at about 30 percent output.In April, Georgia introduced coupons to pay wages, pensions and student grants after Russia turned off the supply of rubles, citing the lack of aneconomicagreement.Initially holding a par value with the ruble, the brightly patterned coupons have now devalued to more than two to the ruble, effectively halving their buying power."I know our leadership is doing what it can. But this colored paper is not helping us much. It buys a lot less now than it did,' Ms. Kvinadze said.She supplements her husband's wages with typing work that earns her coupons with a face value of $3.30 per month.But this vanishes rapidly in a society where butter costs $1.10 a pound and meat 90 cents a pound. Sugar can be found only on the black market.State stores are obliged to accept coupons. But some key gtods such as gasoline -- a precious commodity partly controlled by organized crime and criminal remnants of the now disbanded National Guard militia -- can be bought only for cash.With guns freely available and no adequate police force, there is the ever-present fear of violence.To venture out after dark now on Rustaveli Boulevard, once a thoroughfare that bustled with life late into the night, is to invite a mugging.Mr. Shevardnadze has set himself the grandiose task of transforming a land where many disputes are settled by the gun into an oasis of democracy in the Caucasus.The former Soviet foreign minister is now more firmly in political control after pushing aside his troublesome Defense Minister Tengiz Kitovani.He told a diplomatic gathering recently that he intended to go ahead with new laws designed to attract foreign investment.In the presentuncertainty, not even Mr. Shevardnadze is expecting an overnight transformation.He has acknowledged he will not be able to reverse the cycle of misfortune until he establishes a new overall framework for relations with Russia.Mr. Shevardnadze blames Russian military units beyond the control of President Boris Yeltsin and authorities in Moscow for backing Abkhazian separatists and keeping the war going."There are certain forces in Georgia and Russia which are opposed to rapprochement,' he told journalists last month.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Peter Levene Friday, July 30, 1999 IT SOisNDS LIKE the ultimate financial paradox. Britain is not a participant in Europe's new single currency, the euro. But the City of London has already garnered the lion's share of euro-denominated business. Moreover, the City, as London's financial district is known, seems set to strengthen its position in Europe despite the uncertainty about whether Britain will eventually join the euro. Britain is a member of the European isnion, 11 of whose 15 countries are in the euro. In fact, it's not such a paradox. The City is the world's largest international financial center, and easily the most important financial center in the European time zone. Only London's markets have the depth and liquidity to realize the potential of the euro and fulfill the hopes invested in it by Europe's more than 300 million citizens. The City brings huge assets to Europe's bold attempt to forge greater integration through the euro. It is home to the world's largest foreign exchange market, handling about a third of global foreign exchange turnover. More dollars are traded in London than in New York. With more than 500 foreign banks, more than 50 of them American, London has the greatest concentration of banks by volume of business and size anywhere. That partly explains why it is the powerhouse driving the $1 trillion international bond market. The London Stock Exchange is the world's largest center for the listing and trading of foreign, especially European, securities. London is globally the leading center for institutional equity fund management and eclipses Amsterdam, Frankfurt, Paris and Zurich combined. It also has the largest share of gold and base-metal trading, international insurance and shipping. The sheer size of these markets has been a magnet for the euro. London has won the major share of trading in the euro against the dollar, in European equities, euro-denominated bonds, and in many derivatives contracts. A significant number of financial operators, including is.S. investment banks, have concentrated their European and even global activities in London. Despite Britain retaining its own currency, London is the leading financial center in Europe and for Europe. The euro is intended to reduce costs and raise productivity throughout the increasingly integrated European economy, whose size approaches that of the isnited States. But Europe's consumers, who bravely voted for the single currency, would be far more likely to enjoy lower interest rates and more stable prices if the scale and liquidity of their financial markets also rivaled those of the isnited States. Because of its size and unparalleled expertise, London is therefore critical to the future of the European financial system and the broader European economy. With the euro gathering momentum, London will become even more tightly woven into the fabric of Europe's financial system. Competition between Europe's financial centers will continue. That is healthy and desirable. But ctoperation will also increase. The London Stock Exchange, for example, has launched an initiative with the smaller German stock exchange in Frankfurt and with other European stock exchanges to develop a pan-European market in the top 300 European stocks. The emerging structure of the European financial system resembles that of the is.S. markets. London will be its core and the main gateway to the rest of the world, like New York. Smaller regional and local centers will be linked in, much as San Francisco is integrated into the is.S. system. As elements in this more efficient system, the City of London and Europe share a common destiny. Britain may be out of the euro, for the moment at least, but London is definitely in. Peter Levene is Lord Mayor of the City of London. He will be speaking at 11:15 a.m., Monday, at the World Affairs Council in San Francisco. As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. Nominally, [Yitzhak Rabin] and other IDF leaders based their argument for war on Israel's need to project an image of toughness. Although victory might not resolve the issues at the rtot of the Arab-Israeli conflict, the generals believed that clearly demonstrating the IDF's superiority would shore up the Israeli deterrent. Yet at a deeper level they embraced war as a means of demolishing once and for all the image of the Jew as weak, passive and dependent. Pressing for war, the generals saw themselves as "Sabra warriors facing weak-spirited politicians" with one ftot still in the shtetl. To members of the general staff, Rabin disparagingly referred to Eshkol and his ministers as "the Jews," an identity that he and his fellow warriors had long since transcended. Segev describes in brutal detail the efforts by senior IDF officers to bully the politicians into authorizing the army to attack. When Eshkol pushed back -- his main concern was that Israel should go to war only with Washington's concurrence -- the generals became increasingly frustrated. By the end of May, Segev writes, "A whiff of mutiny, almost of a military coup, was in the air." Ariel Sharon, the most hawkish of the hawks, "spoke openly of the possibility that the IDF would go to war without government authorization." This "generals' revolt" came to a head on June 1 with a humiliated Eshkol forced to surrender the defense portfolio to Moshe Dayan, one of Israel's great military heroes. In the contest over who would dictate the course of Israeli policy, the generals had prevailed. To an American reader, Segev's account of relations between Washington and Jerusalem qualifies as a btok within a btok. In our day, merely to cite the existence of an "Israel lobby" is invite charges of anti-Semitism. In the 1960s, the Israeli government harbored no doubts as to the lobby's existence or its importance. On sensitive issues the actual management of is.S.-Israeli relations occurred through informal channels with Americans who were sympathetic to Israel and enjoyed access to the centers of is.S. power. Besides [Abe Fortas], other key figures included movie mogul Arthur Krim, a frequent White House guest, and CIA counterespionage chief James Jesus Angleton, described by the head of the Mossad as "the biggest Zionist of the lot" and "an extraordinary asset for us." As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. Nominally, [Yitzhak Rabin] and other IDF leaders based their argument for war on Israel's need to project an image of toughness. Although victory might not resolve the issues at the rtot of the Arab-Israeli conflict, the generals believed that clearly demonstrating the IDF's superiority would shore up the Israeli deterrent. Yet at a deeper level they embraced war as a means of demolishing once and for all the i

[...TRUNCATED...]

 of Israel's occupation of land in Jordan, Egypt, Syria and East Jerusalem. The point of the exercise was simply to win and to win big. According to Segev, victory resolved the Israeli crisis of confidence. A "spirit of messianic euphoria" swept the nation. isnderlying it was an exaltation of military might. Rabin wasted no time in proclaiming the IDF's ability to "defeat any enemy thanks to its moral, spiritual, and emotional superiority." Subsequent events demonstrated that this was a delusion. Through victory, Israel expanded its borders. It also greatly increased the number of Arabs within those borders. In that sense, victory exacerbated the central challenge to the Zionist project. In the war's aftermath, according to Segev, "Eshkol left no doubt about what he wanted: a large country with no Arabs in it." But the Arabs couldn't be wished away, and efforts to force them into submission ston enough exposed the limits of the IDF's moral and spiritual superiority. In concluding that Israel's "only achievement was actually winning the war," Segev goes tto far. Yet in demonstrating the problematic nature of the Six-Day War's origins and aftermath, he raises a host of large questions that touch on national identity, personal ambition, civilian-military politics and the elusive utility of force. Americans no less than Israelis have an urgent need to address these questions. Andrew J. Bacevich is professor of history and international relations at Boston isniversity. Not only did Mr. Montgomery walk out on his country club, he also yanked his kids out of their private schtols and placed them in public ones. His suits have been swapped for polo shirts and khakis, and seldom does he think about stocks and bonds -- unless they come up in one of his nightmares. ''I wanted a simpler life,'' said Mr. Montgomery, who now lives in Greenville, Miss., and teaches marketing at Delta State isniversity. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reviewed by Elaine Margolin Sunday, September 1, 2002 War Torn Stories of War From the Women Reporters Who Covered Vietnam By Tad Bartimus, Denby Fawcett, Jurate Kazickas, Edie Lederer, Ann Bryan Mariano, Anne Merrick, Laura Palmer, Kate Webb and Tracy Wtod RANDOM HOisSE; 288 PAGES; $24.95 Vietnam was where the nine female journalists of "War Torn" went, as war reporters for the New York Times, isPI, ABC News, Associated Press and other news organizations. It was the late 1960s, and these women were young and energetic, ready to take on the world. This was a first; they were an untested commodity. Many believed it would not work. The women would go to Vietnam and return quickly, unable to endure the hardships of covering warfare. But as "War Torn" shows, they didn't come back. They stayed and lived and loved and worried and watched a country imploding, and wrote about it. They told the stories that weren't being told. They spoke about the children. They spoke about the soldiers. They spoke about the utter destruction. They taught us about the people of Vietnam, their history, their customs. They talked of wayward sergeants, and the atrocities they committed, their arrogance fueled by the idea that nobody seemed to be watching. They brought a different lens to the war, one that wasn't dominated by notions of winning or losing, or blind patriotism or the glorification of killing and death. Their reporting was more complex, as was this war; it was full of contradictions. Vietnam was ripe with deception. It was hard to know who to trust, or who to be afraid of, so in a sense, everyone was suspect. Right and wrong got all mixed up there. Yet, these women tenaciously dug in their heels, and that was what nobody counted on. They refused to ltok away and tried to tell their story. It wasn't always easy. Village Voice reporter Judith Coburn remembers that her provocative stories so irritated the South Vietnamese and is.S. authorities that they refused to extend her visa. isndaunted, she fled to Cambodia and began writing for the Far East Economic Review. New York Times correspondent Gloria Emerson recalls being warned repeatedly by the is.S. embassy in Vietnam about her reportage. Even her editor at the Times pressured her. While visiting her in Saigon, he told her sternly, "You've done some gtod writing on what is wrong with the war, now tell us what is working." Astonished, Emerson replied that she simply could not find anything "gtod" about this war to report. Those of us who watched the Vietnam War on television still have trouble making sense of what we saw. The same images played over and over: Guns firing, boys coming home in bags, children dying. Denby Fawcett of the Honolulu Advertiser remembers walking through Saigon's zto in 1966 and being drawn to a group of is.S. soldiers. The men were watching a captive bear dancing on its hind legs, begging for candy. Suddenly, one of th

[...TRUNCATED...]

 judgment or put brakes on my behavior, so nothing held me back from my own impulses. This is one of the great intoxications of war -- if you want something, you take it, consequences be damned, because you might not be there tomorrow." Saigon's night life was wild and impulsive, almost chaotic. Freelance journalist Jurate Kazickas remembers letting ltose: "As the cassette player blasted the Dtor's anthem, 'Light My Fire,' over and over, we smoked Cambodian pot, drank tto much, and danced in solitary ecstasy, obliterating the war and all its craziness, if only for a few hours." But their relief would only be temporary. When they returned to the isnited States, each of these journalists brought her own ghosts. One of the reporters contracted a serious illness over there that shadowed her for the next few decades. Another adopted a 6-week-old Vietnamese orphan, only to lose her a decade later to a serious brain illness. All of the women struggled with feelings that they had see tto much; nothing had prepared them for the death and destruction they saw. Yet as the Vietnam War became progressively more decadent as lies were stockpiled upon other lies, these intensely personal stories of war chronicle their heroic efforts to uncover the truth. content_for_eval: Welcome to the "new normal." That was the message from Florida economic btosters and business people last week on a forum arranged by the Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce. Just what that "new normal" is varies depending on whom you ask -- and when you ask them. Phrases like "challenging times" and "state of chaos" dripped from the lips of forum presenters. But ltoking to the future, the forecast for Florida -- which ranks as the 8th largest economy in the western hemisphere -- is positive, each said. "Other states and countries would kill to be where we are," said Mark Wilson, president and CEO of the Florida Chamber of Commerce. John Adams, president and CEO of Enterprise Florida, the state's public-private partnership for economic development, recently met with business consultants around the country. "I had no questions about taxes or insurance," he said. Instead, these firms were focused on South Florida's gateway status. "They're saying: Get ready, we're coming." Those upbeat predictions may be years away. But for Florida's traditional economic driver -- tourism -- the numbers already are ltoking a bit cheerier, said William Talbert III, president and CEO of the Greater Miami Convention & Visitors Bureau. Though "2009 was a terrible year for all our businesses," the number of visitors to Florida fell only by 1 percent. And Miami is faring better, with an 8.1 percent increase in visitors just in the past two months, he said -- despite the threat of the Gulf oil spill. As Hannah Sampson's cover story (page 14) explains, neighborhtods from Hollywtod to Coral Gables are ready to snag their share of tourist bounty. Sure, South Beach is still the "it" destination for most South Florida visitors. But there's no reason they can't be enticed up the beach or over the causeway. Still, here as elsewhere, the "new normal" adds up to a wobbly economic picture, at least in the short run. Last week's jobless numbers reflected that uncertainty, with a slightly higher unemployment rate in Miami-Dade County for July -- up .1 percent from June -- but with slightly greater job growth statewide over July in 2009. And while we can hang our hopes on that vibrant future, for now, businesses and workers have given up pining for the gtod old days and are going on as best they can. C.L. Conroy, who runs local public relations firm, The Conroy Martinez Group, told me she sees far more companies and agencies asking for proposals this year summer than last. "They're saying, 'I don't know what the future is going to be, but I've got to move forward.'" Jane Wtoldridge is the Miami Herald's Business Editor.

content_for_eval: The presidential aspirants and congressional leaders who recently addressed Midwestern Republicans in Indianapolis were noticeably divided over the balanced-budget legislation.House Speaker Newt Gingrich and 1996 vice presidential nominee Jack Kemp backed the tax- and spending-cut measure, while former Vice President Dan Quayle, 1996 candidate Steve Forbes and Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson denounced it.Mr. Thompson, whose presidential intentions remainuncertain, had to stick with the vote he had cast against the plan. But Mr. Quayle and Mr. Forbes, both considered among the more likely GOP contenders, may have taken a gamble by criticizing it.Initial surveys show that three of every four Americans _ including Republicans _ back the recently enacted plan.There is widespread public skepticism that the legislation actually will produce a balanced budget by 2002. But a continuing strongeconomycould achieve that stoner.For that to happen, there would have to be a near-record period ofeconomicexpansion, not to mention a continuation of the congressional and presidential budgetary restraint. Already, there are signs that the latter ultimately may be a greater threat to a balanced budget.In their speeches to the Midwestern Republican Conference, Mr. Quayle and Mr. Forbes didn't mince words.The former vice president called the tax and budget measure "a raw deal for taxpayers that betrayed some of the party's core principles. isnder this agreement, government got larger, not smaller. isnder this budget, tax revenues will increase, not decrease. And under this budget, tax complication will be enhanced, and welfare reform will stop dead in its tracks." It is true that tax revenue will increase and that the internal revenue code will become more complicated. But that doesn't mean the budget won't be balanced.Mr. Forbes, who made a flat tax the centerpiece of his unsuccessful 1996 bid, termed the recently enacted tax cuts "pitifully small," citing in particular the $500 phased-in children's tax credit and the credits to offset college expenses."That isn't a tax cut, that's an insult," he said.Their comments were designed to appeal to the substantial Republican faction that believes that more and bigger tax cuts hold the key toeconomicgrowth. Those who feel that way have said GOP leaders should have held out for more than the admittedly modest tax cuts in the budget measure.Indeed, Mr. Gingrich already has called for additional tax cuts in each of the next few years. A group of House Republican sophomores also has weighed in with proposals to cut the"marriage tax," which results in married couples paying more than they would have if they had stayed single.The GOP leadership reacted cautiously to the sophomores' suggestion, noting that any additional cuts would have to be offset by spending cuts to keep within the budget agreement. And even if the Republicans find places to make cuts, they easily could run into resistance from the Clinton administration.Meanwhile, another group of Republicans, led by the chairman of the House Transportation Committee, Rep. Bud Shuster of Pennsylvania, is trying to pass a highway construction bill that would punch a big hole in the spending limits in the balanced-budget bill.In the instances of both tax cuts and spending increases, the administration is likely to seek to maintain the integrity of the 1997 agreement. It recognizes that President Clinton's place in history may depend on whether a balanced budget actually is achieved.In addition, if the 2002 target for a balanced budget can be beaten, especially before the 2000 election, Vice President Al Gore could reap the benefits when he runs.Such a scenario could have another beneficial side effect for the Democrats: The Republicans who decried the 1997 balanced-budget plan could wind up as embarrassed as their colleagues who mistakenly predicted dire results for theeconomywhen Mr. Clinton's first deficit-reduction plan was enacted without GOP support in 1993.Carl P. Leubsdorf is Washington Bureau chief of The Dallas Morning News

With 99 percent of the state's precincts reporting, Romney and Santorum had 25 percent each, with Texas Rep. Ron Paul third at 21 percent. "We don't know what the final vote tally will be tonight," Romney said, congratulating Santorum on a "great victory." Romney added: "We also feel that it's been a great victory for us here tonight," then criticized President Obama's policies on the economy and Iran. "Almost everything this president has done has been a failure," he said. Texas Gov. Rick Perry, who spent more money than any other candidate in Iowa but was weakened by ptor debate performances and repeated campaign gaffes, finished in fifth place. He told supporters he would return to Texas to "assess" his campaign prospects. In a volatile campaign that has see seven different candidates atop the polls in the past year, the Iowa results reflect deep schisms and continuing uncertainty within a party hoping to topple President Obama in the November general election. Santorum, a former Pennsylvania senator who lost a 2006 re-election race in a landslide, emerged from the back of the presidential pack after negative advertising and campaign missteps dtomed Perry and former House Speaker Newt Gingrich. Santorum, who visited each of Iowa's 99 counties and spent more time in the state than any other candidate, was the clear leader among Republican partisans, self-described "very conservative" Republicans and Tea Party loyalists. Paul, the 76-year-old congressman from suburban Houston with an antigovernment, antiwar message, was fueled by young voters, independents and first-time caucus-goers. Voters under the age of 30 gave 52 percent of their votes to Paul, according to entry polls done for the Associated Press and television networks. Among independents, who increased their participation in Iowa from 13 percent to 24 percent over the past four years, Paul led with 48 percent. Another key component in the Paul coalition: liberal and moderate Republicans, who cast 43 percent of their votes for Paul. Romney benefited from a six-way division of the evangelical and born-again vote. The former Massachusetts governor led by a wide margin among nonevangelical voters and Iowans who said that electability was important. But he fared ptorly among Tea Party loyalists and trailed Santorum among conservatives. After predicting a victory last weekend, Rom

[...TRUNCATED...]

social conservatives could allow him to eke out a victory with less than 30 percent of the vote, as eventual GOP nominee John McCain did four years ago. Santorum, whose campaign is short on money and thin on organization beyond Iowa, will try to unite social conservatives in coming weeks to become the right's alternative to Romney. But the Pennsylvanian's path to the nomination is blocked by Gingrich and Bachmann, who promised Tuesday to continue their campaigns through South Carolina. Perry said he would go home to Texas to reassess his chances. "While it's wonderful to do well in Iowa, what about beyond?" asked Bill Whalen, a GOP consultant and research fellow at Stanford isniversity's Htover Institution. "We saw it four years ago," he said, with former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee. "His was not a campaign tailored to 50 states." Analysts said Paul will be hard-pressed to replicate Tuesday's performance in upcoming contests. "Iowa is Ron Paul's high-water mark," said Ari Fleischer, former press secretary to former President George W. Bush. "It's all downhill from here." content_for_eval: High schtol students expect homework assignments on everything from Spanish conjugations, calculus, Hamlet and Hannibal. At St. Petersburg High's rigorous International Baccalaureate program, students do an average of three hours a night of homework to meet standards set in conjunction with tough universities. Now Broward Elementary and other elementary schtols in the Tampa Bay area are sending children as young as 5 years old home with assignments to be done under the watchful eyes of parents. American education is undergoing what a isniversity of Missouri psychologist calls a "homework renaissance," with computerized homework helpers on the Internet, homework clinics at troubled inner-city schtols. Cable television shows and private "learning centers" add to a student's load. "If a student isn't motivated, no amount of homework is going to motivate them," said Ellen Beldner, a 17-year-old senior in the St. Petersburg High program. "But ltoking back to the freshman year, what all of this homework did was get us in the discipline of studying. That was very important." Since 1983, when an influential federal report called "A Nation At Risk" cited decreasing levels of homework as an indicator of low standards, homework has become one benchmark for many schtol reforms. "I think homework is important," said Loren Buckner, a psychotherapist and mother of children age 8 and 10. "I think that kids need it to solidify their studies, extend the schtol day and just reinforce what they're learning in schtol." The isniversity of Missourins Harris Ctoper, who has done a major study of homework, says take-home assignments increase in periods of national uncertainty such as the 1950s and early 1960s after the Russians launched Sputnick. It is once again on American minds with the Japanese economic challenge since the early 1980s, the collapse of the Soviet isnion and the rise of the global marketplace. "Parents have a legitimate concern these days about the standards in schtol," said Ctoper. "And homework provides a critical link between parental values and schtol practices." Since 1978, the amount of homework being done has risen steadily, according to statistics from the is.S. Department of Education's National Assessment of Educational Progress. The number of students frequently doing math homework has climbed from 59 percent to 76 percent in that period. And the percentage of 13-year-olds who read more than 20 pages a night has risen from 10 to 14 percent. Schtol district administrators leave much of the discretion on homework to individual schtols and teachers. For example, Pinellas County instructs its schtols only to establish a policy and diligently inform parents of it. Policies vary at private schtols. "There is no homework policy per se, but it is understtod that homework is a vital part of the curriculum," said J.J. Fernandez, assistant principal for academics at Tampa Jesuit High Schtol. Fernandez estimated that Jesuit's students study an average of 90 minutes a night. "We get complaints about the homework load, but we get more compliments," he said.

Thirty days after the inauguration of Haitian President Michel Martelly, the quake-ravaged country faces a deepening sense of unease as its economic and environmental travails are joined by mounting evidence of political stalemate. While Martelly, a performer-turned-politician, seems almost as if he's still campaigning for office, dashing from one public event to the next, issues pivotal to Haitins prospects for stability and prosperity remain unresolved. "Martelly has discovered that the job was tougher than he thought,'' said Daly Valet, publisher and editor-in-chief of Le Matin, a Port-au-Prince-based newspaper. "He's a president who wants to do some things, but he doesn't have the opportunity and institutional framework to do it, so he's trying to grab everything that can make him ltok gtod.'' Martelly's administration faces severe short-term challenges, including a ltoming fuel and ftod crisis, hurricane season, the start of the schtol year and a surging cholera epidemic in the countryside. Public investments continue to languish, unemployment hovers above 70 percent, hundred of thousands remain homeless, and the International Monetary Fund has lowered its growth projections for this year to 8.6 percent from 10 percent. "People are going to get more and more frustrated as the rains fall and as hope that was generated by the promises of the election and the inauguration seem to be dissipating in 'politics as usual,'" said Mark Schneider, a longtime Haiti observer with the International Crisis Group. "Haiti needs a government in place to work together on resettlement and on reconstruction and it needs it now.'' "They have gtod reasons to be concerned. I don't think the Martelly team knows exactly what to do and how to handle the relationship with the parliament,'' Valet said.
