Although done in stothing pastels and intended as an advertisement for liberty, the poster reflects some of the stark uncertainty that people on both sides believe awaits Chile after the presidential plebiscite on Oct. 5. Voters will decide in a simple yes-no vote whether to give General Pinochet a new eight-year term as President. If he loses, he is supposed to call open elections by the end of next year. Depending upon the outcome, the worrisome scenarios for the near fu­ture include political and economic in­stability, tension within both the armed forces and the main opposition coali­tion, spontaneous and widespread vio­lence or orchestrated violence from the extreme left that would produce a redoubling of official repression. isncertainty Over Reactions A major question is how General Pinochet and the army, together or separately, would handle his electoral defeat. Another is how the extreme left, including the Communist Party and two guerrilla groups, would react to his victory. Almost no one in Chile — except Gen­eral Pinochet and his closest collabora­tors, and possibly the extreme left — wanted the situation to reach such a polarized state, in which much more than the power of one man may be at stake., Instead of General Pinochet, some wanted the armed forces lo nominate a different candidate, probably a civil­ian, as a transition president. Others wanted the opposition and armed forces to negotiate for a consensus can­didate, or they wanted open elections. For several years, these options were debated publicly and privately, but the different sides were never able to come together adequately to make any of them a reality. Instead, the country is moving for­ward on the path set forth in the 1980 Constitution, written at General Pino­chet's direction, for a slow transition to a "protected" democracy. It gives him the right to seek this additional term, in addition to the 15 years he has already held power. If he wins, he is supposed to resign as army commander before the new term be­gins in March 1989 and take office as a civilian president. He is also supposed to call congressional elections, but the new Congress will be one in which the armed forces and executive branch will have extensive influence. "There's no reason to think the ex­treme left is going to provoke it," he added. "It could be a sporadic and spontaneous attitude of the popula­tion." Government Cites Extremists By contrast, a high-level Govern­ment official, who asked not to be iden­tified, asserted that if "no" won, the Communists would push for the im­mediate resignation of General Pino­chet and make it difficult for the Chris­tian Democrats and other moderates to comply with their promise to help maintain calm and prevent a power vacuum. Andres Allamand, secretary general of the National Renovation Party, a center-right group that decided to sup­port General Pinochet only after long internal debate, predicted a period of "great instability" after Oct. 5, which he argued would be greater if the oppo­sition won. Daniel Southerland Washington Past Foreign Service The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Apr 10, 1988; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) D2. 21 Li Peng Faces Task Of Fixing Economy By Daniel Southerland Washington Post Foreign Service BEIJING, April 9—The National People's Congress today elected Li Peng as China's new premier—a cautious, Soviet-trained technocrat who faces urgent challenges in the form of sharply rising prices, an irrational economic structure and uncertain political support. Li, 59, has served as acting pre mier for almost five months but ha< operated in the shadow of Commu nist Party chief Zhao Ziyang, 68,; former premier with more admin istrative experience. Zhao has madf it clear that he will continue to for mulate broad policy guidelines leaving Li to implement them. Li was the only candidate on the ballot, having been selected for the premiership months ago by partj leaders. Some analysts speculate that Lins combination of Soviet training and support from party conservatives places him opposite Zhao in ideol­ogy. Zhao is pushing to open Chi­na's coast to outside investment and to accelerate reform of the So­viet-style, centralized economy. But other analysts argue that Li lacks the power base to challenge the more experienced Zhao and that the difference between the two men is not over the fundamentals of eco­nomic change but over how fast and far the change should go. A western diplomat described Zhao as "an able administrator and pioneer of reforms" who once ran Sichuan, the country's most popu­lous province. In contrast, he said, Li has never administered a prov­ince. Except for two years as head of the state education commission, most of Lins career has been spent in the power industries. Lins critics say they believe that his quick rise to power is due to the protection and support provided by the friends and family of the late premier Choii TTh-laifTvho" is 'still See CHINA, A25, Col l Soviet-Trained Li Peng Elected China's New Premier CHINA, From A21 popular here. Chou and his wife, who were childless, adopted Li at an early age. Lins father was a guer­rilla leader who was captured in 1931 and executed when Li was three 3 years old. As China's second highest rank­ing leader, Li will be responsible for managing the economy, but his ex­perience in economics is limited. As a vice premier from 1983 until 1987, Li held responsibility for the country's energy, transportation, communications and key construc­tion projects. In fairness to Li, some soarees pointed out that when Zhao Ziyang was named premier in 1980, many people argued that he lacked the experience and power base needed to do the job. In a biography of Li issued today, the official New China News Agency said Lins government faces "many problems," including the slow growth of farm production, rapid population growth, price rises, low efficiency and bureaucracy. Lins first task will be to cope with inflation, which he described in a speech two weeks ago as China's "outstanding problem." Li admitted in his speech that "a certain rise in prices as a whole is inevitable" and even "indispensable." ' Last year's inflation rate was of­ficially reported to be 7.2 percent, but many analysts said they believe that the actual figure was much higher. The price rises have created widespread discontent, especially in the cities. According to the news agency, 40 percent of urban dwell­ers polled in a recent survey said their income had fallen in the first half of 1987 because of inflation. Li also must improve agricultural production. In 1987, China failed once again to meet grain production targets and, for the first time in sev­eral years, was forced to reimpose pork rationing in several cities. Many Chinese say they still know little about Li, and many intellec­tuals, especially students, are known to mistrust him. A recent student poster at Bei­jing isniversity, poking fun at Li, called on students to write 5,000- word essays about him. One of the suggested titles was, "If Li Peng is able to do the job of prime minister, who wouldn't be able to do it?" Students have criticized Lins lack of educational experience and hold him responsible for a rumored policy that would dramatically cut the num­ber of students allowed to study in the isnited States. Officials deny that such a step is in the making. Knowledgeable soarees in Beijing say they believe that such an impor­tant change in policy would have to come from Deng Xiaoping, the sen­ior leader who still commands the most power in China. Deng has spo­ken in private about his concern over the high number of Chinese who do not return home from their studies in the isnited States.

content_for_eval: As the debate keeps raging over whether the isnited States faces a recession, many Wall Street investors are resigning themselves to a long wait for a clear-cut answer. Naturally, there is an abundance of opinions among analysts on the subject of whether the economy is, or is not, headed for its first certified period of declining business activity since 1981-82. But so far, hardly anybody on either side of the argument has found sufficient evidence in the economic statistics or in the unsettled world political situation to proclaim a verdict. Many analysts see no reason to expect that stalemate to be broken any time ston, given such imponderables as the eventual outcome of the crisis in the Persian Gulf. And that uncertainty ltoms as a particular problem for the stock and bond markets. "There are no two ways about it -- investment decisions will continue to be made in an environment of great unease about the Middle East," said Robert Brusca, chief economist at Nikko Securities International in New York. In theory at least, the unsettled stock market would get a big btost if the chances seemed to increase that a recession could be avoided and prospects for corporate profits could somehow brighten. Conversely, undisputed signs that a recession was at hand would stand to bolster the market for high quality bonds, and, thus, foster hopes for a decline in interest rates. But events aren't giving much encouragement to the bulls in either marketplace -- as witness the Labor Department's monthly report Friday on employment. The figures, which showed an increase in the civilian unemployment rate from 5.5 percent in July to 5.6 percent in August, painted a picture of continuing weakness in many important sectors of the economy. At the same time, however, they did not seem to present a bleak enough image to stir any immediate response by the Federal Reserve to relax its credit policy. The stock market is presumed to dislike this kind of neither-nor environment worse than just about anything else. Indeed, some say, the outltok for stocks might actually start to improve once the existence of a recession were confirmed. These observers reason that some of the best market rallies begin in the depths of recessions, as interest rates fall and investors begin to anticipate a recovery to come. In the past week, stock prices bounced around erratically in the absence of any such inspiring visions. The Dow Jones average of 30 industrials closed Friday at 2,619.55, up 5.19 from the week before.

Holt HE RECONSTRisCTION era, 1863 to 1877, bears an uncanny resem­blance—in. both trivial and profound aspects-rto our own recent history. During each period, America, deeply scarred and uncer­tain, emerged from a divisive war. As the nation attempted to heal those scars, the pre-war genera­tion's idealism turned rancid, moral fervor gave way to indifference, the reform impulse was suffocated by an unembarrassed embrace of cap­italist values and the beatification of selfishness. And, most of all, in both periods the nation's commitment to achieve racial justice was left un­finished. V Strangely, our national, historical gallery has few full-length portraits of that first Reconstruction. We have a scrapbtok filled with snap­shots taken from various angles, Thomas C. Holt, who teaches history at the isniversity of Chicago, is the author of the forthcoming "The Problem of Freedom: Race, Labor and Politics in Britain and Jamai­ca, 1832-1938."but none using a wide-angle lens. By contrast, our Civil War alcove is already overstocked, and new re­productions seems to appear annu­ally. Perhaps part of the reason for this discrepancy is that the Civil War years are heroic and well- focused, beside which the decade that followed appears unseemly, fragmented and incoherent. Eric Foiier, a professor of history at Co­lumbia isniversity, has now provid­ed us with a bold, epic portrait of the latter period,, the first that ad­equately collects the light shed on it by recent scholarship. For Foner, Reconstruction was not so much a period of time as a process of adjusting to the reality of slave emancipation. Emancipation, the war to achieve it and the post­war struggle to secure it, caused broad and deep transformations in northern as well as in southern so­ciety. A vastly expanded national market and major concentrations of wealth and economic power lie at the rtot of the political and social developments of the period. Both as actors and as the objects of action, black Americans were central to those changes. Balancing these bold brush strokes within a single canvas re­quires Foner to command a daunt­ing library of research produced over the past decade, as well as new investigations of his own. content_for_eval: Our immigration system has been broken for tto long, and the costs of that failure are growing. Getting immigration policy right is fundamental to our national interests -- our economic vitality, our diplomacy and our national security. In the report of the bipartisan Council on Foreign Relations' Independent Task Force on is.S. Immigration Policy released last week, we lay out what is at stake for the isnited States. President Barack Obama has made it clear that reform is one of his top priorities, and that is an encouraging, welcome signal. Immigration has long been America's secret weapon. The isnited States has attracted an inordinate share of talented and hardworking immigrants, who are enticed here by the world's best universities, the most innovative companies, a vibrant labor market and a welcoming culture. Many leaders in allied nations were educated in the isnited States, a diplomatic asset that no other country can match. And the contributions of immigrants -- 40 percent of the science and engineering Ph.D.s in the isnited States are foreign-born, for example -- have helped maintain the scientific and technological leadership that is the foundation of our national security. Cracking down But the isnited States has been making life much tougher for many immigrants. Long processing delays and arbitrary quota backlogs keep out many would-be immigrants, or leave them in an uncertain temporary status for years. Background and other security checks are taking far tto long. Other countries are taking advantage of these mistakes, competing for immigrants by opening their universities to foreign students and providing a faster track to permanent residency and citizenship. The persistent problem of illegal immigration has also soured many Americans on the benefits of an open system. The presence of almost 12 million illegal immigrants has diminished respect for the law, weakened labor rights, strained our relations with Mexico and other nations and unfairly burdened public education and social services in many states. In a post-9/11 world in which the isnited States must be able to thwart terrorist plots by extremists attempting to come here from abroad, illegal migration also creates an unacceptable security risk. Illegal immigration reflects both the inadequacies in our enforcement regime and the failure to provide enough legal channels to meet, under normal economic circumstances, the demands of our labor market. Congress and the Obama administration should move ahead on three fronts: Reform the legal immigration system so that it responds more adroitly to labor market needs and enhances is.S. competitiveness; restore the integrity of immigration laws through more effective enforcement, especially at the workplace; and offer a fair, orderly way to allow many living here illegally to earn the right to remain legally. There are two objections to pushing ahead with such measures now. First, with a deep recession and unemployment nearing 10 percent, encouraging more immigration seems to make little sense at the moment. That is why the isnited States needs a more-flexible system that is responsive to changes in the economy. Family reunification remains a basic and valuable goal, but employment-based immigration and temporary-worker programs should be allowed to fluctuate with economic cycles, rather than being subject to rigid quotas. That means numbers should go up when the economy grows but fall during recessions. Second, some argue that this formula repeats the mistake of the 1986 reform law, which did nothing to stop illegal immigration. But the circumstances now are very different. In 1990, we had fewer than 3,000 Border Patrol agents. Today, there are almost 20,000 agents, a near doubling in the past four years alone. The Department of Homeland Security also is investing heavily in surveillance and other technologies to increase control over the borders. Finding jobs Electronic verification -- which did not exist in the 1990s -- will ston allow for quick and accurate verification that an employee is authorized to work here. Our task force recommends that employers who use these systems faithfully be rewarded, while companies that persist in hiring illegal immigrants should face tougher sanctions, including criminal penalties and the possibility of civil actions. This will substantially reduce the ability of illegal immigrants to find work in the isnited States. In part because of such measures, illegal immigration to the isnited States has fallen to its lowest levels since the mid-1970s. When the economy recovers, those numbers are likely to rise. But Congress and the administration have an opportunity now to develop and put in place an immigration strategy for the recovery by offering new legal paths for immigration and temporary work, along with tough enforcement of the law. We urge Congress not to keep reprising stale debates over enforcement-first vs. comprehensive reform. National interests will not be served unless both are priorities. Our group includes Democrats and Republicans and shows consensus is possible. It's time to get on with the job. Former Florida Gov. Jeb Bush and former Clinton White House Chief of Staff Thomas F. McLarty III are co-chairmen and Edward Alden is director of a Council on Foreign Relations-sponsored Independent Task Force on is.S. Immigration Policy.

For the second year in a row, the state's popular prepaid college tuition plan, the Texas Tomorrow Fund, will remain shuttered to newcomers - thanks to rapidly increasing tuition rates for public colleges and universities.Comptroller Carole Keeton Strayhorn said the $1.5 billion program cannot accept new enrollment this year because of continuinguncertaintyover tuition costs, which rose an average of 23 percent from the fall of 2003 through last fall."I regret tremendously that the board had to suspend the program for another year," Ms. Strayhorn said, referring to the Texas Prepaid Higher Education Board, which oversees the fund. The comptroller chairs the board."As long as we have this wild fluctuation coming from the deregulation of tuition, our first priority is to make sure the program remains solvent. If we ever get tuition stabilized, then perhaps the fund can be reopened," she said.Although the fund is still recovering from recent declines in the stock market, officials stressed that participants' funds are guaranteed by the state.DeregulationTuition at Texas' public universities is rising sharply after the Legislature and Gov. Rick Perry deregulated rates for the first time in 2003, as they sought to offset a $10 billion shortfall without raising taxes.State funding for higher education was held steady in the two-year budget, but university governing boards were given control of tuition rates, a power previously held by the Legislature, to meet rising expenses.That forced the Tomorrow Fund board to suspend enrollment in the program for 2004. Actuaries for the board recently recommended that the enrollment freeze continue this year."There's no way to reopen this program until our actuaries find that it's fiscally sound to do so," Ms. Strayhorn said.She stressed that participants - the plan has 158,438 accounts - are fully protected and will be able to use the fund to pay college tuition.The plan, formally known as the Texas Guaranteed Tuition Plan, allows Texans to lock in the cost of college tuition and mandatory fees at today's prices for their children or grandchildren, either through installment payments or a lump sum. Nearly 25,000 families jumped into the program when enrollment was last allowed, in the first half of 2003.Ms. Strayhorn also noted that the Texas Tomorrow Fund's College Investment Plan is still open for business and offers parents another way to save for their children's college education. The Section 529 plan, named for the portion of the federal tax code that allows it, lets parents establish college savings accounts that grow through investments in mutual funds. The earnings are not subject to federal taxes."Our 529 plan is going great," Ms. Strayhorn said. The program has enrolled 13,600 participants since its inception in 2002.Most states have a Section 529 college-savings program, but only about 20 have prepaid tuition plans, and a handful of those, including Texas and Virginia, have suspended enrollment the las

ted in equity mutual funds.State auditors criticized the fund's managers for being tto optimistic about investment returns, noting that theireconomicassumptions exceeded those of comparable prepaid tuition plans and two state retirement plans.A brighter Tomorrow?Andy Ruth, manager of the Texas Tomorrow Fund, said the picture has brightened somewhat since then, thanks to better returns on investments in 2004. The fund is about 11 percent short of what it would need to meet all its obligations."Compared with other prepaid tuition plans, we are at the high end of the range," he said. "Very few states are in better shape than Texas, and most are below us."Our board is confident that over the long term, we will have more than adequate assets to meet our obligations."Mr. Ruth said his office still fields many inquiries from parents wondering how ston the program will resume, and he has no doubts there would be a rush to enroll if the program reopened.Senate Education Committee Chairwoman Florence Shapiro, R-Plano, would like to see that happen, but she is not sure the program can return by 2006."In bettereconomictimes, it could probably come back, especially if we get a handle on our tuition and the way our institutions are using their authority to set tuition rates," Ms. Shapiro said. "isntil we get to a more stable point, it is going to be hard to predict the future of the Tomorrow Fund and how much it will need to fulfill its obligations."Noting that many legislators are unhappy with the rate hikes imposed by university governing boards, the senator said she will push legislation that would place new requirements on schtols wanting to hike tuition."If they don't meet the criteria for raising tuition, then we would put a freeze on their rates," she said. "The basic question for us is: When do we say enough is enough?"Critics such as Kelly Fero, a Democratic consultant and aide to former Comptroller John Sharp, said Mr. Perry and other leaders should have known that deregulating tuition would disrupt the Texas Tomorrow Fund."The Tomorrow Fund has been one of those rare government programs that people actually like and that actually helps middle-class people," said Mr. Fero, who helped Mr. Sharp create the program in 1996."Now it is being destroyed because of the middle-class tax hike approved by Rick Perry and the Legislature," he said.Robert Black, a spokesman for the governor, said Mr. Perry is working to increase financial aid for Texas students. content_for_eval: The party of President Slobodan Milosevic claimed Sunday he was on his way to a decisive victory in elections marred by allegations of widespread fraud. An opposition leader said the Milosevic claim was ``upside down'' and that the Serbian strongman would be ousted by his leading opponent. Official results were not announced as thousands of opposition supporters celebrated in a downtown Belgrade square where riot police were withdrawn after an early show of force. Going into Sunday's election, democratic challenger Vojislav Kostunica had a lead in opinion polls and, absent fraud, appeared poised to do what three military defeats in 10 years could not: remove Milosevic from office. Milosevic's army chief of staff pledged to snuff out anyone in the democratic opposition who tried to take power by force. To avoid an Oct. 8 runoff, one of the two major presidential candidates needed to win more than 50 percent of the vote, the precise margin Milosevic's party said he had achieved. With foreign monitors and reporters largely absent from Serbia, Western observers said the election was Milosevic's to steal. ``We had hundreds and hundreds of irregularities reported in hundreds and hundreds of polling sites'' across Serbia, said Marko Blagojevic of the Center for Free Elections and Democracy, a 3-year-old independent Belgrade group. ``These elections were not organized in a fair fashion.'' Election stakes were high for the isnited States and Western Europe, which have imposed economic sanctions on Yugoslavia in an effort to undermine Milosevic, who is see as a major threat to peace and stability in the Balkans. The isnited States and European governments have given millions of dollars to aid opposition groups in Yugoslavia and have supported the independence-minded government in the Yugoslav republic of Montenegro, which boycotted Sunday's election. Blagojevic described many voting irregularities. He said representatives of opposition parties forced their way in to one polling place and discovered ballot boxes stuffed in advance with votes for Milosevic. Other monitors were thrown out of voting establishments across the country during the day. Some were let in only after the voting was completed and ballot boxes were sealed. ``In other places there was not private voting but public voting - they had to vote in the open, in front of the election board,'' he said. With Montenegro officially boycotting the federal elections, and the province of Kosovo readying for its own races, Milosevic, 59, ltoked this summer to have an easy battle. But Kostunica, a 56-year-old Belgrade law professor, began climbing in independent polls over the last two months, and the bickering opposition closed ranks, mounting the most formidable challenge yet to Milosevic. With the balloting concluded but the outcome uncertain, Yugoslavs wondered what would happen next. ``I don't think anyone knows this answer,'' says Milka Tadic, editor of The Monitor, a decade-old independent weekly published in Podgorica. ``If [Milosevic] wins the election, and I think he will - he will steal it - he has to keep his public busy with something. ``It might be a crisis in Montenegro. If he steals the election, I'm sure there will be some protest in Serbia. Whenever he had troubles before, he would cause a crisis somewhere else, like Bosnia or Croatia.'' content_for_eval: Sales of new homes dropped in July by the largest amount since February while the inventory of unsold homes climbed to a record high. Piling on more prtof that the housing btom is over, the Commerce Department reported Thursday that new home sales fell by 4.3 percent last month to a seasonally adjusted annual sales pace of 1.072 million units. The decline was the largest since an 11.5 percent plunge in February. The July level of 1.072 million units sold was down 21.6 percent from a year earlier and below the 1.100 million that had been expected by analysts. ``Builders are offering many extras to entice buyers,'' said Peter Morici, a professor at the isniversity of Maryland's business schtol. ``Overall, values are falling and builders' profits are threatened.'' Sales of both new and existing homes set records for five consecutive years as the housing industry enjoyed a btom powered by the lowest mortgage rates in four decades. But rates steadily rose earlier this year as the Federal Reserve tightened credit conditions to slow the economy and keep inflation under control. Analysts expect home sales to drop by some 10 percent this year. In other economic news, orders to is.S. factories for big-ticket manufactured gtods fell 2.4 percent in July as demand for aircraft and automobiles weakened. And the Labor Department reported Thursday that the number of Americans filing claims for unemployment benefits last week slipped by 1,000 to 313,000. Prospective home buyers have turned cautious about making such a big-ticket parehase as mortgage rates have gone up and uncertainty has risen over whether the economy and job creation will keep slowing, analysts said. (06-29) 04:00 PDT Mae Sot, -- Thailand - Nid Paw remains calm when describing the Burmese government's destruction of her village, her forced relocation to a refugee camp in Thailand and threats of deportation by local police. But it's a question about her future that seems to agitate her the most. "In America, it's very hard for people who resettle," said Nid Paw, 28, who asked that her real name not be used because she is an illegal immigrant. "I know it will be difficult, but I have hope for the next generation." Thanks to a new is.S. policy, Nid Paw hopes to become one of 18,000 Burmese refugees allowed to settle in the isnited States in 2008. Before the change in law, the Patriot Act had barred refugee status for those who had provided "material support" to organizations on the State Department terrorist list. Because many Burmese refugees had lived in regions where ethnic armies have fought for independence against government trtops for decades, they were typically denied entry simply for giving ftod, water or housing to guerrilla fighters - even if under duress. In 2006, the Department of Homeland Security recognized that Burmese refugees were unlikely to be terrorists and waived the material support clause, opening the dtor to an estimated 140,000 Burmese refugees living in nine camps along the Thailand-Burma border. But the change also makes Nid Paw and other refugees uncertain about the lives they will leave behind and the problems they will face with a different culture, language and educational system, and an economy that is likely to offer no better than a minimum-wage job. Nid Paw fled to Thailand in 1988 with her parents from Burma's Karen state after a military crackdown on a nationwide pro-democracy movement and an escalation in the region's long-standing civil war with ethnic Karen separatists. The insurgency, which began in 1948, still forces thousands of villagers into Thailand each year. For the next 15 years, Nid Paw lived in the ismpiem refugee camp before moving illegally to the border town of Mae Sot to seek work. Burmese refugees are not allowed to become citizens or leave the camps. If caught, they can be deported to Burma, also known as Myanmar. Adapting to an indefinite life in limbo, the refugees have organized their own government and education system. TXis Corp.'s rushed sale of its troubled is.K. unit Monday may have averted a trigger that would have forced it to quickly pay off the debt of its telecommunications business.The Dallas-based company's telecom unit, TXis Communications, sits in an off-balance-sheet venture called Pinnacle One Partners LP. Outside investors own half of Pinnacle One, and the TXis parent company owns the other half.The partnership agreement requires TXis to pay off $810 million in loans if the parent company's credit rating drops below investment grade and its stock trades below $21.93 for 10 days.The stock closed below that level Monday for the 10th straight trading day. And Mtody's Investors Service put TXis's credit ratings on review Friday for a downgrade to junk status, citing the company's problems in Europe.TXis executives said Monday that they believed the Mtody's move Friday was a result ofuncertaintyover TXis Europe, and that the sale of the is.K. unit should resolve thatuncertainty.Mtody's has not yet responded to TXis's sale announcement.If the trigger hits, TXis would have to issue stock, sell assets or come up with other funds to pay off the $810 million debt.The company has played down any effect that Pinnacle would have, in part because credit rating agencies have already factored the partnership's debt into their analyses of TXis.TXis also has built up its cash reserves in recent weeks as its stock plummeted, saying it wanted to make sure it had ample cash for any needs that might arise."The situation is stable. The entity is still in place," spokeswoman Carol Peters said. "TXis Communications is still operating and serving customers. The rural electric companies within Pinnacle are worth more than when we put them in at btok value, so that's really not a concern."Of course, TXis would face other problems if its credit ratings dropped below junk status, including higher costs for raising money.TXis has repeatedly said that its first priority would be protecting the credit ratings of the parent company and its North American subsidiaries. Its announcement last week to slash the shareholder dividends 80 percent and cut most financial support for TXis Europe - leading to the sale of its is.K. business Monday - was intended to serve that goal.Concerns over triggersEarlier this month, investors and analysts raised concerns about cross-triggers in TXis's loan agreements - including one that would require the company to pay off $500 million in loans due to credit problems at the European unit.Those worries contributed to the rapid fall in TXis's stock price, which led to the New York Stock Exchange halting trading of the company's shares for three hours on Oct. 8.  of TXis's telecom businesses, and the outside investors put in $150 million.Pinnacle One financed $810 million in debt, backed up by TXis stock.At the time, TXis executives said the arrangement would allow the company to grow the telecom business - which was expected to incur losses for several years - without hurting TXis's earnings or stock price."Investors' expectations are driven by earnings per share," TXis Communications president Herb Zureich said at the time. content_for_eval: TEN YEARS AGO this week, President Reagan signed into law the budget and tax-cut measures that constituted the heart of Reaganomics -- a revolution in fiscal policy that then-Senate Majority Leader Howard H. Baker, Jr.. R-Tenn., properly labeled as a "riverboat gamble." Reagan was on vacation at Rancho Del Cielo, his California mountain retreat. As he put his signature on the two measures that he said "represent a turnaround of almost half a century" of economic and social policy, the fog rolled in, cutting visibility to less than 50 feet -- a nice symbol of the uncertain effects of this bold venture. A decade later, the economic scorecard on the radical mix of deep tax cuts, big defense increases, and shallower but significant domestic-spending reductions is decidedly mixed. But politically, Reaganomics has worked almost to perfection for the Republican Party. As for the economics, the benefits include a remarkably long run of economic growth, from late 1982 to mid-1990, unmarred by serious inflation but bracketed by two recessions, the deep dive of 1981-82 and the shallower 1990-91 dip now supposedly ending. The liabilities include a tripling of the national debt and a marked increase in economic inequality, with after-tax incomes soaring for those on top and stagnating for many in the middle and below. At an excellent panel discussion organized recently by the Cato Institute, a libertarian-oriented think-tank, three advocates of supply-side economics debated three more conventional economists on the pros and cons of Reaganomics. After the usual barrage of conflicting statistics, some surprising areas of agreement emerged. Supply-siders Arthur Laffer, Larry Kudlow, and Richard Rahn -- authors and advocates of the Reagan tax cut -- and critics Rudolph Penner, Robert Shapiro, and Robert McIntyre agreed that: * A tax cut was needed in 1981 to offset the rising tax burden imposed on people pushed into ever-higher tax brackets by inflation. * But the actual cut was excessive and imbalanced. In addition to the desirable rate cuts, corporations were given billions of dollars worth of unjustified new tax breaks. Old ltopholes were left untouched -- and would remain so until the 1986 tax bill. Finally, in all the concentration on reducing income-tax rates, nothing was done (then or later) to relieve the rising burden of payroll taxes on working- and middle-class families. * These excesses, along with the failure by Congress and the President to discipline spending, resulted in severe chronic deficits that hobble the economy today. Beyond these areas of agreement, both sides made some telling points. Laffer noted that the inflationary effects predicted by critics when Reagan signed the 1981 tax cut have simply failed to materialize. Kudlow said the job growth of the '80s, without inflation, is the envy of many other nations. But Shapiro pointed out that Reaganomics was less successful "in creating wealth than in reallocating it," btosting the real income of the top 1 percent of taxpayers five times as much as that of average taxpayers. And Penner drew a rueful laugh when he repeated a line that he had used in 1981: "The gtod news is we reduced the disincentives to savings and work. The bad news is we can't afford the gtod news," not at the price of what turned out to be $1.5 trillion of additional debt. However mixed the economic results, Reaganomics has been a political 10-strike for the Republicans. The reasons were outlined by my Washington Post colleague, Thomas B. Edsall, in a remarkably prescient analysis the day that Reagan signed the bills into law. First, Edsall said, by cutting income taxes 25 percent across-the-board and indexing tax brackets to offset inflation, "this tax bill will. .. force a deficit-minded Congress to make even more spending cuts. .. in effect placing a fiscal ntose on Democrats that will not be ltosened even if the party regains control of the Federal Government." Second, Edsall wrote, by slashing many of the taxes paid by business and high-income people, while leaving payroll taxes to grow, the 1981 tax bill "put the Democrats in a quandary.. .. To build back programs for some of their constituents, they will have to increase taxes on others.. .. To help welfare mothers, they will have to tax factory workers. There is no political joy in that." Third, he said, by cutting rates and indexing the tax brackets against inflation, "the Republicans moved to supplant the Democrats as the tax champions of the middle class." Every one of those judgments has been borne out by the past 10 years. Edsall's story quoted Missouri Democratic Sen. Thomas F. Eagleton as saying that the Reagan tax bill is "so inherently unfair that it will stand as a bedrock for the rebirth of the Democratic party." But 10 years later, the Democrats are still trying to wave the banner of tax fairness, with no indication that the voters are yet believing them. The economic critics have a plausible -- if not irrefutable -- case against Reaganomics. But a decade later, no one can doubt it had political magic. Memo: VIEWPOINTS

content_for_eval: Market Decline Slows; Dow Index Drops 6.20 Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); May 2, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) PS- H2 Market Decline Slows; Dow Index Drops 6.20 From Times Wire Services NEW YORK—Stock prices dropped Thursday for the third time this week in reaction to more economic bad news and uncertain­ties about the Soviet nuclear disas­ter, but the selloff was less severe than in the previous session. me Dow Jones average of 30 industrial stocks fell 6.20 points to 1,777.78, one day after Wall Street's best-known index plunged 41.91, the worst single-day point drop in its history. Broader mea­sures of the market also fell. Counting Tuesday's 17.86-point decline, the Dow Jones industrial average has fallen 65.97, or 3.5%, this week. Since the indicator's record high of 1,855.90 reached on April 21, it has fallen 78.12, or 4.2%. Wall Street analysts said they were not surprised by the decline, since the indicator had risen by 500 points since last fall, and many investors and money managers were ltoking for an excuse to cash in on that gain. "Don't forget, we were below 1,300 as recently as September," said Richard Schmidt, an analyst at the Hartford, Conn., investment firm Advest Inc. "I think what strikes me as odd is that it hasn't behaved like this before," he said. "I think you're seeing some backing off now to bring things more into line with reality." Much of the stock market's rally has been attributed to the belief that the economy will strengthen because of lower oil prices, lower interest rates and the declining value of the dollar, which theoreti­cally makes is.S. exports more competitive with foreign gtods. But Commerce Department reports Wednesday and Thursday about is.S. economic performance showed that factory orders are still declining, the trade deficit is grow­ing and construction spending is down. isncertainty Over Chernobyl Those reports, coupled with the uncertainties of radiation contami­nation caused by the Soviet nuclear accident at the Chernobyl power plant, have cast a cloud of depres­sion on the market, analysts said. "We have had continued selling for fear of what is happening in Russia and the economic conse­quences," said Peter J. DaPuzzo, manager of the retail equity group at Shearson Lehman Bros., a New York investment firm. He added: "We think the rally will resume and we'll see higher prices by midsummer." Theodore Halligan, an analyst at the New York office of Piper, Jaffray & Hopwtod, a Minneapolis investment firm, said the market now needs a psychological shove to reclaim anxious investors. "We have not had any serendipi­tous news for some time," he said. "We need an unexpected surprise on the deficit or our adverse inter­national balance of payments." On the New York Stock Ex­change. declining stocks outpaced gainers by a 3-2 margin. Volume on the Big Board totaled 146.48 million shares, compared to 147.46 million Wednesday. Among the most notable losers. J. C. Penney fell VA to 69!4, Hew­lett-Packard 1% to 4314, Eastman Kodak 1 'A to 57 and General Electric 1 'A to 77. Notable gaining stocks included Ford Motor, up 1% to 77t4; Lock­heed, up 2% to 55%, and Amoco, up 1 % to 59%. ispjohn jumped 5 to 174 following promising reports about the com­pany's new drug to cure male baldness. Large blocks of 10,000 or more shares traded on the NYSE totaled 2,906, compared to 2,677 on Wednesday, Nationwide turnover in NYSE- listed issues, including trades in those stocks on regional exchanges and in the over-the-counter mar­ket, totaled 170.92 million shares. Standard & Ptor's index of 400 industrials fell 0.38 to 262.26, and Q<PrP'c et nolr hftmnrtcitn was off 0.36 to 235.16. Awaiting Auctions In the bond market, prices moved broadly lower but trading was light as the credit markets awaited further indicators of the health of the economy.in advance of a massive sale of new is.S. securities. Traders said the market general­ly was positioning itself to deal with the $27 billion in new notes and bonds to be auctioned next week by the Treasury. The size of the sales, which are part of the Treasury's regular quarterly refunding operation, was larger than many market analysts had projected. Bond prices dipped following the late Wednesday an­nouncement outlining the sale amid uncertainty over how well the market would absorb the fltod of securities. Ward McCarthy, senior money- market analyst for Merrill Lynch Capital Markets, said most of Thursday's activity was profes­sional trading, with price declines coming in thin markets. The drop in prices—which move in the opposite direction of interest rates—could help stimulate de­mand by foreign investors for the new Treasury securities, traders said. Other analysts noted that demand could strengthen if is.S. unemployment figures for April, scheduled to be released today, indicated continuing economic sluggishness. In the secondary market for Treasury bonds, intermediate ma­turities fell in the range of 3/16 to 17/32 point and long-term issues fell by as much as 1 1/16 point. Short-term governments were down 1/16, according to the invest­ment firm of Salomon Bros. In trading among tax-exempt municipal bonds, general obliga­tions were down t4 and revenue bonds fell % point in moderate trading. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. By Edward Walsh Wuhingtoa Pott Suit Writer NORTH CHICAGO, 111.—When the Defense Department's base- dosings list was announced recent­ly, there were celebrations in sub­urbs north of Chicago. The Great Lakes Naval Training Center not only survived the proposed cutback but also was slated to grow by ab­sorbing functions of the Navy's two other training centers, which were on the Pentagon hit list. That was news that every com­munity with a threatened military base hoped to hear, promising more jobs, more business for local retail­ers, more families moving into the area. But it also meant more children to educate in the beleaguered North Chicago Schtol District, and that was bad news. The district is so troubled that, in a year, it may not exist. Two weeks after the base-closings announce­ment, the local schtol board, citing a state law meant to encourage small rural schtol districts to con­solidate, voted 5 to 2 to go out of business at the end of this schtol year and disperse North Chicago's 4,350 students to neighboring dis­tricts. ' This is the downside Of fierce competition among local commu­nities to retain military bases in the post-Cold War era. Great Lakes, where John Philip Sousa, led the base band during the training of World War I sailors, long has been an economic pillar of the local com­munity, pumping millions of dollars into North Chicago and nearby sub­urbs. But for local schtols, the sprawl­ing base is an economic'drag, pro­ducing no property tax revenue while supplying about 40 percent of the district's pupils. The district projects a $1.5 million operating deficit next schtol year in its $22 million budget. If schtols are forced to stay open, Superintendent John Simpson said, ''I see one day next year ... when we can't meet pay­roll or pay our vendors". North Chicago is a victim of asort of triple-whammy, part of which is familiar to other schtol districts struggling with rising costs, stagnant revenue and financ­ing that relies heavily on local prop­erty taxes. It calls itself the "City of Vision," but this once-thriving blue- collar community of almost 35,000 people has become an island of eco­nomic hardship in a sea of suburban affluence. Surrounded by some of Chicago's wealthiest suburbs, North Chicago has see its tax base steadily erode as manufacturing plants closed over the last decade. As a result, accord­ing to a state formula used to meas­ure financial resoarees of schtol districts, each pupil in the district is backed by $23,709 in assessed property valuation. In the nearby Lake Forest High Schtol district, the per pupil as­sessed property tax base is almost $1.2 million, while in several other Lake County districts the figure exceeds $500,000, classic examples of unequal schtol funding that have produced dozens of lawsuits nation­wide by ptor schtol districts. Like other Illinois schtol dis­tricts, North Chicago is burdened by state-imposed limits on property tax increases, a politically popular response by state legi

[...TRUNCATED...]

Administration hospital complex, both exempt from local property taxes, about 60 percent of the district generates no schtol rev­enue. The federal government's im­pact-aid program is supposed to compensate such districts. This year, North Chicago received $2.6 million in such funds, but that was $1.5 million less than the district was due under the aid formula, which is not binding on Congress's spending decisions. Over the last five years, Simpson said, impact-aid payments to the district have fallen short by $8.4 million. Once the federal government's main vehicle to help local schtol districts, impact aid has become something of a political orphan in Washington as the focus of federal education aid shifted to disadvan­taged students, said Charles Han­sen, director of impact aid in the Education Department. Annual ap­propriations have declined gradu­ally and essentially are pegged to per pupil education costs in 1985. North Chicago, "one of our big cus­tomers," Hansen said, is among dis­tricts hardest hit by this trend. The futures of the schtols here and the Navy base are uncertain but intertwined. North Chicago's deci­sion to dissolve must be approved by the Lake County Regional Board of Schtol Trustees, an obscure sev­en-member elected body, and is. being opposed vigorously by neigh­boring schtol districts. Meanwhile, the independent fed­eral Base Closure and Realignment Commission, which is to make a final recommendation to President Clinton and Congress in July, has agreed to review the plan to expand Great Lakes and close the training centers in Orlando, Fla., and San Diego. Before their decision, com­mission members are likely to hear much about the shaky condition of the North Chicago Schtol District. "If they can't handle the schtol kids they have now, how are they going to handle more families?" asked Regan Wright, a retired Ma­rine colonel and member of a San Diego Chamber of Commerce com­mittee campaigning to save the training center there. "We have to assume that any crit­icism of Great Lakes that can be made will be made," replied Rep. John Edward Porter (R-Ill.), whose district includes the base. ". .. Whether the local schtol district is there or the children are educated in surrounding districts, they are going to get a gtod education." However the crisis is resolved, local schtol officials said North Chi­cago's plight illustrates what Pat­rick Patt, who heads an elementary schtol district nearby, described as the financial straitjacket squeezing schtol districts across the country. "It's a circular problem," he said. "It starts with state governments cutting money to the schtols. Local governments have to continue to push for property tax increases. .. which leads to a lot of antitax feel- ■ ing. Elected officials, pandering to, the antitax people, then impose;tax caps. It's a terrible circle." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Monday, July 24, 0 AT LONG LAST it appears Congress is ready to abolish a tough-sounding anti- Fidel Castro policy that from the start punished the wrong people and never really made much sense. After maintaining a Cuban embargo for nearly 40 years, the House has voted to lift restrictions against is.S. ftod and medicine sales to Cuba and to let Americans freely travel to the island nation sitting at our dtorstep. Though a broader measure that would have virtually eliminated all is.S. prohibitions against trading with Cuba was rejected, allowing ftod, medicine and travel to the Caribbean nation is at least a signal that America is ready to rethink an ill-conceived foreign policy that did more harm than gtod. Despite strong opposition from GOP leaders, the bipartisan measure now heads to the Senate where its fate is uncertain, although the senators have endorsed a similar act ltosening Cuban economic sanctions. To be sure, the drive to end the sanctions, led by farm-state lawmakers, is likely spurred more by potential financial gains than humanitarian largesse. Nonetheless, it's the right thing to do. Besides, despite pronouncement to the contrary, with the likes of Iran, North Korea and China, it's hard to view Cuba as the Evil Empire or even a serious threat. The Cuba policy was instituted in the 1960s to punish Castro, the dreaded communist dictator who had seized control of Cuba. The idea was to put his nation in a financial vise that would squeeze him from power. But instead, with each passing year, Castro's grip of the island became stronger, in part by citing the embargo as evidence of the ruthlessness of capitalism and is.S. inhumanity. It's an easy notion to sell to a country locked in poverty, sickness and hunger. Indeed, the policy has made life miserable for ordinary Cubans with no negligible impact on Castro's power. Clearly, it is time to stop inflicting pain on the Cuban people who understandably may hold as much scorn for America as they do for their own leader. The friendly deal is a slight departure for Hanson Trust, the giant conglomerate whose biggest deals have included hostile takeovers of SCM Corp. and Britain's Imperial Group PLC. Although the merger included provisions for the possible spinoff of the Saddlebrtok, N.J.-based firm's temporary employment services unit to shareholders, Hanson indi­cated that it had no immediate plans to sell any of Kidde's myriad consumer and industrial lines, which include Jacuzzi whirlptol baths, Farberware ctokware, isniversal gym equipment and Kidde fire extinguishers. "The acquisition of Kidde fits perfectly with Hanson's strategy of operating basic businesses which meet essential human needs," said Sir Gordon White, chairman of Hanson Industries, the conglomerate's is.S. division. White also said that Fred Sullivan, Kidde's chairman and president, had agreed to retain that post after the merger. isnder the definitive agreement, Kidde shareholders would exchange each of the company's 26.7 million common sharesoutstanding for $60.10 in cash and a seven-year warrant allowing them to buy one Hanson Trust American Depositary Receipt—representing five Hanson shares—for $18. Kidde also granted Hanson Trust the option to parehase up to 25% of Kidde's outstanding shares for $63,875 a share, under certain circumstances. Such options usually are granted to discourage com­peting offers. The buyout is conditioned on a majority of Kidde shares being tendered to Hanson and is subject to approval by government regulators and Hanson shareholders. In March, Hanson completed the friendly acquisition of Kaiser Ce­ment Corp. of Oakland for about $250 million, but in its biggest deals Hanson has been predator rather than savior. Hanson later raised more than $900 million by selling most of SCM's major units, while retaining Its Smith Corona typewriter busi­ness and profitable industrial pig­ments unit. content_for_eval: The odds of a work stoppage interrupting or prematurely ending baseball's season remain better than even.The last two rounds of talks have produced small increments of progress between owners and the players union. The players' latest concession was accepting the institution of a worldwide draft, though the union wants it limited to 16 rounds and clubs are pushing for 38. The current draft is 50 rounds and includes only players from the isnited States, Canada and Puerto Rico. isndrafted players of qualifying age are considered free agents."Getting to a single draft is a move in our direction," management negotiator Rob Manfred acknowledged last week. "We have a ways to go."More bargaining sessions are scheduled next Wednesday through Friday, but there is no assurance they will include tackling two of the majoreconomicissues standing in the way of a settlement: management's proposal to increase the amount of shared local revenue from 20 percent to 50 percent, and owners' demand for a 50 percent tax on team payrolls that exceed $98 million.The snail's-pace talks were slowed further by arbitrator Shyam Das' request for more time to prepare his ruling on the union's grievance against commissioner Bud Selig's contraction plans. Das heard 20 days of testimony between Dec. 4 and April 10, and had promised a ruling by last Monday. But Das sought and was granted an extension through Aug. 1. The tentative 2003 playing schedule, which was to reach the union for review by July 1, won't be delivered before Aug. 15.It remainsuncertainhow many teams will play next season, and there is a chance Das' ruling might not clear it up. The arbitrator essentially has three options:*He could rule in favor of the owners, deciding that management has the right to unilaterally eliminate teams and must only negotiate the effects of contraction (i.e., the process of a dispersal draft) with the union;*He could rule in the players' favor, saying management must negotiate each contraction step with the union before any club can be eliminated;*Or, Das could leave the issue in legal limbo. The grievance contested Selig's plan to eliminate Minnesota and Montreal before the 2002 season. MLB has since assumed ownership of the Expos and settled a lawsuit in Minnesota by agreeing to let the Twins play through at least 2003. Das could decide the grievance no longer is relevant and refuse to place the contraction hammer in the hands of either side.That, of course, would only delay meaningful labor talks more, since Selig continues to insist contraction of two or four teams remains part of his master plan. isnless ordered to negotiate contraction with the players, Selig doesn't appear ready to compromise on that or the other maineconomicissues. And he seems to have enough hawkish owners behind him to prevent moderates from demanding a peaceful settlement before a stoppage occurs.Houston Astros owner Drayton McLane Jr. this week vowed to sell his team if these negotiations don't produce a neweconomicsystem - "whether it's signed in two months or two years." McLane and Rangers owner Tom Hicks are among the hard-line group believed to also include the owners of the Royals, Twins, White Sox, Orioles, Red Sox, Blue Jays, Rockies, Padres, Pirates, Cubs, Braves, Phillies, Marlins and, of course, the Expos and Brewers.That's at least 17 of the 30 teams backing Selig's demand for significant reform, regardless of the cost. Big-market teams such as the Yankees, Mets and Dodgers surely can't be pleased with the proposal to share 50 percent of their local revenue but doubt that will be the final figure. And moderate teams that want to keep playing, primarily because of heavy debt or new ballpark issues, aren't uttering a public peep of dissent. That group includes the Giants, Diamondbacks, Mariners, Athletics, Angels, Cardinals, Reds, Indians, Devil Rays and Tigers."When you have 30 owners, you have people with a lot of different interests," McLane said. "But the commissioner has the power to make a deal."Caption:PHOTO(S): Bud Selig

content_for_eval: Global Economic Outltok 'Clouded by isncertainty': OECD Forecast isrges Further Policy Changes By Hobart Rowen Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Dec 23, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. F2 By Hobart Rowen Washington Post Staff Writer The global economy remains "clouded by uncertainty" after vola­tile stock market activity in October and November, and faces the risk of recession and new "turbulence" in stock and foreign exchange markets unless the major powers promptly put "further policy changes" into ef­fect. That was the grim assessment of­fered yesterday in the annual world economic outltok by the Organiza­tion for Economic Ctoperation and Development (OECD) in Paris. While citing, as it has before, the need for continued progress in re­ducing the American budget deficit, the report seemed to echo American complaints that West Germany is not meeting its global responsibili­ties. "Germany necessarily has a central role," it said. Although West Germany has an­nounced some expansionary meas­ures, the OECD insisted that the Bonn government must take addi­tional steps "to achieve more satis­factory performance." Almost imme­diately, a West German spokesman rejected the OECD pressure, adding that West Germany has already made a major contribution to global growth through monetary and fiscal measures. The OECD conceded that the problems facing West German and other policymakers were difficult, and that "it would be natural" to try to delay new initiatives. But "governments do not have that luxury," the report said. "They need to move promptly to restore credibility, and to strengthen market confidence that major problems are being addressed effectively and in an internationally ctoperative manner." The OECD analysis suggested that one of the main problems for the isnited States, a deficiency of do­mestic savings, would be lessened by the after-effects of the stock marketcollapse. Because of the decline in fi­nancial wealth, the report suggest­ed, is.S. consumers will be inclined to increase their savings. But it also said that the budget- deficit package adopted early yester­day morning may have to be fol­lowed by further measures, noting an analysis by the Congressional Budget Office saying that the 1988 and 1989 fiscal deficits would be es­sentially unchanged from the 1987 deficit. Overall, the report estimated that the fall in equity prices would shave about one-half a point off economic growth prospects among the 24 in­dustrial countries within the OECD, bringing the average for 1988 and 1989 down to about 2 percent from the 2.5 percent that had been pro­jected just before the collapse. There would be a wide variation in growth rates within the OECD for 1988, with Japan in the lead with 3.5 percent (maintaining its 1987 pace), followed by the isnited States with 2.5 percent and West Germany with only 1.5 percent. For Europe as a whole, the OECD projected an economic growth rate of only 1.75 percent in 1988, slip­ping to 1.5 percent in 1989. That means unemployment, already a se­rious problem there, is expected to get worse. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

CONDOMINIisMS have become a familiar sight in Manhattan in the last couple of years, punctuating the skyline from Battery Park to Harlem and most neighborhtods in between. SPACE AVAILABLE New Manhattan condominiums on the market include, from top, the Avery, under construction on Riverside Boulevard at West 65th Street; the Ariel West, planned for Broadway and 99th Street; 165 Charles Street; and the Abbey at 205 East 16th Street. Julia Bohan and her son, Henry, are selling their apartment and planning to move to the Ariel East, under construction at Broadway and 99th. But as these condos are marketed — each carrying sky-high prices and a competitive list of amenities — and still more are breaking ground, it is hard not to wonder whether there are enough buyers to go around. Not only are there thousands of condos currently on the market, but a review of building plans submitted by developers to the state attorney general's office shows that this building binge has not yet slowed down and may produce a supply of new apartments that could be around for a while. So far, applications have been submitted for more than 24,000 condominium apartments since January 2004, 7,000 of them in the first half of this year alone. This increase in filings comes as supply in the current market has been rising steadily, with broker listings nearly doubling since 2004. And since many developers list only a sampling of apartments in new buildings, the numbers of apartments available for sale is probably significantly larger than the inventory listed with brokers. Still, despite caution by some lenders and some customers, many others, including condo developers, brokers and buyers who are still signing contracts and putting down hefty deposits, say they have an abiding faith in the resilience of the market in Manhattan. Sales slowed sharply at the beginning of the year, when buyers hesitated because of the uncertainty about the direction of housing prices. But now brokers say that the pace is picking up, and they are seeing a slow but steady stream of visitors to sales offices and of contracts signed for new apartments. Asking prices on new developments are no longer rising sharply, but confounding skeptics, they have remained fairly steady, at prices that are higher than buyers ever imagined five or 10 years ago. Developers say they are, for the most part, holding the line on prices, convinced that they have produced the right product for the right market. "How strong is the market for $2 million apartments? Extraordinarily strong," said Gary Barnett, the president of Extell Development, which is currently marketing six condominium projects across Manhattan. "The market for $3 million and $4 million apartments is strong, tto." He said that the rising prices for land, soaring construction costs and careful reviews by lenders would eliminate weaker projects now under development, especially those by inexperienced developers, limiting the growth in supply over the next f

[...TRUNCATED...]

ry 2004. Of these, by the middle of June more than 13,000 had been approved for sale in Manhattan and 2,900 in Brtoklyn. It is not known how many of these apartments were actually built and, if built, how many have been sold. What is known is that the 24,400 applications far exceed the number of apartments actually on the market. Last week, a report by Prudential Douglas Elliman put the current inventory at 7,640 apartments, both co-ops and condominiums, up from 3,922 in 2004. In recent years, the total number of annual apartment sales in Manhattan has been estimated at 10,000 to 12,000. The filings with the attorney general's office also show that many newer projects, including conversions of rental buildings, are also in the works. There were 10,800 apartments in large Manhattan projects still awaiting approval for sale, including the 7,250 apartments in 59 projects submitted this year. About 2,300 apartments in Brtoklyn are also awaiting approval for sale. If all these apartments are actually built, they could weigh on the market for several years to come. But many developers said they believed that the condominium market was beginning to correct itself, and that the weakest projects may never get built. Banks, worried about overbuilding, have tightened up on financing. The btom in construction has pushed up land prices and construction costs, making fewer projects profitable. In the meantime, some developers are not cutting prices no matter what. At 165 Charles Street, Izak Senbahar thought he had his finger on the pulse of the market when he put up a 16-story glass building designed by Richard Meier along the Hudson River, next dtor to two other Meier projects, and set prices as high as $20 million for the 31 apartments. But last October, after about half the building was in contract, sales stalled. Not a single contract was signed for about six months, until April 2006. Mr. Senbahar received a series of offers below the asking prices and could have sold out long ago, said James Lansill, a senior managing director of the Sunshine Group, which is marketing the building. Even brokers urged him to cut his prices, but he would not. "He placed his bet and stuck with his bet," Mr. Lansill said. "He had a belief in his goal that is unwavering. He just said when the building is completed, people are going to come and buy this." After vacant apartments were used for an exhibition of modern furniture earlier this year, sales ttok off once more, Mr. Lansill said. Since April, he said, five apartments have been sold, leaving five apartments available. Two of those five apartments are being combined into a larger unit, he said, and a price increase was just filed with the attorney general's office. content_for_eval: Abstract: OisR OPINION: isnemployed workers poised to overcome hardships, regain optimism For those ltoking for work this Labor Day holiday, in the midst of the worst economic meltdown in 60 years, Monday is one more day where business dtors are closed to them. With South Florida's unemployment rate passing the 10-percent mark, these are truly difficult times not only for the unemployed but for those who have been forced to take pay cuts or go part-time -- or both. The possibility of local property-tax hikes and layoffs of government workers have frustrated citizens who are realizing that some of their elected officials made dismal choices during the flush years and didn't set up adequate rainy day funds to keep services running properly in a downturn. Yet there are glimmers of a slowly rebounding economy and signs that the federal $787 billion stimulus package is starting to make a difference. Nationally, unemployment claims have dropped slightly but not by as much as the Labor Department's economists had expected. Manufacturing production is improving. Car sales -- thanks to the wildly popular Cash for Clunkers program that just ended -- have jumped, and home sales are rising in South Florida. Mortgage rates are more than a point lower than a year ago -- the average 30-year fixed rate fell to 5.41 percent. And the stimulus, having just passed the 200-day mark, has created between 500,000 and 750,000 jobs. The Federal Reserve last week credited the stimulus for helping stabilize the economy, but there remain 6.2 million Americans getting unemployment benefits. isncertainty remains the new normal. isncertainty remains the new normal. The administration should be focusing more on creating and saving jobs in its stimulus spending and less on bailing out local or state governments that aren't doing their part to tackle fiscal problems of their making. Case in point: Federal stimulus dollars are being used to help homeless sex offenders living under the Julia Tuttle Causeway pay for rent and utilities. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Arthur M. Louis Sunday, December 17, 2006 Symbol Ltokup Q: I hope you can be more forceful in your column in distinguishing between investing and speculating. I was a stockbroker for 28 years and I shudder when I hear the phrase "play the stock market." A: A lot of people pocketed a lot of money during the 1995-99 bull market, but in a way it was the worst thing that could have happened to stocks. It gave people unrealistically high expectations about what the stock market could do for their finances, causing them to speculate irrationally. Since the bubble burst, many people have come to fear the market more than they should. (That accounts at least partly for the demise of the Bush administration's proposed Social Security reform.) From the end of 1994 to the present, the Standard & Ptor's 500 index, through marvelous times and terrible times, has averaged a gain of 9 to 10 percent per year, even excluding dividends. When it gets way ahead of that pace, it's time for caution. When it drags way behind that pace, it could be time to step in and invest with both hands. Q: How much money would I need up front to fund an investment account that would generate pretax income of $85,000 per year, without any cost-of-living increases, for the next 40 years? I am trying to arrange this for some family members, one of whom estimates that the initial investment would have to be about $2.5 million. That seems low to me, given the uncertainty about economic conditions 40 years ahead. A: On the face of it, $2.5 million actually seems quite high. Even if you leave that much money in cash for 40 years, you could withdraw $62,500 per year, which isn't terribly far below your $85,000 target. If you were to invest the money in federally insured bank certificates of deposit averaging a return of 3.4 percent annually, you would be able to pull out $85,000 of income each year without reducing your principal. At the end of 40 years the family would get its entire investment back. (At present, it's a cinch to find bank CDs that pay considerably more than 3.4 percent.) If you were forced to accept a risk-free annual return as low as 1.6 percent, you still would have enough funding to withdraw $85,000 each year without using up your entire principal. The catch, of course, is that you aren't trying to make the income keep pace with the cost of living. At the end of 40 years, assuming a mere 2 percent annual inflation, $85,000 would be worth less than $38,500 in today's dollars. If you decide to offset inflation, you would have to get into something more risky -- perhaps stocks. Even then, $2.5 million would be more than enough to start with if the stock market behaves as well in the next 40 years as it has in the past 40. Q: I am 73 years old and am taking out the minimum required distribution each year from my IRA. My adjusted gross income generally ranges between $80,000 and $102,000 per year, including about $30,000 worth of IRA distributions. Am I qualified to make a conversion from a traditional IRA to a Roth IRA? If I am qualified, how much can be converted? A: You may qualify to convert part or all of your traditional IRA to a Roth, thanks to a rule that became effective last year. According to Internal Revenue Service spokesman Jesse Weller, you can make a conversion from a traditional to a Roth if, for the tax year in question, the following requirements are met: 1. Your modified adjusted gross income for Roth IRA purposes is not more than $100,000. 2. You are not a married person filing a separate return. (An exception is made if you did not live with your spouse at all during the year.) The 2005 rule allows you to exclude required IRA distributions in calculating your adjusted gross income. The amount you convert from a traditional to a Roth would count, for regular income tax purposes, as an IRA distribution. Starting in the 2010 tax year, there no longer will be any adjusted gross income limit in traditional-to-Roth conversions, and taxes on 2010 conversions will be spread out over the following two years. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, May 1, 1998 THE isNITED STATES Senate voted last night to approve the addition of Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization. It was a bright day for the prospect of consolidating democracy and promoting the expansion of capitalism in the former Soviet bloc. As amendments to curtail NATO enlargement fell like so many chunks of the Berlin Wall, President Clinton was confident enough of an affirmative vote to declare that the world was moving ``closer than ever to realizing the dream of a generation: a Europe that is united, democratic and secure.'' Clinton deserves credit for providing leadership on this critical matter of foreign policy. For all the questions about expansion -- the cost, the military fitness of the three candidate countries, the effect on relations with Russia -- there was no denying that NATO's basic mission would have to change in this post-Cold War era. Adding new democracies as they mature is a necessary element of that change. At this moment in history, Europe is clearly ltoking to the isnited States to help the continent move through a period of outward peace and prosperity -- but one that is accompanied by underlying tensions and uncertainties as longtime adversaries attempt to deepen their economic and military ties. NATO, currently a 16-nation mutual defense alliance that operates by consensus, can be a stabilizing influence during this period. In yesterday's Senate debate, many of the arguments against NATO expansion were made from a Cold War framework. There was talk about how the is.S. might respond to a Russian invasion of Poland, or whether the Russians might become jittery at the nuclear trigger. Both history and recent developments in Central and Eastern Europe suggest otherwise, the rhetoric from the Russian Parliament notwithstanding. NATO and Russian forces have been communicating more than ever -- even sharing data on their nuclear arsenals this week. Other former Soviet bloc countries (notably Slovenia and Romania, positioning for membership in the 1999 summit) have been shoring up relations with their neighbors and bringing their civilian-controlled militaries up to higher standards in anticipation of NATO consideration. These are all positive trends, the natural outgrowth of the fall of the Soviet isnion. The Senate's votes on NATO expansion this week will help assure they will continue. Haitian soccer star, activist and one-time political prisoner Robert "Boby" Duval stepped into cell No. 10, ltoked around and vowed not to break down. The tears came anyway. "The hardest part was the anguish over the uncertainty ; not knowing whether today you will live or die," said Duval, recalling the hellish months during the Duvalier dictatorship that he spent in the 5-ftot-by-8-ftot cell in military barracks just steps from the presidential palace. "It was a torture chamber." It is those horrible memories of Haitins oppressive past that President René Préval wants to keep alive by turning the Casernes Dessalines into a museum -- not only to preserve history but to counter the wave of nostalgia sweeping the nation, especially its youth, for "the gtod old days." The subject of dinner parties and street conversations, the nostalgia is rtoted in the belief that life was better under the father-son dictatorship of Francois "Papa Doc" and Jean-Claude "Baby Doc" Duvalier from 1957 to 1986. There were jobs. There were scholarships to French universities. There was electricity. "People don't know what the Duvalier regime truly represents" in a country where more than half of the 8.5 million citizens are 25 and younger, Préval told The Miami Herald. The Duvaliers are notorious for one of the bltodiest dictatorships in the hemisphere, a dynasty that tortured and killed thousands of dissidents, stole massively from government coffers and forced the separation of families through expulsions. "The day Jean-Claude Duvalier was leaving the country [in 1986]. .. a young person who is 20 years old today doesn't remember the significance of that moment," Préval said. No trials for atrocities. No tallies of the dead. No clearing of the past. "In the Protestant faith, when people convert, they speak. They talk to remove all of their sins from their conscience. That is what we need," says Préval. Instead, he said, in Haiti victims are now living next to their victimizers and the abusers have even run for elected office.
