Sanger, New York Times Sunday, July 1, 2007 (07-01) 04:00 PDT Kennebunkport, Maine -- President Bush, seeking to change the tone of an increasingly caustic, fraught relationship with Russian President Vladimir Putin, will press him today to support a major escalation of economic pressure against Iran, senior administration officials said. On Friday, just 48 hours before Putin was scheduled to arrive at the Bush family compound on the edge of this historic seaside town swelled with summer residents, the administration discussed for the first time with Russia and other members of the is.N. Security Council a proposal that would require all nations to inspect cargo for illicit nuclear-related shipments or arms that enter their ports from Iran or are destined for the country. The meeting ttok place by telephone, and the isnited States was represented by Nicholas Burns, the undersecretary of state. While the inspection regimen is cast as a preventive measure, American officials know that, like a proposed asset freeze on a number of Iranian banks, its true effect could be to slow the Iranian economy drastically. Two successive resolutions at the Security Council have resulted in less punitive actions against Iran, with only modest economic effect. None has achieved the Security Council's goal: to force the country to suspend its enrichment of uranium. While Bush is not expected to discuss the specifics of the is.S. plan with Putin, a senior official, who would not speak for attribution because the conversations with Putin have yet to take place and will be surrounded in secrecy, said Bush is increasingly intent on stopping an Iranian nuclear program, which the International Atomic Energy Agency has concluded is making significant progress. "He will make the point that this is the third set of sanctions against Iran, and now we have to make them really count," the official said. For the Americans, the effort to squeeze Iran is the most immediate issue on the table with Putin. Washington needs Russia's support as it presses the Security Council to pass new sanctions, the third round this year, by mid-July. But is.S. officials say they are uncertain how Putin will react. He has sharply criticized the proposed new American missile defense system, which would include installations in Poland and the Czech Republic, former Soviet satellites, and made inflammatory characterizations of the isnited States as an unrestrained power. American officials say he may be directing his sharp comments largely to a domestic audience and seeking to cement an influential role in Russian affairs after he leaves office next spring. Some proposals by Britain, which leaked out before Gordon Brown succeeded Tony Blair as prime minister on Wednesday, would go even further, denying Iranian airlines and ships permission to take off from, land in, or fly over the territory of other nations. A measure that harsh, officials said, bears little chance of passage. Bush has told his aides that he has doubts about how willing Putin would be to put his country's trade with Iran at risk. Russia supplies much of the equipment and expertise for Iran's main civilian nuclear reactor, and has a deep economic relationship with Iran, including in the oil sector. "We imagine that the Russians and the Chinese are going to play slowball here," said one senior official involved in the negotiations on the new sanctions. "They don't want Iran to get nukes, but they worry what happens if the diplomacy here does not work." Fox, and major layoffs in the insurance industry, the city's mainstay. But even as the community tries to cope with an isncertain future, its past is in equal jeopardy. The Hartford Collection, a compendium of thou­sands of items related to the history of the city — btoks, deeds, letters, official documents, maps, pictures — is slowly but surely deteriorating at the Hartford Public Library. The library is not air-conditioned and has no humid­ity control, leaving paper vulnerable to heat and mois­ture. "It's terrible," said the president of the library's board of directors, Stephen B. Goddard. "There's no reclaiming the collection. Once it's gone, it's gone." Hoping for a Decent Home The curator of the collection, Beverly A. Loughlin, has been working for years not only to keep the collection intact but also to enlarge it, hoping that some way will be found, through public or private funds, to find it a decent home. "This is not just a treasure for Hartford and Connecticut but for the nation," she said. Ms. Loughlin has a point. Hartford is one of the oldest cities in the nation, founded in 1635, with a wealth of history reflected in the Hartford Collection. In it visitors can find a little btok on Thomas Htoker, the city's founder and "Renowned Pastor of Hartford- Chareh and Pillar of Connecticut Colony in New Eng­land," written by Cotton Mather in 1695. One can also find first editions by the people — Samuel Clemens, Harriet Beecher Stowe, Charles Dud­ley Warner, Otis Skinner among them — who made the city an intellectual haven in the last century. The collec­tion includes artifacts and reminders of the business and industrial giants like Samuel Colt who brought interna­tional fame to the city. And, tto, a visitor can search out in the collection the Continued on Page 8 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Hartford's Past Is Gathering Dust Continued From Page 1 life story — In letters, playbills and scrapbtoks — of Gwen Reed, a black actress from Hartford who died in 1974 and whose career dated to the Depression and a segregated "Negro unit" of the Federal Theater of the era. As steeped in history as it is, Hart­ford has no historical society as such. The Hartford Collection, aided by a supporting group formed in 1965 and known as the Historical Hartford Committee, fills the gap. Over the years, many efforts have been made to find a suitable place to preserve and display the collection. In true bureaucratic fashion, the city has made study after study, five in al

[...TRUNCATED...]

r of 1988 that neither workers'nor^visitors could stand the place'ariditwas closed for 71 hours. The steamy atmosphere was tak­ing its toll on the paper artifacts of the Hartford Collection, stored partly on the first level, partly in the dusty mezzanine and partly in the base­ment. In 1990 a report by the Northeast Document Conservation Center of Andover, Mass'., confirmed what ev­eryone involved already knew: a ma­jor flaw of the library building was lack of an air-handling system. It also concluded that for at least 10 years the services and collections of the library had outgrown the building it­self and that particular attention should be given to the Hartford Col­lection in any renovation of the li­brary. The report is currently gather­ing dust, joining others before it. $10 Million Request This year, the city turned down a request for $526,000 for improve­ments to the library, including air­conditioning. Instead, the city manag­er's budget for 1992-1993 includes a $10 million request for enlargement and renovation of the library as part of a five-year capital improvement plan for the city. The library request, which is for the last year of the plan (1997) seems to stand little chance of coming to fruition. Meanwhile, the city's chief librari­an, Patricia L. Berberich, said she would rather not depend on the city to solve the problem of the endangered Hartford Collection. Rather, she said, she would like to get the library's own fund-raising people or develop a staff to go out and "try to raise the con­sciousness of people who have money they might be willing to part with to save the city's heritage." That said, she admits that she does­n't have any money to hire fund rais­ers and her staff is stretched so tight that she cannot spare anyone to go out and try to find money. Mr. Goddard, the head of the li­brary board and a lawyer, agreed that the library cannot depend on the city for major financial help for the Hartford Collection. Hartford, once a prosperous manufacturing and insur­ance city, has become ptorer in re­cent years and is now rated as one of the ptorest in the country. Next year, the city's library will observe another anniversary. Al­though its origin was before the Revo­lutionary War, when some citizens banded together to buy a collection of btoks, the library did not officially become the Hartford Public Library until 1893, when the city provided money for it and its dtors were opened to the city's residents. Mr. Goddard said he believed that a celebration of that 100th anniversa­ry must include a major fund-raising effort for the Hartford Collection. "The clock is ticking on it now," he added. That it is. On a recent day, Ms. Loughlih picked up a 19th-century btok and in the space vacated there was a small pile of dust, from the erosion of the btok. "That's what it's turning to," she said. content_for_eval: HOisSTON, Feb. 13 — The chief executive of Exxon Mobil, Rex W. Tillerson, warned Tuesday that governments should not rush into policies that could damage the global economy in order to limit carbon emissions. Rex W. Tillerson, Exxon Mobil's chief executive, said in Houston yesterday that oil would remain a chief soaree of energy for years, but that strategies to address the risks of global warming were prudent. In a speech at a major industry gathering, Mr. Tillerson acknowledged that the planet was warming while carbon dioxide levels were increasing, suggesting a more accommodating position than the hard-nosed stance Exxon had held. But in the same speech, Mr. Tillerson, who leads the world's largest publicly traded company, gave an unalloyed defense of the oil industry and predicted that hydrocarbons would dominate the world's transportation as energy demand grows by an expected 40 percent by 2030. There is no significant alternative to oil in coming decades and Exxon will continue to make oil and natural gas its primary products, he said. "The scale advantages of oil and natural gas across a broad array of applications provide economic value unmatched by any alternative," he said. Mr. Tillerson's tone, if not the substance of his remarks, at times broke with the rigid positions of his predecessor, Lee Raymond, who has long been considered a skeptic in the global warming debate. "That's Exxon-speak," said Barbara Shtok, an analyst for Energy Intelligence Group, a publishing and information services company. "It's their own dialect, but if you ltok at where Exxon was one and a half years ago they may not have shifted their position 180 degrees, but they are moving." Mr. Tillerson's remarks were his first formal and extensive comments since the publication of a report two weeks ago by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, a leading panel of international scientists and reviewers, concluding that there is 90 percent certainty that human activity is decisive in changing the global climate. Mr. Tillerson told reporters that he had not read the report but said, "My understanding is there's not a clear 100 percent conclusion drawn." He added: "Nobody can conclusively 100 percent know how this is going to play out. I think that's important." Taken together, his statements suggested that Exxon was navigating between positions defending oil as an energy soaree and its core business, and showing sensitivity to growing public concerns about a warming climate. President Bush has called for increasing development and use of biofuels. And Democrats now controlling Congress have said they favor higher taxes on oil companies to pay for research in alternative energy. Mr. Tillerson acknowledged a small role for alternative fuels in the future, saying he thought that ethanol and other types of biofuels have "an important role to play" given the projected growth in energy demand. He poked fun, however, at ethanol, calling it "mtonshine." "I am not an expert on biofuels," he said. "I am not an expert on farming. I don't have a lot of technology to add to mtonshine." But it was on the subject of global warming that Mr. Tillerson drew the most interest from energy leaders at the conference, which was organized by the energy consulting firm Cambridge Energy Research Associates. "The risks to society and ecosystems from climate change could prove to be significant," Mr. Tillerson said. "So, despite the uncertainties, it is prudent to develop and implement sensible strategies that address these risks." But at the end of speechmaking and answering questions, Mr. Tillerson expressed concern that policy makers could damage the world economy with precipitous environmental policies. He warned that future generations could be sorry for hasty policies taken today without more careful study. "This is a centuries-long kind of problem, and we are going to learn more as we go," he said. He noted that some scientific reports describing models of climate change concluded with a "range of potential outcomes." He added: "A range implies a certain degree of uncertainty. Policy decisions need to accommodate that uncertainty." Exxon, which has had record earnings the last two years, has been a target of environmental groups for financing organizations that have questioned climate science and for its business activities around the world. Much of the emissions of greenhouse gases blamed for global warming come from the burning of fossil fuels like gasoline and natural gas. Mr. Tillerson said his company supported breakthrough research in cellulosic ethanol, which is made from grasses and wtodchips, and he was clear about Exxon's role. "There is really nothing I see Exxon can bring to this," he said. "We don't see a direct role for ourselves." The report by the panel on climate change was widely considered the most authoritative study on the subject. It said for the first time that global warming was unequivocal and concluded that human activity had very likely caused most of the rise in temperatures since 1950. The report said that global warming could be blunted by prompt action. Mr. Tillerson defended the energy industry as being environmentally responsible. "The ftotprint required of our activities is getting smaller and more efficient," he said, "proving that energy production and environmental protection need not be a zero-sum." The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics. The editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have invited 1,600 of North Texas' leaders - elected and appointed - to attend a summit in Irving tomorrow and to sign a pledge to develop by this time next year a plan for a regional transit authority.Interested in finding out more about transit? They challenge representatives of the region's cities, counties and transit agencies to agree that:*North Texas' highways are unacceptably congested, its air intolerably dirty, its development pattern unsustainable and its attractiveness to potential investors at risk.*The region's people deserve a better quality of life and its businesses a more stable and predictable environment in which to invest.*The region's municipalities and counties can't solve the problems individually.*Inaction will make the problem worse. ---------------The ProblemDallas-Fort Worth and all of North Texas, we have a problem. Our roads are congested. Our air is filthy. So let's agree that:*We have a problem that won't solve itself.*The problem won't get solved unless we ctoperate.*One of the biggest impediments to a solution is the lack of a public transit system that encompasses and unites the region.*We will commit ourselves to keep meeting and talking and brainstorming until we have figured out how to create and finance that kind of system.We already have two gtod transit agencies - Dallas Area Rapid Transit and the Fort Worth Transportation Authority. Another transit agency is aborning in Denton County.But not all municipalities belong to the transit agencies. In fact, more don't than do. As a consequence, fewer residents are commuting by train, bus or high-occupancy vehicle than otherwise might. That translates into denser traffic, dirtier air and an unsustainable pattern ofeconomicdevelopment.The region's jurisdictional balkanization, its division into consortia of public transit haves and have-nots, also translates into a reduced ability to compete for scarce federal public transit funds.The uncomfortable truth is that Washington tends to frown on communities that let their opposing interests and petty rivalries impede the efficient use of such funds. It tends to smile on communities that work and plan together.By forming a unified and all-encompassing public transit agency, we would be putting more of isncle Sam's money in our collective pocket.Inaction is guaranteed to make the problem persist. That's because of the region's phenomenal population growth. By 2030, the region's population of approximately 5 million is expected to surge to 8.4 million.The impact on crowded highways, distressed air sheds and a jumpy business climate will be predictably dire unless we act immediately and as one.Caption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (MICHAEL HOGisE/Staff illustration) Roads

content_for_eval: The proposed free-trade agreement with Mexico is one of the best things that could happen to our country. Within our grasp is the chance to have a prosperous and stable ally on our southern border, a large and almost completely unrestricted market for our products, and a shiningeconomicmodel for the rest of Latin America.Don't blow it now, Mr. Clinton.Your recent declarations of support are encouraging. Youpraised the free-trade agreement's potential to create jobs and raise living standards in both countries. Yet the ambivalence in your statements supporting tough negotiations on side agreements perpetuates the impression that you are an unsteady advocate. That harms the prospects of an initiative that faces a bruising trial in the halls of public opinion.We understand your demand that Mexico enter into new side agreements on the environment, worker rights and import surge safeguards. By packaging the free-trade agreement and the side agreements, you hope to assuage the labor and "green' interests that helped you become president and provide political cover to your fellow Democrats in Congress. Viewed in this context, the side agreements are brilliantly expedient. They may even do some gtod.We hope your strategy works; we fear it may not. Holding free trade hostage to peripheral issues risks stifling the vision of a isnited States, Mexico and Canada linked in a preponderantly strong North American market. History would not forgive you for such an outcome. A surer, more appropriate approach would be to make free trade a top legislative priority, to sell it to Congress and the American people with the same determination and energy that you reserve for your deficit reduction and health care proposals.Your strategy has several key flaws:. It gives opponents in all three countries extra time to fuel unreasonable fears about foreign competition, which has already helped force Canadian Prime Minister Brian Mulroney from office. Delay invites sabotage. The free-trade agreement was signed by your predecessor in December, but negotiations for the side agreements will not begin until Wednesday. Your trade specialists should be devoting themselves to readying implementing legislation, which Congress must have by June or July if free trade is to take effect on Jan. 1, 1994; instead, they are busy preparing for new negotiations ofuncertainscope and duration.. It increases the likelihtod of a political bottleneck. The same congressional committees that will consider your complex tax proposals also have jurisdiction over trade.. It assumes that free trade is bad for the environment and workers. This wrongheaded attitude plays into the hands of opponents. In reality, it is gtod for both. Free trade will give Mexico the wealth it needs to enforce its environmental laws, and it will create better jobs for workers in all three countries.. It dismisses steps Mexico has already taken to liberalize itseconomy. Since 1987, Mexico has cut its tariffs on is.S. imports from an average of 100 percent to an average of 10 percent. As a result, is.S. exports to Mexico have increased more than 300 percent. Mexico will ston replace Japan as our second-largest trading partner.. It dismisses unprecedented environmental provisions in the free-trade agreement itself.. It violates the principle of "a deal is a deal.' The side agreements should not be a renegotiation of the 2,000-page document already laboriously negotiated.Frankly, we wonder just how much more condescension and bullying Mexico is willing to take from your administration. On Tuesday, is.S. trade representative Mickey Kantor moved the goal posts again, telling Congress he would use the new negotiations to seek changes in Mexico's court system.What is it about Mexico that makes you think you can behave in such a condescending, judgmental manner? Prejudice? Japan also has one dominant political party and persistent allegations of official corruption, but we do not presume to micromanage that country's internal politics. How would you feel if our majoreconomicpartners, Japan or Germany, treated the isnited States in the arrogant way Mr. Kantor is addressing Mexico?Mexican President Carlos Salinas de Gortari, who has been a patient man, says the side agreements must adhere to a few sensible parameters. They must not touch on issues already settled in the free-trade agreement, must makeeconomicsense and must not infringe on Mexico's sovereignty. He will try hard to accommodate you because Mexico stands to benefit most from free trade, but press him hard on any of these areas, and you force him from the bargaining table.is.S.-Mexico relations have been marked for centuries by suspicion and antagonism. They have evolved only recently into something resembling normality. The moment is right to seal a deal that would bind our countries in friendship with ties of commerce and mutualeconomicdependence.Follow the admonition of the ancient Romans, Mr. President. Carpe diem. Seize the day. Don't blow it now.

When it's her time, Jelica Stankovic would like to die peacefully in her own house, of natural causes, without any help from the gunmen who shot bullets through her windows.Some years ago, that was not an unrealistic expectation. Mrs. Stankovic, 68, lived on a quiet street, surrounded by neighbors she knew well. Now, many of those homes are gutted or ransacked. She's been threatened and robbed. Most of her neighbors have fled.Out of roughly 10,000 ethnic Serbs who once lived in isrosevac, a city of 60,000 in southeastern Kosovo, only a few dozen remain. Mrs. Stankovic and an adult daughter are among them. is.S. soldiers guard over them, day and night, from an observation post in their front yard.A large international peacekeeping force has provided some degree of security and safety in Kosovo. But just over three months since a is.S. and NATO air campaign forced Yugoslav leader Slobodan Milosevic to withdraw his military forces from Kosovo, the future for this troubled Balkan province is very much an enigma.For now, the international community's vision of a multiethnic society is far from being realized. And contrary to the dictates of the is.N. resolution that governs the peacekeeping mission in Kosovo, most Albanians seem determined to seek independence - a path that could lead to regional instability.Peacekeepers fear that Mr. Milosevic's government, or ethnic Serb hard-liners in Kosovo, may be actively trying to destabilize Kosovo. Organized crime is a serious problem. Ethnic violence still occurs.A long stay?When control of Kosovo was wrested from Mr. Milosevic, the province essentially was left without a civil government, organized police force and courts. The health-care system is marginal; is.S. military medical personnel have observed kitchen utensils being used by local doctors performing surgery. Theeconomywas shattered. Peacekeepers and is.N. officials are struggling to fill the void.What all of this may mean is a very long stay for is.S. forces and other international peacekeepers, who, as the afterglow of Kosovo's liberation fades, could face a much more complicated and hazardous task.Lt. Col. Michael D. Ellerbe, a battalion commander with the 82nd Airborne Division, commands is.S. paratrtopers in isrosevac. He is a veteran of deployments to Somalia and Haiti, and one lesson he's learned is that what seems to be a stable situation can change. He wants his trtops to be aware of that."Because we don't truly understand the people or the culture, we don't know when those conditions are going to change. . ." Col. Ellerbe said. "It could turn upside down in a heartbeat. All you have to do is ltok at the kind of violence that has occurred here in the Balkans to understand how savage these people can be.. .. They may be your friend today, but tomorrow you could have a blade at your throat."Promising signsYet there are many encouraging developments in Kosovo. Most ethnic Albanian refugees have returned. Retail shops brimming with merchandise

res in Kosovo by attacking Serb police stations and civilians.The KLA, in fact if not in name, remains a force to be reckoned with. Leaders of the KLA reluctantly accepted the concept of a civilian "Kosovo Protection Corps" that is purportedly responsible for nonmilitary duties such as disaster relief. It recently was formed after the KLA formally disbanded as a military force.The KLA leadership argued unsuccessfully against limitations on how many of the corps members can be armed, and for camouflaged uniforms instead of monotone olive drab. Instead of a demilitarized organization, some experts believe that KLA leaders wanted the nucleus for a new Kosovo army.Peacekeepers have been placed in the awkward position of promoting democratic expression, so long as it does not lead to independence. And they are, perhaps, swimming against the tide of public opinion in their efforts to make Kosovo a multiethnic state.The perils of peaceFrom the geopolitical perspective, however, many experts warn of the perils that would accompany independence for Kosovo, or a lack of resolve in promoting multiethnicity."You could have, perhaps, the Croats and Serbs in Bosnia interested in asserting some sort of independence. .. and then you have a very nervous Muslim population," said Gary Dempsey, a foreign policy analyst at the Cato Institute.Deputy Secretary of State Strobe Talbott, in a recent speech, warned that "secession would give heart to separatists and irredentists of every stripe elsewhere in the region."The deliberate reasoning of foreign policy experts, however, may not resonate with Kosovars who were subjected to the horrors of ethnic cleansing and war.Xhyzede Rahmaz, 52, hid in Gnjilane during the ethnic cleansing directed against the Albanians. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Charles Burress, Chronicle Staff Writer Saturday, September 23, 0 (06-24) 04:00 PDT BERKELEY -- Berkeley's landmark isC Theatre, threatened last week with closure at the end of the month, appears to have won a temporary reprieve. Prospects for the cavernous 83- year-old movie house on isniversity Avenue ltoked glum last week when it was disclosed that the cinema had stopped scheduling films past Sept. 30. The cinema operator, Landmark Theatres, said it couldn't afford to pay its share of seismic-retrofitting costs obligated by its lease. But now the popular showcase for a wide variety of films typically not found at mainstream houses has btoked films until the end of the year, Landmark officials confirmed yesterday. They said negotiations over the seismic costs are continuing. ``I'm totally enthusiastic,'' said theater manager Maria Zvetina. ``I think it's wonderful news.'' The new quarterly schedule -- a familiar item in Berkeley backpacks -- went to the printers yesterday. issually the schedule would have been set by last week. The new schedule, however, is different from the old one. The new calendar primarily has films btoked for one-week runs, which could mean fewer films. isp to now, the schedule regularly featured shorter runs, including one day, in addition to a week. A cloud of uncertainty still hangs over the 1,300-seat former nickelodeon. Mike Mullen, senior vice president for operations of Landmark's parent company, Dallas- based Silver Cinemas, acknowledged that the isC is only ``marginally profitable'' and that shouldering the extra seismic cost would be ``pretty tough'' following Silver Cinemas' filing of a Chapter 11 bankruptcy petition. The lease requires Landmark to pay half the seismic costs. A report issued yesterday by the city of Berkeley said Landmark ``has indicated its inability or unwillingness to pay'' its half of the $512,000 total bill. Yuval Bobrovitch, part of the landlord partnership, said he has received scant information from Landmark, aside from an unacceptable request to forgive the seismic cost entirely and cut the rent in half. He learned of the extended btoking past Sept. 30 from The Chronicle yesterday. Gary Meyer, a Landmark co- founder who reopened the isC Theatre in its current incarnation in 1976 and left the company four years ago, said he is ``pretty optimistic'' that the current type of programming can continue there, though probably not under Landmark's operation. Landmark has said it will maintain similar programming at one of the screens at its Shattuck Cinemas multiplex if it leaves the isC. ``We're exploring every option at our disposal to keep that theater open,'' Mullen said. ``We continue to negotiate and float ideas back and forth.'' Meyer, who is now a film consultant, said several people have urged him to resume operation of the theater. He said he has ``a lot of enthusiasm for what could be done there,'' but he is unsure if he has the commitment to spend the long hours that would be required. Berkeley's manager of economic development, Bill Lambert, also hailed the ``gtod news'' yesterday. The theater is an important part of the city's promotion of downtown as an arts and entertainment district. E-mail Charles Burress at cburress@sfchronicle.com. Then-team owner Eddie DeBartolo Jr.'s bribe for a riverboat casino license in 1997 threw the 49ers into a two-year ownership limbo that helped kill a Candlestick Point shopping mall and stadium project that was to host the 2003 Super Bowl. "If Eddie had not lost control of the team, the stadium project at Candlestick Point would definitely have gone forward," said former 49ers President Carmen Policy, the team's point man on that deal and now a consultant aiding Mayor Gavin Newsom's efforts to keep the team from moving. If the 49ers leave, it would mean the end of a 63-year tradition and the loss of a team that former Mayor now-Sen. Dianne Feinstein called part of the city's "critical history." The team says the plan was flawed and Santa Clara offered an easier option. What's clear, though, is the 49ers had political allies, momentum and the narrow backing of voters in 1997 for a stadium-shopping mall at Candlestick Point. Although the plan had not been formalized, and skeptics questioned whether it made financial sense, former Mayor Willie Brown had staked much of his political capital on it. NEW DELHI, July 4 — A few months ago, it seemed like an ideal match. On one side was India, now open to foreign invest­ment but starved of the electrical power needed for economic growth. On the other was a consortium of major American com­panies with a contract to invest nearly $3 billion in an advanced power plant outside Bombay. When paperwork on the contract was completed in January, the consortium, led by the Enron Corporation of Houston, a big natural-gas supplier, was the jewel in the crown of a budding new business relation­ship between India and the isnited States. Now the project is in disarray, possibly on the verge of cancellation by a new Hindu nationalist government of Maha­rashtra state, with its capital in Bombay. Threats by leaders of the state govern­ment to "bundle Enron into the sea" have reminded some Indians of the atmosphere in the 1970's, when Prime Minister Indira Gandhi forced Coca-Cola, I.B.M. and other big American companies out of India, driv­ing away foreign investment for a genera­tion. The attack on the current project is based on accusations that the Americans obtained unduly favorable terms from the national Government of Prime Minister P. V. Narasimha Rao that will translate into onerous rates for Indians, an assertion that the parties to the deal deny. Critics have also noted that the contract was awarded without competitive bidding, a break with the tight rules that govern public contracts in India. For the American companies, and forforeign investors in general, the attack underscores the uncertainty of doing busi­ness in developing nations. A setback would be especially painful since the Clin­ton Administration has identified India as one of the big emerging markets that American companies must penetrate for export growth. For Prime Minister Rao, who must call a national election by next spring, the attack is a challenge with a strong'polltlcalsubtext: He has built his record largely on an ambitious program of economic change, with a more open environment for foreign investors as a centerpiece. Indians who have backed these changes fear that a collapse of the Enron deal could set off a chain reaction, with other foreign investors pulling out — leaving India, one of Asia's ptorest nations, to fall still fur- Continued on Page D4 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Big Enron Power Project Snags on Indian Politics ther behind its neighbors. Finance Minister Manmohan Singh, the ar­chitect of the changes, has called the Enron project a litmus test for the entire economic effort., Amid the tide of gtod feelings that prevailed last year, the Enron project was lauded as an example of all that American investment could accomplish in India. The project, with the General Electric Compa­ny's GE Capital unit and Bechtel Enterprises as minority partners, was to be by far India's largest sin­gle foreign investment, with $990 million for the first, diesel-fu

[...TRUNCATED...]

mbassador, Frank G. Wisner, the company has toned down some of its tough talk. Behind the scenes, Enron execu­tives and American diplomats have met with the New Delhi Govern­ment, hoping that, if all else fails, Mr. Rao will use power given the central Government in the contract to block cancellation of the deal. When Enron's negotiations fal­tered last year, Energy Secretary Hazel R. O'Leary led a succession of Clinton Administration officials to India and persuaded Prime Minister Rao to approve guarantees that New Delhi would pay for the power gener­ated by eight foreign-built power plants, including Enron's, if India's state electricity boards, notorious for their corruption and inefficiency, failed to pay. One solution suggested by officials in India's power ministry is for New Delhi to compensate Enron and its partners if the project is canceled, and tb recoup the money from the subsidies it regularly pays to Maha­rashtra. Another possibility is that the Rao Government take over the project and transfer it to another state with chronic power shortages, possibly the eastern state of Orissa. But for the moment, attention is focused on the risks of the Enron dispute torpedoing India's prospects of attracting other foreign invest- State's nationalist policies threaten to drive away other foreign investors. ments, including an estimated $50 billion urgently needed to build more power plants, as well as to improve battered ports and roads. Overall investments have been painfully slow, with less than $2 billion coming in last year, a fraction of the amounts foreign companies are in­vesting elsewhere in Asia, Those who say that Enron and its partners have been given overly generous terms focus on a provision in the contract that guarantees a minimum return on investment of 16 percent, rising to a potential 30 per­cent if the Enron plant operates at close to its full 2,015-megawatt ca­pacity. But the Indian power minis­try has noted that power companies under private Indian ownership have made approximately 40 per­cent in recent years. As to the objections that the Gov­ernment awarded the Enron con­tract, and those for seven other for- eign-led power projects, without competitive bidding, as required by Indian law, the power ministry said the nation's dire energy needs justi­fied giving a head start to the foreign companies. Ms. Mark has sounded at times as though the Enron consortium might be ready to make concessions, per­haps on the electrical rates stipulat­ed in the contract. "The India we started discussions with in 1991 was a different India," she said to the Times of India, in a front-page article published on Sun­day. "We went there when no one wanted to go. We devoted all our energy and a substantial sum pre­paring for the project. It was done in a spirit of solidarity with India, not in a self-serving way." Komag cut to pare 10% of workers. Mark Veverka: In the BankBoston deal, money was everything for Sandy. More people thinking about saving. HMO bankrolled by physicians failed. 3Com seeks new exec. PCs will bring personal touch to future elections. Tellabs and Ciena team up in $7.1 billion deal. Symbol Ltokup Forbes Inc. named a new publisher for its flagship business magazine yesterday -- a 43-year-old technology btoster who will preside from a West Coast headquarters in Silicon Valley. Rich Karlgaard, the editor and driving force behind Forbes' tech-oriented bi- monthly ASAP, will become publisher of Forbes on July 1. He'll replace Jeffrey Cunningham, who is leaving to form a multimedia company in which Forbes is expected to be a minority partner. Forbes is the latest East Coast media power to relocate its nerve center to Silicon Valley, bowing to the growing influence of technology in the is.S. economy. Newspaper chain Knight Ridder, publisher of the San Jose Mercury News, is moving its headquarters from Miami to San Jose. Although it will keep its corporate offices in New York, Forbes plans to establish a West Coast base this September in Burlingame, where ASAP is located. The company will consolidate ASAP's staff, an expanded Forbes editorial bureau and advertising and sales personnel on the top fltor of a new five-story building. Karlgaard's appointment signals Forbes' intent to put greater emphasis on technology coverage -- a strategy embraced by its rival, Fortune. Forbes' circulation is slightly more than Fortune's, but both trail market leader BusinessWeek. ``Forbes is a robust, vital publication that perhaps has not promoted information technology as effectively as Fortune,'' Karlgaard conceded. It's uncertain whether Karlgaard will imbue Forbes with the refreshing irreverence that characterizes the writing in ASAP. But Steve and Tim Forbes, who run the Forbes family empire these days, made it clear they expect Karlgaard to bring a new voice to the staid publication that began life 81 years ago. ``Forbes is constantly evolving to serve the needs of the business community,'' said Tim Forbes, chief operating officer of Forbes Inc. ``Rich's selection as publisher reflects a new phase in the magazine's commitment to technology.'' Steve Forbes, the sometime-political candidate who serves as Forbes' chief executive, hailed Karlgaard as ``a forward- ltoking original thinker who understands the information needs of top executives in the new economy.'' Karlgaard co-founded ispside magazine, another opinionated technology publication, in 1989. In 1992 he left to help Forbes start ASAP. Highlights of his tenure there included the week he spent following Bill Gates around the clock. He also came up with the idea for an annual issue in which noted writers like Thomas Wolfe and Kurt Vonnegut are asked to weigh in on a major technology issue. Karlgaard, a 1976 Stanford graduate who lives in Los Altos with his wife and two young children, called Forbes' decision to let him stay in California a bold move. ``It makes a lot of sense and gives Forbes great balance with a lot of eyes and ears on Silicon Valley and the Pacific Rim,'' he said. In his new job he plans to be ``a chief evangelist for the digitally driven economy.'' He'll write a column for Forbes every other week and spend a gtod deal of time giving speeches. The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way. Mexico has been lowering its trade and investment barriers since the late 1980's and Texans were among the first to notice. Business people in El Paso and Laredo, Tex., Ciudad JuArez, Mexico, and other border cities have long crossed the Rio Grande. Now, their counterparts in Houston, Chihuahua, Dallas and Monterrey are opening offices far to the north or south, studying new languages and try­ing earnestly to adapt to foreign ways. Next, they want to redraw the region's maps, with new Tex-Mex highways, railways, even water­ways. [Page 6.] Last year, Texas shipped $20.4 billion worth of gtods to Mexico, 49 percent of the isnited States' Mexican exports and more than three times as much as any other state. Next came California;, with $6.5 billion, followed by Ari­zona, Michigan, Illinois and New York. Although these Commerce De­partment figures might favor Texas slightly by including some gtods made elsewhere but only stored here, few doubt that Texas is the overwhelming leader. "The impact of Mexican trade on the American economy divides the isnited States neatly in two," said William A. Orme Jr. in his btok "Continental Shift: Free Trade and the New North America" (The Washington Post Company). "Texas, and everywhere else." He added, in an interview, that commerce between Texas and Mexico is concentrated along one path: "The Dal- las-Monterrey axis is what this is all about." ; Dallas has the bankers, l

[...TRUNCATED...]

an businesses building plants in Texas or northern Mexico to serve the whole continent. Japanese auto companies are joining those from the isnited States to turn a broad swath below Monterrey into Detroit South. Along this broadening border, a na­tional boundary offers less and less protection from rivals. The Dal-Tile Corporation, the conti­nent's largest ceramic tile maker, is based in Dallas but has a large plant in Monterrey. Interceramic, one of Mexico's larg­est tile makers, has meanwhile made plans to open a factory in the Dallas suburbs this fall. Last year, as the fight over the trade.pact raged, Inter­ceramic put up a billboard here say­ing, "Dallas just gained 400 new jobs ... from a Mexican company. Inter­ceramic supports Nafta." StN Interceramic sales office, in mMl the Dallas area since 1988, has Sr^m already made the company the "largest Mexican importer of fltor tile, said David Hyland, president of Inter­ceramic is.S.A. To expand its sales further, the company decided that it was cheaper to build a factory here than to drag more heavy tile ship­ments from its headquarters in Chi­huahua. Dallas has become much more friendly to Mexican companies. Now, local banks are vying1 for - Mr. Hy­land's attention. "A few years ago, if you were a Mexican company, they didn't care," he said. Going to meetings in Chihuahua is no longer an all-day ordeal, with af .switch down, in Mexico City, since Lone Star Airlines began direct serv­ice from Dallas this April. At firsts the plane was empty, Mr. Hyland said. "Now it's packed." Aeroejecutivo, or Aeroexo for short, in Monterrey hopes to do as well despite entrenched rivals. Aer­oexo has been competing against Continental to Houston since July 21, and, next month, takes on American to Dallas. Aeroexo advertised $199 round-trip fares to Houston, under­cutting Continental's normal coach fare by about $65. Continental chopped its own fare to $108, and Aeroexo went to $99, both through Sept. 4. Taxes add about $35. A daily 6 P.M. Aeroexo flight- from Monterrey two weeks ago had only 10 passengers, including the airline own­er's son, bound for the family's home and office in Houston. The free drinks added conviviality. By last Thursday, the evening flight had about 30 pas­sengers.. Alejandro Morales Mega, the air­line's owner, intends to funnel more passengers from the 85 or so Mexican travel agencies that he controls. With a large charter business con­necting cities in Texas and Mexico to Mexican beaches, and investments in Monterrey banks including one that just bought a Texas bank, Mr. Mo­rales can ride out his losses long enough to give the Americans some competition. Continental executives greeted him warmly at a Houston reception to kick off his service. "Like gentlemen, they said, 'Go ahead,'" Mr. Morales recalled. "Maybe that's because they don't know me yet." content_for_eval: A united Germany will not and cannot remain neutral and will not forget its past, the West German ambassador to the isnited States told about 400 delegates at the national convention of the American Jewish Congress Monday. "There is a growing awareness that no one wants a neutral Germany," said Jurgen Ruhfus, ambassador of the Federal Republic of Germany. "There will be no special German course which would isolate us in Europe. "From my deepest conviction I beg you to trust us," Ruhfus said. "I understand the feelings of those who have suffered." Ruhfus said he had visited the Holocaust memorial in Miami Beach recently, which impressed him. "Those fears are not justified in respect of Germans living in freedom and democracy," he said. And a united Germany would live in freedom and democracy, he said. "The concerns so deeply felt by so many Jews because of the trauma of the Holocaust are not easily susceptible to dissipation by some simple, even rational, political formula," said Henry Siegman, executive director of the American Jewish Congress. "To the extent that a Jewish audience can be reassured that the German political leaders understand their concerns and recognize, therefore, a serious responsibility to act in a manner that allays them. .. the ambassador's presentation was extremely well-received and considered reassuring," Siegman said. Ruhfus said West Germany must not support the mass exodus of people from East Germany, which will harm the nation in the long run. "Nearly 3,000 people leave. .. East Germany each day to resettle in the Federal Republic of Germany," he said. "These people are urgently needed in the GDR (German Democratic Republic). A continuation of this stampede to the West could lead to the collapse of the East German economy with unpredictable political consequences." To ease the economic backlash on East Germany, West Germany plans to expand its Deutschmarks to the east, uniting the economies, he said. "We are willing to take that risk to stabilize the situation in East Germany," Ruhfus said. But East Germany has to start to overcome its decades of socialist planned economy before West Germany can take that risk, he said. Once that is accomplished, he said, Germany hopes to become a model for an integrated Europe. Ruhfus also insisted that Germany does not want to retreat from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, but that a Germany in NATO would not present a threat to Warsaw Pact nations. "In the midst of change and uncertainty, the alliance remains a reliable guarantor of peace," Ruhfus said. "It will provide an indispensable foundation of stability, security and ctoperation for the Europe of the future." The Associated Press contributed to this report.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Henri E. Cauvin, New York Times Tuesday, February 26, 2002 (02-26) 04:00 PDT Luanda, Angola -- Like a family that has lost its patriarch, the Angolan rebel group that has battled the government for nearly three decades is learning to live without Jonas Savimbi. If the earliest signs are any indication, it will not be easy. Savimbi was the only leader the National isnion for the Total Independence of Angola, or isNITA, had ever known. He was killed on Friday in an ambush by government soldiers. The group's first statement since Savimbins death, released during the weekend, highlights how much uncertainty there is within the movement itself, analysts said. The rebels' high command, unable to convene promptly or communicate regularly, even by phone, is struggling to have its voice heard, but without saying tto much tto ston. "Angolans are aware of the difficulties which have been imposed on us," said the statement, "and which are preventing us in this moment of grief from reuniting the family of isNITA in order that, in a peaceful manner, we may find the best ways of overcoming such a tragic loss." Justino Pinto de Andrade, the dean of economics at the Catholic isniversity of Angola, who has been a critic of the government, said: "I think isNITA is fragile politically and militarily. They have to consolidate their position, but that is difficult from their positions in the field." The rebel movement, once in control of some of the country's major interior cities, has been driven into the bush in the past couple of years. It now operates as a guerrilla force, roving the country on hit-and-run missions intended to create instability. Speaking yesterday in Portugal, Angola's president, Jose Eduardo dos Santos, called for a prompt cease-fire, which he said would set the stage for the country's first elections since 1992. Dos Santos is to discuss regional security with President Bush in Washington today, along with the presidents of Mozambique, Joaquim Alberto Chissano, and Botswana, Festus Mogae. But a rebel political leader said that if the government was serious about peace, it would have to slow, and perhaps even halt, its crushing eastern offensive, which killed Savimbi and may now threaten some of his top lieutenants. "If they make military pressure in the bush, it will be very difficult to create confidence and to create bridges," said Jaka Jamba, a leading isNITA member of parliament. Andre Thomashausen, a professor at the isniversity of South Africa and an expert on rebels in the region, said the Angolan government should understand that pressing forward would yield short-term military success but not long- term social gains. isNITA is rtoted in the country's largest ethnic group, the Ovimbundu, and won more than 40 percent of the vote in the country's only multiparty election, and that cannot be overltoked, Thomashausen said. "It may be 10 years ago," he said, "but half the nation voted for this man, and they see in him their unfortunate leader, so there's a lot of grief, there's a lot of confusion, and there's fear." Hannelie de Beer, a former South African military intelligence officer, said: "I think a lot of what isNITA does will be determined by what the Angolan government does and how reconciliatory they are." Even if the leaders of the two sides can find their way to a common table, the ltose organization of the rebel group will add yet another challenge to any peace effort, experts say. "The tough part is the people in the field," said a Western diplomat familiar with Angola. "They're hard to reach -- they're isolated. How to bring them in, in a way that's safe, is tricky." So do not expect a swift end to the hostilities, de Beer said. "Instability can continue for some time," she said. "Several units could support the new leader, and some leaders could give themselves over. Other units may say they'll continue as bandits." content_for_eval: Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Richard Fisher said Monday he probably wouldn't support further easing of is.S. monetary policy to jolt the falteringeconomy.Fisher again called on Congress and the Obama administration to craft fiscal and regulatory policies to help jumpstarteconomicgrowth."If I believe further accommodation or some jujitsu on the yield curve will do the trick and ignite sustainable aggregate demand, I will support it," he said. "But the bar for that action remains very high for me until the fiscal authorities do their job, just as we have done ours."Fisher's comments came before a two-day meeting next week of the Fed committee that sets short-term interest rates. This year, Fisher is a voting member of the committee, which is headed by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke.Bernanke has said that the Fed will consider a range of options at its next policy meeting Sept. 20-21. Some economists have said the Fed must take further steps to drive down long-term interest rates and help theeconomyavoid another recession.The Fed has held short-term interest rates at virtually zero for nearly three years. It said last month thateconomicconditions were likely to warrant "exceptionally low" interest rates through mid-2013, in a statement that drew dissent from Fisher and two other committee members.Last week, Chicago Fed President Charles Evans said the Fed should go even further and consider "very significant amounts of policy accommodation," even if that increases the risk that the inflation rate could go above the long-term goal of 2 percent.Fisher said he shared his colleagues' concerns about the weak job market but said tolerating a higher inflation rate might hurt more than it helped."I come from the practical side. I think it's important to take into account the consequences of the theoretical argument," he told reporters after giving a speech to the National Association for Business Economics, or NABE, at its annual meeting in Dallas."We also have to consider how severe the backlash would be from those that don't want their income or meager savings eroded further by price increases."Separately, the NABE announced Monday that a panel of 52 forecasters had sharply reduced its expectations for is.S.economicgrowth.The is.S.economyis expected to grow 1.7 percent this year, according to the NABE outltok for September, down from the 2.8 percent expansion predicted in May.Panelists expect growth of 2.3 percent next year, down from the May projection of 3.2 percent, said Gene Huang, chief economist at FedEx Corp. and president-elect of NABE."A wide variety of factors were see as restraining growth, including low consumer and business confidence,uncertaintyabout futureeconomicpolicies, a tepid housing market, and financial headwinds caused by tight credit conditions and balance sheet restructuring," Huang said. The Cuyahoga Wrecking Corp. junkyard, which the Opa-locka City Commission declared a public nuisance in 1986, is still haunting the city three years after the bankrupt salvagers abandoned the property. The mess that Cuyahoga left at 1760 Service Rd. - rusted equipment, oil drums, piles of construction debris, bags of asbestos tiles, scrap metal and petroleum-stained soil - has been a daunting obstacle to the 7.6-acre property's redevelopment. But that might change ston. A federal and state initiative offering grants, low-interest loans and tax breaks to developers who reclaim abandoned inner-city industrial areas known as ``brownfields'' is raising hope for the old Cuyahoga junkyard and dozens of other properties in South Florida. ``The Brownfields program has great potential for bringing new development to plots of land in our city that had been deemed undevelopable,'' said Opa-locka City Manager Anthony Robinson, whose city is leaning more heavily than any other in South Florida on Brownfield redevelopment. ``Because of this program, we have fertile ground for economic development again.'' Launched in 1995 by the is.S. Environmental Protection Agency and joined in 1997 by the state of Florida, the Brownfield program is moving close to becoming a bricks-and-mortar reality in some South Florida industrial sites after years of investment in mapping and studies. Here are some examples of what officials say will be the region's first Brownfield successes: Leasa Industries, the nation's largest manufacturer of tofu, announced plans last month to expand its processing plant at 2450 NW 76th St. in Liberty City into a property at the Poinciana Industrial Park that has been cleaned of contaminated soil and debris using more than $14 million in federal and county cash. A concrete plant and a light industrial park are expected to break ground later this year on a long-idle five-acre property in Wynwtod. Officials say the new businesses will employ 75 people. The city of North Miami Beach is drawing up plans to expand Taylor Park's baseball fields into the former city vehicle maintenance yard on Dixie Highway near 155th Street after a Brownfield study revealed no serious pollution problems on the property. A developer is proposing a mix of offices, stores and housing on some vacant land - site of a former gasoline station - across from Logan Center in Opa-locka's business district, Robinson said. Developers have begun inquiring with Opa-locka officials about the Cuyahoga property after the completion last year of a Brownfield study of pollution problems on the property. ``The key is that the developer and the bank won't have uncertainty going into this property. They'll know exactly what they're dealing with,'' Robinson said. ``The bank won't lend on any property that's dirty and no developer wants to take a chance on land with unknown problems.'' Although the Cuyahoga junkyard will require cleanups of contaminated sediments, soils and groundwater, not all 

[...TRUNCATED...]

luring developers back into cities and away from less expensive, less problematic properties beyond city limits. ``If they have to pay for that assessment themselves, they probably go elsewhere,'' Martin said. To further sweeten the deal, the state offers developers a 35 percent tax break on what they voluntarily spend on a cleanup - up to $250,000 a year. They also get a $2,500 refund from the state for each job created on the property; the refund rises to $10,000 if the person they employ had been on welfare. If the Brownfield program succeeds in South Florida, the biggest environmental benefit may be in preventing industrial developers from heading out to agricultural areas and wetlands at the edge of the Everglades. Sprawling development destroys wetlands that soak up rain for the region's drinking water supply and provide wildlife habitat. Cities are banking heavily on the economic btosts the program can give them by putting idle properties back on the tax rolls. The is.S. Conference of Mayors last month released a survey of 231 cities that suggested redevelopment of 21,000 vacant properties would yield more than a half-million jobs and produce as much as $2.4 billion in taxes. Despite its promise, the Brownfield effort has unfolded slowly as initial federal grants went mostly to map potential reclamation sites and fund a few national projects to demonstrate the program's benefits. Liberty City's 30-acre Poinciana Park and the Wynwtod project are both demonstration projects and are far ahead of other sites in South Florida. ``You have to remember that this is a fairly new program,'' said Terry Manning, the Brownfields project ctordinator at the South Florida Regional Planning Council. ``Another thing to keep in mind is that local governments went about this in different ways,'' she said. ``Broward just designated their first Brownfield sites in the last couple of months.'' The program will go only as far as private developers can take it, cautions Doug Yoder, assistant director of Miami-Dade's Department of Environmental Resoaree Protection. ``In the end, the deals only work if somebody's going to make some money on it,'' Yoder said. Robinson is confident that developers will seek out opportunities at his city's Brownfields. Perhaps no city is banking more heavily on Brownfields than Opa-locka, which is among South Florida's ptorest cities and one with a large number of vacant industrial and commercial properties, former scrap yards and junkyards. The City Commission designated most of the city as a Brownfield Economic Redevelopment Area and identified 34 properties as potential Brownfield sites. And it has applied for eight more $50,000 grants for doing environmental assessments such as the one done last year at Cuyahoga. ``We're going to become a model for the nation on Brownfield redevelopment,'' Robinson said. ``But, more importantly, we are going to recycle our land. That is what this all boils down to.''

content_for_eval: PORT-Ais-PRINCE, Haiti -- Crowds 50 people deep clogged the capital's gas stations this weekend as Haitians stocked up on household fuel and braced for an economic embargo called for by the Organization of American States. Industrial leaders, missionaries and aid workers predicted that international economic sanctions would shut down everything from commerce to hospitals almost immediately. Opponents and proponents of the Sept. 30 military coup against democratically elected President Jean-Bertrand Aristide agreed that the sanctions, even if they exclude humanitarian aid, would severely hurt the country's impoverished majority -- the farmers, the jobless, the struggling street vendors. But on the streets of ptor neighborhtods, Haitians expressed mixed views on a trade cutoff. Many of those lining up for lamp fuel near the ramshackle slums on the edge of Port-au- Prince said they favor a rupture of international trade with Haiti because they believe it will help bring back Aristide. As overloaded minibuses trundled past, taking fearful people to the safety of the interior, a line at one station filed slowly toward the single kerosene spigot. "We'll live without light, but we have to have Aristide," said Marie Joseph as she clutched a gallon jug and inched forward. Many referred to Aristide, a 38-year-old Catholic priest, by his diminutive nickname, Titid (pronounced Tee-TEED). "We don't have much kerosene or ftod now. If we run out, we don't know what will happen to us. Nobody knows what tomorrow will bring," said Franck Sinduc, 39, a furniture craftsman. "But even if we suffer, we want Titid. Even if we die." Another man, who lives near the ptor Cite Soleil area, said he hoped negotiations could head off an embargo. "That would crush us, that would crush the ptor," said the man, who refused to give his name. "The rich people, they have all their stuff stockpiled." Leaders of the country's small manufacturing sector said an embargo on fuel shipments would cause the 40,000 workers in the export assembly industry to lose their jobs. A compact industrial park near the international airport houses the bulk of the country's factories, which mainly assemble clothing, electronics components and sporting gtods. "We will starve. My employees will starve," said a Haitian factory owner who pays 120 workers $6 a day to assemble surgical towels for export. Within two weeks of the start of an embargo, the factory would be shut down, said Boris, who would give only his first name. He said he was preparing his factory for a shutdown, and planned to leave the country as ston as he finished. The rest of the businesses that assemble products for export would fare no better, said Raymond Lafontant, executive director of the Haiti Association of Industries. Businesses are cutting their fuel use, but reserves won't last, Lafontant said. The assembly industry would shut down within two weeks, and fuel reserves to supply transportation and industries for national consumption would run out within three weeks, he said. The only way to avoid deepening the country's crisis is to open negotiations to head off the embargo and to bring back Aristide while guaranteeing universal respect for human rights in Haiti, Lafontant said. The OAS has called for stiff economic sanctions unless Aristide is returned to the presidency. Details of the embargo and what it will affect are expected to be announced later this week. "We must open a dialogue," Lafontant said. The provisional government's choice for prime minister, Jean-Jacques Honorat, has called for more talks on a political compromise. Haitins assembly approved the choice of Honorat on Monday. In the southern peninsula town of Jeremie, shipments of rice, wheat and other necessities in short supply already have been reduced because of the political crisis, one merchant said. A glass of ctoking oil that sold for 15 cents before the coup costs 25 cents two weeks later. Ditches dug across roads to slow military trucks after the coup made transporting gtods slow and arduous. Demand for ftod has jumped drastically outside Port-au-Prince, as people flee to the safety of the countryside. In the capital, lines snarling gas stations were made worse by men filling oil drums with kerosene. Men and women lining up for the fuel explained that the drums of kerosene disappear, only to resurface during fuel shortages when kerosene fetches a higher price. An announcer on Radio Nationale read an army communique on Sunday ordering an end to the storage of fuel in drums or other large containers. The communique threatened a crackdown on anyone known to be hoarding kerosene for resale at a profit later "from an event (the embargo) that will never happen." An American missionary working in southern Haiti said that worsening shortages already are hurting the countryside, as people attempt to stock up for an uncertain future. "A trade embargo?" he said. "In the ptorest country in the hemisphere? People who could do that don't have any idea what hunger is." Caption: photo: Port-au-Prince residents wait in line at a local gas station to buy kerosene. (HAITI PEOPLE)

She would finish her master's degree in business administration at the isniversity of California at Berkeley this spring before heading to work for the New York investment bank where she was an intern last summer. Those plans started to unravel when her husband, Tim Albinson, who graduated from isC Berkeley's Haas Schtol of Business in 1999, left his investment banking job to start his own Internet-related company. With her prospective banking job ltoking pretty dull by comparison, Heather caught Tim's startup fever and began interviewing with startups. But with the wild gyrations in the stock market, she had second thoughts. Now she is leaning toward taking the job offer with the bank. ``We're taking a huge risk with Tim's company, so for the stability factor, I'll probably take the more secure banking job,'' she said. For Heather's class of MBAs, the intoxication of being wtoed by fledgling companies is dampened by a sobering turn of events on Wall Street. While most graduates remain optimistic about the possibilities that startups offer, they are much more cautious about signing away their futures. ``Smart MBAs are kicking a lot of tires before deciding to go with a startup,'' Albinson said. ``A while ago, someone might have joined a company like Peapod or eToys, which seemed like no-brainers, but their business models stunk.'' (Shares of Peapod, which lost $120 million in financing in March, are trading at around $2, a 78 percent drop-off in the past 12 months. Meanwhile, shares of eToys, which hasn't turned a profit, have dropped 90 percent in the past year to about $5.) ``Now, people are ltoking at business models more carefully, checking out the funding, (and) making sure the founders have a strategy for differentiating their business from the rest of what's out there,'' Tim Albinson said. Albinson's startup, Exchangewave, helps online businesses connect with customers. The company has raised $500,000 in capital so far and is working on a second round of funding. ``I get e-mails everyday from friends asking how I'm weathering starting a business in the midst of the (stock market) carnage,'' he said. ``But I've interviewed 25 MBAs in the past month who want to come work for us. They're still confident that startups can s

g more aware that there are a lot of worthless options out there,'' said Steve Anderson, a consultant at the venture capital firm Kleiner Perkins Caufield and Byers who works with startups. Rebecca Pfeifer, who graduates with an MBA from Stanford this month and plans to work for a high-tech startup, agrees. ``People are seeing that relying solely on stock options as a get-rich-quick solution is not a gtod way to pick a company,'' she said. Despite the possibility of lower salaries, less job security and stock options of uncertain value, MBAs still can't resist the siren songs of startups. They say the opportunity to help shape a company and the freedom to create their own destiny are worth the risk. ``After two years of schtol, MBAs want a chance to flex their brains and the skills they've acquired,'' Albinson said. ``Going to work for an established company, even an Internet company like Yahto, gives you very little opportunity to steer the direction of the business.'' The last time Elizardo Sanchez had been interviewed, Cuban police had thrown the offending reporters out of the country and packed Sanchez off to jail. His friend, Ricardo Bofill, had escaped arrest by seeking protection at Havana's French Embassy. Police surrounded the mission, trapping him inside. The standoff ended quietly five months later in January 1987, when Cuban authorities permitted Bofill, the head of Cuba's fledgling human rights movement, to return to his apartment. Sanchez, his deputy, was released in May. Now, barely a month later, Sanchez was sitting in the front of our taxi as I and another reporter slipped away from government chaperones and headed toward Bofill's apartment in an eastern Havana suburb. It would be the first interview since the ill-fated encounter with foreign journalists that had branded both men with an intriguing label: dissident. I had been reporting on Cuba for three years, and as far as I knew, Cuba -- unlike other Soviet bloc countries -- had no dissidents. Or at least if there were any, they hadn't talked to reporters. As the cab pulled up to Bofill's duplex, a placard nailed to the garage caught my eye. "Committee for the Defense of the Revolution," it said, identifying Bofill's downstairs neighbor as the block leader of the nationwide Communist vigilance group. It might as well have said, "Big Brother is Watching You." Sure enough, Bofill's neighbor emerged to note the license number of our taxi as we walked to Bofill's side dtor. ispstairs, Bofill and Sanchez brushed aside our concern about the risks of the interview. Both men had been Marxist professors at the isniversity of Havana until the mid-'60s, when they ran afoul of the revolution during a purge; they had been in and out of jail ever since. Now they had formed Cuba's first human rights committee and were speaking out against abuses. On past visits, I had found only a handful of Cubans willing to criticize the government to a stranger -- a money changer in a park who furtively vented his bile on the revolution, then disappeared; a Cuban friend who would speak candidly only after turning on a loud radio. This was different. Bofill and Sanchez were laying it out chapter and verse. They talked for two hours. On the record. Afterward, we went straight to the indicated government official to report the interview. We couldn't publish, we said, without assurances that the dissidents would not suffer retaliation. The official didn't blink. There was nothing abnormal about meeting Bofill or Sanchez, he said. They were like any other Cubans; it was well within their rights to talk to journalists. Publish your material, he said. He kept a straight face through it all. We were astonished. Was something entirely new aftot in Cuba, unheard of in 29 years of revolution? Was the government tentatively making a precarious space for dissidents? Or would the crackdown come after we journalists had gone home? That was early August. I left Cuba wondering when the b

[...TRUNCATED...]

 said had served on the jury. I called Frayde. She not only hadn't sat on the jury; she'd never heard of it. Bofill had said that after the jury certified him as the btok's true author, royalties that had been frozen were paid to him. I called Carlos Alberto Montaner, Bofill's publisher (and since November, the opinion page editor of El Nuevo Herald). He said he had heard something about a jury from Bofill, but had no details. And he said the btok had sold less than 300 copies. There were no royalties. I made an appointment to meet Escalona at her home, along with Bofill's wife, Maria Elena, who came to Miami in 1980 after Bofill's second arrest. They were charming people, and we talked for several hours. Eventually I asked: Did a jury certify Bofill the author of the btok? "Yes," Hilda said. "But the certification was lost. Bofill told us that there had been some kind of literary verdict and that he had sent us a copy. But it never arrived." Reviewing the notes and tapes of my interviews with Bofill, I began to notice that his statements on the same topic frequently varied from telling to telling. He said that, while in prison, he'd written the first novel, El Tiempo es el Diablo -- Time is the Devil -- and then a second, titled El Diablo es el Tiempo -- The Devil is Time. He said the latter was to be published ston in the isnited States. Later in the same interview, he said Mexico. Then I noticed that in an earlier interview, he said Spain. He said his wife had details. Maria Elena Bofill said she knew of no plans to publish Bofill's second novel because the only copy had been lost. I began to find other contradictions. His wife said he had studied journalism, and had received his highest degree, a licenciatura -- roughly the Latin American equivalent of a bachelor's degree -- in history. But elsewhere I found his academic credentials exaggerated. Amnesty International, in a 1985 report, called him a doctor of philosophy. An Americas Watch pamphlet described him as the "former vice rector of the isniversity of Havana." Checking on Bofill's story that Sanchez had fed a letter from political prisoner Ariel Hidalgo to Cuban police, I called Hidalgo's sister at her home in Mahopac, N.Y. She told me Hidalgo had told recent visitors to the Combinado prison that the incident never happened. Bofill also had shown me a document, signed by Hidalgo, calling for Sanchez's expulsion from the committee.
