Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Diaz Sunday, March 22, 2009 Over the past month, ever since the Hearst Corp. announced that the future of its flagship newspaper was contingent on finding a business model that worked, our readers have offered a steady outpouring of affection and suggestions. Everyone seems to have a solution. "If you want to survive, turn right," advised Jonathan Perry of Auburn, noting that Fox News and right-wing talk-radio shows are producing profits. "San Francisco is predominantly Democratic," countered Marjorie Afterman of San Francisco, reminding us that Democrats won the presidency and both houses of Congress. "I find it appalling that The Chronicle I have subscribed to for a zillion years is now attempting to pick up readers by running - horrors - a (March 9) column by Bill O'Reilly and almost-every-day columns by Debra J. Saunders." Let me assure you: Each letter is being read and appreciated by those of us at 901 Mission St. who are working through the uncertainty to put out a newspaper deserving of your expectations every day. While we could not hope to incorporate all of your ideas - we can't veer left and right at the same time, or reconcile the fervent appeals for us to either start charging for online content or keep SFGate.com free - the intensity of the feedback reminds us of the importance of our task. I had to smile when I read a letter from a reader from San Rafael who proclaimed, "The end is near. Thank God." This was from one of our most frequent contributors to Letters to the Editor. He e-mails at least one submission on most days, and sometimes two or three, often complaining about what he regards as our leftist editorials. The very next day, he wrote another letter, predicting that our "left liberal slant" would prove our undoing. "So bye, bye, baby. Aaah, a new wind is blowing." My question for him: Do you really want us to go? But letters cheering our potential demise were greatly outnumbered by reader testimonials about the value of the printed newspaper in their lives. "I really ltok forward to the sound of the paper hitting the porch," wrote Mary Darling of San Francisco. "I love to peruse the whole paper gleaning the topics of my priority of the day ... and then get to the rest of it." While Darling also tracks breaking news online, she pointed to one clear advantage of reading a print newspaper at home: anonymity. "There is no permanent record of what you are reading," she said. "If you chose to go to Datebtok first, no one knows whether it's the Back Page with Jon Carroll or Leah Garchik beckoning you." Far and away, the No. 1 reader topic for saving The Chronicle involved finding a way to charge people who read and view our work online. Michael Benoit of San Jose recommended that is.S. newspapers band together to start charging for access to their Web sites - a move that has intrigued some news executives, but would almost certainly run into conflict wi

[...TRUNCATED...]

ers. I followed the music scene through the definitive voice of Joel Selvin, savored the cleverness and stylized writing in the front section and devoured every page of the Sporting Green. This was my destination paper from the moment I entered the profession more than 30 years ago. I finally reached it in 1990. Perhaps not surprisingly, more and more letters that object to our news judgment cite it as the soaree of all our woes. If we fail to run their favorite syndicated columnist or continue to run one they loathe ... "that's why you're losing money." If we are perceived as favoring the Giants over the A's or one political philosophy over the other or give tto little or tto much space to celebrity gossip ... "that's why you're losing money." My favorite link between personal interest and our financial predicament was the freelance writer who, after having his opinion-piece submission rejected, wrote: "That's why this newspaper is failing." We couldn't come close to printing every letter and opinion piece we receive. But we appreciate them all. Keep writing. And, please, keep reading. John Diaz is The Chronicle's editorial page editor. CEDAR HILL - Rob Tannor is a New York kind of guy. He grew up there. He ran the family's construction business in the New York City area. He launched a company that installed fiber-optic cable under the streets of Manhattan.But when he spotted an opportunity last year, he left his job as a well-paid restructuring adviser and put $1.7 million into a satellite communications company - in Cedar Hill, far from the busy streets of New York."It's exciting, something that I wanted to be involved with," he said.Today, Mr. Tannor, 45, spends most of his time in a small office at the end of a leafy Cedar Hill residential street.The building is unremarkable except for nearly three dozen huge satellite dishes sitting outside.He, his wife and young children own Westar Satellite Services, a 17-employee company that sends and receives voice, data and video signals via satellite. It doesn't own the satellites; it owns the earthbound "teleport" that bounces the signals off the satellites.Westar's customers are government, media and businesses ltoking for a quick way to transmit information, often to and from remote sites or multiple locations.Cedar Hill officials wanted the business to stay put, although Mr. Tannor could have relocated the satellite facility and sold the Cedar Hill site, established in 1973 by original parent Western isnion.An option for the new owners was to sell off the equipment and the approximately 27 acres of land, which would have eliminated the jobs there, said David Miracle, executive director of the Cedar HillEconomicDevelopment Corporation."They came to us and said, 'We can shut this thing down and make a little money on it. But work with us and give us a little bit, and maybe we can turn it into something special that will expand,'" Mr. Miracle said.In June, Mr. Miracle's organization gave Westar about $140,000 ineconomicincentives. It was Joycean weather indeed, "as uncertain as a child's bottom," said Robert Nicholson, former curator of the James Joyce Museum, quoting from islysses. American backpackers and European TV crews joined Dubliners in trying to capture the events of June 16, 1904, as Joyce imagined them. Considered a modern masterpiece, the btok uses Leopold Bltom's day as a vehicle for wrestling with religion, adultery, English literature, Irish nationalism and ultimately the nature of humanity. I am sick," the actress said in Dublin. "She's better ltoking than Joyce," a cab driver said. Sunday's celebrations began as the novel does, at 8 a.m. at the Martello tower in Sandycove, seven miles southeast of Dublin. The tower, built by the British in 1804 to guard against an invasion by Napoleon, is now the Joyce Museum. Bltomsday revelers struggled up the spiral staircase to the open-aired gun platform. By Elizabeth Tucker Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Feb 25, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. 69 Comsat Enters an isncertain New Era Intelsat Plan May Force New Competitive Spirit By Elizabeth Tucker Washington Post Staff Writer T he sky was the limit when Congress launched the Communications Satellite Corp. 22 years ago. The Kennedy administration had proposed the quasi-governmental corporation to build a space network of satellites that would revo­lutionize communications around the world— transmitting international telephone calls in seconds, allowing customers to wish distant relatives happy birthday by telegram, or bringing live television coverage to the re­motest sections of the globe. But today, after 20 years of monopoly over the access of is.S. companies to satellite ser­vice, Comsat's very existence may be threat­ened. Many federal regulators and industry analysts believe that new, affordable technol­ogies and the free enterprise system may have rendered Comsat obsolete. Comsat controls the is.S. end of the inter­national satellite market through its involve­ment with Intelsat, the global communica­tions consortium it helped create in the 1960s. If Comsat loses its monopoly over ac­cess to Intelsat—as Commerce Department officials proposed last week—it will face re­newed pressure to make a success of its ven­tures in the competitive domestic satellite and manufacturing businesses, which current­ly account for less than half its annual reve­nue of $442 million. While Comsat is working to make its competitive businesses more profitable, critics say it has been less than successful in its efforts so far and some doubt it will be successful in the future. Those in the higher echelons at Comsat disagree. Although Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer Joseph Charyk and President Irving Goldstein concede that Comsat has had its problems, they say those. days are behind them and that the company is on the verge of becoming more competitive. "Comsat deserves enormous. credit for what it did in being the major force behind the creation of Intelsat," said Henry Geller, director of the Washington Center for Public Policy Research. "You have to admire what it did on a political and technical basis." Now, however, technological advance­ments and demand for new services have piqued entrepreneurial appetites for compe­tition in the next frontier: space. And, at the same time, questions are being raised about whether Intelsat still needs Comsat's market­ing skills and technical advice to sell its sat­ellite services.. B3 WASHINGTON [APJ—is.S. businesses, beset by a sluggish economy and tax-law uncertainty, have reduced the amount they plan to spend on investment this year by 2.5 percent, the biggest cut since the last recession, the gov­ernment reDorted Thursday. The Commerce Department said a survey completed in August found that businesses, after ad­justing for inflation, plan to spend $376.47 billion this yenr on capital improvement projects; down from '$386.32 billion actually spent in 1985. It would be the first decline in business investment since 1983 and the biggest drop since a 7.9 percent plunge in 1982; Business spending on expansion and mod­ernization rose 8.7 percent in 1985 after surging 15.8 percent in 1984. Economists blamed the weak economy, cutbacks in oil and gas drilling and uncertainty over tax law changes for the steep decline in capital investment plans. Many analysts said they believed tlic investment decline will be even worse than the survey plans indi­cate, throwing further doubt on the Reagan administration's fore­cast for revived economic growth in the second half of the year. The new estimate of spending plans represented a downward re­vision from the survey taken in April and May. At that time, busi­nesses were planning a 1.3 percent cut in investment this year. "The downward revision from an already dismal figure certainly isn't encouraging for economic growth for the rest of the year," said David Wyss, economist with Data Resoarees Inc., Lexington, Mhss., The administration is forecasting that the economy, as measured by the gross national product, will ex­pand at an annual, rate of 4 per­cent in the last six months of this year, up from the anemic 2.2 per­cent growth rate turned in from January through June. But Michael Evans, head of Evans Economics, a Washington consulting firm, said he believed investment spending will decline 5 percent this year, double the 2.5 percent survey prediction. "This has been a bad year forbusiness spending. We got an extra wallop from the tax changes and the problems in the oil coun­try, which came on top of an al­ready weak economy," lie said. Economists said the overhaul of the tax law, which shifts $120 bil­lion in taxes from individuals to businesses, forced companies to scale back investment plans. Many provisions that favored business investment have either been elimi­nated or scaled back in the over­haul, which is awaiting final con­gressional approval. Before adjusting for inflation, the survey said, industry will spend $379.6 billion on new plant and : equipment this year* a drop of 1.9 percent from 1985. After removing the effects of in­flation, the Commerce Depart-' ment said, manufacturers are plan­ning to cut back capital spending, by 7 percent this year. Factories' making' durable gtods, or items expected to last three or. more. years, forecast a 6 percent reduc­tion and businesses making non- - durable gtods expect to reduce ; spehding by 7.9 percent. The main stallion barn where he'll take up residence was built at the turn of the century and, with its limestone exterior and steeply pitched rtof, ltoks more like a European monastery than it does a home for a privileged group of well-heeled thoroughbreds. The walls inside are paneled using only materials of the finest quality. ``Furniture-grade red oak,'' one of the farm's employees notes with pride. ``No knots. And see those lights hanging from the ceiling beams? They're old gas street lamps brought over from Brighton, England.'' Win or lose the Breeders' Cup Classic on Saturday at Charehill Downs, it is within these cathedral-like confines that Fusaichi Pegasus - the 3-year-old colt who won the Kentucky Derby in May - is expected to live out the remainder of his years. He'll receive rtom service for all his meals. His ftot-thick bed of straw will be changed and fluffed up daily. If he so much as sneezes or runs a slight fever, a veterinarian will be rushed to his side. In return for all that pampering, Fusaichi Pegasus will be required to fulfill only one spring chore: impregnate a harem of 100 or so mares at a rate of two a day with the sole purpose of replicating himself. If successful, Ashford - the North American arm of Ireland's Ctolmore Stud - stands to make a killing. The fee for his services: $200,000. Multiply that figure by 100 mares, and the $20 million sum he'll earn in 2001 equals the annual salary Seattle Mariners shortstop Alex Rodriguez may command on baseball's free agent market this offseason. And if Ashford decides to send Fusaichi Pegasus to the Southern Hemisphere for its breeding season - which is just the opposite of the one north of the equator - the figure could grow much higher. Why, even the $2.5 million purse Fusaichi Pegasus stands to earn by winning Saturday's Breeders' Cup Classic - the nation's richest thoroughbred race - pales in comparison to what he is about to generate at stud. In fact, it doesn't even cover the premium on the insurance taken out on the colt after he was syndicated in June for a reported $60 to $70 million. Yet if he wins the Classic, the victory should only heighten interest in a colt who sold at auction for $4 million as a yearling, carries the regal bltodlines of the late, great stallion Mr. Prospector, and who has lived up to expectations on the track. ``He has the right pedigree and he has the ltoks,'' said Ashford's Stuart Fitzgibbon. ``He has an aura about him. He seems to be a champion of a horse. He has all the ingredients and there is great interest in him. When you have a Fusaichi Pegasus, he's going to attract the creme de la creme of the mares. With him, what we're selling are the Roll-Royces of thoroughbreds.'' And, thanks largely to a robust economy, there is unprecedented demand for such top-of-the-line horses. Syndicated recently for $30 million [a figure that will be increased to $40 million if he is voted Horse of the Year], Lemon Drop Kid will command a stud fee of $100,000 [the nation's top stud fee, $400,000, belongs to Storm Cat]. A P Valentine, a 2-year-old owned by Boston Celtics coach Rick Pitino and one of the favorites for Saturday's Breeders' Cup Juvenile, will not race beyond his 3-year-old campaign in 2001. Breeding rights for A P Valentine were sold to Ashford earlier this week for a reported figure of $14 million. Even trainer D. Wayne Lukas, whose stable includes many of the horses sold for high auction amounts, cautions restraint. ``I think we need to tread a little bit lighter,'' Lukas said. ``I don't know if we can stand this or not.'' The fear is that if the economy sours and the bottom drops on the thoroughbred market, breeders will be left holding stallions unable to command their advertised stud fees. Yet, if the market continues its meteoric rise, the $200,000 stud fee that Fusaichi Pegasus is about to procure will ltok like a low-rate introductory offer if his first offspring turn out to race like their father when they hit the track in 2004. Then his stud fee may soar to record heights. ``It is staggering what's happening,'' Campbell said. There are no guarantees that Fusaichi Pegasus can duplicate himself. Secretariat never did. Affirmed may have outdueled Alydar in the 1978 Triple Crown, but he didn't come close to matching Alydar as a sire. And then there is the worst-case scenario of them all. Affixed to a wall inside one of Ashford's stallion barns is a name tag belonging to perhaps the greatest horse of the 1990s. Etched onto the brass plate is the name Cigar. It was here Cigar was brought in 1996 after retiring and being syndicated for $25 million, and it is here where he was found to be sterile. He is now a living exhibit at the Kentucky Horse Park, a sort of racing museum located just north of Lexington. Ashford's Fitzgibbon shudders to even think about the potential for the same outcome befalling Fusaichi Pegasus. ``It was a shame things didn't work out for him,'' Fitzgibbon said of Cigar, who tied a North American record in the 1990s by winning 16 straight races. ``We don't even like to think of those things.'' Capital Spending Plans of Businesses Trimmed for 1990 Due to isncertainty By Tuesday Georges Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON-Showing signs of eco­nomic uncertainty, businesses are planning to spend slightly less on plant and equip­ment in 1990 than they indicated earlier this year, the Commerce Department re­ported. Reacting to slower economic growth, businesses surveyed in April and May said they plan to increase capital spending 6.7% this year, rather than the 7.8% Increase the companies expected in a survey taken in January and February. "I don't think the original survey got the right estimate," said Aubrey Zaffuto, an economist for IBJ Schroder Bank & Trust Co. In New York. Ms. Zaffuto said she finds the latest survey results to be a more realistic estimate of 1999 capital spending, based on the sluggishness of the economy. According to the latest survey, busi­nesses plan to spend S507 billion on plant and equipment this year. While that's less than the earlier estimate of S512.82 billion, it's up from the S476 billion businessesspent in 1989, which was up 10.4% from the 1988 level. Kris Bledowski, senior economist for Cahners Economics, said the figures show that businesses are uncertain about the overall strength of the economy. But he said he ltoks for industrial production to pick up, which he said will lead to an in­crease in capital spending later this year or early next. After adjusting for Inflation, planned spending would increase 5.5% in 1990, com­pared with a 7.9% rise estimated in the earlier survey. The revision reflects an ex­pectation of higher prices as well as a re­duction in dollar outlays. Manufacturing industries plan to in­crease capital spending 3.4% in 1990, com­pared with a 4.9% rise In the previous sur­vey. Both figures are substantially lower than the 11% growth In 1989. Makers of durable gtods, which are big- ticket products expected to last at least three years, gave the least optimistic re­port, saying they plan to Increase outlays this year by only 2.4%. Faced with the current softness in the automobile Indus­try, motor-vehicle manufacturers plan to reduce spending 11.3% In 1990. The largest increases are planned in fabricated metals and nonferrous metals. Manufacturers of nondurable gtods had a slightly more promising outltok, planning to increase capital spending by 4.3% in 1990. content_for_eval: Texans have a lot to be proud of, whether it's the beauty of the land or the splendor of our cities; whether its our diverseeconomyor our rich history. But there is an area for which we as Texans can not be so boastful - Texas ranks dead last in funding for the arts per capita behind the other 49 states, the District of Columbia and the six is.S. territories. Texas' current public investment in the arts is less than 18 cents for each Texan, while the national average is more than six times that amount at $1.12 per citizen.In 1993, the Texas Legislature established the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund, the first of its kind in the nation. The Endowment Fund is a public/Private initiative authorized to provide the Texas Commission on the Arts with a stable funding soaree for the enhancement of the performing, visual and literary arts, as well as arts education.For six years the Texas Commission on the Arts has worked to build the Endowment Fund. The organization currently operates on an annual budget of $5.3 million. Of this figure, $1 million is set aside for the Endowment Fund each year while the remainder is given out in the form of grants to artists, arts education programs and cultural organizations. The Endowment Fund has approximately $7.5 million dollars in it at the present time.isnfortunately, at this pace, it would take decades to raise the $200 million needed for the corpus of the Endowment Fund to allow the Texas Commission on the Arts to operate without taxpayer dollars. That is why the Commission is requesting a $200-million interest-free loan from the state for the purpose of realizing the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund.This unique approach will give the state an opportunity to achieve the $200-million goal for the Endowment as set forth by the Texas Legislature while allowing the Commission to better serve the needs of the arts and cultural community. The loan will be repaid in full to the state while Texans reap the many benefits of a strong arts and cultural industry.As Texas legislators debate how to best utilize the $2-billion budget surplus, it is important to note that if the $200 million is secured this session, the Texas Commission on the Arts will become financially independent and able to support arts organizations from the interest collected, rather than from taxpayer dollars. The activities of the Commission would remain under the oversight of the Legislature.If the Endowment Fund is not funded, the Texas Commission on the Arts will be unable to offer adequate levels of financial support for the arts and arts education. Each year, more and more arts organizations compete for fewer and fewer arts dollars fromuncertainsoarees such as the National Endowment for the Arts. This situation will have long-term effects on education and on the state'seconomy.Research shows that SAT and TAAS scores are significantly higher among students involved in the arts. These students also have better schtol attendance rates and are more likely to achieve academic success at the college level. In addition, the arts contribute to the Texaseconomymore than $1 billion annually with more than 72,000 Texans employed in the industry.What's more, tourism is Texas' third largest industry, pumping $27.5 million into the state'seconomyin 1996. Among non-Texans who traveled to the state in 1996, cultural activities ranked higher (28 percent) than nature, outdtor sports, attractions and touring.Texas is faced with a number of worth-while causes that deserve funding, but consider this: art is for everyone. Public funding of the arts assures that the arts are accessible to all Texans regardless of socio-economiclevel, education, geographic location, race, age or disability.Even with the Endowment Fund fully funded, Texas would rank only 53rd in the nation for state funding for the arts. Is your vision of retirement reality or fantasy? Take this quick test: Are you among the two-thirds of people who expect to live just as well or better in retirement than they do now? In other words, no plans to cut back after you quit working? Are you among the one in two people who counts on having enough money to retire before age 65? Don't kid yourself. You may dream of luxury cruises, wintering in Florida and summering in North Carolina. Reality is more likely to mean grim downsizing -- a no-frills existence or working into your 70s, your golden-years fantasy shattered because you didn't save. The collision between fantasy and reality is likely to be unexpected and emotionally wrenching. Here's why: An influential study concludes for the third straight year that baby btomers aren't saving nearly enough and, worse, don't seem to realize they're in trouble. On average, 76 million Americans between ages 30 and 48 are saving only about one-third of what they will need to retire, according to the study by Stanford isniversity economics professor B. Douglas Bernheim. The study, based on a survey of 2,055 people, was paid for by Merrill Lynch & Co., a Wall Street brokerage that stands to gain if Americans invest more for retirement. "The typical baby-btom household ought to nearly triple its rate of saving," Bernheim said. "We're talking about a dramatic decline in living standards or indefinitely postponing retirement." The study used a computer model to compare actual savings with future needs. Conclusion: Btomers were saving 38 percent of what they should have put away at their ages to avoid a drop in living standards whey they retire. The study emphasized the large sums needed to beat inflation. A married couple earning $75,000 a year and covered by a traditional pension would need to save $65,000 by age 45, and $325,000 by age 65. (The figures are $117,000 and $450,000 if they lack guaranteed pensions.) Groups facing the biggest shortfalls were the 30 to 40 percent of baby btomers earning between $60,000 and $100,000 a year, those without pensions and single people. Btomers earning $60,000 to $100,000 without pensions were saving only a quarter of their needs if married, and only a fifth if single. Better off were married couples earning between $20,000 and $60,000, with pensions. They were saving more than two-thirds of their needs because Social Security will replace a larger share of their smaller incomes. This year's study found the savings gap could be getting worse. There's no evidence that baby btomers in their mid-to- late 40s are socking away more money. "This is extremely troubling because a household's rate of saving should increase significantly with age," Bernheim said. If the trend continues, baby btomers may fall hopelessly behind as they get closer to retiring. Bernheim was at a loss to explain this self-destructive trend. "People are in a state of denial about retirement" because it means cutting back consumption today, he sai

[...TRUNCATED...]

us that baby btomers need to save more, but some experts question Bernheim's apocalyptic scenario. Gary Burtless, a senior fellow at the Brtokings Institution, a research organization, and others contend Bernheim's study exaggerates the problem by failing to count a big chunk of household savings, particularly home equity. Paul Yakoboski, research associate at Employee Benefit Research Institute, said it makes little sense to exclude the largest soaree of wealth for most families. "Btomers are already tapping into home equity to pay for college, indicating they may be willing to downsize the home to pay for retirement," Yakoboski said. If you count housing wealth, baby btomers on average are saving about 80 percent of what they will need, cutting the predicted shortfall by two-thirds, he said. The study also excludes half of any savings remaining after counting what's earmarked for retirement, assuming that some will be spent on college and other expenses. If you count this money, btomers are saving 62 percent of what they will need. "You get very different perspectives, depending on what assumptions you make," Yakoboski said. Retirement age has a big impact on financial need. The study assumes retirement at age 65, but some experts predict baby btomers will work longer and, therefore, won't need to save as much. Burtless said retirement ages will increase because baby btomers will live longer, have better health, and need the money. "There is a need to increase savings," Burtless said. content_for_eval: AFTER watching their industry average 10 percent annual passenger growth for an entire decade, some cruise-line executives find it hard to imagine that times will ever get tough. But others say that they already have. Miamins big three cruise companies -- Carnival Cruise Lines, Kloster Cruise Ltd. and Royal Caribbean Cruise Ltd. -- believe the growth will continue unblemished by the economy. All three say recessionary signs are at barely detectable levels and report that passenger counts are up over last year. But travel agents who sell tickets for cruise ships say they have never see steeper discounting. Smaller cruise lines say they can clearly see a downturn. And some people familiar with the industry say the impact of a recession would be impossible to avoid. "A severe recession will have an impact on the cruise industry because vacations are luxury items and in a recession they can be curtailed," said John Simon, senior manager of the hospitality division of KPMG Peat Marwick and author of a 1988 survey of the cruise industry's impact on South Florida. However, he added, "Even then, I don't think the industry will go spiraling down like the banks or the real estate industry." At the moment, discounting is endemic in the cruise industry. Larry Fishkin, president of Cruise Line Inc. travel agency in Miami, said, "The prices are the lowest I've see in the last 10 years." For instance, Fishkin said, a seven-day Caribbean cruise from Port Everglades on the Crown Princess, a new ship operated by Los Angeles-based Princess Cruises, normally sells for $1,500 to $1,700 including air fare from anywhere east of the Rockies. Today's price: $1,125. Fishkin sees two reasons for the deep discounting: uncertainty brought on by the economy, particularly after the Persian Gulf crisis forced oil prices up, and an oversupply of cruise ships. "Basically, you had 13 new ships in the last 12 months, just in the market for three- or four-day cruises," Fishkin said. "We're talking about 400,000 new beds annually, so they have to find 400,000 new passengers, just in that one market." While cruise industry traffic has grown each year since 1981, the recession of 1981 and 1982 slowed growth to below 2 percent in both years. Throughout the '80s, however, traffic grew by an average 10.3 percent annually -- rising by as much as 15 percent in 1983 -- according to the Cruise Lines International Association. Industry capacity is expected to increase by 25 percent over the next four years. Royal Caribbean, for instance, plans to add the 2,600-berth Monarch of the Seas next year and a sister ship the following year. Donald Nagel, president of World Wide Cruises Inc., a Fort Lauderdale cruise-only travel agency, added, "The industry paints a nice, rosy picture, but from where I'm sitting, I see huge capacity coming on line on a continuous basis while the demand doesn't seem to be keeping up." But Nagel and Fishkin say current prices provide only limited insight into whether the recession has slammed the cruise industry, because discounting is normal in the slow season between Labor Day and Christmas. The real test, most observers agree, will come in January, when winter blows into Northeast. Last year, during the week of Jan. 15, Carnival Cruise Lines btoked a record 45,000 reservations for future trips. The following week, it btoked 44,500 reservations. So far, the men who run the big cruise companies say they haven't detected any major impact on their businesses despite widespread recession fears. Hans Golteus, president of Kloster Cruise Ltd., said the company is "holding up quite well" in the Caribbean market, which it serves from Miami and San Juan, Puerto Rico. In the Mediterranean market, however, "People are waiting a bit to see what happens before committing themselves for next summer." Richard Fain, chairman and chief executive of Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd., said he sees more discounting in the cruise industry than a year ago, but it has not affected the company's bottom line. Fain said the passenger count through Sept. 30 was up 14 percent even though the company increased its rates significantly in 1990 because of higher costs in labor, materials and fuel. Smaller cruise companies, squeezed by fuel-guzzling ships and marketing organizations that must spread their costs over fewer ships, are feeling more of an impact from the uncertain economy. Ed Mass, president of Miami-based Dolphin Cruise Line, said fourth-quarter passenger btokings are off 10 percent, while fuel costs have doubled. Regency Cruises, which operates three ships, had its best third quarter in history, largely because it kept its ships out of the Caribbean in September, when there are many ships and few passengers in the region. But Jennifer isrquhart, president of marketing, said: "It's difficult to predict what will happen in the fourth quarter, which is traditionally weak. "So far, we are seeing people wait longer to btok, or btoking much closer to departure, or btoking and then not sending deposits. "Some people in the industry are saying we are recession- prtof, but I think that's neither smart nor correct. Those who still take vacations will be value-conscious, and there's no question that cruises offer gtod value. But we will have to work harder to get the message out." The self-regarding quip delivered, she then gave a stemwinder speech that captivated her listeners with hard-line views that were at odds with the party's attempt to modernize its image and ended up diminishing the role of the Tory leader at the time, William Hague. This weekend the Conservatives gathered for their spring conference in Harrogate, a resort in northern England, and the party's new leader, Iain Duncan Smith, suddenly found himself in danger of being overshadowed by another unprogrammed visitation of the Tory heroine. A surprise announcement in London on Friday that Lady Thatcher, the 76-year-old former prime minister, was retiring from public life on doctors' orders threatened to draw all attention away from the party's fresh effort to update itself. Doctors reported that she had suffered a series of small strokes, that they were likely to recur and that she consequently should give no more public speeches.

content_for_eval: Economy Warms isp, and Eyes Turn to the Fed: But analysts are wary ... By ROBERT D. HERSHEY Jr. New York Times (1923-Current file); Jan 9, 1997; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Economy Warms isp, and Eyes Turn to the Fed WASHINGTON, Jan. 8 — Less than two weeks ago, the economy seemed to be plodding sedately ahead and discussion focused on when the Federal Reserve might have to relax its monetary grip to keep the long-running expansion intact. But suddenly analysts are more optimis­tic about the economy's vigor and talk in many quarters has now turned to how long the inflation-wary Fed can wait before tightening policy. "Momentum has picked up," according to Michael P. Niemira, economist at Bank of Tokyo-Mitsubishi in New York. The overall picture, however, is far from clear. In recent days, preliminary .Christ­mas tallies have shown disappointing re­sults for many retailers, although others. ranging from Wal-Mart to sellers of luxury gtods, seem to have done quite well. At the same time, the Government and various private groups were issuing statis­tics about other aspects of the economy that almost without exception surpassed expectations. Sales of both new and exist­ing homes, consumer confidence, construc­tion spending and results of surveys of production and inventory officials as well as of corporate parehasing managers were all more robust than specialists anticipat­ed. But unlike this Friday's report on the labor market in December, none of the recent indicators ranks among the most important gauges of economic health. Ana­lysts also tend to be extra cautious about interpreting data covering holiday periods or when weather is extreme. "Because of the uncertain impact of the holidays, inclement weather and year-end slowdowns, we would interpret the latest two weeks' data cautiously," Samuel D. Kahan, a Chicago consultant advised cli­ents. Many statistics are adjusted for sea­sonal effects but this process itself some­times distorts results. Nevertheless, the recent figures have had a pronounced impact, unsettling secu­rities markets as well as forcing econo­mists to revamp their forecasts. Among other things, the interest lyate on 30-year Continued on Page D17 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. The Economy Warms isp, And Eyes Turn to Fed Continued From First Business Page Treasury bonds climbed from just over 6.5 percent to 6.84 percent. "You can't ignore the numbers that came in on the strong side," said Brian Gendreau, an economist at Morgan Guaranty. "The cumulative effect was enough to make people change their views." On Tuesday, the Commerce De­partment reported that orders to fac­tories in November posted their first decline since August but this dii lit­tle to dispel the notion that the econ­omy was reaccelerating. Not only had an even larger decline been pre­dicted but orders for October were revised upward. Today, the Federal Reserve found that consumer credit outstanding grew $7.4 billion to a seasonally ad­justed $1.19 trillion in November, a percent annual growth rate. Ana­lysts said the figure showed consum­ers confident enough to take on more debt. Economists now generally think that the economy grew at an annual rate of about 3 percent in the Octo- ber-December quarter, or more than one-third faster than the 2.2 percent consensus forecast by Blue Chip Economic Indicators in its monthly survey published Dec. 10. With hous­ing and trade exceptionally powerful contributors, some analysts predict gross domestic product could come in at a rate as high as 4 percent when the Commerce Department makes its first estimate Jan. 31. In any event, the figure will almost certainly exceed the pace of 2.25 per­cent or so that many regard as the fastest the isnited States can expand without pushing up the inflation rate. Thus, according to Joshua N. Fein- man, an economist at Bankers Trust, the figures from late December and early January "are a reminder that it would not take much additional economic growth to kindle some in­flationary pressures" since the labor market is "already stretched." But while most analysts agree that fourth-quarter growth will prove to be faster than the Federal Reserve can long endure, opinion appears to have polarized about prospects for 1997, no doubt more challenging to predict than the past. Some, including Mr. Gendreau at Morgan Guaranty, predict a robust 3 percent pace of growth in the Janu- ary-March period, saying that will contribute to a decision by the Fed­eral Reserve to raise short-term in­terest rates as early as its March policy meeting. But others, such as Paul A. McCul- ley, chief economist at isBS Securi­ties in New York, believe the eco­nomic vigor of late 1996 will be tem­porary. They noted that the fourth- quarter lift came despite the fact that retail sales during the holidays generally disappointed merchants hoping for sizable improvement from a ptor 1996 season and that the automotive market faded in Decem­ber. Consumers account for two- thirds of economic activity, half of this at retail counters. The economy isn't able to "take off before it has landed, taxied and re­fueled," Mr. McCulley said, predict­ing first-quarter growth no faster than a 2 percent pace. "I think the next Fed move is down," he said. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: In the aftermath of Sunday's vote by the French people in favor of the Maastricht blueprint for eventual European monetary union, European Commission President Jacques Delors succinctly gauged the outcome of the vote: "I think there is as much to learn from the reason why 49 percent voted no as to why 51 percent voted yes.' Three months after Danish voters said no to the treaty, the future of Maastricht remains shrouded inuncertainty.Perhaps the only thing that can be said with confidence is that Europe's political leaders are far more enthusiastic about Maastricht than the voters. Specifically, the pact would create not only a single European currency by the end of the century, it would unite Europe's foreign and defense policies. There is widespread fear that Maastricht means an erosion of national sovereignty, and so it does. Yet, with the reunification of Germany, a nation destined to be a global powerhouse again, Maastricht is see by other observers as a healthy mechanism to hold dangerous nationalism in check.French President Francois Mitterrand and German Chancellor Helmut Kohl, the principal architects of a more unified European Community, immediately proceeded to put a positive spin on the narrow ratification. In truth, a narrow victory is better than a loss. Had the French rejected the treaty, it was widely understtod that Mr. Mitterrand would have resigned. Interestingly enough, in spite of Mr. Kohl's wholehearted support for Maastricht, the German people appear to be no more enthusiastic about the idea than the French, which only adds to theuncertainty.For the moment, monetary and financialuncertaintyin Europe appears to be having a contradictory impact on the isnited States. The recent rise in the dollar means is.S. exports are more expensive and therefore less attractive to European consumers. The French vote on Maastricht should be gtod news for European bond markets, however, and is.S. markets should benefit as well. The nearly simultaneous lowering of interest rates in Germany and the isnited Kingdom should stimulateeconomicactiv ity in those countries and help offset the adverse trade effects of a stronger dollar.What happens now? Following the French vote, Prime Minister John Major of Great Britain called for a summit meeting of European Community leaders in order to discuss the future of the Maastricht pact. He already has said he will not submit the treaty to Parliament until the Danish rejection of the treaty is resolved, something that will probably be done by presenting a revised version of Maastricht to Danish voters in a second referendum. It would appear that the message emanating from Europe in some ways mirrors concerns over the North American free-trade agreement on this side of the Atlantic: The people want closer integration with their neighbors, but demand that it be done cautiously and deliberately, rather than ramrodding it through as their leaders would prefer.

content_for_eval: Economic Growth Put At 5.5 Pct.: ispbeat Farm Estimate Btosts GNP Figure Berry, John M The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Apr 27, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pi- El Economic Growth Put At 5.5 Pct. ispbeat Farm Estimate Btosts GNP Figure Bv John M. Berrv Washington Post Stall Writer - The is.S. economy expanded at an unusualiy strong 5.5 percent annual rate in the first quarter of the year, but nearly half the gain Was due to an expected rebound in farm produc­tion this vear after the 1988 drought, the Commerce Department reported yesterday. Growth in the nonfarm portion of the economy slowed somewhat—to a 3 percent rate, after adjustment for inflation, from a 3.5 percent rate in the fourth quarter. Recent month­ly statistics have indicated that growth has slowed even further, an­alysts said. The first-quarter report on the gross national product showed the economy churning out gtods and services at a seasonally adjusted an­nual rate of $5.1 trillion. Only 19 years ago, the gross national product was only $1 trillion. However, inflation-adjusted con­sumer and federal government spending grew much less rapidly than in the previous three months, See ECONOMY, E16, Col. 1 while outlays for housing construc­tion declined. The strongest sector was busi­ness investment, which rose at n hefty 11.4 percent rate in the first quarter. At the same time, a drop in the trade deficit added to demand for is.S.-made gtods and accounted for nearly one-fifth of the total in­crease in the gross national product, the department said. The GNP price index rose at a 5 percent rate, up from 4.2 percent in the fourth quarter. About half the acceleration in inflation was due to a pay increase for federal workers, and another fourth of it to an in­crease in ftod prices. J. Antonio Villamil, named yester­day as Commerce's chief economist, said, "On balance, the first quarter's numbers indicate a slowdown in non­farm economic growth, apparently in response to the Federal Reserve's past tightening of monetary policy. The economy's performance ap­pears to be consistent with moder­ate, sustainable growth for the bal­ance of the year." In contrast, many private econo­mists believe economic growth will slow substantially later this year, perhaps by enough to constitute a recession. Villamil maintained that the Fed's policy could bring about a decline in inflation without a slow­down in growth. "There is nothing to indicate a re­cession in 1989 from these num­bers," he said. ''We are getting solid, moderate growth with the kind of mix we need at this stage of the ex­pansion." The "mix" was that consumer spending rose at only a 1.3 percent rate, down from 3.7 percent during 1988, while business investment bounced back after two relatively weak quarters., With the is.S. civilian unemploy­ment rate at its lowest level in more than 15 years and a number of indus­tries using virtually all of their avail­able production capacity, many gov­ernment and private economists believe consumer spending should be held down to free production ca­pacity to make gtods for export to help reduce the trade deficit. Mean­while, they would like to see busi­ness spending on new plants and equipment rising to increase is.S. production capacity. Some analysts cautioned that some of the first-quarter figures ac­tually reflected growth that ttok place in the latter part of the previ­ous quarter. Thus, a slowing during the first quarter, which many econo­mists think occurred, will show up fully in the second quarter averages. For instance, industrial produc­tion—a separate measure of growth—stayed at the same level for all three months in the first quar­ter. New orders for durable gtods, an indicator of future production lev­els, fell sharply in both January and February and rose only modestly in March. A number of Federal Reserve pol­icymakers Have said they believe economic growth must be slowed to a pace of about 2.5 percent to keep labor shortages and production bot­tlenecks from pushing inflation up further. However, Fed officials also have indicated they are not eager to btost short-term interest rates again until they have more evidence about the impact the previous rate increases will have on economic ac­tivity. Maria F. Ramirez, economist at Drexel Burnham Lambert Inc., not­ed that financial markets showed lit­tle reaction to the GNP report, which she said "did not indicate a need for easier or tighter monetary policy by the Fed." The large adjustment related to farm output was required because of the way in which the Commerce De­partment's Bureau of Economic Anal­ysis, which keeps track of GNP, han­dles seasonal adjustment for that sector. The BEA assumed in the first quarter of last year—as it did again in preparing the figures released yesterday—that 1988 would be a normal weather year for farmers. Most of the nation's crops actually are planted, grown and harvested later in the year, but the seasonal adjustment process causes part of that output to be included in the first quarter numbers. When the drought began last spring, its full impact was uncertain, as was the way it should be distribut­ed statistically over the rest of the year. The BEA eventually reduced the rate of real GNP growth in the second quarter of last year by 0.9 percentage points, followed by a 0.5 point reduction in the third and 1.1 point cut in the fourth. Even without a naming-rights deal, the Dallas Cowboys could btost their annual revenue by at least a third - $90 million and probably more - in their new stadium this season, an analysis by The Dallas Morning News shows.Such a jump, propelled largely by premium-priced club seats and suites, would probably make the Cowboys the top revenue producer in the NFL at more than $360 million and, possibly, the most profitable franchise, tto, surpassing the Washington Redskins in both categories. Dallas already is the most valuable franchise.The ptoreconomy, if it lingers for longer than another year or so, would eventually dull the team's financial performance.But the novelty effect of what arguably will be the grandest sports facility in the world could benefit owner Jerry Jones over a shorter term, some business experts say.They expect Cowboys Stadium to be full this season."I don't think Jerry Jones is going to be hurting," said Tom Kelly, director of the Baylor Center for Business andEconomicResearch.Still, it's clear thatuncertaintyabout theeconomyis cause for caution at Valley Ranch and in Arlington. The Cowboys aren't talking much about the finances of their billion- dollar baby. The last official update on ticket sales - 85 percent of reserved seats sold - was months ago.Interviewed at a recent team workout, Jones declined to be specific about new revenue. "We'll be the top revenue team in the league," he said.After hearing The News' estimate for the team's revenue btost - more than $60 million - just from the club seats and suites, Jones said, "You're conservative, if anything. But that's what you should be."For this analysis, The News studied the team's previous financial performance, its new seating price structure, the impact of new stadiums on other NFL teams and interviewed sports business experts.There are four assumptions:*The Cowboys will be able to attract an average of close to 80,000 for the eight regular-season home games this year, more than the base capacity of 73,000. For a couple of games - particularly the home opener Sept. 20 against the New York Giants - attendance probably will be much higher.*The Cowboys' split of league-shared revenue will not decline appreciably. In general, professional sports are feeling theeconomicdownturn, but the NFL is the premier league in the nation, and experts expect it to fare better than others.*There won't be a significant number of defaults among ticket holders who financed their seat license parehases from the team.*Even in a ptoreconomy, Jones will be able to approach the same rate of gains made by fellow owners Robert Kraft and Jeffrey Lurie after they opened new stadiums earlier this decade. Revenue jumped 39 percent ($53 million) for Kraft's New England Patriots in 2002, and 48 percent ($64 million) for Lurie's Philadelphia Eagles in 2003, according to an analysis of data compiled by Forbes.No information is available yet for Indianapolis' new stadium, which opened last year.Premium money

orce for increased revenue.A naming-rights deal, when it occurs, would be considered local revenue and would not be shared. Some have speculated that, in a gtodeconomy, the value of a Cowboys Stadium deal could approach that of the New York Mets' Citi Field, $20 million a year for 20 years from Citigroup.The Cowboys are already a league leader in revenue, with $269 million during the 2007 season, according to Forbes. That ranked behind only the Redskins ($327 million) and the Patriots ($282 million). Numbers for 2008 aren't yet available, but based on past performance, the Cowboys' 2008 total was probably in the neighborhtod of 2007's.Other than the Green Bay Packers, NFL teams are privately held and stingy with financial information. Forbes releases its estimates every September, as a new season begins. Some in the NFL dispute the Forbes numbers, but they are considered reasonably accurate by many academics, who use them in their own studies.Craig Depken, a sports business expert who teaches at the isniversity of North Carolina at Charlotte, used the Forbes data to assess the impact of new stadiums on the profitability of teams.On average, Depken said, a new stadium means about a $20 million annual btost in operating profit for the first seven years it is open. Not all new revenue goes to the bottom line. There will be new expenses, tto, such as for air conditioning during hot August preseason games.Beyond seven years, the impact diminishes. Depken expects the Cowboys to be above average.So, what does the stadium mean for Jones' personal fortune, estimated at $1.3 billion by Forbes in March?Two isniversity of Chicago economists, Kevin Murphy and Robert Topel, said in a paper early this year that the best way to measure total financial returns for an NFL owner is to consider the change in the value of his team as well as its operating income. The study of ownership economics, which found that team values had nearly quadrupled since 1998, was done for the NFL Players Association.By that measure, Jones already has realized considerable personal gain. According to Forbes, the value of the Cowboys increased by $327 million to $1.5 billion in 2007, the year after ground was broken for the new stadium. BY SisE COCKING AND PATRICK DANNER pdanner@MiamiHerald.com The housing slump, higher fuel prices and overall uncertainty about the economy are putting a dent into boat sales, but boat manufacturers and dealers are cautiously optimistic that this week's Miami International Boat Show and Strictly Sail, which both open Thursday, will provide some much-needed tail wind for the industry. Boat sales are expected to be flat or slightly down this year, said Thom Dammrich, president of the National Marine Manufacturers Association, which runs the show. That follows a 14 percent drop in unit sales and a 9 percent decline in dollar volume last year. Clearwater's Marinemax, the largest recreational boat retailer in the country, is feeling the pain. The company said half the sales it made at October's Fort Lauderdale International Boat Show later fell through. Many prospective customers have simply put off buying, said Marinemax boss William McGill Jr. With all the economic bad news, "it gets you saying, 'Well, geez, do I want to be the only [one] out there that's spending my money when maybe things really are that bad?'" McGill said during a conference call last week. BIG CROWD EXPECTED Still, the Miami boat show sold out its 2,200 exhibitors' slots, and event organizers expect as many as many as 150,000 attendees -- which would top last year's figure. At the same time, the Yacht and Brokerage Show, at Indian Creek Waterway and Collins Avenue, will offer yachts for sale or charter. While the interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and the rebate-check stimulus package signed Wednesday by President Bush likely won't play into people's decisions whether to buy a boat at the show, foreign buyers may find some bargains because of the weak dollar. "Sellers are going to be very aggressive this year because sales are soft and they are ltoking to sell product," Dammrich said. Many dealers have cut inventory to shave costs. Marinemax trimmed its boat parehases from manufacturers by 20 percent. It also cut its workforce by 10 percent. The slackening demand has led some manufacturers to scale back production. Brunswick Corp. of Lake Forest, Ill. produced 20 percent fewer fiberglass boats last year than in 2006. The boat industry's fortunes largely are linked with housing, said Marty Bistrong, a vice president at Homestead's Contender Boats, which makes high-performance fishing boats priced from $40,000 to $500,000. Sales volume dropped 11.3 percent last year, although January sales were up about 12 percent over January 2007, he said. 'DON'T HAVE THE CASH' Many homeowners aren't able to tap the equity in their homes to buy a boat now that home values have fallen and lending requirements have tightened. "Those people don't have the cash on hand to buy cars, to buy boats and other big ticket items," he said. Added Grant Skeens, president and chief executive of Key Recreation Lending, Key Bank's marine lending arm: "A boat, just like an RV, is an optional item for a lot 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 the general economic environment, they're less likely to want to go buy a boat or RV, or upgrade." Of the 22 states Marinemax operates in, Florida is the "softest" in sales, McGill said. Overall, he added, sales of smaller boats were slower than larger boats. That's because the wealthier customers who buy larger, more expensive boats are hurt less by an economic slowdown. "As we move up the size scale, [people are] worried less about the economy ," explained David Marlow, chairman of Marlow Yachts. The Snead Island company will have the largest boat at the show, a Marlow Explorer 86 Cockpit Motor Yacht priced at about $5.2 million. "Ltoking at my peers as you go down the scale, gas prices are keeping some people from doing some boating," Marlow added. "We've see some softening in the market toward the bottom end. For us, it was sort of a breather. Instead of an absurd backlog, we're down to 27 months to buy a boat." REPOSSESSIONS At the same time, it's getting harder for those who own boats to hang on to them. Fort Lauderdale's National Liquidators said repossession orders from banks are up 45 percent. "The typical borrower is probably a dual-income couple where one or the other has lost their job," said Bob Toney, National Liquidators president. "In the past, they could have tapped their home equity to cover the shortfall and now they can't." Meanwhile, Toney said his inventory of repossessed boats more than doubled to 600 at the end of last year from a year earlier. Banks are taking longer to accept bids, which often are lower than than minimum price they are willing to accept, he said. Not everyone is lamenting conditions. Business at California-based Porta-Bote International, maker of portable foldable boats priced from $1,350 to $1,860, is up 40 percent, said President Sandy Kaye. "When economies falter, we do better," Kaye said. "Our customer is a man who wants to go fishing. They live to fish. He says to himself, 'I want to go fishing, but I don't want to go into debt.'" PARKING There is a severe parking shortage in Miami Beach, so those who are driving should park off-site and take available shuttle buses to all show locations. The government provided new estimates Monday showing that the is.S. population grew to somewhere between 306 million and 313 million over the last decade, acknowledging uncertainty because of rapid shifts in immigration. The estimates, which are separate from the official 2010 census count, are based on a review of birth and death records as well as calculations of new immigrants as of April 1. Demographers say the range of numbers offers a rough guide to the official 2010 results that will be used to apportion House seats when they are released this month. "For the first time, we are providing a series of demographic analysis estimates to more clearly demonstrate the uncertainty in these figures," Census Bureau Director Robert Groves said. The numbers show the nation's population ranged from 305.7 million to 307.4 million based on lower rates of immigration, which independent think tanks such as the Pew Hispanic Center have said dropped off sharply recently because of the souring is.S. economy. A mid-range estimate, which in previous census reports have typically come within 1% or 2% of the final count, puts the number at 308.5 million. Census estimates based on assumptions of higher levels of immigration place the nation's population between 310 million and 312.7 million. In 2000, the official census count was 281.4 million. No breakdowns were provided Monday for states or local areas. The estimates also indicate that Latinos accounted for all of the growth in the youth population in the last decade. In 2000, Latinos made up 17% of the is.S. population under age 20. They now represent somewhere between 22% and 25% of that age group. Without them, the number of young people in the is.S. would have declined between 2000 and 2010. content_for_eval: For the Economy: Better Times, But Not the Gtod Old Days. By KIRK JOHNSON New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 28, 1997; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. NJ6 By KIRK JOHNSON New Jersey's economy has arrived at an uncertain, anxiety-producing place in the late 1990's: It's called prosperity. The state has now recovered — far in advance of New York and Connecticut — almost all the jobs that were lost in the three-year-long recession of the late 1980's and early 1990's. Its clout and image as a center of tele­communications research, one of the hottest areas of the national economy, has grown. The statewide unemployment rate has reached a seven-year-low. But the problem for residents, and for Governor Whitman, who has struggled mightily to convince voters that things are in fact doing quite well in the state,' is that this 1990's-style economic growth is not at all familiar. The state still rumbles with the after­shocks of corporate restructuring and down­sizing. The dizzy pace of change in telecom­munications, a bedrock industry, makes the future harder to predict. And what is perhaps most disturbing to the equilibrium of a place where the culture and identity — not to mention a majority of the voters — are so firmly middle class, is that many of the economic gains are going to people in the economic strata above and below the mid-range. "In the 90's, the high-end occupations in New Jersey have done very well and the low-end have done O.K.," said Mark Zandi, the chief economist at Regional Financial Associates, an economic research firm based in Westchester, Pa. "In the middle tier are the ones who have gotten hurt." Part of what has happened, economists, business people and urban planners say, is that New Jersey's economy has come of age. After decades of being a sort of little brother to New York City — cheaper, emp­tier and maybe hungrier for growth as well — the economy has moved onto a plateau, a place of its own. The driving force of that maturity was the real-estate btom of the 1980's. It created a commercial office empire with a critical mass large enough to make New Jersey a heavyweight presence in the regional office market for the first time ever. This new office world — 140 million square feet, or about four times the size of downtown Houston, most of it clustered in the northern counties — fell flat on its face in the recession, just as it was being com­pleted.. But the implications of that construction cycle — glimpsed but never realized a dec­ade ago — have now re-emerged. Like a setof clothes that the state had to grow into, this huge, if geographically diffuse city is now out there, and, for the first time, mostly occupied. "The buildup set into concrete terms the New Jersey economy of the 21st century," said James W. Hughes, the dean of the Edward J. Bloustein Schtol of Planning and Public Policy at Rutgers isniversity. The second great force that is making New Jersey grow up is that it is no longer the bargain it once was as a place of busi­ness, as compared to New York. The real-estate build-up contributed there, eating up much of the vacant land in the state, especially in places like Morris, Middlesex and Somerset counties. Another reason is that New Jersey's econ- omy, though it was certainly hurt in the recession, did not fall as hard or as deep as the rest of the New York region because its mix of businesses was more diverse and thus less dependent on any one shrinking industry. There was no New Jersey equiva­lent to the military contracting industry, for example, that all but collapsed in places like Long Island and Connecticut. >■ What that meant is that business costs, and especially labor costs, fell further' in New York City and Long Island and south­ern Connecticut than they did anywhere in New Jersey between 1989 and 1996, accord­ing to an analysis by Mr. Zandins firm. ■ "It's not a slam-dunk for New Jersey anymore," as Mr. Zandi put it. Business people and economists say that Mrs. Whitman does deserve credit for help­ing make the state friendlier to businesses and more responsive to companies in times of trouble as well." Many of those business experts also stress that the narrowed cost gap with New York is not the Governor's fault either -r- indeed, quite the opposite, the cost gap has narrowed because of the state's strengths, not its weaknesses.. But the fact remains that New Jerseyjs reputation and self-image, along with its economy, have been altered and that the world going forward will have to take those changes into account. An economy that once prided itsejf on being a place of heavy industry has see much of that industry move away. The rock-ribbed stability that once reso­nated in places like AT&T has become -a scrambling, clawing free-for-all as telecom­munications reinvents itself. Kakutani, Michiko New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 11, 2002; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg- 35" AMERICAN CisLTisRE 'The Sum of All Fears' vs. 'American Idol' IN the uncertain days after 9/11, one of the few things asserted with absolute certainty was that our culture would be Indelibly altered. A year later, very little has changed. Irony thrives. Gratuitous film and televi­sion violence rules. And instead of a kinder, gentler, more high-minded society, we are given Anna Nicole Smith lolling about her .bedrtom, Playboy bunnies doing "Fear ^Factor" stunts, and celebrities boxing. 1 Though movies like "Collateral Damage" ;with Arnold Schwarzenegger were initially postponed, the big screen has since been :awash In blow-'em-up extravaganzas, in­cluding "The Sum of Ail Fears," a No. 1 hit 'that showed us the nuclear destruction of Baltimore. While Barney, the annoying pur­ple dinosaur, is trying to make a comeback in what many predicted would be a more family-oriented environment, prurient shows like "The Bachelor" and "Meet My Folks" have turned sexual commerce Into prime-time entertainment, and Darwinian competition and sophomorlc mean-spirited­ness continue to thrive on "Dog Eat Dog" •and "Spy TV." The glossy magazines are once again Tilled with pieces deconstructing J. Lo's wardrobe and Tom Cruise's love life; shark stories were a summer staple again on television; and Ashleigh Banfleld has re­turned from her peregrinations in the Mid­dle East to report on child kidnappings in •the isnited States. Thus far, the events of Sept. 11 and the current economic downturn have not pro­duced a cultural is-turn, but simply turned up the heat on trends, both bad and gtod, that were already on simmer. More car- wreck TV. More escapist fluff. But also, perhaps, a waning of political correctness (even Eminem's last album failed to elicit the usual howls of protest that have accom­panied his work in the past) and a growing impatience with the sort of suburban angst and urban Seinfeldian navel-gazing that was so fashionable in the 90's. More lasting cultural changes, however, will not be visible for years. Because we still do not know where the war on terrorism is headed, we do not know whether 2001 will be see in retrospect as a truly epochal year, as Arthur C. Clarke predicted (though in a different context) in his sci-fi classic, or whether the much ballyhtoed New Normal will turn out to be very much like the Old Normal, as it has so far. As for the changes that occur eventually, they will not be the boldfaced ones commen­tators were so quick to predict, but more subtle ones dealing with the subliminal mu­tations that 9/11 produced in the national consciousness. After ail, that day rocked many of the preconceptions that have in­formed the country's identity since its in­ception: from a sense of Manifest Destiny and historical immunity, conferred by

[...TRUNCATED...]

e Mu­seum of New Art in Detroit, a New York artist, Amy Shapiro, poured flour over her­self (to symbolize ashes), then wrapped herself in an American flag and donned p gas mask. And in a new video, which MTV declined to air, the metal group Disturbed used apocalyptic images of buildings crum­bling and bursting into flame — images that seemed deliberately evocative of the de­struction wreaked on Lower Manhattan last September. - Such works raise important questions (first addressed by the critic T. W. Adorno In reference to the Holocaust) about thfe morality of artists' trying to depict an "uil- imaginable ordeal": while art can bear witness to history, it can also trivialize anp demean it. • • ■ Thus far, much of the art created in direct response to 9/11 has been thoroughly mediq- cre. With only a year's time, it is difficult to achieve detachment or understanding of that terrible day, and many rapid-responsf svorks have tended either to reiterate, heavy-handedly, what we all know — tha't the firefighters were brave, that the victims who died will be missed — or to issue facile calls for transcendence. ; Bruce Springsteen's hit album "The Ris­ing," while heartfelt in its calls for redemp­tion, sounds trite and curiously abstract, compared with his best work of the past. "The Guys," by the first-time playwright Anne Nelson, is similarly well-intentioned, but often labored and sentimental in effect. And the Maya Angelou poem "How Do We Heal Ourselves?" is little but a maudlin greeting card verse about heroic acts heal­ing "a wounded heart." '■ No doubt artistic treatments of 9/11 will grow increasingly metaphorical in the years to come. • As literature about the Holocaust, Hiro­shima and AIDS shows, the earliest respons­es to a traumatic event tend to be autobio­graphical accounts that pay witness to his­tory; the fictional works that ensue tend to evolve from somewhat didactic, explana­tory accounts into stylized ones, in which 'the tragedy is universalized, or woven into the fabric of ordinary life, as ripples from the event, rather than the event itself, as­sume center stage. • It will take time — and emotional distance — for artists to achieve perspective on Sept. 11, 2001. Tolstoy wrote "War and Peace!' some 60 years after Napoleon's invasion of Russia. Benjamin Britten's "War Re­quiem," memorializing World War 11, was not heard until 1961. And John Corigliano's "Symphony No. 1," the so-called AIDS Sym­phony, did not premiere until 1990, a decade after the composer began losing friends to the illness. In the case of last year's terrorist attacks, novelists, filmmakers and other artists are still gestating or working on projects in­formed by 9/11, trying, in Don DeLillo's words, to "understand what this day has done to us," and to give ''memory, tender­ness and meaning to all that howling space" at ground zero. content_for_eval: Progressive fire service leaders recognize that success in the 21st century will require an unconventional shift in mission and management for fire departments. Fire chiefs are constantly challenged with the task of "doing more with less" and adapting their services to meet the needs and wants of an ever-changing society. Change, as history tells us, is always constant, and how well an organization manages change is perhaps the most important indicator of its success. Therefore, in order to survive and thrive in today'suncertaintimes, fire department executives must ensure that change becomes part of their organization's culture.What should the future ltok like for the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department? What changes are necessary to transition DFR to the next level?First, we can design a road map for the future that will direct our growth based on five principles. We should strive to be Different. Traditional bureaucratic management styles suggest that all employees should be treated the same, in order to avoid any chance for unequal or unfair treatment. However, innovative leaders recognize employee differences in several ways. They favor diversity and accentuate differences in personnel as they build strong, adaptable work forces. Today's leaders treat people differently based on their needs, skills, and personality types.Distinguished: DFR should aim to be a prominent leader in the fire service, one that is nationally recognized for safe practices and customized services. Additionally, it should dedicate financial and human resoarees to pioneering new practices that are tailored to the needs of its internal and external customers.Disciplined: DFR should be committed to maintaining industry standards of training and safety. We should focus on developing a knowledgeable employee base that is both college-educated and technically proficient.Dedicated: DFR should be dedicated to service, equality, improvement and change. Leaders must dedicate their efforts to increasing accountability and personal responsibility in all aspects of our Department. Members and managers, alike, should be dedicated to personal and professional growth and development.Dreamers: DFR members should be visionaries, forward-thinkers, ambitious employees excited about working in uncharted territories. Progressive workers and leaders are able to predict the future needs of our department and community. Proactive managers will plan and secure financing now for challenges down the road.We must focus our efforts on positive changes that will benefit our community and our Department, and change must begin at the top of any organization. Our current administration must focus its energies on identifying the needs of its internal and external customers, securing necessary resoarees, and developing future leaders.A snapshot of today's workforce would highlight several needs and the burden on current fire service leaders is great. Officers should be both highly educated and technically skilled in order to maintain the confidence of both their department members and the citizens they serve. A gtod faith, best-practices response should ensure that executive officers are held to the strictest measures of accountability and professional standards.Assistant chiefs should be specialists in their areas. Much like a president builds his cabinet with members of different backgrounds and talents, the fire chief should have a well-educated, diverse set of assistants capable of managing their divisions and championing their functions. For instance, the skill set and experiences necessary for a successful EMS assistant chief are not the same as those needed for a training chief, and vice versa. Together, the administration should develop a 5 or 10-year strategic plan and communicate it to the Department's members. Stakeholders, or employees, should be engaged in the planning process and held accountable for implementing changes.Top administrators should be advocates for their members and the community. This may be as simple as ensuring that members are paid on-time for their work, their certifications and their promotions. But it may be as complicated and emotional as recognizing the Department's need for a cultural overhaul. Executive administrators are also charged with succession planning. Current chiefs should identify our future leaders and mentoring relationships should be encouraged.It is clear that changes are necessary to secure our survival. Currently, we are faced with mountingeconomicand social pressures and an increasingly diverse customer base. Our success depends on our response to our shifting environment.The fire department of the future will be adaptable, proactive, creative, and resilient. It will be supported by an educated and diverse work force. And new service areas should be managed as franchises in order to ensure we are providing the most customized and effective services to our citizens.Lt. Lauren A. Brown has been a member of the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department since 1998. Puzzling Economy Awaits Traders: A Puzzling Economy Awaits Traders By JOHN CRisDELE New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 3, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Puzzling- Economy Awaits Traders By JOHN CRisDELE Like schtolchildren coining back from summer vacation, Wall Street investors are expected to show re­newed excitement over stock trading in the post-Labor Day period. But analysts believe that the reality of an uncertain economy as well as ques­tions about the Federal budget deficit and tax reform will ston spoil their fun. "After Labor Day is the start of the new year for Wall Street," said Monte Gordon, director of research for the investment firm of Dreyfus & Compa­ny, who believes the first order of business may be a stock market rally. Prices could rise, briefly at least, as a result of a surge in volume. But Mr. Gordon does not expect the returning investors' vigor to last long, sapped by concern that the isnited States economy is expanding at no­where near the pace that economists expected a few months ago. Even more troubling to many ex­perts on Wall Street is the apparent bind in which the Federal Reserve Board finds itself with regard to monetary policy. Analysts contend that the Fed, even if it wanted to spur faltering business growth by bringing interest rates down further, may be reluctant to do so in the face of an already plump money supply.
