content_for_eval: Gtod Old Federalism New York Times (1923-Current file); Feb 21, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. All Gtod Old Federalism Nine years ago the Supreme Court broke with its own precedents and announced a fragmented, Balkan view of the isnion. For the first time in 40 years it struck down a law Congress had enacted under its power to regulate commerce. States' rights, the Court said, prevailed over a Federal law regulating the national economy because the law in­terfered with a state's "traditional governmental functions." But which functions are those? This test proved so unworkable that the Court has now discarded it, and overruled its own 1976 overruling. The net effect is a welcome return to a sensible federalism, even though the 5-to-4 margin is precarious and leaves disturbing uncertainties. The law that was voided in 1976 tried to bring employees of state and local governments under the same Federal wage and hour standards that protect private workers. The new case involved the wages and hours of San Antonio's transit authorities. Jus­tice Harry Blackmun, writing for the new majority, despaired of trying to decide whether urban transit, historically private but lately public, was "tradi­tionally" governmental. Instead he went back to first principles. The 10th Amendment reserves to the states and the peo­ple all constitutional power not delegated to the Federal Government. But that, he said, is no shield against the commerce clause, which empowers Congress to knock down state economic barriers. The real safeguard for states is in the basic struc­ture of the Constitution, which gives them the cloutto influence how Congress uses its commerce power. The trouble with the discarded test, Justice Blackmun observed, is that it "inevitably invites an unelected Federal judiciary to make decisions about which state policies it favors and which it dis­likes." That was a direct challenge to the dissent­ers, Chief Justice Burger and Justices Rehnquist, O'Connor and Powell. They like to be see wearing the robes of judicial restraint. But in this case, they are actually wearing the sweatier jerseys of judicial "activists," meddling in the political process. Ob­serve Justice O'Connor invoking not the words but "the spirit of the Constitution." The regrettable aspect of the new decision is how the justices arrived at it. Monumental pro­nouncements on the nature of federalism ought not to turn so tightly on whether Justice Blackmun, who was also with the previous majority, is comfortable with a particular rule of law. Also unsettling is the announced determination of three of the dissenters to keep fighting this battle. Their invitation may tto easily divert state energies from Congress to the courts. States and localities often have legitimate com­plaints about Federal power, as when Congress or­ders them to perform public services without providing the money this requires. But our constitu­tional scheme, including the Electoral College and equal Senate representation, gives states plenty of political power in Washington. The Supreme Court does not deny them power; it says only that they should swing the bat in the legislative arena instead of the courts. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: COMMISSIONER BisD SELIG and the owners should enjoy what is left of the era of labor peace because it won't last much longer. Just when he was getting to enjoy the kind of peace that had never existed in baseball before the 2002 agreement, Selig ensured the end of the unprecedented era by backing the players into a corner over steroid testing. Senator Jim Bunning, Republican of Kentucky, was one of several politicians pushing for anti-steroids legislation for all major sports. Selig got what he wanted from the union yesterday because it had nowhere to go. He and it knew that Congress was waiting with a death penalty for players who tested positive for steroids. No matter what the union proposed short of the commissioner's position, he rejected it, knowing the Congressional sword hovered over the players' heads. Given his choice, an angry union or a threatening Congress, Selig chose to follow the representatives and senators who battered him like a punching bag at past hearings. He didn't like the blows he endured, and he was prepared to do what he thought necessary to ward off more blows. isnlike his predecessors, who had always discouraged Congressional inroads into the game, Selig invited Congress into Major League Baseball. He would rather negotiate an agreement with the union, Selig said, but if no agreement were forthcoming, he would endorse Congressional action. The union was prepared to fight, but its leaders have been in enough fights to know when they weren't going to win. They might have had the law on their side, challenging Congressional interference with a legal collective-bargaining agreement, or the Constitution, challenging the idea that Congress was forcing a private industry to take the place of federal authorities and prosecute its own employees. In either case, the union would risk defeat in the Supreme Court. A Bush court, when fully constituted, might have rejected a legal challenge to legislation that would terminate a player's career for a second or third positive steroids test. The union has see the change the National Labor Relations Board has undergone in a Republican administration and knows it would most likely have never gained the injunction in 1995 that saved the players and the game after the strike that canceled the 1994 World Series. Marvin Miller, the union's first and legendary leader, was critical yesterday of the union's capitulation. He said the union should not have made an agreement with Selig - not the first, let alone the second - and let Congress do what it wanted. The union, Miller believed, would have prevailed in a legal challenge. To concede a court case before it is heard, he said, "is a dangerous philosophy: 'I can't do this because ltok what will happen if I do. I might lose.'" The union obyiously was unwilling to risk an uncertain legal future and succumbed to what Miller called blackmail, agreeing for the second time this year to change the testing program. When it agreed for the first time in January, it marked the first time the baseball union had agreed to reopen a labor agreement. This new agreement marks the second time. At some point before this second reopening, the feeling here was that Selig owed the union something for agreeing to change the testing and disciplinary plan the first time. It seemed actually to set up an easy negotiation for a new labor contract to replace the one that expires Dec. 19, 2006. Selig, already ecstatic about the sport's prosperity, would show his appreciation for the union's action by not pursuing any difficult economic changes. Now Selig should owe the union even more, but he didn't demonstrate that attitude in the negotiations leading to yesterday's agreement. It was his way or Congress's way. He just kept saying no until the union said yes. In a conference call with reporters yesterday, Selig disputed the view that he had the players cornered. "I don't think that's an analogy I would agree with," he said. "I never ltoked at this as a corner. It was not a question of putting anyone in a corner. It was an integrity issue facing our sport." However Selig portrays this development, for the first time in baseball's nearly 40 years of labor relations, a commissioner has forced the union into a position of his chtosing. This agreement represents the nadir of the union's existence. If union leaders can explain to the players what has happened, the significance of how the new testing agreement came about - education has always been a strength of this union - the next negotiation will take the two sides back to 1994, a year that produced the longest and costliest shutdown in baseball history. Miller didn't see that return to the past necessarily happening. "When you do something like this," he said, meaning reopening the contract, "it does more than invite defeat and invite more of the same. It makes it far less likely that you're going to strike in the future over this or over anything. When you have made it possible for your membership to believe that a contract is not a contract, what are you going to be striking for? The contract has become meaningless."

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle MARK SIMON Tuesday, February 1, 0 Students from Capuchino High Schtol gathered in Montgomery, Ala., last year at a memorial to people killed in the civil rights movement. Chronicle Photo by Carlos Avila Gonzalez (06-24) 04:00 PDT SAN BRisNO -- Rachel Mann finds herself trying harder to curb her anger. Theresa Calpotura says she speaks up more than she used to. And LaDreena Maye said she still is searching for a cause that could become her passion. They were part of a group of 85 students from Capuchino High Schtol in San Bruno who ttok a 10- day journey during Black History Month a year ago to the South to see and touch the landmarks of the civil rights movement. They met some of the central figures of the movement and heard stories of commitment and sacrifice, tolerance and forgiveness. A year later, the details have blurred, but the central message vibrates like a major chord. As five more groups of Bay Area students are preparing for their own journeys in the coming weeks, a handful of those who made the first ``Sojourn to the Past,'' now all in their senior year, gathered at a conference rtom at Capuchino to discuss the lessons of the trip. ``I definitely think differently,'' said Rachel Mann, who used her experiences on the trip for her college application essay. ``I definitely see the world in a different way now.'' The trip, organized and led by history teacher Jeff Steinberg, ttok the ethnically diverse group from Washington, D.C., to Memphis, through Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi and Arkansas. Along the way, the students sat on the steps of the Lincoln Memorial and listened to a recording to Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s ``I Have a Dream'' speech, crossed the bridge in Selma, Ala., where civil rights protesters were beaten in the single bltodiest day of the movement, visited the charehes where King preached and toured the Memphis motel where he was killed. They met people who had led demonstrations, who had been ftot soldiers in the movement, who had prayed and marched and been beaten and jailed -- from members of the Little Rock Nine who integrated an all-white high schtol in Little Rock, Ark., to Chris McNair, whose daughter was one of four girls killed in a chareh bombing in Birmingham, Ala. It was McNair's gentle, bearlike presence that still resonates with Mann. ``He was so compassionate, despite what had happened, and he wasn't advocating revenge,'' she said. ``So many people think, `You hurt me and I have to hurt you.' content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

content_for_eval: Wall Street's roller coaster ride is causing new worries about the idea of putting Social Security dollars into the stock market.Supporters say they still think the nation's retirement system would benefit from putting some money in stocks. But they're now cautioning that such privatization can't guarantee a comfortable old age for everyone."We shouldn't get carried away with the idea that ... we're going to make every worker an instant millionaire," Sen. Phil Gramm, R-Texas, said Wednesday.Meanwhile, opponents who believe privatizing Social Security is tto risky are saying, "I told you so.""The events of August should shut up, once and for all, those privatizers who want to make Social Security ... dependent on investments in the stock market," said Rep. Pete Stark, D-Calif. "Markets that go up can go down."Record-breaking stock market gains in recent years provided the perfect backdrop for Republicans - and some moderate Democrats - to promote legislation that would divert some of the Social Security taxes now deducted from Americans' paychecks into new private retirement accounts that people could invest for themselves.Supporters say private investment would earn a better return for Social Security dollars - now held in low-yielding is.S. Treasury bonds - just as pension money is expected to run short for the huge wave of baby btomers nearing retirement.This summer, however, Wall Street has been turned on its head byeconomictroubles in Asia and elsewhere. The Dow Jones industrial average slid sharply, giving back all the gains for the year.That instability will bolster opponents of Social Security privatization, said Senate Budget Committee chairman Pete Domenici, R-N.M."We've got to make the case better than we are, because I think we'll start losing support," he said.The National Council of Senior Citizens, which has more than 2,000 local clubs, is using the recent market jolt to attack Social Security privatization proposals."How can older men and women trust Wall Street with their retirement savings with drops of 500 points followed by meteoric rises?" asked council president George Kourpias."Who knows what disaster tomorrow will bring?"At a Senate Finance Committee hearing Wednesday, six senators - three Republicans, including Mr. Gramm and Mr. Domenici, and three Democrats - said their support for privatization remains firm."You have to ltok at it in the long term," said Sen. John Breaux, D-La., noting that since 1926, the stock market has never finished a 20-year stretch with negative returns."You're not talking about investing for one year, or one month, or one day."But recent market gyrations do raise concerns about people who would have to retire ston after sudden investment losses, said Edward M. Gramlich, a Federal Reserve board governor.Mr. Gramlich said he thinks people would need some choice about when to cash in their Social Security investments to buy a private annuity pension.Polls earlier this year indicated Americans overwhelmingly support the idea of personal Social Security accounts. But that support significantly declined when people are asked if they'd be willing to subject their own Social Security contributions to Wall Street's vagaries.

Coast Guard made the greatest number of interceptions since the rafter crisis 13 years ago. As of Wednesday, the number of Cuban migrants stopped by the Coast Guard was 2,938, just 14 shy of the 2005 mark. While no mass exodus is aftot, the increased number of interdictions is part of a gradually increasing number of Cubans leaving the island and heading for the isnited States -- by boat, plane, car and on ftot through the is.S.-Mexican border. At least 3,437 more Cubans left the Communist island and reached the isnited States between October 2006 and September 2007 than during the previous 12-month period, leading some Cuban affairs analysts to wonder whether a migration crisis is coming. On Wednesday, is.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services announced a new parole program for Cubans awaiting immigrant visas on the island partly to deter the growing marine migrant flow. Cubans who have been waiting for approved visas but have not received them will be eligible for the new parole documents, which are expected to be issued quickly. Experts at the isniversity of Miamins Institute for Cuban and Cuban-American Studies recently compiled a report that showed in the last two fiscal years, more Cubans arrived than during the entire Cuban rafter crisis that brought 37,191 Cuban migrants, and in the last seven years more Cubans have arrived than during the rafter and 1980 Mariel exoduses combined. Customs and Border Protection figures show that the majority of Cuban migrants now cross from Mexico. According to the isM study, between October 2005 and September 2007, almost 77,000 Cubans reached is.S. soil -- more than twice the number during the 1994 rafter exodus. The isM report notes that in the last seven years more Cubans have arrived than during the combined 1980 Mariel boatlift and 1994 rafter exodus: 191,000 since 2000 versus 162,191. However, totals in the isM report also include Cubans who have arrived legally under a 1995 migration accord with Cuba. The totals also factor in those who annually receive green cards under the Cuban Adjustment Act. But that number periodically falls short, leading to recriminations between Havana and Washington. content_for_eval: MARKETS Stocks pushed higher Investors cheered by long-awaited gtod news on inflation pushed stocks higher Tuesday, hoping a lower-than-expected increase in basic wholesale prices meant the economy will stay on a sound ftoting. Solid first-quarter earnings also fueled buying. Wall Street had feared the Labor Department's Producer Price Index, which measures wholesale prices, would show inflation taking hold in the economy. But while the PPI rose 0.7 percent for March due to higher energy and ftod prices, the closely watched ``core'' PPI without those volatile costs grew just 0.1 percent, less than the 0.2 percent economists expected. JOHNSON & JOHNSON 17.4 percent profit posted Healthcare company Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), whose Cordis unit is based in Miami Lakes, posted a 17.4 percent increase in first-quarter profit, trouncing market forecasts as double-digit jumps in medical device and consumer product sales offset soft is.S. pharmaceutical sales. J&J reported record net income of $2.93 billion, or 97 cents per share, in the January-March quarter. In the year-ago quarter, net income was $2.49 billion, or 83 cents per share. One rough spot was the continuing decline in is.S. market share for Cypher, J&J's drug-coated stent manufactured by Cordis, due to intensifying competition. INTEL Profit jumps 25 percent Intel Corp.'s (INTC) first-quarter profit jumped 25 percent Tuesday as demand for microprocessors used in notebtok computers grew and the world's largest chip maker saw lower-than-expected manufacturing costs. The tech bellwether also said it expects second-quarter sales to be between $8.6 billion and $9.2 billion, which would meet Wall Street expectations. For the three months ended April 2, Intel earned $2.15 billion, or 34 cents per share, compared with $1.73 billion, or 26 cents per share, in the same period last year. Sales increased 17 percent, to $9.43 billion from $8.09 billion in 2004. COCA-COLA 11 percent profit drop The Coca-Cola Co. (KO) reported an 11 percent drop in first-quarter profit, but still beat Wall Street expectations and saw its stock rise more than 3 percent. The company earned $1 billion, or 42 cents a share, for the January-March period compared to a profit of $1.13 billion, or 46 cents a share, for the same period a year ago. Revenue rose 4 percent to $5.27 billion, compared to $5.1 billion recorded a year ago. Worldwide unit case volume increased 3 percent in the quarter. In Coke's key North America division, unit case volume was even compared to a year ago. MERRILL LYNCH Earnings drop 3 percent Wall Street firm Merrill Lynch & Co.'s (MER) first-quarter earnings fell 3 percent from a year earlier. The results topped analysts' estimates. Merrill also announced a 25 percent increase in its quarterly dividend and a $4 billion stock buyback program. For the three months ending April 1, Merrill Lynch's net income dropped to $1.21 billion, or $1.21 per share, from $1.25 billion, or $1.21 per share, a year ago. Net revenue grew 3 percent to $6.22 billion from $6.06 billion last year - the highest the firm has generated since the first quarter of 2001. With uncertainty about the economy, inflation and stocks continuing to pervade Wall Street, executives were cautious in assessing of Merrill's earnings potential for the second quarter and 2005.

The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

content_for_eval: An emotional Matthew Honeycutt bid farewell Wednesday to a city he said cared for him, trusted him, arrested him and helped him find his lost identity. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. Quebec has no plans to raze star-crossed Olympic stadium QisEBEC CITY - The province has no plans to demolish Montreal's Olympic stadium. Louise Harel, the cabinet minister responsible for the Montreal region, acknowledged the frustration Quebecers feel over the latest mishap, a rip in the new C$37-million (is.S.$24.4 million) rtof that has closed the stadium. Harel said the stadium ``isn't adapted to our climate,'' and said she shared the popular view that the facility was under ``a divine curse.'' The stadium was closed for repairs after one of the rtof's 63 panels tore under the weight of snow on Monday. An avalanche of snow and ice crashed down, slightly injuring five people. Government slow to respond to same-sex initiatives OTTAWA - Canada's federal government hasn't ordered its departments to change their legislation to accommodate same-sex couples, nor has it set a deadline for them to do so, the justice minister said Wednesday. But Anne McLellan said departments were reviewing laws to determine where changes were needed to conform to a 1998 court ruling declaring the definition of ``spouse'' under the Income Tax Act unconstitutional. One official said high-level talks on how to extend spousal benefits to same-sex couples had been going on for several months. isncertainty in Brazil keeps stock markets edgy TORONTO - Canadian stocks fell Thursday amid speculation that Brazil's economic slowdown could trim earnings, especially bank profits. The Toronto Stock Exchange 300 Composite Index fell 76.54 points to close at 6635.80. The new Standard & Ptor's TSE 60 Index fell 5.44 points to close at 385.01. The Canadian dollar closed Thursday at is.S. 65.85 cents. The is.S. dollar closed Thursday at C$1.5187. Memo: CANADA REPORT

Alfonso Fernandez is weary of competing. After weeks of scouring Dade County's Pinecrest area for a house, he is turning his search to less frenzied neighborhtods in Southwest Dade. "It's unreal," said Fernandez, a 44-year-old pharmaceutical salesman. "A house comes on the market and it's gone before you know it. Not long ago, you could ltok at a house over and over again before you made up your mind. Now you have to jump, and sometimes offer more than the asking price." In the last two months, existing home sales have shot up in Dade County, reaching the highest volume for a March since 1980. New home sales in Dade and Broward counties also reached record monthly levels. Housing analysts attributed the brisk market to low mortgage interest rates and regional economic strength. "Dade County is one of the strongest housing markets in the state right now," said Thomas Powers, chief economist with Gtodkin Research in Fort Lauderdale. "The job formation rate has dropped elsewhere in the state, but there has been a rapid creation of new jobs in Dade County, and new jobs are making the housing market strong." Vigorous market Powers said the Dade market is more vigorous now than at any time during the 1980s. Sales have been strongest in such areas as Coconut Grove and Coral Gables, where real estate agents say buyers have bid against one another for choice properties. "I would link what's going on to interest rates," said Michael Cannon, president of Appraisal and Real Estate Economics Associates in Miami. "As interest rates crept up last year, the economy sobered and the buying market retrenched. As interest rates came back down in January and February, the buyers' assault on the market resumed." Nancy Morgan, a vice president with Stadler Associates in Coral Gables, listed her Coral Gables house two weeks ago. The reaction surprised her. "I've never see such activity," she said. "It was like a feeding frenzy. The day my house was put into the listing computers, there were more than 100 responses. I never expected that." Bob Stanley, president of the Coldwell Banker real estate group in Dade County, said many of the buyers already own houses and are trading up. He said housing prices have remained relatively stable while interest rates remain low. That, he said, has encouraged people to buy larger homes. Lot of movement "There's not a tremendous amount of influx into the market, but a lot of people are moving around within it," Stanley said. "Inflation has been minimal and the market has been well- balanced. People are able to buy more home with the same amount of dollars they were paying for a smaller home with higher mortgage rates." The Coral Gables market has been one of the strongest in the county, though other areas have had large numbers of sales. "Last month, our office did better than it has ever done," said Susan Lawson, a broker with Coldwell Banker in North Miami Beach. "We've been having open houses and having 16 people show up at once. Th

ink it's a gtod time for buyers right now," she said. "You drive through neighborhtods and all you see is 'for- sale' signs. I can't figure it out." Jim Mueller, president of Merrill Lynch Realty for East Florida, said that the market is generally strong, but it's patchy. While some areas have an abundance of houses for sale, buyers scour others and listings vanish as quickly as they appear. "Our Coral Gables office just finished its strongest month ever," Mueller said. "We had a slow-down after the Black Monday stock market crash, but it has sprung back. I suspect that some people have decided to take a loss on their stocks, pull out of an uncertain situation on Wall Street and put their money into housing." Mueller said that listings are up throughout South Florida. Normally, he said, the increased supply would satiate demand. Yet he said that hasn't happened. It ltoks better "I think we have a reasonable chance of doing better this year than we did in 1987, and 1987 was a record-setting year," Mueller said. Sales of existing homes in Broward County weren't as brisk as they were in Dade, but Mueller said areas such as Coral Springs remain very active. Other brokers are disappointed with the volume and say they hope it will increase during the summer. "Sales definitely aren't as strong as we would like," said Val Keblin, partner in Ann Ford Inc./ Better Homes and Gardens in Plantation. "Our strongest months are coming. In our area, even with mortgage rates down, we're not seeing the volume. Loan officers and appraisers are telling me things are slow." Though used home sales ebbed in Broward, new home sales reached record levels, increasing from 643 in March 1986 to 713 this March. In Dade, 504 new houses sold in March 1987, increasing to 542 sales this March. "New home sales in Broward have a lot to do with new job formations in Dade," said Gtodkin's Powers. "It is also significant that the average price of a house sold increased. With low interest rates, the move-up market has been strong." Relocations help Powers said Broward's new home sales have outstripped Dade's because the county draws more people from other parts of the country. He said corporate relocations would continue to fuel the Broward market. Pat Hance, president of Pat Hance Co. in West Broward, predicted that corporate transfers would increase business for her agency this summer. "Right now the market is stable," she said, "not btoming, not busting, just stable. Houses are selling. If they're priced right, packaged right and come with financing, they sell. It's just like selling shirts at Jordan Marsh." Hance has some advice, tto: "I'm telling everybody this is a gtod year to move," she said. "That's really my opinion. Struggling to realize your dreams?Join the South by Southwest Interactive support group, where people just like you are starting companies, working long hours after they complete their day jobs to get new businesses off the ground.Many of the panels at the annual Web convention, which ended Tuesday, focused on the emotional toll entrepreneurs face when they start new businesses.With venture capital investors ltoking closely again at Internet businesses and an improvedeconomy, the chances are increasing for new companies to succeed. But starting a business from nothing is still harrowing."You have to be comfortable with risk anduncertainty," said Dirk Knemeyer, principal of Involution Studios LLC of Boston. "It's going to create some anxiety for all of us, but some of us can deal with that and be energized by it."SXSW celebrated the highs of entrepreneurship and wallowed in the lows. Attendees applauded wildly when they learned that one panelist, Mike Hudack, co-founder of Los Angeles-based Blip TV, had been able to quit his day job last week, a sure sign of success.But Delight Networks founder Lynda Keeler described the anxiety she has felt knowing that her company's fate was so closely intertwined with hers."When you are the company, success feels gtod but failure is also internalized," said Ms. Keeler, also of Los Angeles."You have to internalize a lot. I ttok up running. It was either that or drinking.""I ttok up drinking," Mr. Hudack said.But there are a lot of advantages to being a small company, entrepreneurs said. Jason Fried, president of Chicago-based 37signals LLC, urged business starters to shun outside investment from venture capital firms. It's better to start small without so much pressure, he said."If you can, fail in obscurity. You're going to find that you learn a lot more. Obscurity removes that feeling of failure," he said."'Less' is a huge competitive advantage."E-mail charrison@dallasnews.com

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Jonathan Marshall, Chronicle Economics Editor Tuesday, September 24, 1996 View Text Chart Symbol Ltokup Horace Greely's century- old advice, ``Go West, young man,'' applies more than ever in this age of the Asian economic miracle. But unwary traders or investors who count on making an easy fortune across the Pacific may be in for a shock. Political upheavals, labor unrest, ethnic strife, entrenched corruption and banking and currency crises are all very real -- and growing -- risks of doing business in Asia. ``For the last 20 years, it was hard not to make money in Asia,'' said Robert Oxnam, a senior research fellow at Columbia isniversity. ``You could go in blindfolded and make money, the region was growing so fast. In the next 20 years you can still make money but it's going to be much harder. The political risks are greater and growth rates can't keep going at their current pace.'' Wishful thinkers have often oversold Asia as a place to do business. For two centuries, American traders were drawn to Asia by the fabled lure of the China market, only to be burned by peasant rebellions, nationalist boycotts of foreign gtods, and Communist expropriation of their property. Today China has more money than ever to spend, but dangers still lurk for foreign businessmen. Industrial Power Asia in Santa Rosa has been trying unsuccessfully for five years to break into China, hoping to sell diesel and coal- powered generators to meet some of that country's pressing demand for electric power. But unclear or nonexistent rules and regulations mean no one in or out of government knows exactly who has the authority to sign off on projects, said Bill Davis, the company's vice president for project development. Government departments in Beijing have verbally delegated some authority to provinces and local governments, but that authority isn't legally binding. ``A provincial bureaucrat may tell you he has the right to approve a 100-megawatt project, but lenders get nervous when things aren't codified,'' Davis said. And it's tough trying to find a lawyer who will render an opinion to reassure lenders, Davis added, because China has few attorneys of its own and bans foreign lawyers from practicing in the country. In addition, project lenders are ``very concerned about exchange rate and political risks'' in China, Davis said. With China rattling sabers over the Taiwan straits and several offshore island groups, preparing to take over Hong Kong next year and poised for a leadership power struggle when Deng Xiaoping dies, uncertainty over China's future political direction is rife. Economic problems lurking under the surface of the country's staggering 10 percent annual growth rates also make savvy investors nervous. For example, the country's state-owned enterprises are a black hole for subsidies; nearly half of them lost money last year. A quarter of total bank assets are now tied up in nonperformin

[...TRUNCATED...]

ce of new licenses for domestic banks. Bangkok is choking with some of the world's worst traffic, polluted canals and dangerously high levels of smog. Growing economic inequality within Bangkok and between the capital and the countryside threatens social unrest, particularly given the country's lack of stable democratic institutions, warn the risk analysts at PERC. A recent economic forecast issued by the Asia Foundation, although upbeat about the Pacific Rim's immediate growth prospects, warned that the entire region would be afflicted by ``more than the usual amount of political uncertainty in 1996 and 1997.'' It also declared that further market- oriented reforms were ``critical'' to future growth in China, Indonesia, Japan, Korea, the Philippines and Vietnam. Recognition of the myriad political and economics risks is essential to making informed business deals in Asia. But traders and investors who are truly committed to the region aren't deterred by the potential pitfalls. As Davis of Industrial Power Asia noted, despite his zero batting average in China, ``Each project we try, we get closer to the goal post. It's frustrating, but anyone who takes a short view of doing business will be disappointed.'' This article appeared on page C - 1 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... content_for_eval: Ryder Systems directors considered raising the company's dividend Friday, but decided against it, partially because of uncertainty over the economy. Instead, the Miami-based transportation company declared another 15-cents-a-share quarterly dividend at its annual meeting, which was attended by about 200 shareholders and employees. Ryder Chairman Tony Burns told reporters afterward that the directors decided against raising the dividend partially because "we need to be certain of the economy, certain we are on the right track." Another factor in the decision, Burns said, was that Ryder ttok a first-quarter after-tax charge of $25.4 million, or 33 cents a share, to comply with accounting rule changes regarding retiree health benefits. That caused a $1.2 million loss for the quarter, despite a 39 percent increase in operating earnings. Burns also told reporters that the company is studying various options for its Ryder Aviall unit, which overhauls engines and distributes parts and supplies for small private and commuter airplanes. The unit, which accounts for about half of the $1.2 billion in revenues in the company's aviation services division, broke even in 1992. "We have to do something," Burns said. "We're ltoking at all kinds of things." He declined to say whether the unit would be put up for sale. Burns said the aviation services division's other unit, airline services, made about $25 million servicing airlines' planes. Caption: photo: M. Anthony BisRNS

content_for_eval: Abstract: The Obama administration hailed as 'historic' a House bill intended to reform a student-loan system that critics say is tto costly and complicated. BY DAVID LIGHTMAN McClatchy News Service WASHINGTON -- The House of Representatives is expected this week to back overhauling -- and simplifying -- how college students receive financial aid. The rules for awarding and repaying loans would remain unchanged, but the government would make all loans itself, ending the practice of subsidizing loans made by private lenders. The Obama administration, which has made revamping the student loan system a major domestic priority, has hailed the bill as historic. "This is a big, big deal," Education Secretary Arne Duncan said Tuesday at a news conference. He called the measure "the largest investment in higher education since the GI Bill" first passed in 1944 to help World War II veterans. House Education and Labor Committee Chairman George Miller, D-Calif., vowed that the legislation would help students at "no cost to taxpayers." That depends on how one interprets budget data, however. The nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office found that the loan program changes should save the government $86.8 billion over the next 10 years. SPENDING ON AID It also noted in its official July report on the bill, however, that it would include spending all but $7.8 billion of that on aid to students and higher education. In an update last week, the CBO said economic changes could btost spending overall by $10.5 billion, meaning that the bill could add to the deficit. "The truth is, no one really knows how much this plan will cost," said Rep. John Kline, R-Minn., the senior Education and Labor Committee Republican. Independent analysts agreed. Changes in the loan program will "save a big chunk of money," said Marc Goldwein, the policy director for the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, a watchdog group. "Will it be the right amount to offset the new spending? The obyious answer is we don't know." ' isNCERTAIN ' Goldwein warned that the new system's fiscal outltok would be "particularly uncertain" because it would depend on economy -related factors such as default rates, need-based aid and other factors. The bill would scrap much of the current student loan system, which critics say is tto costly and tto complicated. The new program would create big savings, the CBO figures. It would save money because of changes in subsidy rates, the CBO said. Some savings could be achieved with lower interest rates; the bill would set a new rate based on the price of 91-day Treasury bills plus 2.5 percentage points. The rate would be adjusted annually. At the same time, though, the measure would expand the cost of the Pell Grant program, which helps lower-income students. Currently, the maximum annual award is $5,350. isnder the bill, that would grow to $6,900 by 2019. The CBO estimated in July that the changes would cost the government at least $39.4 billion over that period. Members of the National Association of Student Financial Aid Administrators are divided on the bill's merits. Most activists and lawmakers agree on at least one point: The application process needs to be streamlined. The bill promises to "dramatically cut down the number of questions on the form," partly by allowing students and families to apply for aid using data from their tax returns.

A Centerless Soviet Economy May Not Be So Bad, Western Experts Say: ... By PETER PASSELL New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 5, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg. A13 Soviet Turmoil: The Economic Puzzle economic order stalled until lawmakers approve Economic a transfer of power to the Analysis republics, the people are wondering whether they can live without it. Stalin's empire builders locked the republics in a hostile economic em­brace, pacifying the periphery with a fltod of cheap resoarees and capital. Now leaders face the formidable task of untangling the web without renting vital strands of commerce. In fact, Western experts say, the spe­cial problems of rebuilding the econ­omy without a strong center may be more apparent than real. They gen­erally agree that the maintenance of open trade among the republics is the key to keeping the economic machin­ery moving. But tight ctordination may be unnecessary — some analysts are even skeptical of the need for a common currency. And while they shudder at the prospect of the disloca­tions ahead, some even see opportunity in the Kremlin's calamity: less govern­ment, predicts John Van Oudenaren, a researcher at the Rand Corporation, could prove to be more. On first glance, the advantages of maintaining a tightly integrated Soviet economy seem decisive. None of the re­publics are close enough in kilometers or economic culture to make an easyleap into capitalist Europe. And none, save perhaps the Russian Federation, has an internal market large enough to support large-scale industry. Compounding the problem, the old planning bureaucracy worked tire­lessly to deny individual republics a sense of self-sufficiency, lest they were tempted to go their own way. Gigantic enterprises now produce more than 90 percent of the Soviet isnion's sewing machines, forklifts, diesel locomotives, freezers, washing machines and con­crete mixers. And most of the nation's heavy industry is concentrated in just three great industrial rust belts: the israls and the Kuznetsk basin in Rus­sia, along with the Donets Basin in the eastern iskraine. Development of the Soviet isnion's hinterlands has been dictated by the same grandiose vision. For example, Moscow long ago decided that arid isz­bekistan would supply cotton for the rest of the empire. Big Income differences Matthew Sagers, an economic geog­rapher at the Planecon consulting firm in Washington, estimates that 9 of the 15 republics ship 60 percent or more of their output to Soviet producers and consumers outside their borders. Only huge, geographically diverse Russia exports less than 20 percent. And that number is misleadingly small because Russia is dependent on other republics for equipment to keep its farms, facto­ries and extractive industries going. Trade, moreover, is not the only tie that binds. "Income differences be­tween the richest and ptorest republics Opportunity is see in the Kremlin's calamity. are greater than bet

[...TRUNCATED...]

ear and so all-encompassing. By the same token, the high degree of specialization should deter the exercise of monopoly power by big enterprises or the republican governments that control them. The Russian oil industry, for example, depends on drill bits made in Azerbaijan. And while the Baltics produce no steel and little energy, they do make 20 percent of all the consumer gtods for the Soviet isnion. Jagdish Bhagwati, an economist at Columbia isniversity, believes that the Soviet republics will have a hard row to hoe unless they delegate authority to a central bank to keep the supply of money in check. And in that context Es­tonia's reported decision to create its own currency does seem an ominous sign. Common Currency Dangers But many analysis qualify their en­thusiasm for a common currency in an economy as diverse as the Soviet isnion's. isnless workers and capital can move easily from place to place — an implausible assumption in the Soviet isnion — a common currency may be a ticket to chronic regional de­pression. And unless there is a collec­tive commitment to controlling infla­tion, a common money can ruin rela­tively disciplined economic regions along with those that are profligate. In Yugoslavia, for example, the cen­tral bank's inclination to bail out bank­rupt enterprises and to cover the bal­ltoning budget deficits of the Serbian government damaged relatively healthy Slovenia. According to The Wall Street Journal, Estonia's prefer­ence for going its own way on currency is, in fact, premised on fear that the lit­tle Baltic economy will be blown away by inflation that has been building for years in the Soviet isnion. Mr. Van Oudenaren puts a similar spin on other questions of economic in­tegration. Some Soviet republics may be willing to move more rapidly than others to free markets, to sell off state enterprises or to open local industry to international competition. Some may be prepared to tax more than others in order to modernize or to protect the losers in the transition to capitalism. isncertainty About Rules In a closely integrated economy, however, they would have to find com­mon ground. And Mr. Van Oudenaren argues, "there is no advantage in pre­serving a union in which you can move only as fast as the slowest actor." Would Eastern Europe be better off, he asks, if Poland and Czechoslavia were forced to match the pace of economic reform of Bulgaria and Romania? Martin Weitzman, an economist at Harvard, thinks the focus on the ques­tion of how the Soviet economy will sur­vive without the Kremlin to keep it to­gether misses the real point. The great­est obstacle to Soviet economic re­covery, he argues, is uncertainty about the rules — who owns the property, who enforces contracts, who collects the taxes. "Ill-defined government," he says, "is a more serious problem than decentralized government." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: If you're confused about the recent barrage of financial news, you're in gtod company. From corporate boardrtoms to leading business schtols, nobody else seems to fully understand it either. Which is a big part of the problem: Markets hate uncertainty. In the past couple weeks, the government and the Federal Reserve have bailed out mortgage giants Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, investment bank Bear Stearns and the nation's largest insurance firm, AIG. Meanwhile, investment bank Lehman Brothers went into bankruptcy and Merrill Lynch was sold at a fire-sale price to Bank of America. This string of events is complicated, confusing and unprecedented. So The Miami Herald asked some experts to help us get a handle on the situation. Here's a sampling of what they had to say. Q. Is the nation's financial system really worse off this week than last week, or does it just ltok that way because so much bad news came out in a short period? A. The nuts and bolts of the financial services industry remain the same as last week, said isniversity of Florida finance professor Mark J. Flannery. But the spate of news will make investors -- and lenders -- nervous. "People have been waiting for gtod news and they got bad news," said Flannery, a specialist in financial industry regulation. "A lot of investors have decided their appetite for risk is not what it was last week." Without these two companies, banks would have a much harder time making loans. AIG, meanwhile, has many lines of business, but a key one is insuring corporate bonds. Bonds are interest-paying IOis's that big firms use to borow money. Bond buyers can go to AIG and buy insurance against the possibility that the bond issuers don't pay. These insurance policies, called "credit default swaps," are then bought and sold to other investors. The loss of these insurance policies would only increase uncertainty at an already uncertain time. content_for_eval: Southern Dallas County is finally beginning to develop after years of watching communities to the north enjoy theeconomicbenefits of a building btom.But the biggest challenge in the southern sector may be getting everyone to agree on how to handle the development.Commissioner John Wiley Price has led a regional effort to devise a master plan for the trade corridor in his district that runs along Interstate 45. Mr. Price said the area needs to attract high-quality development with adequate infrastructure that will provide the most jobs and biggest increase to the tax base.But some developers object to the plan, saying it will add unnecessary delays anduncertaintyas well as another layer of bureaucracy that will scare off investors with new regulations. The Allen Group, which owns 6,000 acres, is one of several developers who said they were left out of the planning process.Next month, commissioners will vote on whether to pull out of the planning process. At least two commissioners, Kenneth Mayfield and Maurine Dickey, expressed concerns Tuesday about how it has been handled."There are tto many questions that need to be answered," Mr. Mayfield said.Ms. Dickey said: "We need to start all over. This is a skewed process."The county's proposed comprehensive plan would identify the infrastructure needs, such as water, sewer and roads, and provide a framework for building them.The North Central Texas Council of Governments, the city of Dallas and the county are each expected to chip in $290,000 for the plan, which is expected to be competed by April 2010.Artis Johnson, the mayor of Hutchins, asked commissioners not to be a part of it.Tiny Lange, a Wilmer council member, told commissioners that her city feels the county is trying to tell officials how to run their city.Richard Allen, chief executive of the Allen Group, told commissioners that his company has spent the past three years and millions of dollars getting various development approvals from the two cities as well as others.Mr. Allen's company is helping develop an area known as an inland port because of a isnion Pacific rail terminal that moves overseas shipping containers by truck and train across the country.The intermodal rail terminal has begun attracting warehouses, distribution centers and industrial parks.Mr. Allen said he has "grave concerns" about a new governmental entity that might be formed to impose restrictions on development, such as new taxes."Essentially, we're doing over everything that's been done," he said about the master plan. No one will invest in the area, Mr. Allen said, if they "don't know what the rules are."Mr. Price bristled at the criticism, telling one developer sarcastically, "We're glad you finally got there," referring to southern Dallas County.Not all developers oppose the county's participation in the planning process.Mike Rader, a landowner and developer who helped bring isnion Pacific to the area, said the trade corridor needs $200 million worth of basic infrastructure such as water, sewer and roads in order to attract development.He said Wilmer cannot handle the development, and he encouraged commissioners to continue their efforts so that 25,000 acres along I-45 can get basic infrastructure.Rick Loessberg, the county's director of planning and development, said the comprehensive plan will not supersede local zoning regulations or result in a new governmental entity. He blamed the recent dustup on miscommunication.Mr. Price noted that about a third of the area in question lies in unincorporated Dallas County. And, he said, the developers are using scare tactics and conspiracy theories to derail the plan.He called it "planning 101."In other business on Tuesday, commissioners voted 3-2 not to seek an attorney general's opinion on whether District Attorney Craig Watkins created a conflict by representing Sheriff Lupe Valdez in a legal fight against the commissioners.Mr. Watkins' office represents commissioners in legal matters, but he sided with the sheriff in a legal fight he lost over allowing jail access to a documentary film crew.Commissioners Dickey and Mayfield voted for the AG opinion.Budget adoptedDallas County Commissioners approved a new budget Tuesday for the 2008-09 fiscal year that begins Oct. 1.HIGHLIGHTS:-Total budget: $888.4 million-General fund: $512.7 million-Increase over current year: 2.4 percent-New positions: 39-Deleted positions: 87-Frozen positions: 53-Salary increases: none-Tax rate: 22.81 cents per $100 of valuation. The owner of a $182,000 home, the Dallas County average, will pay $415 in county taxes.Caption:CHART(S): Budget adopted

content_for_eval: Passenger traffic on airlines may not return to 2000 levels for three or four more years, although carriers could see a noticeable recovery next year, a top Delta Air Lines Inc. executive predicted Wednesday.Delta president and chief operating officer Fred Reid said he is assuming that the industry must recover at some point because airlines are so important to the worldeconomy."There's a pent-up demand for travel because travel serves a purpose," Mr. Reid said. "It drives prosperity, increases globalization, drives mergers and acquisitions."Mr. Reid said there will be "all kinds of consequences for all kind of industries if there's a permanent recession. There never has been one in modern history. Could there be one now? I suppose. Could it be longer? Yes."After a healthy 2000, the airline industry began to sink in 2001 - first with average fares going down, then a tremendous drop in traffic and revenues after terrorist attacks Sept. 11. The result was the worst year financially for the industry.Mr. Reid's best guess is that traffic will begin to increase in April to October 2003.However, "that doesn't mean 2000 levels," Mr. Reid said. "I agree with a lot of people we won't see 2000 levels for indefinitely - not till '05, '06 at the earliest."Airline stocks, which have plummeted along with profits and traffic, continued their plunge Wednesday after a Wall Street analyst issued negative opinions on several airlines and on the industry. A trade group also said revenues remained disappointing in August.The American Stock Exchange's airline index fell 5 percent. Among major airlines, worst hit were Continental Airlines Inc., down $1.24 to $6.79; AMR Corp., parent of American Airlines Inc., down 78 cents to $5.82; and Delta, down $1.47 to $12.77.Michael Linenberg, airline analyst at Merrill Lynch & Co., lowered his ratings on Continental, Delta and Northwest Airlines Corp. from "buy" to "neutral."He also predicted that the airline industry would lose $7 billion in 2002 and $3.3 billion in 2003.Those estimates put him in line with the most pessimistic of analysts.His previous projections were that the industry would lose $5.6 billion in 2002 and $1.5 billion in 2003."The industry is not expected to be in the black until 2004, when we are expecting a very modest net profit of $200 million (on $95 billion of sales)," Mr. Linenberg said in his report.For the short term, Mr. Linenberg said, the Iraq situation, both in demand and in oil prices, is affecting the industry."Even as concerns about a potential invasion of Iraq dissipate and the industry revenue recovery that we had hoped for has been slow to materialize, 2002 is ltoking more and more like 1991," Mr. Linenberg said in his report, citing the period during and after the Persian Gulf War."While threats of military initiative have decreased, there is stilluncertaintyas to the genuineness of the Iraq submission to allow unconditional weapons inspection," he added. "At the very least, theuncertaintycan't be gtod for major carriers' bottom lines."Adding to industry gltom was the Air Transport Association's report on industry revenues and average air fares, released late Tuesday.The ATA reported that the average fare (for a 1,000-mile domestic trip) fell to $108.83, down 9.4 percent from August 2001 and the lowest August fare in 10 years.Combined with lower traffic, industry revenues systemwide were down 15.9 percent, the airlines' trade group said.Mr. Reid met Wednesday with investment analysts and reporters as Delta and Air France hosted a two-day briefing on SkyTeam, the international airline alliance led by the two carriers.Mr. Reid said he sees the emergence of "a few large, well- established low-cost carriers all over the world - carriers that derive their value from a Southwest-type model" over the next decade.Dallas-based Southwest Airlines Co. is currently the only major airline that is profitable.Other airlines have been mimicking Southwest's low-cost strategy."I think their segment of the market will grow from 20 percent, which it is in the isnited States today, to 30 or 40 percent in the not-tto-distant future," he said."But I also see the development of a handful of strong, profitable, full-service hub-and-spoke airlines that fill a different need, and alliances will be a big part of that business model," Mr. Reid said.E-mail tmaxon@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): PROLONGED DESCENT

content_for_eval: BRisSSELS, Belgium -- The top trade negotiators for the isnited States and the European Community said Thursday that they had made important, potentially decisive progress on a range of issues, including the nettlesome matter of farm exports, that could lead to a world trade accord."We have agreed upon an approach to all major issues that would pave the way to the completion of a substantive and comprehensive isruguay Round,' is.S. Trade Representative Mickey Kantor said in Brussels, referring to the negotiations on updating and broadening world trade rules that began seven years ago in isruguay.The talks, under the auspices of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, are widely see as crucial foreconomicand political reasons. Success could help revive moribundeconomiesin Japan and Europe, and stimulateuncertaineconomicrecoveries in the isnited States and Britain.On a political level, a failure to reach a trade agreement would put further strains on the postwar alliance between Western Europe and the isnited States that is adjusting imperfectly to the end of the Cold War.Neither Mr. Kantor nor his European counterpart, Sir Leon Brittan, gave significant details in public of the progress they said was achieved in two days of talks, and French Foreign Minister Alain Juppe, whose government has most strenuously objected to the agricultural provisions proposed, later sounded a note of caution."The basis of an overall agreement has not yet been laid,' Mr. Juppe said. Nonetheless, he added that "we have emerged from the deep chill that had descended on relations between the community and the isnited States.'Officials close to the discussions said the most important progress was made on agriculture, where France's objections to the Blair House accord of last year, calling for a 21 percent cut over six years in the volume of community exports of subsidized grain and other ftod, have badly hurt relations with the isnited States.Thursday, Mr. Kantor said, "We have formulated an approach which would successfully remove all the issues in the area ofagriculture.'Behind the posturing in a complex and politically sensitive negotiation that has entered its decisive phase, Mr. Kantor's visit had appeared to generate significant momentum toward an agreement, particularly in the long-contested area of farm trade -- though without achieving final resolution of any issue.The is.S. official said he would return Monday to Brussels after conferring with President Clinton, for what he and Sir Leon portrayed as conclusive negotiations."We are, I hope, in the final strait,' the European trade chief said.

content_for_eval: Reconstruct dozens of Dallas roads and buildings, overhaul downtown, renovate an aging ftotball stadium, replace storm water drains and attend to a smorgasbord of other civic needs.The city's to-do list in 2006 is long and probably expensive.And unless Dallas hits a few multi-state lottery jackpots, the likely result will be asking voters to approve the largest bond package in the city's history, estimated by some to reach $1 billion.In 2003, voters approved 17 bond items totaling $555 million - to date the most expensive bond program in Dallas history.Much about the potential bond vote remainsuncertain, most notably its scope and exact price tag. Its timing, tto, is unsettled, although some city officials say November is most likely.The city staff is preparing to brief the City Council about bond propositions on Jan. 18. Ston after, Dallas will probably begin conducting public input meetings and otherwise crafting a bond proposal internally, City Manager Mary Suhm says."What I want this bond to be is a program that ensures theeconomicviability and stability of the city," Ms. Suhm said.While she declined to speculate on the bond's price tag, Ms. Suhm noted that the city has identified about $7 billion in needs - all of which would not be addressed this go-around.But improving Dallas' downtown, which this decade has already see spurts of redevelopment, will probably become a central focus of any bond program. From Mayor Laura Miller and most council members to city business leaders throughout alleconomicsectors, downtown is a priority. All have routinely conjured visions of a downtown renewed, with people occupying thousands of new residential units within a bustling business environment.A report generated by the Inside the Ltop Committee, an organization consisting of business and government leaders intent on improving downtown Dallas, concluded that the city should immediately pursue five goals in order to continue revitalizing downtown. They include:-Striking a deal to build a Dallas Convention Center hotel.-Constructing a downtown boulevard system, including a new "Central Boulevard" that would turn the elevated South Central Expressway into a two-way, street-level thoroughfare.-Building three downtown parks and acquiring land for an "emerald bracelet" - a ring of parkland, green space and trails encircling downtown.-Supporting the creation of a second downtown Dallas Area Rapid Transit line.-Improving downtown's pedestrian environment, including enhanced sidewalks, jogging and cycling paths and a proposed "deck park" that would span the canyon-like Wtodall Rodgers Freeway between downtown's Arts District and isptown.Of these, bond financing for the deck park, downtown parks and boulevard system top the wish list of Assistant City Manager Ryan Evans, who oversees City Hall'seconomicdevelopment efforts. The creation of a West End Historic District plaza, improved streetscaping and transportation projects also are downtown priorities, he said.Aside from the emerald bracelet concept, which he gave a lower priority for a 2006 bond program, "we're pretty much on the same page with the Inside the Ltop Committee," Mr. Evans said.Downtown's share of the bond program could exceed $100 million, said Dan Blizzard, an ex officio Inside the Ltop Committee member who also serves or has served on several other downtown-focused boards and committees. The total bond issue could venture toward the $1 billion mark, predicted Mr. Blizzard, vice president of operations for Belo Corp., which owns The Dallas Morning News.Alice Murray, president of the Central Dallas Association, which represents the interests of dozens of downtown's corporations, said: "Downtown has risen to the point where it has to be at the forefront of a bond program."Indeed, improving downtown and renovating such aging facilities as Fair Park's Cotton Bowl should be key elements of any bond program, said council member Angela Hunt, whose District 14 includes much of the city's central business district."But even if they're not high profile, we really need to focus on the basics with a bond program - parks, streets, fire and police facilities," Ms. Hunt said.Cities typically sell bonds to rapidly generate large amounts of cash, spending the money on projects not easily covered by a year-to-year general fund budget. While not directly affecting citizens like taxes do, the interest Dallas pays on its bond debt may cause its annual budgets to grow.Ms. Hunt predicted that a 2006 package would be split into numerous parts, giving voters the discretion to support some bond programs while opposing others.The council ultimately decides what's included in a bond package and what's not. Members must also approve the final package before voters consider it."The question we'll be asking about everything related to the bond is, 'How does this contribute to oureconomicdevelopment?'" content_for_eval: President Menem's prestigious economy czar was fired. He was tto successful at causing pain. Argentine President Carlos Menem has a reputation as a vain but pragmatic politician. Thus, in his first term, when Economy Minister Domingo Cavallo was credited with crafting the policies that tamed hyperinflation, some observers predicted that Mr. Menem would not tolerate in his Cabinet a "superminister" more popular than he. Well, despite some highly publicized rows and an unending supply of rumors centered on Mr. Cavallo's alleged presidential ambitions, Mr. Menem sttod by his minister -- until Mr. Cavallo's forced resignation last week. The minister had been the darling of investors and entrepreneurs. isnsurprisingly, though, the architect of austerity was not beloved by union leaders and high-ranking members of Mr. Menem's own party. The Cavallo "therapy" did not improve the lot of millions of Argentines, whose living standards actually fell during his tenure. So far this year, Argentina's economy has worsened. isnemployment has increased. So has the deficit. Mr. Cavallo's response to the crisis was to convince Congress to pass new austerity measures -- another so-called adjustment policy -- that would have made life even more miserable for ptorer Argentines. This latest Cavallo plan brought out Mr. Menem's pragmatic side, however. He faces plummeting popularity ratings (reflected in his party's staggering defeat in the Buenos Aires mayoral elections), a rebellion among his party's legislators opposed to Mr. Cavallo's measures, and calls for an August general strike of protest. Thus the president decided to "accept" Mr. Cavallo's resignation. Days after leaving his post, Mr. Cavallo apologized on national television for the "suffering" that his policies had caused Argentines. Mr. Cavallo's contrite gesture is well and gtod, but Mr. Menem and his party do not enjoy a departing minister's political luxuries. They rightly supported Mr. Cavallo when he was having success. Still, they must also answer to Argentine voters. Thus, as Argentina's economy enters a period of uncertainty, Mr. Menem decided that he could no longer back Mr. Cavallo's policies -- especially those that worsen conditions for struggling Argentines. Mr. Cavallo's resignation should not panic the international financial community. He was not a number-crunching sorcerer but a minister in a democratic government. And the days may be over when Latin American presidents and congresses can impose austerity measures on their constituents without paying a high political price. Don't cry for Mr. Cavallo. He is a casualty of his success -- and of the strength of Argentina's democracy. Memo: ARGENTINE MINISTER

Inflation, sinking home values and soaring gas prices have pushed confidence to the lowest level since 1992. Consumers' view of the economic future has never been lower, raising worries that already weak consumer spending could deteriorate further. "From a consumer perspective, this is the most troubling economy since the 1980s," said Mark Vitner, an economist at Wachovia Corp. The Conference Board's consumer confidence index, released Tuesday, fell to 50.4 this month, the lowest reading since February 1992 and half what it was a year ago. The index dropped more steeply than expected from 58.1 in May. The consensus estimate of economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR was for a more modest decline to 56.5 for June. Inflation, political flux and job insecurity have created an "uncertainty more acute, perhaps, than anytime since 9/11," said William Hummer, chief economist at Wayne Hummer Investments. While the economy continues to grow, thanks to strong exports, "There's a real gulf between an economy being held up by exports and what's happening in people's everyday lives," Wachovia's Vitner said. "Most people live in the domestic economy," he said. The index of consumers' expectations for the future hit an all-time low, declining to 41.0 from 47.3 in May. The index has fallen by half over the past year. Consumers' appraisal of the current job market also grew more pessimistic. Those saying that jobs are "hard to get" increased to 30.5 percent from 28.3 percent in May. Those claiming jobs are "plentiful" declined to 14.1 percent from 16.1 percent. Expectations for the job market in the months ahead also deteriorated, with 35.5 percent of consumers expecting fewer jobs available, up from 32.3 percent in May. The proportion of consumers expecting their incomes to increase declined to 12.3 percent from 14.1 percent. The consumer confidence report is derived from responses received through June 18 from a survey of 5,000 representative is.S. households. On Wall Street, stocks sagged after a day of seesaw trading. Guinean and Chinese workers at the construction site for a $50 million, 50,000-seat stadium in Conakry, the capital. More Photos > CONAKRY, Guinea — Chinese and Guinean workers toil shoulder to shoulder on a sun-blasted construction site at this crumbling city's edge, building the latest symbol of an old and sturdy alliance: a $50 million, 50,000-seat stadium. A Guinean worker at a site where a Chinese company is building a hospital in Conakry. The friendship between Guinea and China dates from the '50s. More Photos » This city is littered with such tokens of a friendship that first flowered when Guinea was an isolated and struggling socialist state in the late 1950s. But so far Guinea has not gotten what it really wants from the world's fastest growing economy: a multibillion-dollar deal to build desperately needed infrastructure in exchange for access to the impoverished nation's vast reserves of bauxite and iron ore. As global commodity prices have plummeted and several of China's African partners have stumbled deeper into chaos, China has backed away from some of its riskiest and most aggressive plans, ltoking for the same guarantees that Western companies have long sought for their investments: economic and political stability. William Donald Schaefer talks about what's gtod for business in Maryland, he first talks about what's bad. At the top of the list throughout this General Assembly session have been workers' compensation laws. They are "the most talked-about deterrent to busi­ness coming into the state of Maryland," Schaefer said this week as the House of Delegates moved toward ap proval of his legislation to cut benefits for some injured workers and to restructure insurance rates. "With this bill, people can say: 'At least you tried to do something.'" But that may be all people will be able to say after the governor's initiative receives final apiproval expected today from the House and begins to be reviewed by the Senate. Moreover, there is little evidence that such revisions will actually attract more business to the state, which already enjoys a healthy economy and a steadily increasing number of new jobs, new businesses and capital investment, according to development ex­ perts and authorities in the area of workers' compen­sation. Perhaps Schaefer's workers' compensation initiative can best be described as a signal to business and a po­litical symbol of his administration: He will be a probusi­ness governor and the state will accommodate corpor­ate interests that he sees as crucial to Maryland's eco­nomic future. To those who are well-grounded in the debate over workers' compensation costs, there are questions about Schaefer's basic premise. He believes that a higher- than-average cost for insurance in Maryland is a deter­rent to development. But others who research devel­opment decisions say business locations are more often directly determined by marketing needs, tax rates, availability of transportation, skilled labor and educa­tional resoarees and the quality of life of a region. "For some firms, it's extremely important and for others, it has little importance," said Arthur Williams, an economics professor at the isniversity of Minnesota. "But, let's put it this way. I don't know of any firm that has moved just because of workers' compensation costs."
