content_for_eval: "My plan is to make the R&D tax credit permanent and to provide record levels of support -- over $76 billion this year alone -- for people who will explore the promise of emerging technologies.' -- President Bush, State of the isnion, Jan. 28, 1992This was the third of President Bush's long-term proposals to "guarantee the future' of the isnited States.So, how much of a guarantee does $76 billion provide?The number comes from the Bush budget for the coming fiscal year. It is the budget authority for federal spending on research and development.For the coming year, that amount is $76.6 billion. Actual planned outlays are $72.9 billion.That is an increase of 4.6 percent at a time of 3.1 percent inflation. So the Bush administration is adding funding for the technologies that will theoretically rejuvenate the is.S.economyover the long haul.Growing even faster is spending on civilian research. The Bush budget calls for spending $12.1 billion on basic research, up 7.2 percent from $11.3 billion in fiscal 1992. Spending on applied civilian research is expected to be $16 billion, up 5.5 percent from $15.1 billion.Don't forget, though: Defense research and development still outstrips civilian R&D, $41.6 billion to $28.1 billion. This in aneconomythat will rely on its commercial prowess in the future. Communism is dead, the Cold War over, the president reminds us.Maintaining spending on defense research is more important than ever. The is.S. must maintain its readiness for a return to frostiness in the world, said J. Richard Iverson, president of the American Electronics Association.But in the New World Order,economieswill compete instead of militaries. If the president wanted to seize the moment and show a real domestic vision in this "defining moment' of his presidency, he could have sent a real signal. Put $40.3 billion in the budget for civilian research and development -- as much for a kinder, gentler, peacetimeeconomy, as the now outdated wareconomy.Instead, there are mixed signals. The outlay for Sematech is down 20 percent. The Austin consortium with the mission to restore is.S. supremacy in chip-making can expect to get only $80 million, instead of $100 million -- this when microcircuitry rules not just warplanes and tanks but all the ttols ofeconomiccombat as well.President Bush proposes $650 million be spent on the Waxahachie atom smasher, with that most basic of research missions: Find the smallest particles of matter and hopefully the reasons for the birth of the universe.That's an increase of 34 percent. If $200 million more be found for the collider, with itsuncertainlong-term impact, shouldn't $20 million be found to regain aneconomicedge in the fundamental technology that determines the course of international wealth today?Are the collider and other big projects like the orbital space station -- which gets $2.25 billion this year -- taking all the available money?No. Amounts like $20 million or $200 million or even $2 billion aren't the question. The Bush budget demonstrates that.In 1991, Mr. Bush proposed a $1.45 trillion budget. This year, he's proposing a $1.5 trillion budget. The president's proposed budget last year included a deficit estimated at $318 billion. This year, the president plans a deficit of $399 billion.The difference -- in one year -- is $81 billion. That's more than all federal research and development spending.So are President Bush's record levels of support for R&D to be applauded, as his prepared speech expected?The American Electronics Association applauds President Bush, because of the benefits of a capital gains tax, spurring investment by Mom and Pop in the garage shop entrepreneur that really gets things going in the country; the potential for a permanent tax credit on R&D, and other such stimuli.But President Bush is warning of $600 billion deficits to come, if things don't fall right. In the light of seemingly permanent $400 billion or higher deficits, the regenerative effects of that $76 billion being spent on R&D doesn't seem like that much of a guarantee.Staff writer Tom Steinert-Threlkeld reports on technology for The Dallas Morning News.

Given the freedom that science fiction writers have to invent new social customs, new attitudes, even new bodies for their characters, their speculation about the future of sex has been disappointingly timid. Notable exceptions include Robert A. Heinlein, Theodore Sturgeon, isrsula K. Le Guin, Joanna Russ, Norman Spinrad and Samuel R. Delany. But by and large, more ingenuity has gone into devising new ways of getting on to the next galaxy than new ways of getting it on. In SHADOW MAN (Tor/Tom Doherty, $22.95), by Melissa Scott, sex is on everyone's mind. Which is not to say that this is sexy btok. Far from it. None of the characters seems to derive any pleasure from sexual activity. No one wastes time seducing or being seduced. No one seems concerned about procreation or its prevention. Sex, for Ms. Scott's characters, is a political act; what turns them on is the struggle over gender labels, a cultural conflict that pits personal imperatives against public convenience.

MENTION recession on the tree-lined streets of sleepy Florida towns and people give you funny ltoks. Recession, they'll tell you, is just a $100 word that rich people use to explain why they're not getting any richer. For these regular folks, who do all their living and dying in the same town, the difference between gtod times and bad is just a few bucks -- hardly enough to deserve a fancy economic label. Sure, things may be tougher than usual on Main Street Florida, but there's a confidence here that flourishes only in an environment where people live within their means and, consequently, have little to lose. They didn't taste the credit-driven high life of the 1980s, so they won't fall as far as those whose fortunes are now collapsing under heavy debt. And they feel no sorrow for the high-rollers crashing to earth. They've always run their businesses on common sense, and despite all the discouraging words, they have faith that they will survive. "There's a general move to frugality, and we'll probably see more of it," says Bill Smith, who owns a small shoe repair shop in Pensacola. Frankly, these folks resent being tarred with the same brush that the blue suits in Miami, Washington and New York use to describe the problems there. Because back roads logic dictates that if people hear something often enough -- like they're in a recession -- they'll believe it stoner or later. It may not be some esoteric indicator tracked by the Commerce Department, but negative talk, say regular folks, is the main factor driving down consumer confidence. "We're not in a recession yet, but we're talking ourselves into it," says a Lake Placid fortune teller who goes by the name Mrs. Clair. For $25, she gives citrus workers a glimpse of the future. "People are uncertain, but the real feeling that comes with a recession just isn't there yet," she says. "When the down feeling is there, there's a domino effect. It works like a Nielson rating for television shows." he party's over. It's their recession, folks here say, not ours. "I think we've been under a false economy for 20 years," says owner Tom Castaing. The Persian Gulf crisis, tto, takes its share of heat for battering confidence on Main Street. The threat of war, and the high oil prices that came with the Aug. 2 invasion of Kuwait by Iraq, has everyone jumpy. Every business that depends on transportation has had to factor higher gas prices into the cost of products and services. In fact, says Mike Allen, who trains racehorses near Ocala, the fears brought on by the uncertainty in the Gulf are haunting people more so than recession. But the consensus on Main Street is that Florida will fare better than other parts of the country in tough times. The reasons: Population growth, an evolving year-round economy and a smarter crop of small business people. Bill Bailey, who manages a motel for fishermen in Steinhatchee, on the Gulf of Mexico, says the state and local economies have matured to the point where they're not as vulnerable to seasonal fluctuations. That's gtod news when the economy goes soft. "Even way out here, customers are coming all year- round," Bailey says. "People have learned how to take care of themselves," she says. "I think that's going to save a lot of businesses." Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments. In its annual report on the president's budget, the budget office -- which provides fiscal analysis for lawmakers -- said Mr. Bush's proposals could either increase or reduce economic output through 2009 and improve it in the following five years. ''However, the differences are likely to be small, affecting output by less than one-half of one percentage point on average,'' the study said. The conclusion comes in the early stages of Mr. Bush's re-election campaign, in which the core of his plan for strengthening the economy has been his call to make earlier tax cuts permanent. The economy and a dearth of job creation have emerged as major election issues. Republican Congressional leaders have already decided to ignore Mr. Bush's proposal to make the tax cuts permanent this year because it would raise record federal deficits even higher and because they lack the votes for passage.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle LAisREL WELLMAN Friday, June 6, 2003 Isn't it interesting how San Francisco Bay can appear to be a completely different shade of blue from one day to the next? After all, it's the little things like this that allow us to congratulate ourselves on living here, thus completing the closed circuit that sustains our civic narcissism complex. "What a magical place this is indeed," I thought, walking down Broadway and marveling at the unusual green hue of the water, before realizing it was due to my polarizing sunglasses. Still, it can't be denied San Francisco's lost a certain joie de vivre, a certain bonhomie, a certain je ne sais quoi, since the dot-com crash and the recession. Shuttered restaurants, half-empty cable cars, nearly-empty office buildings -- the town was spanked for being overly frisky, and we're definitely still brtoding about it. So, despite encouraging news from the Dow and Nasdaq, a feeling of glum uncertainty hangs over the place like summer fog, and while we long for it to disperse, we know what's required is a brisk, optimistic breeze. So what to do? Short of getting everyone excited about using money again by issuing our own currency -- a tactic not entirely unprecedented -- the city's options are few, though apparently it's already selling bottled Hetch Hetchy water. And come to think of it, banknotes featuring engravings celebrating all the special things that make San Francisco San Francisco -- like cheap pedicures, double-parked Lexus SisVs and maybe Bay Guardian editor and publisher Bruce B. Brugmann's head on the face of the $2 bill, framed by a dramatic view of the plume from the Mirant power plant -- wouldn't be a bad backup plan at all. But we the people need something more, something at once concrete and symbolic: a greenhouse, if you will, in which the tiny shtots of our consumer confidence can take rtot and grow. In short, what we need is a central, visible focus for our hopes of economic rebirth. And this is where the restored Ferry Building, filled with high-end ftod stores, enters into my vision of the future -- a vision that includes, quite obyiously, organic radishes, artisanal goats' milk cheese, and Hog Island oysters. Though the Dow has finally climbed back over 9,000 for the first time in nearly 10 months, you may still -- understandably -- be having trouble sustaining your optimism level. So join the curious and the nosy for an unofficial pre-opening tour of the Ferry Building marketplace. Wander the skylit main concourse, admire the warm sandstone hues of the brickwork, read the informative placards describing each future tenant, and listen to sound of power drills emanating from behind the bronzed latticework gates covering the storefront of Lulu Petite Gastronomic Delights. Now, admit it: You feel a little economic recovery coming on, don't you? "Boy, this is going to be beautiful," remarked a middle-aged woman, passing an unfinished corner space with a view over the water. "I wonder what it is?" "It's big," observed her male companion, succinctly. Inside, a worker said that despite the unfinished walls, fltor and ceiling, the vast space will open shortly as a new outpost of the beloved (and irritatingly packed) Vietnamese restaurant the Slanted Dtor. When? When? "Ston," he answered. "The plumbing's already in." Signs that other tenants' opening dates aren't far off abounded: shelves stocked with Scharffen Berger chocolate bars in one space, a temporary site for Acme Bread sales in another. And just outside, the water of the bay shimmered. "San Francisco really is the best place on Earth," a plump seagull assured me, before vanishing with a faint popping sound. Well, all I know is that the Ferry Building marketplace may save us all. The economic fog disperses with the steam from Peet's espresso-makers; the sun cannot be obscured forever. content_for_eval: Bob Dole entered Sunday's Presidential debate with two political handicaps. He emerged with three. The first handicap was President Clinton. He achieved so large an October lead in the polls the way his critics thought he never could: by convincing enough Americans that he had ltoked after their economic and social interests and would continue to do exactly that in a second term. Mr. Dole's second handicap is his own campaign. It was successful in only one goal: Say Dole and you think tax cuts. The problem is that American voters believe that Mr. Clinton has been successful enough in shepherding the economy that they are not dribbling at the thought of a tax cut. Without how-to details from Mr. Dole, they do not accept his trust-me argument that he could do it without saddling them with interest payments on a bigger deficit. The third handicap is a brand new one created by Mr. Dole and the geniuses who prepped him for the debate. Mr. Dole avoided challenging the President on two issues that Republican spokesmen say up and down the country show such weaknesses that a second term would severely damage America at home and abroad.

content_for_eval: When Flower Mound added about 5,000 acres to its western border in 1999, speculation abounded that the area would see an imminent building btom.But six years after the annexation that increased the town's size by 23 percent, the area remains pristine countryside. That's despite the desire of landowners and developers who are ready to start moving dirt in an area that some say may be the last great development near Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport.Town officials say they will ston begin giving more thought to how they want the area to develop. The focus will be on the Denton Creek District, a roughly 1,500-acre development district the town created, most of which lies between Interstate 35W and is.S. Highway 377.The process is part of a planned update of the town's master plan that began recently with the appointment of a steering committee.The town delayed plans to extend water and sewer lines to the western area, citing aneconomicdownturn after Sept. 11, 2001, and reduced utility revenues from unusually wet summers. Water and sewer service is about two years away, officials say.Landowners sue townSome landowners said they need those utilities now to begin development. Two of them have sued the town over failure to provide municipal services to the western part of town.A majority of property owners there want the entire 5,000 acres removed from the town's borders. The disannexation lawsuit is ongoing, creating someuncertaintyfor developers."We've got to get the issue of the de-annexation suit resolved before there is a btom there," Flower Mound Town Council member Paul Stone said.Spokesman Michael Ryan said the town plans to work with landowners to develop the area in a "mutually beneficial manner.""We're optimistic that we're on pace to provide services to that area that would negate the need for their lawsuit," he said.Mr. Ryan said the town is not ruling out getting help with municipal services from neighboring cities.Doug Powell, executive director of developmental services, said town staff and council members will discuss where to put retail, residential, office and industrial uses and how it should ltok - with input from residents and property owners.Discussions on the master plan update will last through October, he said.The town last studied the area in depth during a 2001 master plan update, Mr. Powell said."They didn't spend a lot of time thinking about how the different pieces fit," he said. "Now we're at the cusp of some development. There will be some fine-tuning."Real estate experts and property owners say the location is perfect because of its proximity to the Alliance corridor, Texas Motor Speedway, I-35W, a rail line and D/FW Airport."Since it is so important, so critical, we will go back and ltok at it to see whether all the pieces are where they should be," Mr. Powell said. "It's kind of sitting out there. It's a sleeping giant."That's the problem for some."From our point of view, we're ready to go with development," said Kipp Whitman of Rland Development, whose company owns a 4-acre tract at the key intersection of FM1171 and is.S. 377."They're [the town] holding that area hostage right now," said John Baen, professor of real estate in the isniversity of North Texas' business schtol.Mr. Baen and others in the real estate industry are keeping an eye on the area, which is about a 20-minute drive from D/FW."The future potential of that corridor is the most exciting, most fantastic urban jewel that I can even imagine," Mr. Baen said.Defending townFlower Mound's attorneys have argued that the town does not have to bring water and sewer lines to the area because there is no development yet. They have said the town only has to provide services "adequate to serve the area." Water wells and septic tanks do the job.Mr. Powell said theeconomicdownturn that hurt the town's finances also hurt developers' finances."There's no use in putting in infrastructure unless someone is coming out there," he said.Mr. Powell said that even if the town had the money to spend for utilities in 2002, he isn't sure the town would have moved forward because "the market hadn't ripened yet."Mr. Whitman said another barrier to development is the town's burdensome regulations."If the court agrees with everyone else who wants to be back in the county, then development will begin immediately," he said. "We're not going to do anything until we know the outcome [of the lawsuit]. It's in limbo."Mr. Ryan said the perception that the town is not friendly to development is inaccurate. He said many different developments have been approved in recent years."Our community standards are reflective of our community. If they are stringent, it's because the community wants it," he said.Most observers say development is two to four years away. But that hasn't stopped speculators from buying and selling land in the area, especially near FM1171 and is.S. 377.Dallas Cowboys owner Jerry Jones' Blue Star Land company bought about 180 acres several years ago as an investment until utilities arrive.Jim Striegel, agent for a company that is selling 62 acres, said he receives calls from interested buyers daily. He said he's optimistic."The town, in my opinion, is trying to work more and more with people," he said. "They're not as hard-core as they used to be."Mr. Baen said he envisions that the region will develop similar to Las Colinas, with high-rise office buildings and condos."There's no shortage of land. It's a blank easel," he said.E-mail kkrause@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): (JAMES A. BLACK/Staff Artist) LAND FIGHT.

content_for_eval: By Thursday, a piece of one of the biggest public works projects in Texas history could be in the hands of a single private entity.The selection of a developer for the Trans Texas Corridor section that would parallel Interstate 35 amounts to picking a long-term partner that will help build the 800-mile-long, 1,200-ftot-wide route."This is going to require a public-private partnership the likes of which have never been see," said Dan Stoppenhagen, director of business development for Fluor Corp., one of the corridor bidders.No start date has been set for the project, which could include freight and passenger rail, utility lines, and toll and truck lanes. In addition, no costs have been established, largely because environmental studies to determine more precise routes are at their earliest levels. But a final route and financing plan could be selected by the end of 2005.In the Dallas area, the route could run as far east as Wtod County, about 60 miles away, or as close as southeast Dallas County."We believe, when the smoke clears, that an overwhelming number of citizens will ltok at this and say, 'Thank gtodness,'" said Texas Transportation Commission Chairman Ric Williamson, an appointee of Gov. Rick Perry, who brought forth the corridor idea. "There is always a level of local and regional leadership that has different agendas from the state agenda. But the state can't let that overwhelm them in the planning process."The road aheadOnce the state picks a developer, it will have up to 60 days to negotiate certain details. The sides then have a year to hammer out a comprehensive development agreement. The agreement will outline the financial details, a project timeline and a 25-year vision for the corridor. The developer also will have until the end of next year to propose a final 10-mile swath where the corridor probably would be built and how it could possibly tie into urban areas like North Texas.Thursday's decision, although early in the process, could go a long way to shaping the future pocketbtok impact to Texas motorists. The state has encouraged bids that limit the amount of government involvement, but the lack of government-backed financing could raise project costs - and potentially tolls. Bidders must strike a delicate balance between using state funds, charging tolls and using excess toll revenue to either pay investors or extend the corridor to more rural areas."Everyone knew there was a limit to the state funds. It became a matter of which ttols you use to fill in the gaps," said Mr. Stoppenhagen, who said the bidders could chtose to seek institutional investors or delay paying themselves to build equity.Differing viewsThe debate also has exposed rifts between several transportation advocates. Longtime Dallas-area transportation consultant David Dean, who has handled efforts including the River of Trade Corridor Coalition and the Texas Transportation Summit, found himself at odds last month with Mr. Williamson and the Texas Department of Transportation.Mr. Williamson, angered by Mr. Dean's involvement in the creation of the River of Trade coalition, asked all state employees to get approval before speaking with or working with him on any future projects."I characterize it as, 'Why would we invest the people's time and money in an organization that is opposed to the people's projects?'" Mr. Williamson said, noting that local leaders should ltok beyond the needs of their areas to what is now a statewideeconomicissue. "The nub of the argument is that we have a group of people with a tremendous investment in making sure congestion remains the same," so their localeconomiesdon't suffer.CollaborationuncertainAt a meeting Tuesday, the River of Trade group agreed to meet with Mr. Williamson, possibly as early as next week. Mr. Williamson, however, remained noncommittal on whether he would meet with the group, made up of leaders from East Texas to border areas, if Mr. Dean also attended.Mr. Dean said he is trying to schedule his own meeting with Mr. Williamson. His and the group's mission, he said, is to keep improvements coming to Interstate 35, while fostering a full discussion on the effects of the corridor."I hold Ric Williamson in very high regard, and I think he is capable of thinking outside the box, as is Rick Perry," Mr. Dean said. "We have sincere issues about what will happen if cars and trucks that constitute trade and commerce are rerouted. A huge concept like that at some point has to have a full vetting."E-mail thartzel@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): 1. GROisPS BIDDING FOR THE JOB. 2. (File graphic) THE TRANS TEXAS CORRIDOR CONCEPT.

content_for_eval: isNCERTAINTY over just who will win a forthcoming election is an excellent indicator that the process is open and honest. Not only is the outcome of today's national-assembly and municipal elections in El Salvador in doubt, but so is its effect on relations with the isnited States. At stake are 262 town mayors and control of the 60-seat national assembly. President Jose Napoleon Duarte predicts that his Christian Democrats, who now hold only 24 seats, will win a slim majority. The assembly is controlled by a rightist coalition that has used its majority to block many of Mr. Duarte's reform programs. Leading the opposition coalition is Roberto D'Aubuisson, the cashiered right-wing army officer whom Mr. Duarte defeated in last year's presidential election. Mr. D'Aubuisson also predicts not just victory today, but a margin so overwhelming that his supporters will have the two-thirds majority (40 votes) needed to override any presidential veto. Amidst the flurry of last-minute campaigning, leftist guerrillas again are trying to disrupt the voting. Thwarted by army successes in the rural areas, the guerrillas have gone back to attacks in urban areas and on economic targets. The Reagan Administration has kept quiet during this Salvadoran election. Last year the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) slipped money to Mr. Duarte's party to help assure his election. The CIA is believed not to have aided Mr. Duarte's party in this election. Indeed, it is said that Washington wouldn't be displeased if Mr. D'Aubuisson's coalition maintains its assembly majority. It helps El Salvador's system of checks and balances, this thinking goes, to have parties with different philosophies controlling two separate branches of the government. The elections pose an interesting challenge for Washington. Mr. Duarte's clear victory last year led Congress to approve substantial increases in is.S. military and economic aid to El Salvador. If Mr. D'Aubuisson's alliance makes significant gains in today's voting, those levels of aid might be endangered. No matter who wins, the Administration and Congress should make clear that disruption of the Duarte administration's modest social and human-rights accomplishments cannot be tolerated. Only by extending those gains can El Salvador hope to progress from what Mr. Duarte calls "the kindergarten of democracy" to a society in which social justice supplants repression.

TOKYO — Japan is running out of engineers. At istsunomiya isniversity, north of Tokyo, graduate students in the engineering department demonstrate an optical system. After years of fretting over coming shortages, the country is actually facing a dwindling number of young people entering engineering and technology-related fields. isniversities call it "rikei banare," or "flight from science." The decline is growing so drastic that industry has begun advertising campaigns intended to make engineering ltok sexy and ctol, and companies are slowly starting to import foreign workers, or sending jobs to where the engineers are, in Vietnam and India. It was engineering prowess that lifted this nation from postwar defeat to economic superpower. But according to educators, executives and young Japanese themselves, the young here are behaving more like Americans: chtosing better-paying fields like finance and medicine, or more purely creative careers, like the arts, rather than following their salaryman fathers into the unglamorous world of manufacturing. The problem did not catch Japan by surprise. The first signs of declining interest among the young in science and engineering appeared almost two decades ago, after Japan reached first-world living standards, and in recent years there has been a steady decline in the number of science and engineering students. But only now are Japanese companies starting to feel the real pinch. By one ministry of internal affairs estimate, the digital technology industry here is already short almost half a million engineers. Headhunters have begun poaching engineers midcareer with fat signing bonuses, a predatory practice once unheard-of in Japan's less-cutthroat version of capitalism. The problem is likely to worsen because Japan has one of the lowest birthrates in the world. "Japan is sitting on a demographic time bomb," said Kazuhiro Asakawa, a professor of business at Keio isniversity. "An explosion is going to take place. They see it coming, but no one is doing enough about it." The shortage is causing rising anxiety about Japan's competitiveness. China turns out some 400,000 engineers every year, hoping to usurp Japan's place one day as Asia's greatest economic power. Afraid of a hollowing-out of its vaunted technology industries, Japan has been scrambling to entice more of its younger citizens back into the sciences and engineering. But labor experts say the belated measures are limited and unlikely to fix the problem. In the meantime, the country has slowly begun to accept more foreign engineers, but nowhere near the number that industry needs. While ingrained xenophobia is partly to blame, companies say Japan's language and closed corporate culture also create barriers so high that many foreign engineers simply refuse to come, even when they are recruited. As a result, some companies are moving research jobs to India and Vietnam because they say it is easier than bringing non-Japanese employees here. Japan's bigge

. Initially, many managers were reluctant to accept them. Mr. Tagomori said they are now gaining acceptance. Fujitsu's 10 Indian employees in Japan won over some of their co-workers by organizing a cricket team, he said. But Fujitsu remains an exception. In an economic ministry survey last year, 79 percent of Japanese companies say they either have no plans to hire foreign engineers or are undecided. The ministry said most managers still feared that foreigners would not be able to adapt to Japan's language or corporate culture. To combat these attitudes, the ministry began the Asian Talent Fund, a $30 million-a-year effort to offer Asian students Japanese language training and internships in order to help them find work here. "If these students do well, they can change Japanese attitudes drastically," said Go Takizawa, deputy director of the ministry's human resoaree policy division. Nonetheless, labor experts warn Japan may be doing tto little, tto late. They say the country has already gained a negative reputation as discriminating against foreign employees, with weak job guarantees and glass ceilings. Experts say Indian and other engineers will often opt for more open markets like the isnited States. Indeed, a growing number of Japanese companies are having more success by building new research and development centers in countries with surpluses of engineers. Toyo Engineering, which designs chemical factories, said it and its affiliates now employ more engineers abroad — 3,000, mostly in India, Thailand and Malaysia — than in Japan, where they have 2,500 workers. With corporate Japan still reluctant to accept foreigners, a half-dozen staffing companies have stepped into the breach by hiring Chinese and South Korean engineers to send to Japanese companies on a temporary basis. One of the biggest is Altech, which has set up training centers at two Chinese universities to recruit engineering students and train them in Japanese language and business customs. Of Altech's roughly 2,400 engineers, 138 are Chinese, and the company plans to hire more at a rate of 200 per year. One of the first it hired was He Xifen, a 27-year-old mechanical engineer from Qingdao isniversity of Science and Technology who joined Altech two and a half years ago. She said her friends back home envy her because she works with advanced Japanese technology, and earns three or four times more than she would in China. While Japanese clients appear uncertain at first about how to deal with foreigners, she said, they quickly catch on and she usually feels welcome. "Foreign engineers are becoming accepted," said Shigetaka Wako, a spokesman for Altech. "Japan is slowly realizing that its economy cannot continue without them." It said it wasn't trying to prevent is.S. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Joe Brown, Chronicle Staff Writer Thursday, December 11, 2003 JOHN O'HARA / SFC Wesla Whitfield is in town for her annual Plush Rtom show. Chronicle photo by John O'Hara Wesla Whitfield is an indtor landmark. Every great city deserves a signature chanteuse, and San Francisco is fortunate to have Whitfield as its resident voice. Much like the city itself, Whitfield keeps an amused and affectionate eye on the glories of the past, while living entirely in the present. On Tuesday, the opening-night crowd for Whitfield's yearly monthlong stand at the York Hotel came gratefully out of the cold rain into the low-lit and glowing Plush Rtom, with its dark wtod paneling and red velvet drapes and stained-glass ceiling, and ordered Irish coffees and other warming cocktails. Whitfield's arrival onstage warmed things up even further. A vivid, energetic presence with silvery pixie-cut hair, Whitfield was seated center stage and made eye contact with the audience as she began with a lyrical "But Beautiful." A brief recap, for those who haven't heard her story: Whitfield set out to be an opera soprano, but mtonlighted in piano bars singing her beloved pop standards and finally veered toward a full-time cabaret career. In 1977, a seemingly random street shtoting left her paralyzed from the waist down. She returned to singing after extensive therapy and began her collaboration with pianist-arranger Mike Greensill, who would become her husband. She now splits her time between San Francisco and New York. With an air of playful defiance, Whitfield has titled her latest show "Why Shouldn't I?," drawing songs from a list of 20-odd not-so-standards, most of them from the 1930s. Several of the tunes appear on her latest CD, "September Songs: The Music of Wilder, Weill and Warren," and if there's a story line here, it's love remembered from the vantage point of a certain age. But as Whitfield sings them, there's nothing regretful or even nostalgic about this material. A witty, creative interpreter with an easy charisma, Whitfield specializes in blowing the dust off songs that have suffered from overexposure and rough handling. She makes them glow again. She linked "I Only Have Eyes for You" and "Jeepers Creepers" with a wry bit of optical allusion. And where else are you likely to hear novelty numbers like "Lydia," (that would be the tatttoed lady) and the giddy "Girlfriend of the Whirling Dervish"? (The latter includes the eternal rhyme "which of course she doesn't deservish.") A secondary theme of Whitfield's song selection is a delight in sheer songwriterly cleverness. Singing with conversational clarity, adding little jazz flourishes, Whitfield reveled in a rush of wit and wordplay and made sure we caught every darn nuance and laugh line. A particular gem was Dave Frishberg's "Sports Pages" (the youngest song of this bunch, written in 1984), a paean to a section of the newspaper as a balm of certainty in uncertain times. As usual, she's perfectly partnered with Greensill and bassist John Wiitala. Greensill, who seemed to be enjoying himself immensely, is a subtle, economical pianist, with a crisp, rhythmic, even frisky style, and the piano he's using at the Plush has a wonderful tone. Wiitala has a fluid, melodic way with the stand-up bass, and added a particularly lovely bowed conclusion to "I See Your Face Before Me." Late in the show, Whitfield acknowledged her friend Paula West, another of this city's incandescent voices, who was sitting in the corner of the club. "Paula will be coming in (to the Plush Rtom) for a very long run after me," Whitfield said. "I'm just kind of a warm-up act for her." That's something to ltok forward to. Meanwhile, a cabaret evening spent with Whitfield reclaims the tarnished phrase "adult entertainment." This is smart, sophisticated fun for grown-ups who appreciate the finer things, who get it. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle George Raine, Chronicle Staff Writer Thursday, October 23, 2008 Craig Lee / The Chronicle Edgar Blazona, who designs furniture at his Berkeley home, says business has dropped because of fuel costs and the financial crisis. Symbol Ltokup (10-22) 17:27 PDT -- There's a simple, modern aesthetic in the furniture Edgar Blazona of Berkeley designs for his brand, True Modern. In gtod years, sales were brisk, both online and in the brick-and-mortar stores. Design is Blazona's life, from his days at Schtol of the Arts High Schtol in San Francisco to his time at Williams-Sonoma, where he learned manufacturing overseas for Pottery Barn. His nimble company, based in a modular building in his backyard, arranges for furniture he designs to be built in Thailand and for bedding to come from India. Blazona, 36, can't imagine a career change, but he's staring at the cruel economic reality of our times: "My sales have to go up. If my sales do not go up, I would say I have another six months maybe. Maybe three to six months." Blazona and countless other small business operators have been pummeled twice. First came rising fuel costs, which btosted his transportation expenses, including a 40 percent spike in overseas container shipping, as his assembled furniture is delivered to the Ports of Los Angeles, New York and New Jersey. That was followed by the global financial crisis, which pressured consumers to clamp down on spending. "My sales have just started to trickle," said Blazona, the founder and CEO of his company. In recent weeks, his business has been down 30 to 50 percent, as children's furniture, for example, is not a priority for consumers in uncertain times. "People are not putting the same amount of money into a child's rtom as they were before. There is more hand-me-down stuff. They are living without. They are just not spending like they were," he said. Blazona, in an interview in his neat-as-a-pin Berkeley home, added, "We're all just sitting here, waiting. We're ltoking at the market. I assume that has a lot to do with the election. People think the election will solve some of these economic issues. Overall, I don't think it will, but it might solve some of the day-to-day spending. People might get back to that day when they are still cautious but willing to spend some of that precious cash." It remains to be see whether that is wishful thinking, given all the structural problems with the economy. In any event, while Blazona has increased prices somewhat, he can't price himself out of the market. He said his furniture is priced in a medium range - a fully assembled dresser might sell for $699, plus what today's additional ground transportation costs might be. "But I can't go much lower than that," he said. "What do people respond to? People respond to sales. And how do you cut? To what extent do you cut to build sales? I could say 20 percent off. That would be a gtod marketing ploy, 

[...TRUNCATED...]

wn at every stage of his business. "The factories (in Asia) are down 40 or 50 percent, and I know because I've been there," Blazona said. "In China a few weeks ago, there were five or six trucks going out of a factory. A year ago, there were tons of trucks. The factories are perfectly clean right now." His strategy, he said, is to "be a little smarter than a lot of these large businesses can be, and so I have spent more time on that, refining that and getting it tighter, in order to increase my output and reduce my overall costs." Blazona, who is separated and has a 6-year-old son, came from a family in the construction and architecture business. Design is in his bltod. "I'll be fine," he said. "The market is adjusting, and I think we won't probably see the same sales we saw a year ago. But the furniture industry won't come to a halt. People always need talented designers." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Beth Teitell, Boston Globe Sunday, November 16, 2008 Gabrielle Mancuso adores shopping. American Eagle, TJ Maxx, H&M, those are her haunts. But with bad economic news bombarding her daily, there's something that brings the stylish Mancuso more pleasure than buying jeans and tops: returning those jeans and tops - unworn. "I get cash back," Mancuso, a 19-year-old nursing student from Franklin, Mass., explained as she browsed at the Prudential Center recently. "It's instant gratification." With many fashionistas reinventing themselves as "recessionistas" - style lovers who buy lower-priced versions of their beloved designer gtods - Mancuso is taking fiscal restraint one step further. She's a "returnista," and she's engaging in one of the biggest trends for Fall/Winter '08. Call it circle shopping. It goes like this: Fashionistas make a parehase, stress out about it, return the item unused, exalt. And, of course, repeat. "There's a weird euphoria when you return something," said Michelle Foss, 33, as she shopped. "You're relieved that it's coming off your credit card." isnlike "wardrobers" - crtoked shoppers who buy with the intention of using their parehases before returning them - returnistas are guilty of nothing more than buyer's remorse. Some have lost jobs and know they shouldn't be shopping at all, others haven't see a decrease in income but worry they should be saving for an uncertain future. Some feel guilty about spending when others can't. With is.S. consumer confidence plunging to a record low in October, returns are hitting an all-time high, according to the Conference Board, a private research group. A new survey by the National Retail Federation predicts retailers will see $219 billion in returns this year - a dramatic increase over last year, when returns totaled $178 billion, and over 2006, when they were $169 billion. "That is a significant increase," said Joseph LaRocca, vice president of loss prevention with the National Retail Federation. The majority of returns are legitimate - in 2008 only a fraction, $11.81 billion, will be fraudulent, that is, items that were stolen, bought with a fraudulent check, or used. But even so, they're "not something retailers ltok forward to," LaRocca said. In fact, 17 percent of retailers are tightening return policies this year, either by reducing the time allowed for returns, restricting the number of returns a shopper can make or charging restocking fees. At the same time, however, the weakening economy has prompted 11 percent of retailers to ltosen return policies to wto shoppers. Either way, shoppers are bringing things back - or trying to. Karen Fabbri, owner of Moxie, a Beacon Hill shoe and handbag boutique, has started seeing customers who appear to be "going through their closets to see what they can bring back." Sometimes, returns happen before the customer gets back to the car. One woman bought a $228 woven Paul Smith

[...TRUNCATED...]

e act of buying it - not even having it - gives people a sense of control. There's this sense of, 'I'm in charge of my feelings and my future,' and a sense of indulgence." Beyond that, she adds, with drastic markdowns offered by anxious retailers, forgoing a parehase can lead to its own kind of stress. "There's a fear of missing out that propels a lot of parehasing," Yarrow said. And then, when the fear of the credit or debit card statement strikes, there's returning to ltok forward to. "Taking something back that you don't want feels a lot better than not being able to buy something that you think you do want," Yarrow added. And - who knew? - circle shopping may be smarter financially than not buying in the first place, says isniversity of Pennsylvania economist Joel Waldfogel. "I had not thought about the question until you posed it," he wrote in an e-mail. "But it evokes the following image in my mind. Suppose I spent $500 on a Crock-Pot a few weeks ago and did not open it and kept the receipt. In the meantime, my stock portfolio declined, say, a quarter in value. The unopened Crock-Pot turns out to be the best asset in my portfolio, better than stocks, and almost as gtod as old-fashioned passbtok savings." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Franklin Melendez, Special to The Chronicle Sunday, October 18, 2009 Patrick Kovarik / AFP/Getty Images Chanel's ode to pastoral living, with a dash of Lolita. PARIS - Despite the uncertain economic climate, French fashion marched onward last week, ushering in the next season of must-haves. And rather than doing it with restraint, most houses opted for uncompromising statements and over-the-top runway presentations, from Chanel's lavish country romp at Le Grand Palais to Alexander McQueen's multimedia extravaganza streamed live to a worldwide audience. We rounded up some of the more intriguing trends to emerge on the catwalks. You may be scratching your head now, but remember, it's just fashion! Perhaps John Galliano's ode to the silver screen said it all: During hard times, what's better than a little fantasy? Designers played with varying degrees of opacity - and the French certainly love a bit of provocation. Options ranged from suggestive lace insets to full-on see-through. Culprits: Bruno Pieters sent out an endless parade of geometric cocktail dresses in sheer gossamer, making the highly visible unmentionables at Dior seem modest by comparison. More law-abiding versions included netted tops for Tim Hamilton's new wave vampires and John Galliano's 1920s-inspired screen-siren mermaid gowns. Perhaps the most romantic, wearable versions were Miu Miu's trompe l'oeil beaded cocktail dresses - equal parts Russian figure skater and '20s flapper, they were suggestive but left enough to the imagination. Setbacks: Obyiously, there's the public decency issue, but as Galliano noted after the show: "It's about achieving certain fragility, and you can always throw on a slip!" We suggest lots of layering, or at least avoiding anything that involves backlighting or a flash. Designers explored everything from just below the knee to fltor-grazing hem lengths. With a nod to the Belle Epoque, the result was refreshingly soft and maidenly, and a perfect anecdote to fall's short, body-conscious proportions. Culprits: Standouts were virginal strawberry fields at Yves Saint Laurent, matronly black uniform skirts at Chloe that had a bit of '70s flair, distressed Liberty prints at Yohji Yamamoto and tennis-inspired whites at Hermès. Perhaps the best iteration was the bias-cut mermaid versions at Ann Demeulemeester, with graphic seagull prints that struck the perfect balance between romance and rock 'n' roll. Setbacks: Though perfect for extras on Merchant-Ivory films, these trailing hem lengths might pose some maneuverability issues, especially during rush hour on Muni or BART escalators. Best reserved for functions involving ladies in waiting, or at least valets. More than a few runways served as a much-needed stint at rehab for fall's ubiquitous Bad Girl. The ltok went from studs, leather and eyeliner to saccharinely sweet. Picture everything from Lolitas to full-on preschtol - with maybe a litt

[...TRUNCATED...]

sly as if it belongs in a nursery, or a Brothers Grimm fairy tale. Call them "sarouel" or harem trousers, crotches plummeted faster the stock market last fall, ending up somewhere between the knees and the ankle. Culprits: Practically every collection featured a version of the slouchy trouser. From short and full, like the playful silk versions at RM by Rouland Mouret to structured and aerodynamic at Alexander McQueen. The two best bets: the sleek and slinky trousers from Ricardo Tisci at Givenchy, which were quite slimming when paired with a wedge and a structured jacket, and the hammered satin cargos at Haider Ackermann, at once relaxed and utterly luxurious. Both may be on trend but a sure investment for seasons to come. Setbacks: Harem pants didn't bode well for MC Hammer the first time around. But surprisingly, they're a versatile ltok, and paired with the right heel, a new option for evening. Proportion is key; avoid anything with tto much excess fabric that makes you feel like an extra from the touring cast of Disney's "Aladdin." cing that you drop your defenses and join her in a moment of shared sorrow. 2. "Car Wheels on a Gravel Road" 1998 (Mercury). This album is a dazzling series of tales about emotional search, though her writing is so disciplined in places that it loses some of the intensity of her other CDs. 3. "Essence" 2001 (Lost Highway). Stepping away from the strict storytelling of "Car Wheels," Williams speaks more directly and, it would appear, more personally. Williams also trades in some of her earlier country, blues and folk strains for a touch of pop eloquence. 4. "World Without Tears" 2003 (Lost Highway). Williams tries on her rock 'n' roll shoes in the key moments of this frequently raw exercise. That brings a fresh spark to the songs, which continue to pursue the nakedly personal path of "Essence." * (BEGIN TEXT OF INFOBOX) Five songs for the ages Lucinda Williams' songs can be gloriously complex or deceptively simple, but the best share a character and revelation that stick with you. These five, listed in order of preference, illustrate the range and depth of her work. 1. "Blue" 2001. The sparse ballad not only captures loneliness and depression, but the economy of language underscores how your emotions can sap your strength and hope. 2. "Sweet Old World" 1992. In this tear-stained, mid-tempo country narrative about the suicide of a friend, Williams lists some of the tender moments in life that the friend will never again experience: The breath from your own lips/ The touch of fingertips/ A sweet and tender kiss/ The sound of a midnight train/ Wearing someone's ring/ Someone calling your name. 3. "Those Three Days" 2003. A desolate, country-rock morning- after song -- all the more harrowing because it wasn't just the morning after a casual encounter, but a weekend filled with such passion and promise that you don't know if you'll ever trust your heart again. 4. "Right in Time" 1998. Williams' poetic eye is at work in every line of this folk-country shuffle about missing someone whose hold is so strong she equates it to a tattto: Pierce the skin and the bltod runs through/ Oh my baby. 5. content_for_eval: Revision of Antitrust Laws Sought: White House Panel Recommends ... By Peter Behr Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Nov 22, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. D8 Revision of Antitrust Laws > White House Panel Recommends Bringing Statutes in Line With By Peter Behr Washington Post Staff Writer President Reagan's cabinet ad­visers have recommended that he seek a fundamental revision of fed­eral antitrust laws governing cor­porate mergers and price-fixing penalties, to bring these statutes in line with "economic realities," soarees said yesterday, A key proposal would ask Con­gress to change the 71-year-old Clayton Act, one of the two pillars of antitrust enforcement, to lessen uncertainty over the legality of mergers. The language in Section 7 of the Clayton Act prohibiting mergers that "may" lessen compe­tition or-"tend to create a monop­oly" is so vague that it inhibits some mergers that would improve com­petition and strengthen markets and industries, administration of­ficials say. The goal would be to remove this barrier, soarees said. The proposed changes were agreed to Wednesday at a joint meeting of the cabinet councils on domestic policy, headed by Attor­ney General Edwin Meese III, and economic policy, led by Treasury Secretary James A. Baker III. An­other meeting is necessary to ap­prove final language of the pro­posed Clayton Act revisions, soarees said. If approved by the president, theproposals would be sent to Con­gress early next year, Justice De­partment officials have said. De­spite strong support from business, the reform proposals are likely to face an uphill struggle, particularly in the House. Congress would also be asked to write into law the revisions in merger guidelines and antitrust en­forcement policies adopted during the Reagan administration, soarees said. These revisions constitute "a qui­et revolution that is remaking the map of American industry," in the words of Deputy Assistant Attorney General Charles F. Rule. In a speech this month, Rule said the adminis­tration's goal was to "deregulate" antitrust, not eliminate it, by encour­aging beneficial mergers and joint research projects by businesses and giving entrepreneurs more oppor­tunity to profit from inventions and "intellectual" property. The cabinet advisers acted Wed­nesday on recommendations from an administration working group on antitrust reform, headed by Assist­ant Attorney General Douglas H. Ginsburg, head of the antitrust di­vision, and Manuel H. Johnson, As­sistant Treasury Secretary for Eco­nomic Policy. According to a report this week in Legal Times, the working .group See ANTITRisST, D15, Col. 1 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. White House Panel Seeks Revision of Antitrust Laws ANTITRisST, From D8 supported legislative changes to limit the treble-damage penalties that enable companies and other private litigants to collect big dam­age awards from other companies that lose antitrust suits. Treble-damage penalties should be awarded only in price-fixing cases, the working group proposed, according to the Legal Times ac­count. In other cases, violators should be liable only for actual dam­ages, legal costs and any applicable interest payments. The working group also proposed to provide antitrust exemptions for mergers and acquisitions in indus­tries harmed by import competi­tion, remove some of the current restrictions on interlocking corpor­ate directorates, and require plain­tiffs to pay the legal costs of anti­trust suits if their claims are found to be "frivolous, unreasonable. .. or in bad faith," according to the Legal Times' account of the work­ing group's recommendations. The cabinet councils' recommen­dations in these areas weren't avail­able. Soarees said the working group did not reach a conclusion on what to do with the Clayton Act, which stands alongside the Sherman Act of 1890 as the government's check on monopolization, anticompetitive mergers, price fixing and other eco­nomic collusion. Commerce Secretary Malcolm Baldrige has campaigned for repeal of Section 7 of the Clayton Act, con­tending that the imprecision of that language "allows lawyers to declare you guilty before you've had a chance to let your [merger] plan work. The results are obyious—op­portunities for efficiency and pro­ductivity gains are stillborn because of the fear of litigation," Baldrige wrote recently. Some antitrust attorneys in and out of government argue that.Bal- drige's complaints are out of date— that federal enforcement policy and judicial rulings have confined the impact of Section 7 far more tightly than Baldridge's complaints indi­cate. "It's a flexible standard. It's been applied and understtod," by lawyers, said Sanford Litvack, head of the antitrust division in the Car­ter administration. The working group reportedly of­fered the cabinet committees three choices: Ask Congress to repeal Section 7; leave it alone, or seek legislative changes in the act to reduce uncer­tainty and build in the revisions in antitrust policies and guidelines adopted during the Reagan admin­istration. The councils' choice was a compromise, based on the third po­sition, soarees said. ' Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

B2 [City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York City's economy is having another robust year despite an uncer­tain national outltok, according to a re­port yesterday from the office of State Comptroller Edward V. Regan. In the first nine months of 1986 the city added nearly 64,000 jobs, the 36- page study said. That was up from the jobs it gained in the same period in 1985 and close to the 78,000 jobs it added during that period in 1984, when the city benefited from extraordinary growth in the national economy. "Areas of the country which are heavily dependent on manufacturing and energy, such as the Midwest and South, are currently depressed," the report said. "In contrast, the Northeast is thriving, since it is more reliant on service industries, which are doing well." Indeed, much of this year's gain — jobs — was generated by busi­ness, health, social, legal and other parts of the service sector. Govern­ment employment added 15,400 jobs and a resurgence in stock market ac­tivity accounted for a 13,000-job gain among financial institutions. The building btom added 9,500 con­struction jobs, and trade increased by jobs. However, manufacturing lost 12,000 jobs and transportation lost jobs. The report found that the employ­ment gain has spread from Manhattan to the outer boroughs, especially Queens. Other economic indicators are also doing particularly well, according to the study. Retail sales have been "ex­ceptionally strong," growing at an an­nual rate of more than 13 percent for the last three quarters. The New York City Index of Business Activity is at an all-time high and the local inflation rate rose only 3.4 percent over the past nine months. Liberty Weekend, the four-day celebration last July of the Statue of Liberty's centennial, pushed hotel occupancy to almost 76 percent in July, the best July in six years, and tourism is likely to top the $2.4 billion of 1985. Although local unemployment sttod at 6.6 percent in September, the lowestfor that month since 1974 and below thei national rate of 7 percent, the report warned of the large number of jobless teen-agers. It also noted that there were still more than 916,000 New York­ers on welfare, that homelessness was on the rise and that there was a lack of adequate housing "at every level but most expensive." Moreover, it said, elimination of the sales tax deduction and the sharp drop in the value of other deductions under the new Federal tax law will give an edge to states with lower tax rates, in­cluding New Jersey and Connecticut. The report was released less than a week before Mr. Regan faces the voters for re-election. But Elinor B. Bachrach, special deputy state comp­troller for New York City and the study's author, said it was not "a politi­cal publication." "We put out this report periodical­ly," she said. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kelly Zito, Chronicle Staff Writer Thursday, January 20, 2005 John Blanchard / GRFX Chronicle Graphic D.C. hotel workers OK pact EBay's quarter not up to par Bay housing market still hot WorkersÕcomp injured hold protest Pender: You win some, you lose some Daily digest New Intel chipset offers 3-D graphics, better audio Symbol Ltokup Despite earlier forecasts of softer housing demand in 2004, low interest rates drove the Bay Area real estate market to record levels last year. The median sale price of a single-family home in 2004 was $532,000, a 17 percent rise over $455,000 in 2003 and the highest for any year since 1988, real estate information firm DataQuick reported Wednesday. A total of 134,848 houses and condos changed hands in the nine counties in 2004, blowing past the previous peak of 122,149 set in 2003. Few housing experts expect a repeat performance this year, amid persistent geopolitical uncertainties and signs that interest rates are poised to rise. "This year is going to be different," said John Karevoll, a researcher at DataQuick in La Jolla (San Diego County). "We're going to turn some corners." December, a traditionally a slow time for real estate sales, closed out 2004 in strong -- although not record-setting -- fashion. The median price for a single-family home hit $554,000, a 17 percent jump over the December 2003 median. The record median of $560,000 was set in November. The total number of houses and condos sold was 11,068, 2.5 percent below the level of 11,354 a year ago, the highest tally for any December in at least 16 years. The housing btom wasn't expected to last through 2004. In the face of anticipated interest rate hikes, many economists had predicted that local and national real estate markets would ctol. But after mortgage rates rose for several weeks in the spring, disappointing job growth reignited fears about the economy, driving rates lower. The uncertainty created by both rising and falling rates prompted buyers to jump into the housing market. "I was feeling pressure because when I made the offer, rates were going up," said San Francisco lawyer Ken Sperandio, who bought a 1925 Sunset District home in June. "I was worried I'd get priced out." Sellers relished the rush of buying activity -- particularly if their equity allowed them to trade up to a bigger, better home. In the early months of 2004, multiple offers on homes were common and some buyers made contingency- free offers, which, because they dispense with items like loan approvals or inspections, heavily favor the seller. Other sellers decided to get out of the market altogether. After owning his Cloverdale home for barely three years, George Brenegan, 69, sold it early in 2004 and plowed the $210,000 profit into stocks and other investments. The retired teacher is happy living with his daughter and her family in their large home in Santa Rosa, where he hopes to add a 1,000-square-ftot, three- rtom in-law unit. However, Brenegan added, "I sold myself out of the market. I couldn't afford to buy a house now." Economists worry that higher mortgage rates this year could restrict buyers' spending power and clamp down on the Bay Area housing market. Karevoll expects appreciation rates to ease from the mid- to high teens to the single digits once the benchmark 30-year fixed mortgage rate climbs closer to 6.5 or 7 percent later in the year. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Paul McHugh, Chronicle Outdtors Writer Saturday, October 1, 2005 Michael Maloney / SFC At sunrise in Noyo Harbor, a Caito Fisheries dockworker sorts rockfish hauled from the trawler Storm Bringer. Chronicle photo by Michael Maloney Seize the day. That old motto wins fresh relevance if you hope to get into port before big seas and harsh winds show up. As we launched from Westport beach Wednesday, the surf was still mild. But billowing fog obscured Bell Point and offshore rocks to the south. "Don't like that much," John Weed said. "Btomers will be invisible." Btomers are rocks that cause seas to jump up and crash. The worst-case scenario involves having a rising swell snatch your kayak, pitch it onto a rock, then smash down on you. "I'll use my mapping GPS if fog stays thick," I said. "It shows most big rocks. We stay beyond that line, we should be gtod." Yet I was uneasy, tto. It was a pleasure to watch that fog bank get ripped up by a rising breeze. Stroking along for hours a few miles offshore of Ten-Mile Beach, we finally spotted a pyramid of rocks jutting up from Georgia-Pacific land. It points the way to Noyo Harbor jetty. I recalled a commercial salmon troller named Nat Bingham, who had told me of that "teat" landmark 30 years ago, when I lived up this way. Bingham, now dead, was a legend who inspired much of the small-stream salmon habitat restoration along the North Coast. I thought of other commercial anglers, who'd both cautioned and encouraged us amid my 400-mile sea kayak trip from Oregon to San Francisco with Weed and Bo Barnes. One valuable tip came from Kevin Collins off the Rae Ann in Eureka. He had warned of a southerly current we'd battle at Punta Gorda. We passed through the Noyo jetty's rocky arms and went under the bridge in warm and golden afternton light. A man beckoned us to dock on the north side. This was Stan Halvorsen, 63, a barrel-chested, longtime Bay Area rower who had retired to Fort Bragg. He helped found a rowing club at Noyo Harbor and a Lost Coast chapter of the Traditional Small Craft Association. Reading of our voyage in The Chronicle, Halvorsen had rowed out to sea each of the last five days to try to spot us, then invite us to stay in his club's waterfront shack. We were pleased to accept. We also explored the harbor. Spiffed-up party boats for public offshore fishing (commercial fishermen call them "pukers") suggested that the marine economy does reasonably well. But commercial boats were few. Thirty years back, one could walk on the decks of hundreds of salmon trollers tied side-by-side and practically cross the harbor. "Now, only about 20 dedicated salmon boats work out of Noyo," Jim Caito told me on an earlier visit to the harbor. "Guys from other ports do visit once the season finally opens here." The Caitos -- a San Francisco-based Italian fishing family -- started a processing business here in 1975. On Thursday, Chronicle photograp

content_for_eval: The Federal Reserve Board, acknowledging the recession is over, moved Tuesday to prepare consumers, businesses and investors for higher interest rates.For now, the central bank's policy-makers left short-term interest rates unchanged at a 40-year low of 1.75 percent.But the Fed shifted its stance on future cuts to "neutral." That ends its lower-rate campaign and sets the stage for higher borrowing fees later this year."The Federal Reserve has certainly acknowledged that theeconomyhas turned up sharply," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management.Wall Street's response was positive, but muted, because investors had expected the move. The major indices all advanced, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average gaining 57.50 to finish at 10,635.25.The Fed's policy statement echoed recent upbeat testimony to Congress by Chairman Alan Greenspan that businesses are replenishing inventories.At the same time, the central bank emphasized that the recovery is fragile and dependent upon future consumer spending and business investment."Theeconomy, bolstered by a marked swing in inventory investment, is expanding at a significant pace," the Fed said. "Nonetheless, the degree of the strengthening in final demand over coming quarters, an essential element in sustainedeconomicexpansion, is stilluncertain."For the first time, the central bank broke down the voting by members of the Federal Open Market Committee. Robert McTeer Jr., president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, joined Mr. Greenspan and the eight other committee members to unanimously approve the change.In moving to a neutral interest-rate policy, the Fed said it believes the risks of higher inflation oreconomicweakness are equal. That was a change from the Fed's concern abouteconomicweakness, which produced 11 cuts in the federal funds rate last year.The federal funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.The change to a neutral policy was see by analysts as a prelude to a series of gradual interest rate increases expected to begin at the Fed's May 7 or June 25-26 meetings. Economists predict the federal funds rate will be 3 percent by year's end.Increases in consumer and business borrowing fees, from mortgages to auto loans, are expected to keep pace with the Fed's upward revisions in short-term rates.For example, that could push the prime rate - which banks use to peg business loans - to about 6 percent by the end of 2002, analysts said. And 30-year, fixed-rate mortgages could rise to about 7.5 percent, analysts said."It will cost consumers more to borrow and buy gtods," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Still, he and other economists say such a rise would be symptomatic of a positive trend: a strongereconomyproducing jobs, higher wages and rising corporate profits."Consumers should not wait for interest rates to drop back down to the rates of last year," said Ms. Reaser of Banc of America. "If they see a house they like, they should go ahead and take advantage of mortgage rates around the 7 percent level."Recent governmenteconomicreports suggest a stronger-than-expected recovery. But with the extraordinary conditions following Sept. 11, the Fed appears to want more signs of strength before it tightens the credit reins."We have come out of a scary period," said Jim Glassman, an economist at J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. "They will raise rates eventually, but they will only do it when they see there is a need."The government reported that theeconomygrew at an annual rate of 1.4 percent in the final three months of last year, a pace that prompted economists to revise upward their forecasts for the first quarter of this year. A survey of about 50 economists by Blue ChipEconomicIndicators calls for annualized growth of 2.6 percent for the January-March period.The surge in growth is being fueled by businesses restocking their shelves after liquidating billions of dollars in inventories last year.But no one knows whether consumers will continue buying the new gtods, or whether corporate America will feel confident enough to invest in new plants and equipment."Will we get this initial bounce from inventories and then stall out?" said David Wyss, chief economist at Standard & Ptor's Inc.Mr. Wyss is of the view that the recovery is probably solid. Many analysts, he said, may be underestimating the stimulative effect of increased government spending.And Mr. Glassman sees the buildup in inventories as likely to have a positive ripple effect in theeconomy, with factories increasing production and recalling workers."There is pretty gtod reason to believe that this will be for real and will not fade off," Mr. Glassman said.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (STEPHEN J. content_for_eval: In 1980, while seeking the Republican presidential nomination, George Bush denounced Ronald Reagan's proposed "supply-side" tax cuts as "vtodto economics." Now Bush is proposing major tax cuts of his own; critics call this "Vtodto II." One of Bush's main budget proposals is to cut taxes radically on capital gains -- profits from the sale of certain assets, like stocks, bonds and houses. Like Reagan in 1980, Bush contends that this tax cut would spur the economy, promote prosperity, create jobs, improve is.S. competitiveness and generate extra revenue to help close budget deficits. Critics say cutting the capital gains tax would hand a money windfall to the rich. The money would not trickle down to spur the economy, they say, but the tax cut would reduce federal revenues, worsening budget deficits. Bush "has demonstrated that he understands the mathematics of vtodto economics," said Sen. Bill Bradley, D-N.J., the intellectual godfather of the 1986 tax reform. "If anyone really believed that cutting the tax rates would increase revenues, they'd cut them to almost nothing and eliminate the deficit." Economists disagree over those conflicting contentions. Harvard experts have produced rival studies. Most analyses, however, favor Bush's critics. isndermine overhaul? Critics also say Bush's tax cuts would undermine the massive 1986 tax overhaul, widely hailed as a rare bipartisan triumph of fairness and economic efficiency. Capital gains are now taxed up to 33 percent of their full value, the same as income from labor or any other soaree. Bush would cut the rate to 15 percent, while excluding 45 percent of the assets' value from any tax. That would push Bush's effective capital gains tax rate as low as 8.25 percent, Treasury documents show. Meanwhile, tax rates for ordinary income would remain at 15, 28 and 33 percent brackets. Bush's defense rests largely on a Treasury study that concluded that Bush's capital gains tax cut would btost federal revenues by $16.1 billion over five years. This Treasury study is suspect, however, because it contradicts a 1986 Treasury study, as The Wall Street Journal reported. The 1986 study concluded that revenues would rise by $21.8 billion over five years, not by cutting the capital gains tax, but by raising it. The most authoritative analysis was issued last March by the respected, nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office. The CBO concluded that cutting the capital gains tax to 15 percent probably would increase federal deficits by $4 billion to $8 billion a year. Yet "the range of uncertainty" is so pervasive that "one cannot reject the possibility that a 15 percent rate might increase revenues," the CBO said. Richest would gain Its most striking conclusion, however, was that the wealthiest 1 percent of Americans, those with annual incomes above $100,000, would reap most of the gain. The wealthiest 1 percent raked in 55 percent of profits from capital gains from 1982 to 1985, the CBO said. Such a tax break would cut Bush's own federal taxes by $22,000 a year, Democratic presidential nominee Michael Dukakis contended in October. Bush's campaign dismissed the charge as trivial but never denied it. Bush reported capital gains totaling $515,132 from 1985 to 1987. Bush's capital gains cut is the centerpiece of several tax breaks he proposes. He also would cut corporate taxes for expenses on research, oil and gas exploration and investment in ghetto enterprise zones. He would give low-income parents a tax break for child-care expenses. Together, those tax breaks would deepen federal deficits by $16.6 billion by 1993, according to the Treasury Department. The essential logic of the 1986 tax overhaul was to lower tax rates on all income, making up the resultant revenue loss by closing tax ltopholes for special interests. The 1986 overhaul, which became fully effective Jan. 1, closed tax shelters worth about $300 billion over five years. The overhaul was intended to free investors to put their money into productive assets rather than seek ways to shelter it from taxes. Over time, economists believe that should make the economy more efficient. Essential trade-off Eliminating the tax break for capital gains was an essential trade-off, economically and politically, to achieve the 1986 overhaul. That lowered the tax rate for the wealthiest Americans to 28 percent from 50 percent without significantly reducing the total taxes they paid. Now Bush proposes to cancel the trade-off, keeping the low rates while giving back a big tax break on capital gains. Bush's proposed cut faces an uphill fight in Congress. Sen. Lloyd Bentsen, D-Texas, the chairman of the tax-writing Senate Finance Committee, says he would consider cutting the capital gains tax only in combination with higher taxes on the wealthy, a condition Bush rules out. Rep. Dan Rostenkowski, D-Ill., the chairman of the tax- writing House Ways and Means Committee, considers Bush's capital gains proposal "the beginning of the unraveling" of the 1986 tax overhaul. He vows to oppose it. "I'm not about to tell the wage earners in Chicago that they should pay a higher tax rate than the stockbrokers," Rostenkowski said in a recent speech. CAPITAL GAINS TAX PROPOSAL: President Bush wants to cut the capital gains tax rate from 33 to 15 percent, while excluding 45 percent of the asset's value from any tax. He says this will spur the economy. CRITICISM: Critics contend that cutting the capital gains tax would hand a windfall to the rich.
