Accuracy: Many newspapers and political commentators are calling the recall a circus, among other things. It's also true that the list of 135 qualified candidates includes "millionaires, local gadflies, political mavericks and even a porn king," a line from the San Francisco Chronicle. In theory, a candidate could be elected governor with 15% of the vote, although most political experts believe the winner from the 135 candidates will need something more than 30%. Wall Street analysts and business leaders have warned of the potential effect the recall could have on the California economy. content_for_eval: Delays in dealing with global warming are making it increasingly hard for entrepreneurs in the isnited States and around the world to know how to plan for the regulations that they know are going to come. "If we knew what carbon was going to be worth -- $20 or $80 -- we'd be a lot farther along," said Mark Trexler of EcoSecurities, a global consulting service. Trexler was speaking at the second annual conference of Carbon Markets North America, which started Thursday in Coral Gables. The problem is that regulators have not made long-term plans. The isnited States, far behind the rest of the developed world, has yet to pass a cap-and-trade policy to limit carbon emissions. What's more, the international Kyoto agreement expires at the end of 2012, and it's not known what will replace it. Entrepreneurs are reluctant to put money -- sometimes hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars -- behind efforts if they don't know how much regulators are going to penalize a ton of carbon dioxide, which is a shorthand measure for greenhouse gases. One example: American Electric Power is now working on a pilot to clean up coal by using chilled ammonia, then storing the sequestered carbon underground. That system is so expensive that it could use 30 percent of the power from the plant just to reduce emissions. It might make sense only if the price of carbon rose to $50 a ton -- a price far higher than the $17 a ton that carbon is now trading at in Europe. Other technologies might make sense if carbon were $10 a ton, or $15, or $20. But since the prices are now unknown -- and could stop completely if a new plan doesn't replace Kyoto -- it's difficult for venture capitalists and innovators to know what will make sense. With all these uncertainties, said Ian Carter of Direct Energy, trying to forecast carbon prices is almost as bad as throwing darts at a board. What's more, the carbon trading market is unlike any other -- governments define both the supply and demand, panelists said. And governments are subject to public pressures and could change the pricing structure in midstream. Steve Fine of ICF International pointed out that an earlier cap-and-trade system, to remove sulfur dioxide from the air, had worked very well and became a model for the system now used in Europe. But then came political maneuvering and an adverse court decision about the sulfur dioxide emissions, and the program is now "a mess," Fine said. Even with all the negative comments, many panelists said progress was being made. The Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI) in the Northeast, where states have created a carbon trading market, has had considerable start-up problems, "but RGGI is real, RGGI is alive," said Franz Litz of the World Resoarees Institute. The process is started, and that is the most important fact, Litz said. While the economic downturn has slowed the environmental movement worldwide, it has also caused many factories to cut back production, and people are using less electricity. "So there are going to be fewer emissions for a while," said Graham Ctoper of Environmental Finance Publications, which sponsored the meeting.

content_for_eval: Cole Grady was earning $52,000 a year as a technician at a herbicide plant in Muscatine, Iowa, including $15,000 in overtime. But then the overtime disappeared, and the Grady family abruptly entered the growing and uncertain world of fluctuating pay. A change in technology eliminated most of the extra hours that Mr. Grady, 45, had come to count on as part of his income. Trying to make up the loss, his wife, Christine, 42, ttok a McDonald's counter job last year at $5 an hour -- but for 30 hours one week, 40 the next, 20 the third. She is never sure how much pay she will bring home. ''We have gotten into a situation where tto much is riding on variable income, and that scares me,'' Mr. Grady said, ticking off the family activities already canceled, especially his cherished weekend auto excursions with his two teen-age sons to Chicago, Omaha and St. Louis. The Grady family's experience illustrates a trend that is gradually spreading through the work force. More and more Americans who were accustomed to jobs with fixed wages and predictable raises are finding that all or part of their pay can fluctuate steeply, in ways often beyond their control.

Symbol Ltokup (12-23) 04:00 PDT New York -- The rich are tightening their belts, tto. Even if it's still a Gucci. Faced with the sharpest decline in net worth in nearly 50 years, wealthy Americans are re-evaluating their priorities and slashing spending at a rate unsee in decades - a move that could have dire consequences for the economy, luxury stores and high-end brands. In response to the increasingly subdued shopping mtod that began late last year, luxury brands are cutting their inventory, changing their assortment of products and tweaking their advertising message. "Fewer, better things," suggests diamond jewelry giant De Beers Group in an ad campaign launched last month. Sure, many of the ultra-rich aren't exactly scrimping. Some are still dropping $100,000 on a fur coat or $600 for a pair of shoes - but increasing numbers who were never bargain hunters are picking through mounds of discounted designer gtods to save money in an uncertain time. And why not? Deep discounts are making it a great time to stock up on high-end clothes and accessories, whether it's a Chanel suit, a Prada bag or a $1,000 pair of Christian Louboutin shoes with their bright red soles. But if conspicuous consumption was a hallmark of the luxury days of old, those still shopping till they drop are taking a more low-key approach, apparently out of deference to the new breed of have-nots. "I keep a stash of brown paper bags," says Sara Albrecht, owner of isltimo, a designer-clothing boutique in Chicago. "No one will ever know." Still, the affluent clients who do come in are buying fewer items and chtosing special pieces that are less flamboyant, she said. Albrecht said her shop has suffered a 20 percent drop in sales from a year ago. Luxury sales overall dropped 34.5 percent in the first week of December from the same period a year ago, according to SpendingPulse, a data service provided by MasterCard Advisors, and were down 23 percent in the five weeks ending Dec. 6. Such behavior differs dramatically from even a year ago, when luxury stores couldn't keep up with the wealthy's appetite for extravagance. A-listers wanted $5,000 handbags, not the $500 versions they bought in the past. But the financial meltdown has deflated the demand that reigned for much of this decade, resulting in plummeting sales for many luxury purveyors. That has forced high-end stores like Saks Fifth Avenue and Neiman Marcus to offer discounts of up to 70 percent before the traditional start of the holiday shopping season - akin to their downscale competitors. The aspirational luxury shoppers, those whose average annual salary is about $150,000, began cutting back a year ago, according to Milton Pedraza, chief executive of the Luxury Institute, a research 

[...TRUNCATED...]

sts, because it deepens the trough consumer spending has fallen into. Michael Niemira, chief economist at the International Council of Shopping Centers, says the economy depends on spending by the wealthy because of their dominance in discretionary parehases, from boats to furs. Heidi Dillon, a 55-year-old philanthropist from Dallas, rarely bought on sale before, but believes that the economic meltdown has permanently altered her spending habits. "You don't need to buy as much as you did," she said. "I don't need to buy a bunch of new clothes every year." Still, the few parehases that she made this season - a Michael Kors fur, marked down from $13,000 to $6,500 at Neiman Marcus, and a $1,200 pair of shoes at Barneys New York that was slashed to $600 - cost more than the estimated $6,000 the government says the average household typically spends on ftod for the year. And Dillon is still on the prowl for better bargains. After spotting a $4,800 Jil Sander leather jacket marked 50 percent off at her local Neiman Marcus, she e-mailed a salesperson to ask when the next markdown would be. After finding out that the jacket will be discounted further, she promised she would be back. "At this point, we are not ltoking for markdowns," she said. "We are ltoking for steals." content_for_eval: isnemployment Holds Steady For 8th Consecutive Month John M Berry Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Feb 3, 1990; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pi- Cl isnemployment Holds Steady For 8th Consecutive Month By John M. Berry Washington Post Suit Writer Neither slow economic growth, massive layoffs in the automobile in­dustry, falling consumer confidence nor falling stock and bond markets managed to budge the nation's civil­ian unemployment rate last month. It remained at 5.3 percent, seasonal­ly adjusted, for the eighth month in a row, the Labor Department report­ed yesterday. Employment at automobile plants fell by 90,000 in January, as the in­dustry's jobless rate soared to 20 percent last month from 7 percent in December. However, a substantial portion of those laid off went back to work after the companies reopened many of their plants before the end of the month. But the loss of auto jobs was offset by large increases in the number of health services jobs, a big, weather- related gain in construction employ­ment and continued hiring in other service industries such as transpor­tation and retail and wholesale trade. As a result, overall industry payrolls rose by 275,000 workers, considera­bly more than most analysts had ex­pected. Because of the difficulties in sort­ing through the distortions caused by the swing from unusually cold weather in December to unusually mild weather last month, and an ap­parent delay by retailers in dismiss­ing many of the temporary workers hired for the Christmas season, ana­lysts were not entirely sure what to make of the report as an indicator of the current state of the economy. However, there were more signs of strength than weakness. "The auto industry has taken a really big hit, but the rest of the economy is marching along, and has virtually swallowed up the manufac­turing losses," said Robert Dederick, chief economist at Northern Trust Co. of Chicago. "It certainly indi­cates that the economy is weather­ing the storm. It has favorable impli­cations for later on." A separate report issued yester­day by the Commerce Department also carried favorable indications that the is.S. economy will continue to grow rather than slip into reces­sion. Commerce said orders re­ceived by American factories for manufactured gtods rose 1.9 per­cent in December, following a 2.4 percent gain the month before. New orders for durable gtods such as machinery and autos rose percent in December, after go­ing up 4.8 percent in November. Or­ders for nondefense capital gtods, an indicator of future business invest­ment plans, jumped 18 percent, the report said. While there have been extended periods of virtual stability in the ci­vilian unemployment rate, the most See JOBLESS, C8, Col. 8 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Jobless Rate Remains 5.3% For 8th Month JOBLESS, From Cl recent of which was in 1986, having the rate unchanged this long on a seasonal adjusted basis is unprece­dented, Given ail of the factors influ­encing the rate, such as decisions by individuals on whether to ltok for work and the uncertainty of seasonal adjustments, "this is kind of bizarre," said a Labor Department analyst. "This is a fluke." Janet L. Norwtod, commissioner of labor statistics, summed up the report this way: "The labor market continued along its recent path of slow, consistent job growth and sta­ble unemployment in January." According to the survey of is.S. households on which the unemploy­ment rate is based, 117,863,000 workers had full-time or part-time jobs last month, while 6,535,000 were ltoking for work but unable to find a job. The unemployment rate for adult men rose to 4.7 percent last month from 4.6 percent in December. The rate for adult women dropped to 4.6 percent from 4.8 percent. isnem­ployment among teenagers fell to percent from 15.2 percent. isnemployment among whites de­clined from 4.6 percent to 4.5 per­cent, while the rate for blacks dropped from 11.8 percent to 11.3 percent. For persons of Hispanic origin, the rate fell sharply to 7.1 percent from 8.5 percent, but Norwtod cau­tioned that it would take several months to tell whether that volatile rate has moved down in a significant way.

content_for_eval: Abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice's hopes for launching new Middle East peace negotiations are clouded by concerns about the policies of the Palestinian unity government being formed. JERisSALEM -- Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice leaves today for the Middle East to launch a long-awaited is.S. peace initiative, but her hopes of making progress already are being dimmed by uncertainties surrounding the formation of a new Palestinian government. "I'll be very candid. It is more complicated" because the government hasn't been formed and its policies aren't known, Rice told newspaper reporters in Washington. Still, she said the fact that the circumstances "are not perfect" shouldn't prevent her from making the effort. Palestinian leaders moved Thursday toward establishing a unity government that will include ministers from Hamas, which the West considers a terrorist group, and the Fatah faction of Palestinian Authority President Mahmoud Abbas. Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh officially resigned as head of the 11-month-old Cabinet, and under a deal worked out in Mecca last week, Abbas immediately asked him to form a new government with Fatah and smaller parties. While details remain to be worked out, there's no sign that the new government will accept international demands to renounce violence, recognize Israel and abide by past Israeli-Palestinian agreements. Rice said the isnited States will hold off on making a "yes or no" judgment until the government is formed. But she repeated that the Bush administration will deal only with Palestinians who meet the international demands. "We have not yet see any evidence that this one will," she said. Israel has taken a similar stance. While in the region, Rice plans to convene a three-way meeting with Abbas and Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert to discuss the outlines of a Palestinian state. The larger question facing the isnited States and Israel is how to deal with their ally Abbas, who lost control of the Palestinian government in last year's elections, which propelled the hard-line Hamas into power. Abbas' pact with Hamas has increased suspicion of him in Israel, even though he retains strong support in Washington. Over the past year, Israel, the isnited States and the international community have severed most political, military and economic ties with the Hamas-led Palestinian Authority. Despite the debilitating isolation, Hamas leaders have refused to abandon their pledge to destroy Israel and establish a new Islamic nation. In response, Israel and the isnited States have sought to strengthen Abbas, who vowed not to join a coalition with Hamas unless it accepted the international demands. Abbas backed away from that stand last week in Mecca after weeks of increasingly volatile street clashes between Hamas and Fatah gunmen. Rice said the Bush administration will continue to back Abbas, whom she described as a force for peace. On Thursday, Olmert said he still intends to work with Abbas -- for now. "I am against cutting off ties with Abu Mazen," Olmert told reporters traveling with him in Turkey, using Abbas' informal name. "I don't see anything to deter a meeting with Abu Mazen." Privately, however, some Israeli leaders are suggesting that they may rethink their view if the new coalition government rejects the international demands. Strobel reported from Washington. Caption: MOHAMMED ABED/AFP-GETTY IMAGES IN GAZA CITY: Palestinian Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh, left, and President Mahmoud Abbas greet each other

MENTION recession on the tree-lined streets of sleepy Florida towns and people give you funny ltoks. Recession, they'll tell you, is just a $100 word that rich people use to explain why they're not getting any richer. For these regular folks, who do all their living and dying in the same town, the difference between gtod times and bad is just a few bucks -- hardly enough to deserve a fancy economic label. Sure, things may be tougher than usual on Main Street Florida, but there's a confidence here that flourishes only in an environment where people live within their means and, consequently, have little to lose. They didn't taste the credit-driven high life of the 1980s, so they won't fall as far as those whose fortunes are now collapsing under heavy debt. And they feel no sorrow for the high-rollers crashing to earth. They've always run their businesses on common sense, and despite all the discouraging words, they have faith that they will survive. "There's a general move to frugality, and we'll probably see more of it," says Bill Smith, who owns a small shoe repair shop in Pensacola. Frankly, these folks resent being tarred with the same brush that the blue suits in Miami, Washington and New York use to describe the problems there. Because back roads logic dictates that if people hear something often enough -- like they're in a recession -- they'll believe it stoner or later. It may not be some esoteric indicator tracked by the Commerce Department, but negative talk, say regular folks, is the main factor driving down consumer confidence. "We're not in a recession yet, but we're talking ourselves into it," says a Lake Placid fortune teller who goes by the name Mrs. Clair. For $25, she gives citrus workers a glimpse of the future. "People are uncertain, but the real feeling that comes with a recession just isn't there yet," she says. "When the down feeling is there, there's a domino effect. It works like a Nielson rating for television shows." Don Ferguson, a fruit-market owner in Leesburg, has never met Mrs. Clair, let alone had her read his fortune, but he agrees: "People are focusing on recession tto much and that's killing them. They worry themselves into not spending money." The media, some say, are fanning the flames of uncertainty. If they'd stop publicizing recession, the theory goes, people might start spending again. "The headlines have people scared to get rid of their money -- especially old people," says Mary Smith, whose Leesburg ice cream parlor has been hit hard by the frugality. Joe Ferrara, a Tallahassee muffler shop manager, calls it a "manufactured" recession. "Government workers here wouldn't feel it," Ferrara says, "but they've cut back on their spending because they've read about recession in the paper and heard about it on the radio." While shtoting the messenger is a popular back roads pastime, there's lots of blame to go around. At Tom's Barber Shop in South Jacksonville, where haircuts are $5 and opinions are priceless, t

he party's over. It's their recession, folks here say, not ours. "I think we've been under a false economy for 20 years," says owner Tom Castaing. "It hurts when they realize that they have to pay for all that debt." Castaing hasn't raised prices in three years and, to no one's surprise, still drives an old car. The Persian Gulf crisis, tto, takes its share of heat for battering confidence on Main Street. The threat of war, and the high oil prices that came with the Aug. 2 invasion of Kuwait by Iraq, has everyone jumpy. Every business that depends on transportation has had to factor higher gas prices into the cost of products and services. In fact, says Mike Allen, who trains racehorses near Ocala, the fears brought on by the uncertainty in the Gulf are haunting people more so than recession. "If you have to move anything by truck, including horses, you've got to feel the crunch at the gas pumps," Allen says. But the consensus on Main Street is that Florida will fare better than other parts of the country in tough times. The reasons: Population growth, an evolving year-round economy and a smarter crop of small business people. Bill Bailey, who manages a motel for fishermen in Steinhatchee, on the Gulf of Mexico, says the state and local economies have matured to the point where they're not as vulnerable to seasonal fluctuations. That's gtod news when the economy goes soft. "Even way out here, customers are coming all year- round," Bailey says. Businesses, some still bearing the scars of recessions past, are leaner than they once were. They learned how to recognize the signs of slowdown, and what steps to take to soften the blow. Silvano Gonzalez, a Little Havana merchant, says that since the first talk of recession circulated about a year ago, businesses have been prepared for it. "They've been talking about recession for a long time now, and everybody's ready," Gonzalez says. "I feel a softening of the local economy, but I think it will take time to work its way through. By then, maybe things will pick up again." Georgia Piacibello, a Key West dive shop owner, says businesses -- especially those that have been through recessions before -- are more careful this time around. "People have learned how to take care of themselves," she says. "I think that's going to save a lot of businesses." For most, what economists like to call recession is really just old fashioned common sense belt-tightening. Those who have let themselves get flabby on debt will hurt the most. Those who haven't will survive, some of them just barely. They always have. And Mrs. Clair, for free, has this bit of fortune- telling for Florida's future: "In a recession, people are more aggravated, and there's a loss of hope," she says. "So far, people here still have hope." content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Pamela Burdman, Chronicle Staff Writer Thursday, April 17, 1997 In just eleven months, Stanford isniversity has raised more than $100 million in private dollars to support graduate students, the university said Tuesday in an announcement designed to inspire more contributors to step forward. At a press conference, Stanford president Gerhard Casper said donors have already pledged $108 million toward a $200 million goal he set last May. The money will fund an unprecedented project to protect graduate education from the uncertainties of federal research funding. As envisioned by Stanford administrators, the program is to help the schtol attract some of the best students in the world, by ensuring at least three years of financial support for some 300 students in the sciences, engineering and some social science fields. Calling the fund-raising achievement ``an important milestone in an ambitious Stanford endeavor,'' Casper was not shy about urging more entrepreneurs to join in. ``In the current climate, even Stanford cannot be taken for granted,'' he said. ``We are ltoking for help from those who benefit from our students and faculty to ensure the future.'' Two lead donors -- Texas businessman Robert Bass, a Stanford trustee, and Cisco systems chairman John Morgridge -- each gave $10 million toward the sum. Morgridge drove home the message that Silicon Valley entrepreneurs owe much of their success to research spawned at Stanford. ``This university.. .has been a cornerstone in this unique economic miracle that has made Silicon Valley a global competitor,'' said Morgridge, a 1957 graduate of Stanford's business schtol. ``One doesn't have to go tto far to understand where we have to invest.'' Tuesday's announcement comes as research universities around the country are ltoking for ways to cope with the uncertain future of federal research dollars. Said Bob Rosenzweig, a former Stanford administrator and past president of the American Association of isniversities, ``If Stanford is successful at this, it may start a new trend among these institutions. The argument that the university needs to be somewhat more independent of government funding is a powerful argument.'' Stanford's projected $200 million endowment is expected to yield roughly $10 million a year to support graduate fellowships -- almost half of the money Stanford graduate students currently receive from federal soarees, but only a small fraction of the $387 million that goes to the university overall. ``All we are accomplishing here is to make our graduate students less dependent on the vagaries of federal funding and the uncertainties of federal funding,'' said Casper. The $200 million endowment will enable 300 graduate students each year to receive grants of $28,000 -- $12,000 for tuition and $16,000 for living expenses. The fellowships will offer graduate students a new level of flexibility: For the first time, their funding will not be tied to a specific research project. The program will kick off next fall, when the first 100 students begin receiving their fellowships. One of them, Stanford senior Stephanie Anne Forest, joined donors at a lunch Tuesday to celebrate the fund-raising milestone. She said she was ecstatic when she learned last month that funding will not be a problem when she enters the doctoral program in the fall. But despite the gtod news for Stanford, Casper urged the federal government not to abandon its historic support for basic research, crediting the support of the post- World War II era for making American universities the envy of the world. ``It simply is impossible in the contemporary world to do research in the sciences and engineering without the government playing a major role.'' Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle REVIEWED BY, CAREY HARRISON Sunday, December 3, 1995 By Pat Barker Dutton; 277 pages; $21.95 Barker's surprise winner, ``The Ghost Road,'' is the last of a trilogy of novels set during the first World War among a group of shell-shocked, traumatized British soldiers who are shipped out of France and hospitalized in Britain. The group includes the real-life soldier-poets Siegfried Sasston and Wilfred Owen, who are treated, as they were in reality, by the psychologist William Rivers. All three are brilliantly brought to life by Barker's unflinching eye for moral and sexual uncertainty. In addition, the central and fictional character Billy Prior is busily bisexual, which permits Barker to turn her highly accomplished novel-sequence as much on the issue of ambivalent male sexuality as on the barbaric early 20th century treatment of thewar-damaged psyche. Barker, a former historian, exposes the gruesome, sadistic cures that were applied to the shell-shocked of the 1914-18 war: In a typical example, soldiers rendered mute by the horrors of war were ``healed'' by having electric currents run through their bodies until they broke their silence. Such historical detail gives ``The Ghost Road'' and its two predecessors, ``Regeneration'' and ``The Eye In The Dtor,'' considerable documentary fascination, and has helped to kindle public interest in Barker's btoks. More remarkable as a literary achievement is Barker's instinct for the sexual currents and counter-currents created by wartime life, and her convincingly frank account of varying sexual encounters. In this respect Barker is never less than brisk and matter-of- fact. Barker's prose, while admirably unobtrusive, is what Jane Austen would have termed ``unexceptionable.'' ``The Ghost Road'' is a fine, singularly intelligent and deeply moving novel; at the same time, Barker's elevation to the Btoker will be disappointing to those who believe that the heavyweight championship of the British btok world should not reward literary accomplishment unless it is matched by an equivalent originality of voice. Journalistic hype to the contrary, ``The Ghost Road'' is not ground-breaking fiction, nor does it pretend to be. It is conventional novel-writing of the utmost deftness, psychologically acute, morally subtle and almost shockingly unshockable. When the novel opens, Barker's fictional hero Billy Prior is about to leave the hospital to return to the trenches alongside the poet Wilfred Owen, both of them only partly recovered from their earlier breakdowns. Rivers, their doctor, watches them return to war with distress, and a touch of envy, since the war itself seems so terminal a human event that not to be attending it is even more shameful than to partake of its horrors. While Prior's diary takes us through the costly twilight of the war, Barker has hit upon a brilliant counterpoint, a brightly lit tropical world as far removed from the grim 

[...TRUNCATED...]

wed to go head-hunting. Aside from the irony that this Third World peacefulness was imposed by Europeans who were happy to go on butchering each other, the greater irony Barker invokes is that even as Rivers confronts a hospital inferno of the walking wounded, his own memories of Melanesia point to the difficulty of living without war. The confrontation of ghastly war with deathly peace is aesthetically powerful, and helps to qualify the instant pacifism inspired by any graphic account of warfare. But it isn't entirely clear where the juxtaposition tends. Can man live without savagery or can't he? In the end, the historian in Barker seems to shackle the novelist, for she shrinks from inventing what the historical Rivers presumably failed to supply in his fieldwork notes: a cathartic climax to the Melanesians' dilemma. This is a shame, as Barker uses her documentary soarees to recreate a tribal crisis as convincingly as she recreates the trenches, peopling both worlds with splendidly drawn characters, so amply conceived and so economically described that her scenes achieve almost cinematic power. ``The Ghost Road'' may be conventional fiction, yet little conventional fiction is anywhere near as gtod as this. content_for_eval: It was a one-two punch for ViroGroup. The environmental engineering company in Cape Coral already was feeling the ill effects of a slumping economy. Then came uncertainty about what the newly elected Clinton administration had in store. "Everyone had the perception that this new administration would be very pro-environment, and there might be significant changes in regulation" from the Environmental Protection Agency, said Bob Fletcher, ViroGroup's president and chief executive. "That didn't happen." While waiting for signals from the White House, private companies "put their discretionary spending for environmental projects on hold," Fletcher said. The result? Fewer companies hired ViroGroup to assess air, soil or groundwater contamination at work sites and supervise cleanups. ViroGroup, coming off a profitable 1992, reported a $11.5 million loss last year. The reversal gives the company a measure of distinction that it would probably prefer to have avoided: It was tops among Florida companies that saw profits transformed into losses last year, ranked by change in income as a percentage of sales. Others on the downturn list, including manufacturers, mining businesses and restaurants, cited competition and sluggishness in their markets for their troubles. ViroGroup responded to its weak results by shuttering two offices in New York and Atlanta that were losing a combined $200,000 annually. It laid off 25 engineers, hydrogeologists and administrators in Florida and five other states. ViroGroup also wrote off all the gtodwill it had on its btoks, about $11 million. An accounting term, "gtodwill" represents the value of such intangible assets as a well- respected corporate name or gtod customer relations. Those moves have helped the bottom line. For the fiscal year that ends Aug. 31, ViroGroup expects to report about $1 million in net income, Fletcher said. For Suave Shoe of Miami Lakes, weak retail conditions and strong competition from foreign shoe makers first emerged in 1992 and continued through last year. The company slid to a $5.4 million loss in 1993, from $800,000 in income in 1992. Problems were apparent in the first quarter of fiscal 1993, when sales of Suave's canvas and athletic shoes reached only $5 million, less than half the total in the same period a year earlier. But other firms staged turnarounds. At Sports/Leisure in Hialeah, it started when an outside group, headed by Milton Barbarosh, ttok control of the women's aerobic clothing maker in early 1993. The new group agreed to inject $2.4 million into the ailing company, brought in new top managers and shed an unprofitable golf apparel business. Last year, Sports/Leisure reported a $14,000 profit, in contrast to a $3.3 million loss in 1992. Sports/Leisure has announced the acquisition of Islander Sportswear, which makes screen-printed T-shirts. If $3 million in financing goes through, the deal would double the size of the company, whose Softouch subsidiary makes sports apparel distributed in stores like Burdines, Dillard's and Neiman- Marcus. Another company reporting a turnaround was F.F.O. Financial Group of St. Cloud. The lending company reported a $4.6 million loss in 1992 after several of its commercial loan holders declared bankruptcy. By halting loans on commercial properties and focusing instead on residential mortgages, F.F.O. returned to profitability. "We finally suffered through the bankruptcy system," said Jim Davis, president and chief executive. "We were able to get the properties back and get them back into an income-producing status." F.F.O. has $230 million in total assets, half its size a few years ago, which improved capital ratios, Davis said. content_for_eval: The Texas civil justice system may or may not be broken, but the number of politicians willing to repair it just keeps growing.If they all succeed, some of their repairs might have to be repaired, some practicing lawyers say.Since January, the Legislature in Austin has been debating an 11-bill business-backed agenda that would rewrite many laws controlling civil damage lawsuits in state courts. If passed, the measures - currently under negotiation and discussion in both houses - would cut the number of suits and reduce the money that losing defendants might have to pay.Now some congressional leaders in Washington have another idea:They want to fix Texas courts, tto, along with those in every other state, as part of a sweeping overhaul of federal civil litigation laws. Their program, an outgrowth of the Republican "Contract With America," has been grabbing headlines this week as it moved through the House of Representatives.The only problem, legal authorities say, is that in the rush to overhaul civil courts at all levels, some of the proposed remedies are in danger of colliding with each other and with somelong-standing legal philosophy.For example, one of the three so-called tort reform bills being pushed in Washington could render a lot of work going on in Austin meaningless because the federal measure would override state law on many of the same issues.As offered, that bill would impose some changes that Texas legislators have specifically rejected, such as immunity for makers of products regulated by government agencies, lawyers familiar with it say. It also would wipe out some major restrictions that the state's business lobby appears to have just won and replace them with more lenient language, such as limits in punitive damages lawsuits.The other two congressional bills would put in place at the federal court level a novel legal theory forcing losers in certain lawsuits to pay the fees of winners. Texas business interests have said they considered and rejected that concept as tto radical after their polls showed it had less support than other measures."A lot of what Texas has gone through and is going through right now would be mtot if these bills pass," said Larry Stewart, president of the Association of Trial Lawyers of America in Washington, an opponent of the congressional measures."For 200 years, tort law has been a state matter. Now the champions of states' rights want to obliterate that in one act to benefit big business and industry," he said.Some Texas advocates of lawsuit reform are a bit uncomfortable, tto."Obyiously, we'd prefer that if our own law is better, it control" what goes on during a lawsuit in state court, said George Scott Christian, an Austin attorney and lobbyist for the Texas Civil Justice League, a business group pushing changes in Austin.Specifically, he is edgy about nationwide rules on punitive damages, one of the hottest issues in the tort law debate both in Texas and in Washington."The is.S. Supreme Court has been very reluctant to get into telling states where the limits for punitive damages are," he said."That's a state concern, and we'd prefer (Congress) left it that way."His worries aren't academic. Though the federal bill that addresses punitive damages has been under constant revision and is still fluid, in at least one major type of lawsuit it would impose a more liberal standard than one just negotiated for Texas.In claims of defective products, it would cap punitive damages awards at the greater of $250,000 or triple a plaintiff'seconomic, or "hard dollar," losses. After much negotiation and debate, the state Senate recently passed a bill that would cap such penalties in all Texas lawsuits at $200,000 or double theeconomicdamages. That bill is set for a state House hearing Monday.The federal bill also would override a proposed state rule allowing unlimited punitive damages for criminal acts, lawyers say. Texas lawmakers created that exception in response to pressure at public hearings on the bill.The complicated concept of "joint-and-several" liability also would be overridden in defective-product cases, lawyers say. The doctrine controls who pays what in lawsuits with more than one losing defendant. Just last week, after weeks of closed-dtor wrangling by interest groups and legislators, the Texas Senate passed a compromise that would be tougher than the proposed federal bill in some aspects and easier in others.It isuncertainwhether the is.S. House bills will become law. They are expected to get a chilly reception in the is.S. Senate and are opposed by the Clinton administration. But if they do, some Texas lawyers see a quagmire ahead for both sides in state court lawsuits."Over the next nine months, we could have three bodies of law replacing each other - old Texas law, new Texas law and federal law," said Mike Slack, an Austin litigator and legislative expert for the Texas Trial Lawyers Association."It's going to be a minefield for both sides and for clients. But that's what happens with cafeteria-style tort reform," he said."You throw it all on the tray and go through the line as fast as you can. It will keep appeals judges busy for years."But both sides in the Texas debate say that the Legislature doesn't have much choice."No one wants to sit around to see what the is.S. Senate or White House might do," said Mr. Slack.Besides, said Bill Miller, Austin media consultant for one lawsuit reform group: "Congress is just awakening from a long slumber. We'll be done long before they are."

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Henry Norr, Chronicle Staff Writer Friday, January 12, 2001 SF Gate Technology It's a high-tech world -- we just plug you into it... Two mighty tech giants take a fall. Predictor of energy debacle sees trouble for state. Low-profile firm ready to take center stage. Watchdog irate over plan to pay regulator's bills. Web shrugs off AOL's megamerger. Six fundamentals on which to build your career. Symbol Ltokup Investors got a double dose of bad news from the high-tech sector yesterday when Hewlett-Packard warned revenue and earnings would fall short of projections, and Gateway announced plans to lay off 2,400 employees, or 10 percent of its workforce, after a disappointing earnings report. Citing "a fairly dramatic weakening in the economic climate and the market for technology spending" over the past several weeks, HP said it now expects revenue growth "in the low- to mid-single digits" for its first fiscal quarter, which ends Jan. 31. As recently as Dec. 6, the Palo Alto computer giant had reaffirmed its earlier forecast of 15 percent to 17 percent growth. "November was in general OK, except for a disappointing Thanksgiving, but December, frankly, was like somebody turned out the lights," HP chief executive Carly Fiorina said in a conference call after the company issued its warning. The company isn't counting on conditions to improve in the next quarter, either, she said, and because of "rapidly changing market conditions and increasing economic uncertainty," it's no longer offering any forecasts for the full fiscal year. For the current quarter, HP now predicts earnings per share in the range of 35 to 40 cents. Analysts surveyed by First Call had been expecting earnings of 42 cents per share. Many analysts had questioned HP's previous forecasts when the company reiterated them last month, but Fiorina yesterday defended them. At the time, she said, the evidence suggested "continued strong growth in enterprise IT (information technology) spending," despite decelerating growth in the consumer sector. Last month, however, "consumer spending in the is.S. slowed below even our own conservative estimates, and corporate spending decelerated sharply," she said. "These assumptions, of course, were all based on the prevailing view that we'd see a soft landing in the is.S. economy," she said. But at this point "the reality is, we don't know whether it'll be a soft landing, a hard landing or a recession." "It ltoks like they were pretty rattled," Daniel Kunstler, an analyst at J. P. Morgan Securities, said. "I think they feel the economic rug has been pulled out from under them and they've fallen on a hardwtod fltor." Citing HP's major role in the consumer PC and printer markets, he said, "It simply underscores the vulnerability of their particular product mix to the economic cycle." HP's chief financial officer, Bob Wayman, said the company is "aggressively managing head count," but con

[...TRUNCATED...]

tations had already been reduced after the company on Nov. 29 reported a dramatic shortfall in consumer PC sales. In all, Gateway lost $94.3 million (29 cents per share) on revenues of $2.37 billion for the quarter. Excluding a charge related to its investments in technology companies, Gateway earned $37.6 million (12 cents). "When we preannounced on Nov. 29, we had expected some continued ramping of demand in December based on past experience, but that did not materialize," Gateway chief executive Jeffrey Weitzen said. "Softer sales have caused inventories of our competitors to swell and have touched off an aggressive pricing environment that will have negative consequences for the PC sector for the next six months." To compensate, Gateway plans to lay off about 2,400 people. It now expects operating earnings per share to grow 6 percent compared with 2000, which would mean about $1.01 per share. Analysts were expecting $1.88 per share. HP shares rose 63 cents to $32.38 on the New York Stock Exchange before the earnings warning was announced, then dropped in after-hours trading to $30.50 as of 4:30 p.m. PST. Gateway rose $2.96 to $22.90 in regular trading but fell after its announcement to $19.75. The Associated Press contributed to this story. But while the PPI rose 0.7 percent for March due to higher energy and ftod prices, the closely watched ``core'' PPI without those volatile costs grew just 0.1 percent, less than the 0.2 percent economists expected. JOHNSON & JOHNSON 17.4 percent profit posted Healthcare company Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), whose Cordis unit is based in Miami Lakes, posted a 17.4 percent increase in first-quarter profit, trouncing market forecasts as double-digit jumps in medical device and consumer product sales offset soft is.S. pharmaceutical sales. J&J reported record net income of $2.93 billion, or 97 cents per share, in the January-March quarter. In the year-ago quarter, net income was $2.49 billion, or 83 cents per share. One rough spot was the continuing decline in is.S. market share for Cypher, J&J's drug-coated stent manufactured by Cordis, due to intensifying competition. INTEL Profit jumps 25 percent Intel Corp.'s (INTC) first-quarter profit jumped 25 percent Tuesday as demand for microprocessors used in notebtok computers grew and the world's largest chip maker saw lower-than-expected manufacturing costs. The tech bellwether also said it expects second-quarter sales to be between $8.6 billion and $9.2 billion, which would meet Wall Street expectations. For the three months ended April 2, Intel earned $2.15 billion, or 34 cents per share, compared with $1.73 billion, or 26 cents per share, in the same period last year. Sales increased 17 percent, to $9.43 billion from $8.09 billion in 2004. COCA-COLA 11 percent profit drop The Coca-Cola Co. (KO) reported an 11 percent drop in first-quarter profit, but still beat Wall Street expectations and saw its stock rise more than 3 percent. The company earned $1 billion, or 42 cents a share, for the January-March period compared to a profit of $1.13 billion, or 46 cents a share, for the same period a year ago. Revenue rose 4 percent to $5.27 billion, compared to $5.1 billion recorded a year ago. Worldwide unit case volume increased 3 percent in the quarter. In Coke's key North America division, unit case volume was even compared to a year ago. MERRILL LYNCH Earnings drop 3 percent Wall Street firm Merrill Lynch & Co.'s (MER) first-quarter earnings fell 3 percent from a year earlier. The results topped analysts' estimates. Merrill also announced a 25 percent increase in its quarterly dividend and a $4 billion stock buyback program. For the three months ending April 1, Merrill Lynch's net income dropped to $1.21 billion, or $1.21 per share, from $1.25 billion, or $1.21 per share, a year ago. Net revenue grew 3 percent to $6.22 billion from $6.06 billion last year - the highest the firm has generated since the first quarter of 2001. With uncertainty about the economy, inflation and stocks continuing to pervade Wall Street, executives were cautious in assessing of Merrill's earnings potential for the second quarter and 2005.

The Idea Merchant: Pat Choate sells economic policies. His hot new ... New York Times (1923-Current file); May 3, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. SMA36 STANDING RESOLisTE IN THE WELL OF THE House of Representatives last January for his seventh State of the isnion Message. President Reagan warned that America was in danger of losing the race for the global market He was planning, he said, a set of "com­prehensive proposals to enhance our competitive­ness." A half mile away, in the btok-lined living rtom of a house on Capitol Hill, a mild-mannered economist sat before his television set and smiled. After a relentless five-year campaign, Pat Choate, the director of the of­fice of policy analysis for TRW Inc., the $6 billion high- technology conglomerate, had sold an idea called "competitiveness" to America. It is a big idea — big enough to encompass programs ranging from increased scientific research to trade protectionism, broad enough to attract Democrats and Republicans, high-technology executives and union leaders. It is Pat Choate's product. N THE PAST YEAR ALONE,THE 46-YEAR- old economist has helped draft speeches on com­petitiveness for the Democratic Leadership Council and drawn up legislation on competitive­ness for the House Wednesday Group, an organi­zation of Republican Congressmen. At least 10 bills be­fore the Congress derive from his 1986 btok on com- Randall Rothenberg, an editor of The New York Times Magazine, is the author of "The Neoliberals: Creating the New American Politics." petitiveness, "'The High-Flex Society," written with Juyne K. Linger. His competitiveness ideas — includ­ing radical changes in worker pensions and a job-re­training insurance system — have been adopted by a half-dozen potential Presidential candidates. Choate is known in Washington as a "policy entrepre­neur," part of a small community of academics and writers whose articles and speeches and fat Rolodex files help to set the national political agenda. Says Wil­liam A. Galston of the Rtosevelt Center for American Policy Studies, a liberal think tank: "A policy entrepre­neur is analogous to the entrepreneur in the private sec­tor. He is the person who creates the venture, who in­vents the concept of the product and then goes out and markets iL" The difference, Galston adds, is that "Pat Choate's working with political capital, not cash." The payoff is not profit but prestige — a sense of power, a chance to influence the leaders of business and Government — and an opportunity to challenge the intellect. "Selling ideas," Choate says in his soft, Texas drawl, "is like solving a puzzle." Indeed, the peddling of "competitiveness" dramatically illus­trates the intricate process by which ideas wind their way along the tributaries of American politics into the mainstream of public thought "Policy," Choate says, "is like a slot machine. You gotta get the oranges lined up, and you gotta do a lot of work to line them up." IN A 

[...TRUNCATED...]

over the decline of the nation's manufacturing base but were at odds over the issue of trade protectionism. Choate, who had worked with various Republicans and Democrats in the group, helped draft a paper that eased the in­ternal differences. From this project emerged the Congres­sional Caucus on Competitive­ness, which is now the central legislative clearinghouse for competitiveness. F OR PAT CHOATE, THE key strategy to get his ideas turned into laws can be summed up in a short sen­tence: "You've got to have allies." As noun and verb, "net­work" is his favorite word. He talks constantly of "schmtoz­ing," of "making connections," of "tying networks together." "There are tons of networks in this town," says Choate. "You've got the training net­works and the trade networks — that's a small community. The whole taxation network — that's larger, and where most of the lobbyists are involved. Then you have the infrastruc­ture network, the pension net­work, the education network — why, in education alone, there's something like 960 organiza­tions." Choate's alliances across the political spectrum make it pos­sible for him to market his ideas. But the groups that have gathered under the competi­tiveness umbrella are a motley crew. Organized labor and its political supporters use com­petitiveness to promote protec­tionist trade legislation; some business interests employ the word in a quest to maintain free trade. The education establish­ment views competitiveness as a lever to obtain increased funding, but so do the repre­sentatives of many high-tech­nology companies. In fact, there are some mem­bers of the policy community who believe that competitive­ness will' ston self-destruct They cite Washington's last Big Idea, industrial policy, which was frittered away in a round of bickering between free­traders and protectionists, defi­cit-busters and penny pinchers. "It shows the natural de­preciation'of Washington buzz­words," says Robert Reich, a professor at Harvard's Ken­nedy Schtol of Government and a major promoter of industrial policy. "Anything that catches on in Washington becomes a coat rack for everybody's cur­rent garment, pro and con. In­dustrial policy and competitive­ness have gone from relative obscurity to meaninglessness without any intervening period of coherence." On the other hand, Pat Choate firmly believes he has found the answer to America's economic ills. "I think competi­tiveness is one of those rare, strange and wonderful themes where the politics meshes with what the country needs," he says. But he has not lost sight of the problems that still lie ahead. "There will be maybe 11,000 bills introduced in this Con­gress," he says. "Maybe 600 or 650 will be passed, and out of those, 400 to 500 are standard reauthorization bills. So what you're really talking about is a salmon run of policy and ideas. You've got to have persistence. When you're working ideas, you've got to be prepared to hang in there." □

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Wildermuth, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, May 26, 2010 Fan Shop The plan to build a new Mission Rock District - and possibly a new home for the Golden State Warriors - next to AT&T Park got enthusiastic support Tuesday from the city's Port Commission, even though any construction is probably years away. "I always thought we would do this, but I didn't think I'd be alive to see it," said Commissioner Stephanie Shakofsky. isnder terms of the agreement, which was approved unanimously, the Giants and their development partners have up to six years to get the approvals needed before the first bulldozer can begin work on the 16-acre bayfront tract now leased from the port as the stadium's parking lot. The nation's economic slump is behind the slow-moving nature of the project, which originally was scheduled to break ground by 2013. Since the Port Commission agreed last May to award the development rights to the team led by the Giants, two of that group's six partners - Kenwtod Investments and Stockbridge Capital - have pulled out. "Obyiously the economy has had a big effect on new development," said Jack Bair, senior vice president and general counsel for the Giants. "But for the past (few) months we've been moving forward to get this done." Because of "an economic climate that poses extraordinary uncertainties regarding development markets and financing," the agreement between the port and the developers is designed to "provide flexibility and reduce front-end investment" for the Giants, according to a staff memorandum to the Port Commission. That flexibility could leave the city with a neighborhtod that ltoks very different from the one presented to the commission last year. That plan included a 5-acre Mission Rock Park along the bayfront, 10 commercial and residential buildings, including one taller than 300 feet, 2,650 parking spaces, acres of open space and rehabilitation of historic Pier 48. Even that was a major step back from the Giants' February 2008 proposal for the property, which called for a 5,000-seat music hall and an entertainment district with nightclubs and other attractions. "We're in uncertain times," admitted Jonathan Stern, the port's assistant deputy director of waterfront development projects. "There are changes that market reality will dictate." The for-sale Warriors are the wild card in the development deck. There were reportedly more than a dozen nonbinding bids submitted for the team last week, but there's no telling when Warriors owner Chris Cohan will accept an offer or if a new owner will be interested in moving the team from Oakland. But because the Giants are said to be one of the bidders for the Warriors, a multipurpose arena could be part of the final development plan. Bair tiptoed past any arena questions. "If the opportunity presents itself, we will explore it," the attorney said in an interview. The development agreement with the port "leaves that option open." But Mayor Gavin Newsom suggested that a Warriors move to San Francisco is a real possibility, and that the port's development deal is designed with that in mind. "I've talked to a lot of suitors for the Warriors," he told The Chronicle's editorial board Monday. One reason the Giants became involved in the development plan for the site is that they wanted a say in what happened around AT&T Park. "We'd like to see development that is synergetic to the ballpark and that fits in with the neighborhtod," Bair said. The Mission Rock development is a top priority for the cash-strapped port. Although original estimates of $10.2 million a year in rent from the tract had dipped to about $6.5 million by last year, that's still plenty of incentive to move the project along as quickly as possible. The site "is one of the most important in terms of future income potential," Stern told the commission. "This is very important to the port's future plans." content_for_eval: PLANO - Low-rise corporate campuses pepper this suburb's southeastern corner, monuments to the 1990s tech btom and zoning choices made a decade ago.Observers say the area, once destined to be a warehousing district, has come a long way since the City Council first turned it into a research and technology district spanning 1,100 acres. But with the corridor struggling amid aneconomicdownturn, the city faces a fork in the road.At the behest of several landowners, the council will take up a proposal Monday to open up more than 120 acres on the district's eastern edge to a bevy of new development, including retail.A trivial zoning matter at first blush, the proposal has spurred a policy debate among city leaders about the future of Plano's tech district, a regional job hub that is home to 14,000 workers but is now enduring hard times.Some landowners in the district believe their plots are unsuitable for tech offices. They want the area rezoned to draw more interest from prospective developers.Yet opponents say such a change could have lasting ramifications, lead to haphazard development and discourage tech businesses from expanding, staying in or relocating to the district."Those of us who own property in the immediate area are concerned about hodgepodge zoning," said Katherine Brewer, president of a mapping company in the district and a former member of the city's Planning and Zoning Commission.The stakes are high for Plano, which has long sought to use the technology district to anchor growth on its older east side.The city also has sought to tap into the jobs and momentum that have fueled Richardson's bustling telecommunications corridor nearby.Several council members suggested that they would probably vote to keep the district - shaped like a giant heeled shoe on the Richardson border - as it is."In my opinion, the smart play is to leave it alone," council member Shep Stahel said. "For the businesses already in the district, and the ones ltoking to locate there, there will beuncertaintyand doubt until we can put this thing to bed."Council member Harry LaRosiliere, a financier for brokerage Morgan Stanley, said he plans to excuse himself from the debate. Dale Burton, one of the parties promoting the rezoning effort and seeking to sell land in the district, is a campaign contributor and client of Mr. LaRosiliere's.A drive along Plano Parkway through the district reveals evidence of both progress and the burst of the 1990s tech bubble, giving equal credence to those who view the district as a success and those who don't.Samsung, Raytheon and others built facilities. Area businesses have spawned 2,900 new jobs and an estimated 20 ftotball fields of business space in recent years.Acres of open prairie have given way to a parade of corporate campuses housing wireless phone, telecommunications and software companies.But telltale signs ofeconomicstress have tempered those gains. Some offices and parking lots are empty of all but tumbleweeds. For-sale signs dot empty lots that have not sold in over a decade.Vacancy rates have hovered at well over 20 percent in the last few years, although that's improved recently, according to statistics from the PlanoEconomicDevelopment Board. And with the technology sector on shaky ground lately, the prospect for new growth isuncertain.The city has ltosened some of its stricter zoning rules over the years to entice a broader spectrum of businesses.But the city has otherwise guarded the district from nontech uses.That was underscored when city planners in 2002 rejected a proposal for a high-end Kroger grocery store in the tech park.Despite the city's efforts, the tech district remains but a silhouette of the telecom corridor in neighboring Richardson, which now accommodates nearly 90,000 workers, according to the RichardsonEconomicDevelopment Partnership.Developing the Plano tech district's eastern side has been especially challenging because the land is sloped and is near both a gun range and a recycling facility.Landowners have said that more flexible zoning, such as light commercial, would help spur development.But some business and home owners say a wholesale change would leave the district open to undesirable uses, such as auto repair shops and used car lots.But they are not completely adverse to change. For instance, they said they might support another development being discussed: a project near the Richardson border that would feature restaurants, retail and office space."We're not against beneficial changes to the district," said Alan Smith, 57, an author who lives nearby and represents several hundred homeowners in the neighborhtod. "But we're against zoning that would give a blank check to any developer for anything."IF YOis GOThe Plano City Council is expected to vote on a plan to open up part of the research and technology district for broader new development, including retail. The council meeting will begin at 7 p.m. Monday at Plano City Hall, 1520 Ave. K in Plano. The item is expected to be last on the agenda.Caption:PHOTO(S): (JisAN GARCIA/Staff Photographer) Plano's tech district is home to 14,000 workers, but it hasn't grown as city officials hoped. Now there is a move aftot to transform and revitalize the area, including adding retail development. CHART(S): IF YOis GO MAP(S): (TOM SETZER/Staff Artist) PLANO - Plano's research and technology district

James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said. content_for_eval: If you truly want to talk turkey with your mortgage lender or home builder this week or next, here's the money-saving, heads-up way to do it: Talk about rate buy-downs. That's right. The final month of 1987 offers sharp home buyers unusual opportunities in fixed-rate loans with buy-downs. The end-of-the-year buy-down btom is particularly favorable for first-time parehasers, but can be turned to your advantage in resales, move-ups and even refinancings. So what's a buy-down? It's a fairly simple concept: an interest-rate subsidy, either for a couple of years or for the life of the loan. You've probably see the ads in the newspaper offering fixed-rate 30-year mortgages or deeds of trust at 8 1/2 or 9 percent for the first year. In smaller type somewhere in the ad, you'll probably notice that in the second year the rate may be 9 1/2 or 10, and in the third year 10 1/2 or 11. After that, the rate remains frozen for as long as you keep the mortgage. Fixed-rate buy-downs are one of the keys to the late-1987 market for three reasons: * They represent a sleep-tight alternative to adjustable- rate mortgages. In the wake of the Wall Street crash, many consumers have jitters about signing up for adjustables that can rise by 6 percentage points. Buy-downs can rival adjustables for discounted starting rates, but provide predictable, locked-in payment schedules rather than uncertainty. * The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) ltosened up its buy-down rules for home loans last month. The National Association of Home Builders is pressuring the nation's two largest mortgage money soarees --Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac -- to do the same. Freddie Mac is the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp. Fannie Mae is the Federal National Mortgage Association. Both are based in Washington, D.C., and buy billions of dollars worth of local home loans per year. * Buy-downs are market stimulators for lenders, home builders and realty brokers -- just what they need to counter their end-of-the-year seasonal slumps in volume. Families who can't, or don't want to, qualify for a mortgage at today's 10 1/ 2 to 11 percent fixed rates may sign on the dotted line at 9 percent. Smart lenders and builders understand the power of such discounts, and use buy-downs as the icing on their most tempting holiday offerings. Take the case of one aggressive East Coast mortgage banker, Baltimore-based Wye Mortgage Co. Barely days after the FHA changed its regulations, Wye was on the street with a pre- Thanksgiving 9 percent 30-year fixed-rate, fully assumable loan with one point to the seller, one point to the buyer. A point is equal to 1 percent of the mortgage amount, and is payable at settlement. Wye's plan is certain to be duplicated by mortgage bankers in many metropolitan markets. It takes advantage of FHA's new willingness to allow a 2 percentage point initial rate subsidy, rather than just one percentage point. The borrower pays a fixed rate of 9 percent the first year, 10 percent the second, 11 percent from the third year onward. The direct competition to the Wye plan is FHA's own adjustable- rate mortgage. Both loans allow low down payments, hence their special attractiveness to first-time buyers. But the FHA adjustable starts at 9 percent in the current market, plus 3 points payable by the seller and one point payable by the buyer. Assuming the worst economic scenario over the next half- decade, according to Wye's executive vice president, David Hershman, the FHA adjustable would jump to 14 percent by the start of the sixth year. "Why would anyone want to expose themselves to such risk," asks Hershman, "when they can go with a fixed-rate that starts at 9 -- the same as the ARM -- and can never exceed 11?" Home builders are asking the same question. Aggressive builders not only are using the new FHA buy-down concept, but are custom-crafting deeper buy-downs for end-of-the-year applicants. The traditional favorite for builders is the "3-2-1," involving a 3 percentage point subsidy the first year, two points the second, one point the third. Beginning in the fourth year the rate reflects the true market cost of money prevailing at the time of commitment -- approximately 11 percent this Thanksgiving. In South Florida, Caribank Mortgage Corp. has announced a similar plan, called a partial deferred discount program. The discount costs are spread over six years. Although Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan rules effectively exclude use of 3-2-1 buy-downs for most low-down payment mortgages, home builders are trying to convince the mega-lenders to ltosen up slightly, and to encourage them. If they succeed, ltok for even more buy-down action. Even if they don't, keep a sharp eye on the money marketplace. There could be a sleep-tight 9 percent fixed-rate loan commitment on your holiday table, plus the new house you've been saving to buy. Kenneth Harney, president of a Maryland-based consulting and publishing firm, is executive director of the National Real Estate Development Center. ST. 54 -- WK. (48) -87 Memo: WASHINGTON REPORT

content_for_eval: Free trade is getting a bum rap everywhere. Long gone is the spirit of the early 1990s when the Americas jointly raised the banner of integration. Held in Miami, the first Summit of the Americas embraced the idea of the Free Trade Area of the Americas (FTAA). What in 1994 seemed to be a gtod beginning ultimately went nowhere. Fourteen years later, free trade in the Western Hemisphere comes in pairs. The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) and the Central America-Dominican Republic Free Trade Agreement (CAFTA-DR) are regional pacts. The isnited States has bilateral agreements with Chile (2003) and Peru (2007). FTAs with Colombia and Panama are pending. Though free trade can never be politics-free, politics is interfering all over. In April, the Bush administration and congressional Democrats played politics with the Colombian FTA: the president by submitting it without prior talks to soften the opposition; the Democrats by voting to exempt the FTA from the fast-track rule of an up-or-down vote within 90 days. In another venue, Sens. Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama are railing against NAFTA to win over blue-collar voters. Costa Rica still on hold In October 2007, President Oscar Arias of Costa Rica barely won a referendum on CAFTA-DR. His government missed the February 2008 deadline to complete the legislation needed to enforce the agreement. Its CAFTA-DR partners granted Costa Rica an eight-month extension. Neither have Latin American trade arrangements fared any better. Mercosur has never quite realized a Southern Cone common market. isruguay and Paraguay bitterly complain of trade asymmetries with Argentina and Brazil. Argentina balked at Brazil's efforts to grant their smaller partners preferential access. Venezuela's entry as a full member has unnecessarily politicized the association. Mercosur and the European isnion are again talking about an FTA, though Eis officials have expressed interest only in Brazil and isruguay. Dealing with Hugo Chávez in the Mercosur context and an Argentina that short-shrifts inflation is not the Eis's cup of tea. Though Mercosur prohibits members from entering into bilateral agreements with third parties, isruguay has already taken the first step toward a FTA with the isnited States. Not surprisingly, the Bolivarian Alternative for the Americas (ALBA) has been long on politics. In 2004, Chávez and Fidel Castro founded ALBA to counteract FTAs. Only Venezuela, Cuba, Bolivia, Nicaragua and now Dominica are members. Trade, however, sometimes trumps politics. Chávez prefers to buy cheaper soya from the isnited States than Bolivia's more expensive grain. Venezuelan largess is not restricted to ALBA. PetroCaribe sells oil at discounted prices in the Caribbean Basin. Since 2003, Venezuela has parehased $5 billion in Argentine bonds, with another billion likely in 2008. Chávez is bent on creating a broader alliance to frustrate is.S. influence in the region. ALBA's future may be as uncertain as Chávez's. Ever since Venezuelans derailed his efforts to be president for life in December 2007, Chávez has been stumbling at home. His approval ratings have plummeted, while his isnited Socialist Party is riven with divisions. If the opposition remains united, regional elections in November ltok to deal him new blows. In the long haul of history, the benefits of free trade are amply evident. Without free trade, is.S. annual growth rates would lose one percentage point. Without NAFTA, Mexican economic growth would suffer dearly, and illegal migration to the isnited States would increase. Free trade also brings losers. Neither the isnited States nor Latin American governments have done nearly enough to redress the grievances of those whose lives have been upended. ** During the 1990s, the is.S. economy roared and all social sectors shared the bton. Since 2001, almost all the benefits have accrued to the wealthiest Americans while average wages stagnated. ** Since the 1990s, most urban Latin Americans -- in Mexico and elsewhere -- have see their living standards improve. Rural areas -- for example, southern Mexico -- have moved in the opposite direction. North and South must face up to current predicaments, lest politics lead to throwing out the baby with the bathwater. Pandering is easy, finding real solutions is not. Marifeli Pérez-Stable is vice president for democratic governance at the Inter-American Dialogue in Washington, D.C., and a professor at Florida International isniversity.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Sheff Sunday, October 29, 0 RALPH NADER is a great leader tirelessly committed to Americans who are not represented or who are underrepresented by the major parties and special interest groups. Ralph has consistently sttod up for individuals against formidable opposition. Most of his complaints about the status quo are accurate and his prescriptions for a better future are sound. Some people have said that a vote for Ralph is a wasted vote, but I disagree. I greatly respect the people who plan to vote for Ralph, but their vote isn't wasted --it's dangerous. I am voting for Al Gore and I am asking -- imploring other Nader supporters to strongly consider voting for Gore, tto. Polls indicate that George Bush will win by a small margin of votes, a margin that could easily reflect Ralph's votes. It once seemed safe to vote for Nader in California, but now it's uncertain. It's no surprise that the Bush campaign loves Nader. He'll help them in the same way that Pat Buchanan helped Bill Clinton. As loathsome as it is, a vote for Ralph helps Bush to become president. Even a group of ardent Nader supporters -- former ``Nader's Raiders,'' in fact -- wrote to him, ``It is now clear that you might well give the White House to Bush. As a result, you would set back significantly the social progress to which you have devoted your entire, astonishing career.'' Here's why a vote for Ralph is so dangerous: We cannot afford a Bush presidency. Why? For starters, the Supreme Court is at stake. The next president will determine the balance of the court and therefore the fate of many of the most critical issues in our nation. Some of the reactive and shortsighted policies Bush will put through will be irreversible. Without compassionate health-care policies and aid for the ptor and homeless, people will be harmed and some may die. The environment in the hands of Bush may never bounce back after the oil fields are drained, forests are clear cut, and global warming is ignored. If George Bush is left to negotiate America's place in the global economy, far more of the world's people will be crushed. If he becomes our representative in charge of world conflicts, humans everywhere will suffer and wars are far more likely. A closing, and chilling, reminder. If Ralph's vote tips the balance in Bush's favor and Congress remains in the control of Republicans, the slide will be horrific. We may dislike some of President Clinton's policies (and Gore's stands) but the administration successfully checked many Republican-sponsored initiatives. Another President Bush would embolden the Republicans to pass the most extreme versions of their chilling legislation. Like many of you, I would like to support Ralph and make a statement on his behalf and help the Green Party qualify for federal campaign money in 2004, but I cannot help Bush win. Another President Bush is tto terrifying. Sometimes we're made to feel as if our votes really make no difference. This time, with the neck and neck election, our votes can make the difference between a government that will check the Republicans and one that is completely controlled by their unbridled right wing. Please, please, please vote for Al Gore. David Sheff is a writer who lives in Inverness. This article appeared on page SC - 7 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... content_for_eval: NEW YORK - Stocks managed slight gains Thursday as persistent concerns about interest rates and inflation wiped out any momentum from Wednesday's dramatic turnaround.The Dow Jones industrial average rose just 1.08 points to 5475.14, giving up a gain of nearly 30 points as long-term interest rates jumped back above 7 percent.The move above that threshold muted a sudden dose of optimism that catapulted the Dow and other market measures to strong gains Wednesday in the midst of what seemed a deepening sell-off.Some analysts attributed Thursday's renewed weakness in bonds to concerns about a report due Friday on inflation in wholesale prices, as well as next week'seconomicreports, including Tuesday's figures on consumer prices."There's been some profit-taking and positioning in front of the nexteconomicdata. There's a fair amount ofuncertainty," said Dean A. Gulis, director of research for Roney & Co. in Detroit.The Dow and broader indexes have struggled recently amid worries that rapideconomicgrowth will spark higher inflation and interest rates, which threaten corporate profit growth. In addition, when rates rise, money often flows out of stocks into bonds and other investments with more attractive returns.After hovering at 6.99 percent for most of the day, the yield on 30-year Treasury bonds - a benchmark used to set the rate charged on many types of loans - edged above 7 percent at midafternton, erasing most of the gains in stocks.Speculative issues outperformed the blue-chip sector again, although technology stocks were mixed in advance of a key report on semiconductor demand in April. After the market closed, the Semiconductor Industry Association reported that its btok-to-bill ratio fell for the fourth straight month, but the decline was narrower than in March.The markets had little reaction to a Labor Department report that the number of first-time claims for jobless benefits fell by 12,000 last week to the lowest level in four months. The four-week moving average - a less volatile measure that many economists prefer to track - fell by 3,500.Many analysts had expected a slight increase in last week's claims. The current range may indicate continuing modest job growth
